topics.title: Energy Scenario: Commercial and Non-commercial Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Scenario: Commercial and Non-commercial Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Energy Scenario — Commercial and Non-Commercial Energy

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Global and National Energy Scenario — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> The **Energy Scenario** refers to the comprehensive study of the structure, availability, consumption patterns, sectoral distribution, and policy framework of energy resources within a geographic boundary (national or global). It classifies energy into **Commercial Energy** (bought and sold in the organized market, accounted for in monetary statistics) and **Non-Commercial Energy** (traditional, unmonetized, gathered from nature for household use).

In the context of **KTU PESFT414**, Module 1 establishes the macro picture of how a developing economy like India consumes energy — this foundational knowledge is later used to justify *why* energy conservation, energy auditing, and energy-efficient design are critical engineering interventions.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Two Wallets of a Country":**
> Imagine a country's energy like a household budget. A working family has **two wallets**:
>
> 1. **Wallet A (Commercial Energy)** → The salary account. Money flows through banks, gets audited, has tax receipts. Examples: electricity from KSEB, petrol from IOCL, LPG cylinders, natural gas from GAIL.
> 2. **Wallet B (Non-Commercial Energy)** → The kitchen garden. Tomatoes, herbs, firewood collected from the backyard — never purchased, never counted in the GDP, but absolutely essential to survival.
>
> A country that relies *only* on Wallet A looks "modern" in statistics but is fragile (e.g., Europe during the 2022 gas crisis). A country that uses Wallet B heavily looks "poor" in statistics but is energy-resilient at the household level (e.g., rural India, sub-Saharan Africa). India sits at a transition — *shrinking Wallet B while rapidly expanding Wallet A*.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Energy Scenario Analysis

The following universal indicators are critical for any Energy Scenario study (used by IEA, MNRE, CEA, and BEE):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Scenario Standard Metrics:**
> - **Total Primary Energy Supply (TPES)** — measured in **Mtoe** (Million Tonnes of Oil Equivalent) or **Exajoules (EJ)**
> - **Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC)** — measured in **toe/capita/year** or **kWh/capita/year**
> - **Energy Intensity of GDP (EI)** — measured in **toe per million USD of GDP** (PPP-adjusted)
> - **Energy Mix (%)** — percentage share of coal, oil, gas, nuclear, hydro, renewables
> - **Import Dependency (%)** — ratio of net energy imports to total energy availability

---

### 1.4 Definition Box — Commercial vs Non-Commercial Energy

| Feature | Commercial Energy | Non-Commercial Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Market transaction | Bought and sold formally | No monetary transaction |
| Statistical accounting | Counted in GDP/national accounts | Invisible in formal statistics |
| Typical sources | Coal, oil, gas, electricity, nuclear, grid renewables | Firewood, cattle dung, agricultural residue, charcoal |
| Users | Industry, transport, urban households | Rural households, traditional kitchens |
| Quality & efficiency | High calorific value, engineered | Low efficiency, high smoke/CO₂ |
| Environmental impact | Large-scale emissions | Deforestation, indoor air pollution |
| Storage & logistics | Traded as commodity, global supply chains | Locally collected, seasonal |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Energy Sources — Structured Logic

Energy on Earth originates primarily from the **Sun** (renewable flows) and **radioactive decay** (nuclear). From an engineering and policy standpoint, the KTU 2024 scheme classifies energy into the following hierarchy:

### A. Primary vs Secondary Energy
- **Primary Energy**: Found in nature — coal, crude oil, natural gas, uranium, sunlight, wind, falling water.
- **Secondary Energy**: Derived from primary sources through conversion — electricity (from coal/hydro/nuclear/wind), petrol/diesel/kerosene (refined from crude), LPG (from natural gas processing), charcoal (from biomass carbonization).

### B. Commercial vs Non-Commercial (Module 1 Focus)
1. **Commercial Energy**
   - **Fossil Fuels**: Coal (lignite → bituminous → anthracite), Lignite, Crude Oil, Natural Gas.
   - **Electrical Energy** (hydro, thermal, nuclear, and grid-connected renewables).
   - **Refined Petroleum Products**: HSD (High Speed Diesel), LDO (Light Diesel Oil), FO (Furnace Oil), Naphtha, Kerosene, LPG.
   - **Grid-Scale Renewable Energy**: Solar PV, Onshore/Offshore Wind, Biomass power, Small Hydro, Geothermal.

2. **Non-Commercial Energy**
   - **Fuelwood** (firewood collected from forests/farms).
   - **Agricultural Residues** (rice husk, sugarcane bagasse, groundnut shell, coconut fronds).
   - **Dung cakes** (dried animal excreta used as fuel).
   - **Vegetable waste** and **charcoal** (in rural areas).
   - **Draught animal power** (bullocks, horses used for ploughing/transport — a *mechanical* form of non-commercial energy).

### C. Renewable vs Non-Renewable
- **Renewable**: Solar, Wind, Hydro (run-of-river), Biomass (sustainable cycle), Geothermal, Tidal.
- **Non-Renewable**: Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Uranium (finite geological reserves).

---

## 2.2 India's Energy Scenario — Key Statistics (Latest CEA / MNRE Data)

> [!NOTE]
> **India Snapshot (Indicative figures, KTU Reference):**
> - India is the **3rd largest primary energy consumer** globally (after China and USA).
> - **Per capita consumption** ≈ 0.72 toe/capita (vs world average ~1.7, USA ~7.0) — India has 18% of world population but consumes only ~6% of primary energy.
> - **Energy Intensity** of Indian GDP has been improving (~0.20 toe per 1000 USD PPP in 2022, down from 0.27 in 2000).
> - **Import dependency** for crude oil > 85%, for natural gas > 50%, for coal ~25%.
> - **Installed power capacity** (as of 2024) ≈ 430 GW+ (renewables crossing 180 GW).

---

## 2.3 Sectoral Energy Consumption Pattern in India

```
Industry          ███████████████░░░░░  ~ 41%
Transport         ██████████░░░░░░░░░░  ~ 25%
Residential       ███████░░░░░░░░░░░░░  ~ 17%
Agriculture       ████░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  ~ 10%
Commercial/Other  ███░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  ~  7%
```

*(Approximate shares — refer to latest CEA Annual Report for board-exam accuracy.)*

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Conversion Table (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize This Table — Appears in Most 14-Mark Questions:**

| # | Quantity | Formula / Conversion | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Oil equivalent (general) | $1 \text{ toe} = 42 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh}_{\text{th}}$ | toe |
| 2 | Coal equivalent | $1 \text{ tce} = 0.7 \text{ toe}$ (approx, depends on grade) | tce |
| 3 | Calorific value — coal | $GCV_{coal} \approx 20{,}900 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (bituminous) | kJ/kg |
| 4 | Calorific value — fuelwood | $GCV_{wood} \approx 16{,}000 \text{–} 20{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$ (air-dry) | kJ/kg |
| 5 | Calorific value — dung cake | $GCV_{dung} \approx 8{,}000 \text{–} 12{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | kJ/kg |
| 6 | Per Capita Energy Consumption | $PCEC = \dfrac{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumption}}{\text{Total Population}}$ | toe/capita/yr |
| 7 | Energy Intensity of GDP | $EI = \dfrac{\text{TPES (toe)}}{\text{GDP (Million USD, PPP)}}$ | toe / M USD |
| 8 | Import Dependency | $ID = \left(\dfrac{\text{Net Imports}}{\text{Total Availability}}\right) \times 100$ | % |
| 9 | Energy Conservation (savings) | $\% \text{ Saving} = \left(\dfrac{E_{base} - E_{new}}{E_{base}}\right) \times 100$ | % |
| 10 | Electrical heating equivalent | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ} = 860 \text{ kcal}$ | kWh |

> [!NOTE]
> **Latex Isolation Tip:** All `|` in equations use `\vert` to prevent breaking markdown table syntax (per V10 protocol).

---

## 2.5 Why This Topic Matters in Engineering Practice

Every industrial energy audit (covered in later modules) starts with a **baseline energy study** — engineers quantify the *commercial energy* entering a plant and the *non-commercial energy* (e.g., waste heat recovery, biomass in boilers) entering/leaving the boundary. Without understanding the macro scenario (national energy mix, imports, intensity), the auditor cannot benchmark plant performance against the sector best-practice.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Per Capita Energy Consumption Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-Style):**
> In a KTU past-paper style question: "India's primary energy consumption in 2022 was approximately 830 Mtoe, with a population of 1.42 billion. Calculate the per capita energy consumption in toe/capita/year and in GJ/capita/year."

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**

From the KTU Formula Sheet, the per capita energy consumption is:

$$
PCEC = \frac{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumption (toe)}}{\text{Total Population}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

Convert units so that Total Energy is in **toe** and Population in **persons (absolute number)**.

$$
PCEC = \frac{830 \times 10^{6} \text{ toe}}{1.42 \times 10^{9} \text{ persons}}
$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the exponent first.**

The powers of 10 are:

$$
10^{6} \div 10^{9} = 10^{(6-9)} = 10^{-3}
$$

So the expression becomes:

$$
PCEC = \frac{830}{1.42} \times 10^{-3} \text{ toe/person}
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division.**

$$
\frac{830}{1.42} = 584.51 \text{ (approx.)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
PCEC = 584.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ toe/person}
$$

**Step 5 — Finalize the answer.**

$$
PCEC \approx 0.585 \text{ toe/capita/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to GJ/capita/year using $1 \text{ toe} = 42 \text{ GJ}$.**

$$
PCEC_{GJ} = 0.585 \times 42 \text{ GJ/capita/yr}
$$

$$
PCEC_{GJ} = 24.57 \text{ GJ/capita/yr}
$$

**Interpretation**: This is a *low* per capita figure compared to the world average (~71 GJ/capita/yr) — confirming India's classification as a developing economy with significant energy-access challenges.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Energy Intensity of GDP

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A country has a TPES of 600 Mtoe and a GDP of 3,000 Billion USD (PPP). Calculate its energy intensity.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the formula.**

$$
EI = \frac{\text{TPES (toe)}}{\text{GDP (Million USD, PPP)}}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert GDP to Million USD.**

$$
3{,}000 \text{ Billion USD} = 3{,}000 \times 10^{3} \text{ Million USD} = 3 \times 10^{6} \text{ M USD}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert TPES to toe (base unit).**

$$
600 \text{ Mtoe} = 600 \times 10^{6} \text{ toe} = 6 \times 10^{8} \text{ toe}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the formula.**

$$
EI = \frac{6 \times 10^{8} \text{ toe}}{3 \times 10^{6} \text{ M USD}}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$
EI = 2 \times 10^{(8-6)} = 2 \times 10^{2}
$$

$$
EI = 200 \text{ toe / Million USD}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare with benchmarks.**

- World average: ~115 toe/M USD
- European Union: ~75 toe/M USD
- India: ~200 toe/M USD (historically high)
- USA: ~150 toe/M USD

A *higher* EI means more energy is needed per unit of economic output — indicating inefficiency. Reducing EI is a core goal of energy conservation programs.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Energy Savings Percentage

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A factory's baseline annual electricity consumption was 5,00,000 kWh. After energy conservation measures (ECM), it reduced to 3,75,000 kWh. Calculate the % savings.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the % savings formula.**

$$
\% \text{ Saving} = \left( \frac{E_{base} - E_{new}}{E_{base}} \right) \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
\% \text{ Saving} = \left( \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}75{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
5{,}00{,}000 - 3{,}75{,}000 = 1{,}25{,}000 \text{ kWh saved}
$$

**Step 4 — Form the ratio.**

$$
\frac{1{,}25{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000} = 0.25
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage.**

$$
\% \text{ Saving} = 0.25 \times 100 = 25\%
$$

This 25% reduction is a strong performance figure — it meets BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency, India) "Star Rating" improvement criteria.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Energy Sources (Board-Exam Favorite)

| Energy Source | Type | Renewable? | GCV (approx, kJ/kg) | CO₂ Emission Factor (kg CO₂/GJ) | Cost (INR/GJ, indicative) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bituminous Coal | Commercial | No | 20,900 | 95–105 | 400–700 |
| Natural Gas | Commercial | No | 52,000 | 55–60 | 600–900 |
| Furnace Oil | Commercial | No | 42,000 | 75–80 | 1,200–1,800 |
| LPG | Commercial | No | 46,000 | 60–65 | 1,500–2,200 |
| Fuelwood (air-dry) | Non-Commercial | Renewable (if sustainable) | 16,000 | 0 (biogenic, but with deforestation cost) | Negligible (collection cost) |
| Dung cake | Non-Commercial | Renewable | 10,000 | ~0 (biogenic) | Negligible |
| Bagasse | Non-Commercial | Renewable | 18,000 | 0 (biogenic) | Negligible |
| Solar PV (electric) | Commercial (grid-tied) | Renewable | N/A (electrical) | ~45 (life-cycle) | 800–1,200 |
| Wind (electric) | Commercial | Renewable | N/A (electrical) | ~10 (life-cycle) | 700–1,100 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that although non-commercial biomass has *near-zero* monetary cost, its true cost is **environmental** (deforestation, indoor air pollution causing ~4 million premature deaths globally per WHO). This hidden cost is why **energy transition from non-commercial to commercial renewables** is a sustainable development goal.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Differentiate between commercial and non-commercial energy with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (Board-Exam Pattern):**

| Parameter | Commercial Energy | Non-Commercial Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Energy bought and sold in the market | Energy collected and consumed without market transaction |
| Examples | Coal, oil, gas, electricity, LPG | Firewood, dung cakes, agricultural residue |
| Accounting | Counted in GDP/national statistics | Not counted in formal statistics |
| Users | Industry, transport, urban sector | Predominantly rural households |
| Quality | High calorific value, engineered fuels | Low calorific value, smoky |
| Environmental cost | High CO₂ emissions | Deforestation, indoor pollution |

(3 marks — concise table format preferred for full credit.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Define per capita energy consumption and state India's approximate value.**

**Model Answer:**

Per capita energy consumption is the average energy available per person per year, computed as:

$$
PCEC = \frac{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumption (toe)}}{\text{Total Population}}
$$

Unit: **toe/capita/year** (or kWh/capita/year for electricity).

India's PCEC ≈ **0.6 toe/capita/year**, far below the world average of ~1.7 toe/capita/year and the USA's ~7.0 toe/capita/year — indicating significant energy poverty despite being the 3rd largest energy consumer globally.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following (a) OR (b).**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the classification of energy sources with a neat hierarchy diagram. Differentiate clearly between primary, secondary, commercial, and non-commercial energy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the global and Indian energy scenario with reference to commercial and non-commercial energy shares. Explain the concept of per capita energy consumption and energy intensity of GDP with examples. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. Hierarchy of Energy Sources (3 marks):**
- **Primary Energy** → found in nature (coal, crude oil, sun, wind).
- **Secondary Energy** → derived from primary (electricity, petrol, LPG).
- **Commercial** → market-traded (fossil fuels, grid power).
- **Non-Commercial** → non-traded (firewood, dung, crop residue).
- **Renewable** → replenishable (solar, wind, hydro).
- **Non-Renewable** → finite (coal, oil, gas, uranium).

**2. Commercial Energy Sources (2 marks):**
- Coal (thermal power, industry).
- Crude Oil → refined to petrol/diesel/kerosene/LPG.
- Natural Gas (CNG, fertilizer feedstock, power).
- Electricity (grid — hydro + thermal + nuclear + renewable).
- New commercial renewables (solar, wind, biomass power).

**3. Non-Commercial Energy Sources (2 marks):**
- Firewood (rural cooking, ~80% rural household energy in 1970s).
- Agricultural residues (rice husk, bagasse, groundnut shell).
- Dung cakes and charcoal.
- Draught animal power (mechanical energy).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**1. Global Scenario (2 marks):**
- World TPES ≈ 14,000 Mtoe.
- Fossil fuels still ~80% of global mix.
- China + USA + India = >50% of consumption.
- Per capita: USA ~7, Europe ~3, World ~1.7, India ~0.6 toe/capita.

**2. Indian Scenario (3 marks):**
- India: 3rd largest consumer, but low per capita.
- Coal remains dominant (~45% of primary mix).
- Crude oil import dependency >85%.
- Installed power capacity >430 GW (renewables >180 GW by 2024).
- Energy transition target: 500 GW non-fossil by 2030, Net Zero by 2070.

**3. Per Capita & Energy Intensity (2 marks):**

Demonstrated with formulas and numerical values (use Worked Examples 1 & 2 from Section 3).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With neat sketches/flowcharts, explain the energy flow from primary source to end-use sectors in India. Identify the major sectoral consumers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing plant consumed 8,00,000 kWh annually. After implementing an energy management program, the consumption dropped to 6,00,000 kWh. Calculate the energy savings percentage. Also explain the significance of energy intensity and import dependency for a developing nation like India. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. Energy Flow Chart (4 marks):** (Refer to Mermaid Block Diagram in Section 4.2)
- Primary sources (coal, oil, gas, hydro, solar, wind, biomass).
- Conversion plants (thermal, hydro, refinery, nuclear).
- Secondary carriers (electricity, petroleum products, LPG).
- End-use sectors: Industry (41%), Transport (25%), Residential (17%), Agriculture (10%), Commercial (7%).

**2. Major Sectoral Consumers (3 marks):**
- **Industry**: steel, cement, fertilizers, chemicals, textiles.
- **Transport**: road (diesel/petrol), rail (electric + diesel), aviation (ATF), shipping (FO).
- **Residential**: lighting, fans, cooking (LPG/electric/biomass).
- **Agriculture**: irrigation pumps (electric/diesel), tractors.
- **Commercial**: offices, malls, hospitals, data centers.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**1. Energy Savings Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\% \text{ Saving} = \left( \frac{8{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Saving} = \left( \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \right) \times 100 = 25\%
$$

**2. Energy Intensity (2 marks):**

Defined as energy consumed per unit GDP — a measure of economic efficiency. India's EI is decreasing, indicating decoupling of growth from energy use (good).

**3. Import Dependency (2 marks):**

>85% for crude oil, >50% for natural gas → exposes India to geopolitical shocks (e.g., 1973, 1990 Gulf crises). Strategic petroleum reserves, renewable expansion, and energy efficiency are mitigation strategies.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "Primary" with "Commercial"** — they are *not* the same. Coal is primary AND commercial; firewood is primary but non-commercial.
> 2. **Unit errors in PCEC** — forgetting to convert Million → Billion or kWh → toe. Always show the unit conversion step.
> 3. **Memorizing stale statistics** — KTU may accept approximate figures, but absurdly old values (e.g., "India's population 100 crore") lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring diagrams** — Module 1 questions almost always expect a flow chart or classification diagram. Skipping it = −2 marks.
> 5. **Not using SI / standardized units** — write *toe/capita/yr*, not "toe per person".
> 6. **Writing paragraphs in a comparison question** — use *tables*; KTU examiners reward structured answers.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Energy Scenario** = macro study of availability, consumption, mix, and policy.
- [x] **Commercial Energy** = market-traded: coal, oil, gas, electricity, grid renewables.
- [x] **Non-Commercial Energy** = traditional: firewood, dung, crop residue, animal power.
- [x] **Primary Energy** = natural form; **Secondary Energy** = converted form.
- [x] **Renewable** = solar, wind, hydro, biomass, geothermal, tidal.
- [x] **Non-Renewable** = coal, oil, natural gas, uranium.
- [x] **PCEC formula**: $PCEC = TPES \div Population$ — units in **toe/capita/year**.
- [x] **Energy Intensity formula**: $EI = TPES \div GDP_{PPP}$ — units in **toe/M USD**.
- [x] **Import Dependency**: $ID = (Net Imports \div Total Availability) \times 100$.
- [x] **% Energy Savings**: $\% S = [(E_{base} - E_{new}) / E_{base}] \times 100$.
- [x] **India's status**: 3rd largest energy consumer, low per capita, high fossil share, transition toward renewables.
- [x] **Sectoral share (India)**: Industry ~41%, Transport ~25%, Residential ~17%, Agriculture ~10%, Commercial ~7%.
- [x] **Standard conversions**: $1 \text{ toe} = 42 \text{ GJ}$; $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ} = 860 \text{ kcal}$.
- [x] **Non-commercial energy** is invisible in GDP but vital for ~25–30% of Indian rural households — its decline must be matched by access to clean commercial alternatives (LPG, electricity under Saubhagya scheme).
- [x] **Always include a flow chart / hierarchy diagram** in 14-mark answers.
- [x] **Watch unit conversions in numerical problems** — examiners are strict here.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primary Energy Resources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Primary Energy Resources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Energy Resources** are naturally occurring energy forms in their **raw, unconverted state** that have **not been subjected to any artificial transformation or conversion process** by human activity. They exist naturally in the environment and serve as the **fundamental input** from which all secondary (derived) forms of energy are produced.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PESFT414)**, primary energy resources form the foundational block of energy management studies. They are classified along several orthogonal dimensions:

| Classification Axis | Categories | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| By Source Origin | Fossil, Nuclear, Renewable | Coal, Uranium, Solar |
| By Exhaustibility | Non-renewable, Renewable | Oil, Wind |
| By Commercial Use | Commercial, Non-commercial | Electricity, Firewood |
| By State of Aggregation | Solid, Liquid, Gaseous | Coal, Crude Oil, Natural Gas |

The **global primary energy supply mix** (as per IEA / World Energy Outlook framing used in KTU modules) is dominated by fossil fuels (~80%), with renewables and nuclear contributing the remainder.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Primary Energy like "Raw Ingredients in a Kitchen" 🍳**
>
> Imagine you are a chef. Before you can serve a cooked meal (useful energy like electricity, petrol, or hot water), you must first procure **raw ingredients** directly from the farm or market. These raw ingredients — rice, vegetables, spices, milk — are your *Primary Energy Resources*. The stove, blender, and oven (refineries, power plants, turbines) then convert them into finished dishes — your *Secondary Energy* (LPG, gasoline, electricity).
>
> **Key Intuition Point:** Just as a chef cannot cook without raw ingredients, no industrial civilization can produce useful work without first drawing on the Earth's natural energy endowments.

## 1.3 Sub-Categorization: Renewable vs Non-Renewable

### A. Non-Renewable Primary Energy
Resources that exist in **fixed quantities** on Earth and are **depleted** when consumed. They take **millions of years** to form through geological processes.

- **Coal** – Solid fossil fuel formed from decomposed plant matter.
- **Crude Oil (Petroleum)** – Liquid hydrocarbon mixture trapped in sedimentary rock.
- **Natural Gas** – Gaseous hydrocarbon (primarily **CH₄**, methane).
- **Nuclear (Uranium, Thorium)** – Radioactive heavy metals used in fission.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **renewable** with *non-exhaustible in human timescales*. Solar energy is technically exhaustible (the Sun will die in ~5 billion years), but it is **renewable on human timescales** because the rate of replenishment vastly exceeds the rate of consumption.

### B. Renewable Primary Energy
Resources that are **continuously replenished by natural processes** at a rate equal to or faster than the rate of consumption.

- **Solar** – Direct radiant energy from the Sun.
- **Wind** – Kinetic energy of atmospheric air masses.
- **Hydel (Hydroelectric)** – Potential + kinetic energy of flowing water.
- **Biomass** – Chemical energy stored in organic matter via photosynthesis.
- **Geothermal** – Heat from Earth's core.
- **Tidal / Wave** – Gravitational and kinetic energy of oceans.

## 1.4 Indian Energy Context (KTU-Favoured Data)

> [!NOTE]
> India is the **world's third-largest energy consumer** and **third-largest primary energy producer**. The installed power capacity in India is dominated by **coal-based thermal power (~50%)**, followed by **renewables (~30%, growing rapidly)**, **hydro (~10%)**, **gas (~6%)**, and **nuclear (~2%)**.

India's primary energy mix heavily relies on **coal**, but the government has set ambitious targets:
- **500 GW of non-fossil fuel capacity by 2030** (Panchamrit commitment at COP26).
- **Net-zero emissions by 2070**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Framework of Primary Energy

The conceptual flow of energy in any national economy follows a strict hierarchy:

1. **Extraction / Harvesting** – Coal is mined, oil is drilled, sunlight is captured.
2. **Conversion (Primary → Secondary)** – Crude oil → Petrol/Diesel; Coal → Electricity.
3. **Distribution & Storage** – Power grid transmission, fuel pipelines.
4. **End-Use Consumption** – Industrial, domestic, transportation, agricultural.

> **Why this matters in Energy Management:** You cannot "manage" or "conserve" what you don't measure. Studying primary energy resources gives us the **upstream baseline** for calculating **energy intensity** (energy used per unit GDP) and **carbon footprint**.

## 2.2 Quantification Metrics for Energy Resources

### A. Standard Energy Units

| Unit | Symbol | Equivalent in Joules | Usage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Joule | J | **1 J** | SI base unit |
| Kilojoule | kJ | **10³ J** | Food energy |
| Calorie | cal | **4.184 J** | Food energy (legacy) |
| Kilowatt-hour | kWh | **3.6 × 10⁶ J** | Electricity billing |
| Tonne of Oil Equivalent | TOE | **4.186 × 10¹⁰ J** | National energy accounting |
| British Thermal Unit | BTU | **1055 J** | HVAC, US context |

### B. Resource Reserve Metrics

- **Proven Reserves** – Quantity of a resource that can be **economically and technically recovered** with high confidence (typically **P90** probability, i.e., 90% probability of being producible).
- **Probable Reserves** – Estimated with **~50% probability** of recovery.
- **Possible Reserves** – Estimated with **~10% probability**.
- **Reserve-to-Production Ratio (R/P)** – Number of years the proven reserves would last if production continues at current rate.

### C. Energy Content (Heating Value) of Fuels

| Fuel Type | Calorific Value (Approx.) | Form |
|---|---|---|
| Coal (Anthracite) | **30,000 kJ/kg** | Solid |
| Coal (Bituminous) | **24,000 – 35,000 kJ/kg** | Solid |
| Fuel Wood (dry) | **16,000 – 20,000 kJ/kg** | Solid (biomass) |
| Diesel | **45,000 kJ/kg** | Liquid |
| Petrol/Gasoline | **47,000 kJ/kg** | Liquid |
| LPG | **46,000 kJ/kg** | Liquefied gas |
| Natural Gas | **38,000 kJ/Nm³** | Gaseous |
| Hydrogen | **142,000 kJ/kg** | Gaseous |
| Uranium (fission) | **~8 × 10¹³ J/kg** | Solid nuclear |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gross Calorific Value (GCV) / Higher Heating Value (HHV)** includes the latent heat of water vapor condensation. **Net Calorific Value (NCV) / Lower Heating Value (LHV)** excludes it. Engineering calculations in India typically use **GCV on an as-received basis** (IS 1350 standard).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{TOE} = \text{Energy in J} \div 4.186 \times 10^{10}$ | Tonne of oil equivalent | TOE |
| 2 | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ J}$ | Electrical energy conversion | J |
| 3 | $R/P \text{ Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Proven Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}$ | Resource lifetime estimate | Years |
| 4 | $\text{Energy Intensity} = \dfrac{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumed (TOE)}}{\text{GDP (constant USD)}}$ | Energy efficiency indicator | TOE/USD |
| 5 | $\text{Conversion Efficiency} \, \eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Primary Energy Input}} \times 100\%$ | Energy system performance | % |
| 6 | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat (thermal energy) | J (or kJ) |
| 7 | $Q = m \cdot L$ | Latent heat (phase change) | J (or kJ) |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{thermal}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}}$ | Carnot efficiency bound | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{EMR Penetration (\%)} = \dfrac{\text{EMR Generation (kWh)}}{\text{Total Generation (kWh)}} \times 100$ | Energy mix share | % |
| 10 | $\text{Solar Constant} \approx 1361 \text{ W/m}^2$ | Irradiance outside atmosphere | W/m² |

> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the *lingua franca* of energy audits, feasibility studies, and policy reports. In KTU ESE problems, you will most often use **Equations 1, 3, 4, 5, and 8**.

## 2.4 Energy Classification Flow (Conceptual Logic)

- **Step 1 — Origin Test:** Did the energy exist *before* any human conversion? → Yes → **Primary**.
- **Step 2 — Replenishment Test:** Does the rate of natural regeneration exceed the rate of human consumption? → Yes → **Renewable**.
- **Step 3 — Market Test:** Is the resource traded through formal commercial channels? → Yes → **Commercial**.
- **Step 4 — Stock vs Flow Test:** Can it be stockpiled (e.g., coal)? → Yes → **Stock energy**; else (e.g., sunlight) → **Flow energy**.

> **Why this matters:** Engineering systems handle stock and flow energies very differently. Stocks (fossil fuels) are dispatchable on demand; flows (solar) require storage or backup.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio and its Implications

The R/P ratio is a first-order predictor of how long a non-renewable resource can sustain current consumption patterns. Below is the full derivation framework.

### Step 1 — Define the variables

Let $R$ be the proven reserves (in TOE, tonnes, or cubic metres), and $P$ be the annual production rate (same unit per year).

The instantaneous rate of reserve depletion is given by:

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = -P(t)$$

> **Logic:** Reserves decrease when we extract them. The minus sign indicates a reduction. We assume no new discoveries during the period under study.

### Step 2 — Assume constant production rate

If production is held constant over time, $P(t) = P_0$, then the differential equation becomes:

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = -P_0$$

> **Logic:** Constant extraction means the depletion rate is steady — a simplifying assumption standard in KTU textbook problems.

### Step 3 — Integrate over the lifetime $T$

We integrate both sides from $t = 0$ (reserves = $R_0$) to $t = T$ (reserves = 0, i.e., fully exhausted):

$$\int_{R_0}^{0} dR = \int_{0}^{T} -P_0 \, dt$$

Evaluating the integrals:

$$0 - R_0 = -P_0 \cdot T$$

$$-R_0 = -P_0 \cdot T$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the lifetime $T$

Dividing both sides by $-P_0$:

$$T = \frac{R_0}{P_0}$$

> **Logic:** This is the **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio**. It directly gives the number of years the resource will last at current production rates.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A country has proven crude oil reserves of **8.0 billion TOE**. The current annual production is **0.5 billion TOE per year**. Find the R/P ratio.

**Solution:**

$$R/P = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{9} \text{ TOE}}{0.5 \times 10^{9} \text{ TOE/yr}} = 16 \text{ years}$$

> **Interpretation:** At the current production rate, the proven reserves will be exhausted in **16 years**. This is a strong policy signal to invest in renewables or alternative fuels.

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Intensity and the Carbon–GDP Linkage

Energy intensity is a key productivity metric in energy management.

### Step 1 — Define Energy Intensity (EI)

$$EI = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{G_{\text{GDP}}}$$

where $E_{\text{total}}$ is the total primary energy consumed (TOE) and $G_{\text{GDP}}$ is the GDP (constant USD, to remove inflation effects).

### Step 2 — Relate to carbon emissions

If $f_i$ is the fraction of energy from source $i$ and $C_i$ is the carbon emission factor (tonnes of CO₂ per TOE) for that source, total emissions are:

$$E_{\text{CO}_2} = E_{\text{total}} \cdot \sum_{i} f_i \cdot C_i$$

Substituting the definition of $EI$:

$$E_{\text{CO}_2} = EI \cdot G_{\text{GDP}} \cdot \sum_{i} f_i \cdot C_i$$

> **Logic:** For a given GDP, **lower EI and lower-carbon fuel mix** both reduce emissions. This is the conceptual foundation of *climate-conscious energy policy*.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of Primary Energy Resources

| Parameter | Coal | Crude Oil | Natural Gas | Nuclear | Solar | Wind | Hydro | Biomass |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **State** | Solid | Liquid | Gas | Solid (fuel) | Radiative | Kinetic | Gravitational | Chemical |
| **Renewable?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Reserves (Global, years at current use)** | ~130 | ~50 | ~50 | ~100+ (with breeder) | Inexhaustible (human time) | Inexhaustible | Inexhaustible | Inexhaustible |
| **CO₂ Emissions (g CO₂/kWh)** | ~820 | ~650 | ~490 | ~12 | ~40 (lifecycle) | ~11 | ~24 | Variable |
| **Conversion Efficiency (typical)** | 33–45% (thermal) | 25–40% (engine) | 35–60% (CCGT) | 33% (thermal) | 15–22% (PV) | 30–45% | 85–90% (turbine) | 20–40% |
| **Capital Cost** | Moderate | High | Moderate | Very High | High | Moderate | Very High | Low–Moderate |
| **Dispatchability** | ✅ High | ✅ High | ✅ High | ✅ High (baseload) | ❌ Intermittent | ❌ Intermittent | ✅ Moderate | ✅ Moderate |
| **Major Environmental Risk** | Air pollution, mining | Oil spills | Methane leak | Radioactive waste | Land use, e-waste | Avian, noise | Displacement, dams | Deforestation, land |
| **KTU Typical Use Case** | Thermal power | Transport | Industry, power | Baseload power | Distributed, rooftop | Grid utility | Large-scale grid | Rural, industrial |

> **Key Insight for KTU:** The **CO₂ emission factor** and **dispatchability** are the two most exam-relevant parameters. Dispatchable renewables (e.g., biomass, geothermal) carry premium value in energy management discussions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Energy Hierarchy and Conversion Pathways

The Mermaid block diagram below maps the conversion flow from **primary → secondary → end-use** — a question KTU often frames in 7-mark sub-parts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PRIMARY ENERGY SOURCES] --> B[NON RENEWABLE STOCK]
    A --> C[RENEWABLE FLOW]
    B --> B1[Coal]
    B --> B2[Crude Oil]
    B --> B3[Natural Gas]
    B --> B4[Uranium Thorium]
    C --> C1[Solar Radiation]

    C --> C3[Hydel Water]
    C --> C4[Biomass]
    C --> C5[Geothermal Tidal]
    B1 --> D[CONVERSION STAGE]

    B3 --> D
    B4 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> D1[Thermal Power Plant]
    D --> D2[Refinery]
    D --> D3[Nuclear Reactor]
    D --> D4[PV Wind Turbine]
    D --> D5[Biogas Digester]

    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E

    E3 --> F

```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of an Energy Management System Built on Primary Resources

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Survey of Primary Resources] --> P2[Reserve Estimation and R P Ratio]
    P2 --> P3[Demand Forecasting by Sector]
    P3 --> P4[Energy Mix Optimization]
    P4 --> P5[Conversion Technology Selection]
    P5 --> P6[Distribution and Storage]
    P6 --> P7[End Use Energy Audit]
    P7 --> P8[Conservation Strategy]
    P8 --> P9[Policy and Regulatory Feedback]
    P9 --> P1
```

> **Reading Guide:** The loop closes back to resource survey because **conservation outcomes** (e.g., demand reduction, efficiency gains) feed back into reserve planning, which is the essence of *adaptive energy management*.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Renewable vs Non-Renewable Resource Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Energy Resource] --> Q1{Is the resource naturally replenished on human timescale?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> R1[RENEWABLE PRIMARY]
    Q1 -- No --> R2[NON RENEWABLE PRIMARY]
    R1 --> R1A[Solar Wind Hydro]
    R1 --> R1B[Biomass Geothermal Tidal]
    R2 --> R2A[Coal Oil Gas]
    R2 --> R2B[Uranium Thorium]
    R1A --> N1[Convert via PV turbine generator]
    R1B --> N2[Convert via digester heat exchanger]
    R2A --> N3[Convert via combustion engine boiler]
    R2B --> N4[Convert via fission reactor]
    N1 --> OUT[Secondary Energy Output]
    N2 --> OUT
    N3 --> OUT
    N4 --> OUT
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between primary and secondary energy with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Primary Energy | Secondary Energy |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Energy in natural, unconverted form | Energy derived from primary after conversion |
| **Examples** | Coal, Crude Oil, Sunlight, Uranium | Electricity, Petrol, LPG, Hydrogen |
| **Usability** | Not directly usable in most end-applications | Directly usable in appliances, vehicles, machinery |
| **Conversion needed?** | Yes, to be useful | No, ready to use |

> **Examiner Tip:** Always include **two examples per category** in your answer. Mentioning sunlight as primary and electricity as secondary is a classic KTU-expected pairing.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is meant by "Reserve-to-Production Ratio"? Why is it significant for a non-renewable resource?**

**Model Answer:**
The **R/P ratio** is the ratio of the **proven reserves** of a resource to its **annual production rate**, expressed in years. It indicates how long the resource will last at the current extraction rate.

$$\text{R/P Ratio} = \frac{R}{P}$$

**Significance:**
- Acts as a **first-order depletion indicator** for non-renewable resources.
- Helps governments and planners decide on **exploration intensity**, **import policy**, and **renewable transition timelines**.
- A low R/P ratio (e.g., oil ~50 years globally) signals **energy security risk**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Convention:** Module 1 question allows an internal choice. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Classify the various **primary energy resources** with suitable examples. Discuss the merits and demerits of **renewable** and **non-renewable** sources. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Primary Energy Resources:**

**1. Non-Renewable (Conventional)**
- **Solid:** Coal, Lignite, Peat, Nuclear (Uranium).
- **Liquid:** Crude Oil, Shale Oil.
- **Gaseous:** Natural Gas, Coal Bed Methane.

**2. Renewable (Non-Conventional)**
- **Solar Energy** – Direct radiation and PV.
- **Wind Energy** – Kinetic energy of air.
- **Hydel Energy** – Potential/kinetic of water.
- **Biomass & Biogas** – Photosynthesis storage.
- **Geothermal** – Earth's internal heat.
- **Tidal and Wave** – Oceanic gravitational energy.

**Merits of Non-Renewable:**
- High **energy density** and **dispatchability**.
- Established **infrastructure** and **technology**.
- Reliable **baseload power** generation.

**Demerits of Non-Renewable:**
- **Finite reserves**; depletion inevitable.
- High **greenhouse gas emissions** → climate change.
- **Pollution** (SOx, NOx, particulates, ash).
- **Geopolitical dependency** and price volatility.

**Merits of Renewable:**
- **Inexhaustible** on human timescales.
- **Zero / low carbon emissions** during operation.
- **Decentralized** generation possible.
- **Energy security** improvement.

**Demerits of Renewable:**
- **Intermittency** (solar/wind).
- **Low energy density** (large land area).
- **High upfront capital cost** (PV, wind turbines).
- **Storage challenges** for off-peak supply.

---

**(b)** A thermal power plant uses **5000 tonnes of coal per day** with a calorific value of **25,000 kJ/kg**. If the plant's overall thermal efficiency is **35%**, calculate: **(7 marks)**
   1. The thermal energy input per day.
   2. The electrical energy output per day (in kWh).
   3. The daily coal consumption if efficiency is improved to **42%** for the same electrical output.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Thermal Energy Input per Day**

Given: $m = 5000 \text{ tonnes/day} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/day}$, $\text{GCV} = 25{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

$$Q_{\text{in}} = m \times \text{GCV} = (5 \times 10^6) \times (25{,}000)$$

$$Q_{\text{in}} = 1.25 \times 10^{11} \text{ kJ/day}$$

**Step 2 — Electrical Energy Output per Day**

Given: $\eta_1 = 35\% = 0.35$.

$$E_{\text{elec}} = \eta_1 \times Q_{\text{in}} = 0.35 \times 1.25 \times 10^{11}$$

$$E_{\text{elec}} = 4.375 \times 10^{10} \text{ kJ/day}$$

Convert to kWh using $1 \text{ kWh} = 3600 \text{ kJ}$:

$$E_{\text{elec}} = \frac{4.375 \times 10^{10}}{3600} \approx 1.215 \times 10^7 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 3 — Coal Needed at 42% Efficiency (Same Output)**

For the same electrical output, with $\eta_2 = 42\% = 0.42$:

$$Q_{\text{in,new}} = \frac{E_{\text{elec}}}{\eta_2} = \frac{4.375 \times 10^{10}}{0.42}$$

$$Q_{\text{in,new}} = 1.0417 \times 10^{11} \text{ kJ/day}$$

New coal requirement:

$$m_{\text{new}} = \frac{Q_{\text{in,new}}}{\text{GCV}} = \frac{1.0417 \times 10^{11}}{25{,}000} = 4.1667 \times 10^6 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$m_{\text{new}} \approx 4166.7 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

**Step 4 — Coal Savings**

$$\Delta m = 5000 - 4166.7 = 833.3 \text{ tonnes/day}$$

> **Result:** Improving efficiency from 35% to 42% saves about **833 tonnes of coal per day**, demonstrating the direct link between **conversion efficiency** and **primary resource conservation**.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the **Indian energy scenario** with reference to primary energy resource consumption. Discuss the role of **coal** and **renewable energy** in India's energy mix. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Indian Energy Scenario — Key Facts:**

- India is the **3rd largest primary energy consumer** globally (after China and USA).
- **Per capita energy consumption** is among the lowest in the world (~0.6 TOE/capita vs world average ~1.8 TOE).
- **Energy import dependence** is high — over **85% of crude oil**, **~50% of natural gas**, and significant **coking coal** are imported.
- **Coal** contributes about **~55% of commercial primary energy** in India.
- **Installed power capacity (as of recent data):** Coal (~50%), Renewables (~32% — and rising), Hydro (~10%), Gas (~6%), Nuclear (~2%).

**Role of Coal:**
- **Abundant domestic reserves** (~360 billion tonnes, 5th largest globally).
- Backbone of **baseload power** generation.
- Provides **employment** in mining regions.
- However: **high emissions**, **air quality issues**, **ash handling**, and **depleting quality** of accessible coal.

**Role of Renewable Energy:**
- India has set a target of **500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030**.
- **Solar potential** ≈ 750 GW (assuming 3% wasteland).
- **Wind potential** ≈ 302 GW at 120m hub height.
- **Initiatives:** National Solar Mission, PM-KUSUM (solar pumps), Rooftop Solar Scheme, Green Hydrogen Mission.
- Renewables **reduce import bill**, **decarbonize** the grid, and enable **energy access in remote areas**.

**Challenges:**
- **Intermittency** of solar and wind.
- **Land acquisition** for large projects.
- **Grid stability** and **storage** requirements.
- **Financing** of capital-intensive projects.

> **Conclusion:** India must pursue a **balanced energy portfolio** — leveraging domestic coal with **clean coal technologies (super-critical, IGCC, CCS)** while **aggressively expanding renewables** to meet climate commitments.

---

**(b)** The global proven reserves and annual production of a few key primary energy resources are given below: **(7 marks)**

| Resource | Proven Reserves | Annual Production |
|---|---|---|
| Coal | 1.07 × 10¹² TOE | 7.7 × 10⁹ TOE/yr |
| Crude Oil | 2.4 × 10¹¹ TOE | 4.8 × 10⁹ TOE/yr |
| Natural Gas | 1.9 × 10¹¹ TOE | 3.8 × 10⁹ TOE/yr |

Calculate the **R/P ratio** for each and comment on the **resource security implications**.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Coal:**

$$\left(\frac{R}{P}\right)_{\text{coal}} = \frac{1.07 \times 10^{12}}{7.7 \times 10^{9}} = 138.96 \approx 139 \text{ years}$$

**2. Crude Oil:**

$$\left(\frac{R}{P}\right)_{\text{oil}} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{11}}{4.8 \times 10^{9}} = 50 \text{ years}$$

**3. Natural Gas:**

$$\left(\frac{R}{P}\right)_{\text{gas}} = \frac{1.9 \times 10^{11}}{3.8 \times 10^{9}} = 50 \text{ years}$$

**Resource Security Comments:**

- **Coal** has the most comfortable R/P ratio (~139 years), but this is **misleading** because it ignores:
  - **Climate change commitments** (coal must be phased down).
  - **Quality degradation** as the best seams are mined first.
  - **Environmental externalities**.
- **Oil and Gas** at ~50 years are **medium-term resources**. India, with **85% oil import dependence**, faces serious **energy security risks**.
- **Strategic implications:**
  - **Diversify into renewables** urgently.
  - Invest in **electric mobility** and **hydrogen economy**.
  - Strengthen **strategic petroleum reserves**.
  - Improve **energy efficiency** in industry and buildings.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Energy Resource Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Primary vs Secondary energy** — A student once wrote "Electricity is a primary source". Examiner awarded **zero** for that statement. Always state: *electricity is a secondary form derived from primary sources*.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — In Step 2 of the worked example, if you write $1.215 \times 10^7$ **without specifying kWh**, you lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Mixing GCV and NCV** — Indian boilers use **GCV (as-received)**. Use LHV only if the question explicitly says so.
>
> 4. **Not mentioning intermittency in renewable discussion** — Examiner allocates 1 mark specifically for acknowledging the dispatchability limitation of solar/wind.
>
> 5. **R/P ratio calculation without interpretation** — KTU expects you to **comment on the result**, not just compute it.
>
> 6. **Skipping reserve-discovery dynamics** — Static R/P ignores **new finds**. For a 7-mark question, briefly mention that new discoveries can **extend** the R/P.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- **Primary energy** = energy in its **natural, unconverted form** (coal, oil, sunlight, uranium).
- **Secondary energy** = **derived from primary** via conversion (electricity, gasoline, LPG).
- **Renewable** = replenished on human timescales (solar, wind, hydro, biomass, geothermal, tidal).
- **Non-renewable** = finite stock (coal, oil, gas, uranium); replenishment takes millions of years.
- **Commercial** = traded in formal markets; **Non-commercial** = traditional biomass, dung cakes.
- **Stock energy** = can be stored (coal, oil); **Flow energy** = must be used as it arrives (sunlight, wind).
- **GCV (HHV)** includes latent heat of condensation; **NCV (LHV)** excludes it.
- **R/P Ratio** = $\dfrac{R}{P}$ — directly gives years of reserve life at current production.
- **Energy Intensity** = $\dfrac{E_{\text{primary}}}{\text{GDP}}$ — measures economic energy productivity.
- **Conversion Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{E_{\text{useful}}}{E_{\text{input}}} \times 100\%$ — basis of all energy audits.
- **India's mix:** Coal dominant; **renewables growing fastest**; **oil and gas heavily imported**.
- **Standard Units:** $1 \text{ TOE} = 4.186 \times 10^{10} \text{ J}$; $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$.
- **CO₂ emission intensity (g CO₂/kWh):** Coal ~820, Oil ~650, Gas ~490, Solar ~40, Wind ~11, Nuclear ~12, Hydro ~24.
- **Global R/P (approximate):** Coal ~139 yrs, Oil ~50 yrs, Gas ~50 yrs, Uranium ~100+ yrs.
- **Renewables need storage** to overcome intermittency — a recurring KTU exam theme.
- **Carnot bound** $\eta = 1 - T_{\text{cold}}/T_{\text{hot}}$ sets the upper limit of any thermal conversion cycle.

> 🎯 **Last-Minute Mnemonic for KTU Viva/Oral:** *"PRIMARY is RAW, SECONDARY is READY"* — raw ingredients in the kitchen vs cooked dish on the table.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commercial Energy Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Commercial Energy Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Commercial Energy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commercial Energy** is defined as the form of energy that is **generated, traded, and sold in the organized energy market** at a measurable monetary price for economic, industrial, domestic, and transportation purposes.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PESFT414 – Energy Management and Conservation), commercial energy encompasses all energy carriers that pass through formal commercial channels — i.e., the consumer **pays a price** to obtain them. This includes:

- **Fossil Fuels**: Coal, Lignite, Crude Oil, Natural Gas
- **Refined Hydrocarbons**: Petrol, Diesel, LPG, Kerosene, Naphtha
- **Electrical Energy** (Grid-supplied)
- **Nuclear Energy** (Uranium-based electricity)
- **Commercial Renewables**: Grid-connected solar PV, wind, biomass power, small hydro

The **counter-term** is **Non-Commercial Energy**, which refers to energy gathered and consumed *without monetary transaction* (e.g., firewood, cow-dung cakes, agricultural residue, animal/human muscle power, traditional charcoal).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 1 emphasis is on understanding the **energy mix of a nation**, the **commercial sources of primary energy**, and the **transformation pathways** from raw resource to end-use energy carrier.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Energy Supermarket"

Imagine a country as a **giant kitchen**:
- **Non-commercial energy** is like picking vegetables from your own backyard — free, unaccounted, limited, and not bought from a store.
- **Commercial energy** is like buying groceries from a supermarket — every gram is weighed, priced, billed, and accounted for in the national accounts (like a country's GDP and energy balance sheets).

Just as a modern household **cannot function entirely on backyard produce** (we need processed/packaged goods for convenience, scale, and quality), a modern industrial economy **cannot function on non-commercial energy alone** — it needs the reliability, scalability, and energy density of commercial carriers.

## 1.3 Why Does This Classification Matter?

| Perspective | Why Commercial Energy is Studied |
|---|---|
| Economic | Traded commodities affect GDP, forex, inflation |
| Strategic | Energy import dependence = national security concern |
| Engineering | All industrial processes (boilers, furnaces, motors) use commercial energy |
| Environmental | Commercial energy is the **largest source of CO₂ emissions** globally |
| Policy | Every climate commitment (NDC, COP pledges) is built on commercial-energy accounting |

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics are **standard for commercial energy accounting** worldwide:

- **1 Toe (Tonne of Oil Equivalent)** = $4.1868 \times 10^{10}$ J ≈ **42 GJ** ≈ **11.63 MWh** (thermal)
- **1 kWh** = **3.6 MJ** (exactly)
- **1 BTU (British Thermal Unit)** = **1055.06 J**
- **1 Quad** = $10^{15}$ BTU = **$1.055 \times 10^{18}$ J**
- **1 Standard Cubic Meter of Natural Gas** ≈ **35.315 SCF**; energy ≈ **38.3 MJ/SCM** (typical GCV)
- **Calorific value of gasoline (typical)** ≈ **44 MJ/kg**
- **Net calorific value of Indian coal (bituminous)** ≈ **3500 – 5000 kcal/kg** (≈ **14.6 – 20.9 MJ/kg**)

> [!NOTE]
> In India, the **Ministry of Power** and **Central Electricity Authority (CEA)** publish energy statistics in **MU (Million Units = Million kWh)** and **MT (Million Tonnes)** of coal/oil equivalent.

## 1.5 Commercial vs. Non-Commercial — A Comparative Snapshot

| Parameter | Commercial Energy | Non-Commercial Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Pricing | Market-priced, invoiced | Free / barter / unaccounted |
| Source | Mines, wells, grids, reactors | Farm, forest, animal power |
| Scale | Industrial to national | Household / village level |
| Quality control | Standardized (GCV, ash %, sulfur %) | Variable, often low-quality |
| Environmental data | Audited emissions | Largely unaccounted (e.g., indoor smoke) |
| Examples | Coal, diesel, grid power, LPG, PNG | Firewood, cow-dung, agri-residue, charcoal |
| Share in India's energy mix (approx.) | **> 80%** of useful energy | < 20%, but **> 30%** in rural households |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Commercial Energy Sources

Commercial energy sources can be classified along **three orthogonal axes** — students must internalize all three for KTU long-answer questions.

### Axis A: By Primary/Secondary Form

**Primary energy** is extracted directly from nature.
**Secondary energy** is derived from primary energy through conversion.

- **Primary commercial sources**: Coal, Crude oil, Natural gas, Uranium, Solar radiation, Wind, Hydel head, Tidal, Geothermal heat, Biomass (when sold commercially)
- **Secondary commercial sources**: Electricity, Petrol, Diesel, Kerosene, LPG, CNG, Hydrogen (grey/blue/green), Refined furnace oil, Coke, Coal-tar, Producer gas, Town gas

> [!NOTE]
> **Electricity is a pure secondary energy** — it does **not exist as a primary resource** in nature in usable form for the grid.

### Axis B: By Renewability

- **Non-Renewable (Finite Stock)**:
  - Fossil fuels — Coal, Oil, Natural gas
  - Nuclear — U-235, Th-232 (finite, but reserves last centuries)
- **Renewable (Flux Resources)**:
  - Continuous flux — Solar, Wind, Hydro (run-of-river), Tidal, Wave
  - Bio-regenerative — Biomass, Biogas, Biofuels
  - Stored flux — Hydel reservoirs, Pumped storage, Green hydrogen

### Axis C: By Commercialization Stage

- **Mature commercial** — Coal, oil, gas, hydro, grid solar
- **Emerging commercial** — Offshore wind, green hydrogen, advanced biofuels
- **Pre-commercial / R&D** — Fusion, ocean thermal, space-based solar

## 2.2 The Five Pillars of Commercial Energy — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Coal (The "Black Diamond")

- **Forms**: Peat → Lignite → Sub-bituminous → Bituminous → Anthracite (increasing carbon %, decreasing moisture %)
- **Indian context**: India is the **2nd largest coal producer globally**; ~70% of electricity generation is coal-based.
- **Key quality parameter**: **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** and **Ash content** (Indian coal typically has 30–40% ash — among the highest in the world).
- **Conversion pathway**: Coal → (Boiler + Steam Turbine) → Electricity
- **Environmental concern**: Highest CO₂ intensity per kWh (~**0.95 kg CO₂/kWh** for Indian coal plants).

### 2.2.2 Oil & Petroleum

- **Crude oil** refined into Petrol, Diesel, Kerosene, LPG, Naphtha, Lubricants, Bitumen.
- **India's dependence**: Imports > 85% of crude oil requirement (major forex drain).
- **Energy density**: Diesel ~ **43 MJ/kg**, Petrol ~ **44 MJ/kg**, LPG ~ **46 MJ/kg** — among the highest of all commercial fuels.
- **Conversion pathway**: Crude → Refinery → Multiple products → Engines/Boilers.

### 2.2.3 Natural Gas

- **Composition**: Mostly CH₄ (85–95%), C₂H₆, C₃H₈, plus inerts (N₂, CO₂, H₂S).
- **Forms**: CNG (compressed), LNG (cryogenic liquid at −162 °C), PNG (piped city gas).
- **GCV range**: **35–55 MJ/SCM** depending on composition.
- **Environmental advantage**: Lowest CO₂ intensity among fossil fuels per unit heat (~**56 kg CO₂/GJ** vs. coal ~95 kg CO₂/GJ).
- **Indian fields**: KG Basin (Krishna-Godavari offshore), Assam, Rajasthan, Gujarat.

### 2.2.4 Nuclear Energy

- **Fuel cycle**: U-235 (fissile) → Fission in reactor → Heat → Steam → Turbine → Electricity
- **Breeder reactors** (e.g., India's three-stage nuclear program): use Th-232 → U-233 breeding cycle.
- **Capacity factor**: Nuclear plants achieve **85–90%** (vs. solar ~20%, coal ~60%), making them a **baseload** source.
- **CO₂ lifecycle emissions**: ~**12 g CO₂/kWh** — among the lowest of any generation source.

### 2.2.5 Commercial Renewables (Grid-connected)

- **Solar PV**: Direct photon → electron conversion; capacity factor 15–25% in India.
- **Wind**: Kinetic energy of air → mechanical → electrical; capacity factor 25–40% at good sites.
- **Large Hydro**: Potential head of water → mechanical → electrical; capacity factor 40–60%.
- **Biomass power**: Direct combustion or gasification of bagasse, rice husk, etc.
- **Small hydro (<25 MW)**: Run-of-river, mini/micro hydro.

## 2.3 The Energy Conversion Chain — End-to-End Flow

A typical **commercial energy transformation chain** has **four stages**:

1. **Resource extraction** (Mine, well, farm, dam)
2. **Processing / refining** (Refinery, washery, enrichment)
3. **Conversion to useful form** (Power plant, gasifier, refinery)
4. **Delivery & end-use** (Grid, pipeline, fuel station)

> Every stage incurs **energy losses** (1st Law) and **exergy destruction** (2nd Law). Total round-trip efficiency from resource to end-use is often **< 35%** in fossil pathways.

## 2.4 The Indian Commercial Energy Landscape — Structural Snapshot

| Sector / Source | Approximate Share in Primary Commercial Energy | Major Stakeholder |
|---|---|---|
| Coal | ~ 44% | Coal India Ltd (CIL), Singareni Collieries |
| Oil | ~ 25% | ONGC, IOCL, BPCL, HPCL, Reliance |
| Natural Gas | ~ 6% | ONGC, GAIL, Reliance, Cairn (Vedanta) |
| Renewables (large) | ~ 13% | NTPC-RE, Adani Green, Tata Power, NHPC |
| Hydro (large) | ~ 9% | NHPC, SJVN, state utilities |
| Nuclear | ~ 3% | NPCIL |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Insight (KTU-favourite question):** India imports ~**85% of crude oil**, ~**50% of natural gas**, and **100% of uranium concentrate** — making **energy security** a foreign-policy and forex-management issue, not merely a technical one.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> All formulas below are **directly applicable** in KTU numerical problems. Pay close attention to **units**.

| # | Formula / Identity | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E \text{ (J)} = P \text{ (W)} \times t \text{ (s)}$ | Electrical energy = power × time | J, kWh, MU |
| 2 | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$ | Exact conversion | J ↔ kWh |
| 3 | $1 \text{ Toe} = 42 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh}_{th}$ | Tonne of oil equivalent | Toe ↔ J |
| 4 | $1 \text{ Quad} = 1.055 \times 10^{18} \text{ J}$ | US national energy unit | J |
| 5 | $GCV = NCV + h_{fg} \cdot \left(9 \cdot \frac{H}{100}\right)$ | Gross vs. Net calorific value (where $h_{fg} = 2260 \text{ kJ/kg}$ and H = % hydrogen) | kJ/kg, MJ/kg |
| 6 | $Q = m \cdot GCV$ | Heat released from burning mass $m$ | J or kJ |
| 7 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{3600 \cdot P_{e}(kW)}{m_{f} \cdot GCV}$ | Thermal efficiency of a plant | dimensionless (%) |
| 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_{m} \times \eta_{g}$ | Overall = thermal × mechanical × generator | dimensionless (%) |
| 9 | $\text{SHR} = \dfrac{3600 \cdot E}{\dot{m}_{steam} \cdot (h_{g} - h_{f})}$ | Station Heat Rate (kJ/kWh) — inverse of efficiency | kJ/kWh |
| 10 | $SFC = \dfrac{3600 \cdot m_{f}}{E \cdot GCV}$ | Specific Fuel Consumption | kg/kWh |
| 11 | $\text{Heat Rate (kJ/kWh)} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{overall}}$ | Inverse of overall efficiency | kJ/kWh |
| 12 | $E_{annual} \text{ (kWh)} = P_{rated} \times 8760 \times PLF$ | Annual energy from a plant of rated power with Plant Load Factor | kWh |
| 13 | $PLF = \dfrac{\text{Energy generated in a period}}{P_{rated} \times \text{Hours in period}}$ | Plant Load Factor | dimensionless |
| 14 | $CUF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{installed} \times 8760}$ | Capacity Utilization Factor (RE plants) | dimensionless |
| 15 | $\text{Specific CO}_2 = \dfrac{3.6 \times C_{f}}{GCV_{f} \times \eta_{overall}}$ | kg CO₂ per kWh; $C_f$ = carbon fraction in fuel | kg/kWh |
| 16 | $R/P = \dfrac{\text{Reserves}}{\text{Annual production}}$ | Reserve-to-Production ratio (years of supply) | years |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When solving numericals, **always state the units** in every step. The examiner often gives **0.5–1 mark** purely for unit consistency. A correct number with wrong units = **partial credit only**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between Energy Units (Toe ↔ kWh ↔ Joules)

This is a **commonly-asked KTU numerical** — *“Convert 5 MTOe of primary energy into (a) GJ, (b) GWh of thermal, (c) GWh of electrical at 33% conversion efficiency.”*

### Step 1 — Write the basic definition

By definition, **1 Tonne of Oil Equivalent (Toe)** is the amount of energy released by burning **1 metric tonne (1000 kg)** of crude oil, which has an average gross calorific value of approximately **42 GJ per tonne**.

$$
1 \text{ Toe} = 42 \text{ GJ} = 42 \times 10^9 \text{ J}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert Joules to kWh

Using the exact identity **1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J** = 3.6 MJ:

$$
1 \text{ Toe} = \frac{42 \times 10^{9} \text{ J}}{3.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/kWh}}
$$

Perform the division step-by-step:

$$
\frac{42 \times 10^{9}}{3.6 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{42}{3.6} \times 10^{9-6} = 11.666... \times 10^{3}
$$

$$
\Rightarrow 1 \text{ Toe} = 11{,}666.67 \text{ kWh} \approx 11.63 \text{ MWh}_{th}
$$

So, **1 MToe** = $10^6$ Toe = $11.63 \times 10^{6}$ kWh = **11.63 TWh (thermal)**.

### Step 3 — Apply to the KTU-style problem

> **Problem:** A country consumes 5 MToe of commercial primary energy per year. How many GWh of *electrical* energy can be generated if the overall conversion efficiency of the power sector is 33%?

**Step 3a — Convert MToe to GJ:**

$$
5 \text{ MToe} = 5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Toe} \times 42 \text{ GJ/Toe}
$$

$$
5 \text{ MToe} = 210 \times 10^{6} \text{ GJ} = 2.1 \times 10^{8} \text{ GJ}
$$

**Step 3b — Convert GJ to GWh (thermal):**

$$
2.1 \times 10^{8} \text{ GJ} = 2.1 \times 10^{8} \times 10^{9} \text{ J} = 2.1 \times 10^{17} \text{ J}
$$

$$
E_{th} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{17} \text{ J}}{3.6 \times 10^{15} \text{ J/GWh}} = 58.33 \text{ GWh (thermal)}
$$

**Step 3c — Apply conversion efficiency to get electrical energy:**

$$
E_{el} = E_{th} \times \eta_{overall} = 58.33 \text{ GWh} \times 0.33
$$

$$
E_{el} = 19.25 \text{ GWh (electrical)}
$$

> **Answer:** 5 MToe yields **≈ 19.25 GWh of electricity** at 33% overall efficiency.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Thermal Efficiency of a Coal Power Plant

This derivation appears in **almost every KTU past paper**. The examiner expects a clean, **3-step** working.

### Problem Statement

A coal-fired thermal power plant has the following data:
- Coal consumption: $m_f = 100$ tonnes/day
- Gross calorific value of coal: $GCV = 4000$ kcal/kg
- Net electrical output: $P_e = 40$ MW

**Find:** (a) Daily heat input, (b) Daily electrical output, (c) Overall efficiency.

### Step 1 — Heat input per day (in kcal)

$$
Q_{in} = m_f \cdot GCV
$$

$$
Q_{in} = (100 \times 1000 \text{ kg/day}) \times 4000 \text{ kcal/kg}
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 4 \times 10^{8} \text{ kcal/day}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert heat input to kWh (1 kcal = 1.163 × 10⁻³ kWh)

$$
Q_{in} = 4 \times 10^{8} \times 1.163 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kWh/day}
$$

$$
Q_{in} = 4.652 \times 10^{5} \text{ kWh/day} = 465{,}200 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

### Step 3 — Electrical output per day (assuming 24-hour operation at 40 MW)

$$
E_{out} = P_e \times t = 40 \text{ MW} \times 24 \text{ h} = 960 \text{ MWh/day} = 960{,}000 \text{ kWh/day}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute overall efficiency

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{E_{out}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{960{,}000}{465{,}200} = 2.063
$$

This value > 1 is **impossible** and signals an **error in assumption** — the plant is not running 24 hours at 40 MW. Let us instead assume **PLF = 80%**:

$$
E_{out} = 40 \text{ MW} \times 8760 \text{ h} \times 0.80 = 280{,}320 \text{ MWh/yr}
$$

Daily equivalent: $280{,}320 / 365 = 768$ MWh/day = $768{,}000$ kWh/day

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{768{,}000}{465{,}200} = 1.65
$$

Still > 1, indicating the **coal flow is too low** to support 40 MW continuously. This is a **useful KTU-style trap** — students must check feasibility.

**Corrected check:** The **minimum coal flow** for 40 MW output (assume η = 35%):

$$
m_{f, \min} = \frac{P_e \times 24}{\eta \cdot GCV} = \frac{40{,}000 \text{ kW} \times 24 \text{ h}}{0.35 \times 4000 \times 1.163 \text{ kWh/kg}}
$$

$$
m_{f, \min} = \frac{960{,}000}{1628.2} \approx 589 \text{ tonnes/day}
$$

> **Answer to part (c) once feasibility is enforced:** With a realistic coal flow of ~600 t/day, the **overall efficiency is ~33–35%**, consistent with Indian subcritical coal plants.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Plant Load Factor (PLF) vs. Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)

These are **favourite 3-mark KTU questions**.

### PLF Derivation

By definition, **Plant Load Factor** is the ratio of **actual energy generated in a period** to the **maximum possible energy** if the plant ran at rated capacity for the entire period.

$$
PLF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times T}
$$

where:
- $E_{actual}$ = energy generated in period $T$ (kWh or MWh)
- $P_{rated}$ = installed capacity (kW or MW)
- $T$ = total hours in the period

**Worked example:** A 100 MW plant generated 525,600 MWh in one year.

$$
PLF = \frac{525{,}600 \text{ MWh}}{100 \text{ MW} \times 8760 \text{ h}} = \frac{525{,}600}{876{,}000} = 0.60 = 60\%
$$

### CUF Derivation

CUF is used specifically for **renewable plants** (solar/wind) and uses the **installed DC or nameplate capacity**:

$$
CUF = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{installed} \times 8760}
$$

For the **same energy** of 525,600 MWh and 100 MW nameplate:

$$
CUF = 0.60 = 60\%
$$

### Why Both Exist (and Why Examiners Love This)

| Parameter | Used For | Period | Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| PLF | Conventional (coal, gas, nuclear) | Any period (day/month/year) | Numerator may include partial-load operation |
| CUF | Renewables (solar, wind) | Typically annual | Reflects **resource variability**, not plant performance |

> [!NOTE]
> A low CUF at a solar plant (15–25%) does **not** mean poor plant performance — it means **the sun shone for only 4–6 hours at full irradiance equivalent**. The plant may still be **98% available**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio — Energy Security Metric

The R/P ratio tells a country how many years its **proven reserves** will last at **current production rates**.

$$
R/P \text{ (years)} = \frac{\text{Proven Reserves at year-end}}{\text{Annual production in that year}}
$$

### Worked Example

Suppose a country has:
- Proven coal reserves: $R = 100$ billion tonnes
- Annual coal production: $P = 0.5$ billion tonnes/year

$$
R/P = \frac{100 \times 10^{9} \text{ t}}{0.5 \times 10^{9} \text{ t/yr}} = 200 \text{ years}
$$

> **Caveat:** R/P is a **static snapshot** — it assumes constant production, ignores new discoveries, demand growth, and technology shifts. In KTU answers, always **state this caveat** for full marks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Commercial Source Wins or Loses

| Source | Energy Density (MJ/kg) | CO₂ Intensity (kg/kWh) | Capacity Factor | Land Footprint | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coal (bituminous) | 24 | 0.95 | 60–70% | Medium (mining + ash) | Emissions, ash disposal |
| Diesel | 43 | 0.27 | n/a (mobile) | n/a | Import dependence |
| Natural Gas | 53 (per SCM ~ 38 MJ) | 0.45 | 60–80% | Low | Distribution infra |
| Nuclear | ~ 8 × 10⁶ (U-235) | 0.012 | 85–90% | Small | Waste, capex, public acceptance |
| Solar PV | n/a (flux) | 0.04 | 15–25% | High | Intermittency |
| Wind | n/a (flux) | 0.02 | 25–40% | High | Intermittency |
| Large Hydro | n/a (flux) | 0.02 | 40–60% | Large (reservoir) | Displacement, ecology |
| Biomass | 14–18 | 0.45 (net can be low) | 60–80% | Medium | Supply chain, land |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is **examination gold**. The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests the ability to **compare and select** energy sources for a given application.

---

## 3.6 Numerical: Estimating India's Annual Energy from Coal (for 2024 base)

A frequently tested calculation. Assume (illustrative values, current-order):
- Annual coal-based electricity generation: ~ **1200 BU** (Billion Units = TWh)
- Average plant efficiency: **33%**
- Average GCV of Indian coal: **4000 kcal/kg = 16.74 MJ/kg**

**Find:** Annual coal consumption in million tonnes.

$$
E_{th} = \frac{E_{el}}{\eta} = \frac{1200 \text{ TWh}}{0.33} = 3636.4 \text{ TWh}_{th}
$$

$$
E_{th} = 3636.4 \times 3.6 \times 10^{12} \text{ J} = 1.309 \times 10^{16} \text{ J}
$$

$$
m_{coal} = \frac{E_{th}}{GCV} = \frac{1.309 \times 10^{16}}{16.74 \times 10^{6}} = 7.82 \times 10^{8} \text{ kg} = 782 \text{ million tonnes}
$$

> **Answer:** Approximately **780–800 million tonnes** of coal per year, consistent with India's actual reported coal consumption for power generation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification of Commercial Energy — Mermaid Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Commercial Energy Sources] --> B[Non-Renewable]
    A --> C[Renewable]
    A --> D[Nuclear - Finite but Low-Carbon]

    B --> B1[Coal - Lignite Bituminous Anthracite]
    B --> B2[Oil - Crude Refined Products]
    B --> B3[Natural Gas - CNG LNG PNG]

    C --> C1[Solar - PV Thermal CSP]
    C --> C2[Wind - Onshore Offshore]
    C --> C3[Hydro - Large Small Pumped]
    C --> C4[Biomass - Bagasse Biogas Biofuels]
    C --> C5[Geothermal - Tidal - Wave]

    D --> D1[Fission - U-235 Pu-239]
    D --> D2[Breeder - Th-232 to U-233]

    B1 --> P1[Primary Form]
    B2 --> P1
    B3 --> P1
    D1 --> P1
    C1 --> P1
    C2 --> P1
    C3 --> P1
    C4 --> P1

    P1 --> S1[Secondary Form - Conversion Required]
    S1 --> S2[Electricity via Power Plant]
    S1 --> S3[Refined Fuels via Refinery]
    S1 --> S4[Processed Gas via Gasifier]
```

## 4.2 Commercial Energy Conversion Chain — Block Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Raw Resource - Mine / Well / Field / Sun / Wind] --> B1[Extraction and Primary Processing]
    B1 --> C1[Transport - Rail Pipeline Tanker Grid]
    C1 --> D1[Conversion Plant - Thermal Hydro Nuclear Wind Solar]
    D1 --> E1[Secondary Carrier - Electricity / Refined Fuel / Steam]
    E1 --> F1[Distribution Network - Grid / Pipeline / Road]
    F1 --> G1[End Use - Industry Transport Commercial Domestic]
    G1 --> H1[Useful Work - Mechanical Heat Light Motion]

    B1 -. Losses .-> L1[Stage 1 Losses - 5 to 15 percent]
    D1 -. Losses .-> L2[Stage 2 Losses - 60 to 70 percent in fossil]
    F1 -. Losses .-> L3[Stage 3 Losses - 5 to 10 percent]
```

## 4.3 Indian Commercial Energy Flow Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
graph TD
    SourceA[Coal - CIL Singareni Captive Mines] --> PlantA[Thermal Power Plant]
    SourceB[Crude Oil - ONGC Import Refineries] --> RefA[Refinery - IOCL BPCL HPCL RIL]
    SourceC[Natural Gas - ONGC GAIL Import LNG] --> PlantB[Gas Turbine / Combined Cycle]
    SourceD[Uranium - UCIL Imports] --> PlantC[Nuclear Plant - NPCIL]
    SourceE[Hydro Reservoirs - NHPC State Utilities] --> PlantD[Hydel Plant]
    SourceF[Solar Radiation - Private and Public] --> PlantE[Solar PV / CSP]
    SourceG[Wind Kinetic Energy - IPPs] --> PlantF[Wind Turbine Farm]

    RefA --> FuelA[Petrol Diesel LPG Kerosene]
    RefA --> FuelB[Naphtha Fuel Oil Lubricants]
    FuelA --> Use1[Transport Sector]
    FuelB --> Use2[Industry and Power]
    PlantA --> GridA[National Grid - PowerGrid CTU]
    PlantB --> GridA
    PlantC --> GridA
    PlantD --> GridA
    PlantE --> GridA
    PlantF --> GridA
    GridA --> End1[Industrial Consumers]
    GridA --> End2[Domestic Consumers]
    GridA --> End3[Commercial and Agricultural Consumers]
```

## 4.4 Typical Conversion Efficiency Ladder — Sankey-Style Block

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[100 units of Primary Chemical Energy in Coal] --> B[Boiler: 88 percent]
    B --> C[Steam Cycle: 38 percent of input]
    C --> D[Turbine Mechanical: 36 percent]
    D --> E[Generator Electrical: 35 percent]
    E --> F[Transmission and Distribution: 33 percent at consumer end]
    F --> G[End-use device efficiency: 20 to 30 percent at point of use]
    G --> H[Useful Work delivered]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagram:** Out of 100 units of chemical energy in coal, only **~7–10 units of *useful* work** (e.g., spinning a motor shaft, lighting a room) actually reach the consumer. The rest is lost as **flue-gas heat, condenser rejection, line losses, and motor/lighting inefficiencies**. This is the **single most important reason** energy management and conservation matter.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

### Q1. Define commercial energy. How is it different from non-commercial energy? Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Commercial energy is energy that is **produced, traded, and sold in the organized market** at a measurable monetary price for industrial, commercial, transport, and organized domestic use. Non-commercial energy is gathered and consumed **without monetary transaction**, typically at household or village level, and is generally unaccounted in formal energy statistics. Examples: **Commercial** — coal, grid electricity, LPG, petrol, natural gas. **Non-Commercial** — firewood, cow-dung cakes, agricultural residue, animal power.
*(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 1 mark distinction + 1 mark examples.)*

---

### Q2. What is meant by "primary" and "secondary" energy? Classify electricity, coal, and diesel.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Primary energy** is energy extracted directly from natural resources without any conversion. **Secondary energy** is derived from one or more primary sources through a conversion process. Classification: **Coal** — primary, **Diesel** — secondary (refined from crude oil), **Electricity** — secondary (generated from coal/hydro/nuclear/solar). *(1 + 1 + 1 marks.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-stage conversion chain** of commercial energy from a coal-fired thermal power plant. State typical efficiency values at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 210 MW coal-fired plant operates at PLF of 70% using coal of GCV **3500 kcal/kg**. Annual coal consumption is **1.5 million tonnes**. Determine: (i) the annual electrical energy generated, (ii) the overall plant efficiency, and (iii) the specific coal consumption in kg/kWh. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The four stages are:
1. **Coal extraction & handling** (mining, transportation, stock-piling) — Energy loss: ~3–5% (in mine-mouth handling, transport).
2. **Combustion in boiler** — Energy loss: ~10–15% (flue gas, radiation, unburnt carbon).
3. **Steam cycle & turbine-generator** — Energy loss: ~50–55% (condenser, friction).
4. **Transmission & distribution (T&D)** — Energy loss: ~5–8% (line losses, transformer losses).

Cumulative **net efficiency at consumer end ≈ 30–35%** for an Indian subcritical plant. A block diagram should show input coal → boiler → turbine → generator → grid → consumer with the four loss arrows. *(5 marks for diagram + stages, 2 marks for typical loss values.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $P_{rated} = 210$ MW, $PLF = 0.70$, $GCV = 3500$ kcal/kg = $3500 \times 4.184$ kJ/kg = **14,644 kJ/kg**, annual coal $m = 1.5 \times 10^6$ t = $1.5 \times 10^9$ kg.

**(i) Annual electrical energy generated:**

$$
E_{el} = P_{rated} \times 8760 \times PLF = 210 \times 10^3 \text{ kW} \times 8760 \text{ h} \times 0.70
$$

$$
E_{el} = 1.288 \times 10^9 \text{ kWh} = 1287.72 \text{ MU}
$$

**(ii) Overall plant efficiency:**

Heat input per year:

$$
Q_{in} = m \cdot GCV = 1.5 \times 10^9 \text{ kg} \times 14{,}644 \text{ kJ/kg} = 2.1966 \times 10^{13} \text{ kJ}
$$

Convert to kWh: divide by 3.6

$$
Q_{in} = \frac{2.1966 \times 10^{13}}{3.6} = 6.1017 \times 10^9 \text{ kWh}
$$

Overall efficiency:

$$
\eta_{overall} = \frac{E_{el}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{1.288 \times 10^9}{6.1017 \times 10^9} = 0.2111 = 21.11\%
$$

**(iii) Specific coal consumption:**

$$
SCC = \frac{m}{E_{el}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^9 \text{ kg}}{1.288 \times 10^9 \text{ kWh}} = 1.165 \text{ kg/kWh}
$$

> **Final answers:** (i) 1287.72 MU, (ii) ≈ 21.1%, (iii) ≈ 1.165 kg/kWh.
> *(1 mark part i + 3 marks part ii + 1 mark part iii + 2 marks for unit handling = 7.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting to convert GCV from kcal/kg to kJ/kg** is the #1 error — costs 1 mark.
> - **Mixing up PLF × hours** with calendar hours is the #2 error — costs 0.5 mark.
> - **Not converting million tonnes to kg** before dividing by GCV is a common slip.
> - **No unit on the final answer** = −0.5 mark guaranteed.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]
