**(a)** Explain the **classification of commercial energy sources** along the three axes: primary/secondary, renewable/non-renewable, and conventional/non-conventional. Provide at least two examples for each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** India consumed approximately **35 MToe of commercial primary energy** in a reference year. The breakdown was: coal 50%, oil 30%, natural gas 8%, hydro 4%, nuclear 1%, and renewables 7% (figures are illustrative). Compute: (i) the energy contributed by each source in PJ, (ii) the equivalent GWh of thermal energy from coal alone, and (iii) the electrical energy obtainable from coal at an average conversion efficiency of 35%. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Axis 1 — Primary/Secondary:**
- Primary: coal, crude oil, natural gas, uranium, solar radiation, wind, hydro head
- Secondary: electricity, petrol, diesel, kerosene, LPG, CNG, hydrogen, town gas, coke

**Axis 2 — Renewable/Non-Renewable:**
- Non-renewable: coal, oil, natural gas, uranium (lifetime-limited stock)
- Renewable: solar, wind, hydro, biomass, tidal, geothermal, wave

**Axis 3 — Conventional/Non-conventional:**
- Conventional (mature, mainstream): coal, oil, gas, large hydro, nuclear
- Non-conventional (newer/emerging, often renewable): solar, wind, small hydro, biomass, tidal, fuel cells, green hydrogen

*(2 + 2 + 2 + 1 marks for example sufficiency.)*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** Total = 35 MToe = $35 \times 10^6$ Toe.

**Step 1 — Energy per source in Toe (millions):**

$$
E_{coal} = 35 \times 0.50 = 17.5 \text{ MToe}
$$
$$
E_{oil} = 35 \times 0.30 = 10.5 \text{ MToe}
$$
$$
E_{gas} = 35 \times 0.08 = 2.8 \text{ MToe}
$$
$$
E_{hydro} = 35 \times 0.04 = 1.4 \text{ MToe}
$$
$$
E_{nuclear} = 35 \times 0.01 = 0.35 \text{ MToe}
$$
$$
E_{RE} = 35 \times 0.07 = 2.45 \text{ MToe}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert MToe to PJ (1 Toe = 42 GJ = 0.042 PJ):**

$$
E_{coal} = 17.5 \times 0.042 = 0.735 \text{ EJ} = 735 \text{ PJ}
$$
$$
E_{oil} = 10.5 \times 0.042 = 441 \text{ PJ}
$$
$$
E_{gas} = 2.8 \times 0.042 = 117.6 \text{ PJ}
$$
$$
E_{hydro} = 1.4 \times 0.042 = 58.8 \text{ PJ}
$$
$$
E_{nuclear} = 0.35 \times 0.042 = 14.7 \text{ PJ}
$$
$$
E_{RE} = 2.45 \times 0.042 = 102.9 \text{ PJ}
$$

*(1 mark for percentages, 2 marks for Toe-to-PJ conversion logic.)*

**Step 3 — Coal energy in GWh (thermal):**

$$
E_{coal,th} = 735 \text{ PJ} = 735 \times 10^{15} \text{ J}
$$

$$
E_{coal,th} = \frac{735 \times 10^{15}}{3.6 \times 10^{12}} \text{ GWh} = 204{,}167 \text{ GWh} = 204.17 \text{ TWh}
$$

*(2 marks for conversion.)*

**Step 4 — Electrical energy from coal at 35% efficiency:**

$$
E_{coal,el} = 204.17 \text{ TWh} \times 0.35 = 71.46 \text{ TWh} = 71{,}460 \text{ GWh}
$$

*(2 marks for final answer.)*

> **Final answers:** (i) as above in PJ; (ii) **204.17 TWh (thermal)**; (iii) **≈ 71.46 TWh (electrical)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Energy-share problems):**
> - The exam often gives percentages that **do not sum to exactly 100%** (e.g., 99% or 101%) due to rounding — always state this and proceed.
> - Forgetting to convert **PJ → J** before dividing by 3.6×10¹² is the most common error.
> - The examiner expects **two decimal places** in intermediate steps to show rigour.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — read this 3 times before the exam.**

- **Definition**: Commercial energy = *priced, traded, market-accounted* energy. Non-commercial = *free, household-level* energy.
- **Primary vs Secondary**: Primary is from nature (coal, uranium, sun, wind, crude). Secondary is converted (electricity, refined fuels, hydrogen).
- **Renewable vs Non-renewable**: Renewables are flux resources (replenished naturally); non-renewables are finite stocks (coal, oil, gas, U-235).
- **Five pillars of commercial energy**: Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Nuclear, Commercial Renewables (Solar/Wind/Hydro).
- **Energy units (memorize these 6):**
  - 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ (exact)
  - 1 Toe = 42 GJ ≈ 11.63 MWh
  - 1 BTU = 1055 J
  - 1 Quad = 10¹⁵ BTU
  - 1 calorie = 4.184 J
  - 1 MU = 10⁶ kWh
- **Conversion chain has 4 stages**: Extraction → Processing → Conversion → Delivery. Each stage has losses.
- **Indian context (memorize):**
  - ~70% of electricity from coal
  - ~85% crude oil imported
  - ~50% natural gas imported
  - 100% uranium concentrate imported (mining of U ore is domestic, but processing depends on imports)
- **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** = $E_{actual} / (P_{rated} \times T)$ — used for conventional plants.
- **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)** — used for renewables.
- **R/P ratio** = Reserves / Annual Production — gives years of supply (static indicator).
- **Specific Coal Consumption (SCC)** = coal mass / electrical energy = typical value **0.6 – 0.7 kg/kWh** for modern plants; ~1.0–1.2 kg/kWh for older Indian subcritical plants.
- **Energy density (highest to lowest among fossils)**: LPG > Petrol > Diesel > Natural gas (per SCM) > Coal.
- **CO₂ intensity (lowest to highest)**: Nuclear < Wind < Solar < Hydro < Natural gas < Oil < Coal.
- **Why commercial energy matters in energy management**: Without commercial-energy accounting, **baseline setting, M&V (Measurement & Verification), and ISO 50001 audits are impossible**.
- **India's installed renewable capacity is growing rapidly**, but the **absolute share of fossil fuels (especially coal)** will remain dominant for at least 2 more decades — a key KTU point.
- **For every numerical**: *write GIVEN data → write TO FIND → write FORMULA → substitute → BOX the answer with units*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. Confusing **GCV and NCV** (NCV < GCV because of latent heat of vaporization of water from fuel-bound hydrogen).
> 2. Confusing **PLF with CUF** (and vice versa).
> 3. Using **kcal/kg** without conversion to kJ/kg in SI calculations.
> 4. Forgetting the difference between **thermal** and **electrical** MWh/TWh in power-plant questions.
> 5. Treating **electricity as a primary source** in classification questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Final Energy Consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Final Energy Consumption # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition > **Final Energy Consumption (FEC)** is defined as the energy commodity (electricity, petroleum products, natural gas, coal, renewables, etc.) that is delivered to the end-use sectors — **Industry, Transport, Residential, Commercial/Institutional, Agriculture, and Non-energy Use** — for the purpose of running machinery, lighting, heating, cooling, motive power, or as feedstocks. It represents the energy crossing the *final boundary* of the energy system, after the deduction of all conversion, transformation, and distribution losses associated with energy production and delivery. In the **IEA (International Energy Agency)** energy balance framework — which is the standard adopted by KTU's Energy Management curriculum — the global energy chain is split into: $$\text{Primary Energy} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Transformation}} \;\text{Final Energy} \;\xrightarrow{\text{End-Use}} \;\text{Useful Energy} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Service}} \;\text{Energy Service}$$ The arrows represent energy losses (entropy, friction, heat dissipation). At each arrow, a portion of energy is destroyed or degraded. > [!NOTE] KTU Syllabus Highlight > The 2024 Scheme explicitly distinguishes *Final Energy* from *Primary Energy* and emphasizes sector-wise accounting as a prerequisite to Energy Auditing and Demand Side Management (DSM). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine a **water supply system** in a city: - **Reservoir + Treatment Plant** = Primary Energy (raw, unrefined energy: coal in the mine, crude oil underground, solar radiation). - **Water pumped into your apartment's overhead tank** = Final Energy (delivered, in a usable commodity form — electricity at your socket, gasoline at the pump, LPG in your cylinder). - **Water flowing out of your tap to wash clothes** = Useful Energy (after appliance conversion efficiency losses). - **Clean clothes in your wardrobe** = Energy Service. Final Energy Consumption, therefore, is like the **water in the overhead tank** — it has been *purified* and *delivered*, but it has not yet been *used* for its intended purpose. The losses between the tank and your clothes are the *end-use efficiency losses* of washing machines, geysers, etc. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants and metrics are used universally in KTU 2024 Scheme energy accounting: | Metric | Value/Unit | Purpose | |---|---|---| | **1 TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent)** | 41.868 GJ = 11.63 MWh | Standard energy currency | | **1 kWh** | 3.6 MJ (exactly) | Electrical energy unit | | **1 kcal** | 4.1868 kJ | Heat energy | | **1 BTU** | 1055.06 J | Imperial heat unit | | **Standard Calorific Values** | Variable per fuel | Converts mass → energy | | **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** | Used by IEA/UN | Excludes latent heat of vaporization | | **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** | Used in industry | Includes latent heat | > [!TIP] Examiner's Tip > KTU board examiners consistently expect students to state **NCV (Net Calorific Value)** when reporting FEC in TOE, because latent heat in flue gases is unrecoverable in most applications. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Energy Conversion Chain — Structured Logic The journey from Primary Energy to Final Energy involves several sequential transformations, each characterized by a specific **conversion efficiency** $(\eta)$: **Step 1 — Primary Energy Extraction:** Raw fuels (coal, crude, uranium, sunlight) are extracted from nature. For renewables, the "primary equivalent" is the energy content before conversion (e.g., solar radiation falling on a PV panel = primary solar energy). **Step 2 — Energy Transformation:** Coal → Electricity (Thermal Power Plant), Crude → Refined Products, Uranium → Steam → Turbine. The conversion efficiency here is governed by the **Carnot limit** and practical irreversibilities. $$\eta_{\text{thermal plant}} = \frac{W_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} \approx 33\% \text{ to } 42\%$$ **Step 3 — Transmission & Distribution (T&D):** Electricity flows through high-voltage lines, transformers, and distribution networks. T&D losses in India average **18–22%**, compared to the global benchmark of **<8%**. **Step 4 — Final Energy Delivery:** The energy commodity reaches the end-user meter, fuel pump, or storage tank. This is the **FEC** boundary. **Step 5 — End-Use Conversion:** The end-user device (motor, boiler, vehicle engine, LED) converts final energy into useful work or heat. ## 2.2 Sectoral Classification of Final Energy Consumption KTU 2024 Scheme follows the **IEA/IPCC sectoral taxonomy**: | S.No. | Sector | Major End-Uses | Typical Share (India, ~2022) | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Industry** | Motors, boilers, process heat, captive power | ~46% | | 2 | **Transport** | Road (diesel/petrol), rail (electricity/diesel), aviation, shipping | ~32% | | 3 | **Residential** | Cooking (LPG/biomass), lighting, space cooling (AC), fans, water heating | ~10% | | 4 | **Commercial & Public Services** | Office AC, lighting, elevators, IT equipment | ~6% | | 5 | **Agriculture** | Pump-sets (electricity/diesel), tractors | ~3% | | 6 | **Non-energy Use** | Petrochemical feedstocks, lubricants, bitumen | ~3% | > [!NOTE] Why This Matters for KTU > Energy Conservation Act (2001) and the **Perform, Achieve & Trade (PAT)** scheme in India explicitly target **Designated Consumers (DCs)** in the Industry sector because it dominates the FEC pie. ## 2.3 Conversion Factors — The Engineer's Toolset Since FEC is reported in multiple units (kWh, TOE, Joule, BTU), a uniform metric is essential: **1. From Mass of Fuel to Energy:** $$E_{\text{final}} = m \times \text{NCV}$$ where $m$ = mass of fuel (kg or tonnes) and $\text{NCV}$ = Net Calorific Value (kJ/kg or MJ/kg). **2. From Electrical Energy to TOE:** $$E_{\text{TOE}} = \frac{E_{\text{kWh}}}{11630}$$ **3. From Volume of Liquid Fuel to Energy:** $$E_{\text{final}} = V \times \rho \times \text{NCV}$$ where $\rho$ = density (kg/m³ or kg/L). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Variables | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_F = m \cdot \text{NCV}$ | Mass $m$, NCV | kJ, MJ | Fuel → energy conversion | | 2 | $\text{FEC}_{\text{TOE}} = E_{\text{kWh}} / 11630$ | Electrical energy | kWh, TOE | Electricity → TOE | | 3 | $E_P = E_F / \eta_{\text{conversion}}$ | Primary $E_P$, Final $E_F$ | MJ | Primary–Final back-calculation | | 4 | $EI = \frac{\text{FEC}}{\text{GDP}}$ | Energy intensity $EI$ | TOE / crore ₹ | Macro-level efficiency | | 5 | $C_f = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}}$ | Conversion factor $C_f$ | dimensionless | Sectoral efficiency | | 6 | $C_{\text{CO}_2} = \text{FEC} \times \text{EF}$ | Carbon intensity | tCO₂ | Emissions estimation | | 7 | $C_{\text{specific}} = \frac{E}{P}$ | Specific energy consumption | kWh/tonne, MJ/kg | Industrial SEC | | 8 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot \eta_{\text{T\&D}} \cdot \eta_{\text{end-use}}$ | Cascade efficiency | dimensionless | System efficiency | | 9 | $K = P \times \frac{E}{G} \times \frac{C}{P}$ | Kaya Identity | dimensionless | Decomposition of emissions | | 10 | $\Delta E = E_{\text{base}} - E_{\text{actual}}$ | Energy savings | kWh, TOE | Audit calculations | > [!WARNING] Pitfall Alert > Never confuse **Energy Intensity ($EI$)** (which decreases with efficiency gains) with **Energy Demand** (which generally increases with growth). A falling $EI$ does *not* mean falling FEC. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Final Energy Consumption data drives: - **National Energy Policy:** The Ministry of Power and MNRE use FEC projections to plan capacity addition. - **PAT Scheme Benchmarking:** Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) targets are set per industry subsector. - **Carbon Trading:** India’s Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (2023) uses FEC × Emission Factor to determine compliance. - **Climate Action (NDC):** India’s Nationally Determined Contribution targets 45% reduction in *emission intensity* of GDP — a function of FEC. - **Energy Audit (BEE-certified):** All ISO 50001 audits begin with the FEC baseline. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Primary–Final–Useful Energy Cascade The energy chain can be mathematically derived from First Law of Thermodynamics applied to each stage. Let $E_P$, $E_F$, $E_U$ represent Primary, Final, and Useful energy respectively. **Stage 1 — Transformation Loss:** At the power plant or refinery, the energy input $E_P$ is converted to final energy $E_F$ with efficiency $\eta_1$: $$E_F = \eta_1 \cdot E_P$$ The energy lost in transformation is: $$L_1 = E_P - E_F = (1 - \eta_1) E_P$$ **Stage 2 — Transmission & Distribution Loss:** The final energy delivered at the user boundary is: $$E_F^{\text{user}} = \eta_2 \cdot E_F = \eta_2 \cdot \eta_1 \cdot E_P$$ where $\eta_2$ is the T&D efficiency. In India, $\eta_2 \approx 0.78$ to $0.82$ typically. **Stage 3 — End-Use Conversion:** At the end-user device (motor, boiler, lamp), the final energy is converted to useful energy: $$E_U = \eta_3 \cdot E_F^{\text{user}} = \eta_3 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_1 \cdot E_P$$ where $\eta_3$ is the end-use device efficiency. **Cascade Expression:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_U &= \eta_{\text{overall}} \cdot E_P \\ \eta_{\text{overall}} &= \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_3 \end{aligned} $$ **Numerical Illustration:** For electricity from coal: - $\eta_1$ (coal-to-electricity thermal plant) = $0.35$ - $\eta_2$ (T&D) = $0.80$ - $\eta_3$ (electric motor) = $0.90$ $$ \eta_{\text{overall}} = 0.35 \times 0.80 \times 0.90 = 0.252 \approx 25.2\% $$ > **Conversion Logic:** This means that **74.8%** of the original chemical energy in coal is lost before the motor shaft produces useful mechanical work. This is the core motivation for energy conservation and renewable energy adoption. ## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Intensity (EI) Energy Intensity is the macroeconomic metric used by KTU and BEE to track energy efficiency at a national/sectoral level: $$EI = \frac{\text{Total FEC}}{\text{GDP}}$$ with units typically **TOE per crore of GDP** (India) or **MJ per USD** (international). **Derivation in differential form:** $$ \begin{aligned} d(EI) &= d\left(\frac{F}{G}\right) \\ &= \frac{G \cdot dF - F \cdot dG}{G^2} \\ &= \frac{1}{G} dF - \frac{F}{G^2} dG \\ &= EI \cdot \frac{dF}{F} - EI \cdot \frac{dG}{G} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{d(EI)}{EI} &= \frac{dF}{F} - \frac{dG}{G} \end{aligned} $$ where $F$ = FEC, $G$ = GDP. The percentage change in $EI$ equals the percentage change in FEC minus the percentage change in GDP. This is the **Log-Mean Divisia Index (LMDI)** decomposition principle. **Engineering Interpretation:** - If GDP grows at 7% and FEC grows at 4%, then EI falls at 3% per year → decoupling is happening. - A negative correlation between EI and GDP is the desired trajectory for sustainable development. ## 3.3 Derivation: Kaya Identity The Kaya Identity (Yoichi Kaya, 1990) decomposes national CO₂ emissions into multiplicative drivers: $$ \begin{aligned} C &= P \times \frac{E}{P} \times \frac{C}{E} \\ \text{where: } C &= \text{CO}_2 \text{ emissions (tCO}_2\text{)} \\ P &= \text{Population} \\ \frac{E}{P} &= \text{Per-capita FEC (TOE/person)} \\ \frac{C}{E} &= \text{Carbon intensity of energy (tCO}_2\text{/TOE)} \end{aligned} $$ **Re-derivation from first principles:** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= \text{FEC} \times \text{EF}_{\text{FEC}} \\ \text{FEC} &= P \times \frac{\text{FEC}}{P} \\ \therefore \quad C &= P \times \frac{\text{FEC}}{P} \times \text{EF}_{\text{FEC}} \end{aligned} $$ where $\text{EF}_{\text{FEC}}$ is the average emission factor of the final energy mix (weighted by fuel shares). > **Conversion Logic:** Kaya Identity shows that emissions can be reduced by acting on **any of the three drivers independently**: decarbonizing energy supply (lower $C/E$), reducing per-capita FEC (efficiency, behavior), or population stabilization. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sectoral Conversion Factors | Sector | Final Energy Form | Conversion to Useful | Typical $\eta_3$ | |---|---|---|---| | Industry — Electric motor | Electricity | Mechanical work | 88–95% | | Industry — Boiler | Coal / Furnace oil | Process steam | 70–85% | | Transport — SI Engine | Petrol | Mechanical work at wheel | 25–35% | | Transport — Diesel Engine | Diesel | Mechanical work at wheel | 30–40% | | Residential — Incandescent bulb | Electricity | Light | 5–10% | | Residential — LED | Electricity | Light | 40–50% | | Residential — Room AC | Electricity | Cooling | 2.5–3.5 (COP) | | Commercial — Lift/Elevator | Electricity | Mechanical work | 70–85% | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A thermal power plant consumes **5,000 tonnes of coal per day**. The NCV of coal is **24 MJ/kg**. The T&D loss is **20%**. The end-use is an electric motor with efficiency **92%**. Calculate: (i) Daily FEC at the user boundary (in kWh) (ii) Useful energy output (in MJ) (iii) Overall cascade efficiency **Solution:** **Step (i) — Primary Energy Input:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_P &= m \times \text{NCV} \\ &= 5000 \times 1000 \text{ kg} \times 24 \text{ MJ/kg} \\ &= 1.2 \times 10^8 \text{ MJ/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step (ii) — Plant Conversion:** Assume typical plant efficiency $\eta_1 = 0.35$: $$ \begin{aligned} E_F^{\text{plant}} &= 0.35 \times 1.2 \times 10^8 \\ &= 4.2 \times 10^7 \text{ MJ/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step (iii) — T&D Stage:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_F^{\text{user}} &= (1 - 0.20) \times 4.2 \times 10^7 \\ &= 3.36 \times 10^7 \text{ MJ/day} \end{aligned} $$ Converting to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ): $$ \begin{aligned} E_F^{\text{user}} &= \frac{3.36 \times 10^7}{3.6} \\ &\approx 9.33 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step (iv) — Useful Energy at Motor Shaft:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_U &= 0.92 \times 3.36 \times 10^7 \\ &= 3.0912 \times 10^7 \text{ MJ/day} \end{aligned} $$ **Step (v) — Overall Cascade Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{overall}} &= 0.35 \times 0.80 \times 0.92 \\ &= 0.2576 \approx 25.76\% \end{aligned} $$ > **Conversion Logic:** From 5,000 tonnes of coal, only **~25.76%** reaches the motor as useful mechanical work. The rest (74.24%) is lost to flue gas, condenser heat, line losses, and motor losses. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Energy Conversion Chain — Block Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Primary Energy
Coal, Crude, Uranium, Solar] B[Transformation
Power Plant, Refinery] C[Transmission and Distribution
T and D Grid] D[Final Energy Consumption
FEC at User Meter] E[End Use Device
Motor, Boiler, Lamp] F[Useful Energy
Mechanical, Heat, Light] G[Energy Service
Comfort, Mobility, Production] A -->|Loss L1| B B -->|Loss L2| C C -->|Loss L3| D D -->|Loss L4| E E -->|Loss L5| F F --> G L1[Transformation Loss
65 to 70 percent] L2[T and D Loss
18 to 22 percent] L3[Final Boundary
Zero loss by definition] L4[End Use Loss
10 to 75 percent] L5[Service Realisation
User dependent] ``` ## 4.2 Sectoral Final Energy Consumption Map ```mermaid flowchart TD FEC[Final Energy Consumption
FEC] FEC --> IND[Industry
46 percent share] FEC --> TRA[Transport
32 percent share] FEC --> RES[Residential
10 percent share] FEC --> COM[Commercial
6 percent share] FEC --> AGR[Agriculture
3 percent share] FEC --> NEU[Non Energy Use
3 percent share] IND --> IND1[Electric Motors] IND --> IND2[Process Boilers] IND --> IND3[Captive Power] TRA --> TRA1[Road Transport] TRA --> TRA2[Railways] TRA --> TRA3[Aviation] TRA --> TRA4[Shipping] RES --> RES1[Cooking Fuels] RES --> RES2[Lighting] RES --> RES3[Space Cooling] RES --> RES4[Water Heating] COM --> COM1[HVAC Systems] COM --> COM2[Office Equipment] COM --> COM3[Vertical Transport] AGR --> AGR1[Irrigation Pumps] AGR --> AGR2[Tractors] NEU --> NEU1[Petrochemicals] NEU --> NEU2[Lubricants] NEU --> NEU3[Bitumen] ``` ## 4.3 Kaya Identity Decomposition Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD CO2[National CO2 Emissions] CO2 --> POP[Population P] CO2 --> PE[Per Capita FEC E over P] CO2 --> CI[Carbon Intensity of Energy C over E] POP --> P1[Demographic Trends] POP --> P2[Urbanisation] PE --> PE1[Economic Activity Level] PE --> PE2[Energy Efficiency] PE --> PE3[Lifestyle Choices] CI --> CI1[Fuel Mix Shift] CI --> CI2[Renewable Penetration] CI --> CI3[Carbon Capture] ``` > **Diagram Reading Note:** The Kaya tree above shows that reducing emissions requires multi-pronged intervention. A common KTU board question asks students to identify which lever is *most cost-effective* — typically the carbon intensity of energy (renewable substitution) yields the largest leverage. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2023] **Define Final Energy Consumption (FEC). How is it different from Primary Energy Consumption?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Final Energy Consumption is the energy in the form of electricity, petroleum products, natural gas, coal, or renewables that is delivered to the end-use sectors (Industry, Transport, Residential, Commercial, Agriculture) for running appliances, machinery, lighting, heating, or transportation, *after* all transformation and distribution losses have been accounted for. **Difference from Primary Energy (1.5 Marks):** | Aspect | Primary Energy | Final Energy | |---|---|---| | Boundary | At source (mine, well, sun) | At user boundary (meter, pump) | | Losses | Not yet deducted | Conversion + T&D losses deducted | | Example | Coal in mine (chemical energy) | Electricity at factory switchboard | | Conversion stage | Before transformation | After transformation | --- ### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List any six sectoral categories of Final Energy Consumption as per the IEA framework.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — 0.5 marks each):** 1. **Industry** — manufacturing, process industries, captive power 2. **Transport** — road, rail, aviation, inland waterway, pipeline 3. **Residential** — households, cooking, lighting, appliances 4. **Commercial and Public Services** — offices, hotels, hospitals, government buildings 5. **Agriculture and Forestry** — irrigation pumps, tractors, harvesters 6. **Non-energy Use** — petrochemical feedstocks, lubricants, bitumen, asphalt --- ## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > Note: In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B Module-1 questions carry an internal choice. **Answer ANY ONE** of the two options below. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **energy conversion chain** from Primary Energy to Energy Services, clearly identifying where Final Energy Consumption is demarcated. State typical efficiency values for each stage. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (3 Marks):** The energy conversion chain consists of the following sequential stages: 1. **Primary Energy Source** — Coal, crude oil, natural gas, uranium, hydro, solar, wind, biomass. 2. **Energy Transformation** — Conversion to electricity, refined petroleum, or processed gas. Examples: thermal power plant, refinery, LNG plant. 3. **Transmission and Distribution (T&D)** — Movement of final energy carriers (electricity grid, pipeline, fuel transport) to the end-user. 4. **Final Energy Consumption (FEC) Boundary** — The point at which energy is delivered to the end-user meter or storage. 5. **End-Use Conversion** — Conversion of final energy to useful work or heat via motors, boilers, lamps, engines. 6. **Useful Energy** — Mechanical shaft work, process heat, illumination, cooling. 7. **Energy Service** — The final desired output: comfort, mobility, production, communication. **Typical Efficiencies (4 Marks — listed below, 0.5 each):** | Stage | Efficiency | Loss Component | |---|---|---| | Thermal power plant | 33–42% | Flue gas, condenser, auxiliaries | | Combined cycle gas plant | 55–60% | Lower stack temperature | | Refinery | 88–92% | Process losses | | T&D (electricity, India) | 78–82% | Line losses, theft, technical losses | | Electric motor | 88–95% | Copper + iron losses | | Incandescent lamp | 5–10% | Heat dissipation | | LED lamp | 40–50% | Heat, optics | | SI engine (vehicle) | 25–35% | Exhaust, cooling, friction | | Diesel engine | 30–40% | Exhaust, cooling, friction | | Steam boiler | 70–85% | Flue gas, radiation | **FEC Demarcation:** FEC begins *after* the T&D stage. It includes all energy that crosses the boundary into the end-user's premises or vehicle tank, irrespective of whether it is subsequently converted to useful work. --- **(b)** A captive power plant uses **furnace oil** with NCV = **42 MJ/kg**. The plant consumes **2,000 kg/day** and generates electricity at an overall generation efficiency of **30%**. The electricity is used in a factory with a T&D loss of **4%** and a motor efficiency of **90%**. Calculate: (i) Daily Primary Energy Input (in MJ) (ii) Daily Final Energy at Factory Boundary (in kWh) (iii) Useful Mechanical Work Output (in MJ) (iv) Overall Cascade Efficiency **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step (i) — Primary Energy Input:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_P &= m \times \text{NCV} \\ &= 2000 \text{ kg/day} \times 42 \text{ MJ/kg} \\ &= 84{,}000 \text{ MJ/day} \end{aligned} $$ **(Marks: 1.5)** **Step (ii) — Electricity Generated at Plant:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{gen}} &= 0.30 \times 84{,}000 \\ &= 25{,}200 \text{ MJ/day} \end{aligned} $$ Convert to kWh (÷ 3.6): $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{gen, kWh}} &= \frac{25{,}200}{3.6} \\ &= 7000 \text{ kWh/day} \end{aligned} $$ After T&D loss of 4%, FEC at factory: $$ \begin{aligned} E_F &= (1 - 0.04) \times 7000 \\ &= 6720 \text{ kWh/day} \end{aligned} $$ In MJ: $6720 \times 3.6 = 24{,}192$ MJ/day. **(Marks: 2)** **Step (iii) — Useful Mechanical Work:** $$ \begin{aligned} E_U &= 0.90 \times 24{,}192 \\ &= 21{,}772.8 \text{ MJ/day} \end{aligned} $$ **(Marks: 1.5)** **Step (iv) — Overall Cascade Efficiency:** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{\text{overall}} &= \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \eta_3 \\ &= 0.30 \times 0.96 \times 0.90 \\ &= 0.2592 = 25.92\% \end{aligned} $$ **(Marks: 2)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** What is the **Kaya Identity**? Derive it mathematically. Discuss its significance in formulating national climate policy. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (2 Marks):** The Kaya Identity, proposed by Yoichi Kaya in 1990, is a decomposition equation that expresses a nation's total CO₂ emissions as the product of four socio-economic factors: $$ C = P \times \frac{G}{P} \times \frac{E}{G} \times \frac{C}{E} $$ where: - $C$ = total CO₂ emissions - $P$ = population - $G/P$ = GDP per capita (economic activity) - $E/G$ = energy intensity of GDP - $C/E$ = carbon intensity of energy mix **Mathematical Derivation (3 Marks):** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= \text{FEC} \times \text{EF}_{\text{FEC}} \quad &\text{(Energy balance)} \\ \text{FEC} &= G \times \frac{\text{FEC}}{G} \quad &\text{(Define energy intensity)} \\ G &= P \times \frac{G}{P} \quad &\text{(Define per-capita GDP)} \\ \therefore \quad C &= P \times \frac{G}{P} \times \frac{\text{FEC}}{G} \times \text{EF}_{\text{FEC}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad C &= P \times \frac{G}{P} \times \frac{E}{G} \times \frac{C}{E} \end{aligned} $$ **Significance in Climate Policy (2 Marks):** - **Diagnostic Tool:** Identifies which factor is the largest emission driver for a given nation. - **Policy Targeting:** Helps design targeted interventions (e.g., India focuses on reducing $E/G$ through PAT scheme, while EU focuses on $C/E$ through renewables). - **Scenario Modeling:** Forms the basis of IPCC AR6 scenarios (SSP1–SSP5) for projecting future emissions. - **Decoupling Analysis:** Reveals whether economic growth is being decoupled from emissions. --- **(b)** The following data is given for a nation: - Population: **1.4 billion** - GDP: **₹230 lakh crore** - Total FEC: **870 MTOE** - Average Emission Factor: **2.45 tCO₂/TOE** Calculate: (i) Per-capita FEC (ii) Energy Intensity of GDP (TOE per crore ₹) (iii) Total CO₂ Emissions (iv) Carbon Intensity of Energy Comment on the decoupling status if the GDP grows at 6% and FEC grows at 3.5% per annum. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step (i) — Per-capita FEC:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{E}{P} &= \frac{870 \times 10^6 \text{ TOE}}{1.4 \times 10^9} \\ &= 0.6214 \text{ TOE/person} \\ &\approx 0.62 \text{ TOE/person} \end{aligned} $$ **(Marks: 1.5)** **Step (ii) — Energy Intensity:** $$ \begin{aligned} EI &= \frac{E}{G} = \frac{870 \times 10^6 \text{ TOE}}{230 \times 10^5 \text{ crore ₹}} \\ &= \frac{870 \times 10^6}{2.3 \times 10^7} \\ &= 37.83 \text{ TOE/crore ₹} \end{aligned} $$ **(Marks: 1.5)** **Step (iii) — Total CO₂ Emissions:** $$ \begin{aligned} C &= \text{FEC} \times \text{EF} \\ &= 870 \times 10^6 \text{ TOE} \times 2.45 \text{ tCO}_2\text{/TOE} \\ &= 2.1315 \times 10^9 \text{ tCO}_2 \\ &= 2131.5 \text{ MtCO}_2 \end{aligned} $$ **(Marks: 1.5)** **Step (iv) — Carbon Intensity:** $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{C}{E} &= 2.45 \text{ tCO}_2\text{/TOE} \quad \text{(given, verified by calculation)} \end{aligned} $$ **(Marks: 0.5)** **Decoupling Comment (2 Marks):** With GDP growth = 6% and FEC growth = 3.5%, Energy Intensity change is: $$ \frac{\Delta(EI)}{EI} = \Delta E - \Delta G = 3.5\% - 6.0\% = -2.5\% $$ Energy intensity is falling at **2.5% per annum**, indicating **relative decoupling** — economic growth is outpacing energy demand growth. However, since FEC itself is still increasing (absolute emissions will rise), it is **not yet absolute decoupling**. To achieve absolute decoupling, renewable penetration and aggressive efficiency measures must reduce FEC in absolute terms. --- > [!WARNING] KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > - **Forgetting to convert TOE to kWh (or vice versa):** Examiners strictly deduct 1 mark for unit errors. Remember 1 TOE = 11,630 kWh. > - **Using GCV instead of NCV:** IEA standard for FEC accounting uses **NCV**. Using GCV in a worked problem will be marked incorrect. > - **Confusing Energy Intensity with Energy Demand:** A falling EI does NOT mean falling FEC. State both metrics explicitly. > - **Skipping the Kaya Identity derivation steps:** Examiners look for the four-factor decomposition explicitly; do not write it as a single equation without showing the substitution logic. > - **Ignoring the FEC boundary:** When asked to compute FEC, do NOT include the transmission loss. The boundary is *after* T&D. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [ ] **FEC** is energy delivered to the end-use sector; it is the energy *between* T&D and the user device. - [ ] **Energy Chain:** Primary → Transformation → T&D → **FEC** → End-Use → Useful → Service. - [ ] **Cascade Efficiency:** $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_1 \cdot \eta_2 \cdot \eta_3$ — product of all stage efficiencies. - [ ] **Sectoral Shares (India):** Industry (46%) > Transport (32%) > Residential (10%) > Commercial (6%) > Agriculture (3%) > Non-energy (3%). - [ ] **TOE conversion:** 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ = 11,630 kWh = 10 Gcal (approx). - [ ] **NCV vs GCV:** Use NCV for FEC accounting; GCV includes latent heat of vaporization. - [ ] **Kaya Identity:** $C = P \times (G/P) \times (E/G) \times (C/E)$ — four multiplicative drivers of emissions. - [ ] **Energy Intensity ($EI$):** FEC per unit GDP — falling EI = improving efficiency, but not necessarily falling FEC. - [ ] **Decoupling:** Relative decoupling (EI falling) ≠ Absolute decoupling (FEC falling). - [ ] **Conversion:** $E = m \times \text{NCV}$; $E_{\text{TOE}} = E_{\text{kWh}} / 11630$; $E_{\text{kWh}} = E_{\text{MJ}} / 3.6$. - [ ] **PAT scheme** (India) and **ISO 50001** (international) both rely on the FEC baseline for benchmarking. - [ ] **India's T&D loss** is among the highest globally (18–22%); BEE's UJALA and smart-grid initiatives target reduction to <12%. - [ ] **Renewable energy reduces $C/E$** (carbon intensity) without affecting $E$ or $G$ directly — the most politically feasible lever. - [ ] **Energy Audit** always begins with the **FEC baseline** before device-level analysis.

topics.title: Indian Energy Scenario
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Indian Energy Scenario

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🇮🇳 Indian Energy Scenario — A Foundational Study

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

The **Indian Energy Scenario** refers to the comprehensive study of the current state of energy resources, their production, distribution, consumption patterns, and the policy framework governing them within the geographic and political boundaries of India. It encompasses the evaluation of **primary energy sources** (coal, crude oil, natural gas, nuclear, hydro, solar, wind, biomass) and the **secondary forms** (electricity, refined petroleum products) along with the macroeconomic, geopolitical, and environmental factors shaping India's energy landscape.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this topic lays the foundation for understanding the **energy balance**, **energy intensity**, **per capita energy consumption**, **energy import dependency**, and the **renewable transition roadmap** of the nation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Scenario (Official Definition):** A holistic snapshot of a nation's energy sector that describes the availability of energy resources, the technologies used for their conversion, the demand from various sectors (industrial, domestic, agricultural, transport), and the policies that govern production, distribution, and consumption.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **massive kitchen** preparing meals for **1.4 billion people** (India's population). This kitchen has:
- **Stoves running on different fuels** (coal, gas, oil, uranium, sun, wind, water) — these are the *primary energy sources*.
- **A limited pantry of ingredients** — some are imported from other countries (crude oil, natural gas, coal), and some are homegrown (solar, wind, hydro).
- **Different cooking stations** — industries, homes, vehicles, offices, farms — all demanding energy at different times.
- **A strict budget** and a growing demand where tomorrow's meals will be larger than today's.

Just as a chef must balance flavor, cost, health, and sustainability, India's energy planners must balance **affordability, accessibility, security, and sustainability**. The "Indian Energy Scenario" is the report card of this gigantic kitchen — it tells us how much fuel we have, how much we use, where we are wasteful, and what new recipes (technologies) we need to invent.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Key Highlights)

The following constants and standard units are used throughout the energy scenario analysis:

- **Standard Energy Unit:** **1 TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) = 41.868 GJ ≈ 42 GJ ≈ 11.63 MWh (thermal)**
- **Electrical Energy Conversion:** **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal**
- **Barrel of Crude Oil:** **1 barrel = 159 litres ≈ 6.12 GJ**
- **Million Tonnes of Oil Equivalent (MTOE):** The national-level reporting unit used by the **Ministry of Power** and **NITI Aayog**
- **Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC):** Measured in **kg of oil equivalent (kgoe)** per person per year
- **Energy Intensity (EI):** Measured as **TPES / GDP** in **kgoe per 1000 USD of GDP (PPP)**
- **Installed Capacity Unit:** **GW (Gigawatt) = 10⁹ Watts**
- **1 MW (thermal) ≈ 3,000 tonnes of coal/year** (typical Indian coal-fired plant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PESFT414, Module 1):** Students must be able to describe the structure of India's energy supply, the dominant role of coal, the heavy crude oil import dependency, the rapid expansion of renewables, and the key national targets (500 GW non-fossil, Net Zero by 2070).

## 1.4 Salient Features of the Indian Energy Scenario

1. **Third-largest energy consumer** in the world (after China and USA) and the **third-largest electricity producer**.
2. **Coal-dominated energy mix:** Coal accounts for roughly **~50%** of installed power capacity and **~70%** of electricity generation.
3. **High import dependency for crude oil (~85%) and natural gas (~50%).**
4. **Low per capita energy consumption** compared to the global average — a development indicator.
5. **Fastest-growing renewable energy programme** in the world (Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass).
6. **Universal access to electricity** declared achieved in 2018-19 (all inhabited villages electrified).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📚 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of India's Energy Economy

The Indian energy economy can be deconstructed into **five interlinked pillars**. Each pillar has a specific role, a specific stakeholder ecosystem, and a specific set of policy levers.

### Pillar I — Primary Energy Resources

India's primary energy basket is a **mix of fossil fuels, nuclear, and renewables**.

| Resource | Domestic Reserve Status | Share in TPES* | Key Concern |
|----------|------------------------|---------------|-------------|
| Coal | 5th largest globally (~360 Bn Tonnes) | ~44% | Pollution, mine productivity |
| Crude Oil | Limited reserves (0.3% world) | ~23% | 85% import dependency |
| Natural Gas | Modest (~1% world) | ~6% | 50% import dependency |
| Nuclear | Thorium-rich (world's largest) | ~1% | Capital intensity, public perception |
| Hydro | ~145 GW economically viable | ~1% | Monsoon dependency, displacement |
| Solar | ~300 sunny days/yr, 5,000 TWh/yr potential | ~2% (rising) | Storage, land acquisition |
| Wind | ~302 GW onshore, 174 GW offshore | ~2% (rising) | Grid intermittency |
| Biomass / Bagasse | ~25 GW cogeneration | ~2% | Supply chain logistics |

*TPES = Total Primary Energy Supply

### Pillar II — Sectoral Energy Demand

India's energy demand arises from four end-use sectors:

1. **Industrial Sector (~50%)** — steel, cement, fertilizers, petrochemicals, textiles.
2. **Transport Sector (~15%)** — road, rail, aviation, shipping.
3. **Domestic/Residential (~22%)** — lighting, cooking, space conditioning.
4. **Agriculture (~8%)** — irrigation pumps, farm machinery.
5. **Commercial & Others (~5%)** — offices, data centres, street lighting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for KTU Energy Management?** Effective energy conservation efforts must **target the industrial sector first**, as it accounts for the largest share. This is why Perform, Achieve, and Trade (PAT) scheme, Energy Star labelling, and ISO 50001 audits focus on industry.

### Pillar III — Conversion Technologies

Primary resources must be **transformed** into useful forms (electricity, refined fuels, heat). The key conversion pathways are:

- **Coal → Steam → Turbine → Generator → Electricity** (thermal power plants)
- **Coal → Gasification → Syngas → Chemicals/CTL fuel** (clean coal)
- **Uranium/Thorium → Fission → Steam → Turbine → Electricity** (nuclear power)
- **Water → Kinetic Energy → Turbine → Electricity** (hydropower)
- **Photons → PV Cell → DC → Inverter → AC** (solar PV)
- **Kinetic Wind → Blade → Gearbox → Generator → AC** (wind turbines)
- **Biomass → Anaerobic Digestion → Biogas** or direct combustion

### Pillar IV — Transmission & Distribution Infrastructure

India has one of the **largest synchronous electricity grids in the world** (single frequency 50 Hz grid), managed by:
- **PowerGrid Corporation** (central transmission utility)
- **State DISCOMs** (distribution companies)
- **POSOCO / Grid Controller of India** (system operation)

The **"One Nation One Grid"** vision links all five regional grids (Northern, Western, Southern, Eastern, North-Eastern) into a single national grid enabling cross-regional power transfer.

### Pillar V — Policy & Regulatory Framework

| Policy / Mission | Year | Objective |
|------------------|------|-----------|
| Electricity Act | 2003 | Liberalization, open access, regulatory commissions |
| National Tariff Policy | 2006 | Affordable tariffs, renewable purchase obligation |
| Jawaharlal Nehru National Solar Mission (JNNSM) | 2010 | 100 GW solar by 2022 (revised to 300 GW by 2030) |
| UDAY Scheme | 2015 | Financial turnaround of DISCOMs |
| UJALA (LED distribution) | 2015 | Energy-efficient lighting at scale |
| Saubhagya Scheme | 2017 | 100% household electrification |
| National Hydrogen Mission | 2021 | Green hydrogen ecosystem |
| LiFE Movement | 2022 | Lifestyle for Environment — demand-side conservation |
| National Green Hydrogen Mission | 2023 | 5 MMT green H₂ by 2030 |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Key Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** For this topic, numerical problems are rare. However, the following formulas and conversions are **frequently asked** in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) questions. Memorize them.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol | Unit | Purpose |
|---|------------------|--------|------|---------|
| 1 | Energy Intensity $EI = \dfrac{TPES}{GDP}$ | EI | kgoe / 1000 USD PPP | Measures energy efficiency of economy |
| 2 | Per Capita Energy Consumption $PCEC = \dfrac{TPES}{Population}$ | PCEC | kgoe / person / yr | Standard of living indicator |
| 3 | Import Dependency $ID = \dfrac{Imports}{Total Consumption} \times 100$ | ID | % | Energy security metric |
| 4 | Renewable Share $RS = \dfrac{Renewable\ Capacity}{Total\ Installed\ Capacity} \times 100$ | RS | % | Decarbonization progress |
| 5 | Plant Load Factor $PLF = \dfrac{Actual\ Energy\ Generated}{Max\ Possible\ Energy} \times 100$ | PLF | % | Plant efficiency |
| 6 | Specific Coal Consumption $SCC = \dfrac{Coal\ Used\ (kg)}{Unit\ Generated\ (kWh)}$ | SCC | kg/kWh | Coal plant efficiency |
| 7 | Auxiliary Consumption $AC = \dfrac{Internal\ Use\ Power}{Gross\ Generation} \times 100$ | AC | % | Plant parasitic load |
| 8 | Heat Rate $HR = \dfrac{Heat\ Input\ (kcal)}{Energy\ Output\ (kWh)}$ | HR | kcal/kWh | Thermal plant efficiency |
| 9 | Thermal Efficiency $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{860}{HR} \times 100$ | η | % | Plant efficiency |
| 10 | Standard Energy Conversion | 1 kgoe = 41.868 MJ | — | Cross-fuel comparison |

**Critical Reminder for KTU Boards:** The vertical bar `|` is **strictly avoided** in this table. Use `\vert` or `\mid` if absolute value is needed in equations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the Indian Energy Scenario is **not academic** — it is fundamental to:
- **Investment decisions** in power generation (a thermal plant analyst must know coal reserves, PLF, and demand-supply gap).
- **Demand forecasting** for grid planning.
- **Policy advocacy** in MNRE, BEE, CEA.
- **Industrial energy audits** (ISO 50001) where the auditor contextualizes facility consumption against national benchmarks.
- **Carbon accounting** and **ESG reporting** in corporates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🔬 Analytical Matrix, Calculations & Numerical Demonstrations

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Energy Mix Percentage Calculation

**Problem (KTU Style):** India's total installed power capacity is approximately **430 GW**. Out of this, the contribution is: Coal 205 GW, Gas 25 GW, Diesel 0.5 GW, Nuclear 8 GW, Hydro 47 GW, Solar 82 GW, Wind 47 GW, Biomass 10 GW, Small Hydro 5 GW. Calculate the percentage share of (a) Coal, (b) Renewables (Solar + Wind + Biomass + Small Hydro), and (c) Non-fossil (Nuclear + Hydro + Renewables).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify total installed capacity.**

$$
C_{total} = 205 + 25 + 0.5 + 8 + 47 + 82 + 47 + 10 + 5
$$

$$
C_{total} = 429.5\ \text{GW} \approx 430\ \text{GW}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Coal share.**

$$
\%Coal = \frac{205}{430} \times 100 = 47.67\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Renewables share** (Solar + Wind + Biomass + Small Hydro).

$$
C_{re} = 82 + 47 + 10 + 5 = 144\ \text{GW}
$$

$$
\%Renewables = \frac{144}{430} \times 100 = 33.49\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Non-fossil share** (Nuclear + Hydro + Renewables).

$$
C_{nf} = 8 + 47 + 144 = 199\ \text{GW}
$$

$$
\%Non\text{-}fossil = \frac{199}{430} \times 100 = 46.28\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** India is close to the **50% non-fossil milestone** it pledged at COP26 (Glasgow, 2021). The target is **500 GW of non-fossil installed capacity by 2030**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Per Capita Electricity Consumption

**Problem:** India's annual electricity consumption is approximately **1,500 TWh** and the population is **1.42 billion (2023)**. Calculate the per capita electricity consumption in **kWh/person/year** and compare it with the global average of **3,500 kWh/person/year**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert TWh to kWh.**

$$
E_{annual} = 1500\ \text{TWh} = 1500 \times 10^{9}\ \text{kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Divide by population.**

$$
PCEC = \frac{1500 \times 10^{9}}{1.42 \times 10^{9}}
$$

$$
PCEC = 1056.34\ \text{kWh/person/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with global average.**

$$
\text{Global multiple} = \frac{3500}{1056.34} = 3.31
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** India's per capita electricity consumption is roughly **one-third** of the world average. This reflects India's developmental status and is a **key driver** for the projected electricity demand growth at **5-6% CAGR** through 2030.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Energy Intensity Computation

**Problem:** India's TPES is **~870 MTOE** and the GDP (PPP) is approximately **$14 trillion**. Calculate India's energy intensity in **kgoe per 1000 USD of GDP (PPP)**. The global average is **~120 kgoe / 1000 USD PPP**. Comment on the result.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert TPES from MTOE to kgoe.**

$$
TPES = 870\ \text{MTOE} = 870 \times 10^{6}\ \text{TOE} = 870 \times 10^{9}\ \text{kgoe}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert GDP to 1000 USD units.**

$$
GDP = 14\ \text{trillion USD} = 14 \times 10^{12}\ \text{USD} = 14 \times 10^{9}\ \text{(1000 USD units)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute energy intensity.**

$$
EI = \frac{870 \times 10^{9}}{14 \times 10^{9}}
$$

$$
EI = 62.14\ \text{kgoe / 1000 USD PPP}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment.**

Since India's energy intensity (62.14) is **lower** than the global average (120), this **suggests** that India's economy is more energy-efficient per unit of output. However, the **real reason** is the dominance of the **services sector** (which is low-energy-intensive) in GDP, and the **under-industrialization** of low-income states. Energy intensity alone can be misleading — it must be read alongside per capita consumption.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not equate "low energy intensity" with "high energy efficiency." It often reflects economic structure (services vs. industry mix), not technological efficiency. Always contextualize.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Import Dependency and Energy Security

**Problem:** India's crude oil consumption is **~5 million barrels per day (mbpd)**. Domestic production is **~0.7 mbpd**. Calculate the import dependency ratio and discuss the implications for energy security.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute imports.**

$$
\text{Imports} = 5.0 - 0.7 = 4.3\ \text{mbpd}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute import dependency.**

$$
ID = \frac{4.3}{5.0} \times 100 = 86\%
$$

**Step 3 — Discuss implications.**

A **86% import dependency** means:
- India is exposed to **global oil price volatility** (e.g., the 2008 and 2022 oil price shocks).
- **Forex reserve pressure**: India spends over **$120 billion annually** on crude imports.
- **Strategic vulnerability**: Any disruption in the Strait of Hormuz or Suez Canal paralyzes the supply chain.
- **Strategic response**: Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR), Ethanol Blending Programme (E20 by 2025), Refinery diversification, Renewable transition.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Table — Key Indian Energy Statistics

| Parameter | Value (Approx. 2023-24) | Global Context |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------|
| Installed Power Capacity | 430 GW | 3rd largest |
| Per Capita Electricity Consumption | ~1,200 kWh | World avg. ~3,500 kWh |
| Total Primary Energy Supply | ~870 MTOE | 3rd largest consumer |
| Energy Intensity | ~62 kgoe/1000 USD PPP | World avg. ~120 |
| Crude Oil Import Dependency | ~85-87% | High vulnerability |
| Natural Gas Import Dependency | ~50% | LNG dependence |
| Coal Share in Generation | ~70% | Major emitter |
| Renewable Installed Capacity | ~190 GW | 4th largest globally |
| Solar Installed Capacity | ~82 GW | 2nd largest (after China) |
| Wind Installed Capacity | ~47 GW | 4th largest |
| CO₂ Emissions | ~3.4 GtCO₂ | 3rd largest emitter |
| Per Capita CO₂ Emissions | ~2.4 tCO₂ | World avg. ~4.7 tCO₂ |
| Net-Zero Target Year | 2070 | Among the latest targets |
| Non-fossil Target by 2030 | 500 GW | COP26 Glasgow pledge |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Section A — Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: Define the term "Per Capita Energy Consumption." Why is it considered a development indicator? Comment on India's per capita energy consumption in comparison with the global average.**

**Model Answer:**

**Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC)** is defined as the average energy consumed by one individual of a country in one year. Mathematically, it is the total primary energy supply (TPES) divided by the total population.

$$
PCEC = \frac{TPES}{Population}
$$

It is measured in **kgoe (kilogram of oil equivalent) per person per year** for total energy, or **kWh per person per year** for electricity.

It is considered a development indicator because it directly correlates with the **quality of life** — higher PCEC implies better access to modern amenities like lighting, heating, cooling, mobility, industrial productivity, healthcare, and digital services. Historically, developed nations like the USA (~7,000 kgoe) and EU (~3,000 kgoe) have high PCEC, while developing nations like India (~640 kgoe) have lower values.

India's per capita electricity consumption is approximately **1,200 kWh/year**, which is roughly **one-third** of the world average of **~3,500 kWh/year**. This gap indicates substantial room for growth as India's economy and population incomes rise, and it is a key driver of the projected energy demand growth.

*(3 marks: Definition – 1, Indicator – 1, India comparison – 1)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: What is meant by "Energy Intensity" of an economy? How is it calculated? Discuss India's current energy intensity and its implications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Energy Intensity (EI)** of an economy is the amount of energy required to produce one unit of economic output. It is a measure of the **energy efficiency** of the overall economy (though it can be influenced by economic structure).

**Formula:**

$$
EI = \frac{TPES}{GDP\ (PPP)}
$$

The unit is **kgoe per 1000 USD of GDP (PPP)**. A **lower** EI value indicates a more energy-efficient economy (or an economy dominated by services rather than heavy industry).

India's current energy intensity is approximately **~62 kgoe/1000 USD PPP**, which is significantly **lower than the world average** of ~120 kgoe/1000 USD PPP. While this appears favorable, the lower value is largely a result of India's economic structure (dominance of low-energy-intensive services and agriculture) rather than technological efficiency. As India industrializes further, energy intensity may rise before structural shifts lower it again. The implication is that **technology upgrades and industrial efficiency** must be prioritized through PAT scheme, BEE star labelling, and ISO 50001.

*(3 marks: Definition + formula – 1.5, India context – 1.5)*

---

## Section B — Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Discuss in detail the Indian Energy Scenario with reference to the following aspects: (a) Primary energy resources of India and their share in the energy mix (7 marks). (b) The major energy challenges facing India and the national strategies adopted to address them (7 marks).**

#### Part (a) — Primary Energy Resources and Their Share

**Model Answer:**

India's primary energy basket is **diverse but coal-dominated**. The major primary energy resources are:

1. **Coal:** The dominant resource, accounting for approximately **~44% of TPES** and **~47%** of installed power capacity. India has the **5th largest coal reserves** globally (~360 billion tonnes). Coal is used predominantly in thermal power plants and the steel sector.

2. **Crude Oil:** India is highly import-dependent (~85%) with very limited domestic reserves. Crude oil accounts for **~23%** of TPES, primarily used in transport and petrochemical sectors.

3. **Natural Gas:** A cleaner fossil fuel, contributes **~6%** of TPES. Import dependency is ~50%, met largely through LNG terminals.

4. **Nuclear Power:** Provides **~2%** of electricity. India has a **three-stage nuclear programme** based on its vast thorium reserves. Currently, 8 GW is operational, with a target of 22 GW by 2031.

5. **Hydropower:** A mature renewable, ~47 GW installed. Only ~30% of the **145 GW economically viable potential** is tapped, with major projects in the Himalayan and Western Ghats.

6. **Solar Power:** The fastest-growing segment, ~82 GW installed, targeting **300 GW by 2030**. India receives **300 sunny days/year** with average insolation of **4-7 kWh/m²/day**.

7. **Wind Power:** ~47 GW installed, primarily in Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Karnataka, Rajasthan. Offshore wind potential of 174 GW.

8. **Biomass and Bagasse Cogeneration:** ~10 GW, used in sugar mills and rice straw-based plants.

9. **Small Hydro (≤25 MW):** ~5 GW across hilly states.

The overall **energy mix** is approximately: **Coal 44%, Oil 23%, Gas 6%, Biofuels/Waste 12%, Renewables 10%, Nuclear 1%, Hydro 4%** (TPES basis).

*(7 marks: 1 mark each for resources + 1 mark for table/summary)*

#### Part (b) — Energy Challenges and National Strategies

**Model Answer:**

India faces **four fundamental energy challenges**, each addressed by specific national strategies:

**Challenge 1: Energy Security (High Import Dependency)**
- 85% of crude oil and 50% of natural gas is imported.
- Strategy: Strategic Petroleum Reserves (5.33 MMT at Vizag, Mangalore, Padur); Ethanol blending (E20 by 2025); EV adoption (FAME-II scheme); diversification of import sources; promoting renewables to displace fossil imports.

**Challenge 2: Universal Access and Affordability**
- 18,000 villages were unelectrified pre-2014.
- Strategy: **Saubhagya Scheme** (free connections to 2.7 crore households); **UJALA** (LED bulb distribution at ₹70); **DDUGJY** (rural infrastructure); subsidy for poor households.

**Challenge 3: Climate Change and Emissions**
- India is the 3rd largest CO₂ emitter (~3.4 GtCO₂).
- Strategy: **Panchamrit** pledge at COP26 (500 GW non-fossil, 50% non-fossil share, 45% emissions intensity reduction, Net Zero by 2070); **Perform Achieve and Trade (PAT)** for industry; **Renewable Purchase Obligation (RPO)** for DISCOMs; **Green Hydrogen Mission** (5 MMT by 2030); **LiFE movement** for demand-side conservation.

**Challenge 4: Financial Health of DISCOMs**
- AT&C losses of ~20%, ₹6 lakh crore debt.
- Strategy: **UDAY** scheme; tariff rationalization; smart metering (250 million meters); **Revamped Distribution Sector Scheme (RDSS)** of ₹3 lakh crore outlay.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "energy strategy" with "renewable targets." Energy strategy is a **broad framework** that includes **access (Saubhagya), efficiency (PAT, BEE), finance (UDAY), and decarbonization (renewables, hydrogen)**. Don't reduce it to just renewable targets.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) Explain the major initiatives and policies adopted by the Government of India to promote renewable energy and energy conservation. Discuss their impact (7 marks). (b) Explain the concept of energy security in the context of India. What are the strategic measures adopted to enhance energy security (7 marks)?**

#### Part (a) — Renewable Energy & Conservation Policies

**Model Answer:**

The Government of India has launched **multiple flagship schemes** for renewable energy promotion and energy conservation:

**Renewable Energy Promotion:**

1. **Jawaharlal Nehru National Solar Mission (JNNSM)** — Launched in 2010 under the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC). Target upgraded to **300 GW solar by 2030**. Implemented through viability gap funding, rooftop solar subsidies, and Ultra Mega Solar Parks.

2. **National Wind-Solar Hybrid Policy (2018)** — Promotes hybridization to improve grid stability and reduce land footprint.

3. **PM-KUSUM Scheme** — Solar pumps for farmers (3 crore target), solarization of grid pumps, and solarization of barren lands.

4. **Green Energy Open Access Rules (2022)** — Allows commercial/industrial consumers to procure renewable power directly, bypassing DISCOMs.

5. **National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023)** — 5 MMT green H₂ production by 2030, with ₹19,744 crore outlay.

6. **Offshore Wind Programme** — 4 GW tendered off Gujarat and Tamil Nadu coasts.

**Energy Conservation Initiatives:**

1. **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** — Standards & Labelling programme (star ratings for ACs, refrigerators, fans, LEDs), Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) for commercial buildings.

2. **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) Scheme** — Mandatory energy efficiency targets for Designated Energy Consumers (DECs) in 13 sectors. Covered ~75% of industrial energy consumption.

3. **UJALA Programme** — Distributed 37+ crore LED bulbs, saving ~9,500 MW peak demand and 38 billion kWh/year.

4. **Standards & Labelling for Industrial Equipment** — Mandatory star ratings for transformers, motors, pumps.

5. **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC) 2017 and Eco Niwas Samhita (ENS)** — For commercial and residential buildings respectively.

6. **National Mission for Enhanced Energy Efficiency (NMEEE)** — Market-based mechanisms like PAT, ESCO, and Energy Efficiency Financing Platform.

**Impact:** As of 2024, renewable installed capacity has crossed **190 GW**, saving ~85 million tonnes of CO₂ annually. Energy efficiency programs have saved ~60 Billion Units of electricity cumulatively.

*(7 marks: 6 initiatives × 1 mark each + impact 1 mark)*

#### Part (b) — Energy Security in India

**Model Answer:**

**Energy security** is defined as the **uninterrupted availability of energy sources at an affordable price**. In India's context, this is a critical issue due to:
- High import dependency for fossil fuels
- Geopolitical exposure to oil-exporting regions
- Climate change-induced disruptions
- Domestic resource constraints

**Strategic Measures for Energy Security:**

1. **Diversification of Supply Sources:** Importing oil from multiple countries (Russia, Middle East, Africa, Americas) to avoid over-dependence on any one source.

2. **Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR):** Storage capacity of **5.33 MMT** (Vizag, Mangalore, Padur) providing ~10 days of cover, expandable to 12+ days.

3. **Ethanol Blending Programme:** Target of **E20 (20% ethanol blending in petrol) by 2025** to reduce crude oil demand.

4. **Electric Mobility (FAME-II and PM E-Drive):** Target of 30% EV penetration by 2030, displacing ~5 MMT of fuel annually.

5. **Renewable Energy Push:** Targeting 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030 reduces fossil fuel dependence structurally.

6. **Green Hydrogen Mission:** Decarbonization of hard-to-abate sectors (steel, fertilizers, refining, transport).

7. **Energy Efficiency:** PAT scheme reduces industrial energy intensity, lowering fuel demand.

8. **Bilateral Agreements:** Long-term LNG contracts (Qatar, USA, Australia), India-Middle East-Europe Economic Corridor (IMEC) for supply chains.

9. **Domestic Exploration:** OALP (Open Acreage Licensing Policy) for oil & gas; CBM (Coal Bed Methane) exploration.

10. **Refinery Capacity:** India is now a net exporter of refined petroleum products, providing forex cushion and supply security.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Energy security is NOT only about "importing more oil." It is a **composite strategy** of (a) diversifying imports, (b) building stockpiles, (c) substituting fossil fuels with renewables, (d) improving efficiency, and (e) shifting to electric mobility. Writing only about SPR or only about renewables will fetch partial marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Preparation)**

- ✅ **India is the 3rd largest energy consumer and electricity producer** globally.
- ✅ **Coal contributes ~44% to TPES and ~70% to electricity generation** — the single most important source.
- ✅ **Per capita electricity consumption of India ≈ 1,200 kWh** vs. world average of ~3,500 kWh.
- ✅ **Crude oil import dependency ≈ 85-87%** — the central driver of energy security policy.
- ✅ **COP26 Panchamrit Pledge (2021):** 500 GW non-fossil by 2030; 50% non-fossil share; 45% emission intensity reduction (over 2005); **Net Zero by 2070**.
- ✅ **Renewable installed capacity ≈ 190 GW** (4th largest globally); Solar ~82 GW (2nd largest); Wind ~47 GW (4th largest).
- ✅ **Energy Intensity (EI) = TPES / GDP** — India's value is ~62 kgoe/1000 USD PPP, lower than global average due to services-sector dominance.
- ✅ **Standard conversion:** 1 kgoe = 41.868 MJ; 1 TWh = 10⁹ kWh; 1 GW = 10⁹ W; 1 TOE ≈ 11.63 MWh (thermal).
- ✅ **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** is the nodal agency for energy conservation under the Energy Conservation Act, 2001.
- ✅ **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** is the flagship market-based industrial energy efficiency scheme.
- ✅ **Energy intensity can be misleading** — always contextualize with economic structure (services vs. industry mix).
- ✅ **UDAY, Saubhagya, UJALA, RDSS, FAME-II, Green Hydrogen Mission** are **must-remember** schemes.
- ✅ **MNRE** (Ministry of New and Renewable Energy) is the nodal ministry for renewables.
- ✅ **The Three-Stage Nuclear Programme** of India is based on the vast thorium reserves.
- ✅ **"One Nation, One Grid"** — single synchronous 50 Hz national grid for cross-regional power transfer.
- ✅ **"LiFE" (Lifestyle for Environment)** movement focuses on **demand-side conservation** — unique to India.
- ✅ **Five regional grids:** Northern, Western, Southern, Eastern, North-Eastern — all interconnected.
- ✅ **Energy storage** is the next frontier — National Framework for Promoting Energy Storage Systems (2019).
- ✅ **SAUBHAGYA = Pradhan Mantri Sahaj Bijli Har Ghar Yojana** — 100% household electrification.
- ✅ **Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation (AMRUT)** — energy efficiency in urban infrastructure.

> 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** When asked about "Indian Energy Scenario" in 14-mark questions, structure your answer as **(i) Primary energy resources with shares, (ii) Sectoral demand, (iii) Challenges, (iv) Policy responses**. This 4-part structure fetches full marks consistently.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sectoral Energy Consumption (Domestic, Industrial, and Other Sectors)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sectoral Energy Consumption** refers to the end-use classification of total national/regional energy demand into distinct consumer categories based on the purpose for which energy is utilized. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Energy Management and Conservation*, the three principal sectors are:

1. **Domestic (Residential) Sector** – Energy consumed in households for lighting, space conditioning (heating/cooling), cooking, water heating, refrigeration, and operating household appliances.
2. **Industrial Sector** – Energy consumed by manufacturing, processing, and production industries for motive power, process heat, steam generation, electrolysis, lighting, and auxiliary operations.
3. **Other Sectors** – Comprising the **Commercial (buildings/services)**, **Transportation**, and **Agriculture** sectors, collectively referred to as "Other" in standard energy balance sheets (e.g., those published by the Ministry of Power / BEE / IEA).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The 2024 scheme explicitly requires understanding of sector-wise energy consumption patterns, identification of energy-intensive sub-systems, and basic energy conservation opportunities (ECOs) in each sector. Numerical problems typically involve calculating connected load, energy bills, payback periods, and percentage savings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** The standard energy unit used in all KTU numerical problems is the **kilowatt-hour (kWh)** for electrical energy and **Tonne of Oil Equivalent (TOE)** for aggregate/primary energy. Conversion: **1 TOE ≈ 11,630 kWh ≈ 41.868 GJ**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Power Budget"

Imagine a city's energy as a large monthly **household budget** that must be distributed across several "family members" (sectors). 

- The **Industrial Sector** is the **eldest working sibling** — it earns the company money but also spends the most (≈ 35–45% of total national energy) on heavy machinery, furnaces, and motors.
- The **Domestic Sector** is the **middle sibling** — moderate consumption (≈ 20–25%) used for daily comforts like fans, lights, AC, and cooking.
- The **Transport Sector** is the **mobile sibling** — spends heavily on "fuel for movement" (≈ 15–20%).
- The **Commercial & Agriculture Sectors** are the **youngest siblings** — they grow fast and have unique needs (offices, malls, pumps, tractors).

Just as a financial advisor studies **where each rupee goes** to optimize savings, an energy manager must perform a **Sectoral Energy Audit** to find where electricity is wasted, where efficiency measures will yield the highest return, and which sector offers the largest payback potential.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following conversion factors are **permitted for use in KTU 2024 Scheme end-semester examinations** and are aligned with the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) and IEA conventions:

| Quantity | Standard Value |
|----------|----------------|
| 1 kWh | 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal |
| 1 TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) | 41.868 GJ = 11,630 kWh |
| 1 Calorie (thermochemical) | 4.184 J |
| 1 BTU | 1055.06 J |
| 1 Horsepower (HP) | 0.7457 kW = 746 W |
| 1 TR (Ton of Refrigeration) | 3.517 kW |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Sector-by-Sector Analysis

### A. Domestic (Residential) Sector

The domestic sector is characterized by **highly fragmented consumption** spread across millions of households. Major energy-consuming sub-systems include:

- **Lighting** (≈ 20–30% of household electricity): Incandescent bulbs are being rapidly replaced by LEDs (Light Emitting Diodes) under the **UJALA / UJALA 2.0 scheme** of BEE.
- **Space Cooling** (Fans, Air Conditioners): A typical 1.5 TR split AC consumes ≈ **1.5 to 2.0 kWh per hour** of operation; ceiling fans consume ≈ **75 W** (older) down to **28–32 W** (BEE 5-star).
- **Space Heating / Water Heating**: Geysers and room heaters are highly energy-intensive (2 kW typical geyser running 1 hour = 2 kWh).
- **Cooking**: LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) and electric induction cooktops.
- **Refrigeration**: A 5-star rated 200 L refrigerator consumes ≈ **170–200 kWh/year**.
- **Standby Loads (Phantom Loads)**: Devices drawing power when "off" — TV in standby ≈ 5 W, set-top box ≈ 15 W, chargers ≈ 1–2 W.

### B. Industrial Sector

The industrial sector is the **largest energy consumer in India (≈ 40–45% of total)**. Key sub-sectors and their energy intensity:

- **Process Industries** (Cement, Steel, Aluminium, Fertilizer, Pulp & Paper): Extremely energy-intensive. Cement industry uses ≈ **3.0–3.5 GJ per tonne of cement** (thermal) + ≈ **90–110 kWh per tonne** (electrical).
- **Boilers and Steam Systems**: Fuel (coal, oil, gas) + electric auxiliaries. Efficiency 60–85% for modern units.
- **Electric Motors and Drives**: Responsible for **≈ 65–70% of industrial electricity** consumption. Standard 3-phase induction motor efficiency ranges from **85% to 96%** depending on rating.
- **Compressed Air Systems**: Highly inefficient; typical leakages account for **20–30% of compressed air generation cost**.
- **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation & Air Conditioning) in industrial buildings**.
- **Pumps, Fans, Blowers**.
- **Lighting in factories**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designated Consumers (DCs) under the Energy Conservation Act, 2001:** Industries consuming > 1.5 MTOE (Million Tonne of Oil Equivalent) per year or with a connected load > 20 MW are mandated to conduct **mandatory energy audits** and appoint **Certified Energy Managers (CEM)**.

### C. Other Sectors (Commercial, Transport, Agriculture)

- **Commercial Sector**: Shopping malls, IT parks, hospitals, hotels, offices — dominated by HVAC (40–60% of building load), lighting (20–30%), and elevator/escalator systems. Energy intensity: ≈ **200–300 kWh/m²/year** for Grade A office buildings.
- **Transport Sector**: Road (≈ 80% of transport energy), Rail, Air, Water. Dominant fuels: Diesel (> 40%), Petrol, Aviation Turbine Fuel (ATF), CNG, Electric.
- **Agriculture Sector**: Primarily **irrigation pump sets** (electric and diesel). India has ≈ **20–22 million agricultural pump sets**, consuming ≈ **18–20% of national electricity** in many states.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas are **most frequently tested** in Module 1 numerical problems:

| S. No. | Formula / Relation | Description / Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = P \times t$ | Energy = Power × Time (basic electrical energy, kWh) |
| 2 | $E_{\text{annual}} = P \times \text{Hours/day} \times \text{Days/year}$ | Annual energy consumption calculation |
| 3 | $\text{TOE} = \dfrac{\text{Energy in kWh}}{11{,}630}$ | kWh to TOE conversion |
| 4 | $\text{Energy Cost} = E_{\text{kWh}} \times \text{Tariff (₹/kWh)}$ | Electricity bill calculation |
| 5 | $\text{Simple Payback Period (years)} = \dfrac{\text{Investment (₹)}}{\text{Annual Savings (₹/year)}}$ | Payback period for energy conservation measures |
| 6 | $\% \text{ Savings} = \dfrac{E_{\text{before}} - E_{\text{after}}}{E_{\text{before}}} \times 100$ | Percentage energy reduction |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful Output}}{\text{Energy Input}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency of any device (boiler, motor, lamp) |
| 8 | $\text{Power Factor (PF)} = \cos \phi = \dfrac{\text{Real Power (kW)}}{\text{Apparent Power (kVA)}}$ | Power factor in AC systems |
| 9 | $\text{Connected Load (kW)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Total installed load of a building/plant |
| 10 | $\text{Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)} = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy (kWh)}}{\text{Production Output (tonnes / units)}}$ | Energy intensity per unit of product |
| 11 | $\text{Demand Charge (₹)} = \text{Maximum Demand (kVA)} \times \text{Rate (₹/kVA)}$ | HT (High Tension) consumer billing component |
| 12 | $\text{Annual $\mathrm{CO_2}$ Emissions (kg)} = E_{\text{kWh}} \times \text{Emission Factor}$ | GHG accounting; India grid factor $\approx$ **0.82 kg $\mathrm{CO_2}$/kWh** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **demand charge** (₹/kVA, charged on Maximum Demand recorded during the billing cycle) with the **energy charge** (₹/kWh, charged on actual units consumed). The total electricity bill = **Energy Charge + Demand Charge + Fixed Charge + Tax/Duty**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sectoral energy analysis is the **first step** of any **Energy Audit (EA)** as defined by **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency, India)**. The methodology follows the standard **ASME / ISO 50002** audit framework:

- **Walk-through Audit** → Identify the dominant energy-consuming sector and sub-system.
- **Detailed Audit** → Measure, monitor, and benchmark SEC against industry norms.
- **Investment-grade Audit** → Recommend capital-intensive Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) with payback < 5 years.

A typical energy audit report for a **cement plant** would allocate energy to:
- Raw material crushing & grinding (≈ 5–10%)
- Pyro-processing (clinkerization) (≈ 50–60%) — **the dominant energy sink**
- Cement grinding (≈ 20–25%)
- Packing & dispatch (≈ 2–5%)

This **sub-sectoral energy flow** allows the energy manager to target interventions (e.g., waste heat recovery systems, vertical roller mills, variable frequency drives on ID/FD fans) where the maximum impact lies.
