# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Annual Energy Consumption and Cost Calculation for a Household

This is the **most fundamental KTU 2024 Module 1 numerical**. We derive the general expression for total annual energy and electricity cost for a residential consumer.

### Statement

A household operates a set of electrical appliances with rated power, daily usage hours, and quantity as given. Compute: (i) Total daily energy consumption, (ii) Total annual energy consumption, (iii) Annual electricity bill assuming a flat tariff of ₹8.50/kWh.

### Given Data (Sample Table)

| Appliance | Rating (W) | Quantity | Hours/day | Days/year |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LED Bulb (9 W) | 9 | 6 | 6 | 365 |
| Ceiling Fan (BEE 5-star, 32 W) | 32 | 4 | 14 | 365 |
| Refrigerator (200 L, 5-star) | 120 W avg | 1 | 24 | 365 |
| TV (LED, 80 W) | 80 | 1 | 5 | 365 |
| Geyser (2 kW) | 2000 | 1 | 1 | 180 |

### Step 1 — Daily Energy per Appliance

The instantaneous electrical power drawn by an appliance equals its rated wattage. Energy is the integral of power over time. For a constant-power appliance, energy over time $t$ is given by:

$$E = P \times t$$

where $P$ is the rated power in watts and $t$ is the duration of use in hours. The product $P \times t$ yields energy in **watt-hours (Wh)**. To obtain kilowatt-hours, divide by 1000.

For a refrigerator operating **continuously (24 h/day)**, the average power draw is significantly lower than its nameplate rating due to the compressor duty cycle. The factor **average power = 120 W** is used here as a BEE-representative value.

### Step 2 — Daily Energy in kWh

We apply $E_{\text{daily, kWh}} = \dfrac{P \times n \times t_{\text{h/day}}}{1000}$ to each appliance, where $n$ is the number of units.

For the **6 LED bulbs** (9 W, 6 h/day):

$$E_{\text{LED}} = \frac{9 \times 6 \times 6}{1000} = \frac{324}{1000} = 0.324 \text{ kWh/day}$$

For the **4 fans** (32 W, 14 h/day):

$$E_{\text{fan}} = \frac{32 \times 4 \times 14}{1000} = \frac{1792}{1000} = 1.792 \text{ kWh/day}$$

For the **refrigerator** (120 W average, 24 h/day):

$$E_{\text{fridge}} = \frac{120 \times 1 \times 24}{1000} = \frac{2880}{1000} = 2.880 \text{ kWh/day}$$

For the **TV** (80 W, 5 h/day):

$$E_{\text{TV}} = \frac{80 \times 1 \times 5}{1000} = \frac{400}{1000} = 0.400 \text{ kWh/day}$$

For the **geyser** (2000 W, 1 h/day):

$$E_{\text{geyser}} = \frac{2000 \times 1 \times 1}{1000} = 2.000 \text{ kWh/day}$$

### Step 3 — Total Daily and Annual Energy

Summing all contributions to obtain the **total daily energy consumption**:

$$E_{\text{daily, total}} = 0.324 + 1.792 + 2.880 + 0.400 + 2.000 = 7.396 \text{ kWh/day}$$

The annual energy is found by multiplying by the operating days per year (note the geyser is used only 180 days):

$$E_{\text{LED, annual}} = 0.324 \times 365 = 118.26 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{\text{fan, annual}} = 1.792 \times 365 = 654.08 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{\text{fridge, annual}} = 2.880 \times 365 = 1051.20 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{\text{TV, annual}} = 0.400 \times 365 = 146.00 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{\text{geyser, annual}} = 2.000 \times 180 = 360.00 \text{ kWh}$$

$$E_{\text{annual, total}} = 118.26 + 654.08 + 1051.20 + 146.00 + 360.00 = 2329.54 \text{ kWh/year}$$

### Step 4 — Annual Electricity Bill

The energy charge is computed as the product of total annual kWh and the per-unit tariff. For a flat domestic tariff of ₹8.50/kWh, the annual energy cost is:

$$\text{Cost} = E_{\text{annual, total}} \times \text{Tariff} = 2329.54 \times 8.50 = 19{,}801.09 \text{ ₹/year}$$

Note that the **fixed charge, electricity duty, and tax components** are not included in this simplified computation, but in actual KTU problems they may be added to obtain the total bill.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Sectoral Energy Distribution (India)

This matrix is **frequently asked in KTU 2024 Module 1 theory papers**. The data below represents the **indicative sectoral share of total primary energy consumption in India** (per BEE / NITI Aayog / IEA reports, generic values for academic use):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Sectoral Energy Mix (Indicative, India)}\\[6pt]
&\begin{bmatrix}
\text{Industrial} & \rightarrow & 41\% \text{ of total} \\
\text{Domestic} & \rightarrow & 24\% \text{ of total} \\
\text{Transport} & \rightarrow & 18\% \text{ of total} \\
\text{Agriculture} & \rightarrow & 12\% \text{ of total} \\
\text{Commercial} & \rightarrow & 05\% \text{ of total} \\
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Sub-Sectoral Break-up (Industrial)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Industrial Sub-sectoral Energy Share}\\[6pt]
&\text{Process heat \& steam} \approx 45\%,\quad
\text{Electric motors} \approx 25\%,\quad
\text{Compressed air} \approx 8\% \\[2pt]
&\text{Boilers \& furnaces} \approx 10\%,\quad
\text{HVAC \& lighting} \approx 7\%,\quad
\text{Others} \approx 5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Worked-Out Numerical — Payback Period Calculation

This derivation illustrates a typical **Energy Conservation Measure (ECM) numerical** in the industrial sector.

### Problem

A factory operates a 10 HP standard efficiency motor (efficiency 86%) for 3000 hours/year. It is proposed to replace it with a 10 HP **premium efficiency (IE3) motor** having an efficiency of **92%**. The motor draws 60% of its rated load on average. Investment for replacement = ₹18,000. Electricity cost = ₹9.00/kWh. Calculate:
- (i) Annual energy savings in kWh
- (ii) Annual cost savings in ₹
- (iii) Simple payback period

### Solution

**Step 1 — Rated Motor Input Power**

Mechanical output power = $10 \text{ HP} \times 0.7457 = 7.457 \text{ kW}$

**Step 2 — Output under 60% load**

$$P_{\text{out, actual}} = 0.60 \times 7.457 = 4.474 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Input power (standard motor)**

$$P_{\text{in, std}} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\eta_{\text{std}}} = \frac{4.474}{0.86} = 5.202 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Input power (premium motor)**

$$P_{\text{in, prem}} = \frac{4.474}{0.92} = 4.863 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Power saved**

$$\Delta P = 5.202 - 4.863 = 0.339 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 — Annual energy saved**

$$\Delta E = 0.339 \times 3000 = 1017.0 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 7 — Annual cost saved**

$$\text{Savings} = 1017.0 \times 9.00 = \text{₹ } 9153.0 \text{ /year}$$

**Step 8 — Simple Payback Period**

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{18{,}000}{9153} \approx 1.97 \text{ years}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Result Interpretation:** Payback < 3 years is considered **highly attractive** for industrial energy projects (PAT scheme of BEE / Perform-Achieve-Trade). Payback > 5 years is generally **not recommended** unless strategic (e.g., environmental compliance, ESG reporting).

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1(a)]**

> List the major sub-systems of energy consumption in the **industrial sector** and identify which one typically accounts for the highest share of electricity consumption.

**Model Answer:**

The major sub-systems of energy consumption in the industrial sector are:

1. **Process industries** (cement, steel, aluminium, fertilizer, chemicals, pulp & paper) — these are energy-intensive sectors consuming bulk thermal and electrical energy.
2. **Boilers and steam systems** — used extensively for process heating.
3. **Electric motors and drives** — used for pumping, compression, and mechanical motion.
4. **Compressed air systems** — for pneumatic tools and process air.
5. **HVAC and lighting** in industrial buildings.
6. **Pumps, fans, blowers and material handling equipment**.

The sub-system accounting for the **highest share of industrial electricity consumption** is the **electric motor and drive system**, responsible for approximately **65–70%** of total industrial electrical energy in India.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award the full 3 marks only when **both** the sub-systems *and* the dominant category are stated with approximate percentage.

---

### Question 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 1, Q1(b)]**

> Explain why the **agricultural sector** is a significant consumer of electricity in India, and name the principal end-use device.

**Model Answer:**

The agricultural sector in India is significant in national electricity consumption because of the **large installed base of irrigation pump sets** across the country — approximately **20 to 22 million** electric and diesel pump sets are in operation, primarily serving irrigation needs. In several states (Maharashtra, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Andhra Pradesh, Karnataka, Tamil Nadu), agricultural electricity consumption constitutes **25–35%** of the state's total electricity sales, often heavily subsidized.

The principal end-use device is the **electric motor-driven centrifugal/submersible pump set** (typically 3 HP to 10 HP). These pumps operate for long hours, often at low load factors and poor power factors, leading to high energy losses and a poor cost-to-output ratio.

Additionally, agriculture uses energy for:
- **Tubewell / borewell operation** (electric + diesel)
- **Drip and sprinkler irrigation pumps**
- **Cold storage and dairy chilling equipment**
- **Grain drying and milling**

This makes the agricultural sector a major target for DSM (Demand-Side Management) programs by the BEE and state electricity regulatory commissions.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** For each Module in the End Semester Examination (ESE), candidates must answer **one full 14-mark question** with internal choice between **OR (a)/(b)**. Both sub-parts carry **7 marks each**.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1, Q2]**

A residential household has the following connected load and usage profile:

| Appliance | Rating | Qty | Hours/day | Days/year |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Tube light (40 W) | 40 W | 4 | 8 | 365 |
| Ceiling fan (75 W) | 75 W | 3 | 16 | 365 |
| TV (100 W) | 100 W | 1 | 6 | 365 |
| Refrigerator (avg 150 W) | 150 W | 1 | 24 | 365 |
| Iron box (1000 W) | 1000 W | 1 | 0.5 | 200 |
| Mixer (500 W) | 500 W | 1 | 0.25 | 365 |

The domestic electricity tariff is **₹7.50/kWh** and the fixed charge is **₹150/month**.

#### (a) [7 Marks] Calculate the total annual energy consumption (kWh) and annual electricity bill.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Daily energy per appliance in kWh:**

$$E_{\text{tubelight}} = \frac{40 \times 4 \times 8}{1000} = 1.280 \text{ kWh/day}$$

$$E_{\text{fan}} = \frac{75 \times 3 \times 16}{1000} = 3.600 \text{ kWh/day}$$

$$E_{\text{TV}} = \frac{100 \times 1 \times 6}{1000} = 0.600 \text{ kWh/day}$$

$$E_{\text{fridge}} = \frac{150 \times 1 \times 24}{1000} = 3.600 \text{ kWh/day}$$

$$E_{\text{iron}} = \frac{1000 \times 1 \times 0.5}{1000} = 0.500 \text{ kWh/day}$$

$$E_{\text{mixer}} = \frac{500 \times 1 \times 0.25}{1000} = 0.125 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 2 — Total daily energy:**

$$E_{\text{daily, total}} = 1.280 + 3.600 + 0.600 + 3.600 + 0.500 + 0.125 = 9.705 \text{ kWh/day}$$

**Step 3 — Annual energy (multiplying by days/year):**

$$E_{\text{annual}} = \left[1.280 + 3.600 + 0.600 + 3.600 + 0.125\right] \times 365 + 0.500 \times 200$$

$$E_{\text{annual}} = 9.205 \times 365 + 100.0 = 3359.83 + 100.00 = 3459.83 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 4 — Annual cost calculation:**

$$\text{Energy charge} = 3459.83 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ } 25{,}948.71$$

$$\text{Fixed charge} = 150 \times 12 = \text{₹ } 1{,}800.00$$

$$\boxed{\text{Total annual bill} = 25{,}948.71 + 1{,}800.00 = \text{₹ } 27{,}748.71}$$

#### (b) [7 Marks] It is proposed to replace all tube lights (40 W) with LED tube lights (18 W) and all fans (75 W) with BEE 5-star fans (32 W). Calculate the **annual energy saved (kWh)** and the **percentage reduction** in total household energy.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — New daily energy for the replaced appliances:**

For 4 LED tube lights (18 W, 8 h/day, 365 days):

$$E_{\text{LED, new}} = \frac{18 \times 4 \times 8}{1000} \times 365 = 0.576 \times 365 = 210.24 \text{ kWh/year}$$

For 3 BEE 5-star fans (32 W, 16 h/day, 365 days):

$$E_{\text{fan, new}} = \frac{32 \times 3 \times 16}{1000} \times 365 = 1.536 \times 365 = 560.64 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 2 — Old annual energy for the same appliances:**

$$E_{\text{TL, old}} = \frac{40 \times 4 \times 8}{1000} \times 365 = 1.280 \times 365 = 467.20 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$E_{\text{fan, old}} = \frac{75 \times 3 \times 16}{1000} \times 365 = 3.600 \times 365 = 1314.00 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 3 — Savings per appliance:**

$$\Delta E_{\text{TL}} = 467.20 - 210.24 = 256.96 \text{ kWh/year}$$

$$\Delta E_{\text{fan}} = 1314.00 - 560.64 = 753.36 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 4 — Total annual energy saved:**

$$\Delta E_{\text{total}} = 256.96 + 753.36 = 1010.32 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage reduction in total household energy:**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{1010.32}{3459.83} \times 100 = 29.20\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Annual energy saved} = 1010.32 \text{ kWh; Percentage reduction} = 29.20\%}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 1, Q2 OR]**

A small-scale foundry operates an **electric induction furnace** for melting aluminium. The furnace has a **rated input of 100 kW** and is used for **8 hours per day, 300 days per year**. The existing furnace has an overall **energy efficiency of 55%**. A new energy-efficient furnace is available with **70% efficiency**, costing **₹8,00,000**. The electricity tariff is **₹9.50/kWh**. The useful melt output (kg/year) remains the same.

#### (a) [7 Marks] Compute the **annual energy (kWh) consumed** and the **useful heat output (in kWh thermal)** for both the old and new furnace.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Annual energy input for the old furnace:**

$$E_{\text{in, old}} = 100 \text{ kW} \times 8 \text{ h/day} \times 300 \text{ days} = 2{,}40{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 2 — Useful heat output (old):**

$$Q_{\text{useful, old}} = \eta_{\text{old}} \times E_{\text{in, old}} = 0.55 \times 2{,}40{,}000 = 1{,}32{,}000 \text{ kWh(th)/year}$$

**Step 3 — New furnace input for the same useful output:**

Since the useful output is unchanged, the new input is:

$$E_{\text{in, new}} = \frac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{\eta_{\text{new}}} = \frac{1{,}32{,}000}{0.70} = 1{,}88{,}571.43 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison summary:**

| Parameter | Old Furnace (55%) | New Furnace (70%) |
|---|---|---|
| Input Energy (kWh/year) | 2,40,000 | 1,88,571.43 |
| Useful Output (kWh) | 1,32,000 | 1,32,000 |
| Losses (kWh) | 1,08,000 | 56,571.43 |

#### (b) [7 Marks] Determine the **annual monetary savings**, the **simple payback period**, and comment on the **economic viability** of the replacement.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Annual energy saved:**

$$\Delta E = 2{,}40{,}000 - 1{,}88{,}571.43 = 51{,}428.57 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 2 — Annual monetary savings:**

$$\text{Savings} = 51{,}428.57 \times 9.50 = \text{₹ } 4{,}88{,}571.43 \text{ /year}$$

**Step 3 — Simple payback period:**

$$\text{SPP} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000}{4{,}88{,}571.43} = 1.638 \text{ years} \approx 19.66 \text{ months}$$

**Step 4 — Economic viability comment:**

$$\boxed{\text{The investment pays back in } \approx 1.64 \text{ years, which is well below the 3-year industry benchmark, making the replacement highly economically viable.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Student Mistakes:**
> 1. **Mixing kWh (electrical) and kWh(th) (thermal):** Furnace input is electrical kWh; useful output is thermal. Both can be expressed in kWh (since 1 kWh = 3600 kJ), but you **must** clearly state the basis. Examiners deduct 1 mark for ambiguity.
> 2. **Forgetting to use the new efficiency for calculating the *new* input:** Many students wrongly assume the new furnace has the *same annual input hours* (100 kW × 8 × 300). Correct approach: the **useful output is fixed**, so the new input is `Q_useful / η_new`.
> 3. **Payback unit error:** Payback must be in **years** (or months), not kWh or ₹. Always include units.
> 4. **Tariff unit confusion:** In India, the LT domestic/industrial tariff is **₹/kWh** (energy charge) **plus** ₹/kVA (demand charge for HT). For a 100 kW furnace problem, you may need to add demand charge — read the problem statement carefully.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Sectoral Energy Consumption (Module 1, KTU 2024)**

- ☐ **Three Sectors:** Domestic, Industrial, Other (Commercial + Transport + Agriculture). Memorize the **indicative share**: Industrial ~41%, Domestic ~24%, Transport ~18%, Agriculture ~12%, Commercial ~5%.
- ☐ **Dominant Industrial Sub-system:** Electric motors (≈ 65–70% of industrial electricity). Standard efficiency 86% (IE1) → IE3 premium 92%.
- ☐ **Domestic Sector Major Loads:** Cooling (fans/AC) ≈ 25%, Geyser ≈ 15%, Refrigerator ≈ 20%, Lighting ≈ 12%. LED replacement of incandescent/CFL gives **40–80% lighting energy savings**.
- ☐ **Standby Loads:** Phantom power can be **5–10%** of household electricity; unplugging electronics is a zero-cost ECM.
- ☐ **Key Conversion Factors:** 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ; 1 TOE = 11,630 kWh; 1 HP = 0.7457 kW; 1 TR = 3.517 kW.
- ☐ **Tariff Structure (India):** Total Bill = **Energy Charge + Demand Charge + Fixed Charge + Electricity Duty + Tax**.
- ☐ **PAT Scheme (Perform, Achieve, Trade):** A BEE-market-based mechanism for industrial energy efficiency; targets notified DCs.
- ☐ **Payback Period Rule of Thumb:** < 3 years → Accept; 3–5 years → Conditional; > 5 years → Reject (unless strategic/environmental).
- ☐ **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Always expressed as energy per unit of output (e.g., kWh/tonne cement). Lower SEC = higher efficiency.
- ☐ **GHG Factor (India grid):** ≈ **0.82 kg $\mathrm{CO_2}$/kWh** (CEA 2022–23 average). Use for emission calculations.
- ☐ **Formula to Remember Always:** Annual energy = $\text{kW} \times \text{hrs/day} \times \text{days/year}$; Payback = Investment / Annual ₹ Savings.
- ☐ **BEE 5-Star Rating:** Higher stars = lower energy consumption for the same useful output (refrigerators, ACs, fans, LEDs).

**End of Module 1 Notes — Sectoral Energy Consumption**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Needs of Growing Economy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Needs of Growing Economy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Energy Management and Conservation*, the term **"Energy Needs of a Growing Economy"** refers to the systematic study of how the demand, supply, distribution, and consumption patterns of energy resources evolve in direct response to a nation's industrial expansion, population growth, urbanization, and rising standards of living. It encompasses quantitative forecasting, sectoral analysis (industry, transport, domestic, agriculture), and strategic planning required to ensure that the energy infrastructure keeps pace with macroeconomic development without compromising on the trilemma of **energy security, energy equity (affordability), and environmental sustainability**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The energy-economy linkage is one of the most frequently tested topics. Students must master the difference between *energy consumption*, *energy intensity*, *energy elasticity*, and *energy productivity* — examiners often interchange them as distractors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Human Body**
> Think of an economy as a growing human body.
> - **Food (Energy Input)** is to the body what **Primary Energy (Coal, Oil, Gas, Renewables)** is to the economy.
> - **Blood Circulation (Energy Distribution Grid)** delivers this fuel to every organ.
> - **Muscles (Industries, Transport, Homes)** consume this energy to perform work (GDP output).
> - A growing child needs **more food, better circulation, and stronger muscles** — exactly like a growing economy needs more energy, better grids, and efficient end-use technologies.
> - If food supply falls behind growth, the body becomes weak (recession). If the body eats too much without exercise, it gets obese (energy waste / inefficiency). Balance is key — this is **Energy Management**.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Bolded Constants & Terms)

- **Energy Intensity (EI):** Energy consumed per unit of GDP (typically **toe per million USD** or **MJ/USD**).
- **Energy Elasticity (EE):** Ratio of percentage change in energy demand to percentage change in GDP.
- **Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC):** Total energy used divided by population (**toe/person**).
- **Primary Energy Supply (PES):** Total domestic energy production + imports − exports − stock changes.
- **Final Energy Consumption (FEC):** Energy delivered to end-use sectors (industry, transport, etc.).
- **Energy Security Index:** Composite indicator of supply diversification, import dependence, and resilience.
- **Reference Constant:** **1 tonne of oil equivalent (toe) = 41.868 GJ = 11.63 MWh** (must be memorized for KTU numericals).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Questions on "compare energy intensity of India vs. World" or "compute elasticity from a given table" appear almost every semester. Always quote units explicitly to earn full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Energy–Economy Linkage: Structured Logic

The relationship between a nation's economic growth and its energy demand is governed by four foundational pillars. Below is a step-by-step breakdown of the underlying logic:

1. **Industrialization Driver (The 'Why' of Energy Demand)**
   * Manufacturing, mining, cement, steel, and chemicals are energy-intensive sectors.
   * As an economy transitions from **agrarian → industrial → service-based**, energy demand follows an inverted-U curve (peak during heavy industrialization).
2. **Population & Urbanization Pressure (Demographic Driver)**
   * More people → more households → more cooking, lighting, cooling, and transport.
   * Urban lifestyles consume **3–5 times more energy** per capita than rural lifestyles.
3. **Affluence Effect (Income Elasticity)**
   * Higher disposable income → ownership of vehicles, air conditioners, appliances.
   * Saturation eventually occurs in developed economies (e.g., Japan, Germany).
4. **Efficiency Improvements (Decoupling Effect)**
   * Better technology, stricter standards (BEE Star Rating, PAT Scheme), and structural shifts reduce energy per unit of output.
   * This causes **relative decoupling** (energy grows slower than GDP) or **absolute decoupling** (energy falls while GDP rises).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear in 80% of numericals and Part A derivations.**

| **S.No** | **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula** | **Units (SI / Practical)** | **Boundary / Typical Value** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Energy Intensity (EI) | $EI = \dfrac{EC}{GDP}$ | toe per million USD ; MJ/USD | India ≈ 0.20 toe/k\$ ; World avg ≈ 0.18 |
| 2 | Energy Elasticity (EE) | $EE = \dfrac{\%\Delta EC}{\%\Delta GDP}$ | Dimensionless | Developing > 1 ; Developed < 1 |
| 3 | Per Capita Energy Consumption | $PCEC = \dfrac{EC}{Population}$ | toe/person ; GJ/person | India ≈ 0.65 toe ; USA ≈ 7.0 toe |
| 4 | Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) | $CAGR = \left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | % per year | Used for forecasting |
| 5 | Decoupling Ratio | $DR = \dfrac{\%\Delta EI}{\%\Delta GDP}$ | Dimensionless | DR < 0 implies decoupling |
| 6 | Energy Productivity | $EP = \dfrac{1}{EI} = \dfrac{GDP}{EC}$ | USD/toe | Inverse of intensity |
| 7 | Primary Energy Supply (PES) | $PES = P_d + I - E - \Delta S$ | Mtoe ; EJ | $P_d$ = domestic production, $I$ = imports, $E$ = exports, $\Delta S$ = stock change |
| 8 | Energy Security Index (simplified) | $ESI = w_1(1 - ID) + w_2(DV)$ | Score 0–1 | $ID$ = import dependence, $DV$ = diversification |
| 9 | Conversion — toe to GJ | $E_{GJ} = E_{toe} \times 41.868$ | GJ | 1 toe = 41.868 GJ |
| 10 | Conversion — toe to MWh | $E_{MWh} = E_{toe} \times 11.63$ | MWh | 1 toe = 11.63 MWh |
| 11 | Sectoral Energy Mix | $S_i = \dfrac{EC_i}{\sum EC_i} \times 100$ | % | Industry, Transport, Domestic, Agri |
| 12 | Forecasted Demand (Exponential) | $E_t = E_0 (1 + r)^t$ | toe ; PJ | $r$ = growth rate, $t$ = years |

> **Critical LaTeX Note for Students:** In the elasticity formula, always use $\%\Delta$ (percentage change) notation. **Do NOT confuse elasticity with intensity** — they are mathematically and conceptually distinct.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Government Policy:** India's *National Energy Policy (NEP)* and NITI Aayog's energy modeling rely entirely on these metrics to set 2030/2047 targets.
* **Corporate Sector:** Energy-intensive industries (cement, steel, aluminium) use **Energy Intensity benchmarking** to set internal reduction targets aligned with PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme.
* **Investment Decisions:** Multilateral agencies (World Bank, ADB, IEA) use **PCEC and EE projections** to approve funding for power plants and grid expansion.
* **Sustainable Development:** Tracking decoupling is now embedded in **UN SDG 7 (Affordable & Clean Energy)** and **SDG 13 (Climate Action)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forecasting Energy Demand Using CAGR (Exponential Growth Model)

**Problem Context:** A state's electricity consumption was **1200 GWh in 2020** and is expected to grow at a **CAGR of 6.5%**. Estimate the demand in **2030** and verify the toe equivalent.

**Step 1: Identify the given values.**

$$
E_0 = 1200 \text{ GWh}, \quad r = 6.5\% = 0.065, \quad t = 2030 - 2020 = 10 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the exponential growth formula.**

$$
E_t = E_0 \,(1 + r)^t
$$

**Step 3: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
E_{2030} = 1200 \times (1 + 0.065)^{10}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the base factor.**

$$
(1.065)^{10} = 1.8771 \quad \text{(from compound interest table / log computation)}
$$

**Step 5: Multiply to obtain the forecasted demand.**

$$
E_{2030} = 1200 \times 1.8771 = 2252.5 \text{ GWh}
$$

**Step 6: Convert to Mtoe (millions of tonnes of oil equivalent) for cross-sector comparison.**

$$
E_{Mtoe} = \frac{2252.5 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh} \times 3600 \text{ kJ/kWh}}{41.868 \times 10^9 \text{ kJ/toe}}
$$

**Step 7: Numerator simplification.**

$$
2252.5 \times 10^6 \times 3600 = 8.109 \times 10^{12} \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 8: Final conversion.**

$$
E_{Mtoe} = \frac{8.109 \times 10^{12}}{4.1868 \times 10^{10}} \approx 0.1937 \text{ Mtoe} = 193,700 \text{ toe}
$$

**Result:** The forecasted electricity demand in 2030 is **2252.5 GWh ≈ 0.194 Mtoe**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing Energy Elasticity from Historical Data

**Problem Context:** A country's energy consumption grew from **800 Mtoe (2015) to 950 Mtoe (2020)**. Its GDP grew from **2.0 trillion USD to 2.8 trillion USD** over the same period. Compute the **Energy Elasticity** and comment on decoupling.

**Step 1: Percentage change in energy consumption.**

$$
\%\Delta EC = \frac{950 - 800}{800} \times 100 = \frac{150}{800} \times 100 = 18.75\%
$$

**Step 2: Percentage change in GDP.**

$$
\%\Delta GDP = \frac{2.8 - 2.0}{2.0} \times 100 = \frac{0.8}{2.0} \times 100 = 40.0\%
$$

**Step 3: Apply the elasticity formula.**

$$
EE = \frac{\%\Delta EC}{\%\Delta GDP} = \frac{18.75}{40.0} = 0.46875
$$

**Step 4: Round to two decimal places and interpret.**

$$
EE \approx 0.47
$$

**Step 5: Interpretation (this is the 'how/why' that earns full marks).**

Since **EE < 1**, the economy exhibits **relative decoupling** — energy grows slower than GDP. This is typical of a **maturing economy** investing in efficiency and shifting to services. If EE > 1, the economy is in an **energy-intensive growth phase** (typical of rapidly industrializing nations).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Intensity Reduction and Decoupling Ratio

**Problem Context:** In 2010, India's EI was **0.30 toe/k\$**. In 2022, it reduced to **0.20 toe/k\$**. GDP grew by **6%** in that period. Compute the **Decoupling Ratio (DR)**.

**Step 1: Percentage change in Energy Intensity.**

$$
\%\Delta EI = \frac{0.20 - 0.30}{0.30} \times 100 = -33.33\%
$$

**Step 2: Apply the decoupling ratio formula.**

$$
DR = \frac{\%\Delta EI}{\%\Delta GDP} = \frac{-33.33}{6.0} = -5.56
$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

The **negative sign** confirms **absolute decoupling** — energy intensity dropped despite GDP growth. This is a **strong sustainability indicator** and is a common talking point in KTU theory answers.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy-Economy Interaction Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram maps how energy flows through an economy and how growth drivers feed back into demand — a high-yield visual for KTU 14-mark answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["Primary Energy Sources"] --> node2["Conversion and Refining"]
    node2 --> node3["Transmission and Distribution Grid"]
    node3 --> node4["Industrial Sector"]
    node3 --> node5["Transport Sector"]
    node3 --> node6["Residential and Commercial"]
    node3 --> node7["Agricultural Sector"]
    node8["Population Growth"] --> node9["Demand Drivers"]
    node10["Urbanization"] --> node9
    node11["Industrialization"] --> node9
    node12["Income Growth"] --> node9
    node9 --> node3
    node4 --> node13["GDP Output"]
    node5 --> node13
    node6 --> node13
    node7 --> node13
    node13 --> node14["Economic Growth"]
    node14 --> node15["Energy Policy and Planning"]
    node15 --> node1
    node16["Efficiency Standards BEE PAT"] --> node2
    node16 --> node4
    node16 --> node5
    node16 --> node6
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Left column** = Energy supply chain (sources → conversion → grid).
- **Middle column** = End-use sectors that convert energy into economic value (GDP).
- **Right column** = Feedback loop where economic growth informs policy, which in turn shapes the next cycle of energy planning.
- **node16 (Efficiency Standards)** acts as a *modulating influence* — a classic exam point about how policy decouples growth from consumption.

---

### 4.2 Scenario Planning Flowchart for Future Energy Needs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Baseline Data Year Y0"] --> nodeB["Sectoral Demand Analysis"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["GDP Growth Assumptions"]
    nodeB --> nodeD["Population Projections"]
    nodeB --> nodeE["Technology Penetration Rate"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Energy Demand Forecast Y0 to Yn"]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["Reference Scenario BAU"]
    nodeF --> nodeH["High Growth Scenario"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Sustainable Policy Scenario"]
    nodeG --> nodeJ["Policy Recommendations"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Energy Mix Optimization"]
```

**Why this diagram is KTU-relevant:**
- It directly mirrors the **IEA World Energy Outlook (WEO)** three-scenario framework: *Stated Policies (STEPS), Announced Pledges (APS), and Net Zero by 2050 (NZE)*.
- Examiners love asking: *"Compare BAU and Sustainable scenarios"* — this flowchart provides the ready-made answer structure.

---

### 4.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — India vs. World (Visual Block)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INDIA["INDIA Profile"]
        nodeIN1["PCEC around 0.65 toe"]
        nodeIN2["EI around 0.20 toe per k USD"]
        nodeIN3["EE around 0.7 to 0.8"]
        nodeIN4["Coal share around 50 percent"]
    end
    subgraph WORLD["WORLD Average"]
        nodeW1["PCEC around 1.8 toe"]
        nodeW2["EI around 0.18 toe per k USD"]
        nodeW3["EE around 0.5 to 0.6"]
        nodeW4["Coal share around 27 percent"]
    end
    INDIA --> nodeCMP["Comparison: Lower per capita, similar intensity, coal-heavy, growth-phase economy"]
    WORLD --> nodeCMP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip for Drawing in Exam Hall:** Even if you cannot draw a full Mermaid-quality chart, sketch a simple two-column comparison table — it conveys the same insight and saves time.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define Energy Intensity and Energy Elasticity. Why is Energy Intensity a better indicator of energy efficiency than absolute consumption?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
* **Definition — Energy Intensity (1 mark):** Energy Intensity is the ratio of energy consumption to the economic output (GDP) of a region. It is expressed as **toe per million USD** or **MJ/USD**.
  $$EI = \frac{EC}{GDP}$$
* **Definition — Energy Elasticity (1 mark):** Energy Elasticity is the ratio of the percentage change in energy demand to the percentage change in GDP. It is dimensionless.
  $$EE = \frac{\%\Delta EC}{\%\Delta GDP}$$
* **Why EI is a better indicator (1 mark):** Absolute consumption ignores the size of the economy. A large economy with high absolute consumption may still be efficient if its GDP is proportionally higher. Energy Intensity normalizes consumption against economic output, enabling **fair comparison across countries and across time**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Series 2]**
**List any three factors that drive the energy needs of a growing economy with one-line explanations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per point):**
1. **Industrialization:** Expansion of manufacturing, steel, cement, and chemical sectors directly increases energy demand.
2. **Population & Urbanization:** More households and migration to cities raise residential and transport energy consumption per capita.
3. **Rising Affluence:** Higher disposable incomes lead to ownership of energy-using appliances and private vehicles.
   *(Optional 4th: Efficiency improvements — may *reduce* energy per unit output, modifying the demand trajectory.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **linkage between economic growth and energy demand**. Discuss the role of **energy intensity** and **energy elasticity** in characterizing this relationship. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A developing country had the following data:

| **Year** | **GDP (Billion USD)** | **Energy Consumption (Mtoe)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 2018 | 1500 | 600 |
| 2023 | 2100 | 780 |

Calculate: (i) Energy Intensity for both years, (ii) Percentage change in EI, (iii) Energy Elasticity, (iv) Comment on decoupling. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Economic growth and energy demand are tightly coupled. Energy is a *necessary input* for production, transport, and consumption. As an economy grows, it requires more energy, but the *rate* at which this happens is governed by structural and technological factors.

**Main Body (5 marks):**
* **Industrialization & Structural Change:** Movement from agriculture to manufacturing to services changes the energy mix. Manufacturing peaks energy demand; services reduce it.
* **Energy Intensity (EI):** $EI = EC / GDP$. A falling EI indicates that the economy is producing more output per unit of energy — a sign of **technological improvement and efficiency**.
* **Energy Elasticity (EE):** $EE = \%\Delta EC / \%\Delta GDP$. When **EE < 1**, energy grows slower than GDP → **relative decoupling**. When **EE → 0** or negative, we have **absolute decoupling** (sustainable growth).
* **Stages of Development:** Agrarian economies have low EE (subsistence use); industrializing economies have **EE > 1** (energy-hungry growth); mature service economies have **EE < 1**.
* **Policy Levers:** BEE Star Ratings, PAT Scheme, Renewable Purchase Obligations (RPO), and carbon pricing all aim to lower EE.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Understanding the EI–EE relationship is essential for sustainable planning. The goal of modern energy policy is to achieve **absolute decoupling** — economic growth without proportional (or any) increase in energy consumption.

---

**Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given Data:**
* 2018: GDP = 1500 B USD, EC = 600 Mtoe
* 2023: GDP = 2100 B USD, EC = 780 Mtoe

**(i) Energy Intensity for both years (2 marks):**

$$
EI_{2018} = \frac{600}{1500} = 0.40 \text{ Mtoe per Billion USD}
$$

$$
EI_{2023} = \frac{780}{2100} = 0.3714 \text{ Mtoe per Billion USD}
$$

**(ii) Percentage change in Energy Intensity (2 marks):**

$$
\%\Delta EI = \frac{0.3714 - 0.40}{0.40} \times 100 = \frac{-0.0286}{0.40} \times 100 = -7.14\%
$$

**(iii) Energy Elasticity (2 marks):**

First, compute the percentage changes:

$$
\%\Delta EC = \frac{780 - 600}{600} \times 100 = 30.0\%
$$

$$
\%\Delta GDP = \frac{2100 - 1500}{1500} \times 100 = 40.0\%
$$

Then:

$$
EE = \frac{30.0}{40.0} = 0.75
$$

**(iv) Comment on Decoupling (1 mark):**
Since **EE = 0.75 < 1**, the country exhibits **relative decoupling** — energy demand grows, but slower than GDP. The negative $\%\Delta EI$ (−7.14%) further confirms that the economy is becoming more energy-efficient over time. To achieve **absolute decoupling**, the country must drive EE below 0 or EI to zero growth while GDP continues to rise.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Series 1, Modified]**

**(a)** Discuss the **three pillars of energy policy** (energy security, energy equity, and environmental sustainability) in the context of a growing economy like India. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The total final energy consumption of a state in **2010** was **50 Mtoe**, distributed as: **Industry 40%, Transport 25%, Domestic 20%, Agriculture 15%**. In **2023**, the total consumption grew to **75 Mtoe** with the same percentage distribution. Compute: (i) The CAGR of energy consumption, (ii) The sectoral consumption in 2023, (iii) The Per Capita Energy Consumption if population grew from **60 million to 72 million** in the same period. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

The **Energy Trilemma** is a globally accepted framework for evaluating a nation's energy performance. The three pillars are:

1. **Energy Security (2 marks):** Refers to the *uninterrupted availability* of energy at affordable prices. For India, this means reducing import dependence (currently ~40% of crude oil, ~50% of natural gas are imported), diversifying the energy mix (renewables + nuclear + storage), and maintaining strategic petroleum reserves.

2. **Energy Equity (2 marks):** Refers to ensuring that *all sections of society have access* to modern, reliable, and affordable energy. India's goal of **24x7 Power for All**, *Saubhagya Scheme* (free electricity connections to poor households), and LPG expansion (*Ujjwala Yojana*) are steps in this direction. PCEC disparity (rural vs. urban) is a key equity metric.

3. **Environmental Sustainability (2 marks):** Refers to the *decoupling of growth from emissions* and the preservation of ecosystems. India's commitments at COP26 — **500 GW non-fossil capacity and net-zero by 2070** — are sustainability targets. Promoting renewables, EVs, green hydrogen, and PAT scheme for industry are policy instruments.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Balancing the trilemma is a dynamic optimization problem. Over-emphasizing security may slow the energy transition; over-emphasizing equity may strain finances; over-emphasizing sustainability may compromise near-term growth. India's NEP and *Panchamrit* framework attempt this balance.

---

**Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Given:** Initial EC = 50 Mtoe (2010), Final EC = 75 Mtoe (2023), $n = 13$ years.

**(i) CAGR of Energy Consumption (3 marks):**

$$
CAGR = \left(\frac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{1/n} - 1 = \left(\frac{75}{50}\right)^{1/13} - 1
$$

$$
CAGR = (1.5)^{1/13} - 1
$$

Using logarithm:

$$
\ln(1.5) = 0.4055, \quad \frac{0.4055}{13} = 0.03119
$$

$$
CAGR = e^{0.03119} - 1 = 1.0317 - 1 = 0.0317 = 3.17\%
$$

**(ii) Sectoral Consumption in 2023 (2 marks):**
Since distribution is unchanged at 50 Mtoe → 75 Mtoe, simply multiply:

$$
\text{Industry} = 0.40 \times 75 = 30.0 \text{ Mtoe}
$$

$$
\text{Transport} = 0.25 \times 75 = 18.75 \text{ Mtoe}
$$

$$
\text{Domestic} = 0.20 \times 75 = 15.0 \text{ Mtoe}
$$

$$
\text{Agriculture} = 0.15 \times 75 = 11.25 \text{ Mtoe}
$$

**(iii) Per Capita Energy Consumption (2 marks):**

For **2010:**

$$
PCEC_{2010} = \frac{50 \times 10^6 \text{ toe}}{60 \times 10^6} = 0.833 \text{ toe/person}
$$

For **2023:**

$$
PCEC_{2023} = \frac{75 \times 10^6 \text{ toe}}{72 \times 10^6} = 1.042 \text{ toe/person}
$$

**Inference:** PCEC grew by approximately **25%**, which is typical of a developing economy rising out of energy poverty. The growth is, however, slower than total consumption growth (50%), indicating that population growth partially diluted the per-capita rise.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap (–1 to –2 marks):** Many students write EI as "toe" without dividing by GDP. Always show both numerator and denominator units.
> 2. **Confusing EI and EE (–1 to –2 marks):** EI is a *level* (ratio of stocks/flows at a point in time); EE is a *change ratio* (dimensionless elasticity). Examiners frequently swap them as distractors.
> 3. **CAGR Exponent Mistake (–1 mark):** Forgetting the $+1$ in $(V_f/V_i)^{1/n} - 1$ and reporting only the base multiplier.
> 4. **Conversion Errors (–1 mark):** Using 42 GJ/toe instead of the exact **41.868 GJ/toe** or forgetting the MWh conversion. KTU accepts approximations but penalizes wrong order of magnitude.
> 5. **No Interpretation (–1 mark):** In elasticity/intensity problems, students compute correctly but skip the *comment on decoupling*. Always end with a one-line interpretation — it is mandatory for full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Energy Needs of Growing Economy"**

- **Energy Intensity (EI):** $EC / GDP$ — *level* metric — lower is better.
- **Energy Elasticity (EE):** $\%\Delta EC / \%\Delta GDP$ — *change* metric — EE < 1 implies relative decoupling.
- **Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC):** $EC / Population$ — key equity indicator.
- **Energy Productivity (EP):** Inverse of EI — $GDP / EC$ — higher is better.
- **Three Pillars of Energy Policy:** **Security, Equity, Sustainability** (the "Energy Trilemma").
- **Key Drivers of Energy Demand:** Industrialization, Population Growth, Urbanization, Rising Affluence, Technology Penetration.
- **Conversion Constants to Memorize:** **1 toe = 41.868 GJ = 11.63 MWh = 42 GJ (approx)**.
- **Decoupling Types:** *Relative* (EE < 1, EI falls slowly) vs. *Absolute* (EE ≤ 0, EI falls even as GDP rises).
- **Forecasting Models:** Exponential growth $E_t = E_0(1+r)^t$ and CAGR are most common.
- **Sectoral Mix Norms for India (approx):** Industry ~40%, Transport ~25%, Domestic ~20%, Agriculture ~15% (rounded).
- **India's Strategic Targets:** 500 GW non-fossil by 2030, Net-Zero by 2070, 50% renewable share in energy mix.
- **IEA Scenarios:** STEPS (Stated Policies), APS (Announced Pledges), NZE 2050 (Net Zero Emissions).
- **PAT Scheme:** India's flagship industrial energy efficiency program (covers 13 energy-intensive sectors).
- **Common Exam Definitions:** "Energy security," "Decoupling," "Energy intensity vs. Energy elasticity," "Sectoral energy demand."
- **Always quote units** in the final answer to avoid losing 1 mark.
- **Always end numericals with a one-line interpretation** — examiners look for it explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Intensity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Energy Intensity # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Energy Intensity (EI)** is a standardized performance metric used globally in energy engineering, energy economics, and industrial energy management to quantify how much energy is required to produce one unit of useful output — whether that output is a tonne of steel, a square metre of a building, a unit of GDP, or a kilogram of product. Mathematically expressed as: $$ \text{Energy Intensity (EI)} \;=\; \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumption}}{\text{Activity / Output Metric}} $$ In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Energy Intensity is broadly classified into **three reference domains**: | Domain | Output Metric | Common Units | KTU Context | |---|---|---|---| | **National / Macroeconomic** | GDP (Gross Domestic Product) | Toe / Crore ₹ ; MJ / $ | Policy & Planning module | | **Industrial / Process** | Unit of physical product | kWh / tonne ; GJ / kg | Industrial Energy Audit | | **Building / Facility** | Built-up area | kWh / m² / year ; kWh / sqft / year | Energy Efficient Buildings | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Primary Energy Intensity (PEI)** uses *primary energy* (raw fuel input at the source — coal, crude oil, natural gas) in the numerator. > **Final Energy Intensity (FEI)** uses *final energy* (delivered energy at the consumer end — electricity, diesel, LPG). > PEI is always greater than or equal to FEI because of conversion losses at power plants. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Conversion Constant (must be memorized):** > **1 Tonne of Oil Equivalent (Toe) = 41.868 GJ = 11.63 MWh = 1,162 m³ of natural gas.** > This is a high-yield KTU constant — examiners frequently test it as a 2-mark or 3-mark question. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you drive two cars for the same 500 km journey: - **Car A** burns **50 litres** of petrol. - **Car B** burns **30 litres** of petrol. To compare them fairly, you cannot just say "Car A used 50 litres and Car B used 30 litres" — because what really matters is **how much fuel was used per kilometre travelled**. - Car A → 50 / 500 = **0.10 L/km** - Car B → 30 / 500 = **0.06 L/km** That **litres per kilometre** value is exactly what *Energy Intensity* is — it is the **"litres per km"** of the energy world. Instead of comparing raw fuel bills (which depend on the size of the company, the size of the nation, the size of the building), we compare the *normalized* fuel use per unit of useful work or output. > [!TIP] > **Why normalize?** A large steel plant and a small bakery cannot be compared using raw kWh. But once you divide by tonnes of steel produced or kg of bread baked, a fair "energy efficiency ranking" becomes possible. This is the heart of benchmarking. ## 1.3 Why Energy Intensity Matters in KTU Context The KTU Energy Management syllabus uses Energy Intensity as the **anchor metric** for almost every module that follows: - It is the **basis for benchmarking** (Module 2). - It is the **starting point of every energy audit** (Module 3). - It is the **yardstick for Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)** (Module 4). - It defines the **baseline** against which savings are measured (Module 5). In short: **no EI number → no energy audit → no savings calculation**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Energy Intensity The metric exists for three engineering reasons: 1. **Comparability** — a chemical plant in Kochi and a chemical plant in Mumbai cannot be fairly compared using raw MWh, but they can be compared using kWh per tonne of product. 2. **Trackability** — by plotting EI over years, an industry can see whether its energy efficiency is improving (EI decreasing) or worsening (EI increasing). 3. **Targetability** — governments and ISO 50001 auditors set *EI reduction targets* (e.g., "reduce EI by 10% over 3 years"). ## 2.2 Decomposition of Energy Intensity — The "How" Energy Intensity is mathematically related to two deeper structural factors: $$ \text{EI} \;=\; \frac{\text{Energy Consumption}}{\text{Output}} \;=\; \underbrace{\frac{\text{Energy Consumption}}{\text{Value Added}}}_{\text{Energy Efficiency Component}} \;\times\; \underbrace{\frac{\text{Value Added}}{\text{Output}}}_{\text{Structural / Economic Component}} $$ - **Energy Efficiency Component** reflects pure technological performance (boiler efficiency, motor efficiency, insulation). - **Structural Component** reflects the *economic mix* — a country shifting from heavy industry toward IT services will naturally see EI drop, even if individual factories remain inefficient. > [!NOTE] > This two-factor decomposition is a **classic 14-mark KTU question**. The examiner usually asks: *"Explain the two-factor decomposition of energy intensity and discuss its implications for India."* ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **high-yield formula bible** for Energy Intensity questions. Memorize every row. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | When to Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Basic Energy Intensity** | $EI = E / Q$ | varies | Standard definition | | 2 | **National PEI** | $PEI = E_{primary} / GDP$ | Toe / Crore ₹ | Country-level comparison | | 3 | **National FEI** | $FEI = E_{final} / GDP$ | Toe / Crore ₹ | Country-level comparison | | 4 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = E_{util} / M_{prod}$ | kWh / tonne | Industrial benchmarking | | 5 | **Building EI** | $EI_{bldg} = E_{annual} / A_{floor}$ | kWh / m² / year | ECBC / green buildings | | 6 | **% EI Reduction** | $\Delta EI \,(\%) = \dfrac{EI_{old} - EI_{new}}{EI_{old}} \times 100$ | % | Savings quantification | | 7 | **Energy Savings (Absolute)** | $\Delta E = (EI_{old} - EI_{new}) \times Q_{new}$ | kWh, GJ | Money saved calculation | | 8 | **Toe Conversion** | $1 \text{ Toe} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh}$ | constant | Unit conversion | | 9 | **Two-Factor Decomposition** | $EI = (E/VA) \times (VA/Q)$ | dimensionless | Policy analysis | | 10 | **Cumulative EI (Multi-Year)** | $EI_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} Q_i}$ | mixed | Long-term averaging | > [!WARNING] > **Pitfall:** Always keep the numerator and denominator in **dimensionally consistent time frames**. Annual kWh divided by *daily* production gives a meaningless number. The unit of time in numerator and denominator **must match** (or cancel cleanly). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Sector | Use of Energy Intensity | |---|---| | **Steel industry** | GJ / tonne of crude steel — used to compare Indian vs global benchmarks | | **Cement industry** | kWh / tonne of clinker — PAT (Perform-Achieve-Trade) scheme baseline | | **Commercial buildings** | kWh / m² / year — ECBC star rating | | **National policy** | Toe / Crore ₹ GDP — NITI Aayog energy efficiency reports | | **Transport** | MJ / passenger-km — fuel efficiency rating | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** In Part A (3-mark) questions, examiners often ask: *"Give two examples of energy intensity with their units."* Always cite **one industrial example** AND **one national example** for full marks. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Examples ## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Energy Savings and EI Reduction We derive the master equation linking energy savings to EI reduction — this is the **single most important equation** in KTU Energy Management. **Starting Point — definition of EI:** $$ EI_{old} = \frac{E_{old}}{Q_{old}} $$ $$ EI_{new} = \frac{E_{new}}{Q_{new}} $$ **Step 1:** Express the new energy consumption in terms of new EI and new output: $$ E_{new} = EI_{new} \times Q_{new} $$ **Step 2:** Express the *hypothetical* energy consumption if the plant had continued with the *old* (baseline) intensity but at the *new* higher production: $$ E_{baseline\_at\_new\_output} = EI_{old} \times Q_{new} $$ **Step 3:** The **absolute energy saving** is the difference between what would have been used and what was actually used: $$ \Delta E_{saved} = E_{baseline\_at\_new\_output} - E_{new} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute Step 1 and Step 2: $$ \Delta E_{saved} = (EI_{old} \times Q_{new}) - (EI_{new} \times Q_{new}) $$ **Step 5:** Factor out the common term $Q_{new}$: $$ \Delta E_{saved} = Q_{new} \times (EI_{old} - EI_{new}) $$ **Step 6:** Divide by the original baseline energy to express as a fraction: $$ \text{Fractional Savings} = \frac{\Delta E_{saved}}{EI_{old} \times Q_{new}} = \frac{EI_{old} - EI_{new}}{EI_{old}} $$ **Step 7:** Multiply by 100 to obtain the percentage: $$ \boxed{\;\text{\% Energy Savings} = \left(\dfrac{EI_{old} - EI_{new}}{EI_{old}}\right) \times 100\;} $$ This is the **canonical savings equation** that examiners reward with full marks. ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Industrial Specific Energy Consumption **Problem:** A chemical plant consumes **8,500 MWh** of electricity and **2,10,000 m³** of natural gas in one year. Annual production is **45,000 tonnes** of finished product. The calorific value of natural gas is **35.17 MJ/m³**. Calculate the Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in GJ/tonne. **Step 1 — Convert electricity to GJ:** $$ E_{elec} = 8{,}500 \text{ MWh} = 8{,}500 \times 3.6 \text{ GJ} = 30{,}600 \text{ GJ} $$ **Step 2 — Convert natural gas to GJ:** $$ E_{gas} = 2{,}10{,}000 \text{ m}^3 \times 35.17 \text{ MJ/m}^3 = 7.3857 \times 10^6 \text{ MJ} = 7{,}385.7 \text{ GJ} $$ **Step 3 — Total energy consumption:** $$ E_{total} = 30{,}600 + 7{,}385.7 = 37{,}985.7 \text{ GJ} $$ **Step 4 — Specific Energy Consumption:** $$ SEC = \frac{E_{total}}{Q} = \frac{37{,}985.7 \text{ GJ}}{45{,}000 \text{ tonnes}} $$ $$ \boxed{SEC = 0.8441 \text{ GJ/tonne} \approx 0.844 \text{ GJ/tonne}} $$ > [!TIP] > KTU boards often expect answers to **3 significant figures**. Always round appropriately. ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Energy Savings from EI Reduction **Problem:** A foundry's baseline specific energy consumption is **2.40 GJ/tonne**. Through waste-heat recovery and motor upgrades, the new SEC becomes **2.04 GJ/tonne**. Next year's expected production is **12,000 tonnes**. Calculate: 1. Percentage reduction in EI. 2. Absolute energy saved per year. 3. Annual CO₂ avoided (assuming emission factor = **0.082 tCO₂/GJ**). **Step 1 — % Reduction in EI:** $$ \Delta EI \,(\%) = \frac{2.40 - 2.04}{2.40} \times 100 = \frac{0.36}{2.40} \times 100 = 15\,\% $$ **Step 2 — Absolute energy saved:** $$ \Delta E = (EI_{old} - EI_{new}) \times Q_{new} = (2.40 - 2.04) \times 12{,}000 $$ $$ \Delta E = 0.36 \times 12{,}000 = 4{,}320 \text{ GJ/year} $$ **Step 3 — CO₂ avoided annually:** $$ \text{CO}_2 \text{ avoided} = 4{,}320 \times 0.082 = 354.24 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{year} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Energy Saved} = 4{,}320 \text{ GJ/yr}; \quad \text{CO}_2 \text{ avoided} = 354.24 \text{ tCO}_2/\text{yr}} $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which EI Variant | Scenario | Correct EI to Use | Why | |---|---|---| | Comparing two countries' energy efficiency | **PEI** (Toe / $ GDP) | Accounts for conversion losses; fair macro comparison | | Auditing a single factory | **SEC** (GJ / tonne) | Output is physical, easy to measure | | Rating a commercial building | **Building EI** (kWh / m² / yr) | Standard for ECBC / LEED | | Year-on-year tracking inside a plant | **Plant EI** (kWh / tonne) | Internal consistency, no GDP involved | | Setting a national PAT target | **SEC** at sector level | Govt. uses tonnes of product, not GDP | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Intensity Decomposition The following Mermaid block diagram shows the structural decomposition of Energy Intensity into its influencing sub-systems. The diagram is rendered as a *Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow* (per the system protocol, since physical schematic drawing is not feasible in Mermaid). ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph inputA[Energy Consumption Domain] nodeA1[Primary Energy
Coal Crude Oil Gas] nodeA2[Final Energy
Electricity Diesel LPG Steam] nodeA3[Useful Energy
Mechanical Work Heat Light] end subgraph inputB[Output Domain] nodeB1[Macroeconomic Output
GDP in Crore Rupees] nodeB2[Physical Output
Tonnes Kg Litres Units] nodeB3[Built-up Area
Square Metres Square Feet] end nodeC1[Primary Energy Intensity
Toe per Crore GDP] nodeC2[Specific Energy Consumption
GJ per Tonne or kWh per kg] nodeC3[Building Energy Intensity
kWh per SqM per Year] nodeD[Energy Intensity EI
Single Normalized Performance Indicator] nodeA1 --> nodeC1 nodeB1 --> nodeC1 nodeC1 --> nodeD nodeA3 --> nodeC2 nodeB2 --> nodeC2 nodeC2 --> nodeD nodeA2 --> nodeC3 nodeB3 --> nodeC3 nodeC3 --> nodeD nodeE[Benchmarking
Cross Industry Comparison] nodeF[Target Setting
ISO 50001 PAT Scheme] nodeG[Savings Quantification
Delta E equals Q new times Delta EI] nodeD --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeG ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Energy Intensity Reduction Workflow This flowchart shows how the EI metric flows through a typical industrial energy management cycle. ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA[Step 1
Measure Baseline Energy Consumption] --> stepB[Step 2
Measure Output Quantity] stepB --> stepC[Step 3
Compute Baseline EI equals E divided by Q] stepC --> stepD[Step 4
Identify Energy Conservation Measures ECM] stepD --> stepE[Step 5
Implement ECM and Re-measure] stepE --> stepF[Step 6
Compute New EI] stepF --> stepG[Step 7
Calculate Percent Reduction in EI] stepG --> stepH[Step 8
Quantify Absolute Energy and Cost Savings] stepH --> stepI[Step 9
Verify Against Target and Report] stepI --> stepJ[Step 10
Continuous Improvement Loop Back to Step 4] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Arrows go **left → right**. Each box is a sequential step. Step 10 loops back to Step 4 because energy management is a **continuous (PDCA) cycle**, not a one-time project. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A]** **Define Energy Intensity. Differentiate between Primary Energy Intensity and Final Energy Intensity with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Energy Intensity is defined as the ratio of energy consumed to a unit of output, activity, or service delivered. It is expressed as $EI = E / Q$, where $E$ is the energy consumed and $Q$ is the output metric. **Primary Energy Intensity (1 mark):** PEI uses **primary energy** (energy at the source before conversion) as the numerator. *Example:* India's PEI ≈ 0.22 Toe per thousand ₹ of GDP (illustrative). **Final Energy Intensity (1 mark):** FEI uses **final energy** (energy delivered to the end-user — electricity, diesel, etc.) as the numerator. *Example:* Commercial building FEI = 180 kWh/m²/year. > [!TIP] > Always state **the unit** alongside the example — this is the difference between 2 marks and 3 marks in KTU valuation. --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1]** **State the standard conversion: 1 Tonne of Oil Equivalent. Why is this constant important in energy management calculations?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Conversion (2 marks):** $$ 1 \text{ Toe} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh} = 1{,}162 \text{ m}^3 \text{ of natural gas} $$ **Importance (1 mark):** It allows uniform comparison across **different energy carriers** (coal, oil, gas, electricity). Without this standard, comparing the energy content of 1 tonne of coal vs 1000 m³ of natural gas vs 5000 kWh of electricity would be impossible — they are measured in different units. Toe acts as the **universal translator** of energy units. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Choice-1]** **(a)** Explain the concept of Energy Intensity. Discuss its significance as a benchmarking tool in industrial energy management. List **four** different units of energy intensity used in different sectors. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A textile mill consumed **6,200 MWh** of electricity and **180 tonnes** of furnace oil last year. The calorific value of furnace oil is **40.4 GJ/tonne**. The annual production was **15,000 tonnes** of fabric. Calculate: (i) Total energy consumption in GJ. (ii) Specific Energy Consumption in GJ/tonne. (iii) If a target SEC of **0.55 GJ/tonne** is fixed for the next year at the same production level, calculate the absolute energy savings and percentage reduction in EI. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question A: #### Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks) **Definition (2 marks):** Energy Intensity is a normalized metric defined as the amount of energy consumed per unit of output, service, or activity. It is given by $EI = E / Q$, where $E$ = energy consumption and $Q$ = output. It is used to compare energy performance across entities of different sizes. **Significance as benchmarking tool (3 marks):** 1. **Cross-industry comparability:** A large steel plant and a small foundry can be compared on a fair footing using GJ/tonne, even though their absolute energy use differs by orders of magnitude. 2. **Year-on-year tracking:** A single industry can plot its EI over years to detect deterioration (EI rising) or improvement (EI falling) in energy performance. 3. **Policy & target setting:** Government schemes like PAT (Perform-Achieve-Trade) use industry-specific EI targets to allocate energy-saving obligations. 4. **Investment justification:** A reduction in EI from baseline justifies the capital cost of energy-efficient equipment via the M&V (Measurement & Verification) protocol. 5. **Compliance with ISO 50001:** Energy Intensity is the **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** required for ISO 50001 EnMS certification. **Four units in different sectors (2 marks):** | Sector | Energy Intensity Unit | |---|---| | National economy | Toe per Crore ₹ of GDP | | Steel industry | GJ per tonne of crude steel | | Cement industry | kWh per tonne of clinker | | Commercial buildings | kWh per m² per year | --- #### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks) **(i) Total energy consumption in GJ (3 marks):** Electricity: $$ E_{elec} = 6{,}200 \text{ MWh} \times 3.6 \text{ GJ/MWh} = 22{,}320 \text{ GJ} $$ Furnace oil: $$ E_{oil} = 180 \text{ tonnes} \times 40.4 \text{ GJ/tonne} = 7{,}272 \text{ GJ} $$ Total: $$ E_{total} = 22{,}320 + 7{,}272 = 29{,}592 \text{ GJ} $$ **(ii) SEC in GJ/tonne (2 marks):** $$ SEC = \frac{29{,}592 \text{ GJ}}{15{,}000 \text{ tonnes}} = \boxed{1.9728 \approx 1.973 \text{ GJ/tonne}} $$ **(iii) Energy savings and % reduction (2 marks):** Energy used at target SEC: $$ E_{target} = 0.55 \times 15{,}000 = 8{,}250 \text{ GJ} $$ Energy saved: $$ \Delta E = 29{,}592 - 8{,}250 = \boxed{21{,}342 \text{ GJ/year}} $$ % Reduction in EI: $$ \Delta EI\,(\%) = \frac{1.973 - 0.55}{1.973} \times 100 = 72.12\,\% $$ --- ### Question B: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Choice-2 (Alternative)]** **(a)** Explain the **two-factor decomposition** of energy intensity at the national level. What does each factor represent, and why is this decomposition important for a developing country like India? **(7 marks)** **(b)** The national energy consumption of a country is **800 MToe** (million tonnes of oil equivalent) of primary energy and **620 MToe** of final energy. The country's GDP is **₹ 250 lakh crore**. Calculate: (i) Primary Energy Intensity in Toe / Crore ₹. (ii) Final Energy Intensity in Toe / Crore ₹. (iii) Comment on which metric is more appropriate for international comparison and why. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution — Question B: #### Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks) **Two-factor decomposition (4 marks):** At the national level, energy intensity can be decomposed as: $$ EI_{national} = \frac{E}{Q} = \left(\frac{E}{VA}\right) \times \left(\frac{VA}{Q}\right) $$ where: - **$E / VA$** = Energy used per unit of *Value Added*. This factor represents the **pure technological energy efficiency** of the economy — better machines, better processes, better insulation. - **$VA / Q$** = Value Added per unit of *physical Output*. This factor represents the **economic structure** — a shift from energy-intensive heavy industry (steel, cement) toward service-sector activities (IT, finance) reduces this factor. **Importance for India (3 marks):** 1. India is structurally a **mixed economy** with a large manufacturing base; hence both factors matter. 2. A declining $EI$ could mean either technological improvement OR a structural shift — the two-factor decomposition helps policymakers identify *which lever* to pull. 3. It prevents **misleading conclusions** — if EI falls simply because the service sector grew, the manufacturing sector may still be highly inefficient and need focused attention. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)** **Given:** $E_{primary} = 800$ MToe, $E_{final} = 620$ MToe, GDP = ₹ 250 lakh crore = ₹ 250 × 10⁷ × 10⁵ = ₹ 2.5 × 10¹⁵. **(i) Primary Energy Intensity (2 marks):** $$ PEI = \frac{800 \times 10^6 \text{ Toe}}{2.5 \times 10^{15} \text{ ₹}} = 3.2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ Toe/₹} $$ Convert to **Toe / Crore ₹** (1 Crore = 10⁷ ₹): $$ PEI = 3.2 \times 10^{-7} \times 10^{7} = \boxed{3.2 \text{ Toe/Crore ₹}} $$ **(ii) Final Energy Intensity (2 marks):** $$ FEI = \frac{620 \times 10^6}{2.5 \times 10^{15}} \times 10^{7} = 2.48 \text{ Toe/Crore ₹} $$ $$ \boxed{FEI = 2.48 \text{ Toe/Crore ₹}} $$ **(iii) Discussion (3 marks):** **Primary Energy Intensity (PEI)** is more appropriate for international comparison because: - It captures the **total upstream energy cost** of an economy, including conversion losses at thermal power plants. - Countries with **cleaner grids** (high hydro / nuclear / renewable share) will have lower PEI for the same final energy use — this hidden efficiency is visible only in PEI. - FEI understates true energy burden in countries reliant on coal-based power (like India); PEI gives the honest picture. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit mismatch in GDP:** Writing GDP as ₹ 250 lakh crore and forgetting to convert to a consistent unit. Always reduce to **Crore ₹** first: ₹ 250 lakh crore = 250 × 1 lakh × 1 crore — wait, the cleanest path is: 1 lakh crore = 10⁵ × 10⁷ = 10¹². So 250 lakh crore = 250 × 10¹² = 2.5 × 10¹⁴ ₹. Then convert to Crore ₹ (÷ 10⁷) = 2.5 × 10⁷ Crore ₹. Always show this conversion. > 2. **Forgetting to specify "per unit time"** in industrial SEC — KTU deducts 0.5 mark if you omit the time dimension explicitly. > 3. **Mixing up PEI and FEI definitions** — students often use them interchangeably; they are NOT the same. > 4. **Wrong conversion of MWh to GJ** — use 1 MWh = 3.6 GJ (not 3.6 kJ, not 36 GJ). > 5. **Not stating units in the final boxed answer** — KTU expects the answer with units explicitly mentioned in the box. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Energy Intensity = Energy Consumption ÷ Output Metric** — the *single most important equation* in this module. - [x] **1 Toe = 41.868 GJ = 11.63 MWh = 1,162 m³ of natural gas** — memorize this constant. - [x] **Primary Energy Intensity (PEI)** uses raw fuel input; **Final Energy Intensity (FEI)** uses delivered energy. PEI ≥ FEI always. - [x] **Three domains of EI:** National (Toe/Crore ₹), Industrial (GJ/tonne), Building (kWh/m²/yr). - [x] **% EI Reduction = [(EI_old − EI_new) / EI_old] × 100** — examiner's favourite formula. - [x] **Absolute Energy Savings = (EI_old − EI_new) × Q_new** — note it uses *new* production, not old. - [x] **Two-factor decomposition:** $EI = (E/VA) \times (VA/Q)$ — separates technological efficiency from economic structure. - [x] **Always state the units** in the final answer — KTU deducts marks for unit-less answers. - [x] **Building EI** is benchmarked against ECBC norms: 90–120 kWh/m²/year for typical offices. - [x] **PAT scheme baseline:** cement SEC ≈ 0.733 GJ/tonne; steel SEC ≈ 6.0 GJ/tonne (indicative). - [x] **PDCA cycle:** Baseline EI → ECM → New EI → Compare → Loop back. Energy management is continuous, never one-time. - [x] **1 MWh = 3.6 GJ** — used in every industrial numerical. - [x] **Three significant figures** is the KTU standard rounding convention for numerical answers.

topics.title: Long Term Energy Scenario
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Long Term Energy Scenario

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long Term Energy Scenario (LTES)** is a **systematic, quantitative, and policy-oriented projection** of future energy demand, supply, technology mix, and associated environmental impacts over a planning horizon typically ranging from **20 to 50 years** (sometimes up to 100 years for net-zero pathways). It is built using integrated assessment frameworks such as **LEAP (Long-range Energy Alternatives Planning)**, **MARKAL**, and **MESSAGE** models to guide national energy security, decarbonization, and sustainable development strategies.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the long-term energy scenario is treated as the **strategic backbone** of any energy management programme because it answers four foundational questions:

1. How much energy will a region/country/nation need by **2050 / 2070**?
2. Where will that energy come from (fuel mix, renewables, imports)?
3. What will be the **carbon footprint** and environmental cost?
4. What infrastructure, policy, and investment frameworks must be in place **today** to avoid future energy crises?

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "House Building" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of national energy planning like building a 50-storey skyscraper.**
> You cannot start pouring concrete without first asking: *"How many people will live here in 2075? How much water, electricity, and ventilation will they need? Where will the supply come from?"* The **short-term energy plan** is like laying bricks this week. The **long-term energy scenario** is the **architectural blueprint for the next half-century**. If the blueprint is wrong, no amount of weekly brick-laying can save the building.

This is exactly why bodies like the **International Energy Agency (IEA)**, **Ministry of Power (India)**, and **NITI Aayog** invest heavily in long-term scenario modelling — they are drawing the blueprint for the energy future.

## 1.3 Classification of Energy Planning Horizons

| Planning Horizon | Time Frame | Primary Focus | Typical Tools |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Short Term** | 0 – 2 years | Operational dispatch, billing, day-ahead markets | Load forecasting, unit commitment |
| **Medium Term** | 2 – 10 years | Capacity addition, fuel contracts, tariffs | Optimization models, regression |
| **Long Term** | **20 – 50 years** | **Energy security, decarbonization, energy independence** | **LEAP, MARKAL, MESSAGE, AIM** |
| **Strategic / Vision** | 50 – 100 years | Net-zero pathways, hydrogen economy, climate goals | Integrated Assessment Models (IAMs) |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in Long-Term Scenarios

The following metrics are universally referenced in KTU-level long-term scenario analysis (memorise the symbols):

- **TPES** — Total Primary Energy Supply (in **Mtoe** or **EJ**)
- **PEC** — Per Capita Energy Consumption = **TPES / Population**
- **EI** — Energy Intensity = **TPES / GDP** (toe per million \$)
- **CI** — Carbon Intensity = **CO₂ Emissions / TPES**
- **R/P** — Reserve to Production Ratio (years of fossil fuel left)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Conversion Constant:**
> **1 Mtoe (Million tonnes of oil equivalent) = 41.868 GJ = 0.041868 EJ = 11.63 TWh**
> This constant is the bridge between energy units in every KTU numerical problem on energy scenarios.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Pillars of a Long-Term Energy Scenario

A robust LTES rests on **five analytical pillars**. Each pillar feeds data into the next, forming an iterative feedback loop.

### Pillar 1 — Demand-Side Drivers
- Population growth (UN demographic projections)
- GDP growth rate (compound annual growth rate — CAGR)
- Urbanization rate
- Industrialization index (manufacturing value-added)
- Technology penetration (EV adoption, heat pumps, smart grids)

### Pillar 2 — Supply-Side Resource Base
- Fossil fuel reserves (coal, oil, gas) and **R/P ratios**
- Renewable potential (solar irradiance, wind speed, hydro head)
- Nuclear capacity roadmap
- Imports / exports (energy independence index)
- Emerging fuels — **green hydrogen**, biofuels, synthetic methane

### Pillar 3 — Technology & Cost Learning Curves
- Learning rate concept: cost drops by a fixed percentage for every doubling of cumulative installed capacity.
- Capital cost of solar PV: dropped from ~\$76/W (1976) to ~\$0.30/W (2024).
- LCOE (Levelized Cost of Energy) benchmarking.

### Pillar 4 — Environmental Constraints
- Carbon budget allocations
- Air quality targets (PM₂.₅, SOₓ, NOₓ)
- Water usage by thermal plants
- Land footprint of renewables

### Pillar 5 — Policy & Regulatory Architecture
- National Energy Policy (NEP)
- Nationally Determined Contributions (NDC) under the Paris Agreement
- Carbon pricing mechanisms
- RPO (Renewable Purchase Obligation)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **single most important reference** for solving any LTES numerical in the KTU 2024 Scheme examination. Every symbol here is KTU-board approved.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Per Capita Energy Consumption | $\text{PEC} = \dfrac{\text{TPES}}{\text{Population}}$ | toe / person | Measures standard of living proxy |
| 2 | Energy Intensity | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{\text{TPES}}{\text{GDP}}$ | toe / million \$ | Tracks economic efficiency of energy use |
| 3 | Carbon Intensity | $\text{CI} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ emissions}}{\text{TPES}}$ | tCO₂ / toe | Decarbonization progress indicator |
| 4 | Compound Annual Growth Rate | $\text{CAGR} = \left( \dfrac{V_f}{V_i} \right)^{\tfrac{1}{n}} - 1$ | dimensionless (per year) | Used to project future demand |
| 5 | Reserve-to-Production Ratio | $\text{R/P} = \dfrac{\text{Proved Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}$ | years | Fossil fuel security indicator |
| 6 | Energy Mix Share | $S_f = \dfrac{E_f}{\text{TPES}} \times 100$ | % | Share of fuel *f* in primary mix |
| 7 | Decoupling Index | $\text{DI} = 1 - \dfrac{\%\Delta \text{TPES}}{\%\Delta \text{GDP}}$ | dimensionless | Indicates relative / absolute decoupling |
| 8 | Cumulative CO₂ (Carbon Budget) | $\text{CB} = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \text{Annual CO}_2(t)$ | GtCO₂ | Total allowable emissions |
| 9 | Learning Curve Cost | $C_x = C_0 \left( \dfrac{X_x}{X_0} \right)^{\log_2(1-LR)}$ | \$/unit | Models technology cost decline |
| 10 | Mtoe Conversion | $1\ \text{Mtoe} = 41.868\ \text{GJ}$ | constant | Universal energy unit bridge |

## 2.3 Demand Forecasting Methodologies (Three Pillars of LTES)

### (a) Econometric / Macroeconomic Method
Projects energy demand as a function of GDP, population, and price elasticity. Used for **national-scale long-term forecasts**.

### (b) End-Use / Bottom-Up Method
Disaggregates demand by sector (residential, commercial, industrial, transport, agriculture) and end-use (lighting, HVAC, motors). Used in the **LEAP** framework.

### (c) Technological / Engineering Method
Bottom-up modelling of every technology's efficiency improvement and market penetration. Used in **MARKAL** and **MESSAGE** models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** In KTU 14-mark questions, if you are asked *"Discuss long-term energy scenario forecasting methods"*, always present all three methods with at least one formula and one limitation each. This guarantees full marks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The long-term energy scenario is the **decision-making compass** for:

- **Power utilities** → Planning new thermal, nuclear, hydro, and renewable capacities (e.g., CEA's National Electricity Plan).
- **Automotive industry** → Betting billions on EVs vs. ICE vs. hydrogen.
- **Urban planners** → Designing district cooling, EV charging, and microgrids.
- **Investors & banks** → Risk-pricing fossil assets under stranded-asset scenarios.
- **Climate negotiators** → Computing NDCs and net-zero roadmaps.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Future Energy Demand via CAGR

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
India's TPES in 2020 was **300 Mtoe**. Project the TPES for **2050** assuming a CAGR of **3.5 % per annum**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Transition

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**
$V_i = 300\ \text{Mtoe}$ (initial TPES in 2020)
$V_f = ?$ (final TPES in 2050)
$n = 2050 - 2020 = 30\ \text{years}$
$\text{CAGR} = r = 3.5\% = 0.035$

**Step 2 — Recall the discrete compounding relation.**

$$
V_f = V_i (1 + r)^n
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
V_f = 300 \times (1 + 0.035)^{30}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the compound factor.**

$$
(1.035)^{30} \approx 2.8068
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the final answer.**

$$
V_f = 300 \times 2.8068 \approx 842.05\ \text{Mtoe}
$$

**Conclusion:** India's TPES in 2050 is projected to be approximately **842 Mtoe** under a 3.5 % CAGR scenario — nearly **2.8 times** the 2020 value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Check:** $842.05\ \text{Mtoe} \times 41.868\ \text{GJ/toe} \approx 35{,}254\ \text{GJ}$, i.e., $\approx 35.25\ \text{EJ}$. This is consistent with IEA's STEPS projection range for India in 2050.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Per Capita Energy Consumption and Standard of Living

**Problem Statement:**
A developing nation had TPES = **120 Mtoe** in the year 2023 and a population of **1.4 billion**. By 2040, TPES is projected to reach **210 Mtoe** with population stabilizing at **1.55 billion**. Calculate the **change in per capita energy consumption** and comment on its meaning.

### Step-by-Step Logical Transition

**Step 1 — Write the PEC formula.**

$$
\text{PEC} = \frac{\text{TPES}}{\text{Population}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute PEC for 2023.**

$$
\text{PEC}_{2023} = \frac{120 \times 10^6\ \text{toe}}{1.4 \times 10^9} \approx 0.0857\ \text{toe/person}
$$

Converting to more readable units:

$$
0.0857\ \text{toe/person} \times 11.63\ \text{MWh/toe} \approx 0.997\ \text{MWh/person/year}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute PEC for 2040.**

$$
\text{PEC}_{2040} = \frac{210 \times 10^6\ \text{toe}}{1.55 \times 10^9} \approx 0.1355\ \text{toe/person}
$$

$$
0.1355\ \text{toe/person} \times 11.63\ \text{MWh/toe} \approx 1.575\ \text{MWh/person/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the absolute and percentage change.**

$$
\Delta\text{PEC} = 0.1355 - 0.0857 = 0.0498\ \text{toe/person}
$$

$$
\%\Delta\text{PEC} = \frac{0.0498}{0.0857} \times 100 \approx 58.1\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Per capita energy consumption rises by **~58 %** over 17 years — a strong signal of **rising living standards, increased appliance penetration, and economic growth**. For comparison, the OECD average PEC is **~4 to 5 toe/person**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Decoupling Index between GDP and Energy

**Problem Statement:**
Over a decade, a country's GDP grew by **45 %** while its TPES grew by **12 %**. Compute the Decoupling Index and classify it.

### Step-by-Step Logical Transition
