**Step 1 — Recall the decoupling formula.**

$$
\text{DI} = 1 - \frac{\%\Delta\text{TPES}}{\%\Delta\text{GDP}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the percentage changes.**

$$
\text{DI} = 1 - \frac{12}{45} = 1 - 0.2667 \approx 0.7333
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

$$
\text{DI} > 0 \Rightarrow \text{Decoupling (Relative)}
$$

Since energy grew slower than GDP, the country has **achieved relative decoupling**, meaning it is producing more economic value per unit of energy. Strong decoupling would require energy to actually **decline** while GDP grows.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reserve-to-Production Ratio (R/P) for Coal Security

**Problem Statement:**
A nation has proved coal reserves of **98 billion tonnes** and produces **700 million tonnes** annually. Compute the R/P ratio.

### Step-by-Step Logical Transition

**Step 1 — State the formula.**

$$
\text{R/P} = \frac{\text{Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
\text{R/P} = \frac{98 \times 10^9}{700 \times 10^6} = 140\ \text{years}
$$

**Conclusion:** Theoretically, the nation has **140 years of coal left**. However, the KTU caveat is that this assumes constant production — in reality, production scales with rising demand, so the practical horizon is much shorter.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Carbon Budget for 1.5 °C Pathway

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024-style):**
From 2024 onward, the global remaining carbon budget for **1.5 °C** with 50 % probability is **~275 GtCO₂**. Annual emissions are currently **~37 GtCO₂/year**. How many years until the budget is exhausted at constant emissions?

### Step-by-Step Logical Transition

**Step 1 — Define remaining carbon budget.**

$$
\text{Years Left} = \frac{\text{Remaining Budget}}{\text{Annual Emissions}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
\text{Years Left} = \frac{275}{37} \approx 7.4\ \text{years}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**

$$
\text{Carbon budget for 1.5°C will be exhausted by ~2031 at current rates.}
$$

This is why the long-term scenario insists on **rapid decarbonization** — otherwise the 1.5 °C target becomes mathematically impossible.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Scenario Tool

| Tool | Time Horizon | Strength | Limitation | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LEAP** | 20–50 yr | Sectoral end-use detail | Limited technology learning | National energy policy |
| **MARKAL** | 30–50 yr | Optimized technology mix | Data-hungry | Power-sector planning |
| **MESSAGE** | 50–100 yr | Multi-regional coupling | Complex | IEA WEM modelling |
| **AIM** | 50–100 yr | Climate–land–energy linkage | Steep learning curve | IPCC pathways |
| **CAGR extrapolation** | 5–30 yr | Quick, simple | Ignores structural change | First-cut estimate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Long-Term Energy Scenario Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Inputs Drivers] --> nodeB[Demand Forecast CAGR Econometric EndUse]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Reference Energy System RES]
    nodeD[Supply Resources Fossil Renewable Nuclear Hydrogen] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE[Optimization Layer LEAP MARKAL MESSAGE]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Scenario Output]
    nodeF --> nodeG[TPES Forecast Mtoe]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Fuel Mix Share percent]
    nodeF --> nodeI[CO2 Emission GtCO2]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Energy Intensity toe per GDP]
    nodeG --> nodeK[Policy and Investment Decisions]
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL[National Energy Security Net Zero Pathway]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Inputs, Process, and Outputs of an LTES Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inpA[Population Growth] --> procA[LTES Engine]
    inpB[GDP Trajectory] --> procA
    inpC[Technology Cost Curves] --> procA
    inpD[Fossil Fuel Reserves] --> procA
    inpE[Climate Policy Constraints] --> procA
    procA --> outA[Demand by Sector 2050]
    procA --> outB[Supply Mix by Fuel]
    procA --> outC[Emission Trajectory]
    procA --> outD[Energy Security Index]
    procA --> outE[Investment Requirements USD trillion]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Reference Energy System (RES) Concept

The **Reference Energy System (RES)** is the *spinal cord* of any long-term energy model. It is a network diagram showing all energy carriers from primary resource extraction (coal mines, oil wells, solar farms) through conversion (refineries, power plants, electrolysers) to end-use services (lighting, mobility, space conditioning).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    resA[Primary Resources Coal Oil Gas Solar Wind Hydro Nuclear Biomass] --> resB[Conversion Sector Refineries Power Plants Electrolysers Biogas Plants]
    resB --> resC[Secondary Carriers Electricity Diesel Petrol Hydrogen CNG LPG Biofuels]
    resC --> resD[End Use Sectors Residential Commercial Industrial Transport Agriculture]
    resD --> resE[Energy Services Lighting Mobility Cooking Comfort HVAC Process Heat]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Interpretation:** When a question asks you to *"draw the block diagram of a long-term energy scenario model"*, the **RES network** above, or a simplified version, is what examiners expect. Always include the **three layers** — Primary, Conversion, End-use.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sem VIII]`
**Define Long Term Energy Scenario. List any four drivers that influence long-term energy demand projections.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Long Term Energy Scenario (LTES) is a quantitative projection of a region's energy demand, supply mix, technology deployment, and emissions over a horizon of **20–50 years**, used for strategic infrastructure, policy, and decarbonization planning. Four key demand drivers:
1. **Population growth** and demographic transition.
2. **GDP growth rate** and structural economic change.
3. **Technology penetration** (efficiency, EVs, renewables).
4. **Policy and carbon-pricing frameworks** that alter fuel choices.

*(1 mark for definition, ½ mark each for the four drivers — KTU 2024 split pattern.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Sem VIII]`
**Differentiate between short-term, medium-term, and long-term energy planning with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Horizon | Time | Example Activity |
|---|---|---|
| Short Term | 0–2 yr | Day-ahead load scheduling, DSM billing |
| Medium Term | 2–10 yr | Adding a 500 MW unit, signing 5-yr LNG contracts |
| **Long Term** | **20–50 yr** | **Net-zero roadmap, capacity mix for 2050, hydrogen economy** |

(1 mark per row × 3 = 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in ESE Module questions. Two fully independent alternatives — Question A and Question B — are provided below. Each must be attempted only **ONE**, in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) Explain in detail the various methodologies used for long-term energy demand forecasting. Discuss their relative merits and limitations.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A developing country had TPES = 80 Mtoe in 2010 and population = 900 million. In 2023, TPES = 150 Mtoe and population = 1.05 billion. Compute (i) CAGR of TPES, (ii) Energy Intensity if GDP grew from \$ 1.5 trillion to \$ 4.2 trillion, and (iii) Per capita energy consumption for both years.** *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Forecasting Methodologies (7 marks)

1. **Econometric / Macroeconomic Method** *(2 marks)*
   - Uses historical correlations between energy demand and macro variables like GDP, population, prices.
   - Merits: simple, requires less data.
   - Limitations: cannot capture disruptive technology shifts.

2. **End-Use / Bottom-Up Method** *(2 marks)*
   - Disaggregates demand by sector (residential, industrial, etc.) and end-use.
   - Merits: high resolution; ideal for DSM analysis.
   - Limitations: data-intensive.

3. **Technological / Engineering Method** *(2 marks)*
   - Models each technology's efficiency and market share.
   - Merits: best for low-carbon transition analysis.
   - Limitations: highly data-demanding; sensitive to cost assumptions.

4. **Hybrid / Integrated Approach** *(1 mark)*
   - Combines top-down and bottom-up, used in **MARKAL**, **LEAP**, **MESSAGE**.
   - Example: India's INDC modelling used a hybrid approach.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**(i) CAGR of TPES (3 marks)**

$$
\text{CAGR} = \left(\frac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{n}} - 1
$$

Here $V_i = 80$ Mtoe (2010), $V_f = 150$ Mtoe (2023), $n = 13$ years.

$$
\text{CAGR} = \left(\frac{150}{80}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{13}} - 1 = (1.875)^{0.0769} - 1
$$

$$
\text{CAGR} \approx 0.0497 \approx 4.97\ \%
$$

**(ii) Energy Intensity for both years (2 marks)**

$$
\text{EI}_{2010} = \frac{80\ \text{Mtoe}}{1.5 \times 10^6\ \text{Million USD}} \approx 0.0533\ \text{toe/Million \$}
$$

$$
\text{EI}_{2023} = \frac{150\ \text{Mtoe}}{4.2 \times 10^6\ \text{Million USD}} \approx 0.0357\ \text{toe/Million \$}
$$

Energy intensity dropped by **~33 %**, indicating **successful decoupling**.

**(iii) Per Capita Energy Consumption (2 marks)**

$$
\text{PEC}_{2010} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{900 \times 10^6} \approx 0.0889\ \text{toe/person}
$$

$$
\text{PEC}_{2023} = \frac{150 \times 10^6}{1050 \times 10^6} \approx 0.1429\ \text{toe/person}
$$

PEC rose by **~60.7 %** — a sign of improving living standards.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) Discuss the role of carbon budgeting and decarbonization pathways in framing long-term energy scenarios. Include the IPCC SSP framework in your answer.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Compute the carbon budget exhaustion year if the current global emissions are 40 GtCO₂/year and the remaining budget for 1.5 °C is 300 GtCO₂. Also calculate the annual reduction rate (linear) required to reach net-zero by 2050.** *(7 marks)*

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Carbon Budgeting and SSP Framework (7 marks)

1. **Carbon Budget Concept** *(2 marks)*
   - Cumulative CO₂ that can be emitted while keeping global warming below a target (e.g., 1.5 °C).
   - Linked to Transient Climate Response to Cumulative Emissions (TCRE).

2. **IPCC SSP (Shared Socioeconomic Pathways)** *(3 marks)*
   - **SSP1: Sustainability (Green Road)** — rapid decarbonization.
   - **SSP2: Middle of the Road** — current trends continue.
   - **SSP3: Regional Rivalry** — fragmented, fossil-heavy.
   - **SSP4: Inequality** — energy access disparities.
   - **SSP5: Fossil-fuelled Development** — high growth, high emissions.

3. **Decarbonization Pathway Design** *(2 marks)*
   - Stage 1: Efficiency & electrification (2024–2035).
   - Stage 2: Massive renewable scale-up + grid modernization (2035–2045).
   - Stage 3: Hard-to-abate sector decarbonization via hydrogen, CCS, CDR (2045–2070).

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**(i) Carbon Budget Exhaustion Year (3 marks)**

$$
\text{Years Left} = \frac{300\ \text{GtCO}_2}{40\ \text{GtCO}_2/\text{yr}} = 7.5\ \text{years}
$$

Starting 2024, exhaustion year = **2031.5**.

**(ii) Linear Annual Reduction Required (4 marks)**

$$
\text{Total Reduction} = 40\ \text{GtCO}_2/\text{yr}\ \text{to}\ 0 \quad \text{in}\ 26\ \text{years (2024–2050)}
$$

$$
\text{Annual Reduction} = \frac{40}{26} \approx 1.538\ \text{GtCO}_2/\text{year}
$$

In percentage terms (compounded):

$$
r = \left(\frac{0}{40}\right)^{\tfrac{1}{26}} - 1 \approx -100\%\ \text{(asymptotic linear model preferred)}
$$

The KTU-acceptable answer is **~1.54 GtCO₂/year** linear cut.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistakes** — Mixing Mtoe with EJ or kWh. Always quote **both** units and the conversion factor in long-answer numericals.
> 2. **Skipping formula statements** — KTU 2024 board rewards **explicit formula → substitution → result** triplet. A bare final number gets **zero marks**.
> 3. **Confusing R/P with reserves alone** — R/P requires annual production in denominator. Many students write only the reserve number.
> 4. **Forgetting units in decoupling index** — Both percentages must be in the same time window.
> 5. **Ignoring policy context** — A long-term scenario without mention of **NDC, RPO, or net-zero target** is considered incomplete in 14-mark answers.
> 6. **Not drawing the RES diagram** — When asked, a textual description without a block diagram loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **LTES horizon = 20–50 years**; strategic horizon = up to 100 years.
- **TPES** is the master metric; everything else (EI, PEC, CI) is derived from it.
- **Conversion constant:** **1 Mtoe = 41.868 GJ = 11.63 TWh**. Carry this in every KTU answer.
- **Energy Intensity (EI)** ↓ over time = **decoupling** (efficient growth).
- **Carbon Intensity (CI)** ↓ = successful decarbonization.
- **Decoupling Index (DI) > 0** = relative decoupling.
- **R/P ratio** measures fossil fuel security (coal ~140 yr, oil ~50 yr, gas ~50 yr globally).
- **Three forecasting methods:** Econometric, End-Use, Technological. Hybrid is the modern standard.
- **Three core LTES tools:** **LEAP**, **MARKAL**, **MESSAGE**.
- **Reference Energy System (RES)** — three layers: Primary → Conversion → End-Use.
- **Five SSP scenarios** in IPCC AR6 frame every modern long-term pathway.
- **Net-zero math:** Current ~40 GtCO₂/yr; budget for 1.5 °C ~ 275–300 GtCO₂ → ~7 years at constant rate.
- **CAGR formula** is the workhorse for any "project the demand/supply/emissions" KTU question.
- **Carbon budget = cumulative CO₂** — not annual emission — that matters for climate stability.
- **Renewable cost learning curves** drive the optimistic supply-side scenarios (solar/wind).
- Always close any LTES discussion with **policy levers**: NDC, RPO, carbon tax, hydrogen mission.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Pricing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Pricing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Pricing** is the structured mechanism by which energy utilities (electricity, gas, thermal) determine and levy charges on consumers for the energy supplied, considering multiple cost components such as **energy consumption (kWh)**, **maximum demand (kVA or kW)**, **power factor**, **time of use**, and **load factor**. It serves as both a **revenue recovery tool** for utilities and an **economic instrument** for demand-side energy management.

The KTU 2024 Energy Management syllabus classifies energy pricing into several tariff structures designed to:
- Recover the **fixed cost** (capacity, infrastructure, depreciation) and **variable cost** (fuel, O&M) of energy generation and distribution.
- Influence consumer behavior to flatten the **load curve** and improve overall system efficiency.
- Penalize poor power factor and reward efficient consumption patterns.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Water Bucket Pricing Model**
>
> Imagine you are buying water from a municipal supply. The price you pay is NOT just based on the total liters you consume. The water company also considers:
> 1. **The size of the pipe** you installed (Maximum Demand charge — you pay for keeping the *capability* of drawing a lot, even if you don't use it).
> 2. **When you use water** — peak hours (morning showers) cost more than off-peak hours (midnight).
> 3. **How efficiently you used the pipe** — if your pipe is leaky (poor power factor), you are charged a penalty because the company had to push more water than you actually used.
> 4. **The actual volume of water consumed** (energy charge in kWh).
>
> Energy pricing works exactly the same way! You don't just pay for the units (kWh); you pay for the infrastructure, the timing, the efficiency, and the actual energy used.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Frequency:** **50 Hz** (India, KTU region)
- **Standard Voltage Levels (LT):** **230 V (single phase)**, **415 V (three phase)**
- **Standard HT Voltage:** **11 kV, 22 kV, 66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV**
- **Power Factor Benchmark:** **0.85 lag** (penalty threshold in India) to **0.95–1.0** (incentive zone)
- **Currency Reference:** INR (₹) per kWh or ₹ per kVA
- **Maximum Demand Reference Period:** Typically **15-minute or 30-minute integration window** in Indian utilities (KSEB, TNEB, MSEB)

### 1.4 Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 — Key Focus Areas for Energy Pricing:**
> - Tariff design objectives
> - Cost components (fixed, semi-fixed, variable)
> - Types of tariffs (flat rate, block rate, two-part, three-part, TOD, seasonal, demand-based)
> - Time of Day (TOD) tariffs and Time of Use (TOU) tariffs
> - Power factor penalty/reward structure
> - Interruptible tariffs and incentive-based pricing

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Objectives of Energy Pricing (Why Tariffs Exist)

Energy tariffs are not arbitrary — they are carefully engineered to satisfy **four core objectives**:

1. **Revenue Adequacy** — Recover all costs (fixed + variable) of generation, transmission, and distribution.
2. **Economic Efficiency** — Send correct price signals to consumers so that they shift loads to off-peak hours.
3. **Equity / Fairness** — Different consumer classes (domestic, commercial, industrial) pay proportionally to their cost of service.
4. **Demand Management** — Discourage wasteful peak-hour consumption through pricing signals.

### 2.2 Components of an Electricity Bill (Cost Structure)

Every electricity bill in India (and globally) is composed of **three major heads**:

- **A. Fixed Charges** → Independent of energy consumed. Based on **sanctioned load** or **recorded maximum demand** and the number of billing days.
- **B. Energy Charges** → Directly proportional to **kWh** (or kVAh) consumed.
- **C. Demand / Penalty / Incentive Charges** → Maximum demand penalty, power factor penalty, TOD differential, fuel cost adjustment (FCA), electricity duty, taxes.

### 2.3 Classification of Energy Tariffs

| **Tariff Type** | **Description** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat Rate Tariff** | Uniform rate (₹/kWh) for all units consumed regardless of quantity or time | Domestic, small commercial |
| **Block Rate Tariff** | Rate changes in blocks (e.g., 0–100 units @ ₹3, 101–200 @ ₹5, >200 @ ₹7) | Domestic, encourages conservation |
| **Two-Part Tariff** | Fixed charge (₹/kW or ₹/kVA) + Variable charge (₹/kWh) | Industrial, commercial HT |
| **Three-Part Tariff** | Fixed charge + Variable charge + Maximum demand charge (₹/kVA) | Large industrial consumers |
| **TOD / TOU Tariff** | Different rates for peak, off-peak, and shoulder hours | Industries, EV charging stations |
| **Seasonal Tariff** | Different rates for summer/winter/monsoon | Agricultural pumps, seasonal industries |
| **Interruptible Tariff** | Utility can cut supply during peak; consumer gets discount | Industries with backup (DG sets) |
| **Real-Time Pricing (RTP)** | Spot price changes hourly based on market | Large consumers, open access |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Energy Pricing (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mandatory** for solving Part B (14-mark) problems in KTU 2024 ESE.

| **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $E = P \times t$ | Energy = Power × Time | kWh |
| $MD = \frac{\text{Peak load recorded in integration period}}{1}$ | Maximum Demand (15-min or 30-min) | kW or kVA |
| $LF = \frac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Maximum Demand}} = \frac{\text{Units consumed in a period}}{MD \times \text{Hours in period}}$ | Load Factor (always $\leq 1$) | dimensionless or % |
| $DF = \frac{MD}{\text{Connected Load}}$ | Diversity Factor (for group) | dimensionless |
| $PF = \frac{kW}{kVA} = \cos\phi$ | Power Factor | dimensionless or lag/lead |
| $kVA = \frac{kW}{PF}$ | Apparent power from real power | kVA |
| $PF \text{ Penalty} = \text{Tariff} \times kVA \times \text{Penalty Rate}$ | PF penalty charge (e.g., for $PF < 0.85$) | ₹ |
| $PF \text{ Rebate} = \text{Energy Charge} \times \text{Rebate \%}$ | PF rebate (e.g., for unity or $> 0.95$) | ₹ |
| $Bill = (FC \times MD) + (EC \times kWh) + (Penalty) - (Rebate)$ | Composite electricity bill | ₹ |
| $Average Rate = \frac{\text{Total Bill}}{\text{Units consumed (kWh)}}$ | Effective cost per unit | ₹/kWh |
| $TOD\ Differential = (Peak\ kWh \times Peak\ Rate) + (OffPeak\ kWh \times OffPeak\ Rate)$ | Time-of-day bill | ₹ |
| $MD\ Penalty = (Recorded\ MD - Sanctioned\ MD) \times Penalty\ Rate$ | Excess demand charge | ₹ |

**CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** In all written solutions, use $\vert$ or $\mid$ (not $\vert x \vert$ with the pipe character) when absolute value is required. For example, write $PF = \vert \cos\phi \vert$ not $PF = |cos\phi|$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Industrial Energy Auditors** use these formulas to compute the financial impact of poor PF and high MD.
- **Demand-Side Management (DSM)** engineers design tariffs that flatten the load curve.
- **Renewable Energy Planners** use TOD pricing to incentivize solar generation at peak hours.
- **Smart Grid Designers** build automated billing systems on real-time pricing architectures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Two-Part Tariff Bill Computation

The **two-part tariff** is the most fundamental pricing model in industrial energy billing. Let us derive the general bill formula from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the Cost Components**

A utility incurs two distinct cost categories:

$$C_{total} = C_{fixed} + C_{variable}$$

where:
- $C_{fixed}$ = cost independent of energy (infrastructure, depreciation, salaries, interest) → recovered as **Fixed Charge (FC)** per kW or kVA of **Maximum Demand (MD)**.
- $C_{variable}$ = cost proportional to energy (fuel cost) → recovered as **Energy Charge (EC)** per kWh consumed.

**Step 2 — Express the Fixed Charge in Terms of MD**

If the utility charges a fixed rate $R_f$ (in ₹/kW or ₹/kVA) for the billing period, and the consumer's recorded maximum demand is $MD$:

$$C_{fixed} = R_f \times MD$$

**Step 3 — Express the Variable Charge in Terms of kWh**

If the utility charges an energy rate $R_e$ (in ₹/kWh) and the consumer used $E$ units (kWh) during the period:

$$C_{variable} = R_e \times E$$

**Step 4 — Combine the Two Components**

$$Bill = R_f \times MD + R_e \times E$$

This is the **canonical two-part tariff formula**. Industry-standard form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Bill (₹)} &= (\text{Fixed Charge Rate} \times \text{Maximum Demand in kVA}) \\
&\quad + (\text{Energy Charge Rate} \times \text{Units consumed in kWh})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Extended Form with Penalties and Incentives**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Bill} &= (R_f \times MD) + (R_e \times kWh) + \text{PF Penalty} \\
&\quad - \text{PF Rebate} + \text{TOD Differential} + \text{Taxes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic in Words:** The two-part tariff simply splits the utility's revenue requirement into a *capacity-based fixed slice* (you pay for the right to draw a certain peak) and an *energy-based variable slice* (you pay for what you actually consume). This bifurcation ensures that the utility recovers its capital cost even if a consumer's load factor is low.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Power Factor Penalty Calculation

**Step 1 — The Problem**

A consumer draws $P$ kW of real power but operates at a lagging power factor of $\cos\phi = PF$. The utility must supply:

$$kVA = \frac{kW}{PF} = \frac{P}{\cos\phi}$$

**Step 2 — The Penalty Logic**

If the tariff is expressed in ₹/kVA (kVA-based billing), a poor PF means the consumer draws **more kVA** for the same kW, increasing the utility's infrastructure burden (transformers, cables must be rated higher).

**Step 3 — Penalty Formula**

Many Indian utilities (KSEB, BESCOM, TNEB) charge a penalty if $PF < 0.85$. A typical structure:

$$
\text{PF Penalty (₹)} = R_{kVA} \times kVA \times \left(0.85 - PF_{actual}\right) \times k
$$

where $k$ is a multiplier constant defined by the utility (e.g., $k = 1\%$ per 0.01 fall below 0.85).

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example**

**Given:** $P = 500$ kW, $PF_{actual} = 0.75$ lag, $R_{kVA} = ₹400$/kVA, penalty factor $k = 1\%$ per 0.01 lag below 0.85.

**Step 4a — Compute kVA drawn:**

$$kVA = \frac{500}{0.75} = 666.67 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 4b — Compute the PF deficit:**

$$\Delta PF = 0.85 - 0.75 = 0.10$$

**Step 4c — Compute penalty percentage:**

$$\text{Penalty \%} = k \times \frac{\Delta PF}{0.01} = 1\% \times 10 = 10\%$$

**Step 4d — Compute penalty amount:**

$$
\text{Penalty} = 400 \times 666.67 \times \frac{10}{100} = ₹26{,}667
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Every 0.01 drop below the threshold PF of 0.85 attracts a 1% penalty on the kVA charges. So, going from 0.85 to 0.75 (a 0.10 drop) attracts a 10% penalty on the kVA bill.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — TOD (Time of Day) Tariff Computation

**Step 1 — Define Time Slots**

For a typical Indian state electricity board:

| **Slot** | **Time Window** | **Rate (₹/kWh)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Normal Hours** | 06:00 – 18:00 | $R_n$ |
| **Evening Peak (P1)** | 18:00 – 22:00 | $R_p = 1.5 R_n$ |
| **Night Off-Peak (P2)** | 22:00 – 06:00 | $R_{op} = 0.75 R_n$ |

**Step 2 — Energy Consumed in Each Slot**

$$E_{total} = E_n + E_p + E_{op}$$

**Step 3 — TOD Tariff Bill**

$$
\text{TOD Bill} = (E_n \times R_n) + (E_p \times R_p) + (E_{op} \times R_{op})
$$

**Step 4 — Conversion to Effective Rate**

$$R_{effective} = \frac{\text{TOD Bill}}{E_{total}}$$

**Real-World Logic:** Industries that shift their heavy machinery loads (compressors, chillers, induction furnaces) to off-peak hours (22:00 – 06:00) save significantly. A textile unit consuming 1,00,000 kWh/month can save up to **15–25%** of its energy bill through proper TOD management.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Tariff

| **Consumer Type** | **Best Tariff** | **Reasoning** |
|---|---|---|
| Small domestic (1 kW load) | Block rate tariff | Encourages conservation; simple to administer |
| Large industry (continuous load) | Three-part tariff (kVA-based) | Recovers demand + energy cost fairly |
| Industry with flexible process | TOD / TOU tariff | Can shift loads to off-peak; lower cost |
| Seasonal industry (rice mill) | Seasonal tariff | Pay lower rate in off-season |
| Process industry (no flexibility) | Flat or kVA tariff | Cannot shift loads; TOD gives no benefit |
| Data center with DG backup | Interruptible tariff | Get discount in exchange for shutdown rights |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Components of an Industrial Electricity Bill

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electricity Bill] --> B[Fixed Charges]
    A --> C[Energy Charges]
    A --> D[Demand Charges]
    A --> E[PF Penalty]
    A --> F[PF Rebate]
    A --> G[TOD Differential]
    A --> H[Taxes and Duties]
    B --> B1[Sanctioned Load x Rate]
    C --> C1[kWh Consumed x Rate]
    D --> D1[Recorded MD x Rate]
    E --> E1[If PF less than 0.85]
    F --> F1[If PF greater than 0.95]
    G --> G1[Peak kWh x Peak Rate]
    H --> H1[Electricity Duty, GST]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart — Power Factor Penalty Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measure PF] --> B{PF Check}
    B -->|PF greater than 0.95| C[Award Rebate]
    B -->|PF between 0.85 and 0.95| D[No Penalty, No Rebate]
    B -->|PF less than 0.85| E[Calculate Penalty]
    E --> F[Penalty equals Rate x kVA x Deficit x Factor]
    F --> G[Add to Monthly Bill]
    C --> H[Deduct from Monthly Bill]
    D --> I[Bill Unchanged]
    G --> J[Final Bill Payable]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

### 4.3 Architecture Flow — Time of Day Tariff Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Industrial Load Profile] --> B{Load Classification}
    B -->|Shiftable Load| C[Shift to Off Peak 22:00 to 06:00]
    B -->|Critical Load| D[Run During Normal 06:00 to 18:00]
    B -->|Avoidable Load| E[Defer from Peak 18:00 to 22:00]
    C --> F[Lowest Rate Applied]
    D --> G[Normal Rate Applied]
    E --> H[Highest Rate Applied]
    F --> I[Optimized Energy Bill]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical Breakdown — Tariff Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Tariffs] --> B[Single Part Tariff]
    A --> C[Two Part Tariff]
    A --> D[Three Part Tariff]
    A --> E[Special Tariffs]
    B --> B1[Flat Rate]
    B --> B2[Block Rate]
    C --> C1[Fixed plus Energy]
    C --> C2[Demand plus Energy]
    D --> D1[Fixed plus Energy plus MD]
    E --> E1[TOD Tariff]
    E --> E2[Seasonal Tariff]
    E --> E3[Interruptible Tariff]
    E --> E4[Real Time Pricing]
```

### 4.5 System-Level Block Diagram — Energy Pricing in Smart Grid Context

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Smart Meter at Consumer Premise] --> B[Data Acquisition]
    B --> C[Load Profiling]
    C --> D{Tariff Engine}
    D -->|Peak Hours| E[High Rate Block]
    D -->|Off Peak Hours| F[Low Rate Block]
    D -->|Normal Hours| G[Standard Rate Block]
    E --> H[Bill Computation]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Display on Consumer App]
    H --> J[Utility Billing System]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "Two-Part Tariff" and state its two components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Two-Part Tariff** is an electricity pricing structure in which the total bill is split into two distinct cost components:

1. **Fixed Charge (or Demand Charge):** A fixed amount per kW or kVA of maximum demand, levied regardless of the actual energy consumed. This recovers the utility's fixed costs (infrastructure, capital, depreciation).

2. **Energy Charge (or Running Charge):** A variable amount proportional to the actual energy consumed (kWh) during the billing period. This recovers the utility's variable costs (fuel, operation).

**Formula:**

$$Bill = (R_f \times MD) + (R_e \times kWh)$$

This tariff is widely used for **industrial and large commercial consumers** because it fairly allocates fixed costs based on the peak demand imposed on the grid. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is Time of Day (TOD) tariff? Why is it used?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Time of Day (TOD) tariff is an electricity pricing mechanism in which the rate per unit (₹/kWh) varies according to the time slot during which the energy is consumed. Typically, the rate is:
- **Highest** during evening peak hours (18:00 – 22:00)
- **Normal** during daytime (06:00 – 18:00)
- **Lowest** during night off-peak hours (22:00 – 06:00)

**Why it is used:**
1. To **flatten the load curve** by incentivizing consumers to shift non-critical loads (e.g., water pumping, charging, heating) to off-peak hours.
2. To **reduce peak demand** on the grid, avoiding the need for costly peaking power plants.
3. To **improve system efficiency** and reduce the cost of generation.
4. To enable **demand-side management (DSM)** through economic signals rather than administrative rationing. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 1 Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following two questions.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** A factory has the following consumption data for a billing month (30 days):
- **Maximum Demand recorded:** 850 kVA
- **Energy consumed:** 4,50,000 kWh
- **Average Power Factor:** 0.78 lag
- **Sanctioned Load:** 1000 kVA

The tariff structure is:
- **Fixed Charge:** ₹350 per kVA per month
- **Energy Charge:** ₹6.50 per kWh
- **PF Penalty:** 1% on the energy charges for every 0.01 fall in PF below 0.85

Compute:
1. The Fixed Charge.
2. The Energy Charge.
3. The PF Penalty.
4. The total bill amount.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the different types of energy tariffs used in India with suitable examples. List the **four objectives** of a well-designed tariff.

**(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute Fixed Charge:**

$$FC = 350 \times 850 = ₹2{,}97{,}500$$

**Step 2 — Compute Energy Charge:**

$$EC = 6.50 \times 4{,}50{,}000 = ₹29{,}25{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compute PF Penalty:**

PF deficit: $\Delta PF = 0.85 - 0.78 = 0.07$

Number of 0.01 units in deficit: $n = 0.07 / 0.01 = 7$

Penalty % on energy charge: $1\% \times 7 = 7\%$

$$PF\ Penalty = 7\% \times 29{,}25{,}000 = 0.07 \times 29{,}25{,}000 = ₹2{,}04{,}750$$

**Step 4 — Compute Total Bill:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Bill} &= FC + EC + PF\ Penalty \\
&= 2{,}97{,}500 + 29{,}25{,}000 + 2{,}04{,}750 \\
&= ₹34{,}27{,}250
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Effective Rate per Unit:**

$$R_{eff} = \frac{34{,}27{,}250}{4{,}50{,}000} = ₹7.62 \text{ per kWh}$$

*(7 marks — full working and correct answers)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b):**

**Types of Energy Tariffs in India:**

1. **Flat Rate Tariff:** A single uniform rate (e.g., ₹7/kWh) is charged irrespective of consumption level. Used for domestic and small consumers. *Limitation:* does not encourage conservation.

2. **Block Rate Tariff:** The consumption is divided into blocks with progressively higher rates. E.g., 0–100 units @ ₹3, 101–200 units @ ₹5, >200 units @ ₹7. Used for domestic consumers to penalize high consumption.

3. **Two-Part Tariff:** A fixed charge (₹/kW or ₹/kVA) plus an energy charge (₹/kWh). Used for industrial and commercial consumers. Recovers both fixed and variable costs of the utility.

4. **Three-Part Tariff:** Fixed charge + Energy charge + Maximum demand charge (₹/kVA). Used for large HT industrial consumers.

5. **TOD/TOU Tariff:** Different rates for different time slots (peak, normal, off-peak). Used to shift loads away from peak hours.

6. **Seasonal Tariff:** Different rates for different seasons. Used for agricultural pumps and seasonal industries.

7. **Interruptible Tariff:** Utility reserves the right to disconnect during peak hours in exchange for a discount. Used for industries with captive DG backup.

**Four Objectives of a Well-Designed Tariff:**

1. **Revenue Adequacy** — Must recover total cost of service.
2. **Economic Efficiency** — Should send correct price signals to consumers.
3. **Equity (Fairness)** — Different consumer classes should pay proportionally to cost imposed.
4. **Demand Management** — Should encourage load shifting and conservation.

*(7 marks — all points covered with examples)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** A consumer's monthly bill is as follows:
- **Energy consumed:** 50,000 kWh
- **Maximum Demand:** 400 kVA
- **Tariff:** Fixed charge = ₹400/kVA/month; Energy charge = ₹5/kWh
- **PF Penalty:** 2% of the total bill (EC + FC) for PF below 0.85; PF rebate = 1% of EC for PF above 0.95

Calculate:
1. The bill at unity power factor.
2. The bill at 0.80 PF lag.
3. The bill at 0.98 PF lag.
4. The effective rate per kWh in each case.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a **Time of Day (TOD) tariff**. Discuss its advantages to both the consumer and the utility.

**(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a):**

**Case 1: Unity Power Factor (PF = 1.0)**

- Fixed Charge: $400 \times 400 = ₹1{,}60{,}000$
- Energy Charge: $5 \times 50{,}000 = ₹2{,}50{,}000$
- PF Penalty: Nil (PF ≥ 0.95 attracts rebate, not penalty)
- PF Rebate: $1\% \times 2{,}50{,}000 = ₹2{,}500$ (since PF = 1.0 > 0.95)
- **Net Bill:** $1{,}60{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 - 2{,}500 = ₹4{,}07{,}500$
- **Effective Rate:** $4{,}07{,}500 / 50{,}000 = ₹8.15$/kWh

**Case 2: PF = 0.80 Lag**

- Fixed Charge: $400 \times 400 = ₹1{,}60{,}000$
- Energy Charge: $5 \times 50{,}000 = ₹2{,}50{,}000$
- Subtotal (FC + EC): $₹4{,}10{,}000$
- PF Penalty: $2\% \times 4{,}10{,}000 = ₹8{,}200$ (since PF = 0.80 < 0.85)
- **Net Bill:** $1{,}60{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 + 8{,}200 = ₹4{,}18{,}200$
- **Effective Rate:** $4{,}18{,}200 / 50{,}000 = ₹8.36$/kWh

**Case 3: PF = 0.98 Lag**

- Fixed Charge: $400 \times 400 = ₹1{,}60{,}000$
- Energy Charge: $5 \times 50{,}000 = ₹2{,}50{,}000$
- PF Rebate: $1\% \times 2{,}50{,}000 = ₹2{,}500$ (since PF = 0.98 > 0.95)
- **Net Bill:** $1{,}60{,}000 + 2{,}50{,}000 - 2{,}500 = ₹4{,}07{,}500$
- **Effective Rate:** $4{,}07{,}500 / 50{,}000 = ₹8.15$/kWh

**Inference:** Operating at poor PF (0.80) costs the consumer ₹10,700 more per month than operating at 0.98 PF. This justifies investment in power factor correction capacitors. *(7 marks)*

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b):**

**TOD Tariff — Diagram:**

```
[24-Hour Timeline]
─────────────────────────────────────────────
06:00              18:00         22:00    06:00
  │─── Normal ─────│─── Peak ────│─ Off-Peak ─│
  Rate: Rs 6/kWh    Rate: Rs 9/kWh   Rate: Rs 4.5/kWh
─────────────────────────────────────────────
```

**Explanation:**

In TOD tariff, the 24-hour day is divided into time slots, each with a different rate per kWh. The structure is:

- **Normal Period (06:00 – 18:00):** Standard rate
- **Peak Period (18:00 – 22:00):** 1.5× normal rate
- **Off-Peak Period (22:00 – 06:00):** 0.75× normal rate

**Advantages to the Consumer:**
1. Substantial savings (10–25%) by shifting non-critical loads to off-peak hours.
2. Better load planning and process scheduling.
3. Avoidance of maximum demand penalties (since peak loads are shifted).
4. Predictable cost structure for energy budgeting.

**Advantages to the Utility:**
1. Flattened load curve → higher **load factor** → better asset utilization.
2. Reduced need for peaking power plants → lower generation cost.
3. Lower transmission and distribution losses.
4. Improved grid stability and voltage profile.
5. Deferred capital investment in new generation capacity.

**Conclusion:** TOD tariff is a **win-win economic instrument** that aligns consumer interest (lower bills) with utility interest (lower cost of service) by exploiting the time-value of electricity. *(7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU Students Lose Marks in Energy Pricing Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Fixed Charge component** — Many students compute only the energy charge. Always read the question carefully for "₹/kVA per month" type fixed charges.
>
> 2. **Confusing kVA and kW** — If MD is given in kVA and tariff is ₹/kVA, use the kVA value directly. Do NOT multiply by power factor.
>
> 3. **Wrong PF Penalty Direction** — A penalty is applied when PF is **less than** the threshold (e.g., 0.85), and a **rebate** is given when PF is **greater than** the incentive threshold (e.g., 0.95). Reversing this logic costs full marks.
>
> 4. **Unit Mismatch in Load Factor** — Load Factor = (Total kWh) / (MD × Hours in period). If period is 30 days, use 30 × 24 = 720 hours.
>
> 5. **Not Showing Step-by-Step Working** — KTU examiners reward **method marks**. Even if the final answer is wrong, full method marks are awarded for correct steps. Always write intermediate equations.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the Demand Charge Cap** — Many utilities (KSEB) charge MD only up to 90% of contracted demand. Always read the specific utility's tariff order in the question.
>
> 7. **Misapplying TOD Rates** — TOD rates are differential; you cannot just average them. Compute weighted average based on kWh consumed in each slot.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — ENERGY PRICING (Module 1)**

- 🔑 **Two-Part Tariff Formula:** $Bill = R_f \times MD + R_e \times kWh$
- 🔑 **Three-Part Tariff adds MD charge:** $Bill = (R_f \times MD) + (R_{md} \times MD_{recorded}) + (R_e \times kWh)$
- 🔑 **Load Factor** must be ≤ 1.0; higher LF = better for utility.
- 🔑 **PF Penalty** kicks in when PF < 0.85 lag (India standard).
- 🔑 **PF Rebate** is awarded when PF > 0.95 lag (or unity in some utilities).
- 🔑 **kVA = kW / PF** — fundamental relationship for tariff math.
- 🔑 **TOD Tariff** has 3 slots: Peak (highest rate), Normal, Off-Peak (lowest rate).
- 🔑 **Interruptible Tariff** = Discount in exchange for utility's right to cut off supply.
- 🔑 **Real-Time Pricing (RTP)** = Spot price changes hourly (used in deregulated markets).
- 🔑 **Four objectives of tariff:** Revenue adequacy, Economic efficiency, Equity, Demand management.
- 🔑 **Effective rate** = Total bill / Total kWh consumed.
- 🔑 **Demand penalty** = Excess over sanctioned MD × penalty rate.
- 🔑 **Block tariff** is a *disincentive* structure (penalizes high consumption).
- 🔑 **TOD differential** is computed slot-wise — never use a simple average.
- 🔑 **Power factor correction (PFC)** with capacitor banks is the most cost-effective way to avoid PF penalty.
- 🔑 **Maximum demand integration period** in India = 15 or 30 minutes.
- 🔑 **Always state units** in the final answer (₹, ₹/kWh, kVA, etc.) to fetch the unit mark.
- 🔑 **Use `kWh` (not `units`)** in technical answers to score full marks.

---

**END OF MODULE 1 — ENERGY PRICING NOTES**

topics.title: Energy Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Security (IEA Definition):** Energy security is defined as the *uninterrupted availability of energy sources at an affordable price*. It encompasses the reliable supply of energy to meet the demand of households, industries, transportation, and defense sectors, while ensuring economic viability, environmental sustainability, and geopolitical stability.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** and the broader framework of energy management, energy security is viewed as a **multi-dimensional strategic concept** that integrates:

- **Physical availability** of energy resources
- **Geopolitical stability** of supply regions
- **Economic affordability** of energy for end-users
- **Environmental sustainability** of energy systems
- **Infrastructure resilience** against disruptions

### 1.2 The "Four A's" Framework of Energy Security

Modern energy security is universally understood through the **"Four A's"** paradigm:

| Pillar | Description | Engineering Perspective |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Availability** | Existence of sufficient primary energy resources | Reserve estimation, geological surveys |
| **Accessibility** | Ability to extract, refine, and transport energy | Pipeline networks, shipping routes, port infrastructure |
| **Affordability** | Energy priced within consumer purchasing power | Tariff structures, subsidies, market dynamics |
| **Acceptability / Sustainability** | Alignment with environmental and social norms | Carbon footprint, renewable integration, ESG goals |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Students must remember that energy security is NOT merely the absence of energy shortage — it is a **holistic balance** between supply, demand, price, and sustainability.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Household Water Tank Analogy:**
> 
> Imagine your home's water supply. Energy security is analogous to ensuring:
> 
> 1. **Availability** → The water tank (reservoir) has enough water (like proven oil/gas reserves).
> 2. **Accessibility** → The pipes (pipeline infrastructure) are not broken and can deliver water to your tap.
> 3. **Affordability** → The water bill (tariff) is reasonable for your monthly budget.
> 4. **Acceptability** → The water is clean and safe (renewable/clean energy, not polluting).
> 
> If any one of these fails, your household faces a "water security" crisis — similarly, a nation faces an "energy security" crisis.

### 1.4 Why Energy Security Matters for Engineers

For a B.Tech student, energy security is relevant because:

- **Power system design** must account for supply disruptions
- **Industrial energy audits** depend on stable, affordable energy
- **Sustainable engineering** requires balancing demand with clean supply
- **Project feasibility** analysis is built on long-term energy price stability

The **standard energy security benchmark** used globally is the **IEA's 90-day net oil import rule**, which mandates member countries to maintain strategic petroleum reserves equivalent to **90 days** of net imports.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Energy Security — A Structural Breakdown

Energy security operates at **three interconnected levels**:

#### 2.1.1 Macro (National) Level
- **National energy independence**: Ratio of domestic production to total consumption
- **Strategic reserves**: Stockpiles of crude oil, natural gas, coal
- **Diplomatic relations**: Long-term supply contracts, bilateral energy agreements
- **Energy import diversification**: Avoiding over-dependence on a single supplier

#### 2.1.2 Meso (Sectoral) Level
- **Power sector security**: Grid stability, generation adequacy
- **Industrial energy security**: Reliable feedstock for fertilizers, steel, petrochemicals
- **Transport sector security**: Fuel availability for mobility and logistics

#### 2.1.3 Micro (Household / Consumer) Level
- **Energy poverty avoidance**: Ensuring last-mile electricity access
- **Affordable tariffs**: Preventing energy-induced inflation
- **Service quality**: Voltage stability, frequency regulation, supply hours

### 2.2 Pillars of Energy Security Strategy

The **Why** and **How** of each strategic pillar:

1. **Diversification of Energy Mix**
   - *Why?* Reduces vulnerability to single-source shocks
   - *How?* Combining renewables, nuclear, hydro, and fossil fuels in the generation portfolio

2. **Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR)**
   - *Why?* Buffers against sudden supply shocks (e.g., 1973 oil crisis, 1990 Gulf War)
   - *How?* Underground cavern storage, salt-dome storage

3. **Energy Efficiency and Conservation**
   - *Why?* Lowers demand, thereby reducing import dependence
   - *How?* Audits, BEE star ratings, efficient appliances, process optimization

4. **Renewable Energy Deployment**
   - *Why?* Inexhaustible, domestic, low-carbon
   - *How?* Solar, wind, biomass, tidal, geothermal integration

5. **Demand-Side Management (DSM)**
   - *Why?* Shifts load from peak to off-peak hours
   - *How?* Time-of-Use (ToU) tariffs, smart grids, load shedding schedules

6. **International Cooperation and Trade**
   - *Why?* Geopolitical leverage and supply assurance
   - *How?* Pipelines (e.g., TAPI, Nord Stream), LNG terminals, FTAs

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Energy Security — Risk Taxonomy

Energy security risks can be classified into **four broad categories**:

- **Physical risks**: Natural disasters, equipment failure, pipeline sabotage
- **Economic risks**: Price volatility, currency fluctuations, market manipulation
- **Geopolitical risks**: Trade wars, sanctions, OPEC decisions, regional conflicts
- **Environmental/regulatory risks**: Carbon taxes, pollution norms, ESG compliance

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Energy security principles are applied in:

- **Smart grid design** with microgrids and islanding capability
- **Hybrid renewable systems** (solar + wind + battery storage)
- **Combined cycle power plants** to optimize fuel flexibility
- **Cogeneration and trigeneration** systems in industrial estates
- **Strategic power plant siting** near fuel source or load center

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the most frequently tested formulas for Energy Security in KTU examinations.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{1. Energy Security Index (ESI) - Composite Metric:} \nonumber \\
&\text{ESI} = w_1 \cdot D + w_2 \cdot A + w_3 \cdot S + w_4 \cdot E
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Diversification score (normalized 0–1)
- $A$ = Availability score (reserves-to-consumption ratio)
- $S$ = Affordability score (price-to-income ratio)
- $E$ = Efficiency score (energy intensity inverse)
- $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4$ = Weighted coefficients such that $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 = 1$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{2. Import Dependency Ratio (IDR):} \nonumber \\
&\text{IDR} \,(\%) = \frac{\text{Net Energy Imports}}{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumption}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{3. Energy Intensity (EI):} \nonumber \\
&\text{EI} = \frac{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumption (toe)}}{\text{Gross Domestic Product (GDP, million USD)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Units: **toe / million USD** (toe = tonnes of oil equivalent)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{4. Herfindahl-Hirschman Energy Index (HHEI):} \nonumber \\
&\text{HHEI} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $s_i$ = share of $i^{th}$ energy source in total mix (as fraction), $N$ = number of sources.
- HHEI closer to **1** → low diversification (high risk)
- HHEI closer to **$1/N$** → perfect diversification (low risk)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{5. Shannon-Wiener Diversity Index (SWDI):} \nonumber \\
&\text{SWDI} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i \cdot \ln(s_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

Higher SWDI → better diversification.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{6. Strategic Reserve Adequacy Ratio (SRAR):} \nonumber \\
&\text{SRAR} = \frac{\text{Strategic Reserves Available}}{\text{90-day Average Net Imports}}
\end{aligned}
$$

SRAR $\geq 1$ indicates **IEA compliance**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{7. Reserve-to-Production Ratio (R/P):} \nonumber \\
&\text{R/P} \,(\text{years}) = \frac{\text{Proven Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Formula | Symbol | Unit | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ESI | — | Dimensionless (0–1) | Composite energy security rating |
| IDR | $\text{IDR}$ | Percentage | Quantifies import reliance |
| EI | $\text{EI}$ | toe / million USD | Decoupling of growth and energy use |
| HHEI | $\text{HHEI}$ | Dimensionless | Measures fuel mix concentration |
| SWDI | $\text{SWDI}$ | Dimensionless | Quantifies diversity of supply |
| SRAR | $\text{SRAR}$ | Dimensionless | Verifies strategic stock compliance |
| R/P | $\text{R/P}$ | Years | Depletion timeline of reserves |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A country like India with high HHEI (coal-dominated mix) and high IDR (>40% for crude oil) is energy-insecure on these metrics, hence the push for solar and electric mobility under national missions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Import Dependency Ratio (IDR)

The **Import Dependency Ratio** quantifies a nation's reliance on foreign energy sources.

**Given:**
- Total Primary Energy Consumption (TPEC) = $C_{\text{total}}$ (in Mtoe)
- Domestic Energy Production (DEP) = $P_{\text{domestic}}$
- Net Energy Imports = $C_{\text{total}} - P_{\text{domestic}}$

**Step 1:** Express the net import in terms of consumption and domestic production.

$$
\text{Net Imports} = C_{\text{total}} - P_{\text{domestic}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the IDR definition.

$$
\text{IDR} \,(\%) = \frac{C_{\text{total}} - P_{\text{domestic}}}{C_{\text{total}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify.

$$
\text{IDR} = \left(1 - \frac{P_{\text{domestic}}}{C_{\text{total}}}\right) \times 100
$$

**Step 4:** Boundary conditions.
- If $P_{\text{domestic}} = C_{\text{total}}$, then $\text{IDR} = 0\%$ (fully self-sufficient).
- If $P_{\text{domestic}} = 0$, then $\text{IDR} = 100\%$ (fully import-dependent).

---

### 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Herfindahl-Hirschman Energy Index (HHEI)

The HHEI measures concentration in an energy mix.

**Given:** An energy mix with $N$ sources, each with share $s_i$ (in fraction, so $\sum s_i = 1$).

**Step 1:** The concentration index is defined as the sum of squared shares.

$$
\text{HHEI} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i^2
$$

**Step 2:** Minimum value of HHEI (when all sources contribute equally).

Setting all $s_i = 1/N$:

$$
\text{HHEI}_{\min} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(\frac{1}{N}\right)^2 = N \cdot \frac{1}{N^2} = \frac{1}{N}
$$

**Step 3:** Maximum value of HHEI (when a single source dominates).

Setting $s_1 = 1$ and $s_2 = s_3 = \ldots = s_N = 0$:

$$
\text{HHEI}_{\max} = 1^2 + 0 + 0 + \ldots = 1
$$

**Step 4:** The Normalized Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (NHHEI) is sometimes used:

$$
\text{NHHEI} = \frac{\text{HHEI} - 1/N}{1 - 1/N}
$$

**Step 5:** NHHEI ranges from **0** (perfectly diversified) to **1** (monopolistic).

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Energy Security Index

**Problem:** A country has the following data:
- Total energy consumption = **500 Mtoe**
- Domestic production = **150 Mtoe**
- Energy mix: Coal 60%, Oil 20%, Gas 10%, Renewables 10%
- GDP = **USD 1.5 trillion**
- Strategic reserves = **30 million tonnes of crude**

Calculate: (i) IDR, (ii) HHEI, (iii) Energy Intensity, (iv) Comment on energy security.

**Solution:**

**(i) Import Dependency Ratio:**

$$
\text{IDR} = \left(1 - \frac{150}{500}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.30) \times 100 = 70\%
$$

The country imports **70%** of its energy — high vulnerability.

**(ii) Herfindahl-Hirschman Energy Index:**

Shares: $s_1 = 0.60$, $s_2 = 0.20$, $s_3 = 0.10$, $s_4 = 0.10$

$$
\text{HHEI} = (0.60)^2 + (0.20)^2 + (0.10)^2 + (0.10)^2
$$

$$
\text{HHEI} = 0.36 + 0.04 + 0.01 + 0.01 = 0.42
$$

$$
\text{HHEI}_{\min} \,(\text{if perfectly diversified}) = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25
$$

Since 0.42 > 0.25, the energy mix is **moderately concentrated** (coal-heavy).

**(iii) Energy Intensity:**

$$
\text{EI} = \frac{500 \text{ Mtoe}}{1{,}500{,}000 \text{ million USD}} = 3.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ toe/million USD}
$$

Equivalent to approximately **0.33 toe per 1000 USD GDP** — moderate.

**(iv) Comment on Energy Security:**

The country has **low energy security** because:
- High import dependency (70%)
- Coal-dominant mix (low diversification)
- Energy intensity indicates inefficient consumption
- Strategic reserves require comparison with 90-day import rule

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Energy Security Levers vs Outcomes

| Energy Security Lever | Mechanism | Expected Outcome | Engineering Tool |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Renewable Energy Adoption | Replaces imported fossil fuel | Lower IDR, lower HHEI | Solar PV, wind turbines |
| Energy Efficiency Programs | Reduces TPEC per GDP | Lower EI | Audit, BEE ratings |
| Strategic Reserves | Stockpiles for emergencies | SRAR $\geq 1$ | Underground caverns |
| Diversification of Suppliers | Multiple import origins | Geopolitical resilience | Bilateral FTAs |
| DSM and Smart Grids | Load shifting, peak shaving | Lower peak demand | ToU tariffs, AMI |
| Nuclear Power | Baseload clean energy | Lower coal share, stable supply | EPR, SMR designs |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
