
> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The supply side feeds the infrastructure, which serves demand. Regulatory and policy blocks act as *control variables* that modulate both supply and demand. The feedback loop (dotted line) represents policy revision based on consumption patterns.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Define the term **"Energy Security"** as per the IEA framework. List the **"Four A's"** of energy security with a one-line description of each.

**Model Answer:**

Energy Security, as defined by the **International Energy Agency (IEA)**, is the *uninterrupted availability of energy sources at an affordable price*. It ensures that households, industries, and the transport sector have reliable access to energy without facing disruptions or unaffordable price shocks.

The **"Four A's"** of energy security are:

- **Availability** — Existence of sufficient primary energy resources (e.g., proven fossil reserves, renewable potential).
- **Accessibility** — Ability to extract, refine, and transport energy via robust infrastructure (pipelines, grids, ports).
- **Affordability** — Energy prices are within the purchasing power of consumers and competitive for industries.
- **Acceptability** — Energy supply aligns with environmental and social sustainability goals (low carbon, ESG compliance).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Differentiate between **Import Dependency Ratio (IDR)** and **Energy Intensity (EI)**. State the units of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Import Dependency Ratio (IDR) | Energy Intensity (EI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Fraction of total energy consumption met by net imports | Energy consumed per unit of GDP |
| **Formula** | $\left(1 - \frac{P_{\text{domestic}}}{C_{\text{total}}}\right) \times 100$ | $\frac{\text{TPEC}}{\text{GDP}}$ |
| **Unit** | Percentage ($\%$) | toe / million USD |
| **Insight** | High IDR $\Rightarrow$ vulnerable to supply shocks | High EI $\Rightarrow$ energy-inefficient economy |

IDR focuses on **supply-side vulnerability**, while EI captures **demand-side efficiency**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse the Four A's** with the three pillars of sustainability (economic, social, environmental). Memorize the *Four A's* distinctly as **Availability, Accessibility, Affordability, Acceptability**.
> - When calculating **IDR**, do not forget the multiplication by 100 to express it as a percentage.
> - In **HHEI** problems, ensure all $s_i$ values are in **fraction** (decimal), not percentage. A common mistake is plugging $s_i = 60$ instead of $0.60$, which yields erroneous results.
> - Always **state units explicitly** (e.g., Mtoe, toe, million USD) — failure to do so costs at least **1 mark** in KTU valuation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set A]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain in detail the **"Four A's" framework** of energy security. With a suitable diagram, describe how each pillar contributes to national energy resilience.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A developing country reports the following energy statistics for the year 2023:
- Total Primary Energy Consumption (TPEC) = **800 Mtoe**
- Domestic Production = **320 Mtoe**
- Energy mix: Coal 50%, Oil 25%, Natural Gas 15%, Renewables 10%
- GDP = **USD 2 trillion**

Calculate:
- (i) Import Dependency Ratio
- (ii) Herfindahl-Hirschman Energy Index (HHEI)
- (iii) Energy Intensity

Comment on the **energy security status** of the country.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four A's Framework:**

The Four A's framework is a comprehensive model used to assess energy security from multiple dimensions.

1. **Availability:** Refers to the physical existence of energy resources. For fossil fuels, this is measured using the **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio**. For renewables, it is measured by **technical potential** (e.g., solar irradiation, wind speed). A country with high availability has abundant domestic resources or reliable import contracts.

2. **Accessibility:** Refers to the capability to extract, process, and deliver energy. This includes **infrastructure** such as pipelines, refineries, ports, transmission lines, and LNG terminals. Even if resources exist, inaccessibility due to logistical or geopolitical constraints creates energy insecurity.

3. **Affordability:** Ensures that energy prices remain within the paying capacity of consumers and the competitiveness threshold of industries. High tariffs suppress demand, while excessively low tariffs discourage investment. Affordability is measured via **price-to-income ratio** and **industrial energy cost share**.

4. **Acceptability (Sustainability):** Aligns energy systems with environmental and social goals. The transition to **renewables**, compliance with **Paris Agreement** commitments, and **carbon pricing** are key. A coal-dominant economy with high emissions fails the acceptability criterion.

*Contribution to National Resilience:* The Four A's operate synergistically. Strong availability without infrastructure (accessibility) is futile. Cheap energy (affordability) from polluting sources (acceptability failure) is unsustainable.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Import Dependency Ratio:**

$$
\text{IDR} = \left(1 - \frac{P_{\text{domestic}}}{C_{\text{total}}}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{320}{800}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{IDR} = (1 - 0.40) \times 100 = 60\%
$$

**The country imports 60% of its energy requirements — high import vulnerability.**

**(ii) Herfindahl-Hirschman Energy Index:**

Shares: $s_{\text{coal}} = 0.50$, $s_{\text{oil}} = 0.25$, $s_{\text{gas}} = 0.15$, $s_{\text{RE}} = 0.10$

$$
\text{HHEI} = (0.50)^2 + (0.25)^2 + (0.15)^2 + (0.10)^2
$$

$$
\text{HHEI} = 0.25 + 0.0625 + 0.0225 + 0.01 = 0.345
$$

For a perfectly diversified 4-source mix, $\text{HHEI}_{\min} = 1/4 = 0.25$.

Since 0.345 > 0.25, the energy mix is **moderately concentrated**, with coal dominance being the main concern.

**(iii) Energy Intensity:**

$$
\text{EI} = \frac{800 \text{ Mtoe}}{2{,}000{,}000 \text{ million USD}} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ toe/million USD}
$$

Equivalent to **0.4 toe per 1000 USD of GDP**.

**Comment on Energy Security:**

- **IDR = 60%**: Indicates heavy reliance on imports — a strategic vulnerability to price shocks and supply disruptions.
- **HHEI = 0.345**: Energy mix is not well-diversified, primarily due to coal dominance.
- **EI = 0.4 toe/1000 USD**: Indicates moderate energy efficiency, with room for improvement through conservation programs.

**Overall Verdict:** The country is **moderately energy-insecure** and should focus on:
- Boosting domestic renewable capacity (especially solar)
- Improving energy efficiency in industry
- Diversifying import sources
- Building strategic reserves

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative to Question A)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set B]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **strategic measures** a nation can adopt to ensure long-term energy security. Highlight the role of **renewable energy, strategic petroleum reserves, and energy efficiency** in this context.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Define the **Shannon-Wiener Diversity Index (SWDI)**. For a country whose energy mix is: Hydro 30%, Coal 40%, Natural Gas 15%, Nuclear 10%, Solar 5%, calculate the SWDI. Comment on whether the energy mix is well-diversified.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Strategic Measures for Long-Term Energy Security:**

A nation can adopt the following strategic measures to strengthen its long-term energy security:

1. **Renewable Energy Deployment:** Solar, wind, hydro, biomass, and geothermal are **indigenous, inexhaustible, and low-carbon** sources. They reduce import dependence, hedge against fossil fuel price volatility, and support climate goals. Government incentives like **feed-in tariffs, tax rebates, and renewable purchase obligations (RPOs)** accelerate adoption.

2. **Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR):** SPRs are **stockpiles of crude oil and petroleum products** maintained to cushion against supply shocks. As per **IEA norms**, member countries must maintain reserves equivalent to **90 days of net imports**. India, for example, has SPRs at Visakhapatnam, Mangalore, and Padur.

3. **Energy Efficiency and Conservation:** Reducing energy demand through efficient appliances, industrial audits, green building codes, and BEE star ratings **decreases import dependence** without sacrificing economic output. Energy efficiency is often called the **"fifth fuel"** (after coal, oil, gas, and renewables).

4. **Diversification of Supply Sources:** Importing from multiple countries reduces vulnerability to single-supplier disruptions (e.g., the 1970s oil crisis highlighted this risk).

5. **Demand-Side Management (DSM):** Smart grids, ToU tariffs, and load shifting reduce peak demand and improve grid reliability.

6. **Diplomatic and Trade Agreements:** Long-term LNG contracts, pipeline agreements (e.g., TAPI), and bilateral energy cooperation strengthen geopolitical resilience.

7. **Nuclear Power:** Provides **baseload, low-carbon** energy, reducing coal dependence and enhancing energy security.

**(b) Shannon-Wiener Diversity Index (SWDI):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** The SWDI is an information-theoretic index that quantifies the diversity of an energy mix. Higher values indicate greater diversification and lower energy security risk.

**Formula:**

$$
\text{SWDI} = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i \cdot \ln(s_i)
$$

**Calculation:**

Given: $s_{\text{hydro}} = 0.30$, $s_{\text{coal}} = 0.40$, $s_{\text{gas}} = 0.15$, $s_{\text{nuclear}} = 0.10$, $s_{\text{solar}} = 0.05$

$$
\text{SWDI} = -[(0.30)\ln(0.30) + (0.40)\ln(0.40) + (0.15)\ln(0.15) + (0.10)\ln(0.10) + (0.05)\ln(0.05)]
$$

Computing each term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.30)\ln(0.30) &= 0.30 \times (-1.204) = -0.3612 \\
(0.40)\ln(0.40) &= 0.40 \times (-0.916) = -0.3664 \\
(0.15)\ln(0.15) &= 0.15 \times (-1.897) = -0.2846 \\
(0.10)\ln(0.10) &= 0.10 \times (-2.303) = -0.2303 \\
(0.05)\ln(0.05) &= 0.05 \times (-2.996) = -0.1498
\end{aligned}
$$

Sum of terms: $-0.3612 - 0.3664 - 0.2846 - 0.2303 - 0.1498 = -1.3923$

$$
\text{SWDI} = -(-1.3923) = 1.3923
$$

**Comment on Diversification:**

The **maximum possible SWDI** for a 5-source mix is $\ln(5) = 1.609$ (when all sources are equal at 20% each).

Since $1.3923 < 1.609$, the energy mix is **moderately diversified** but not optimal. The high share of coal (40%) and low share of solar (5%) are the main reasons for the suboptimal score. Increasing the share of solar and nuclear would improve the SWDI and reduce concentration risk.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Energy Security**

- **Definition:** Uninterrupted availability of energy at affordable prices (IEA).
- **Four A's:** Availability, Accessibility, Affordability, Acceptability (Sustainability).
- **IDR Formula:** $\left(1 - \frac{P_{\text{domestic}}}{C_{\text{total}}}\right) \times 100$ — high IDR = high vulnerability.
- **Energy Intensity:** TPEC / GDP in toe per million USD — high EI = inefficient economy.
- **HHEI:** $\sum s_i^2$ — closer to 1 = monopoly, closer to $1/N$ = perfect diversification.
- **SWDI:** $-\sum s_i \ln(s_i)$ — maximum value is $\ln(N)$.
- **IEA Rule:** Strategic reserves $\geq$ **90 days** of net imports.
- **R/P Ratio:** Proven reserves / annual production — depletion timeline in years.
- **Strategic Levers:** Renewables, SPR, energy efficiency, diversification, DSM, nuclear, trade agreements.
- **India Context:** High IDR for crude oil (>85%), coal-dominant mix, push for solar under **National Solar Mission** and **Panchamrit** targets (500 GW non-fossil by 2030).
- **Common Pitfalls:** Always express shares in fraction, always state units, never confuse IDR with EI, never confuse the Four A's with the three pillars of sustainability.
- **Key Exam Tip:** When asked to "comment on energy security," always compute **at least 2 quantitative metrics** (IDR + HHEI, or EI + SWDI) before giving a qualitative verdict.
- **Real-World Linkages:** 1973 OPEC oil crisis → SPR concept; 2022 Russia-Ukraine war → European energy diversification; India's LPG coverage → energy access dimension.
- **Engineering Linkages:** Smart grid design, microgrids, hybrid renewable systems, BEE-rated equipment, process heat integration in industries.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Conservation and its Importance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Energy Conservation and its Importance ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Conservation** is defined as the practice of reducing the quantity of energy consumed during the production, distribution, and utilization of goods and services, achieved through technological improvements, behavioural changes, managerial practices, and policy interventions, **without compromising the quality, output, or comfort** of the end-use process. In the context of the **KTU Energy Management & Conservation** framework, energy conservation is the **first pillar** of the energy management hierarchy (the others being **Energy Efficiency** and **Energy Substitution**). It is fundamentally a **demand-side management (DSM)** strategy that operates on the principle of "**doing more with less**". Mathematically, the energy conservation principle is rooted in the **First Law of Thermodynamics**: $$\text{Energy In} = \text{Energy Out} + \text{Energy Stored} \pm \text{Energy Losses}$$ For a steady-state system with no storage: $$\sum E_{in} = \sum E_{out}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (Frequently tested in KTU):** > - **Energy Conservation** = Reducing energy *use* (e.g., switching off lights, behavioural change) > - **Energy Efficiency** = Using the same energy to produce *more* output (e.g., LED replacing incandescent, same light at 1/10th the wattage) > These two terms are **NOT synonymous**, though they are often used interchangeably in popular discourse. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Leaky Bucket Principle** Imagine you have a **bucket of water** (representing your **energy budget**) with a **small hole at the bottom** (representing **wasteful losses**). Two strategies can stretch your water supply: - **Conservation**: Carry the bucket more carefully so less water spills out (i.e., reduce wasteful practices). - **Efficiency**: Patch the hole, OR use a larger cup to water the same garden with less water (i.e., get the same service with less input). **Energy conservation**, in plain English, simply means **not wasting energy**. If a room is empty, switch off the fan. If a machine is idle, turn it off. If a building is over-cooled, raise the thermostat by 1°C. These behavioural and operational choices — when aggregated across industry, transport, and households — can reduce a nation's energy demand by **20%–40%** with little to no capital investment. ### 1.3 Core Metrics and Constants | Metric | Symbol / Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | Global average energy intensity of GDP | $\sim 4.6 \text{ MJ}/\$$ (2022) | Benchmark for energy productivity | | **Tonne of Oil Equivalent** | $1 \text{ TOE} = 41.868 \text{ GJ}$ | Standard energy unit in national accounting | | India's per capita energy consumption | $\approx 0.72 \text{ TOE}$ (2023) | Far below world average of ~1.8 TOE | | **Energy payback ratio** | Output Energy / Input Energy | Measures conservation effectiveness | | Energy intensity (India 2023) | $\approx 0.22 \text{ TOE}/\text{lakh INR GDP}$ | Room for ~30% improvement via conservation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Conservation" matters for India specifically:** > India imports **~85% of its crude oil** and **~50% of its natural gas**. Every unit of energy saved is a unit of foreign exchange preserved, a kilogram of CO₂ prevented, and a megawatt of generation capacity deferred. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Energy Conservation Strategy 1. **Reduction of Losses** — Plugging waste (heat leaks, electrical $I^2R$ losses, idle running). 2. **Optimization of Operations** — Running equipment closer to its Best Efficiency Point (BEP). 3. **Behavioural & Managerial Discipline** — Awareness, SOPs, ISO 50001 audits, monitoring & targeting (M&T). ### 2.2 Theoretical Foundations #### (A) First Law Basis (Conservation of Energy) Energy can neither be created nor destroyed — only converted. Hence, any input energy that does **not** leave the system as useful work must be accounted for as loss, waste, or storage: $$\Delta U = Q - W$$ For a **control volume** at steady state: $$\dot{m}\left(h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gz_1\right) + \dot{Q}_{cv} = \dot{m}\left(h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gz_2\right) + \dot{W}_{cv}$$ #### (B) Second Law Basis (Quality Degradation) Even when energy is "conserved" by the First Law, its **exergy** (availability for useful work) is destroyed irreversibly: $$\dot{I} = T_0 \cdot \dot{S}_{gen} \geq 0$$ Conservation efforts must therefore target **exergy destruction**, not just energy quantity. #### (C) Energy Performance Index (EPI) The **Energy Performance Index** is a normalized measure of a building's or system's energy use: $$\text{EPI} = \frac{\text{Total Annual Energy Consumed (kWh or MJ)}}{\text{Conditioned Floor Area (m}^2\text{)} \times \text{Hours of Use}}$$ Lower EPI = better conservation. **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** mandates EPI $\leq 120 \text{ kWh/m}^2/\text{year}$ for commercial buildings in India. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Typical Use | |---|---|---| | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Thermal efficiency | Boilers, heat engines | | $\eta_{\text{II}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot (ideal) efficiency | Theoretical ceiling | | $\dot{Q}_{loss} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat loss through insulation | Buildings, furnaces | | $P_{loss} = I^2 R$ | Electrical resistive loss | Cables, transformers | | $\text{Saving} = E_{baseline} - E_{actual}$ | Absolute energy saving | Audits | | $\%\text{Saving} = \dfrac{E_{baseline} - E_{actual}}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | Percentage saving | Audit reports | | $\text{Simple Payback} = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Monetary Saving}}$ | Years to recover cost | Project appraisal | | $\text{EPBT} = \dfrac{E_{embodied}}{E_{annual\_saved}}$ | Energy Payback Time | Sustainability analysis | | $\text{Cost of Saved Energy} = \dfrac{\text{Annual Saving (\$)}}{\text{Lifetime Energy Saved (kWh)}}$ | $/kWh | Comparable metric | | $\text{NPV} = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t} - C_0$ | Net Present Value | Financial viability | | $\text{CO}_2\text{ avoided} = E_{saved} \times 0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{/kWh}$ | Grid emission factor (India) | Environmental impact | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Exam Tip:** In KTU valuation, when a numerical is asked, examiners specifically look for: > 1. **Correct baseline identification** (what is "saved" *from*?) > 2. **Units carried throughout** the calculation > 3. **Final answer with units and a one-line interpretation** ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Sector | Typical Conservation Measure | Typical Saving | |---|---|---| | **Industry** | Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) on pumps/fans | 20–50% motor energy | | **Buildings** | ECBC-compliant envelope, LED, BMS | 25–50% HVAC + lighting | | **Transport** | Fleet route optimization, tyre pressure | 8–15% fuel saving | | **Power Sector** | Reducing T&D losses (currently ~18% in India) | ~50 BU/year potential | | **Agriculture** | Micro-irrigation, pump retrofitting | 30–60% pumping energy | | **Residential** | 5-star appliances, star labelling | 20–40% appliance energy | ### 2.5 The Strategic Importance of Energy Conservation 1. **Economic Security** — Reduces import dependency, improves trade balance. 2. **Environmental Protection** — Mitigates climate change, air pollution, water use. 3. **Energy Access** — Reduces peak demand, postpones costly new generation. 4. **Industrial Competitiveness** — Lowers unit production cost. 5. **Social Equity** — Lowers tariffs for low-income consumers. 6. **Resource Sustainability** — Extends finite fossil fuel reserves. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Conservation in a Boiler System **Problem Statement (Typical KTU style):** A fire-tube boiler uses **200 kg of coal per hour** with a calorific value of **25 MJ/kg**. Steam is generated at the rate of **1500 kg/h at 10 bar absolute**. Feed water enters at **40 °C** and steam leaves at **saturated conditions** (specific enthalpy of saturated steam at 10 bar $\approx 2778$ kJ/kg; specific enthalpy of water at 40 °C $\approx 167.5$ kJ/kg). The stack gas losses and surface losses together account for **12%** of the input energy. Determine: 1. Boiler efficiency 2. Energy saved per hour if efficiency improves by 5 percentage points 3. CO₂ avoided per day if grid equivalent is used --- **Given Data:** - $m_{coal} = 200 \text{ kg/h}$ - $GCV = 25{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - $m_{steam} = 1500 \text{ kg/h}$ - $h_{steam} = 2778 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - $h_{feedwater} = 167.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - Combined losses $= 12\%$ of input --- **Step 1: Energy Input (Fuel Energy)** $$\dot{E}_{in} = m_{coal} \times GCV$$ $$\dot{E}_{in} = 200 \text{ kg/h} \times 25{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\dot{E}_{in} = 5{,}000{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$ --- **Step 2: Useful Energy Output (Heat gained by water/steam)** $$\dot{E}_{useful} = m_{steam} \times (h_{steam} - h_{feedwater})$$ $$\dot{E}_{useful} = 1500 \text{ kg/h} \times (2778 - 167.5) \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$\dot{E}_{useful} = 1500 \times 2610.5$$ $$\dot{E}_{useful} = 3{,}915{,}750 \text{ kJ/h}$$ --- **Step 3: Verify via Loss Account (Sanity Check)** $$\dot{E}_{loss} = 0.12 \times 5{,}000{,}000 = 600{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$ $$\dot{E}_{useful,\,expected} = 5{,}000{,}000 - 600{,}000 = 4{,}400{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$ (Note: the actual useful output from steam enthalpy is slightly less — indicating unaccounted losses like incomplete combustion, moisture in fuel, etc. The discrepancy = $4{,}400{,}000 - 3{,}915{,}750 = 484{,}250$ kJ/h = **9.7%** additional unmeasured losses.) --- **Step 4: Actual Boiler Efficiency (Measured)** $$\eta_{actual} = \frac{\dot{E}_{useful}}{\dot{E}_{in}} \times 100\%$$ $$\eta_{actual} = \frac{3{,}915{,}750}{5{,}000{,}000} \times 100\%$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{actual} = 78.32\%}$$ --- **Step 5: New Efficiency after Conservation Retrofit** $$\eta_{new} = 78.32\% + 5\% = 83.32\%$$ New useful output: $$\dot{E}_{useful,new} = 0.8332 \times 5{,}000{,}000 = 4{,}166{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$ Increase in useful energy: $$\Delta \dot{E} = 4{,}166{,}000 - 3{,}915{,}750 = 250{,}250 \text{ kJ/h}$$ In MJ: $$\Delta \dot{E} = 250.25 \text{ MJ/h}$$ Over a year (assuming 6000 operating hours): $$\Delta E_{annual} = 250.25 \times 6000 = 1{,}501{,}500 \text{ MJ/year}$$ $$\Delta E_{annual} \approx 1501.5 \text{ GJ/year} \approx 35.87 \text{ TOE/year}$$ --- **Step 6: CO₂ Avoided per Day (Grid Equivalent)** Using India's grid emission factor $\approx 0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kWh}$, but here we are in kJ/h. Converting: $$\Delta \dot{E} = 250{,}250 \text{ kJ/h} = \frac{250{,}250}{3600} \text{ kWh} = 69.51 \text{ kWh/h}$$ CO₂ avoided per hour: $$m_{CO_2,h} = 69.51 \times 0.82 = 57.0 \text{ kg/h}$$ CO₂ avoided per day (24 h operation): $$m_{CO_2,d} = 57.0 \times 24 = 1368 \text{ kg/day}$$ $$\boxed{m_{CO_2,d} \approx 1.37 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{day}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Annual Monetary Savings from a VFD Retrofit **Scenario:** A **15 kW induction motor** driving a centrifugal pump runs **5000 hours/year**. A **VFD is proposed** to reduce speed by **20%** during off-peak periods. Pump affinity laws state: $$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \qquad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2, \qquad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$ **Step 1: Baseline annual energy** $$E_1 = 15 \text{ kW} \times 5000 \text{ h} = 75{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$ **Step 2: Assume 40% of hours are at reduced speed (20% lower N)** At $N_2 / N_1 = 0.8$: $$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = (0.8)^3 = 0.512$$ **Step 3: Energy at reduced load** Reduced load power: $P_2 = 15 \times 0.512 = 7.68$ kW Reduced hours: $0.40 \times 5000 = 2000$ h $$E_2 = 7.68 \times 2000 = 15{,}360 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 4: Energy at full speed (remaining 60%)** Full-speed hours: $0.60 \times 5000 = 3000$ h $$E_3 = 15 \times 3000 = 45{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 5: Total post-retrofit energy** $$E_{new} = 15{,}360 + 45{,}000 = 60{,}360 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 6: Energy saved** $$\Delta E = 75{,}000 - 60{,}360 = 14{,}640 \text{ kWh/year}$$ Percentage saving: $$\%\text{Saving} = \frac{14{,}640}{75{,}000} \times 100 = 19.52\%$$ **Step 7: Monetary saving (tariff ₹8/kWh)** $$\text{Saving} = 14{,}640 \times 8 = ₹1{,}17{,}120/\text{year}$$ **Step 8: Cost of VFD ≈ ₹1,50,000** Simple payback: $$\text{SP} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{1{,}17{,}120} \approx 1.28 \text{ years}$$ --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Conservation Measure vs. Energy Type | Conservation Measure | Targeted Energy Form | Governing Equation | % Saving Range | |---|---|---|---| | LED retrofit | Electrical (lighting) | $E = P \cdot t$ | 50–75% | | Insulation upgrade | Thermal | $\dot{Q} = UA\Delta T$ | 20–40% | | VFD on motors | Electrical (mechanical) | $P \propto N^3$ | 20–50% | | Heat recovery | Thermal (waste heat) | $\dot{Q}_{recov} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ | 10–30% | | Power factor correction | Electrical (reactive) | $kW = kVA \cdot \cos\phi$ | 2–8% of bill | | Cogeneration | Both thermal + electrical | $\eta_{combined}$ | 15–30% fuel | | Star-rated appliances | Electrical (residential) | $E = P \cdot t \cdot n_{use}$ | 20–40% | ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Energy Management Strategy ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Energy Resources
Coal Oil Gas Renewables]:::source nodeB[Energy Conversion
Generation and Refining]:::process nodeC[Energy Distribution
Grid and Pipeline]:::process nodeD[End Use Application
Industry Building Transport]:::process nodeE[Useful Output
Work Heat Light Motion]:::output nodeF[Losses and Waste
Heat Friction Leakage]:::loss nodeG[CONSERVATION
Reduce Waste]:::cons nodeH[EFFICIENCY
Better Technology]:::eff nodeI[ALTERNATIVE
Renewable Fuel]:::alt nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE nodeD -.-> nodeF nodeG -.reduces.-> nodeF nodeH -.improves.-> nodeD nodeI -.replaces.-> nodeA classDef source fill:#FFF3B0,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef process fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#1E3A8A,color:#000 classDef output fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#1B4332,color:#000 classDef loss fill:#FFADAD,stroke:#7F1D1D,color:#000 classDef cons fill:#FFD6A5,stroke:#9A3412,color:#000 classDef eff fill:#CAFFBF,stroke:#166534,color:#000 classDef alt fill:#9BF6FF,stroke:#155E75,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Sankey-Style Flow: Energy Conservation Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR input[Energy Input
100 Units]:::source useful[Useful Work
baseline 60 Units]:::baseline loss[Wasteful Losses
40 Units]:::loss input --> useful input --> loss after[Energy Input
Same 100 Units]:::source useful2[Useful Work
AFTER 78 Units]:::afterU loss2[Wasteful Losses
22 Units]:::loss after --> useful2 after --> loss2 useful -. replaced by .-> useful2 loss -. reduced to .-> loss2 classDef source fill:#FFE066,stroke:#92400E,color:#000 classDef baseline fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#1E3A8A,color:#000 classDef afterU fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#1B4332,color:#000 classDef loss fill:#FFADAD,stroke:#7F1D1D,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Concept Map: Why Energy Conservation Matters (Multi-Dimensional) ```mermaid flowchart TD central[ENERGY CONSERVATION
THE MULTIPLIER STRATEGY]:::central econ[ECONOMIC
Import bill reduction
Lower tariffs
Industrial competitiveness]:::econ env[ENVIRONMENTAL
CO2 mitigation
Air pollution control
Water conservation]:::env soc[SOCIAL
Energy access
Job creation in MSMEs
Energy security]:::soc tech[TECHNICAL
Defer new capacity
Improve reliability
Reduce T and D losses]:::tech pol[POLICY
PAT scheme
ECBC code
Star labelling]:::pol central --> econ central --> env central --> soc central --> tech central --> pol econ <--> env env <--> soc tech <--> pol pol <--> econ classDef central fill:#FFD60A,stroke:#000,color:#000,stroke-width:3px classDef econ fill:#FFADAD,stroke:#7F1D1D,color:#000 classDef env fill:#B5E48C,stroke:#1B4332,color:#000 classDef soc fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#1E3A8A,color:#000 classDef tech fill:#FDFFB6,stroke:#854D0E,color:#000 classDef pol fill:#E0BBE4,stroke:#581C87,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Process Flow: Energy Audit Methodology (Tied to Conservation) ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA[Step 1 Walk Through Audit
Identify obvious wastes]:::p stepB[Step 2 Data Collection
Utility bills logs operating hours]:::p stepC[Step 3 Detailed Measurement
Instruments power loggers flue gas]:::p stepD[Step 4 Baseline Establishment
EPI per unit per kWh]:::p stepE[Step 5 Identify Conservation Opportunities ENCONs]:::p stepF[Step 6 Economic Evaluation
NPV IRR Payback]:::p stepG[Step 7 Implementation Monitoring Targeting]:::p stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG stepG -.continuous M and T.-> stepC classDef p fill:#DDEEFF,stroke:#1E3A8A,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Define energy conservation. Distinguish between energy conservation and energy efficiency with one example each. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Conservation** is the practice of reducing the amount of energy consumed by turning off equipment, optimizing operations, and eliminating waste, without altering the end-use service. **Distinction:** | Aspect | Energy Conservation | Energy Efficiency | |---|---|---| | Approach | Demand reduction | Technology upgrade | | Means | Behavioural + operational | Equipment/process redesign | | Example | Switching off lights when leaving a room | Replacing a 60 W incandescent bulb with a 9 W LED bulb producing the same light output | | Investment | Usually nil / very low | Moderate to high capital | In conservation, the **activity is reduced**; in efficiency, the **same activity uses less energy**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **List any six strategic benefits of energy conservation for a developing nation like India. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Reduction in fossil fuel imports** — India imports ~85% of crude oil and ~50% of natural gas; conservation eases forex outflow. 2. **Mitigation of CO₂ emissions** — Direct climate-change benefit. 3. **Improved industrial competitiveness** — Lower unit cost of production. 4. **Deferral of new power generation capacity** — Saves ₹5–8 Cr per MW deferred. 5. **Reduced T&D losses** — By lowering peak demand. 6. **Social upliftment** — Lower tariffs for low-income households; rural electrification sustainability. --- ### Part B Question (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 typically offers two 14-mark alternatives. Students answer ONE. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 / Model Paper Adapted]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **First Law** and **Second Law** of thermodynamics in the context of energy conservation. Show how the **concept of exergy destruction** guides high-quality conservation efforts. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A textile mill consumes **8,00,000 kWh/year** at a tariff of **₹7.50/kWh**. After an energy audit, the mill replaces its old motors with **IE4 premium efficiency motors** consuming **6,40,000 kWh/year**. The retrofit cost is **₹18,00,000**. Compute: - (i) Annual monetary saving - (ii) Percentage energy saving - (iii) Simple payback period - (iv) Annual CO₂ avoided (use 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh) **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(a): The **First Law of Thermodynamics** states that energy cannot be created or destroyed — only transformed. $$\Delta E_{system} = Q - W$$ For a steady-state system: $E_{in} = E_{out}$. **Application in conservation**: Every unit of input energy must reappear as useful output or loss. Conservation aims to **maximize the useful output fraction** (efficiency). The **Second Law** introduces entropy: $$\Delta S_{universe} = \Delta S_{system} + \Delta S_{surroundings} \geq 0$$ Energy has both **quantity** (First Law) and **quality** — its ability to do work, quantified as **exergy**. $$Ex = \dot{m} \left[\left(h - h_0\right) - T_0\left(s - s_0\right)\right]$$ **Exergy destruction** $I$ due to irreversibility: $$I = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$ A pure-quantity (First Law) view may consider "low-grade" heat at 50 °C as "wasted energy," but a Second-Law / exergy view shows that some of it can still drive an absorption chiller or preheat feedwater. Thus, **Second-Law-aware conservation** prioritizes: - Reducing high-exergy destruction (combustion irreversibilities). - Cascading waste heat to lower-temperature users. - Avoiding over-engineered temperature differences. This dual perspective makes conservation efforts more **thermodynamically rational** and cost-effective. --- #### Model Solution to Q.A(b): **Given:** - $E_1 = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$ (baseline) - $E_2 = 6{,}40{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$ (after retrofit) - Tariff = ₹7.50/kWh - Investment $C_0 = ₹18{,}00{,}000$ - Emission factor = $0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kWh}$ **(i) Annual monetary saving:** $$\Delta E = 8{,}00{,}000 - 6{,}40{,}000 = 1{,}60{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$ $$\text{Saving} = 1{,}60{,}000 \times 7.50 = ₹12{,}00{,}000/\text{year}$$ **(ii) Percentage energy saving:** $$\%\text{Saving} = \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{8{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 20\%$$ **(iii) Simple payback:** $$SP = \frac{18{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} = 1.5 \text{ years}$$ **(iv) Annual CO₂ avoided:** $$m_{CO_2} = 1{,}60{,}000 \times 0.82 = 1{,}31{,}200 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year}$$ $$\boxed{m_{CO_2} = 131.2 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year avoided}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative** **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 / Model Paper Adapted]** **(a)** Discuss the **strategic importance of energy conservation** in the Indian context, covering economic, environmental, social, and energy-security dimensions. Mention at least **two flagship national initiatives** promoting conservation. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A compressed air system has a **30 kW compressor** running **6000 hours/year**. Leakage survey reveals **18% leakage**. If leaks are fixed, compute: - (i) Annual kWh saved - (ii) Annual ₹ saved at ₹6.50/kWh - (iii) CO₂ reduction in tonnes/year - (iv) If the leak repair cost is ₹65,000, what is the simple payback? **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(a): **Energy conservation is a strategic imperative for India** for the following reasons: **1. Economic Dimension:** India's energy import bill exceeds **$160 billion/year (2023)**. Every unit saved reduces pressure on the rupee and current account deficit. Conservation also lowers the unit cost of production, boosting export competitiveness of MSMEs. **2. Environmental Dimension:** India is the **world's third-largest CO₂ emitter**. Power generation, transport, and industry together account for ~75% of emissions. Conservation directly avoids Scope-2 emissions without requiring new technology deployment. **3. Social Dimension:** Lower aggregate demand translates to **lower tariffs**, benefiting low-income households. Programs like **UJALA (LED distribution)** have saved 9,500 MW of peak demand and ₹50,000 crore in consumer bills. **4. Energy Security:** Conservation **defers the need for new generation capacity** (~₹6 Cr/MW), reduces exposure to volatile global fuel prices, and lowers strategic dependence on the Strait of Hormuz oil route. **National Initiatives:** - **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT)** scheme — a market-based mechanism for industries to meet energy-efficiency targets and trade Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts). - **Standards & Labelling Programme** — mandates star-rating for appliances (AC, fan, refrigerator) guiding consumer choice toward efficient products. - **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** — sets EPI limits for commercial buildings. - **UJALA & SLNP** (Street Lighting National Programme) — direct demand-side conservation. - **National Mission for Enhanced Energy Efficiency (NMEEE)** — under the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC). --- #### Model Solution to Q.B(b): **Given:** - Compressor power $P = 30 \text{ kW}$ - Annual hours $t = 6000 \text{ h}$ - Leakage fraction = 18% - Tariff = ₹6.50/kWh - Repair cost = ₹65,000 **(i) Annual energy saved:** Leakage power = $0.18 \times 30 = 5.4$ kW $$\Delta E = 5.4 \times 6000 = 32{,}400 \text{ kWh/year}$$ **(ii) Annual monetary saving:** $$\text{Saving} = 32{,}400 \times 6.50 = ₹2{,}10{,}600/\text{year}$$ **(iii) CO₂ avoided:** $$m_{CO_2} = 32{,}400 \times 0.82 = 26{,}568 \text{ kg} \approx 26.57 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}$$ **(iv) Simple payback:** $$SP = \frac{65{,}000}{2{,}10{,}600} \approx 0.309 \text{ years}$$ $$\boxed{SP \approx 3.7 \text{ months}}$$ --- ### KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Energy Conservation Questions:** > 1. **Confusing conservation with efficiency** — Examiners deduct 1 mark if these terms are used interchangeably. Always state the distinction first. > 2. **Wrong baseline** — Many students compute "% saving" using post-retrofit value instead of baseline. Always use: $(E_{baseline} - E_{new})/E_{baseline}$. > 3. **Missing units in numerical answers** — Every number MUST have units (kWh, ₹, tonnes CO₂). > 4. **Ignoring the "strategic" angle** — A 7-mark question on importance expects **multi-dimensional analysis** (economic, environmental, social). One-line answers fetch only 2–3 marks. > 5. **No mention of national policy/programme** — Always anchor your answers in **PAT, ECBC, BEE, NMEEE, UJALA** — these are examiner-favourite triggers for "extra credit." > 6. **Not showing the energy-balance check** — For boiler/numerical problems, examiners love a sanity-check step (input = useful + losses). Skipping it may cost 1 mark. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Energy Conservation & Its Importance** - **Definition:** Reducing energy use without sacrificing end-use service. - **Conservation ≠ Efficiency:** Conservation = "use less"; Efficiency = "use better." - **First Law basis:** $E_{in} = E_{out}$ — every joule must be accounted for. - **Second Law basis:** High-exergy destruction = high priority for conservation. - **Three pillars of strategy:** Reduce losses, optimize operations, behavioural discipline. - **India-specific drivers:** 85% oil import dependency, low per-capita energy, huge T&D losses (~18%), large industrial demand. - **Key policy instruments:** PAT scheme, ECBC, BEE Star Labelling, UJALA, NMEEE. - **Key metrics:** - $\eta = W_{out}/Q_{in}$ - % Saving = $(E_{base} - E_{new})/E_{base} \times 100$ - Simple Payback = Investment / Annual Saving - EPI = kWh/m²/year - **Units to memorize:** - 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ - Grid emission factor (India) ≈ 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh - **Typical conservation savings by sector:** Industry 20–30%, Buildings 25–50%, Transport 10–15%, Agriculture 30–60%. - **High-yield exam keywords:** "Exergy," "Demand-side management," "Energy intensity," "Energy Performance Index," "MONETARY saving," "CO₂ avoidance." - **Common numerical traps:** Always subtract from baseline, always carry units, always interpret result in one sentence. - **Always cite policy** in long-answer questions on "importance" — it's a sure-shot 1-mark differentiator.

topics.title: Energy Strategy for the Future
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Strategy for the Future

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Strategy for the Future** is a comprehensive, multi-decade, multi-sectoral roadmap that integrates the **three intertwined pillars of energy policy** — *Energy Security, Energy Equity (Affordability/Access),* and *Environmental Sustainability* — to guide a nation, region, or organization from its present energy mix toward a low-carbon, resilient, and economically viable energy future.

The strategy is operationalized through:

- **Demand-side levers**: efficiency improvements, demand response, electrification, behavioural change.
- **Supply-side levers**: scaling renewable energy (RE), modernizing grids, deploying storage, hydrogen, nuclear, and bio-energy.
- **Enabling frameworks**: policy, regulation, finance, R&D, international cooperation, and market design.

Formally, the IEA (International Energy Agency) frames a future energy strategy around the **"Energy Trifecta"**:

$$
\text{Future Energy Strategy} = f(\text{Security},\ \text{Affordability},\ \text{Sustainability})
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Family's 30-Year Financial Plan**
>
> Imagine a family living in a house powered by an old diesel generator (dirty, expensive, unreliable). The father sits down to draft a 30-year plan:
> 1. **Year 1–5:** Install solar panels on the roof and LED bulbs (efficiency + renewables).
> 2. **Year 5–10:** Add battery storage to ride through cloudy days and night-time.
> 3. **Year 10–20:** Replace the diesel car with an EV, and switch the cooking stove to PNG/biogas (electrification + cleaner fuels).
> 4. **Year 20–30:** Add a hydrogen fuel-cell backup and sell surplus solar power back to the grid (prosumer + emerging tech).
>
> This family plan **is** an energy strategy for the future. A nation does the same thing — but on a much larger scale, balancing cost, reliability, and the planet's health.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of an Energy Strategy

| Pillar | Core Question | Typical Metric |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Energy Security** | Can we keep the lights on without depending on hostile or unstable suppliers? | Import dependency (%) |
| **Energy Equity / Affordability** | Can every household and industry access modern energy affordably? | Energy poverty (%) |
| **Environmental Sustainability** | Are emissions, local pollutants, and ecological damage within planetary limits? | CO₂ intensity (kg CO₂ / kWh) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme PESFT414 syllabus places this topic at the very start of Module 1 because every subsequent module (auditing, HVAC, lighting, motors, financial analysis) is built on the **strategic context** of *why* energy must be managed and conserved in the first place.

## 1.4 The Global Context — Why This Topic Exists Now

- World primary energy demand in **2023** was approximately **620 EJ** (exajoules), with fossil fuels supplying **~81 %**.
- The **Paris Agreement (2015)** committed signatory nations to limit global warming to **well below 2 °C**, preferably **1.5 °C**, above pre-industrial levels.
- **COP28 (Dubai, 2023)** explicitly called for *tripling global renewable capacity* and *doubling energy efficiency improvements* by **2030**.
- India's commitments under **Panchamrit** (Glasgow, 2021):
  - **500 GW** of non-fossil installed capacity by **2030**.
  - **50 %** of energy from non-fossil sources by **2030**.
  - Net-Zero by **2070**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Net Zero:**
> Net Zero means anthropogenic (human-caused) CO₂ emissions are balanced by CO₂ removals over a specified period. It is **not** zero emissions — it is a balance.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in Energy Strategy (Defined Boldly)

- **Primary Energy Supply (PES):** Total energy consumed in original forms before any conversion (coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind, uranium). Unit: **EJ** or **Mtoe**.
- **Final Energy Consumption (FEC):** Energy delivered to end-use sectors (residential, industrial, transport, agriculture). Unit: **EJ** or **Mtoe**.
- **Energy Intensity (EI):** Primary energy consumed per unit of GDP.
  $$\text{EI} = \frac{\text{Primary Energy Supply (MJ)}}{\text{GDP (USD or INR)}}$$
  Unit: **MJ/USD** or **toe/₹ crore**.
- **Carbon Intensity (CI):** CO₂ emitted per unit of primary energy.
  $$\text{CI} = \frac{\text{CO}_2 \text{ emitted (kg)}}{\text{Primary Energy (MJ)}}$$
  Unit: **kg CO₂ / MJ** or **t CO₂ / toe**.
- **Per-Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC):**
  $$\text{PCEC} = \frac{\text{Total Primary Energy}}{\text{Population}}$$
  Unit: **GJ/person** or **kWh/person**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Layer Cake of an Energy Future

An energy strategy is best understood as a **five-layer architecture**. Each layer is built on the one below; weakness in any layer collapses the layers above.

### Layer 1 — Resource Endowment
What does the country/region physically have?

- *India's basket:* coal (≈ 4th largest reserves globally), solar irradiance ≈ 4–7 kWh/m²/day across most of the peninsula, wind (especially in Tamil Nadu, Gujarat, Rajasthan, Kerala's Western Ghats), hydro potential ≈ 145 GW (technically exploitable), thorium reserves (≈ 25 % of world) supporting a 3-stage nuclear programme, agricultural residues for biomass.

### Layer 2 — Demand Profile
How is energy used and by whom?

- India's sectoral energy mix (typical, ≈ 2022): **Industry ~ 55 %**, **Transport ~ 15 %**, **Residential ~ 12 %**, **Agriculture ~ 5 %**, **Commercial ~ 5 %**, **Others ~ 8 %**.
- Per-capita electricity consumption in India: **≈ 1,300 kWh/person** (2023) — about **one-third** of the world average and **one-tenth** of the USA. Aspiration level: **2,500–3,000 kWh/person** by 2047 (India @ 100).

### Layer 3 — Technology Stack
What conversion, storage, and end-use technologies are deployable?

- **Generation:** utility-scale solar PV, onshore/offshore wind, CSP, small hydro, biomass, geothermal, nuclear (PWR, PHWR, SMRs), green hydrogen.
- **Storage:** pumped hydro, Li-ion batteries, flow batteries, CAES, hydrogen (as a chemical carrier).
- **End-use:** EVs, heat pumps, induction cooking, electric arc furnaces, green ammonia, electrified industrial process heat.

### Layer 4 — Infrastructure
- Smart grids, microgrids, EV charging corridors, gas pipelines, hydrogen pipelines, carbon capture & storage (CCS) hubs, trans-national interconnections (e.g., the **One Sun One World One Grid** — OSOWOG vision).

### Layer 5 — Policy, Market & Finance
- **Policy:** NEP (National Energy Policy), NDC (Nationally Determined Contribution), state policies (e.g., Kerala State Energy Policy 2023).
- **Markets:** Day-ahead, real-time, ancillary services, green open-access rules, carbon markets.
- **Finance:** Viability Gap Funding, Production-Linked Incentives (PLI), green bonds, sovereign climate funds, blended finance.

## 2.2 The Strategic Time Horizons

| Horizon | Span | Strategic Theme | Indian Anchor |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Short term** | 0–5 yrs | Efficiency first, capacity addition, financial health of DISCOMs | PAT scheme, UJALA, Saubhagya |
| **Medium term** | 5–15 yrs | Renewable scale-up, storage, grid modernization, EV penetration | 500 GW non-fossil by 2030 |
| **Long term** | 15–30 yrs | Deep decarbonization, hydrogen economy, sectoral electrification | Net-Zero by 2070 |
| **Visionary** | 30+ yrs | Fusion, space-based solar, circular carbon economy | Beyond 2070 |

## 2.3 Strategic Pillars in Detail

### Pillar A — Energy Security
The strategic imperative is to **reduce import dependence**. India's import bill for crude oil and LNG is a major forex drain. Strategic actions:

- Diversifying supply sources.
- Strategic Petroleum Reserves (India already operates ≈ 5.33 MMT SPR at Vizag, Mangalore, Padur).
- Indigenization of RE supply chains (PLI for solar PV modules, electrolyser manufacturing).
- Energy independence through domestic RE.

### Pillar B — Energy Affordability & Equity
- 24 × 7 Power for All (achieved in letter, gap in quality).
- Saubhagya scheme: 100 % household electrification.
- Ujjwala: LPG to BPL households.
- Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) for LPG.
- PM-KUSUM: solarization of agricultural pumps.

### Pillar C — Environmental Sustainability
- NDC targets: 45 % reduction in emissions intensity of GDP by 2030 (vs 2005).
- 50 % cumulative electric power from non-fossil sources by 2030.
- Net Zero by 2070.
- Mission LiFE (Lifestyle for Environment) for behavioural change.

## 2.4 The Energy Transition Pathway

The classic **3Ds + E** framework (sometimes called the *Avoid–Shift–Improve* approach in transport) underpins the strategy:

1. **Decarbonize** the supply side: replace coal with renewables + storage + nuclear.
2. **Digitalize** the grid: smart meters, IoT, AI-driven demand forecasting.
3. **Decentralize** generation: rooftop solar, microgrids, peer-to-peer energy trading.
4. **Electrify** end-uses: EVs, heat pumps, induction, electric boilers.
5. *(Implicit fifth D)* — **Diversify** fuels: green hydrogen, sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), green ammonia for shipping.

## 2.5 Future Energy Sources — The Strategic Portfolio

| Source | Strategic Role | Maturity | Key Limitation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Solar PV** | Workhorse of new capacity | Mature, cost-competitive | Diurnal, land use |
| **Onshore Wind** | Bulk power | Mature | Site availability |
| **Offshore Wind** | Coastal states (Gujarat, TN, Kerala) | Emerging | CapEx, marine logistics |
| **Green Hydrogen** | Hard-to-abate sectors (steel, ammonia, shipping) | Pilot → scale-up | Cost, electrolyser efficiency |
| **Nuclear (SMRs)** | Baseload clean power | Re-emerging | Capital cost, public acceptance |
| **Pumped Storage** | Bulk storage | Mature | Site specificity |
| **Battery Storage (Li-ion)** | Short-duration balancing | Mature, fast-growing | Cycle life, raw materials |
| **Bioenergy + CCUS** | Carbon-neutral liquid fuels | Developing | Land competition |
| **Fusion (ITER, DEMO)** | Long-horizon (2050+) | R&D | TRL still low |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **only** quantitative formulas a KTU student must memorize for this topic. This is a largely qualitative subject, but the few metrics below appear in both Part A and Part B questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI Unit | Strategic Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Energy Intensity (EI) | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{\text{Primary Energy}}{\text{GDP}}$ | $\text{MJ/USD}$ | Tracks decoupling of growth from energy |
| 2 | Carbon Intensity (CI) | $\text{CI} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2}{\text{Primary Energy}}$ | $\text{kg CO}_2 / \text{MJ}$ | Tracks decarbonization of supply |
| 3 | Per-Capita Energy | $\text{PCEC} = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy}}{\text{Population}}$ | $\text{GJ/person}$ | Measures energy equity |
| 4 | Import Dependency | $\text{ID} = \dfrac{\text{Imports}}{\text{Total PES}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | Measures security |
| 5 | Renewable Share | $\text{RS} = \dfrac{\text{RE Generation}}{\text{Total Generation}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | Tracks transition |
| 6 | Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) | $\text{CAGR} = \left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | $\text{yr}^{-1}$ | Projects future demand |
| 7 | Simple Payback | $\text{SP} = \dfrac{\text{CapEx}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | $\text{years}$ | Used in clean-energy projects |
| 8 | Capacity Factor | $\text{CF} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Energy}}{\text{Installed Cap} \times 8760}$ | $\text{fraction}$ | Benchmarks generation techs |
| 9 | Specific CO₂ Emission | $\text{SCE} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2}{\text{Generation (kWh)}}$ | $\text{kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$ | Per-unit emissions |
| 10 | Energy Return on Investment (EROI) | $\text{EROI} = \dfrac{\text{Useful Energy Delivered}}{\text{Energy Invested}}$ | dimensionless | Long-term sustainability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention used in this sheet:** The vertical bar symbol for absolute value is rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking Markdown tables. In exam answer scripts, students may use the standard `|x|` notation freely.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Framework

- **Power-system planners** (e.g., CEA, POSOCO/Grid-India) use the EI, CI, and CAGR formulas to project 2030/2047 demand and design the optimal generation mix.
- **Industrial energy managers** use the renewable share and SCE metrics to set company-level ESG targets.
- **Investors** use payback, CF, and EROI to compare clean-energy assets (solar farm vs. wind farm vs. green-hydrogen plant).
- **State DISCOMs** (KSEB in Kerala) use the import-dependency metric to plan for 100 % self-reliance targets.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This topic is largely qualitative, but a small set of *quantitative derivations* appears in KTU Part B questions. Each derivation below is exhaustively worked out, step by step, so a student can reproduce it on an exam script.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Energy Intensity Reduction Trajectory

**Problem Statement (model KTU-style):**
India's primary energy supply in 2005 was **21.4 EJ**, and its GDP (constant 2015 USD PPP) was **₹ 105 lakh crore** equivalent. In 2023, the primary energy supply is **40 EJ** and GDP is **₹ 232 lakh crore**. Calculate:

- (a) The energy intensity in 2005 and 2023.
- (b) The percentage reduction in energy intensity.
- (c) The annual rate of decoupling (CAGR of EI).

### Step 1 — Convert to common units

We will use MJ and USD-equivalent. For simplicity in a KTU answer, we keep units in **EJ / ₹ lakh crore** because the ratio is what matters.

### Step 2 — Compute EI for 2005

$$
\text{EI}_{2005} = \frac{21.4 \text{ EJ}}{105 \text{ lakh crore}}
$$

Expressing EJ in **MJ**: $1 \text{ EJ} = 10^{18} \text{ J} = 10^{12} \text{ MJ}$.

$$
\text{EI}_{2005} = \frac{21.4 \times 10^{12} \text{ MJ}}{105 \text{ lakh crore}}
$$

$$
\text{EI}_{2005} = 0.2038 \times 10^{12} \text{ MJ / lakh crore}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{EI}_{2005} \approx 2.038 \times 10^{11} \text{ MJ / lakh crore}}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute EI for 2023

$$
\text{EI}_{2023} = \frac{40 \times 10^{12} \text{ MJ}}{232 \text{ lakh crore}}
$$

$$
\text{EI}_{2023} = 0.1724 \times 10^{12} \text{ MJ / lakh crore}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{EI}_{2023} \approx 1.724 \times 10^{11} \text{ MJ / lakh crore}}
$$

### Step 4 — Percentage Reduction

$$
\Delta \text{EI} \,(\%) = \frac{\text{EI}_{2005} - \text{EI}_{2023}}{\text{EI}_{2005}} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta \text{EI} \,(\%) = \frac{2.038 - 1.724}{2.038} \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta \text{EI} \,(\%) = \frac{0.314}{2.038} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta \text{EI} \approx 15.4 \,\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** Over 18 years, India produced 15.4 % less energy per unit of GDP — i.e., its economy has **decoupled modestly** from energy use.

### Step 5 — CAGR of EI

$$
\text{CAGR}_{\text{EI}} = \left(\frac{\text{EI}_{2023}}{\text{EI}_{2005}}\right)^{1/n} - 1
$$

where $n = 18$ years.

$$
\text{CAGR}_{\text{EI}} = \left(\frac{1.724}{2.038}\right)^{1/18} - 1
$$

$$
\text{CAGR}_{\text{EI}} = (0.8459)^{0.0556} - 1
$$

Taking the natural-log route for clarity:

$$
\ln(0.8459) = -0.1674
$$

$$
-0.1674 \times 0.0556 = -0.00930
$$

$$
e^{-0.00930} - 1 = 0.9907 - 1 = -0.0093
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CAGR}_{\text{EI}} \approx -0.93 \,\%/\text{yr}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Energy intensity has been falling at an average of **0.93 % per year** — much slower than the **~3 %/yr** rate that experts say is required to stay on a 2 °C-compatible trajectory.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — Renewable Share Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A state has an installed capacity of **10,000 MW**: Thermal 5,500 MW, Hydro 1,500 MW, Solar 2,000 MW, Wind 800 MW, Biomass 200 MW. Annual capacity factors: Thermal 0.55, Hydro 0.40, Solar 0.20, Wind 0.30, Biomass 0.65. Calculate the renewable share in generation.

### Step 1 — Annual energy from each source

Thermal:

$$
E_{\text{th}} = 5{,}500 \times 0.55 \times 8{,}760 = 26{,}499{,}000 \text{ MWh}
$$

Hydro:

$$
E_{\text{hy}} = 1{,}500 \times 0.40 \times 8{,}760 = 5{,}256{,}000 \text{ MWh}
$$

Solar:

$$
E_{\text{so}} = 2{,}000 \times 0.20 \times 8{,}760 = 3{,}504{,}000 \text{ MWh}
$$

Wind:

$$
E_{\text{wi}} = 800 \times 0.30 \times 8{,}760 = 2{,}102{,}400 \text{ MWh}
$$

Biomass:

$$
E_{\text{bm}} = 200 \times 0.65 \times 8{,}760 = 1{,}138{,}800 \text{ MWh}
$$

### Step 2 — Total generation

$$
E_{\text{total}} = 26{,}499{,}000 + 5{,}256{,}000 + 3{,}504{,}000 + 2{,}102{,}400 + 1{,}138{,}800
$$

$$
E_{\text{total}} = 38{,}500{,}200 \text{ MWh}
$$

### Step 3 — Total renewable generation (hydro + solar + wind + biomass)

$$
E_{\text{RE}} = 5{,}256{,}000 + 3{,}504{,}000 + 2{,}102{,}400 + 1{,}138{,}800
$$

$$
E_{\text{RE}} = 12{,}001{,}200 \text{ MWh}
$$

### Step 4 — Renewable share

$$
\text{RS} = \frac{12{,}001{,}200}{38{,}500{,}200} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{RS} \approx 31.2 \,\%}
$$

**Interpretation:** A state can be **non-fossil** in capacity (hydro + solar + wind + biomass = 4,500 MW = 45 %) but produce a much smaller share of actual energy because solar and wind have low CFs. Capacity ≠ Generation — this is a classic KTU trap question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — Projection of Primary Energy Using CAGR

**Problem Statement:** India's primary energy supply in 2023 is 40 EJ. Assuming a CAGR of **3.0 %/yr**, project the PES in 2030 and 2047. If NDC target is to keep EI constant, what must be the GDP CAGR?

### Step 1 — Project 2030 PES (n = 7)

$$
\text{PES}_{2030} = \text{PES}_{2023} \times (1.03)^7
$$

$$
(1.03)^7 = e^{7 \ln 1.03} = e^{7 \times 0.02956} = e^{0.2069} = 1.2299
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PES}_{2030} = 40 \times 1.2299 \approx 49.2 \text{ EJ}}
$$

### Step 2 — Project 2047 PES (n = 24)

$$
\text{PES}_{2047} = 40 \times (1.03)^{24}
$$

$$
(1.03)^{24} = e^{24 \times 0.02956} = e^{0.7095} = 2.033
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PES}_{2047} \approx 81.3 \text{ EJ}}
$$

### Step 3 — For EI to remain constant, GDP must grow at the same CAGR

$$
\boxed{\text{GDP CAGR} = 3.0 \,\%/\text{yr}}
$$

**Interpretation:** If India's energy demand doubles by 2047, its GDP must also double to maintain the *same* carbon/energy footprint. Achieving that requires **aggressive efficiency + renewables** so that PES grows slower than 3 %/yr — closer to **2 %/yr** — while GDP grows at **6–7 %/yr**, forcing EI to drop.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Strategic Options

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **comparison matrix** is the single most-asked table-type question in KTU Part A for this topic. Memorize it.

| Strategic Lever | Primary Energy Effect | Cost | Speed of Deployment | Co-Benefits |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Energy Efficiency (EE)** | Large negative (down) | Negative cost (saves money) | Immediate | Air quality, jobs, energy security |
| **Renewable Energy (RE)** | Zero / negative (substitutes fossil) | Moderate capex, zero fuel | 2–5 yrs | Decentralization, water savings |
| **Carbon Capture (CCS)** | Zero on supply; reduces emissions | Very high | 10+ yrs | Allows continued fossil use |
| **Hydrogen Switching** | Reduces fossil in industry | High | 5–10 yrs | Decarbonizes hard-to-abate |
| **Electrification + EV** | Shifts to grid (which may be cleaner) | Moderate–high | 5–15 yrs | Local air quality, urban noise |
| **Behavioural / Lifestyle** | Modest reduction | Very low | Immediate | Mission LiFE, low equity risk |

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — Decarbonization Math for a 1-GW Coal Plant

**Problem Statement:** A 1,000 MW coal plant operates at CF 0.85 with a specific emission of **0.95 t CO₂ / MWh**. Calculate annual CO₂ emissions and the offset required by solar to neutralize it.

### Step 1 — Annual generation

$$
E = 1{,}000 \times 0.85 \times 8{,}760 = 7{,}446{,}000 \text{ MWh/yr}
$$

### Step 2 — Annual CO₂

$$
\text{CO}_2 = 7{,}446{,}000 \times 0.95 = 7{,}073{,}700 \text{ t/yr} \approx 7.07 \text{ Mt/yr}
$$

### Step 3 — Solar offset (assume CF 0.20)

$$
\text{Solar MW needed} = \frac{1{,}000 \times 0.85}{0.20} = 4{,}250 \text{ MW}
$$

**Interpretation:** To neutralize a single 1 GW coal plant, you need **≈ 4.25 GW of solar PV** because of the lower capacity factor — a powerful insight for KTU exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
