# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The question bank below mirrors the **KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern: Part A short answers (3 marks each) and Part B long answers (14 marks each, with internal choice between two full questions).

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Energy Strategy for the Future". List its three pillars.**

**Model Answer:**

Energy Strategy for the Future is a long-term, multi-sectoral roadmap designed to balance energy security, energy equity (affordability and access), and environmental sustainability. Its three pillars are:

1. **Energy Security** — uninterrupted availability of energy at stable prices.
2. **Energy Equity** — affordable access to modern energy for all citizens.
3. **Environmental Sustainability** — energy services delivered within planetary ecological limits.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is "Energy Intensity"? Why is its reduction considered a strategic indicator of decoupling?**

**Model Answer:**

Energy Intensity (EI) is the ratio of primary energy consumed to GDP generated. Its unit is MJ/USD (or toe/₹ crore). A falling EI over time indicates that the economy is producing more goods and services per unit of energy — i.e., *economic growth is decoupling from energy consumption*. This decoupling is strategically vital because it allows GDP to grow while energy demand (and therefore emissions and imports) grows more slowly. For example, India's EI fell by ~15 % between 2005 and 2023, indicating a modest decoupling.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Choice-1]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss in detail the **three pillars** of an energy strategy for the future. Illustrate with Indian examples for each pillar.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat sketches/flowcharts, describe the **3D + E framework** of energy transition and explain how India is applying it.

#### Model Solution

**(a) The Three Pillars — Indian Context**

- **Energy Security:**
  - India imports ≈ 85 % of its crude oil, ≈ 50 % of its natural gas, and ≈ 25 % of its coal.
  - Strategic actions: setting up Strategic Petroleum Reserves (5.33 MMT at Vizag, Mangalore, Padur), diversifying suppliers (Russia, USA, West Asia), indigenous manufacturing of solar modules and electrolyzers under PLI schemes, and the National Solar Mission to substitute imported coal.
  - Outcome target: reduce import dependence to less than 50 % by 2047.

- **Energy Equity:**
  - Saubhagya scheme provided free electricity connections to **≈ 2.86 crore** un-electrified households.
  - Ujjwala distributed **≈ 10 crore** LPG connections to BPL families, replacing wood/dung.
  - PM-KUSUM solarizes agricultural pumps, slashing farmer electricity bills.
  - KSEB (Kerala) has achieved near 100 % household electrification and is now focusing on quality and affordability.

- **Environmental Sustainability:**
  - India is the only G20 nation on track to meet its 2 °C-compatible NDC.
  - Renewable installed capacity (≈ 190 GW as of mid-2024) is 43 % of total.
  - National Hydrogen Mission with a target of **5 MMT green H₂ by 2030**.
  - Net-Zero target by 2070, codified in the Long-Term Low Emission Development Strategy (LT-LEDS) submitted to the UNFCCC.

**(b) The 3D + E Framework Applied to India**

- **Decarbonize the supply** — India is on track for 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030. Major tenders for RE + storage hybrids; nuclear capacity to grow from 8 GW to 22 GW by 2031.
- **Digitalize the grid** — Smart Meter National Programme (250 million meters), Green Open Access Rules 2022, time-of-day tariffs, AI-based load forecasting by Grid-India.
- **Decentralize generation** — PM-Surya Ghar Yojana (4 crore rooftop solar), KSEB's ANERT rooftop push, peer-to-peer energy trading regulations.
- **Electrify end-uses** — FAME-II and PM E-Drive for EVs, induction cooking under Saubhagya, electric arc furnaces in steel, heat pumps in buildings, electric boilers in process industries.

A flowchart showing these four parallel streams converging on the future clean-energy system is expected in the answer script (see SECTION 4.2).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 1 Choice-2]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **role of renewable energy, green hydrogen, and energy storage** in India's energy strategy for the future. Mention the relevant national missions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A state has installed capacity: Thermal 6,000 MW, Hydro 1,800 MW, Solar 3,000 MW, Wind 1,200 MW, Biomass 300 MW. Capacity factors: Thermal 0.55, Hydro 0.40, Solar 0.22, Wind 0.32, Biomass 0.65. Calculate (i) annual generation from each source, (ii) total generation, and (iii) renewable share. Comment on the result.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Role of Key Strategic Levers**

- **Renewable Energy (RE):** India targets 500 GW non-fossil by 2030. National Solar Mission (since 2010), National Wind-Solar Hybrid Policy, and PM-KUSUM support utility-scale and distributed RE. RE is the *workhorse* of the strategy because of falling LCOE (₹ 2.5/kWh for solar in 2024).

- **Green Hydrogen:** Under the **National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023)**, India targets 5 MMT/year production by 2030, with a budget of ₹ 19,744 crore. Green hydrogen is essential for *hard-to-abate* sectors — steel (replacing coking coal), ammonia (replacing grey H₂), refining, shipping (as NH₃), and long-duration storage.

- **Energy Storage:** Without storage, RE cannot be dispatched at will. India has a 2030 storage target of **42 GWh** (BESS, pumped storage). The 2022 *Incentive Scheme for BESS* and the waiver of ISTS charges for storage-backed projects are key enablers. Pumped storage potential is estimated at 96 GW.

- **Cross-cutting Missions:** National Hydrogen Mission, PM-Surya Ghar Yojana (₹ 75,000 crore for rooftop), Faster Adoption of EVs (FAME-II + PM E-Drive), and the recently notified Carbon Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS 2023).

**(b) Numerical Solution**

- (i) Annual energy from each source:
  - Thermal: $6{,}000 \times 0.55 \times 8{,}760 = 28{,}908{,}000 \text{ MWh}$
  - Hydro: $1{,}800 \times 0.40 \times 8{,}760 = 6{,}307{,}200 \text{ MWh}$
  - Solar: $3{,}000 \times 0.22 \times 8{,}760 = 5{,}781{,}600 \text{ MWh}$
  - Wind: $1{,}200 \times 0.32 \times 8{,}760 = 3{,}363{,}840 \text{ MWh}$
  - Biomass: $300 \times 0.65 \times 8{,}760 = 1{,}708{,}200 \text{ MWh}$

- (ii) Total generation:
  - Sum = $28{,}908{,}000 + 6{,}307{,}200 + 5{,}781{,}600 + 3{,}363{,}840 + 1{,}708{,}200 = 46{,}068{,}840 \text{ MWh}$

- (iii) Renewable share:
  - RE = $6{,}307{,}200 + 5{,}781{,}600 + 3{,}363{,}840 + 1{,}708{,}200 = 17{,}160{,}840 \text{ MWh}$
  - $\text{RS} = \dfrac{17{,}160{,}840}{46{,}068{,}840} \times 100 \approx 37.3 \,\%$

**Comment:** Although the state's non-fossil *installed capacity* is $(1{,}800+3{,}000+1{,}200+300)/12{,}300 = 51.2\,\%$, the actual *generation share* of renewables is only ≈ 37 %. This is because solar and wind have low capacity factors. **Capacity ≠ Generation** is the key takeaway.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing capacity with generation.** A 1,000 MW solar plant does NOT produce 8,760 GWh/yr — only about 1,750 GWh/yr (CF ≈ 0.20). Many students write this and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing units** in energy intensity calculations. Convert everything to MJ/USD (or toe/₹ crore) and *show* the conversion.
> 3. **Treating "Net Zero" as "Zero Emissions".** Net Zero allows residual emissions balanced by carbon removal. Writing "Net Zero = no emissions" costs a mark.
> 4. **Ignoring the demand side.** A "future energy strategy" answer that only talks about supply (solar, wind, hydrogen) and forgets demand-side management, electrification, and efficiency is incomplete — expect 1–2 marks deduction.
> 5. **CAGR confusion.** Sign of CAGR for energy intensity is **negative** (declining). Some students write the magnitude without the sign and lose partial credit.
> 6. **Forgetting Kerala-specific context.** A 14-mark answer that doesn't mention the *Kerala State Energy Policy 2023*, the role of **ANERT**, or the **KSEB**'s targets will be marked incomplete by a KTU examiner.
> 7. **Skipping units in final answers.** A bare number like "37.3" with no "%" or "EJ" will be penalized for missing units.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** Energy Strategy for the Future = a long-term, multi-sectoral plan balancing **Security + Equity + Sustainability** (the IEA *Trifecta*).
- ✅ **Three pillars:** Energy Security, Energy Equity, Environmental Sustainability.
- ✅ **Strategic levers (3D + E):** Decarbonize supply, Digitalize grid, Decentralize generation, Electrify end-uses.
- ✅ **Time horizons:** Short (0–5 yr), Medium (5–15 yr), Long (15–30 yr), Visionary (30+ yr).
- ✅ **India's NDC anchors (Panchamrit):** 500 GW non-fossil by 2030; 50 % non-fossil electricity; 45 % emissions-intensity reduction by 2030 vs 2005; Net Zero by 2070.
- ✅ **Key missions:** National Solar Mission, National Green Hydrogen Mission, PM-Surya Ghar, FAME-II, PM-KUSUM, Saubhagya, Ujjwala, Smart Meter National Programme, CCTS.
- ✅ **Future fuels:** RE (workhorse), Green H₂ (hard-to-abate), Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF), Green ammonia (shipping), Bioenergy with CCUS (carbon-neutral liquids), SMRs (baseload clean), Fusion (2050+).
- ✅ **Key formulas to memorize:** EI = PES/GDP, CI = CO₂/PES, RS = RE_gen/Total_gen, CAGR = (Vf/Vi)^(1/n) − 1, CF = E_actual/(Cap × 8760).
- ✅ **Key fact:** Capacity ≠ Generation — always multiply by Capacity Factor.
- ✅ **Decarbonization math:** ~4.25 GW of solar is needed to offset the annual generation of 1 GW of coal (CF 0.85, SCE 0.95 t/MWh, solar CF 0.20).
- ✅ **Strategic dimensions to cover in any 14-mark answer:** Technology, Policy, Finance, Market Design, International Cooperation, Behavioural change.
- ✅ **Kerala-specific touch:** Kerala State Energy Policy 2023, ANERT, KSEB, Western Ghats wind, rooftop solar on tiled roofs, the 200 MW floating solar at Idukki (planned), the State's goal of carbon neutrality by 2050.
- ✅ **Common pitfall:** Never say "Net Zero = Zero emissions." Always use the wording *"balance between residual emissions and carbon removals."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Conservation Act 2001 and its Features; Basics of Energy and its Various Forms and Conservation; Electricity Basics - Direct Current and Alternating Currents
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Conservation Act 2001 and its Features; Basics of Energy and its Various Forms and Conservation; Electricity Basics - Direct Current and Alternating Currents

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Energy Conservation Act, 2001 (ECA 2001)

**Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
The Energy Conservation Act, 2001 (No. 52 of 2001) is an Act of the Parliament of India enacted on 29th September 2001 to provide for efficient use of energy and its conservation. It established the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** at the central level and designated the **State Designated Agencies (SDAs)** at the state level as the implementing machinery.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Act provides the legal and institutional framework for the National Energy Conservation Programme, which includes standards, labelling, and certification of energy-efficient equipment.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of the Energy Conservation Act as the "Traffic Rules" for energy usage in a country. Just as traffic rules prevent road chaos, the ECA 2001 prevents energy wastage. It creates a regulatory body (BEE) that acts like a "Traffic Police" for energy, setting speed limits (efficiency standards) and rewarding efficient vehicles (star-labelled appliances). Without this law, industries and consumers would have no incentive or obligation to save energy, leading to a national "energy traffic jam."

### 1.2 Energy and Its Various Forms

**Formal Definition:**
**Energy** is defined as the capacity of a system to do work or to produce change. It is a scalar physical quantity that can neither be created nor destroyed, but can be transformed from one form to another. The SI unit of energy is the **Joule (J)**, and other practical units include **kilowatt-hour (kWh)**, calorie, and British Thermal Unit (BTU).

> [!NOTE]
> **1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J** (Joules)
> **1 calorie = 4.186 J** (Joules)

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Energy is like **money** in a bank account. You can deposit it (store it), withdraw it (use it), or transfer it (convert it from one form to another), but the total balance remains constant (conservation). Different forms of energy are like different currencies (thermal, electrical, mechanical) that can be exchanged at an "energy bank."

**Major Forms of Energy:**

| Form | Description | Common Examples |
|------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Mechanical Energy** | Energy of motion (kinetic) or position (potential) | Wind turbines, falling water |
| **Thermal/Heat Energy** | Energy related to temperature of a system | Boilers, geothermal systems |
| **Electrical Energy** | Energy from movement of electrons | Power grids, batteries |
| **Chemical Energy** | Energy stored in chemical bonds | Fossil fuels, food, batteries |
| **Nuclear Energy** | Energy from atomic nucleus splitting/fusion | Nuclear reactors, stars |
| **Radiant/Light Energy** | Energy in electromagnetic waves | Solar panels, lasers |
| **Sound Energy** | Energy carried by vibrations | Speakers, ultrasound |

### 1.3 Conservation of Energy

**Formal Definition:**
The **Law of Conservation of Energy** states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system remains constant.

$$E_{total} = E_{kinetic} + E_{potential} + E_{thermal} + E_{chemical} + \ldots = \text{constant}$$

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a waterfall. Water at the top has *potential energy* (stored). As it falls, this converts to *kinetic energy* (motion). When it hits the bottom and creates a splash, the kinetic energy converts to *heat, sound, and turbulence energy*. The total energy content of the water system remains the same — only the form has changed. Nature's "accounting system" is perfect; no energy is ever lost without a trace.

### 1.4 Direct Current (DC) and Alternating Current (AC) — The Basics

**Direct Current (DC):**
DC is the unidirectional flow of electric charge. The current magnitude may vary, but the direction remains constant over time. Sources include batteries, solar cells, and DC generators.

**Alternating Current (AC):**
AC is the bidirectional flow of electric charge where the current periodically reverses direction and changes magnitude continuously with time, typically in a sinusoidal pattern. Sources include AC generators (alternators), power grids, and inverters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Comparison Table:**
> **Frequency in India = 50 Hz** (Cycles per second)
> **Standard Domestic Voltage = 230 V (single-phase), 415 V (three-phase)**
> **Standard DC Industrial Voltage = 12V, 24V, 48V (telecom), 110V (traction)**

**Conceptual Analogy:**
- **DC** is like a one-way street where vehicles (electrons) only flow from point A to point B. The traffic is steady and unidirectional.
- **AC** is like a crowded two-way street where vehicles keep changing direction in a smooth, rhythmic flow — like pedestrians on a moving walkway that periodically reverses. This rhythmic nature allows easy voltage transformation using transformers, which is why AC dominates long-distance power transmission globally.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Features of the Energy Conservation Act, 2001

The Act has several salient features structured around institutional, regulatory, and incentivization mechanisms:

**(a) Establishment of Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE):**
- Established under Section 3 of the Act
- Headquarters: New Delhi
- Headed by a Director General
- Functions: Coordinate policies, develop standards, promote research
- Plays the role of the central nodal agency

**(b) State Designated Agencies (SDAs):**
- Designated under Section 15(d) of the Act
- Each state/UT has an SDA (e.g., KSEBL in Kerala)
- Coordinate with BEE at the state level
- Enforce provisions within their jurisdiction

**(c) Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC):**
- Provides norms for energy efficiency in commercial buildings
- Adopted by commercial establishments having a connected load of **100 kW or more** (or contract demand of 120 kVA)

**(d) Standards and Labelling Programme:**
- Mandatory and voluntary labelling for appliances
- Star rating from **1 to 5 stars** (5 = most efficient)
- Applies to: Air conditioners, refrigerators, fans, LED lights, etc.

**(e) Designated Consumers:**
- Industries in sectors like aluminium, cement, chlor-alkali, fertilizer, iron & steel, paper, petrochemicals, textiles, etc.
- Required to appoint a **Certified Energy Manager (CEM)** and **Certified Energy Auditor (CEA)**

**(f) Penalties and Enforcement:**
- Section 26: Penalty for failure to comply with ECBC — fine up to ₹10,000
- Section 27: Penalty for obstruction — imprisonment up to 1 month or fine up to ₹10,000
- Section 28: Penalty for non-compliance of standards — fine up to ₹10,000 per equipment

### 2.2 Why and How: The Energy Hierarchy

Energy is categorized by quality and usability:
- **Primary Energy**: Found in nature (coal, oil, sun, wind)
- **Secondary Energy**: Derived from primary sources (electricity, gasoline)
- **Final Energy**: Reaches the end user (electricity at the socket)
- **Useful Energy**: Actually performs the desired task (light from a bulb)

The conversion efficiency drops at each stage, which is why conservation at the consumer end is critical.

### 2.3 Conservation of Energy — The Detailed Mechanism

In a closed system, no mass crosses the boundary, but energy can. The first law of thermodynamics states:

$$\Delta U = Q - W$$

Where:
- $\Delta U$ = Change in internal energy (J)
- $Q$ = Heat supplied to the system (J)
- $W$ = Work done by the system (J)

In a cyclic process: $\oint dU = 0$, hence $\oint Q = \oint W$.

### 2.4 DC vs AC — Deep Analysis

**Direct Current (DC) Characteristics:**
- Constant polarity
- Ripple-free DC: $i(t) = I$ (constant)
- Pure DC: $\frac{di}{dt} = 0$
- Cannot be easily transformed (transformers rely on changing magnetic flux)

**Alternating Current (AC) Characteristics:**

For a sinusoidal waveform:
$$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi_v)$$
$$i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + \phi_i)$$

Where:
- $V_m$, $I_m$ = Peak (maximum) values
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = Angular frequency (rad/s)
- $f$ = Frequency (Hz)
- $\phi_v$, $\phi_i$ = Phase angles (radians)
- $\phi = \phi_v - \phi_i$ = Phase difference

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $E = P \times t$ | Energy = Power × Time | J = W × s |
| 2 | $E_{kWh} = \frac{P(W) \times t(h)}{1000}$ | Energy in kWh | kWh |
| 3 | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$ | Conversion | J |
| 4 | $V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS Voltage | V |
| 5 | $I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS Current | A |
| 6 | $P_{avg} = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos(\phi)$ | AC Real Power (Single-Phase) | W |
| 7 | $P_{DC} = V \cdot I$ | DC Power | W |
| 8 | $P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos(\phi)$ | Three-Phase AC Power | W |
| 9 | $f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$ | Frequency-Angular relation | Hz, rad/s |
| 10 | $V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}$ | Average Voltage (Full Sine) | V |
| 11 | $PF = \cos(\phi) = \frac{P}{S}$ | Power Factor | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $S = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms}$ | Apparent Power | VA |
| 13 | $Q = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \sin(\phi)$ | Reactive Power | VAR |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important KTU Tip:** The form factor for a pure sine wave is $K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11$ — a high-yield exam value!

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ECA 2001**: Foundation for India's energy security policy, drives the Perform-Achieve-Trade (PAT) scheme for industries
- **Conservation Law**: Basis for designing heat exchangers, refrigerators, power plants (all energy balance equations derive from this)
- **DC**: Used in electronic circuits, EVs, solar PV systems, battery storage, telecom
- **AC**: Used in power generation, transmission, distribution, induction motors, household supply

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of RMS Value of a Sinusoidal AC Wave

The **Root Mean Square (RMS)** value is the DC equivalent that produces the same heating effect in a resistor as the AC signal. It is the most important AC parameter for power calculations.

**Starting Point:** Power dissipated in a resistor $R$ at any instant:
$$p(t) = i^2(t) \cdot R$$

**Step 1:** Express instantaneous current for a sinusoidal AC:
$$i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into power equation:
$$p(t) = (I_m \sin(\omega t))^2 \cdot R = I_m^2 \cdot R \cdot \sin^2(\omega t)$$

**Step 3:** Average power over one complete cycle $T$:
$$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T I_m^2 \cdot R \cdot \sin^2(\omega t) \, dt$$

**Step 4:** Use the trigonometric identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:
$$P_{avg} = \frac{I_m^2 \cdot R}{T} \int_0^T \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, dt$$

**Step 5:** Split the integral:
$$P_{avg} = \frac{I_m^2 \cdot R}{2T} \left[ \int_0^T 1 \, dt - \int_0^T \cos(2\omega t) \, dt \right]$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate both integrals:
- $\int_0^T 1 \, dt = T$
- $\int_0^T \cos(2\omega t) \, dt = \left[ \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega} \right]_0^T = \frac{\sin(4\pi) - \sin(0)}{2\omega} = 0$

**Step 7:** Therefore:
$$P_{avg} = \frac{I_m^2 \cdot R}{2T} \cdot T = \frac{I_m^2 \cdot R}{2}$$

**Step 8:** Equate with equivalent DC power $P_{DC} = I_{rms}^2 \cdot R$:
$$I_{rms}^2 \cdot R = \frac{I_m^2 \cdot R}{2}$$

**Step 9:** Solve for RMS:
$$I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \cdot I_m$$

$$\boxed{I_{rms} = \frac{I_m}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \text{and} \quad V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Average Power in a Single-Phase AC Circuit

**Step 1:** Instantaneous power:
$$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$$

**Step 2:** Substituting sinusoidal forms:
$$p(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

**Step 3:** Use identity: $\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$:
$$p(t) = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \left[ \cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t - \phi) \right]$$

**Step 4:** Average over one cycle (the cosine of frequency $2\omega$ averages to zero):
$$P_{avg} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2} \cos(\phi)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute $V_m = \sqrt{2} V_{rms}$ and $I_m = \sqrt{2} I_{rms}$:
$$P_{avg} = \frac{\sqrt{2} V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2} I_{rms}}{2} \cos(\phi)$$

**Step 6:** Simplify:
$$\boxed{P_{avg} = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos(\phi)}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Three-Phase Power Formula

For a balanced three-phase system with line voltage $V_L$ and line current $I_L$:

**Step 1:** Power in each phase:
$$P_{phase} = V_{ph} \cdot I_{ph} \cdot \cos(\phi)$$

**Step 2:** For star connection: $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$, $I_L = I_{ph}$
For delta connection: $V_L = V_{ph}$, $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph}$

**Step 3:** Total power in both cases:
$$P_{total} = 3 P_{phase} = 3 \cdot \frac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot I_L \cdot \cos(\phi)$$

$$\boxed{P_{3\phi} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos(\phi)}$$

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A single-phase AC motor draws a current of 10 A at 230 V, 50 Hz, with a power factor of 0.8 lagging. Calculate: (a) Real Power, (b) Reactive Power, (c) Apparent Power, (d) Peak current.

**Given:** $V = 230$ V, $I = 10$ A, $f = 50$ Hz, $\cos\phi = 0.8$ lagging

**Step 1 — Apparent Power (S):**
$$S = V \cdot I = 230 \times 10 = 2300 \text{ VA} = 2.3 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 2 — Real Power (P):**
$$P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = 230 \times 10 \times 0.8 = 1840 \text{ W} = 1.84 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Reactive Power (Q):**
$$\sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.8^2} = \sqrt{0.36} = 0.6$$
$$Q = V \cdot I \cdot \sin\phi = 230 \times 10 \times 0.6 = 1380 \text{ VAR} = 1.38 \text{ kVAR}$$

**Step 4 — Verification using Power Triangle:**
$$S^2 = P^2 + Q^2$$
$$S = \sqrt{1.84^2 + 1.38^2} = \sqrt{3.3856 + 1.9044} = \sqrt{5.29} = 2.3 \text{ kVA} \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Peak Current:**
$$I_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{rms} = 1.414 \times 10 = 14.14 \text{ A}$$

**Step 6 — Energy consumed in 8 hours (in kWh):**
$$E = P \times t = 1.84 \text{ kW} \times 8 \text{ h} = 14.72 \text{ kWh}$$

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Apparent Power | S | 2.3 | kVA |
| Real Power | P | 1.84 | kW |
| Reactive Power | Q | 1.38 | kVAR |
| Peak Current | $I_m$ | 14.14 | A |
| Phase Angle | $\phi$ | 36.87 | degrees |
| Energy (8 hr) | E | 14.72 | kWh |

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix: DC vs AC

| Parameter | DC | AC (Sinusoidal) |
|-----------|-----|-----------------|
| Direction | Unidirectional | Bidirectional (periodic) |
| Magnitude | Generally constant | Varies sinusoidally |
| Frequency | 0 Hz | 50/60 Hz |
| Voltage Transformation | Difficult (DC-DC converter) | Easy (transformer) |
| Transmission Losses | High (no easy step-up) | Low (step-up possible) |
| Power Formula | $P = VI$ | $P = VI\cos\phi$ |
| Peak to RMS Ratio | 1 | $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ |
| Average Value (full cycle) | $V$ | 0 (over full cycle) |
| Form Factor | 1 | $\pi/(2\sqrt{2}) \approx 1.11$ |
| Examples | Battery, Solar cell | Grid supply, Generator |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Institutional Framework of the Energy Conservation Act, 2001

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Government of India] --> B[Ministry of Power]
    B --> C[Bureau of Energy Efficiency BEE]

    D --> J[Tamil Nadu SDA TEDA]
    D --> K[Karnataka SDA KREDL]
    E --> L[Star Rating 1 to 5]
    F --> M[Commercial Buildings 100kW load]
    H --> N[Certified Energy Manager CEM]
    H --> O[Certified Energy Auditor CEA]
    G --> P[Energy Intensive Sectors Cement Steel Aluminium]
```

    D --> I

    I --> O
    J --> O
    K --> O
    L --> O

```

flowchart TB

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**State the salient features of the Energy Conservation Act, 2001.**

**Model Answer:**
The salient features of the Energy Conservation Act, 2001 are:
1. **Establishment of Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** as the central nodal agency at the national level with headquarters in New Delhi.
2. **Creation of State Designated Agencies (SDAs)** in each state/UT for state-level coordination and enforcement.
3. **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** for regulating energy use in commercial buildings having connected load of 100 kW or above.
4. **Standards and Labelling Programme** for appliances and equipment, providing star ratings (1 to 5) based on energy efficiency.
5. **Designation of Energy-Intensive Industries** as "Designated Consumers" (e.g., cement, aluminium, fertilizer, steel, paper, textiles, petrochemicals).
6. **Mandatory appointment of Certified Energy Managers (CEM) and Certified Energy Auditors (CEA)** in designated establishments.
7. **Penalty Provisions** under Sections 26, 27, and 28 for non-compliance.
8. **PAT (Perform, Achieve and Trade) Scheme** to incentivize industrial energy efficiency.

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Distinguish between Direct Current (DC) and Alternating Current (AC).**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Direct Current (DC) | Alternating Current (AC) |
|-----------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Direction of flow | Unidirectional | Bidirectional (periodic) |
| Magnitude | Generally constant | Varies sinusoidally with time |
| Frequency | 0 Hz | 50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz (USA) |
| Voltage transformation | Difficult (needs DC-DC converter) | Easy (using transformer) |
| Power formula | P = V × I | P = V × I × cos φ |
| Transmission efficiency | Poor (no easy step-up) | High (step-up reduces $I^2R$ losses) |
| Examples | Battery, Solar PV, DC motor | AC generator, Induction motor, Grid |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the institutional framework established under the Energy Conservation Act, 2001. Discuss the role of BEE and SDA with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An industrial motor draws a current of 25 A at 415 V, 50 Hz, three-phase, with a power factor of 0.85 lagging. Calculate the total power consumed, the reactive power, and the line current's peak value. If the motor runs for 12 hours daily for 30 days, calculate the total energy consumed in kWh and the electricity cost at ₹7.50 per kWh. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Institutional Framework:**

The Energy Conservation Act, 2001 establishes a **three-tier institutional framework**:

1. **Central Tier — Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE):**
   - Established under **Section 3** with HQ at New Delhi
   - Headed by Director General appointed by the Central Government
   - Functions: Develop policies, coordinate with ministries, certify energy auditors/managers, implement star labelling and ECBC
   - Manages the **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme** for industries

2. **State Tier — State Designated Agencies (SDAs):**
   - Designated under **Section 15(d)** of the Act
   - Each state has an SDA (e.g., **Kerala State Electricity Board Ltd (KSEBL)** is the SDA for Kerala)
   - Coordinate with BEE, enforce state-level energy regulations, conduct awareness programs
   - Examples: TEDA (Tamil Nadu), KREDL (Karnataka), KSEBL (Kerala)

3. **Industrial/Building Tier — Designated Consumers:**
   - High-energy-consuming industries in 9 sectors
   - Required to appoint CEM and CEA
   - Must conduct periodic energy audits (every 3 years)
   - Must comply with ECBC for new commercial buildings

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $V_L = 415$ V, $I_L = 25$ A, $\cos\phi = 0.85$, $f = 50$ Hz, 3-phase

**Step 1 — Real Power (P):**
$$P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 25 \times 0.85$$
$$P = 1.732 \times 415 \times 25 \times 0.85 = 15,275.4 \text{ W} \approx 15.275 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Reactive Power (Q):**
$$\sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - 0.85^2} = \sqrt{1 - 0.7225} = \sqrt{0.2775} = 0.5268$$
$$Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi = 1.732 \times 415 \times 25 \times 0.5268$$
$$Q = 9466.6 \text{ VAR} \approx 9.47 \text{ kVAR}$$

**Step 3 — Peak Line Current:**
$$I_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_L = 1.414 \times 25 = 35.35 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Total Energy in 30 days:**
$$\text{Total hours} = 12 \times 30 = 360 \text{ hours}$$
$$E = P \times t = 15.275 \times 360 = 5499 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 5 — Total Cost:**
$$\text{Cost} = 5499 \times 7.50 = \text{₹}41{,}242.50$$

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Real Power (P) | 15.275 kW |
| Reactive Power (Q) | 9.47 kVAR |
| Apparent Power (S) | $\sqrt{3} \times 415 \times 25 = 17.97$ kVA |
| Peak Current | 35.35 A |
| Energy (30 days) | 5499 kWh |
| Cost | ₹41,242.50 |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** State and explain the Law of Conservation of Energy. Discuss various forms of energy with one practical example for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase AC circuit has a resistance of 30 Ω in series with an inductance of 95.5 mH. The supply is 230 V, 50 Hz. Calculate: (i) Impedance, (ii) Current, (iii) Power Factor, (iv) Real Power, (v) Reactive Power, (vi) Apparent Power, and (vii) Energy consumed in 24 hours. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Law of Conservation of Energy:**

**Statement:** "Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system remains constant."

**Mathematical Form:**
$$\sum E_{in} = \sum E_{out} + \sum E_{stored}$$

For the first law of thermodynamics:
$$\Delta U = Q - W$$

**Various Forms of Energy:**

| Form | Example |
|------|---------|
| Mechanical (Kinetic + Potential) | Hydroelectric dam converts PE of water → KE → Electrical |
| Thermal/Heat | Coal-fired boiler: Chemical → Heat → Steam → Mechanical |
| Electrical | Solar PV cell: Light (radiant) → Electrical |
| Chemical | Digestion: Chemical (food) → Mechanical + Thermal |
| Nuclear | Nuclear reactor: Nuclear → Thermal → Mechanical → Electrical |
| Radiant/Light | Solar water heater: Light → Heat |
| Sound | Speaker: Electrical → Sound |

The total energy in all these transformations is conserved, though some useful energy is lost as waste heat.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $R = 30 \text{ Ω}$, $L = 95.5 \text{ mH} = 95.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ H}$, $V = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz

**Step 1 — Inductive Reactance ($X_L$):**
$$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 50 \times 0.0955 = 30 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 2 — Impedance (Z):**
$$Z = \sqrt{R^2 + X_L^2} = \sqrt{30^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{900 + 900} = \sqrt{1800} = 42.43 \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3 — RMS Current (I):**
$$I = \frac{V}{Z} = \frac{230}{42.43} = 5.42 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Power Factor:**
$$\cos\phi = \frac{R}{Z} = \frac{30}{42.43} = 0.7071 \text{ (lagging)}$$

**Step 5 — Real Power (P):**
$$P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi = 230 \times 5.42 \times 0.7071 = 881.7 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6 — Reactive Power (Q):**
$$Q = V \cdot I \cdot \sin\phi = 230 \times 5.42 \times 0.7071 = 881.7 \text{ VAR}$$

**Step 7 — Apparent Power (S):**
$$S = V \cdot I = 230 \times 5.42 = 1246.6 \text{ VA} \approx 1.247 \text{ kVA}$$

**Step 8 — Energy Consumed in 24 hours:**
$$E = P \times t = 0.8817 \text{ kW} \times 24 \text{ h} = 21.16 \text{ kWh}$$

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Inductive Reactance ($X_L$) | 30 Ω |
| Impedance (Z) | 42.43 Ω |
| Current (I) | 5.42 A |
| Power Factor ($\cos\phi$) | 0.7071 lagging |
| Real Power (P) | 881.7 W |
| Reactive Power (Q) | 881.7 VAR |
| Apparent Power (S) | 1246.6 VA |
| Energy (24 h) | 21.16 kWh |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Students forget to convert $X_L = 2\pi fL$ correctly. Watch the units — inductance must be in Henries, not milliHenries, in the formula.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** For three-phase problems, ensure you use **line voltage ($V_L$)** and **line current ($I_L$)** with the $\sqrt{3}$ factor, NOT phase values.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** In star connection, $V_L = \sqrt{3} V_{ph}$ and $I_L = I_{ph}$. In delta, $V_L = V_{ph}$ and $I_L = \sqrt{3} I_{ph}$. Mixing these is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** RMS = Peak / $\sqrt{2}$ is for **sinusoidal** waveforms only. For square/triangular waves, the factor is different.
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** When asked about ECA 2001, students often skip mentioning the **penalty provisions** or the **PAT scheme** — both are favorite valuation areas.
> 6. **Common Mistake 6:** The Act established BEE in **2002** (functionally), not 2001. The Act was passed in 2001. Know the distinction.
> 7. **Common Mistake 7:** Always mention "Energy Conservation Act, 2001" and not "Energy Saving Act" — incorrect name = direct mark cut.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Energy Conservation Act, 2001:**
- Passed on **29 September 2001**; came into force on **1 March 2002**
- Establishes **BEE** (central) and **SDAs** (state)
- ECBC applies to commercial buildings with **≥100 kW connected load**
- Industries must appoint **Certified Energy Manager (CEM)** and **Certified Energy Auditor (CEA)**
- Star rating: 1 (least efficient) to **5 stars (most efficient)**
- 9 designated industrial sectors under the Act
- Penalty for non-compliance: Up to **₹10,000** under various sections

**Energy Forms:**
- Energy is a **scalar quantity**; SI unit = **Joule (J)**
- Major forms: Mechanical, Thermal, Electrical, Chemical, Nuclear, Radiant, Sound
- **Primary Energy** → from nature; **Secondary Energy** → derived/converted

**Conservation of Energy:**
- Energy can neither be created nor destroyed
- First Law of Thermodynamics: $\Delta U = Q - W$
- Energy degrades in quality (becomes less useful) over transformations — basis for **Energy Audit**

**DC vs AC:**
- **DC**: $i(t) = $ constant, $P = VI$, used in batteries, solar, electronics
- **AC (Sinusoidal)**: $v(t) = V_m\sin(\omega t + \phi_v)$, $i(t) = I_m\sin(\omega t + \phi_i)$
- **$V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$** for pure sine waves
- **Frequency in India = 50 Hz**, Domestic Voltage = 230 V
- 3-Phase Power: $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$
- Power Factor = $\cos\phi$ (real/apparent)
- AC is preferred for transmission because voltage can be stepped up, reducing $I^2R$ losses

**High-Yield Constants to Memorize:**
- $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$
- $2/\sqrt{2} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$
- $2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.828$
- $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$
- Form factor of sine wave = $\pi/(2\sqrt{2}) \approx 1.11$
- Crest factor of sine wave = $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$
- $\pi \approx 3.1416$, $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electricity Tariff** is the schedule of rates, charges, and pricing structures levied by an electricity supply utility (e.g., KSEB - Kerala State Electricity Board) on consumers for the energy (kWh), demand (kVA / kW), and associated services delivered through the electrical network. It is the *economic bridge* between the **cost of electrical energy generation, transmission, and distribution** on the utility side and the **revenue recovery and demand-side discipline** required from the consumer side.

Mathematically, a generalized tariff expression for an industrial consumer is:

$$
\text{Total Bill (Rs)} = a \cdot \text{MD} + b \cdot \text{kVArh} + c \cdot \text{kWh} + d \cdot \text{FP} + \text{FixedCharges} + \text{Tax}
$$

Where the coefficients $a$, $b$, $c$, $d$ are the rate components fixed by the regulatory commission (e.g., KSERC in Kerala).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Menu" of Power

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of electricity the way a restaurant treats water — a *necessity* that still needs a fair billing menu. The restaurant (utility) charges you a *flat cover charge* (fixed/demand charge) for occupying the table (sitting on the grid), plus a *per-glass water charge* (energy charge per kWh), plus sometimes a *premium for ice cubes* (power factor penalty if you overuse reactive power). Just as the menu is designed to recover the restaurant's costs *and* encourage fair usage, an electricity tariff is structured to **recover utility costs** while **discouraging wasteful consumption** and **rewarding efficient behaviour**.

## 1.3 Core Objectives of an Electricity Tariff

A scientifically designed tariff must simultaneously satisfy three stakeholders — the **utility**, the **consumer**, and the **society**. The five canonical objectives are:

- **Fairness & Equity** — recovery of cost-of-service from each consumer class in proportion to their use.
- **Revenue Adequacy** — sufficient income for the utility to remain financially solvent.
- **Economic Efficiency** — send the *right price signal* so consumers shift load to off-peak hours and avoid waste.
- **Simplicity & Transparency** — easy to understand, easy to compute, easy to audit.
- **Demand-Side Management (DSM)** — discourage low power factor, encourage energy-efficient equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Tariff Design (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **kWh** — Active energy consumed.
> - **kVA / kW** — Maximum Demand (MD).
> - **kVArh** — Reactive energy consumed.
> - **Power Factor (cos φ)** — between **0.0 and 1.0** (typical industrial target: **0.95 lag**).
> - **Load Factor (LF)** — ratio of average load to peak load.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Electricity Tariffs (KTU Board-Exam Favourite)

Electricity tariffs are broadly classified into **two families**:

1. **Simple (Uniform) Tariffs** — Single rate per unit.
2. **Multi-Part / Advanced Tariffs** — Multiple rate components (fixed, demand, energy, PF, time-of-use).

### 2.1.1 Flat Rate / Uniform Rate Tariff
- A single uniform rate is charged per kWh irrespective of the magnitude of consumption or time of use.
- **Bill** $\;=\; R \times \text{kWh}$
- **Drawback:** Penalizes low-consumption domestic users; encourages no demand discipline.
- **Application:** Domestic and small commercial users (e.g., BPL connections in Kerala).

### 2.1.2 Block Rate Tariff (Sliding / Step / Tiered)
- Different blocks of energy consumption are charged at progressively different rates.
- **Block-1:** 0–100 units @ Rs 5/unit.
- **Block-2:** 101–200 units @ Rs 7/unit.
- **Block-3:** 201+ units @ Rs 9/unit.
- The rate is *per kWh* of *that block only* (sliding scale) — **not** a flat per-unit rate across all units.
- **Purpose:** Energy conservation at the high-consumption end.

### 2.1.3 Two-Part Tariff (MD + Energy)
The **most common industrial tariff** in India (used by KSEB for HT consumers).
- **Part A — Fixed / Demand Charge:** Recovers semi-fixed costs (capacity, infrastructure, depreciation).
- **Part B — Energy / Running Charge:** Recovers variable fuel + O + M cost.

$$
\text{Total Bill (Rs)} = R_c \cdot \text{MD}_{\text{kVA}} \;+\; R_e \cdot \text{kWh}
$$

Where $R_c$ is Rs per kVA of maximum demand and $R_e$ is Rs per kWh of energy.

### 2.1.4 Three-Part Tariff (kVA + kVArh + kWh)
Adds a **reactive energy charge** to penalize poor power factor. Used for large inductive loads (foundries, arc furnaces, heavy industries).

$$
\text{Total Bill (Rs)} = R_a \cdot \text{MD}_{\text{kVA}} \;+\; R_r \cdot \text{kVArh} \;+\; R_e \cdot \text{kWh}
$$

### 2.1.5 Maximum Demand Tariff
- Bills the consumer based on the **highest kVA demand** recorded during the billing cycle (typically 15-minute integrated demand).
- Discourages peaky loads; promotes **load factor improvement**.

### 2.1.6 Power Factor Tariff
Three sub-variants:
- **PF Penalty Tariff** — Bills are *surcharged* when PF < 0.95 (or 0.90) lag.
- **PF Rebate Tariff** — Bills get a *discount* when PF > a threshold (e.g., > 0.98).
- **kVA Tariff** — Bills are computed on **kVAh** rather than kWh — automatically penalizes low PF since $\text{kVA} = \dfrac{\text{kW}}{\cos\varphi}$.

### 2.1.7 Time-of-Use (ToU) Tariff
- Different rates for **peak hours** (e.g., 6 PM – 10 PM), **normal hours**, and **off-peak hours** (e.g., 10 PM – 6 AM).
- Used to flatten the system load curve and shift DSM-conscious industries to night shifts.

### 2.1.8 Seasonal Tariff
- Higher rates in summer (high agricultural + AC load), lower in monsoon.
- Common in agricultural states like Kerala, Punjab, Tamil Nadu.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam tested**. Memorize the variable definitions along with the equations.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Variable Definitions | Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Bill} = R \cdot E$ | $R$ = flat rate, $E$ = energy | Rs, kWh | Flat rate |
| 2 | $\text{Bill} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (E_i \cdot R_i)$ | $i$-th block energy & rate | Rs, kWh | Block rate |
| 3 | $\text{Bill} = R_c \cdot \text{MD} + R_e \cdot E$ | $R_c$ = demand rate, $R_e$ = energy rate | Rs, kVA, kWh | Two-part |
| 4 | $\text{Bill} = R_a \cdot \text{MD} + R_r \cdot Q + R_e \cdot E$ | $Q$ = reactive energy (kVArh) | Rs, kVA, kVArh, kWh | Three-part |
| 5 | $\text{MD}_{\text{billed}} = \text{MD}_{\text{recorded}} \cdot \text{DF}_{\text{min}}$ | DF = Diversity Factor / 1.0 floor | kVA | MD cap rule |
| 6 | $\text{LF} = \dfrac{\text{Average Load}}{\text{Peak Load}} = \dfrac{E / T}{\text{MD}}$ | $T$ = hours in billing period | dimensionless | Load factor |
| 7 | $\text{DF} = \dfrac{\text{Sum of Individual Max Demands}}{\text{Max Demand on System}}$ | System vs. consumer peaks | dimensionless | Diversity |
| 8 | $\text{PF Surcharge \%} = \left(\dfrac{0.95 - \cos\varphi}{0.95}\right) \times 100$ | When $\cos\varphi < 0.95$ | % | KSEB rule |
| 9 | $\text{kVA} = \dfrac{\text{kW}}{\cos\varphi}$ | Active vs. apparent power | kVA, kW | kVA tariff |
| 10 | $\text{ToU Bill} = E_p \cdot R_p + E_n \cdot R_n + E_o \cdot R_o$ | Peak, normal, off-peak components | Rs, kWh, Rs/kWh | ToU |

> **Note on the PF Surcharge formula (row 8):** Many utilities use the alternate form $\text{Surcharge} = \dfrac{0.9 - \cos\varphi}{0.9} \times 100$. Always check which base your problem states. **KSEB uses 0.95 lag as reference for HT consumers.**

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Energy Auditors (BEE Certified Auditors in Kerala):** Use the **two-part tariff** to recommend PF correction capacitors — a one-time capex saves recurring surcharges.
- **Demand Management:** The **kVA maximum demand** charge forces factories to stagger their pump/motor startups, reducing the recorded MD.
- **Renewable Integration:** With **ToU tariffs**, industries install rooftop solar (peak shaving) and battery storage (arbitrage) — a major NEP 2020 industry-academia theme.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Two-Part Tariff with PF Penalty

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Problem (Industrial Billing):** A HT industrial consumer in Kerala has the following monthly consumption data:
> - Energy consumed: $\;E = 45{,}000 \; \text{kWh}$
> - Maximum demand recorded: $\;\text{MD}_{\text{rec}} = 250 \; \text{kVA}$
> - Average power factor: $\;\cos\varphi = 0.82 \; \text{lagging}$
>
> **Tariff Schedule (KSEB HT-1 Industrial example):**
> - Demand charge: $R_c = \text{Rs } 350 \; \text{per kVA per month}$
> - Energy charge: $R_e = \text{Rs } 7.50 \; \text{per kWh}$
> - PF reference: $\cos\varphi_{\text{ref}} = 0.95 \; \text{lag}$
> - PF surcharge formula: $\;\Delta = \left(\dfrac{0.95 - \cos\varphi}{0.95}\right) \times 100\;\%$
>
> **Compute:**
> (a) The demand charge.
> (b) The energy charge.
> (c) The PF surcharge amount in Rs.
> (d) The total monthly bill.

### Solution (Step-by-Step — No Step Skipped)

**Step 1 — Compute the demand charge:**

The billed maximum demand equals the recorded MD (no minimum cap applied in this problem, i.e., $\text{DF}_{\text{min}} = 1.0$).

$$
\text{Demand Charge} = R_c \times \text{MD}_{\text{rec}}
$$

$$
\text{Demand Charge} = 350 \; \text{Rs/kVA} \times 250 \; \text{kVA}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Demand Charge} = \text{Rs } 87{,}500}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the energy charge:**

$$
\text{Energy Charge} = R_e \times E
$$

$$
\text{Energy Charge} = 7.50 \; \text{Rs/kWh} \times 45{,}000 \; \text{kWh}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Energy Charge} = \text{Rs } 3{,}37{,}500}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the PF surcharge percentage:**

Since $\cos\varphi = 0.82$ is *less than* the reference 0.95, a surcharge applies.

$$
\Delta = \left(\dfrac{0.95 - 0.82}{0.95}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta = \left(\dfrac{0.13}{0.95}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\Delta = 0.13684 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta = 13.684\%}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the surcharge on the energy charge** (KSEB convention applies PF surcharge on the *energy charge* portion, not the demand charge).

$$
\text{PF Surcharge (Rs)} = \text{Energy Charge} \times \dfrac{\Delta}{100}
$$

$$
\text{PF Surcharge} = 3{,}37{,}500 \times 0.13684
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{PF Surcharge} = \text{Rs } 46{,}183.42}
$$

**Step 5 — Total monthly bill:**

$$
\text{Total Bill} = \text{Demand Charge} + \text{Energy Charge} + \text{PF Surcharge}
$$

$$
\text{Total Bill} = 87{,}500 + 3{,}37{,}500 + 46{,}183.42
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Total Bill} = \text{Rs } 4{,}71{,}183.42}
$$

### 3.2 Energy-Auditor Insight (Bonus Step)

If the consumer installs a **PF correction capacitor bank** to raise $\cos\varphi$ to **0.98**:

- **New PF Surcharge %:** $\left(\dfrac{0.95 - 0.98}{0.95}\right) \times 100 = -3.158\%$ (a *rebate*, not a surcharge).
- **New Energy-equivalent Rebate (Rs):** $3{,}37{,}500 \times (-0.03158) = -\text{Rs } 10{,}658.10$ (savings).
- Plus the **recorded MD drops** to $\text{MD}_{\text{new}} = 250 \times \dfrac{0.82}{0.98} \approx 209.18 \; \text{kVA}$, saving an additional $350 \times (250 - 209.18) \approx \text{Rs } 14{,}287$ in demand charges.
- **Total annual savings:** ~Rs 3 lakh — easily pays for the capacitor bank in < 1 year. This is the **classical energy-conservation-via-tariff** argument central to PESFT414.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Tariff to Recommend?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a *high-yield* table for KTU viva / short-answer questions.

| Consumer Type | Best-Suited Tariff | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Domestic (BPL, low use) | **Flat rate / Lifeline block rate** | Simplicity, low bills, low fixed cost |
| Domestic (middle-class) | **Block / Tiered tariff** | Conservation signal at higher blocks |
| Commercial (shops, malls) | **Two-part (kVA + kWh)** | Penalizes poor PF, recovers demand |
| Heavy Industry (continuous) | **kVA ToU Tariff** | Shifts load to off-peak, rewards high PF |
| Seasonal Industry (rice mills) | **Seasonal Tariff** | Lower rates in monsoon idle months |
| Arc Furnace / Foundry | **Three-part (kVArh explicit)** | Directly penalizes reactive energy |

graph TD

```

> [!NOTE]
> In Kerala (KSEB HT ToU Tariff), typical industrial **ToU windows** are:
> - **Peak (6 PM – 10 PM):** Highest rate.
> - **Normal (6 AM – 6 PM):** Standard rate.
> - **Off-Peak (10 PM – 6 AM):** Lowest rate — incentivizing night-shift manufacturing.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

### Q1. Define Electricity Tariff. List any four desirable characteristics of a good tariff.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An **electricity tariff** is the schedule of rates and charges fixed by a supply utility (e.g., KSEB) under regulatory commission (KSERC) approval, levied on consumers for the electrical energy (kWh) and demand (kVA) supplied, plus related services.
>
> **Four desirable characteristics** *(any 4, 1/2 mark each = 2 marks):*
> 1. **Fairness & Equity** — cost recovery proportional to usage.
> 2. **Revenue Adequacy** — utility remains financially solvent.
> 3. **Economic Efficiency** — right price signal to consumers.
> 4. **Simplicity** — easy to understand and compute.
> 5. **Demand-Side Management (DSM)** — discourages low PF and peaky load.

### Q2. What is a Two-Part Tariff? Why is it commonly applied to industrial consumers?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **two-part tariff** divides the consumer's bill into two components:

$$
\text{Bill} = R_c \cdot \text{MD} \;+\; R_e \cdot \text{kWh}
$$

- The **first part (fixed/demand charge)** recovers the utility's *semi-fixed costs* (capacity, depreciation, infrastructure).
- The **second part (energy charge)** recovers *variable costs* (fuel, O&M).

**Why for industries (1 mark):**
- Industries have a **separate, measurable Maximum Demand (MD)** with installed tri-vector meters.
- It **penalizes peaky load** and **rewards load factor improvement**.
- It enables **PF-based surcharges** and **ToU overlays** — features that matter only for large consumers.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / May 2024 Style]**

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q. A (a) Explain in detail the various types of electricity tariffs used in India. Mention the merits and demerits of each.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

The following major tariff types are used in India:

**1. Flat Rate Tariff** *(1 mark)*
- A single rate per kWh.
- **Merits:** Extremely simple, easy to bill.
- **Demerits:** No conservation signal; unfair to low users; cannot recover demand cost.

**2. Block Rate Tariff** *(1 mark)*
- Different rates for different energy blocks (e.g., 0–100, 101–300, 300+ units).
- **Merits:** Promotes conservation at high consumption.
- **Demerits:** Complex billing; boundary issues; discourages usage just below block threshold.

**3. Two-Part Tariff** *(1.5 marks)*
- Bill = $R_c \cdot \text{MD} + R_e \cdot \text{kWh}$.
- **Merits:** Fair; recovers both fixed and variable cost; enables demand management.
- **Demerits:** Requires tri-vector meters (costly); needs accurate MD recording.

**4. Maximum Demand Tariff** *(1 mark)*
- Bills based on the highest 15-minute integrated demand.
- **Merits:** Discourages peaky loads.
- **Demerits:** Heavy penalty for brief coincident peaks; not suited to seasonal industries.

**5. Power Factor Tariff** *(1.5 marks)*
- PF penalty when $\cos\varphi < 0.95$; rebate when $\cos\varphi > 0.98$.
- **Merits:** Directly rewards energy efficiency and capacitor installation.
- **Demerits:** Requires accurate PF measurement; disputes over PF averaging method.

**6. Three-Part Tariff** *(0.5 mark)*
- Bill = $R_a \cdot \text{MD} + R_r \cdot \text{kVArh} + R_e \cdot \text{kWh}$.
- Used for large reactive loads (induction furnaces, arc welders).

**7. Time-of-Use (ToU) Tariff** *(0.5 mark)*
- Different rates for peak / normal / off-peak hours.
- Smart-meter-driven; flattens system load curve.

**Q. A (b) An HT industrial consumer has a monthly consumption of 80,000 kWh, a maximum demand of 400 kVA, and an average power factor of 0.85 lagging. The tariff is: Demand charge Rs 400 per kVA, Energy charge Rs 8.00 per kWh, PF surcharge applicable if PF < 0.95 with formula ((0.95 − PF)/0.95) × 100 %. Compute the total monthly bill. What savings will result if PF is corrected to 0.98?** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Demand Charge**

$$
\text{Demand Charge} = 400 \times 400 = \text{Rs } 1{,}60{,}000
$$

**Step 2: Energy Charge**

$$
\text{Energy Charge} = 8.00 \times 80{,}000 = \text{Rs } 6{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 3: PF Surcharge at 0.85**

$$
\Delta = \left(\dfrac{0.95 - 0.85}{0.95}\right) \times 100 = 10.526\%
$$

$$
\text{PF Surcharge} = 6{,}40{,}000 \times 0.10526 = \text{Rs } 67{,}368.42
$$

**Step 4: Total Bill at PF 0.85**

$$
\text{Total Bill}_{0.85} = 1{,}60{,}000 + 6{,}40{,}000 + 67{,}368.42 = \text{Rs } 8{,}67{,}368.42
$$

**Step 5: New MD at PF 0.98 (kW load is same)**

$$
\text{kW} = 400 \times 0.85 = 340 \; \text{kW}
$$

$$
\text{MD}_{\text{new}} = \dfrac{340}{0.98} = 346.94 \; \text{kVA}
$$

**Step 6: New Demand Charge at PF 0.98**

$$
\text{Demand Charge}_{\text{new}} = 400 \times 346.94 = \text{Rs } 1{,}38{,}775.51
$$

**Step 7: PF Surcharge at 0.98 (becomes a rebate)**

$$
\Delta_{\text{new}} = \left(\dfrac{0.95 - 0.98}{0.95}\right) \times 100 = -3.158\%
$$

$$
\text{PF Rebate} = 6{,}40{,}000 \times (-0.03158) = -\text{Rs } 20{,}210.53
$$

**Step 8: New Total Bill**

$$
\text{Total Bill}_{0.98} = 1{,}38{,}775.51 + 6{,}40{,}000 - 20{,}210.53 = \text{Rs } 7{,}58{,}564.98
$$

**Step 9: Savings**

$$
\boxed{\text{Monthly Savings} = 8{,}67{,}368.42 - 7{,}58{,}564.98 = \text{Rs } 1{,}08{,}803.44}
$$

**Step 10: Annual Savings**

$$
\text{Annual Savings} = 1{,}08{,}803.44 \times 12 = \text{Rs } 13{,}05{,}641.28 \; (\approx \text{Rs 13.06 lakh})
$$

A capacitor bank of ~Rs 5–7 lakh pays back in **< 6 months** — a classic energy-conservation ROI.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**Q. B (a) What is a Time-of-Use (ToU) tariff? Explain its significance in demand-side management (DSM) and load curve flattening.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

A **Time-of-Use (ToU) Tariff** is a pricing structure in which the **unit rate of electricity varies according to the time of day** at which the energy is consumed.

**Rate Windows (typical Indian industrial tariff):**
- **Peak hours (6 PM – 10 PM):** Highest rate (e.g., Rs 9–12/kWh).
- **Normal hours (6 AM – 6 PM, 10 PM – 12 AM):** Standard rate (Rs 7–8/kWh).
- **Off-peak hours (12 AM – 6 AM):** Lowest rate (Rs 4–5/kWh).

**Significance in DSM (3.5 marks):**

1. **Load Curve Flattening** — Shifts flexible industrial loads (compressor, pump, HVAC) from peak to off-peak hours, reducing the system peak-to-average ratio.
2. **Peak Shaving** — Reduces stress on generation, transmission, and distribution capacity — deferring capital expenditure for new power plants.
3. **Consumer Cost Savings** — Industries with flexible processes gain directly by timing their production cycles.
4. **Renewable Integration** — Off-peak ToU pricing pairs naturally with solar-peak displacement and battery-storage arbitrage.
5. **Smart-Grid Enablement** — ToU is a *pre-requisite* for AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure) and demand-response programs.

**Q. B (b) Compare flat rate, block rate, and two-part tariffs on the basis of (i) fairness, (ii) simplicity, (iii) DSM effect.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

| Criterion | Flat Rate | Block Rate | Two-Part Tariff |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Fairness** | Low — charges low and high users equally | Medium — high users pay more per block | High — charges by *both* demand and energy |
| **(ii) Simplicity** | Highest — single multiplication | Medium — multiple tiered lookups | Lowest — needs tri-vector meter + MD record |
| **(iii) DSM Effect** | Very Low — no conservation signal | High for high-tier users | Highest — penalizes peak MD and low PF |
| **Cost-of-Service Recovery** | Poor (no fixed cost recovery) | Partial | Excellent (recovers fixed + variable) |
| **Best Suited To** | BPL domestic | Domestic / Commercial | HT Industrial / Large Commercial |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
- For **domestic consumers** → **Block Rate Tariff** strikes the right balance.
- For **industrial consumers** → **Two-Part Tariff + PF Surcharge + ToU overlay** is the most economically efficient structure, and is the KSEB / KSERC norm.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Electricity Tariff Questions (KTU 2024 Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kVA to kW** when computing PF — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Applying PF surcharge on the wrong base** — KSEB applies it on the *energy charge only*, not the demand charge. Check the problem wording carefully.
> 3. **Confusing "kVA MD" with "kW MD"** — if the problem says "MD = 250 kW and PF = 0.85", you must convert to kVA before applying the demand rate: $250 / 0.85 = 294.12 \; \text{kVA}$.
> 4. **Sign error in PF rebate** — at $\cos\varphi > 0.95$ the surcharge becomes *negative* (i.e., a rebate). Students often write it as a positive penalty.
> 5. **Skipping the unit** in the final answer — KTU deducts 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 6. **Writing "Flat Rate Tariff" when the question asks for "Sliding Block"** — read the variant carefully (sliding = per-block rate applied *only* to that block, not to the entire consumption).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Recap — Memorize Before Every KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Electricity Tariff** = Pricing schedule by utility; objective is *cost-of-service recovery + DSM + equity*.
- ✅ **Flat Rate** = $R \times \text{kWh}$. *Simple, unfair, no DSM.*
- ✅ **Block Rate** = Sum over blocks $E_i \cdot R_i$. *Conservation signal for high users.*
- ✅ **Two-Part Tariff** = $R_c \cdot \text{MD}_{\text{kVA}} + R_e \cdot \text{kWh}$. *Industry workhorse.*
- ✅ **Three-Part Tariff** = adds $R_r \cdot \text{kVArh}$. *For high-reactive loads.*
- ✅ **PF Surcharge** at $\cos\varphi < 0.95$: $\Delta = \left(\dfrac{0.95 - \cos\varphi}{0.95}\right) \times 100\;\%$.
- ✅ **PF Rebate** at $\cos\varphi > 0.95$: $\Delta$ becomes *negative*.
- ✅ **kVA Tariff** = billed on apparent energy; $\text{kVA} = \text{kW} / \cos\varphi$ — automatically penalizes low PF.
- ✅ **ToU Tariff** = Peak / Normal / Off-peak rates; flattens load curve, enables DSM.
- ✅ **Maximum Demand** is the highest 15-minute integrated demand in the billing cycle.
- ✅ **Load Factor** = Average Load / Peak Load = (kWh / T) / MD. Always between 0 and 1.
- ✅ **PF Correction** with capacitors reduces both *demand charge* (lower kVA) and *PF surcharge* — **double benefit**, the most common energy-auditor recommendation.
- ✅ For KSEB HT consumers: **Two-part + PF penalty + ToU overlay** is the current industrial norm (2024).
- ✅ The **analogy** to keep handy for viva: *Tariff is a restaurant menu — fixed cover charge + per-item cost + premium for premium ingredients.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Basics - Fuels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Thermal Basics - Fuels ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Definition of a Fuel (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Board Standard)** > A **fuel** is a combustible substance, containing **carbon (C)** and/or **hydrogen (H)** as the principal combustible elements, which upon combustion with **oxygen (O₂)** releases a substantial amount of **heat energy** in a controlled manner, suitable for utilisation in domestic, industrial, transportation, and power-generation applications. Mathematically, the heat released is expressed as: $$Q = m \times CV$$ where $Q$ is the total heat liberated (kJ or kCal), $m$ is the mass of fuel burnt, and $CV$ is the **Calorific Value** of the fuel. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Battery of the Industrial World" > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Intuition** > Think of a fuel as a **chemical battery**. Just as a charged electrical battery stores electrical energy in chemical form, a fuel stores **chemical energy locked inside the bonds of carbon and hydrogen atoms**. When you "discharge" the fuel (combust it), the bonds break and the energy is released as heat — exactly like letting the current flow out of a battery to power a device. > - A **matchstick** = small chemical battery (small fuel). > - A **bucket of diesel** = medium chemical battery. > - A **lump of coal** = slow-discharge, dense battery used in thermal power plants. > > The *quality* of the fuel depends on **how much energy is stored per kg** (Calorific Value) and **how cleanly it releases** that energy (combustion efficiency). ### 1.3 Classification of Fuels > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme — High-Yield Classification Table** Fuels are classified primarily on the basis of their **physical state of occurrence** and **method of procurement**. | **Basis of Classification** | **Category** | **Examples** | **Key Property** | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | Physical State | **Solid Fuels** | Wood, Coal, Coke, Charcoal, Bagasse | Heterogeneous, high ash content | | Physical State | **Liquid Fuels** | Petroleum, Diesel, Petrol, Kerosene, LPG (as liquid) | Homogeneous, easy transport | | Physical State | **Gaseous Fuels** | Natural Gas, LPG (as gas), Biogas, Producer Gas | Cleanest combustion, easy mixing | | Occurrence | **Primary / Natural** | Wood, Coal, Crude Oil, Natural Gas | Found directly in nature | | Occurrence | **Secondary / Derived** | Coke, Charcoal, Petrol, Diesel, Producer Gas | Manufactured from primary fuels | | Energy Source | **Conventional** | Coal, Oil, Natural Gas | Fossil-based, non-renewable | | Energy Source | **Non-Conventional** | Biogas, Hydrogen, Solar-derived fuels | Renewable / sustainable | ### 1.4 Standard Reference Constants for Fuel Calculations > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants Used in Fuel Calculations** > - **Standard Reference Temperature:** $25^\circ C$ ($298\,\text{K}$) > - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $1.01325\,\text{bar}$ ($101.325\,\text{kPa}$) > - **Molecular Weight of Air:** $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97\,\text{kg/kmol} \approx 29\,\text{kg/kmol}$ > - **Mole Fraction of O₂ in Air:** $0.21$ (by volume) / $0.232$ (by mass) > - **N₂ : O₂ Volume Ratio in Air:** $3.76 : 1$ > - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314\,\text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$ > - **Calorific Unit Conversion:** $1\,\text{kCal} = 4.186\,\text{kJ}$ ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Calorific Value (CV) — The Heart of Fuel Analysis > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Board Expectation)** > **Calorific Value (CV)** is defined as the **amount of heat energy released** when a **unit mass (or unit volume) of the fuel is completely burnt** and the products of combustion are cooled back to the **initial temperature of the reactants** (typically $25^\circ C$). **Two Types of Calorific Value — Must-Know for KTU Board:** #### (a) Higher Calorific Value (HCV) / Gross Calorific Value (GCV) - Also called **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)**. - Heat recovered when the **water vapour formed during combustion is fully condensed** back to liquid. - The latent heat of condensation of water (≈ $2454\,\text{kJ/kg}$ of water at $25^\circ C$) is *included*. - Measured by the **Bomb Calorimeter** (constant-volume method). #### (b) Lower Calorific Value (LCV) / Net Calorific Value (NCV) - Also called **Net Calorific Value (NCV)**. - Water vapour leaves as vapour (does **not** condense). - Latent heat of vaporisation is **not recovered** — it is *lost* with the flue gases. - Practically **more useful** in boiler/furnace design (since flue gases always exit hot). #### Mathematical Relationship Between HCV and LCV The key derivation relation (KCU/HCV-LCV link) is: $$LCV = HCV - \left[ 9 \times \frac{H}{100} \times h_{fg} \right]$$ For fuels burnt in **air** (where hydrogen content of the combustion air is negligible): $$\boxed{LCV = HCV - \left[ 9 \times \frac{H}{100} \times 2454 \right]\,\text{kJ/kg}}$$ where $H$ is the percentage of hydrogen in the fuel by mass, and $9$ is the mass of water (kg) produced per kg of hydrogen burnt ($2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$, so $4\,\text{kg H} \rightarrow 36\,\text{kg H}_2\text{O}$, i.e. $9\,\text{kg water per kg H}$). > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb for Quick Recall (KTU Examiner's Favourite):** > $LCV \approx HCV - 0.09 \times H \times 2454$ where $H$ is in % If a fuel contains moisture ($M$%) and combustion air carries hydrogen too, a more accurate relation is: $$LCV = HCV - 9 \times \frac{H}{100} \times 2442 - \frac{M}{100} \times 2442$$ --- ### 2.2 Combustion Stoichiometry of Hydrocarbon Fuels > [!IMPORTANT] > **General Combustion Equation for a Hydrocarbon Fuel** > For a fuel with the chemical formula $C_xH_y$ (or simply given in elemental % form), the stoichiometric combustion reaction is: > $$C_xH_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) O_2 \longrightarrow x\,CO_2 + \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O$$ **For practical fuels (containing C, H, S, O), the oxygen required per kg of fuel is:** $$O_2^{\,\text{stoich}} = \frac{32}{12}\,C + \frac{32}{4}\times\frac{H}{2}\times\frac{1}{1} + 32\,(S - \text{O correction})$$ The simplified, **most-asked version (1 kg basis):** $$\boxed{O_2^{\,\text{stoich}}\;(\text{kg/kg fuel}) = \frac{8}{3}\times\frac{C}{100} + \frac{8}{1}\times\frac{H}{100} + \frac{S}{100} - \frac{O}{100}}$$ Because air contains only **21% O₂ by volume (≈ 23% by mass)**, the **stoichiometric air required** is: $$\boxed{A_0 = \frac{100}{23}\times\left[\frac{8}{3}\times\frac{C}{100} + 8\times\frac{H}{100} + \frac{S}{100} - \frac{O}{100}\right]\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}}$$ Equivalently, on a **mole basis**: $$A_0 = \frac{100}{21}\times\left[\frac{C}{12} + \frac{H}{4} + \frac{S}{32} - \frac{O}{32}\right]\;\text{kmol air/kg fuel}$$ --- ### 2.3 Theoretical Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F)ₜₕ > [!NOTE] > **Definition** > The **Theoretical (Stoichiometric) Air-Fuel Ratio** is the **minimum mass of air required** to burn **1 kg of fuel completely** with no oxygen left over. $$\boxed{\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_{th} = \frac{A_0}{1} = \frac{100}{23}\left[\frac{8}{3}\frac{C}{100} + 8\frac{H}{100} + \frac{S}{100} - \frac{O}{100}\right]}$$ **Typical A/F values (Kerala KTU Board Expectation):** | **Fuel** | **(A/F)ₜₕ (kg air/kg fuel)** | **% of O₂ in Dry Flue Gas** | |:---|:---:|:---:| | Methane (CH₄) | 17.16 | 0 | | Propane (C₃H₈) | 15.57 | 0 | | Petrol (Octane, C₈H₁₈) | 15.13 | 0 | | Diesel (C₁₂H₂₆) | 14.93 | 0 | | Coal (≈ 80% C, 5% H, 5% O) | ~9.5 to 11 | 0 | ### 2.4 Excess Air and Equivalence Ratio > [!IMPORTANT] > **Excess Air (EA)** is supplied in real combustion to ensure **complete burning** because mixing is never perfect. $$\text{Excess Air (}\%\text{)} = \frac{A_{\text{actual}} - A_0}{A_0}\times 100$$ $$\text{Equivalence Ratio}\,(\phi) = \frac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$$ - $\phi < 1$ → **lean mixture** (excess air) - $\phi = 1$ → **stoichiometric** - $\phi > 1$ → **rich mixture** (deficient air) ### 2.5 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Reference — All KTU Exam Essentials** | **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Notes** | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---| | 1 | Heat released | $Q = m \cdot CV$ | kJ | Mass basis | | 2 | LCV from HCV | $LCV = HCV - 9\cdot\frac{H}{100}\cdot 2442$ | kJ/kg | $H$ in % | | 3 | Stoichiometric O₂ (mass) | $\frac{8}{3}\frac{C}{100} + 8\frac{H}{100} + \frac{S}{100} - \frac{O}{100}$ | kg O₂/kg fuel | C, H, S, O in % | | 4 | Stoichiometric Air | $A_0 = \frac{100}{23}\times \text{O}_2^{\text{req}}$ | kg air/kg fuel | Air = 23% O₂ by mass | | 5 | Stoichiometric Air (mole) | $\frac{100}{21}\left[\frac{C}{12}+\frac{H}{4}+\frac{S}{32}-\frac{O}{32}\right]$ | kmol air/kg fuel | Air = 21% O₂ by vol | | 6 | Excess Air | $EA(\%) = \frac{A_a - A_0}{A_0}\times 100$ | % | $A_a$ = actual air | | 7 | Actual A/F | $(A/F)_a = A_0 (1 + EA)$ | kg/kg | $EA$ in decimal | | 8 | Dry Flue Gas (DFG) | $m_{DFG} = m_{\text{fuel}} + A_a - m_{H_2O}$ | kg/kg | Mass balance | | 9 | % CO₂ in dry flue gas | $\%\text{CO}_2 = \frac{C/12}{(C/12) + (A_a - A_0)}$ | mole basis | Simplified | | 10 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\text{Heat output}}{\text{Heat input}}\times 100$ | % | Energy basis | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why This Matters in Energy Management** > - In **boiler efficiency audits** (mandatory under the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** in India), the **Orsat flue gas analysis** combined with stoichiometric calculations lets an energy auditor determine **% excess air**, **dry flue gas loss**, and **combustion efficiency** — directly translating to **fuel cost savings** of 1–5% in industrial plants. > - In **internal combustion engines**, the A/F ratio governs **flame speed, knock tendency, and emissions**. > - In **gas turbine power plants**, fuel-bound nitrogen and excess air strategies dictate **NOₓ emissions**, a KTU-favourite energy-environment linkage. ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Oxygen Requirement for 1 kg Fuel > [!NOTE] > **Starting Premise** > Consider 1 kg of a solid fuel having the ultimate analysis (by mass): > $C\,\%$, $H\,\%$, $O\,\%$, $S\,\%$, with the remaining $(100 - C - H - O - S)\,\%$ being **nitrogen + ash + moisture** (non-combustibles in stoichiometric oxygen terms). **Step 1 — Mass of each combustible element in 1 kg of fuel:** $$m_C = \frac{C}{100}\,\text{kg},\quad m_H = \frac{H}{100}\,\text{kg},\quad m_S = \frac{S}{100}\,\text{kg}$$ **Step 2 — Combustion reactions (per kg of each element):** $$\text{Carbon:}\quad C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$$ - $12\,\text{kg C}$ requires $32\,\text{kg O}_2$ → $1\,\text{kg C}$ requires $\frac{32}{12} = \frac{8}{3}\,\text{kg O}_2$ $$\text{Hydrogen:}\quad 2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O$$ - $4\,\text{kg H}$ requires $32\,\text{kg O}_2$ → $1\,\text{kg H}$ requires $\frac{32}{4} = 8\,\text{kg O}_2$ $$\text{Sulphur:}\quad S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$$ - $32\,\text{kg S}$ requires $32\,\text{kg O}_2$ → $1\,\text{kg S}$ requires $1\,\text{kg O}_2$ **Step 3 — Total O₂ required (before subtracting fuel oxygen):** $$O_2^{\,\text{required}} = \frac{8}{3}\cdot\frac{C}{100} + 8\cdot\frac{H}{100} + 1\cdot\frac{S}{100}\;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$ **Step 4 — Subtract the oxygen already present inside the fuel:** The oxygen in the fuel is *already combined* with hydrogen (and carbon) and thus **not available** to react with air. However, this internal oxygen is *bound* and we conservatively subtract its equivalent mass. > [!NOTE] > **Conservation logic:** If fuel contains $O$% oxygen, the hydrogen tied to it has already been partially oxidised. The oxygen that can be released to assist combustion corresponds to the fraction that is *not* bound. In the **standard simplified formula** (KCU exam convention), the term $-\frac{O}{100}$ is included directly. $$\boxed{O_2^{\,\text{stoich}} = \frac{8}{3}\cdot\frac{C}{100} + 8\cdot\frac{H}{100} + \frac{S}{100} - \frac{O}{100}\;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}}$$ **Step 5 — Convert O₂ to air using the mass ratio of air:** Since air contains **23% O₂ by mass**: $$A_0 = \frac{O_2^{\,\text{stoich}}}{0.23} = \frac{100}{23}\left[\frac{8}{3}\frac{C}{100} + 8\frac{H}{100} + \frac{S}{100} - \frac{O}{100}\right]$$ $$\boxed{A_0 = 11.6\,C + 34.8\,H + 4.35\,S - 4.35\,O \;\;\text{(kg air/kg fuel, C,H,S,O in \%)}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Example — Stoichiometric Air for Coal > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Worked Problem (KTU Board Pattern)** **Given:** A coal sample with the following ultimate analysis: - $C = 80\%$, $H = 5\%$, $S = 1\%$, $O = 3\%$, rest = N₂ + ash **Find:** (a) Theoretical O₂ required, (b) Theoretical air required, (c) Actual air for 25% excess air, (d) A/F ratio, (e) Mass of dry flue gas per kg fuel. **Step (a) — O₂ Stoichiometric:** $$O_2 = \frac{8}{3}\times 0.80 + 8\times 0.05 + 0.01 - 0.03$$ $$\begin{aligned} O_2 &= \frac{8}{3}\times 0.80 + 8\times 0.05 + 0.01 - 0.03 \\ &= 2.1333 + 0.4000 + 0.0100 - 0.0300 \\ &= 2.5133\;\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel} \end{aligned}$$ **Step (b) — Theoretical Air:** $$A_0 = \frac{O_2}{0.23} = \frac{2.5133}{0.23} = 10.93\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$ **Step (c) — Actual Air with 25% Excess:** $$A_a = A_0 \times (1 + 0.25) = 10.93 \times 1.25 = 13.66\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$ **Step (d) — Actual A/F Ratio:** $$\left(\frac{A}{F}\right)_a = 13.66\;\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$ **Step (e) — Mass of Dry Flue Gas per kg Fuel:** Dry flue gas contains **CO₂, SO₂, and N₂** (H₂O is wet; the O₂ from excess air remains too). $$\begin{aligned} m_{CO_2} &= \frac{44}{12}\times\frac{C}{100} = 3.667 \times 0.80 = 2.933\;\text{kg} \\ m_{SO_2} &= \frac{64}{32}\times\frac{S}{100} = 2.0 \times 0.01 = 0.020\;\text{kg} \\ m_{N_2\;\text{from air}} &= 0.77 \times A_a = 0.77 \times 13.66 = 10.52\;\text{kg} \\ m_{N_2\;\text{from fuel}} &= \frac{(100 - C - H - O - S)}{100} \approx \frac{(100 - 80 - 5 - 3 - 1)}{100} = 0.11\;\text{kg (approx)} \\ m_{O_2\;\text{excess}} &= 0.25 \times O_2^{\text{stoich}} = 0.25 \times 2.5133 = 0.628\;\text{kg} \end{aligned}$$ **Total Dry Flue Gas (DFG):** $$m_{DFG} = 2.933 + 0.020 + 10.52 + 0.11 + 0.628 = 14.21\;\text{kg DFG/kg fuel}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Cross-check:** Air in = $13.66$ kg + Fuel in = $1$ kg = $14.66$ kg. Subtract water formed = $\frac{9}{100}\times 5 = 0.45$ kg → DFG = $14.66 - 0.45 = 14.21$ kg ✓ Matches. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: LCV-HCV Link > [!NOTE] > **Detailed Derivation of the LCV-HCV Relation** **Step 1 — Combustion of Hydrogen:** $$2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O_{(l)} \quad \Delta H = -571.6\,\text{kJ per 2 kmol }H_2$$ For $1\,\text{kg H}$, water formed $= \frac{18}{2} = 9\,\text{kg}$. **Step 2 — Energy released with condensed water (HCV scenario):** All $9\,\text{kg}$ water releases its latent heat on condensation: $9 \times 2442 = 21,978\,\text{kJ}$ per kg of H. **Step 3 — Energy in LCV scenario:** Water leaves as vapour; latent heat is **carried away** in the flue gas. Hence: $$LCV = HCV - 9 \times \frac{H}{100} \times 2442\;\text{kJ/kg fuel}$$ (Using $2442\,\text{kJ/kg}$ as the latent heat of vaporisation at $25^\circ C$.) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Some textbooks use $2460$ or $2500$ — KTU 2024 board expects **$2442\,\text{kJ/kg}$** (latent heat at $25^\circ C$, also written as $2454$ in older sources). State clearly which value you are using in the exam. --- ### 3.4 Worked Example — HCV to LCV Conversion **Given:** A fuel oil has $HCV = 44{,}000\,\text{kJ/kg}$ and $H = 12\%$ by mass. **Find:** LCV. $$\begin{aligned} LCV &= 44{,}000 - 9 \times \frac{12}{100} \times 2442 \\ &= 44{,}000 - 9 \times 0.12 \times 2442 \\ &= 44{,}000 - 2637.36 \\ &= 41{,}362.6\,\text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** For a typical hydrocarbon fuel with 12% hydrogen, the LCV is roughly 6% lower than HCV — matches the industry thumb rule. --- ### 3.5 Derivation: Relation Between % Excess Air and % O₂ in Dry Flue Gas > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Equation (Often Asked for 14-Mark Proof in KTU Board Exam)** **Step 1 — Excess O₂ in flue gas (mole basis):** If $m$% excess air is supplied, then the O₂ that appears in the dry flue gas is: $$\text{O}_2^{\,\text{excess}} = m \times (\text{O}_2^{\text{stoich}})$$ **Step 2 — Total moles of dry flue gas per kg fuel:** $$\begin{aligned} n_{DFG} &= \frac{C/12}{1} + \frac{S/32}{1} + \frac{m\cdot O_2^{\text{stoich}}}{32} + \frac{N_2^{\text{air}}}{28} \\ &= \frac{C}{12} + \frac{S}{32} + \frac{0.21 m \cdot A_0^{\text{vol}}}{0.79 \times 32 / 28} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Percentage O₂ in dry flue gas:** $$\boxed{\%\text{O}_2\;(\text{DFG}) = \frac{m}{1 + 3.76 \left[\frac{C/12 + S/32 + O_2^{\text{stoich}}/32}{m\cdot O_2^{\text{stoich}}/32}\right]}}$$ A simpler **engineering approximation** (for hydrocarbon fuels where S and N are small): $$\%\text{O}_2\;(\text{DFG}) \approx \frac{21 \cdot m}{1 + 3.76}\times \text{correction factor} \approx \frac{m \cdot 21}{4.76}\cdot k$$ For most fuels $k \approx 0.9$ to $0.95$, giving the widely used **rule of thumb**: $$\boxed{\%\text{O}_2\;(\text{DFG}) \approx \frac{m}{2} \quad \text{(rough estimate for hydrocarbon flames)}}$$ ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Fuel Classification Hierarchy (KTU 2024 Visual Aid) ```mermaid graph TD rootA[FUELS] --> rootB[Classification Basis] rootB --> rootC[Physical State] rootB --> rootD[Occurrence] rootB --> rootE[Energy Source] rootC --> c1[SOLID FUELS] rootC --> c2[LIQUID FUELS] rootC --> c3[GASEOUS FUELS] c1 --> c1a[Wood] c1 --> c1b[Coal] c1 --> c1c[Coke] c1 --> c1d[Bagasse] c2 --> c2a[Petroleum Crude] c2 --> c2b[Diesel] c2 --> c2c[Petrol] c2 --> c2d[Kerosene] c2 --> c2e[Furnace Oil] c3 --> c3a[Natural Gas] c3 --> c3b[LPG] c3 --> c3c[Biogas] c3 --> c3d[Producer Gas] c3 --> c3e[Water Gas] rootD --> d1[Primary Natural] rootD --> d2[Secondary Derived] d1 --> d1a[Wood, Coal, Crude, NG] d2 --> d2a[Coke, Petrol, Diesel, PG] rootE --> e1[Conventional Fossil] rootE --> e2[Non-Conventional] e1 --> e1a[Coal, Oil, Gas] e2 --> e2a[Biogas, H2, Solar Fuels] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Combustion Stoichiometry Flow Architecture ```mermaid graph LR in1[Fuel Composition
C H S O percent] --> procA[Step 1
Compute O2 Stoichiometric] procA --> procB[Step 2
Convert O2 to Air mass] procB --> procC[Step 3
Apply Excess Air factor] procC --> procD[Step 4
Compute A over F ratio] procD --> procE[Step 5
Compute Dry Flue Gas mass] procE --> procF[Step 6
Compute CO2 and O2 percent in DFG] procF --> out1[Orsat Analysis Match] in2[Orsat Readings
