percent CO2, percent O2, percent CO] --> procF out1 --> finalE[Combustion Efficiency and Heat Loss] ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: HCV versus LCV Energy Cascade ```mermaid graph TD startA[Fuel Energy Input] --> boxA[Combustion Reaction] boxA --> boxB[HCV GROSS Calorific Value] boxB --> split1[Water Vapour Path] split1 --> path1[Condensed to Liquid
Latent Heat Recovered] split1 --> path2[Leaves as Vapour
Latent Heat Lost in Flue Gas] path2 --> boxC[LCV NET Calorific Value] boxC --> boxD[Sensible Heat Lost in Flue Gas] boxD --> boxE[Stack Loss] boxE --> boxF[Net Useful Heat] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Orsat Flue Gas Analysis Block Architecture ```mermaid graph TD gasIn[Flue Gas Sample from Chimney] --> orsatA[Orsat Apparatus] orsatA --> absorb1[A1
Absorbs CO2
KOBr or KOH] orsatA --> absorb2[A2
Absorbs O2
Pyrogallol] orsatA --> absorb3[A3
Absorbs CO
Ammoniacal CuCl] orsatA --> residue[N2 Residue] absorb1 --> r1[Volume Drop 1 = percent CO2] absorb2 --> r2[Volume Drop 2 = percent O2] absorb3 --> r3[Volume Drop 3 = percent CO] residue --> r4[Remaining = percent N2] r1 --> comp[Efficiency Computation] r2 --> comp r3 --> comp r4 --> comp comp --> finalA[Excess Air and Combustion Efficiency] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Diagram Note:** The Orsat apparatus is the **only experimental tool** in Module 1 that students may be asked to *sketch and label* in the 14-mark question paper. Memorise the **sequence of absorption tubes** and the **reagent in each tube** — this is a guaranteed 2-mark mark grabber. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** Define **Higher Calorific Value (HCV)** and **Lower Calorific Value (LCV)** of a fuel. Why is LCV preferred over HCV in industrial furnace design? **Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Expected Pattern):** > - **HCV (1 mark):** The total amount of heat released when 1 kg of fuel is completely burnt and the products of combustion are cooled to the original fuel temperature, with the **water vapour formed during combustion condensed back to liquid**. > - **LCV (1 mark):** The total heat released when 1 kg of fuel is completely burnt and the products of combustion leave as **vapour (no condensation)** — i.e., latent heat of water is not recovered. > - **Why LCV preferred in industry (1 mark):** In real furnaces/boilers, the flue gases always leave at a temperature **well above $100^\circ C$** — so the water vapour **never condenses** inside the equipment. Hence the heat actually available in the furnace = LCV. The latent heat of vaporisation is a *real loss* through the stack, not a recoverable gain. --- **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 72%, H = 5%, S = 1.5%, O = 6%, N = 1.5%, ash = 14%. Calculate the **minimum (stoichiometric) mass of air required** for complete combustion of 1 kg of this coal. **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Step 1 — Stoichiometric O₂ per kg fuel:** $$O_2 = \frac{8}{3}\cdot\frac{C}{100} + 8\cdot\frac{H}{100} + \frac{S}{100} - \frac{O}{100}$$ $$\begin{aligned} O_2 &= \frac{8}{3}\times 0.72 + 8\times 0.05 + 0.015 - 0.06 \\ &= 1.9200 + 0.4000 + 0.0150 - 0.0600 \\ &= 2.275\,\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Stoichiometric air:** $$A_0 = \frac{O_2}{0.23} = \frac{2.275}{0.23} = 9.89\,\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$ **Final Answer (3 Marks):** $A_0 = 9.89\,\text{kg air/kg fuel}$. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module Rule:** Each Module carries 14 marks with **internal choice (either-or)** in the End-Semester Examination. Solve any **ONE** of the two alternatives. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]** **(a) [7 Marks]** A fuel oil has the following composition by mass: C = 84%, H = 12%, S = 2%, O = 1%, ash = 1%. The HCV of the fuel is **44,200 kJ/kg**. 1. Calculate the **LCV** of the fuel. 2. Calculate the **stoichiometric air required** per kg of fuel. 3. Calculate the **actual air supplied** if combustion is carried out with **20% excess air**. **(b) [7 Marks]** With the same fuel and 20% excess air, calculate: 1. The **mass of dry flue gas** produced per kg of fuel. 2. The **percentage composition of dry flue gas by mass**. 3. The **theoretical flame temperature** (assume products reach combustion temperature adiabatically, with mean specific heat of products $= 1.05\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$; use LCV). --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A** ### Part (a) — Solution **Step 1 — Calculate LCV:** Using the formula $LCV = HCV - 9 \times \frac{H}{100} \times 2442$: $$\begin{aligned} LCV &= 44{,}200 - 9 \times 0.12 \times 2442 \\ &= 44{,}200 - 2637.36 \\ &= 41{,}562.6\,\text{kJ/kg} \end{aligned}$$ > **Mark 1:** Correct substitution. **Mark 2:** Final answer ≈ 41,562 kJ/kg. **Step 2 — Stoichiometric Air $A_0$:** $$\begin{aligned} O_2^{\text{stoich}} &= \frac{8}{3}\times 0.84 + 8\times 0.12 + 0.02 - 0.01 \\ &= 2.2400 + 0.9600 + 0.0200 - 0.0100 \\ &= 3.210\,\text{kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel} \end{aligned}$$ $$A_0 = \frac{3.210}{0.23} = 13.96\,\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$ > **Mark 3:** $O_2 = 3.21$ kg. **Mark 4:** $A_0 = 13.96$ kg. **Step 3 — Actual Air (20% Excess):** $$A_a = A_0 \times 1.20 = 13.96 \times 1.20 = 16.75\,\text{kg air/kg fuel}$$ > **Mark 5:** $A_a = 16.75$ kg. **Mark 6:** Mention "20% excess" applied. > **Mark 7:** All three quantities clearly tabulated with units. ### Part (b) — Solution **Step 1 — Mass of Dry Flue Gas:** $$\begin{aligned} m_{CO_2} &= \frac{44}{12}\times 0.84 = 3.080\,\text{kg} \\ m_{SO_2} &= \frac{64}{32}\times 0.02 = 0.040\,\text{kg} \\ m_{O_2^{\text{excess}}} &= 0.20 \times 3.210 = 0.642\,\text{kg} \\ m_{N_2} &= 0.77 \times A_a + \text{N}_2^{\text{fuel}} = 0.77 \times 16.75 + 0.0 = 12.90\,\text{kg} \end{aligned}$$ $$m_{DFG} = 3.080 + 0.040 + 0.642 + 12.90 = 16.66\,\text{kg DFG/kg fuel}$$ > **Mark 8:** CO₂ = 3.08 kg. **Mark 9:** N₂ = 12.90 kg. **Mark 10:** DFG = 16.66 kg. **Step 2 — Percentage Composition (by mass):** $$\begin{aligned} \%\text{CO}_2 &= \frac{3.080}{16.66}\times 100 = 18.49\% \\ \%\text{SO}_2 &= \frac{0.040}{16.66}\times 100 = 0.24\% \\ \%\text{O}_2 &= \frac{0.642}{16.66}\times 100 = 3.85\% \\ \%\text{N}_2 &= \frac{12.90}{16.66}\times 100 = 77.43\% \end{aligned}$$ > **Mark 11:** Correct %CO₂. **Mark 12:** Total sums to ≈ 100.01% ✓. **Step 3 — Theoretical Adiabatic Flame Temperature (TAFT):** Energy balance: $LCV = m_{DFG} \cdot c_{p,\text{avg}} \cdot (T_{ad} - 25)$ $$\begin{aligned} T_{ad} &= 25 + \frac{LCV}{m_{DFG} \cdot c_p} \\ &= 25 + \frac{41{,}562.6}{16.66 \times 1.05} \\ &= 25 + 2375.5 \\ &= 2400.5^\circ C \end{aligned}$$ > **Mark 13:** Correct energy balance equation. **Mark 14:** $T_{ad} \approx 2400^\circ C$ (typical for hydrocarbon flames). --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE** **(a) [7 Marks]** What is **Calorific Value**? Explain the working of a **Bomb Calorimeter** with a neat labelled sketch. Derive the relation between HCV and LCV. **(b) [7 Marks]** A gaseous fuel has the following volumetric composition: CH₄ = 50%, C₂H₆ = 20%, H₂ = 10%, CO = 10%, N₂ = 5%, O₂ = 5%. 1. Calculate the **stoichiometric air required** per m³ of fuel. 2. Calculate the **% excess air** if actual air supplied is **17 m³/m³ of fuel**. 3. Determine the **composition of dry flue gas** by volume. --- #### **MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B** ### Part (a) — Solution Outline > **Mark 1:** Calorific Value definition. > **Marks 2–4:** Bomb calorimeter labelled diagram and working (sample ignition in O₂, water-jacketed bucket, temperature rise → $HCV = \frac{(W + w)\cdot \Delta T}{m}$, cooling correction, fuse-wire correction, acid correction). > **Mark 5:** Combustion equation for H₂ → H₂O. > **Mark 6:** Latent heat calculation: $9 \times \frac{H}{100} \times 2442$. > **Mark 7:** Final relation: $\boxed{LCV = HCV - 9 \cdot \frac{H}{100} \cdot 2442}$. ### Part (b) — Solution **Step 1 — Stoichiometric air per m³ of fuel (mole basis, since 1 m³ = 1 kmol at NTP):** For each component in 1 m³ of fuel: | Component | Volume (m³) | O₂ Stoich (m³) | Combustion Reaction | |:---|:---:|:---:|:---| | CH₄ | 0.50 | $2 \times 0.50 = 1.00$ | CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O | | C₂H₆ | 0.20 | $3.5 \times 0.20 = 0.70$ | C₂H₆ + 3.5O₂ → 2CO₂ + 3H₂O | | H₂ | 0.10 | $0.5 \times 0.10 = 0.05$ | H₂ + 0.5O₂ → H₂O | | CO | 0.10 | $0.5 \times 0.10 = 0.05$ | CO + 0.5O₂ → CO₂ | | N₂ | 0.05 | 0 | Inert | | O₂ | 0.05 | $-0.05$ | Already present (subtract) | $$\text{Total O}_2^{\,\text{stoich}} = 1.00 + 0.70 + 0.05 + 0.05 - 0.05 = 1.75\,\text{m}^3\text{O}_2/\text{m}^3\text{fuel}$$ $$A_0 = \frac{1.75}{0.21} = 8.33\,\text{m}^3\text{air}/\text{m}^3\text{fuel}$$ > **Marks 8–10:** Component-wise O₂ table + total $A_0 = 8.33$. **Step 2 — % Excess Air:** $$\%EA = \frac{17 - 8.33}{8.33}\times 100 = 104.1\%$$ > **Mark 11:** $A_a = 17$, $A_0 = 8.33$, EA = 104.1% (≈ 100% excess — very lean flame). **Step 3 — Dry Flue Gas Composition:** Volume of dry products per m³ fuel (excluding H₂O): - CO₂: $0.50 + 2\times 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.00\,\text{m}^3$ - SO₂: 0 - N₂ from air: $0.79 \times 17 = 13.43\,\text{m}^3$ - N₂ from fuel: $0.05\,\text{m}^3$ - O₂ excess: $0.21 \times (17 - 8.33) = 0.21 \times 8.67 = 1.82\,\text{m}^3$ **Total DFG:** $1.00 + 13.43 + 0.05 + 1.82 = 16.30\,\text{m}^3$ $$\begin{aligned} \%\text{CO}_2 &= \frac{1.00}{16.30}\times 100 = 6.13\% \\ \%\text{O}_2 &= \frac{1.82}{16.30}\times 100 = 11.17\% \\ \%\text{N}_2 &= \frac{13.48}{16.30}\times 100 = 82.70\% \end{aligned}$$ > **Marks 12–14:** DFG composition table — all three percentages shown. --- ### 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in This Topic** > 1. **Confusing mass and mole air percentages:** Air is 21% O₂ *by volume* and 23% O₂ *by mass*. Using the wrong one gives ~10% error. **Always check** whether the question says "by volume" or "by mass". > 2. **Forgetting to subtract fuel's own oxygen:** The $-\frac{O}{100}$ term is frequently missed. KCU-style questions deliberately include a 2–3% oxygen in the fuel to test this. > 3. **HCV → LCV conversion with wrong latent heat value:** Use **2442 kJ/kg** (or **2454 kJ/kg** if your textbook uses the older IUPAC value). Mentioning "latent heat of vaporisation of water at 25°C" earns the full mark. > 4. **Orsat apparatus sequence:** The order of absorption is **CO₂ → O₂ → CO → N₂ (residue)**. Writing O₂ first is a guaranteed -1 mark penalty. > 5. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing kcal and kJ without conversion factor ($1\,\text{kCal} = 4.186\,\text{kJ}$) is a frequent KCU deduction. > 6. **Excess Air formula sign:** Some students write $(A_0 - A_a)/A_a$. Correct is $(A_a - A_0)/A_0$. --- ### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision — Module 1: Thermal Basics — Fuels** - [x] **Fuel =** substance releasing heat on combustion of C and/or H with O₂. - [x] **Three states:** Solid (coal, coke), Liquid (diesel, furnace oil), Gaseous (NG, biogas, LPG). - [x] **HCV** = water condenses; **LCV** = water escapes as vapour. - [x] **Master link:** $LCV = HCV - 9 \cdot \frac{H}{100} \cdot 2442\,\text{kJ/kg}$. - [x] **Stoichiometric O₂ (per kg fuel):** $\frac{8}{3}\frac{C}{100} + 8\frac{H}{100} + \frac{S}{100} - \frac{O}{100}$. - [x] **Stoichiometric air:** divide O₂ by **0.23** (mass basis) or by **0.21** (volume basis). - [x] **% Excess Air** = $(A_a - A_0)/A_0 \times 100$. - [x] **Orsat sequence:** CO₂ → O₂ → CO → N₂ (residue). - [x] **Dry flue gas (DFG):** contains CO₂, SO₂, O₂ (excess), N₂; **excludes** H₂O. - [x] **Rule of thumb:** % O₂ in DFG ≈ half the % excess air. - [x] **A/F ratio** for hydrocarbons ≈ 14–17; for coal ≈ 9–11; for wood/biomass ≈ 5–7. - [x] **Air composition by mass:** 23% O₂, 77% N₂ → N₂/O₂ mass ratio = 77/23 ≈ 3.35. - [x] **Air composition by volume:** 21% O₂, 79% N₂ → N₂/O₂ mole ratio = 3.76. - [x] **Constant to memorise:** 1 kcal = 4.186 kJ; 1 kmol of ideal gas = 22.414 m³ at NTP. - [x] **Real-world link:** Every 1% reduction in excess air ≈ 0.5% boiler efficiency gain.

topics.title: Thermal Energy Contents of Fuel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermal Energy Contents of Fuel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Thermal Energy Content of a Fuel** — formally known as the **Calorific Value (CV)** or **Heating Value (HV)** — is defined as the *amount of heat energy released per unit mass (or per unit volume, for gaseous fuels) when the fuel undergoes complete combustion with oxygen, under standard conditions of temperature and pressure, with the products of combustion cooled back to the original temperature of the reactants*.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
q = \frac{Q}{m} \quad \text{or} \quad q = \frac{Q}{V}
$$

where $Q$ is the total heat liberated, $m$ is the mass of the fuel, and $V$ is the volume of the gaseous fuel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PESFT414 Module 1**
> The thermal energy content forms the *foundation stone* of all energy audit calculations. Without an accurate CV, no boiler efficiency, no fuel cost analysis, and no combustion optimization is possible.

### 1.2 Two Principal Variants of Calorific Value

| Symbol | Full Name | Also Known As | Combustion Product State |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **GCV** | Gross Calorific Value | Higher Heating Value (**HHV**) | Water → **Liquid** |
| **NCV** | Net Calorific Value | Lower Heating Value (**LHV**) | Water → **Vapour** |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel Bank Account"

Imagine a fuel as a **bank account storing energy in three locked compartments**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Three-Compartment Model**
> 1. **Locked Compartment 1 (Sensible Heat):** Energy released as direct flame heat — this is what your boiler actually "spends" to make steam.
> 2. **Locked Compartment 2 (Latent Heat of Vaporization):** Energy "trapped" inside the steam vapour produced during combustion. It was liberated but instantly absorbed by the water turning to vapour.
> 3. **Locked Compartment 3 (Stack Loss Heat):** Energy that escapes through the chimney — heat carried away by hot exhaust gases.
>
> **GCV** = Compartment 1 + Compartment 2 (Total potential if *all* heat is recovered by cooling flue gases below 100 °C)
> **NCV** = Compartment 1 only (Realistic industrial case where flue gas exits above 100 °C)

In an industrial furnace, the flue gases typically leave at **150 °C to 250 °C**, so the moisture leaves as vapour — meaning **NCV is the practical value** used in all boiler efficiency calculations.

### 1.4 Standard Units of Calorific Value

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units — Memorize for KTU Board Exam**
> - **Solid & Liquid Fuels:** kcal/kg, kJ/kg, BTU/lb
> - **Gaseous Fuels:** kcal/m³ (at NTP), kJ/m³, BTU/ft³
> - **Conversion constants:**
>  - 1 kcal = **4.187 kJ**
>  - 1 BTU = **1.055 kJ**
>  - 1 kWh = **3600 kJ = 859.85 kcal**

### 1.5 Why Two Values Exist — The Latent Heat Gap

The numerical difference between GCV and NCV is precisely the **latent heat of vaporization of the water produced** during combustion. For every kilogram of water (formed from hydrogen in fuel), **2460 kJ (≈ 587 kcal)** of heat is "hidden" inside the vapour.

$$
\text{GCV} - \text{NCV} = 9 \cdot H \times \frac{2460}{100} \quad \text{kJ/kg of fuel}
$$

where $H$ is the mass percentage of hydrogen in the fuel. This identity is the single most tested formula on this topic in KTU papers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Calorific Value Concept

The thermal energy content of any fuel is governed by its **ultimate (elemental) composition**. For a typical hydrocarbon-based fuel, the principal combustible elements are:

- **Carbon (C)** — Contributes ~33,000 kJ/kg when burned to CO₂
- **Hydrogen (H₂)** — Contributes ~1,43,000 kJ/kg when burned to H₂O
- **Sulphur (S)** — Contributes ~9,000 kJ/kg when burned to SO₂

The non-combustible elements — **oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), ash (A), moisture (M)** — *carry no heating value*; in fact, they act as dead weight, diluting the energy density.

### 2.2 The Logic Chain — From Fuel to Useful Heat

> [!NOTE]
> **The Why-and-How Chain of Combustion Energy Release**
> 1. **Why** does a fuel release heat? → Because the chemical bonds in CO₂ and H₂O are *stronger* (lower energy state) than the bonds in the original fuel. The energy difference is released as heat.
> 2. **How** is heat measured? → Through experimental calorimetry (Bomb Calorimeter for solids/liquids, Boy's Gas Calorimeter for gases).
> 3. **How** is it predicted without experiment? → Through empirical formulas based on ultimate analysis (Dulong's Formula).
> 4. **How** does it differ in real boilers? → Because of moisture in flue gas, dissociation losses, and incomplete combustion, the *actual* useful heat is less than NCV.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — The Complete Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Must memorize for KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{GCV} = 8080 \cdot C + 34500 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot S$ | Dulong's Formula (when C, H, O, S in % by mass) | kcal/kg |
| 2 | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 9 \cdot H \cdot \dfrac{h_{fg}}{100}$ | NCV from GCV (where $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization) | kcal/kg |
| 3 | $h_{fg,\text{water}} = 587 \text{ kcal/kg} = 2460 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | Latent heat at 100 °C, 1 atm | kcal/kg |
| 4 | $h_{fg,\text{water}} = 606 \text{ kcal/kg} = 2536 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | Latent heat at room temperature (25 °C) — used when GCV measured in bomb | kcal/kg |
| 5 | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09 \cdot H \cdot 587$ | Practical form (H in % by mass, GCV in kcal/kg) | kcal/kg |
| 6 | $\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09 \cdot H \cdot 2460$ | SI form (H in % by mass, GCV in kJ/kg) | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $m_{\text{air}} = 11.6 \cdot C + 34.8 \cdot \left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35 \cdot S \quad \text{(kg/kg fuel)}$ | Theoretical air from ultimate analysis | kg air/kg fuel |
| 8 | $\text{A/F ratio} = \dfrac{23}{21} \cdot \left(O_2 + \dfrac{N_2}{3.76}\right)$ | Air-fuel ratio by volume for gaseous fuels | m³ air/m³ fuel |
| 9 | $\eta_{\text{combustion}} = \dfrac{\text{Heat in steam}}{\text{Heat in fuel}} \times 100$ | Boiler / Combustion efficiency | % |
| 10 | $\text{Stack loss \%} = \dfrac{m_g \cdot c_{pg} \cdot (T_g - T_a)}{\text{GCV}} \times 100$ | Siegert's empirical formula (approx.) | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL — Unit Inconsistency in GCV–NCV Relation**
> KTU examiners *deliberately* mix units in the GCV–NCV formula. Always confirm whether $h_{fg}$ is taken as **587 kcal/kg** (at 100 °C) or **606 kcal/kg** (at 25 °C, Bomb Calorimeter condition). The 19 kcal/kg difference can flip an answer's decimal.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The thermal energy content of fuel drives every energy audit decision in industry:

- **Fuel Purchasing:** Cost is quoted *per kcal* or *per MJ*, never per kg alone. A cheaper fuel per kg may be costlier per kcal.
- **Boiler Efficiency Rating:** NCV is the denominator in $\eta = \frac{\text{Heat output}}{\text{Heat input}}$. An error in NCV directly mis-rates the boiler.
- **Fuel Blending:** When two coals are mixed (e.g., Indian + Imported), the blended CV is a *mass-weighted average*: $\text{CV}_{\text{blend}} = x_1 \cdot \text{CV}_1 + x_2 \cdot \text{CV}_2$.
- **Cogeneration Economics:** The CHP plant profitability depends on the gap between GCV and NCV — recovered latent heat becomes *additional revenue*.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — The Relationship Between GCV and NCV

**Statement to Prove:**

$$
\text{GCV} - \text{NCV} = 0.09 \cdot H \cdot h_{fg}
$$

where $H$ is the percentage of hydrogen by mass in the fuel, and $h_{fg}$ is the latent heat of vaporization of water.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let the mass of fuel considered be **1 kg**, and let the mass fraction of hydrogen in the fuel be $\frac{H}{100}$ (i.e., mass of H in 1 kg of fuel = $\frac{H}{100}$ kg).

The combustion reaction of hydrogen is:

$$
2 H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2 H_2O
$$

By stoichiometry, 4 kg of $H_2$ produces 36 kg of $H_2O$. Therefore, 1 kg of $H_2$ produces $\frac{36}{4} = 9$ kg of $H_2O$.

So, for $\frac{H}{100}$ kg of hydrogen in 1 kg of fuel, the mass of water produced is:

$$
m_{\text{water}} = 9 \times \frac{H}{100} \text{ kg of water per kg of fuel}
$$

In the **GCV** definition, this water is condensed back to liquid at 25 °C, releasing the latent heat $h_{fg}$. In the **NCV** definition, this water escapes as vapour, *carrying away* that latent heat.

Therefore, the heat "lost" as latent heat (per kg of fuel) is:

$$
\Delta Q = m_{\text{water}} \cdot h_{fg} = \frac{9H}{100} \cdot h_{fg}
$$

Hence the GCV–NCV gap is:

$$
\boxed{\text{GCV} - \text{NCV} = \frac{9H \cdot h_{fg}}{100} = 0.09 \cdot H \cdot h_{fg}}
$$

**Numerical Validation:** For a coal with $H = 5\%$, using $h_{fg} = 2460$ kJ/kg:

$$
\Delta Q = 0.09 \times 5 \times 2460 = 1107 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

This means if the GCV is 25,000 kJ/kg, the NCV is **23,893 kJ/kg** — a 4.4% reduction. In real boilers, this is the energy that literally "flies up the chimney" as invisible water vapour.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Dulong's Empirical Formula for GCV

**Theoretical Basis:** Each combustible element contributes a fixed amount of heat per kg. The presence of bound oxygen (as in carbohydrates like $C_x(H_2O)_y$) "pre-burns" some hydrogen internally, reducing the net contribution.

Starting from the heats of combustion per kg of each element (oxygen already accounted for reduces available hydrogen):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= 8080 \cdot C + 34500 \cdot \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot S \\
&\quad - \text{(correction for moisture \& ash)} \quad \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Element-by-Element Justification:**

- **Carbon contribution:** Combustion: $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$. The heat released per kg of C = $\frac{97,000 \text{ kcal}}{12 \text{ kg}} = 8083 \approx 8080$ kcal/kg C.
- **Hydrogen contribution:** Combustion: $H_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$. Heat per kg of $H_2$ = $\frac{68,400}{2} = 34,200 \approx 34,500$ kcal/kg.
- **Oxygen correction term:** Oxygen already combined with hydrogen inside the fuel "consumes" some hydrogen. The oxygen reacts with hydrogen in ratio 8:1 (mass). Hence $\frac{O}{8}$ kg of H is internally oxidized, leaving $H - \frac{O}{8}$ kg of free hydrogen.
- **Sulphur contribution:** $S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$. Heat released = $\frac{71,000}{32} = 2219 \approx 2240$ kcal/kg.

**Practical Form for Indian Coals (with moisture & ash corrections):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= 8080 \cdot C + 34500 \cdot \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot S \\
&\quad - 587 \cdot (9H + M) \quad \text{kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

where $M$ is the percentage of moisture. This full form directly gives the **NCV on dry basis** in many engineering references.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Statement

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Comparison Matrix — Frequently Tested in KTU**

| Parameter | GCV (HHV) | NCV (LHV) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| State of water in products | Liquid | Vapour |
| Latent heat recovery | Yes (assumed) | No (assumed) |
| Measured in | Bomb Calorimeter (constant volume) | Calculated from GCV |
| Typical application | Fuel purchase specifications | Boiler design, efficiency calculations |
| Magnitude | Higher | Lower (always $\leq$ GCV) |
| Used in custody transfer (legal billing) | Yes (international trade uses GCV) | Less common in coal trade |
| Indian Standard practice (BIS) | Preferred for solid fuels | Used for gaseous fuels (NCV more common for gases) |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical — Full KTU-Style Solution

**Problem:** A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 78%, H = 5%, O = 8%, N = 1.5%, S = 0.5%, Ash = 7%. Calculate the GCV using Dulong's formula and the NCV using the latent-heat method.

**Solution:**

Given: $C = 78$, $H = 5$, $O = 8$, $S = 0.5$ (all in %).

**Step 1 — Apply Dulong's Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= 8080 \cdot C + 34500 \cdot \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot S \\
&= 8080 \times 78 + 34500 \times \left(5 - \frac{8}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate Each Term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
8080 \times 78 &= 6,30,240 \\
5 - \frac{8}{8} &= 5 - 1 = 4 \\
34500 \times 4 &= 1,38,000 \\
2240 \times 0.5 &= 1,120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the Contributions:**

$$
\text{GCV} = 6,30,240 + 1,38,000 + 1,120 = 7,69,360 \text{ kcal/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute NCV:**

Using $h_{fg} = 587$ kcal/kg (Bomb calorimeter, 25 °C condition):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV} &= \text{GCV} - 0.09 \cdot H \cdot h_{fg} \\
&= 7,69,360 - 0.09 \times 5 \times 587 \\
&= 7,69,360 - 264.15 \\
&= 7,69,096 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\text{GCV} \approx 7693.6 \text{ kcal/kg}, \quad \text{NCV} \approx 7690.96 \text{ kcal/kg}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observational Insight:** Notice that the NCV is barely 0.34% lower than GCV. This is because the hydrogen content is only 5%. If this were a fuel oil (H = 11–13%), the gap would be ~6–7%, which is a *huge* efficiency penalty in furnaces.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Calorific Value Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Energy Content] --> B[Combustion Released Heat]
    B --> C[Sensible Heat in Flue Gas]
    B --> D[Latent Heat of Water Vapour]
    B --> E[Heat Lost as Stack Loss]
    C --> F[Useful Heat for Process]
    D --> G[Lost in Vapour form]
    E --> H[Lost to Atmosphere]
    A --> I[GCV Gross Calorific Value]
    I --> J[GCV equals C plus D]
    A --> K[NCV Net Calorific Value]
    K --> L[NCV equals C only]
    J --> M[Measured by Bomb Calorimeter]
    L --> N[Calculated by Formula GCV minus 0.09 times H times hfg]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Bomb Calorimeter Experimental Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Take 1 gram of fuel sample] --> B[Place in Crucible inside Bomb]
    B --> C[Fill Bomb with 25 to 30 atm Oxygen]
    C --> D[Submerge Bomb in known mass of Water]
    D --> E[Connect Ignition Wires to Fuse Wire]
    E --> F[Record Initial Water Temperature T1]
    F --> G[Pass Electric Current to Ignite Sample]
    G --> H[Stir Water and Record Maximum Temperature T2]
    H --> I[Apply Cooling Correction using Dickinson Formula]
    I --> J[Compute Heat Released Q equals W times delta T plus corrections]
    J --> K[Calculate GCV equals Q divided by mass of sample]
    K --> L[Apply Latent Heat Correction for Acid Formation]
    L --> M[Final Calorific Value Report in kcal per kg]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Energy Audit Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT DATA]
        A1[Ultimate Analysis C H O N S Ash Moisture]
        A2[Proximate Analysis VM FC Ash Moisture]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS[CALCULATION ENGINE]
        B1[Compute GCV using Dulong Formula]
        B2[Compute NCV using Latent Heat Subtraction]
        B3[Compute Theoretical Air Fuel Ratio]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[AUDIT OUTPUTS]
        C1[Fuel Cost per million kcal]
        C2[Boiler Efficiency Estimate]
        C3[Flue Gas Loss Percentage]
        C4[Recommended Excess Air Setting]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B3 --> C4
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Diagram — Why Two Heating Values Exist (Schematic Description)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Schematic Interpretation — The Three-Stage Energy Partition**
> Imagine the fuel's stored energy as a single horizontal bar of length = **GCV**.
> - The leftmost segment (largest, ~85–95%) = **Sensible Heat** recovered in the furnace = NCV.
> - The middle segment (~4–6%) = **Latent Heat** in water vapour.
> - The rightmost segment (~1–2%) = **Other losses** (incomplete combustion, radiation).
>
> **NCV = GCV − Latent Heat**
>
> In a **condensing boiler** (where flue gas is cooled below 55 °C, forcing water vapour to condense), the latent heat segment is *also* recovered. Such boilers achieve **> 95% efficiency**, but they are uneconomical in industries because the corrosive condensate damages chimney linings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Define the terms **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** and **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** of a fuel. Why are both values needed in engineering practice?

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Examiner's Key):**

> **GCV:** It is the total heat released when unit mass (or volume) of fuel is burned completely and the products of combustion are cooled back to the original temperature such that **the water formed is condensed to liquid** (releasing its latent heat). *(1 mark)*
>
> **NCV:** It is the heat released when unit mass of fuel is burned completely but the **products are allowed to leave at the combustion temperature**, with water remaining as vapour (latent heat not recovered). *(1 mark)*
>
> **Why both are needed:** GCV is used in *fuel purchase contracts* (legal billing, international trade) because it represents the *maximum* heat content. NCV is used in *boiler design and efficiency calculations* because industrial flue gases always exit above 100 °C, so latent heat cannot be recovered in practice. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

A fuel contains 6% hydrogen by mass. If its GCV is 8500 kcal/kg, calculate its NCV. (Use $h_{fg} = 587$ kcal/kg).

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Formula:**

$$
\text{NCV} = \text{GCV} - 0.09 \cdot H \cdot h_{fg}
$$

**Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV} &= 8500 - 0.09 \times 6 \times 587 \\
&= 8500 - 316.98 \\
&= 8183.02 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\text{NCV} = 8183.02 \text{ kcal/kg} \approx 8183 \text{ kcal/kg}$ *(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for substitution, 1 for final value)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper — Modified]**

**(a)** The ultimate analysis of a coal sample is as follows: C = 70%, H = 6%, O = 9%, N = 1%, S = 2%, Ash = 8%, Moisture = 4%. **(7 marks)**

**Calculate:**

(i) The Gross Calorific Value using Dulong's formula.

(ii) The Net Calorific Value using $h_{fg} = 587$ kcal/kg.

(iii) The minimum mass of air required for complete combustion of 1 kg of this coal.

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **Bomb Calorimeter**. Discuss why the acid-correction is applied. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a)(i) — GCV Calculation (3 marks):**

Applying Dulong's formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV} &= 8080 \cdot C + 34500 \cdot \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 2240 \cdot S \\
&= 8080 \times 70 + 34500 \times \left(6 - \frac{9}{8}\right) + 2240 \times 2 \\
&= 8080 \times 70 + 34500 \times (6 - 1.125) + 4480 \\
&= 5,65,600 + 34500 \times 4.875 + 4480 \\
&= 5,65,600 + 1,68,187.5 + 4480 \\
&= 7,38,267.5 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**GCV ≈ 7382.68 kcal/kg** *(3 marks: 1 each for substitution, intermediate, final)*

**Part (a)(ii) — NCV Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NCV} &= \text{GCV} - 0.09 \cdot H \cdot h_{fg} \\
&= 7382.68 - 0.09 \times 6 \times 587 \\
&= 7382.68 - 317.0 \\
&= 7065.68 \text{ kcal/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**NCV ≈ 7065.68 kcal/kg** *(2 marks: 1 formula + 1 final)*

**Part (a)(iii) — Theoretical Air Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{air}} &= 11.6 \cdot C + 34.8 \cdot \left(H - \frac{O}{8}\right) + 4.35 \cdot S \\
&= 11.6 \times 70 + 34.8 \times 4.875 + 4.35 \times 2 \\
&= 812 + 169.65 + 8.7 \\
&= 990.35 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum air ≈ 9.90 kg per kg of coal** *(2 marks)*

**Part (b) — Bomb Calorimeter Working (7 marks):**

> **Construction:** A strong stainless-steel bomb (capacity ~250–500 ml) with a screwed lid carrying two electrodes, an oxygen inlet valve, and a sample crucible. *(1 mark)*
>
> **Procedure:** A known mass (~1 g) of fuel is placed in the crucible. A fuse wire (chromel/constantan) is stretched between electrodes, touching the fuel. The bomb is sealed, filled with **pure oxygen at 25–30 atm pressure**, and immersed in a known mass of water inside a calorimeter jacket. A stirrer, thermometer (reading to 0.01 °C), and ignition circuit are arranged. *(2 marks)*
>
> **Operation:** Initial water temperature $T_1$ is noted. Current is passed briefly to ignite the fuse, which in turn ignites the fuel. The fuel burns rapidly under high-pressure oxygen, releasing heat that transfers to the surrounding water. The water is stirred continuously; the maximum temperature $T_2$ is recorded. The cooling correction (using Regnault-Pfaundler or Dickinson formula) is applied to get the true temperature rise $\Delta T$. *(2 marks)*
>
> **Calculation:** $\text{GCV} = \dfrac{(W + w) \cdot \Delta T - \text{(corrections)}}{m}$, where $W$ = water equivalent of calorimeter, $w$ = water mass, $m$ = fuel mass. *(1 mark)*
>
> **Acid Correction:** During combustion, sulphur and nitrogen in the fuel form **H₂SO₄** and **HNO₃** by reacting with the water formed inside the bomb. The heat released by these acid-forming reactions is *included* in the measured GCV but *not* in the actual heating value (since in real furnaces, sulphur leaves as SO₂, not H₂SO₄). Hence, an **acid correction** (≈ 1.5 × mass of sulphur × 22.5 kcal/g) is *subtracted* to get the corrected GCV. *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper — Modified]**

**(a)** A furnace burns natural gas with the following composition by volume: CH₄ = 90%, C₂H₆ = 6%, C₃H₈ = 3%, N₂ = 1%. Calculate the volume of air required for complete combustion of 1 m³ of this gas at NTP. Also calculate the GCV of the gas in kcal/m³. **(7 marks)**

**Given molecular weights:** C = 12, H = 1. Heats of combustion: CH₄ = 191,759 kcal/kmol, C₂H₆ = 3,56,000 kcal/kmol, C₃H₈ = 5,20,000 kcal/kmol.

**(b)** Discuss the significance of calorific value in energy management. Explain the factors that affect the calorific value of a coal sample. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Combustion Stoichiometry (4 marks):**

For 1 m³ of fuel gas, the volume of each component in m³ equals its volume percent (since 1 kmol = 22.4 m³ at NTP, by Avogadro's law, volumes are proportional to moles).

**Combustion Equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CH_4 + 2O_2 &\rightarrow CO_2 + 2H_2O \\
C_2H_6 + 3.5 O_2 &\rightarrow 2CO_2 + 3H_2O \\
C_3H_8 + 5 O_2 &\rightarrow 3CO_2 + 4H_2O \\
N_2 &\rightarrow N_2 \text{ (inert)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Oxygen Required (per m³ of fuel gas):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
O_2 \text{ from } CH_4 &= 0.90 \times 2 = 1.800 \\
O_2 \text{ from } C_2H_6 &= 0.06 \times 3.5 = 0.210 \\
O_2 \text{ from } C_3H_8 &= 0.03 \times 5 = 0.150 \\
\hline
\text{Total } O_2 &= 2.160 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Theoretical Air Required** (air contains 21% O₂ by volume):

$$
\text{Air} = \frac{2.160}{0.21} = 10.286 \text{ m}^3 \text{ of air per m}^3 \text{ of gas}
$$

**Part (a) — GCV Calculation (3 marks):**

Per m³ of fuel gas, the moles of each component = (volume fraction / 22.4) kmol/m³? Actually, since 1 m³ at NTP = $\frac{1}{22.4}$ kmol, the contribution to GCV per m³ of fuel is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GCV}_{CH_4} &= \frac{0.90}{22.4} \times 1,91,759 = 7704.8 \text{ kcal} \\
\text{GCV}_{C_2H_6} &= \frac{0.06}{22.4} \times 3,56,000 = 953.6 \text{ kcal} \\
\text{GCV}_{C_3H_8} &= \frac{0.03}{22.4} \times 5,20,000 = 696.4 \text{ kcal} \\
\hline
\text{Total GCV} &= 9354.8 \text{ kcal/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**GCV ≈ 9355 kcal/m³** *(3 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistake**
> Many students forget to divide by 22.4 m³/kmol when converting volume percentages to kmoles. They incorrectly write $0.90 \times 1,91,759 = 1,72,583$ kcal — an answer off by a factor of **22.4**. This is the **#1 mark-losing error** in gaseous fuel problems. Always remember: **Volume % = Mole %**, but **moles = Volume / 22.4**.

**Part (b) — Significance and Factors Affecting CV (7 marks):**

> **Significance in Energy Management (3.5 marks):**
> - **Fuel Costing:** Energy managers compare fuels on cost per *kcal*, not cost per kg. A fuel at ₹30/kg with GCV 5000 kcal/kg is *cheaper* than a fuel at ₹25/kg with GCV 3500 kcal/kg.
> - **Boiler Efficiency:** NCV forms the denominator; an accurate CV is essential to avoid efficiency over-estimation.
> - **Energy Audit Reporting:** All loss percentages are calculated relative to fuel input heat (= mass × GCV or NCV).
> - **Fuel Substitution Decisions:** When switching from furnace oil to natural gas or biomass, CV compatibility with existing burners must be verified.
> - **Carbon Emission Calculations:** CO₂ emissions per MJ of heat output depend directly on the C/H ratio of the fuel and its CV.

> **Factors Affecting CV of Coal (3.5 marks):**
> - **Carbon Content:** Higher fixed carbon → higher CV. Anthracite (90% C) > Bituminous (70% C) > Lignite (45% C).
> - **Hydrogen Content:** Hydrogen contributes disproportionately (~4×) more heat per kg than carbon. Hydrogen-rich fuels (oils, gases) have very high CV.
> - **Moisture:** Each 1% moisture reduces CV by ~60–70 kcal/kg (latent heat loss for evaporation).
> - **Ash Content:** Inert material with no heating value; reduces CV by dilution effect.
> - **Oxygen Content:** Indicates pre-oxidation (as in lignite); reduces effective hydrogen available for combustion.
> - **Volatile Matter:** Indirect effect — high VM fuels ignite easily but may have lower CV due to lower fixed carbon.
> - **Sulphur Content:** Adds marginal heating value (2240 kcal/kg S) but is environmentally undesirable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks on This Topic**
> 1. **Mixing up GCV and NCV in efficiency formulas** — Always use **NCV** for boiler efficiency, never GCV (unless condensing boiler).
> 2. **Ignoring the $\frac{O}{8}$ correction** in Dulong's formula — without it, hydrogen-rich fuels like biomass produce wrong (overestimated) GCV.
> 3. **Using $h_{fg} = 587$ when question says $h_{fg} = 2460$** — Unit mismatch loses the entire 3-mark sub-part.
> 4. **Forgetting moisture and ash corrections** in the practical Dulong formula — gives "theoretical" CV which differs from "actual" CV by 5–10%.
> 5. **Wrong Avogadro conversion** in gaseous fuel problems — most common cause of zero in combustion questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 1 / Thermal Energy Contents of Fuel**

- **Calorific Value** = Heat released per unit mass/volume on complete combustion. **Units:** kcal/kg, kJ/kg, kcal/m³ (for gases).
- **GCV (HHV):** Water condensed → latent heat recovered. **Higher value.** Measured by Bomb Calorimeter.
- **NCV (LHV):** Water as vapour → latent heat not recovered. **Lower value.** Used in boiler efficiency.
- **Key Identity:** $\text{GCV} - \text{NCV} = 0.09 \cdot H \cdot h_{fg}$ — must be memorized with units.
- **Dulong's Formula:** $\text{GCV} = 8080C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240S$ — works on mass-% ultimate analysis.
- **Latent heat values:** $h_{fg} = 587$ kcal/kg at 100 °C; $606$ kcal/kg at 25 °C.
- **Conversion constants:** 1 kcal = 4.187 kJ; 1 BTU = 1.055 kJ; 1 kWh = 3600 kJ.
- **Theoretical air formula:** $11.6C + 34.8(H - O/8) + 4.35S$ kg air/kg fuel.
- **Bomb Calorimeter:** Constant-volume device, gives GCV directly; acid correction for S and N must be subtracted.
- **Boy's Gas Calorimeter:** Used for gaseous fuels; continuous flow type.
- **In gaseous fuel problems:** Volume % = Mole %; convert to kmoles by dividing by 22.4 m³ at NTP.
- **GCV is for purchase/legal use; NCV is for engineering/efficiency calculations.**
- **Factors reducing CV:** Moisture, Ash, Oxygen content, Inerts. **Factors increasing CV:** Carbon, Hydrogen, Sulphur content.
- **Condensing boilers recover NCV + latent heat → efficiency > 95%.**
- **For KTU numericals: Always show the formula first (1 mark), then substitution (1 mark), then arithmetic (1 mark).**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature and Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature and Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Temperature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Temperature** is a physical quantity that expresses the degree of hotness or coldness of a body. It is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the molecules in a thermodynamic system and determines the direction of spontaneous heat flow between two bodies in thermal contact.

**SI Unit:** **Kelvin (K)**
**Other Scales:** Celsius (°C), Fahrenheit (°F), Rankine (°R)
**Symbol:** *T*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have two cups of water — one steaming hot and one filled with ice. The difference you *feel* is the temperature. Now, think of the molecules in each cup like a crowd of children in a playground:
- In hot water, the children (molecules) are running around wildly — high average kinetic energy → **high temperature**.
- In ice water, the children are barely moving, almost standing still — low average kinetic energy → **low temperature**.

Temperature is essentially the "activity meter" of molecular motion. The higher the average kinetic energy, the higher the temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics (Foundation of Temperature Measurement):**
> If body A is in thermal equilibrium with body C, and body B is in thermal equilibrium with body C, then bodies A and B are in thermal equilibrium with each other. This law forms the basis of all temperature measurement using thermometers.

---

## 1.2 Pressure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Pressure** is defined as the normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area on a surface. In thermodynamics, it is a fundamental state variable that describes the internal stress of a gas or fluid. Mathematically, **P = F / A**.

**SI Unit:** **Pascal (Pa)** = **N/m²**
**Other Units:** bar, atm, psi, mmHg (Torr), kgf/cm²
**Symbol:** *P* or *p*

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine poking a sharp pin versus a flat thumbtack into a balloon — using the same force:
- The **pin** (small area) easily punctures the balloon → **high pressure**.
- The **thumbtack** (larger area) barely makes a dent → **low pressure**.

Pressure is the "concentration of force" — the same force spread over a smaller area creates more pressure. For gases in a closed container, molecules continuously bombard the walls; the sum of these impacts per unit area gives the gas pressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Atmospheric Pressure (Standard):**
> Standard atmospheric pressure at sea level = **1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg = 14.696 psi**.
> It is the pressure exerted by the weight of the entire atmosphere on a unit area of Earth's surface.

---

## 1.3 Importance in Energy Management

| Parameter | Why It Matters in Energy Management |
|-----------|-------------------------------------|
| **Temperature** | Determines efficiency of heat engines, refrigeration cycles, HVAC systems, boilers, and heat exchangers |
| **Pressure** | Influences combustion efficiency, fluid flow, pumping work, and turbine output in power plants |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A 1% improvement in combustion temperature accuracy can save up to 0.5% fuel in industrial boilers — measurement accuracy of T and P is directly tied to energy cost.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Temperature Scales — Operational Logic

There are four primary temperature scales in engineering practice. Each has a defined reference point and a unit interval.

### 2.1.1 The Four Scales

1. **Celsius (°C):** Sets the freezing point of water at **0 °C** and boiling point at **100 °C** at 1 atm. Used worldwide in day-to-day and scientific work.
2. **Fahrenheit (°F):** Sets the freezing point of water at **32 °F** and boiling at **212 °F**. Used primarily in the United States.
3. **Kelvin (K):** The absolute thermodynamic scale. **0 K** is absolute zero (theoretical cessation of all molecular motion). 1 K = 1 °C in size. **This is the SI base unit of temperature.**
4. **Rankine (°R):** The absolute scale corresponding to Fahrenheit. **0 °R = absolute zero.** 1 °R = 1 °F in size. Used in some U.S. engineering calculations.

### 2.1.2 Why Two Different Absolute Scales?

- For **SI-based calculations** (everywhere except U.S. customary): use **Kelvin**.
- For **U.S. customary unit** calculations (Rankine cycle, BTU, etc.): use **Rankine**.
- The **gas constant** value changes depending on which absolute scale you use, so never mix units.

---

## 2.2 Pressure Scales — Operational Logic

Pressure can be expressed in three reference systems:

### 2.2.1 Types of Pressure

1. **Absolute Pressure (P_abs):** Measured from a perfect vacuum (zero reference).
2. **Gauge Pressure (P_gauge):** Measured above atmospheric pressure. **P_gauge = P_abs − P_atm**
3. **Vacuum Pressure (P_vac):** Measured below atmospheric pressure. **P_vac = P_atm − P_abs**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Rule:** Always use **absolute pressure** in thermodynamic equations (Ideal Gas Law, Steam Tables, etc.). Mixing gauge pressure into such equations is a common KTU exam error.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print or memorize this table** — it covers every standard T & P conversion you will encounter in the KTU 2024 board exam.

### 2.3.1 Temperature Conversion Formulas

| Conversion | Formula | Reference Points |
|------------|---------|------------------|
| Celsius → Fahrenheit | $F = \left(\dfrac{9}{5}\right) C + 32$ | 0 °C = 32 °F |
| Fahrenheit → Celsius | $C = \left(\dfrac{5}{9}\right) (F - 32)$ | 100 °C = 212 °F |
| Celsius → Kelvin | $K = C + 273.15$ | 0 K = −273.15 °C |
| Kelvin → Celsius | $C = K - 273.15$ | 273.15 K = 0 °C |
| Fahrenheit → Rankine | $R = F + 459.67$ | 0 °R = −459.67 °F |
| Rankine → Fahrenheit | $F = R - 459.67$ | 459.67 °R = 0 °F |
| Rankine → Kelvin | $K = \left(\dfrac{5}{9}\right) R$ | 0 K = 0 °R |
| Kelvin → Rankine | $R = \left(\dfrac{9}{5}\right) K$ | 1 K = 1.8 °R |

### 2.3.2 Pressure Conversion Formulas

| Conversion | Formula |
|------------|---------|
| Absolute → Gauge | $P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{abs}} - P_{\text{atm}}$ |
| Gauge → Absolute | $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$ |
| Vacuum → Absolute | $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac}}$ |
| Pascal → bar | $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ |
| atm → Pa | $1 \text{ atm} = 101{,}325 \text{ Pa}$ |
| atm → mmHg | $1 \text{ atm} = 760 \text{ mmHg}$ |
| psi → Pa | $1 \text{ psi} = 6{,}894.76 \text{ Pa}$ |

### 2.3.3 Fundamental Governing Equations

| Law / Equation | Mathematical Form | Where Used |
|----------------|-------------------|------------|
| Ideal Gas Law | $P V = n R T$ | Steam and gas cycle analysis |
| Combined Gas Law | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ | Process calculations |
| Boyle's Law (T = const) | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | Isothermal process |
| Charles's Law (P = const) | $\dfrac{V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{T_2}$ | Isobaric process |
| Gay-Lussac's Law (V = const) | $\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$ | Isochoric process |
| Pressure definition | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Fluid mechanics |

> **Universal Gas Constant Values (to remember):**
> - $R = 8.314 \, \text{J/(mol·K)}$ (SI)
> - $R = 0.0821 \, \text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$
> - $R = 1.986 \, \text{BTU/(lbmol·°R)}$

---

## 2.4 Temperature & Pressure Measurement Devices

### 2.4.1 Temperature Measurement
- **Liquid-in-Glass Thermometer** (mercury/alcohol) — laboratory use
- **Thermocouple (Type K, J, T, etc.)** — industrial, wide range
- **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD — Pt100)** — high accuracy
- **Thermistor** — narrow range, high sensitivity
- **Infrared Pyrometer** — non-contact, high temperatures
- **Bimetallic Strip** — thermostats

### 2.4.2 Pressure Measurement
- **Manometer (U-tube, inclined)** — laboratory
- **Bourdon Tube Gauge** — industrial gauge pressure
- **Diaphragm Gauge** — low pressure applications
- **Piezoelectric Sensor** — dynamic pressure
- **Strain Gauge** — precision applications
- **Barometer** — atmospheric pressure

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In a 500 MW thermal power plant, accurate temperature sensors on superheater tubes (precision ± 2 °C) and pressure transducers on boiler drums (precision ± 0.1 bar) directly control fuel consumption, steam quality, and hence plant efficiency. Poor T & P instrumentation can cause **3-8% excess fuel use**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: General Temperature Conversion Formula

### Problem Setup

Derive the relationship between Celsius and Fahrenheit using the two fixed reference points: ice point and steam point of water.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the fixed reference points.**

The ice point (freezing of water at 1 atm) and steam point (boiling of water at 1 atm) are linearly related on both scales.

On the Celsius scale:

$$T_{C,\text{ice}} = 0 \, °C, \quad T_{C,\text{steam}} = 100 \, °C$$

On the Fahrenheit scale:

$$T_{F,\text{ice}} = 32 \, °F, \quad T_{F,\text{steam}} = 212 \, °F$$

**Step 2 — Set up the linear relationship.**

Since both scales are linear, we can write:

$$F = aC + b$$

where $a$ and $b$ are constants to be determined.

**Step 3 — Substitute the ice point.**

$$32 = a(0) + b \implies b = 32$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the steam point.**

$$212 = a(100) + 32$$

$$a \times 100 = 212 - 32 = 180$$

$$a = \frac{180}{100} = \frac{9}{5}$$

**Step 5 — Write the final formula.**

$$F = \frac{9}{5} C + 32 \quad \text{(Celsius to Fahrenheit)}$$

**Step 6 — Invert the formula.**

$$F - 32 = \frac{9}{5} C \implies C = \frac{5}{9} (F - 32) \quad \text{(Fahrenheit to Celsius)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Celsius to Kelvin Relationship

### Problem Setup

Show that the Kelvin and Celsius scales differ only by a constant offset of 273.15.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define absolute zero.**

The theoretical point where all molecular motion ceases is called **absolute zero** = 0 K.

**Step 2 — Identify the triple point of water.**

The triple point of water (where solid, liquid, and gas coexist) is defined as exactly **273.16 K** on the Kelvin scale. On the Celsius scale, this point is **0.01 °C**.

**Step 3 — Use the linear relationship.**

The size of 1 K is defined to equal the size of 1 °C (same unit interval), so:

$$K = C + (\text{offset})$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the offset.**

At the triple point:

$$273.16 = 0.01 + (\text{offset})$$

$$\text{offset} = 273.16 - 0.01 = 273.15$$

**Step 5 — Write the final formula.**

$$K = C + 273.15$$

**Step 6 — Conclude.**

Thus, the two scales differ by **exactly 273.15 units**, and **0 K = −273.15 °C**, which is the lowest possible temperature in the universe (Third Law of Thermodynamics).

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Full Worked Solution

### Worked Example 1: Temperature Conversion in Energy Audit Context

> **Problem:** During an energy audit of a furnace, the surface temperature of a flue gas duct is measured as **350 °C**. A foreign instrument is reading the same temperature in Fahrenheit. (a) Convert to Fahrenheit. (b) Convert to Kelvin. (c) A safety threshold is **842 °F**. Will the duct exceed this threshold?

### Solution

**Part (a) — Celsius to Fahrenheit:**

$$F = \frac{9}{5} C + 32$$

$$F = \frac{9}{5} (350) + 32$$

$$F = 1.8 \times 350 + 32 = 630 + 32$$

$$F = 662 \, °F$$

**Part (b) — Celsius to Kelvin:**

$$K = C + 273.15 = 350 + 273.15 = 623.15 \, K$$

**Part (c) — Threshold Check:**

$$T_{\text{measured}} = 662 \, °F, \quad T_{\text{threshold}} = 842 \, °F$$

Since $662 < 842$, the duct temperature does **NOT** exceed the safety threshold. The duct is operating within safe limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The flue gas duct is at 662 °F, well below the 842 °F threshold. However, in combustion efficiency analysis, this elevated temperature (350 °C) suggests **significant heat loss** through the duct walls — a key finding for energy conservation.

---

### Worked Example 2: Pressure Conversion in Boiler System

> **Problem:** A boiler pressure gauge reads **8.5 bar (gauge)**. The local barometer reads **752 mmHg**. (a) Find absolute pressure in kPa. (b) Convert to psi. (c) Is the boiler operating within the safe limit of **150 psi absolute**?

### Solution

**Part (a) — Absolute Pressure in kPa:**

Convert atmospheric pressure from mmHg to kPa:

$$P_{\text{atm}} = 752 \text{ mmHg} \times \frac{101.325 \text{ kPa}}{760 \text{ mmHg}}$$

$$P_{\text{atm}} = 752 \times 0.13332 = 100.25 \text{ kPa}$$

Convert gauge pressure to kPa:

$$P_{\text{gauge}} = 8.5 \text{ bar} \times 100 \text{ kPa/bar} = 850 \text{ kPa}$$

Compute absolute pressure:

$$P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}} = 850 + 100.25 = 950.25 \text{ kPa}$$

**Part (b) — Convert to psi:**

$$P_{\text{abs}} = \frac{950.25 \text{ kPa}}{6.89476 \text{ kPa/psi}}$$

$$P_{\text{abs}} = 137.82 \text{ psi}$$

**Part (c) — Safety Check:**

$$P_{\text{abs}} = 137.82 \text{ psi} < 150 \text{ psi (limit)}$$

Yes, the boiler is operating within the safe limit, with a margin of **12.18 psi**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Temperature & Pressure Effect on Gas Behavior

| Process | Constant Variable | Varying Variables | Governing Equation | Energy Management Implication |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|--------------------|------------------------------|
| Isothermal | Temperature (T) | P, V | $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ | Compression/expansion of gases in pipelines |
| Isobaric | Pressure (P) | V, T | $\dfrac{V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{V_2}{T_2}$ | Heating of air in a furnace at constant pressure |
| Isochoric | Volume (V) | P, T | $\dfrac{P_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2}{T_2}$ | Pressure rise in a sealed steam boiler |
| Combined | None (all vary) | P, V, T | $\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}$ | General process calculations |

> [!TIP]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Papers:** When applying gas laws, students often forget to convert temperature to **absolute units (K or °R)** before substituting. This produces incorrect answers and **loses 2-3 marks per question**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Relationship Map — Temperature & Pressure in Energy Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy System e.g. Boiler, HVAC, Turbine] --> B[State Variables]
    B --> C[Temperature T]
    B --> D[Pressure P]
    B --> E[Volume V]
    C --> F[Measured in K degC degF degR]
    D --> G[Measured as Absolute or Gauge]
    C --> H[Governs Heat Transfer and Efficiency]
    D --> I[Governs Work Output and Combustion]
    H --> J[Energy Management Decisions]
    I --> J
    F --> K[Instrumentation]
    G --> K
    K --> L[Thermocouple RTD Manometer Bourdon Gauge]
    L --> M[Data Logger SCADA]
    M --> N[Performance Analysis]
    N --> O[Fuel Savings and Emission Reduction]
    J --> O
    O --> P[Energy Conservation Goals]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of T & P Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Physical Quantity T or P] --> S2[Primary Sensor Thermocouple or Pressure Transducer]
    S2 --> S3[Signal Conditioning Amplification and Filtering]
    S3 --> S4[Analog to Digital Conversion ADC]
    S4 --> S5[Microprocessor / Data Acquisition]
    S5 --> S6[Display and HMI]
    S5 --> S7[Data Storage and Historian]
    S6 --> S8[Operator Decision]
    S7 --> S9[Energy Audit and Optimization Module]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Control Action Valve / Damper / Heater]
    S10 --> S1
```

## 4.3 Temperature Scale Conversion Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Input Temperature Value] --> T2{Which Scale?}
    T2 -->|Celsius| T3[Use Formulas with Offset 273.15 and Slope 9/5]
    T2 -->|Fahrenheit| T4[Use Formulas with Offset 32 and Slope 5/9]
    T2 -->|Kelvin| T5[Subtract 273.15 for Celsius or Multiply by 9/5 for Rankine]
    T2 -->|Rankine| T6[Multiply by 5/9 for Kelvin or Subtract 459.67 for Fahrenheit]
    T3 --> T7[Output in Target Scale]
    T4 --> T7
    T5 --> T7
    T6 --> T7
    T7 --> T8[Verify with Physical Reference Points 0 K, 100 C, 212 F]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Temperature and State Its SI Unit

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model Question)]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Temperature is a thermodynamic state variable that measures the degree of hotness or coldness of a body, corresponding to the average kinetic energy of its constituent molecules.

**SI Unit:** **Kelvin (K)**, defined as 1/273.16 of the triple point of water.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Correct definition — **2 marks**
- SI unit with reference — **1 mark**

---

### Question 2: Distinguish Between Absolute and Gauge Pressure

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model Question)]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Absolute Pressure | Gauge Pressure |
|---------|-------------------|----------------|
| Reference | Zero (perfect vacuum) | Local atmospheric pressure |
| Value range | Always positive | Positive, zero, or negative |
| Thermodynamic use | Used in all gas law calculations | Used in field instrumentation |
| Relation | $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$ | $P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{abs}} - P_{\text{atm}}$ |

**Mark Distribution:**
- Reference difference — **1 mark**
- Value range — **1 mark**
- Mathematical relation — **1 mark**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

> **As per KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** Module questions typically feature an internal choice between two questions. We present both **Question A** and **Question B** so students can practice either.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Temperature Conversion and Energy Audit Application

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Model Question)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A furnace wall is measured at **450 °C** by a thermocouple. (i) Convert this temperature to **Fahrenheit**, **Kelvin**, and **Rankine**. (ii) The energy auditor's software only accepts absolute temperature in SI units. Identify the correct value to enter, with reasoning.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Conversions:**

**Celsius to Fahrenheit:**

$$F = \frac{9}{5} \times 450 + 32 = 1.8 \times 450 + 32 = 810 + 32 = 842 \, °F$$

**Celsius to Kelvin:**

$$K = 450 + 273.15 = 723.15 \, K$$

**Celsius to Rankine:**

$$R = (C + 273.15) \times \frac{9}{5} = 723.15 \times 1.8 = 1301.67 \, °R$$

**(ii) Correct SI Absolute Value:**

The software requires **Kelvin**, which is the **SI absolute scale**.

$$T_{\text{abs, SI}} = 723.15 \, K$$

**Reasoning:** For thermodynamic calculations (e.g., ideal gas law, Carnot efficiency), the absolute temperature is required because all such equations assume $T = 0$ at absolute zero. Using 450 °C (a relative scale) would give physically meaningless results.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 3 conversions (3 × 1.5 = 4.5) → **5 marks**
- Identifying Kelvin + reasoning → **2 marks**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A combustion process has a theoretical flame temperature of **2000 °C** and is to be converted to **Rankine** for a U.S. client. (i) Show all conversion steps explicitly. (ii) Verify the answer using two independent pathways.

**Model Solution:**

**Pathway 1: Celsius → Kelvin → Rankine**

**Step 1:** Convert Celsius to Kelvin:

$$K = 2000 + 273.15 = 2273.15 \, K$$

**Step 2:** Convert Kelvin to Rankine:

$$R = K \times \frac{9}{5} = 2273.15 \times 1.8 = 4091.67 \, °R$$

**Pathway 2: Celsius → Fahrenheit → Rankine**

**Step 1:** Convert Celsius to Fahrenheit:

$$F = \frac{9}{5} \times 2000 + 32 = 3600 + 32 = 3632 \, °F$$

**Step 2:** Convert Fahrenheit to Rankine:

$$R = F + 459.67 = 3632 + 459.67 = 4091.67 \, °R$$

**Verification:**

Both pathways give the **same result** of $4091.67 \, °R$, confirming the conversion is correct.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Pathway 1 with all steps → **3 marks**
- Pathway 2 with all steps → **2 marks**
- Verification statement → **2 marks**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Pressure Systems and Safety Analysis

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Model Question)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q:** A steam boiler operates at **15 bar (gauge)**. The local atmospheric pressure is **740 mmHg**. (i) Determine the absolute pressure in **kPa**, **bar**, and **psi**. (ii) The boiler's design limit is **220 psi (absolute)**. Determine the safety margin.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert atmospheric pressure to kPa.**

$$P_{\text{atm}} = 740 \text{ mmHg} \times \frac{101.325 \text{ kPa}}{760 \text{ mmHg}}$$

$$P_{\text{atm}} = 740 \times 0.13328 = 98.62 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Convert gauge pressure to absolute pressure (kPa).**

$$P_{\text{gauge}} = 15 \text{ bar} = 15 \times 100 = 1500 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}} = 1500 + 98.62 = 1598.62 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3: Convert absolute pressure to bar.**

$$P_{\text{abs}} = \frac{1598.62}{100} = 15.986 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 4: Convert absolute pressure to psi.**

$$P_{\text{abs}} = \frac{1598.62 \text{ kPa}}{6.89476 \text{ kPa/psi}} = 231.87 \text{ psi}$$

**Step 5: Compute safety margin.**

$$\text{Margin} = 231.87 - 220 = 11.87 \text{ psi}$$

**Step 6: Verify if within limit.**

Since $231.87 > 220$, the boiler has **exceeded** its design limit by **11.87 psi**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> **Common errors students make in this question type:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add atmospheric pressure** — using 15 bar as absolute pressure directly. This **loses 2 marks**.
> 2. **Wrong conversion factors** — using 1 bar = 14.5 psi (which is slightly different from 14.696 psi). Always use 1 bar = 14.504 psi or 1 psi = 6.895 kPa for accuracy.
> 3. **Converting mmHg to Pa incorrectly** — must use 760 mmHg = 101.325 kPa, not 100 kPa.
> 4. **Not verifying with multiple units** — KTU expects the answer in the asked units and a final safety check statement.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Atmospheric pressure in kPa — **1 mark**
- Absolute pressure in kPa — **2 marks**
- Conversion to bar and psi — **2 marks**
- Safety margin calculation + conclusion — **2 marks**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q:** An industrial gas cylinder contains **8 m³ of nitrogen at 200 kPa (absolute) and 300 K**. The gas is compressed isothermally to a volume of **2 m³**. (i) Calculate the new pressure using Boyle's Law. (ii) If instead the process is isobaric and the temperature rises to **450 K**, find the new volume using Charles's Law.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (i) — Isothermal Compression (Boyle's Law):**

$$P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$$

**Given:**
- $P_1 = 200 \, \text{kPa}$, $V_1 = 8 \, \text{m}^3$, $V_2 = 2 \, \text{m}^3$

**Solving for $P_2$:**

$$P_2 = \frac{P_1 V_1}{V_2} = \frac{200 \times 8}{2} = \frac{1600}{2} = 800 \, \text{kPa}$$

**Part (ii) — Isobaric Heating (Charles's Law):**

$$\frac{V_1}{T_1} = \frac{V_2}{T_2}$$

**Given:**
- $V_1 = 8 \, \text{m}^3$, $T_1 = 300 \, \text{K}$, $T_2 = 450 \, \text{K}$, $P = \text{const}$

**Solving for $V_2$:**

$$V_2 = V_1 \times \frac{T_2}{T_1} = 8 \times \frac{450}{300} = 8 \times 1.5 = 12 \, \text{m}^3$$

**Final Answers:** $P_2 = 800 \, \text{kPa}$ and $V_2 = 12 \, \text{m}^3$

**Mark Distribution:**
- Part (i) — Boyle's Law application + calculation → **3 marks**
- Part (ii) — Charles's Law application + calculation → **4 marks** (including correct use of absolute temperature)

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Charles's Law requires **absolute temperature in Kelvin**. If a student uses Celsius (450 °C = 723 °C) instead of 450 K, the answer becomes physically meaningless. **Always convert to Kelvin first.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day**

### Key Definitions
- **Temperature:** Measure of average kinetic energy of molecules; SI unit is **Kelvin (K)**.
- **Pressure:** Normal force per unit area; SI unit is **Pascal (Pa)**; standard atmosphere is **101.325 kPa**.
- **Absolute Zero:** **0 K = −273.15 °C = −459.67 °F = 0 °R** — theoretical lowest temperature.

### Key Conversion Factors
- °C ↔ °F: $\dfrac{9}{5}$ slope, **32 offset**
- K ↔ °C: **+273.15** offset, 1:1 slope
- K ↔ °R: $\dfrac{9}{5}$ slope, 0 offset
- 1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar = 760 mmHg = 14.696 psi
- 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 14.504 psi

### Key Gas Laws
- Boyle's: $P_1 V_1 = P_2 V_2$ (isothermal)
- Charles's: $V_1/T_1 = V_2/T_2$ (isobaric)
- Gay-Lussac's: $P_1/T_1 = P_2/T_2$ (isochoric)
- Combined: $P_1 V_1/T_1 = P_2 V_2/T_2$
- Ideal Gas: $PV = nRT$

### Pressure Relationships
- $P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + P_{\text{atm}}$
- $P_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{abs}} - P_{\text{atm}}$
- **Always use absolute pressure in thermodynamic equations**

### Energy Management Context
- T & P accuracy directly impacts combustion efficiency, HVAC performance, and boiler safety.
- Standard pressure reference = **1 atm at sea level**.
- Standard temperature reference for many processes = **25 °C = 298.15 K** (SATP) or **0 °C = 273.15 K** (STP).

### Common Exam Traps
- ❌ Using °C in gas law equations — must convert to K.
- ❌ Using gauge pressure in ideal gas law — must convert to absolute.
- ❌ Confusing 0 °C and 0 K (they are different by 273.15).
- ❌ Forgetting to verify units via dimensional analysis.
- ✅ Always state units explicitly in final answers.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Aid:** Think **"K for Kelvin, A for Absolute"** — any time you see a thermodynamic equation, both **T (in K) and P (absolute)** must be used, **never their relative/counterparts**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Heat Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat Capacity (C):** The heat capacity of a body is defined as the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of the body by **one degree Kelvin (or one degree Celsius)**, measured under specified conditions of the process (constant pressure or constant volume).

Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio of an infinitesimal amount of heat supplied ($\delta Q$) to the resulting infinitesimal change in temperature ($dT$):

$$C = \lim_{\Delta T \to 0} \frac{\delta Q}{dT} = \frac{\delta Q}{dT}$$

Its SI unit is **Joules per Kelvin (J/K)** or **kJ/K** for larger systems.

## 1.2 Related Derived Quantities

The base quantity "Heat Capacity" gives rise to three critical engineering metrics:

| Term | Symbol | Definition | SI Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Heat Capacity** | $C$ | Heat required to raise temperature of the whole body by 1 K | J/K |
| **Specific Heat Capacity** | $c$ | Heat required to raise temperature of **unit mass** by 1 K | J/(kg·K) |
| **Molar Heat Capacity** | $\bar{C}$ | Heat required to raise temperature of **one mole** of substance by 1 K | J/(mol·K) |

The mathematical relations between these three are:

$$C = m \cdot c = n \cdot \bar{C}$$

where **$m$** is the mass of the substance in kg, and **$n$** is the number of moles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Distinction:** Heat capacity is an **extensive property** (depends on the amount of matter), while specific heat capacity and molar heat capacity are **intensive properties** (independent of the amount of matter).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Visualization)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Bucket":**
> Imagine two buckets on a hot summer day. One bucket is small (holds 1 litre), and the other is a massive drum (holds 200 litres). Sunlight pours in.
> - The **small bucket** warms up quickly. It needs only a little heat to raise its temperature by 1 °C. → **Low Heat Capacity.**
> - The **large drum** takes a long time to warm up. It needs a huge amount of heat for the same 1 °C rise. → **High Heat Capacity.**
> - The drum and bucket are made of the same material. So, the *quality* of storing heat is the same — but the *quantity* differs. The "quality" is **specific heat capacity** (c); the "quantity" for the whole body is **heat capacity (C)**.
> - Translation: $C = m \times c$ → More mass = more total heat storage.

## 1.4 Constant-Pressure vs Constant-Volume Heat Capacity

For gases, the condition under which heat is supplied drastically changes the value of heat capacity. Two KTU-critical variants exist:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$C_p$ (Heat Capacity at Constant Pressure):** The heat required to raise the temperature of a unit mass of gas by 1 K when the process is carried out at **constant pressure**.
>
> **$C_v$ (Heat Capacity at Constant Volume):** The heat required to raise the temperature of a unit mass of gas by 1 K when the process is carried out at **constant volume**.

The heat supplied to a substance of mass $m$ is therefore given by:

$$\delta Q = m c \cdot dT$$

At constant pressure: $\delta Q_p = m c_p \, dT$
At constant volume: $\delta Q_v = m c_v \, dT$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** For liquids and solids, the difference between $C_p$ and $C_v$ is very small (because the expansion work is negligible). For gases, $C_p > C_v$ **always**, because at constant pressure the gas expands and does boundary work that consumes some of the supplied heat.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Origin of Heat Capacity (Microscopic View)

From a molecular standpoint, when heat is added to a substance, the absorbed energy is distributed among the **degrees of freedom** of its molecules:

1. **Translational kinetic energy** — movement of the molecule as a whole (3 degrees of freedom).
2. **Rotational kinetic energy** — rotation about the centre of mass (2 for linear molecules, 3 for non-linear).
3. **Vibrational kinetic energy + potential energy** — internal atomic oscillations (dominates at high temperatures).

> [!NOTE]
> **Equipartition Theorem (KTU Key Concept):** Each quadratic degree of freedom contributes $\frac{1}{2} R T$ of energy per mole (or $\frac{1}{2} k_B T$ per molecule). The molar heat capacity at constant volume is therefore the sum of contributions from all active degrees of freedom.

## 2.2 Mayer's Relation — The Bridge Between $C_p$ and $C_v$

For an **ideal gas** undergoing a reversible process, the First Law of Thermodynamics gives:

$$\delta Q = dU + \delta W$$

At constant volume, $\delta W = 0$, so:
$$\delta Q_v = dU \quad \Rightarrow \quad m c_v \, dT = dU$$

At constant pressure, $\delta W = p \, dV$. Using the ideal gas equation $pV = mRT$, we have $p \, dV = mR \, dT$. Therefore:
$$\delta Q_p = dU + mR \, dT = m c_v \, dT + mR \, dT = m(c_v + R) \, dT$$

Comparing with $\delta Q_p = m c_p \, dT$, we obtain **Mayer's Relation**:

$$c_p - c_v = R$$

On a molar basis:
$$\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Applicable To |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{\delta Q}{dT}$ | Definition of (total) heat capacity | All systems |
| 2 | $Q = m \, c \, \Delta T$ | Heat for a body of mass $m$ | Solids, liquids, gases |
| 3 | $Q = n \, \bar{C} \, \Delta T$ | Heat for $n$ moles of substance | Gases, reactions |
| 4 | $c_p - c_v = R$ | Mayer's relation (per unit mass, ideal gas) | Ideal gas |
| 5 | $\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = R$ | Mayer's relation (per mole, ideal gas) | Ideal gas |
| 6 | $\gamma = \dfrac{c_p}{c_v}$ | Ratio of specific heats (adiabatic exponent) | Ideal gas |
| 7 | $c_v = \dfrac{R}{\gamma - 1}$ | $c_v$ in terms of $R$ and $\gamma$ | Ideal gas |
| 8 | $c_p = \dfrac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$ | $c_p$ in terms of $R$ and $\gamma$ | Ideal gas |
| 9 | $\bar{C} = 3R \approx 24.94$ J/(mol·K) | Dulong–Petit Law | Solid elements at room T |
| 10 | $U = m \, c_v \, T$ (ideal gas) | Internal energy from $c_v$ | Ideal gas |
| 11 | $H = m \, c_p \, T$ (ideal gas) | Enthalpy from $c_p$ | Ideal gas |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **HVAC Systems:** Calculating cooling/heating loads uses $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ for air (with $c_p \approx 1.005$ kJ/kg·K).
- **Engine Design:** The air-standard Otto cycle uses $\gamma$ to compute thermal efficiency $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$.
- **Calorimetry:** Determining calorific value of fuels experimentally.
- **Material Selection:** Materials with high $c$ (e.g., water at 4.186 kJ/kg·K) are chosen for thermal storage in solar systems.
- **Energy Auditing:** Heat losses from furnaces, boilers, and pipelines are calculated using sensible heat equations.

> [!TIP]
> **Typical values to remember for KTU problems:**
> - Air: $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), $c_v = 0.718$ kJ/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.4$
> - Water: $c = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K)
> - Copper: $c = 0.385$ kJ/(kg·K)
> - Steel: $c = 0.466$ kJ/(kg·K)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Mayer's Relation ($c_p - c_v = R$)

**Starting Point — The First Law for a Closed System (per unit mass):**

$$q = du + w$$

where $q$ is heat supplied per unit mass, $du$ is change in internal energy per unit mass, and $w$ is the work done per unit mass.

---

**Step 1 — Process at Constant Volume**

At constant volume, $dv = 0$, so the boundary work $w = \int p \, dv = 0$.

The first law reduces to:

$$q_v = du$$

By the definition of specific heat at constant volume:

$$q_v = c_v \, dT$$

Therefore, the change in internal energy of an ideal gas is:

$$du = c_v \, dT$$

This is a **defining property of an ideal gas**: internal energy depends only on temperature.

---

**Step 2 — Process at Constant Pressure**

At constant pressure, the work done per unit mass is:

$$w = p \, dv$$

By the ideal gas equation (per unit mass), $p v = R T$. Differentiating:

$$p \, dv + v \, dp = R \, dT$$

Since the process is at constant pressure, $dp = 0$. Hence:

$$p \, dv = R \, dT$$

So the work done per unit mass becomes:

$$w = R \, dT$$

---

**Step 3 — Apply the First Law at Constant Pressure**

Using the first law $q_p = du + w$:

$$q_p = c_v \, dT + R \, dT = (c_v + R) \, dT$$

By definition, the specific heat at constant pressure gives:

$$q_p = c_p \, dT$$

---

**Step 4 — Equate and Isolate**

Equating the two expressions for $q_p$:

$$c_p \, dT = (c_v + R) \, dT$$

Dividing throughout by $dT$ (assuming $dT \ne 0$):

$$\boxed{c_p - c_v = R}$$

This is **Mayer's Relation** for unit mass. Multiplying both sides by the molar mass $M$ (kg/kmol) gives the **molar form**:

$$\bar{C}_p - \bar{C}_v = M R = \bar{R} = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of $c_v$ and $c_p$ in Terms of $\gamma$

We define the ratio of specific heats:

$$\gamma = \frac{c_p}{c_v}$$

Combined with Mayer's relation $c_p - c_v = R$, we can express both quantities using $\gamma$ and $R$.

**Solving the system:**

$$c_p = \gamma \, c_v$$

Substitute into Mayer's relation:

$$\gamma \, c_v - c_v = R \quad \Rightarrow \quad c_v (\gamma - 1) = R$$

Therefore:

$$c_v = \frac{R}{\gamma - 1}$$

And:

$$c_p = \gamma \cdot c_v = \frac{\gamma R}{\gamma - 1}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Internal Energy & Enthalpy of an Ideal Gas

From Step 1 above, $du = c_v \, dT$. Integrating from a reference state (assumed zero at 0 K, or any arbitrary datum):

$$u_2 - u_1 = c_v (T_2 - T_1)$$

For an ideal gas, enthalpy $h = u + pv = u + RT$ (per unit mass). Differentiating:

$$dh = du + R \, dT = c_v \, dT + R \, dT = c_p \, dT$$

Integrating:

$$h_2 - h_1 = c_p (T_2 - T_1)$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** 5 kg of air is heated from 300 K to 500 K at constant pressure. Calculate:
(a) the heat supplied, (b) the work done, (c) the change in internal energy, and (d) the change in enthalpy.
Given: $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K), $\gamma = 1.4$.

**Solution:**

For air, using $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K):

$$c_v = \frac{R}{\gamma - 1} = \frac{0.287}{1.4 - 1} = \frac{0.287}{0.4} = 0.7175 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

$$c_p = \gamma \cdot c_v = 1.4 \times 0.7175 = 1.0045 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

---

**(a) Heat supplied at constant pressure:**

$$Q_p = m \, c_p \, \Delta T = 5 \times 1.0045 \times (500 - 300) = 5 \times 1.0045 \times 200$$

$$\boxed{Q_p = 1004.5 \text{ kJ}}$$

---

**(b) Work done at constant pressure:**

For an ideal gas, $W = m R (T_2 - T_1)$:

$$W = 5 \times 0.287 \times 200 = 287 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{W = 287 \text{ kJ}}$$

---

**(c) Change in internal energy:**

$$\Delta U = m \, c_v \, \Delta T = 5 \times 0.7175 \times 200 = 717.5 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta U = 717.5 \text{ kJ}}$$

---

**(d) Change in enthalpy:**

$$\Delta H = m \, c_p \, \Delta T = Q_p = 1004.5 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta H = 1004.5 \text{ kJ}}$$

---

**Verification of First Law:** $Q_p = \Delta U + W = 717.5 + 287 = 1004.5$ kJ ✓

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** Always verify your numerical answers using the First Law. Marks are often awarded for a "consistency check" step.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Heat Capacity (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Heat Energy Supplied to a Body] --> B{Type of Substance / Condition?}
    B -->|Total Body| C[Heat Capacity C, J/K]
    B -->|Per Unit Mass| D[Specific Heat Capacity c, J per kg per K]
    B -->|Per Mole| E[Molar Heat Capacity C-bar, J per mol per K]

    C -->|C equals m times c| F[Q equals m c delta T]
    D --> F
    E -->|C-bar equals M times c| G[Q equals n C-bar delta T]

    F --> H{Process Type?}
    G --> H
    H -->|Constant Pressure| I[Heat Capacity at Constant Pressure, Cp or c-p]
    H -->|Constant Volume| J[Heat Capacity at Constant Volume, Cv or c-v]

    I --> K[Mayer Relation: Cp minus Cv equals R]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Ratio gamma equals Cp over Cv]

    L --> M{Application Domain?}
    M -->|Solids, Dulong-Petit| N[CM approx 3R equals 24.94 J per mol K]
    M -->|Monatomic Gas, Argon, Helium| O[gamma equals 1.667]
    M -->|Diatomic Gas, Air, N2| P[gamma equals 1.4]
    M -->|Triatomic Gas, CO2| Q[gamma equals 1.286]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Heat Flow in a Thermodynamic System

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Heat Source, Q in] --> B[System Boundary]
    B --> C{Process Path}
    C -->|Isochoric| D[All Q goes to dU, Internal Energy]
    C -->|Isobaric| E[Q splits: dU plus Boundary Work]
    E --> F[Boundary Work, p dV]
```
