# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Condensation** is a phase-change heat transfer process in which a vapor (or gas) in contact with a surface at a temperature lower than its saturation temperature converts into the liquid state, releasing its latent heat of vaporization to the cooler surface. It is the reverse of boiling and is one of the most energy-intensive processes in thermal engineering.

In the context of **Energy Management and Conservation (PESFT414)**, condensation plays a pivotal role in:
- Steam power plant condensers (Rankine cycle)
- Refrigeration and HVAC condenser coils
- Distillation columns and process heat recovery
- Waste heat recovery boilers (economizers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Condensation on a vertical plate and horizontal tubes using **Nusselt's theory of film condensation** is a high-yield topic. Expect derivation/proof-type questions worth 7 to 14 marks in the ESE.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

Imagine pulling a chilled cold-drink bottle from the refrigerator on a humid Kerala monsoon day. Within seconds, the bottle's outer surface becomes wet — that wet film is **condensate** forming from the water vapor in the warm ambient air. The bottle is the cold surface, the humid air is the vapor, and the heat released when vapor turns into liquid is exactly the **latent heat of vaporization ($h_{fg}$)** escaping into your cold drink.

The same physics happens inside a steam condenser: steam (vapor) hits the cold tubes, gives up its hidden latent heat, and drips away as liquid water. **The faster this happens, the more efficient the condenser — and that speed is governed by the condensation heat transfer coefficient.**

### 1.3 Types of Condensation

| Mode | Description | Typical $h$ Range |
|------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Film-wise** | Liquid wets the surface and forms a continuous film | Low ($h \approx 5000\text{–}15000 \ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$) |
| **Drop-wise** | Vapor condenses as discrete droplets; surface not fully wetted | High ($h \approx 50000\text{–}100000 \ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$) |
| **Homogeneous** | Bulk condensation without a foreign surface (e.g., cloud formation) | Rare in engineering |
| **Heterogeneous** | Surface-nucleated, the practical engineering case | Nusselt's theory applies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Physical Constants Used:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \ \mathrm{m/s^2}$
> - Saturation temperature: $T_{sat}$ (K)
> - Wall temperature: $T_w$ (K)
> - Latent heat of vaporization: $h_{fg}$ (J/kg)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Nusselt's Theory of Laminar Film Condensation (1916)

Wilhelm Nusselt (1916) was the first to analytically model the condensate film on a vertical plate. The condensate film is assumed to flow **laminarly** downward under gravity while transferring heat to the cold surface through pure conduction across the film.

### 2.2 Assumptions of Nusselt's Analysis

1. **Steady-state**, laminar flow of condensate film.
2. **Constant fluid properties** (ρ, μ, k) evaluated at the **mean film temperature**: $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_w)/2$.
3. **Inertia of the condensate is negligible** compared to viscous and gravity forces.
4. The vapor is **stationary** (no shear at the liquid–vapor interface — zero vapor velocity).
5. **Heat transfer across the film is by pure conduction** (linear temperature profile).
6. **Sensible cooling (subcooling) of condensate below $T_{sat}$ is negligible.**
7. Wall temperature $T_w$ is **uniform and constant.**
8. The liquid film is **thin** ($δ \ll L$).

### 2.3 Why and How the Theory Works — Stepwise Logic

- **Why a film forms:** The vapor that contacts the cold surface loses its latent heat and liquefies. Gravity pulls this liquid down, while more vapor continuously condenses at the free surface.
- **How the film grows:** As the condensate flows downward, more vapor condenses, so the film thickness $δ$ **increases with distance $x$** from the leading edge.
- **Why the temperature profile is linear:** With negligible convection inside the thin film and no internal heat generation, conduction gives a linear profile from $T_{sat}$ at the free surface to $T_w$ at the wall.
- **How heat transfer coefficient varies:** Because the film thickens with $x$, the **local** $h_x$ decreases with $x$, while the **average** $h_{avg}$ is computed over the full plate.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `\vert` (NOT the pipe symbol `\vert`) in markdown tables — pipe breaks table syntax!**

| # | Geometry | Governing Equation (Nusselt) | Units of $h$ |
|---|----------|-----------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Local h on vertical plate at $x$ | $h_x = \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{4 \, \mu \, (T_{sat}-T_w) \, x}\right]^{1/4}$ | $\mathrm{W/m^2K}$ |
| 2 | Average h over vertical plate of height $L$ | $h_{avg} = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{4 \, \mu \, (T_{sat}-T_w) \, L}\right]^{1/4}$ | $\mathrm{W/m^2K}$ |
| 3 | Film thickness on vertical plate at $x$ | $\delta(x) = \left[\dfrac{4 \, \mu \, k \, (T_{sat}-T_w) \, x}{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$ | m |
| 4 | Single horizontal tube (OD = $D$) | $h = 0.725 \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{\mu \, (T_{sat}-T_w) \, D}\right]^{1/4}$ | $\mathrm{W/m^2K}$ |
| 5 | Vertical tier of $N$ horizontal tubes | $h_N = 0.725 \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{N \, \mu \, (T_{sat}-T_w) \, D}\right]^{1/4}$ | $\mathrm{W/m^2K}$ |
| 6 | Modified form using Reynolds number of film, $Re = 4 \Gamma / \mu$ | $h_{avg} = 0.0076 \, \dfrac{\rho \, (g \, k^3 / \nu^2)^{1/3} \, Re^{0.4}}{?}$ (turbulent correction via Labuntsov) | $\mathrm{W/m^2K}$ |
| 7 | Heat transfer rate (vertical plate) | $Q = h_{avg} \cdot W \cdot L \cdot (T_{sat} - T_w)$ | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Property symbols:** $\rho$ = liquid density (kg/m³), $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (kg/m·s), $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $h_{fg}$ = latent heat (J/kg), $\nu = \mu / \rho$ = kinematic viscosity.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

- **Power Plant Condensers (Kerala's NTPC Kayamkulam, etc.):** Tube bundles condense steam at near-vacuum pressures. Higher $h$ → smaller condenser footprint → capital savings.
- **HVAC Refrigeration Coils:** Air-conditioning units in commercial buildings rely on condensation to reject heat to the outside ambient.
- **Energy Audit Context (PESFT414):** Identifying whether condensers operate in laminar/turbulent film regime tells the auditor whether surface treatments (e.g., dropwise promoters like **gold nanoparticles or oleic acid**) can yield 5×–10× improvements in $h$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Heat Transfer Coefficient on a Vertical Plate

### Step 1 — Velocity Profile in the Falling Film

Apply the **momentum equation** (boundary layer form) in the $x$-direction. With $u = u(y)$ (fully developed, parallel flow), the Navier–Stokes reduces to:

$$\mu \dfrac{d^2 u}{d y^2} = -\rho g$$

This is a second-order ODE describing gravity-driven laminar flow.

### Step 2 — Apply Boundary Conditions

- **No-slip at wall:** $u(0) = 0$
- **Zero shear at free surface:** $\left.\dfrac{du}{dy}\right|_{y=\delta} = 0$

Integrate the first time:

$$\dfrac{du}{dy} = -\dfrac{\rho g}{\mu} \, y + C_1$$

Apply the second BC: $C_1 = \dfrac{\rho g \delta}{\mu}$, hence:

$$\dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{\rho g}{\mu}(\delta - y)$$

Integrate again with $u(0) = 0$:

$$u(y) = \dfrac{\rho g}{\mu}\left(\delta y - \dfrac{y^2}{2}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Mass Flow Rate Per Unit Width ($\Gamma$)

$$\Gamma = \int_0^{\delta} \rho \, u \, dy = \int_0^{\delta} \rho \cdot \dfrac{\rho g}{\mu}\left(\delta y - \dfrac{y^2}{2}\right) dy = \dfrac{\rho^2 g}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\delta^3}{3}$$

$$\Gamma = \dfrac{\rho^2 g \, \delta^3}{3 \mu}$$

### Step 4 — Energy Conservation Across a Differential Strip $dx$

For a vertical plate width $W$, consider an elemental strip between $x$ and $x+dx$. The mass flow rate increases by $d\Gamma$ as more vapor condenses:

$$\dfrac{d\Gamma}{dx} \cdot h_{fg} \, dx = q''(x) \cdot W \, dx$$

Here $q''$ is the local heat flux by conduction through the film:

$$q''(x) = \dfrac{k(T_{sat} - T_w)}{\delta(x)}$$

Therefore:

$$\dfrac{d\Gamma}{dx} \cdot h_{fg} = \dfrac{k \cdot \Delta T}{\delta} \cdot W$$

where $\Delta T = T_{sat} - T_w$.

### Step 5 — Eliminate $d\Gamma/dx$

From Step 3, differentiate $\Gamma$ with respect to $x$:

$$\dfrac{d\Gamma}{dx} = \dfrac{\rho^2 g}{\mu} \, \delta^2 \dfrac{d\delta}{dx}$$

Substituting back:

$$\dfrac{\rho^2 g \, \delta^2}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{d\delta}{dx} \cdot h_{fg} = \dfrac{k \, \Delta T}{\delta}$$

Rearrange:

$$\delta^3 \, d\delta = \dfrac{\mu \, k \, \Delta T}{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg}} \, dx$$

### Step 6 — Integrate with Boundary Condition $\delta(0) = 0$

$$\int_0^{\delta} \delta^3 \, d\delta = \dfrac{\mu \, k \, \Delta T}{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg}} \int_0^{x} dx$$

$$\dfrac{\delta^4}{4} = \dfrac{\mu \, k \, \Delta T \, x}{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg}}$$

Solve for $\delta(x)$:

$$\delta(x) = \left[\dfrac{4 \, \mu \, k \, \Delta T \, x}{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$$

### Step 7 — Local Heat Transfer Coefficient $h_x$

Since $q'' = h_x \cdot \Delta T$ and $q'' = k \Delta T / \delta$:

$$h_x = \dfrac{k}{\delta(x)} = \dfrac{k}{\left[\dfrac{4 \, \mu \, k \, \Delta T \, x}{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}}$$

$$\boxed{h_x = \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{4 \, \mu \, \Delta T \, x}\right]^{1/4}}$$

### Step 8 — Average Heat Transfer Coefficient $h_{avg}$ Over Plate Height $L$

$$h_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^{L} h_x \, dx = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^{L} C \cdot x^{-1/4} \, dx$$

where $C = \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{4 \mu \Delta T}\right]^{1/4}$.

$$h_{avg} = \dfrac{C}{L} \cdot \dfrac{x^{3/4}}{3/4}\bigg|_0^L = \dfrac{4}{3} \cdot \dfrac{C}{L} \cdot L^{3/4}$$

$$h_{avg} = \dfrac{4}{3} \cdot C \cdot L^{-1/4}$$

Substituting $C$ and noting that $4/3 \times (1/4)^{1/4} \approx 0.943$:

$$\boxed{h_{avg} = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{4 \, \mu \, \Delta T \, L}\right]^{1/4}}$$

### Step 9 — Total Heat Transfer Rate

$$Q = h_{avg} \cdot (W \cdot L) \cdot \Delta T = 0.943 \, W \, \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg} \, k^3 \, \Delta T^3 \, L^3}{4 \mu}\right]^{1/4}$$

## 3.2 Validity Range (Nusselt's Correlation)

- Film Reynolds number: $Re_\delta = 4 \Gamma / \mu$
- **Laminar regime:** $Re_\delta < 1800$ → Nusselt equation valid
- **Turbulent regime:** $Re_\delta > 1800$ → Use Labuntsov or Uehara correlation

## 3.3 Effects of Superheat and Vapor Velocity (Correction Factors)

| Real Effect | Correction |
|-------------|------------|
| Vapor velocity $u_\infty$ downward | Increases $h$ (sweeps condensate away) — multiply Nusselt $h$ by correction factor $\left(1 + 0.68 \, J a\right)$ where $Ja = u_\infty^2 \, c_p \, \Delta T / (g h_{fg} L)$ — **Kutateladze–Borishanskii** |
| Vapor moving upward | Impedes condensate drainage, **reduces** $h$ |
| Vapor superheat | Add corrected $h_{fg}' = h_{fg} + 0.68 \, c_{p,\text{vapor}} \, \Delta T_{superheat}$ |
| Non-condensable gases (air, CO₂) | Severely degrade $h$ — must be vented from condensers |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture of Film Condensation

The diagram below maps the physics of Nusselt's film condensation on a vertical plate, showing the energy and mass flows in a way that mirrors how a KTU examiner expects you to sketch it on the answer sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["SATURATED VAPOR AT T_sat"] --> node2["VAPOR LIQUEFIES AT FREE SURFACE"]
    node2 --> node3["RELEASES LATENT HEAT h_fg"]
    node3 --> node4["HEAT CONDUCTS ACROSS FILM delta x"]
    node4 --> node5["HEAT ENTERS COLD WALL AT T_w"]
    node5 --> node6["VERTICAL PLATE WIDTH W HEIGHT L"]
    node6 --> node1
    node2 --> node7["CONDENSATE FILM delta x ACCUMULATES"]
    node7 --> node8["GRAVITY PULLS FILM DOWNWARD"]
    node8 --> node9["VELOCITY PROFILE u y parabolic"]
    node9 --> node10["MASS FLOW RATE Gamma INCREASES WITH x"]
    node10 --> node7
    node4 --> node11["h_x DECREASES WITH x POWER 1 OVER 4"]
    node11 --> node12["h_avg OVER PLATE LENGTH L via NUSSELT"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Condensation Pathway Inside an Industrial Condenser

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inletA["Inlet Saturated Steam T_sat"] --> chamberA["Shell Side Condenser Chamber"]
    chamberA --> tubeBankA{"Tube Bank Geometry"}
    tubeBankA --> vertA["Vertical Plate Analogy Nusselt"]
    tubeBankA --> horizA["Horizontal Tube Analogy"]
    horizA --> singleA["Single Tube h = 0.725 ... D to the power 1 over 4"]
    horizA --> multiA["N Tubes Stacked Vertically h divided by N to the power 1 over 4"]
    vertA --> h_eq["Average h_avg calculated"]
    singleA --> h_eq
    multiA --> h_eq
    h_eq --> Q_total["Total Heat Rejected Q = h_avg A delta T"]
    Q_total --> outletA["Condensate Exits at T_w"]
    outletA --> pumpA["Condensate Pump Returns to Boiler"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for KTU exams:** The "1/4 power" law is the hallmark of film condensation. Anytime you see $\delta \propto x^{1/4}$ or $h \propto x^{-1/4}$, the answer must invoke Nusselt's film theory.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle]** Differentiate between film-wise and drop-wise condensation. Which mode gives a higher heat transfer coefficient and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Film-wise Condensation | Drop-wise Condensation |
|--------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Wetting | Liquid wets the entire surface | Surface not fully wetted; discrete droplets |
| Surface renewal | Continuous film — no renewal | Droplets coalesce and shed → fresh surface exposed |
| Thermal resistance | High (continuous liquid film) | Low (drops expose bare metal) |
| $h$ value | $\sim 5000\text{–}15000 \ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$ | $\sim 50000\text{–}100000 \ \mathrm{W/m^2K}$ |

Drop-wise condensation gives a higher heat transfer coefficient (up to **10× higher**) because the periodic shedding of droplets continually exposes fresh cold surface to incoming vapor, eliminating the insulating liquid film. (3 marks)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Likely Pattern]** State Nusselt's assumptions for film condensation on a vertical plate. Why is $h_{avg}$ proportional to $L^{-1/4}$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):** Nusselt's assumptions: steady state, laminar flow, constant properties at film temperature, negligible inertia and subcooling, stationary vapor, linear temperature profile across the film, uniform wall temperature $T_w$.

$h_{avg} \propto L^{-1/4}$ arises because as the condensate flows down, the film thickens ($\delta \propto x^{1/4}$) due to continuous condensation; a thicker film offers more conductive resistance, so the average $h$ drops weakly (only as the **fourth root**) with plate height. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Vertical Plate Film Condensation*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Adapted Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **local heat transfer coefficient** $h_x$ at a distance $x$ from the leading edge of a vertical plate during film-wise condensation of a saturated vapor. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Saturated steam at atmospheric pressure ($T_{sat} = 100^\circ\mathrm{C}$, $h_{fg} = 2257 \times 10^3 \ \mathrm{J/kg}$) condenses on a vertical plate of height $L = 0.5 \ \mathrm{m}$ maintained at $T_w = 80^\circ\mathrm{C}$. Properties of condensate at $T_f = 90^\circ\mathrm{C}$:
- $\rho = 965 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$
- $\mu = 0.32 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{kg/m \cdot s}$
- $k = 0.677 \ \mathrm{W/m \cdot K}$
- $g = 9.81 \ \mathrm{m/s^2}$

Compute: (i) Average heat transfer coefficient $h_{avg}$, (ii) Total heat transfer rate per metre width, (iii) Film thickness at the bottom of the plate. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 marks:**

Refer to the **complete derivation in Section 3.1**, Steps 1 through 8. Key results the examiner expects you to box:

$$\boxed{h_x = \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{4 \, \mu \, \Delta T \, x}\right]^{1/4}}$$

Each assumption (8 in total) carries 0.5 marks; derivation steps 1–7 carry the remaining marks. Draw the **condensate film coordinate sketch** (vertical plate, $x$ measured from top, $y$ measured outward from wall, parabolic velocity profile) for full credit.

---

**Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $\Delta T = 100 - 80 = 20 \ \mathrm{K}$, $L = 0.5 \ \mathrm{m}$, $W = 1 \ \mathrm{m}$

**(i) Average heat transfer coefficient:**

Substitute into the Nusselt formula:

$$h_{avg} = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{(965)^2 (9.81)(2257 \times 10^3)(0.677)^3}{4 \cdot (0.32 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 20 \cdot 0.5}\right]^{1/4}$$

Compute numerator step-by-step:

$$(965)^2 = 9.31225 \times 10^5$$
$$9.31225 \times 10^5 \times 9.81 = 9.135 \times 10^6$$
$$9.135 \times 10^6 \times 2.257 \times 10^6 = 2.0618 \times 10^{13}$$
$$(0.677)^3 = 0.3103$$
$$\text{Numerator} = 2.0618 \times 10^{13} \times 0.3103 = 6.398 \times 10^{12}$$

Denominator:

$$4 \cdot (0.32 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 20 \cdot 0.5 = 1.28 \times 10^{-2}$$

Ratio:

$$\dfrac{6.398 \times 10^{12}}{1.28 \times 10^{-2}} = 5.0 \times 10^{14}$$

Fourth root of $5.0 \times 10^{14}$:

$$(5.0 \times 10^{14})^{1/4} = (5.0)^{1/4} \times 10^{3.5} = 1.495 \times 3162.3 \approx 4728$$

$$h_{avg} = 0.943 \times 4728 \approx \mathbf{4459 \ \mathrm{W/m^2K}}$$

**(ii) Total heat transfer rate per metre width:**

$$Q = h_{avg} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 4459 \cdot (1 \cdot 0.5) \cdot 20 = \mathbf{44{,}590 \ \mathrm{W/m}}$$

**(iii) Film thickness at $x = L = 0.5$ m:**

$$\delta = \left[\dfrac{4 \mu k \Delta T L}{\rho^2 g h_{fg}}\right]^{1/4}$$

$$\delta = \left[\dfrac{4 \cdot (0.32 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.677 \cdot 20 \cdot 0.5}{(965)^2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2257 \times 10^3}\right]^{1/4}$$

Numerator: $4 \cdot 0.32 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.677 \cdot 10 = 8.665 \times 10^{-3}$

Denominator: $9.31225 \times 10^5 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2.257 \times 10^6 = 2.0618 \times 10^{13}$

Ratio: $8.665 \times 10^{-3} / 2.0618 \times 10^{13} = 4.20 \times 10^{-16}$

Fourth root: $(4.20 \times 10^{-16})^{1/4} = (4.20)^{1/4} \times 10^{-4} = 1.43 \times 10^{-4}$

$$\delta \approx \mathbf{0.143 \ \mathrm{mm}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Horizontal Tube Condensation*

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted Pattern]**

**(a)** Starting from the modified Nusselt equation for film condensation on a **vertical tier of $N$ horizontal tubes** of outer diameter $D$, derive the expression and explain why stacking tubes vertically **reduces** the average $h$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A condenser has **12 horizontal tubes in a vertical stack**, each of outer diameter $D = 25 \ \mathrm{mm}$. Saturated steam at $T_{sat} = 120^\circ\mathrm{C}$ condenses on the tubes maintained at $T_w = 90^\circ\mathrm{C}$. Condensate properties at $T_f = 105^\circ\mathrm{C}$:
- $\rho = 955 \ \mathrm{kg/m^3}$, $\mu = 0.27 \times 10^{-3} \ \mathrm{kg/m \cdot s}$, $k = 0.682 \ \mathrm{W/m \cdot K}$, $h_{fg} = 2202 \times 10^3 \ \mathrm{J/kg}$

Calculate: (i) Average heat transfer coefficient for the entire stack, (ii) Heat transfer rate per metre tube length. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 marks:**

For a single horizontal tube, the condensate film drains around the circumference. The effective "height" over which the film flows is comparable to the tube diameter $D$ for a single tube. When $N$ tubes are stacked vertically, condensate from the upper tube **drains onto the lower tube**, so the lower tube receives a thicker combined film acting like a film flowing over an equivalent height of $N \cdot D$.

Therefore, replacing $L = D$ in the vertical plate Nusselt equation by the equivalent height $N \cdot D$ (or by direct scaling argument: $h \propto L^{-1/4}$), we get:

$$h_N = 0.725 \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{N \, \mu \, \Delta T \, D}\right]^{1/4}$$

Thus $h_N = h_1 / N^{1/4}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $N = 12$, $D = 0.025 \ \mathrm{m}$, $\Delta T = 120 - 90 = 30 \ \mathrm{K}$, $L_t = 1 \ \mathrm{m}$ per tube.

**(i) Average heat transfer coefficient:**

$$h = 0.725 \left[\dfrac{(955)^2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 2202 \times 10^3 \cdot (0.682)^3}{12 \cdot 0.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 30 \cdot 0.025}\right]^{1/4}$$

Numerator components:
- $(955)^2 = 9.12025 \times 10^5$
- $\times \, 9.81 = 8.947 \times 10^6$
- $\times \, 2.202 \times 10^6 = 1.970 \times 10^{13}$
- $(0.682)^3 = 0.3172$
- Numerator $= 1.970 \times 10^{13} \times 0.3172 = 6.249 \times 10^{12}$

Denominator: $12 \cdot 0.27 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 30 \cdot 0.025 = 2.43 \times 10^{-3}$

Ratio: $6.249 \times 10^{12} / 2.43 \times 10^{-3} = 2.572 \times 10^{15}$

Fourth root: $(2.572 \times 10^{15})^{1/4} = (2.572)^{1/4} \cdot 10^{3.75} = 1.268 \times 5623 = 7130$

$$h = 0.725 \times 7130 \approx \mathbf{5170 \ \mathrm{W/m^2K}}$$

**(ii) Heat transfer rate per metre tube length:**

Total external surface of stack per metre length:

$$A = N \cdot \pi D \cdot L_t = 12 \cdot \pi \cdot 0.025 \cdot 1 = 0.9425 \ \mathrm{m^2}$$

$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 5170 \cdot 0.9425 \cdot 30 = \mathbf{146{,}170 \ \mathrm{W}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Where Students Commonly Lose Marks):**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant 0.943 or 0.725.** Both are crucial — omitting them costs a full mark.
> 2. **Wrong temperature for property evaluation.** Properties must be at the **film mean temperature** $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_w)/2$, NOT at $T_{sat}$. A common error.
> 3. **Mixing units.** $k$ in W/m·K, $h_{fg}$ in J/kg, $\mu$ in kg/(m·s), $\rho$ in kg/m³. Mixed units (e.g., kcal) lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Using $L$ instead of $D$** for the horizontal tube formula, or vice versa.
> 5. **Forgetting the $N^{1/4}$ factor** for vertical stacks — a favourite KTU trap.
> 6. **Not drawing the film coordinate diagram.** Always draw the vertical plate with $x$ (downward) and $y$ (outward), parabolic velocity profile, linear temperature profile.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Condensation** = phase change from vapor to liquid with **latent heat release**.
- **Two modes:** Film-wise (low $h$) and Drop-wise (high $h$ — up to 10× greater).
- **Nusselt's theory** applies to **laminar film-wise** condensation ($Re_\delta < 1800$).
- **Local $h_x$ on vertical plate:** $h_x = \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{4 \mu \Delta T \, x}\right]^{1/4}$ — **decreases** with $x$.
- **Average $h_{avg}$ on vertical plate:** $h_{avg} = 0.943 \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{4 \mu \Delta T \, L}\right]^{1/4}$.
- **Film thickness:** $\delta(x) \propto x^{1/4}$.
- **Horizontal tube (single):** $h = 0.725 \left[\dfrac{\rho^2 g \, h_{fg} \, k^3}{\mu \Delta T \, D}\right]^{1/4}$.
- **Vertical stack of $N$ tubes:** $h_N = h_1 / N^{1/4}$ — stacking **reduces** $h$.
- **Properties must be evaluated at the film mean temperature** $T_f = (T_{sat} + T_w)/2$.
- **Non-condensable gases** drastically degrade condensation — always vent the condenser.
- **Vapor velocity correction:** downward flow enhances $h$; upward flow impairs it.
- **Turbulent film ($Re_\delta > 1800$):** switch to Labuntsov or Uehara correlation.
- **Practical boosters:** surface coatings (oleic acid, gold, PTFE) can promote dropwise condensation and dramatically improve $h$ — a hot topic in **Energy Management & Conservation** audits.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steam

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steam** is the gaseous phase of water formed when water absorbs sufficient latent heat at a given pressure to overcome its sensible heat content. In the context of **Energy Management and Conservation (PESFT414)**, steam is treated as the most widely used **working fluid** in thermal power generation, process industries, and space heating because of its high latent heat carrying capacity, non-toxicity, and ease of controlled generation.

**Thermodynamic Definition:**
Steam is water vapour in the **saturation region** (wet steam) or above it (**dry saturated / superheated steam**). At a given pressure, the substance water exists in three phases: **saturated liquid (water)**, **two-phase mixture (wet steam)**, and **saturated vapour (dry steam)**. The boundary between the liquid and vapour phases on a **Pressure–Temperature (P–T) saturation curve** defines the conditions under which steam is produced.

**Classification of Steam (KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping):**

| Classification | Physical State | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Saturated Liquid (Compressed Liquid)** | Liquid at sub-cooled temperature | Boiler feedwater |
| **Wet Steam (Two-Phase)** | Liquid + Vapour mixture (with dryness fraction $x$) | Process heating |
| **Dry Saturated Steam** | Pure vapour at saturation temperature | Industrial processes, sterilization |
| **Superheated Steam** | Vapour heated above saturation temperature | Power generation (Turbine input) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Alignment (CO1 – KTU 2024):** This topic enables the student to *"understand the thermodynamic properties of steam and apply steam tables / Mollier diagram in energy auditing of steam systems."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **sponge (saturated water)** held at exactly $100^\circ\text{C}$ in a sealed container. The sponge is fully soaked but cannot absorb any more water — it is on the verge of evaporating. Now if you slowly pour **energy (heat)** into it drop by drop, water begins to evaporate *without* any temperature change. The temperature of the sponge stays locked at $100^\circ\text{C}$ until the last drop has evaporated. This is the magic of **latent heat**.

- **Sensible heat** = heat that changes the *temperature* (what your hand feels on a hot cup).
- **Latent heat** = "hidden" heat that changes the *phase* (the energy that turns the liquid into vapour without a thermometer noticing).
- **Superheating** = continuing to add heat *after* all water has turned to steam, again raising the temperature — like reheating the now-empty sponge.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Pressure Cooker:** When you lock a pressure cooker, the boiling point of water rises because pressure rises. The steam inside is at a higher temperature and carries far more energy than steam at atmospheric pressure. This is the same reason **high-pressure boilers in power plants** can extract more energy per kilogram of steam — more pressure, more stored latent energy.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are mandatory for KTU numerical problems:

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$
- **Specific Gas Constant for Water Vapour:** $R_v = 0.4615 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- **Latent Heat at $100^\circ\text{C}$, $101.325 \text{ kPa}$:** $h_{fg} = 2257 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- **Specific Heat of Superheated Steam:** $c_p \approx 2.1 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- **Specific Heat of Liquid Water:** $c_p \approx 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$

> [!NOTE]
> All KTU energy calculations default to **SI units**: Pressure in **kPa** or **MPa**, Temperature in **$^\circ$C** or **K**, Enthalpy in **kJ/kg**, Entropy in **kJ/(kg·K)**, Specific Volume in **m³/kg**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formation of Steam — Stage-Wise Process

The process of converting liquid water at room temperature into superheated steam occurs in **five distinct thermodynamic stages**. Each stage is critical for an energy manager to identify **where heat is being added** and **where losses can be minimized**.

### Stage 1 — Sensible Heating of Liquid (Sub-cooled Region)
Water at ambient temperature $T_1 < T_{sat}$ absorbs **sensible heat** until it reaches the **saturation temperature** $T_{sat}$ corresponding to the boiler pressure. The temperature *rises* during this stage.

### Stage 2 — Saturated Liquid State (Point on Saturation Line)
At this point, water is at saturation temperature but has *not yet* started to vaporize. Its enthalpy is denoted $h_f$ (enthalpy of saturated liquid).

### Stage 3 — Latent Heat Addition (Two-Phase Region)
This is the **biggest energy-absorbing phase**. Water begins to vaporize at constant temperature $T_{sat}$ and pressure $P$. The mixture is called **wet steam**. The energy absorbed is $h_{fg}$ (latent heat of vaporization). The proportion of vapour by mass in this mixture is the **dryness fraction** $x$.

### Stage 4 — Dry Saturated Steam
The last drop of liquid has evaporated. The steam is at saturation temperature, fully dry, with enthalpy $h_g = h_f + h_{fg}$.

### Stage 5 — Superheating
Additional heat is supplied to raise the steam temperature *above* $T_{sat}$. The steam is now **superheated** and behaves as a near-ideal gas. Its temperature is $T_{sup} > T_{sat}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Management Insight:** Most heat is consumed in **Stage 3 (latent heat)**. Improving **boiler efficiency** and **insulation** in this stage yields the largest energy savings. The enthalpy of dry saturated steam $h_g$ is the **calorific value** reference for steam-based energy audits.

---

## 2.2 Critical Property Definitions

### Dryness Fraction ($x$)
The dryness fraction is the **mass of dry saturated vapour** present in a unit mass of wet steam.

$$x = \frac{m_v}{m_v + m_l} = \frac{m_v}{m_{total}}$$

where $m_v$ is the mass of vapour and $m_l$ is the mass of liquid in suspension. Values range from $0$ (saturated liquid) to $1$ (dry saturated vapour).

### Specific Enthalpy ($h$)
The total heat content of 1 kg of steam measured from a reference state (saturated liquid at $0^\circ\text{C}$).

### Specific Entropy ($s$)
A measure of the *unavailable* energy in steam. It is essential for determining the **theoretical efficiency** of Rankine cycles used in steam power plants.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **EXAM ALERT:** The following table is the single most referenced set of equations in PESFT414 Module 1. Memorize the units and the conditions of applicability.

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Applicable Region |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Dryness Fraction | $x = m_v / m_{total}$ | Dimensionless | Wet Steam |
| 2 | Specific Enthalpy (Wet) | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | Wet Steam |
| 3 | Specific Enthalpy (Superheated) | $h = h_g + c_{p,sup}(T_{sup} - T_{sat})$ | kJ/kg | Superheated |
| 4 | Specific Volume (Wet) | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ | m³/kg | Wet Steam |
| 5 | Specific Entropy (Wet) | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Wet Steam |
| 6 | Internal Energy | $u = h - Pv$ | kJ/kg | All States |
| 7 | Latent Heat at Pressure $P$ | $h_{fg}(P)$ from steam tables | kJ/kg | Phase Change |
| 8 | Total Heat of Steam | $Q = m \cdot (h_f + x \cdot h_{fg})$ | kJ | Wet Steam |
| 9 | Mass of Steam | $m = Q / (h_g - h_f)$ | kg | Heating |
| 10 | Steam Quality (Energy) | $E_x = m(h - h_0 - T_0(s - s_0))$ | kJ | Exergy Analysis |

> **Note on Notation:** $h_f$ = enthalpy of saturated liquid, $h_g$ = enthalpy of saturated vapour, $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ = latent heat, $v_f$ = specific volume of saturated liquid, $v_g$ = specific volume of saturated vapour, $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **Energy Management**, the steam property $h_g$ (total enthalpy of dry saturated steam) acts as the **benchmark for energy audit calculations** of boilers. The **steam flow rate** required to deliver a known thermal load $Q$ is computed as:

$$m_{steam} = \frac{Q_{load}}{(h_{steam,out} - h_{feedwater,in})}$$

This single equation drives **boiler efficiency analysis**, **waste heat recovery sizing**, and **cogeneration feasibility studies** — all of which are core PESFT414 assessment areas.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Enthalpy of Wet Steam

**Starting Premise:** Wet steam is a homogeneous two-phase mixture. By the principle of **additivity of extensive properties**, the total enthalpy of 1 kg of wet steam equals the sum of enthalpies contributed by the liquid and vapour fractions.

Let $m_v$ = mass of vapour, $m_l$ = mass of liquid. Then $m_v + m_l = 1 \text{ kg}$ and $m_v = x$, $m_l = 1 - x$.

**Step 1 — Reference State Selection**
Take saturated liquid at the same pressure as the reference. Its enthalpy is $h_f$ (kJ/kg).

**Step 2 — Enthalpy of the Liquid Fraction**
Mass of liquid = $(1 - x)$ kg. Enthalpy contribution:
$$H_l = (1 - x) \cdot h_f$$

**Step 3 — Enthalpy of the Vapour Fraction**
Mass of vapour = $x$ kg. The vapour is at saturation, so its enthalpy is $h_g$. Enthalpy contribution:
$$H_v = x \cdot h_g$$

**Step 4 — Total Enthalpy**
$$h_{wet} = H_l + H_v = (1 - x)h_f + x \cdot h_g$$

**Step 5 — Algebraic Simplification**
Expand the first term:
$$h_{wet} = h_f - x \cdot h_f + x \cdot h_g$$
$$h_{wet} = h_f + x(h_g - h_f)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute Latent Heat Definition**
Since $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$, the final compact form is:

$$\boxed{h_{wet} = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}}$$

This is the **master equation** for all wet-steam enthalpy calculations in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Specific Volume of Wet Steam

By the same additivity principle applied to volume:

$$v_{wet} = (1 - x) v_f + x \cdot v_g$$

Subtracting and adding $x \cdot v_f$:

$$v_{wet} = v_f - x \cdot v_f + x \cdot v_g = v_f + x(v_g - v_f)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{v_{wet} = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}}$$

where $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ is the change in specific volume during vaporization. **KTU Numerical Tip:** Because $v_g \gg v_f$ (often by a factor of $10^3$ or more), the approximation $v_{wet} \approx x \cdot v_g$ is frequently used in **preliminary energy audits** for error tolerance of $<1\%$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Internal Energy of Steam

From the **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a closed system, the thermodynamic identity relating enthalpy, internal energy, and flow work is:

$$h = u + Pv$$

Rearranging for internal energy:

$$u = h - Pv$$

For wet steam, substituting the enthalpy expression derived in Section 3.1:

$$u_{wet} = (h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}) - P(v_f + x \cdot v_{fg})$$

Expanding:

$$u_{wet} = h_f - P \cdot v_f + x(h_{fg} - P \cdot v_{fg})$$

Recognizing that $h_f = u_f + P v_f$ and $h_{fg} = u_{fg} + P v_{fg}$, this reduces elegantly to:

$$\boxed{u_{wet} = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}}$$

This parallel structure demonstrates that **all specific properties of wet steam follow the same linear interpolation in $x$** between the saturated liquid and saturated vapour values.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Entropy of Wet Steam

The differential change in entropy for a phase change at constant temperature and pressure is:

$$ds = \frac{dQ_{rev}}{T}$$

For vaporization, the heat absorbed per kg equals $h_{fg}$ at constant $T_{sat}$. Hence the **specific entropy of vaporization** is:

$$s_{fg} = \frac{h_{fg}}{T_{sat}}$$

Following the same additivity argument as enthalpy:

$$s_{wet} = (1 - x) s_f + x \cdot s_g$$

By algebraic manipulation analogous to the enthalpy derivation:

$$\boxed{s_{wet} = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Management Implication:** Entropy is the gateway to **Second-Law (Exergy) Analysis**. In a steam power plant, the **isentropic efficiency** of the turbine compares the *actual* exit entropy to the *ideal* (isentropic) exit entropy. A KTU numerical will often ask you to compute isentropic work using $w_s = h_1 - h_{2s}$, where $h_{2s}$ is found by tracing constant entropy on the Mollier diagram.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A boiler produces wet steam at $P = 1.0 \text{ MPa}$ with dryness fraction $x = 0.85$. The feedwater enters at $40^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate (a) the enthalpy of the wet steam, (b) the specific volume, and (c) the total heat supplied per kg of steam generated.

**Steam Table Data at $P = 1.0 \text{ MPa}$:**
- $h_f = 762.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $h_{fg} = 2015.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $v_f = 0.001127 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$
- $v_g = 0.19436 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$

**Part (a) — Enthalpy of wet steam:**
Apply the master equation:
$$h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$$

Substituting values:
$$h = 762.81 + 0.85 \times 2015.3$$

$$h = 762.81 + 1713.005 = 2475.815 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Part (b) — Specific volume:**
$$v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$$
$$v_{fg} = v_g - v_f = 0.19436 - 0.001127 = 0.193233 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$
$$v = 0.001127 + 0.85 \times 0.193233$$
$$v = 0.001127 + 0.164248 = 0.165375 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Part (c) — Total heat per kg of steam:**
The feedwater enthalpy at $40^\circ\text{C}$ is approximately $h_{fw} = c_p \cdot T = 4.186 \times 40 = 167.44 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

Heat added per kg:
$$Q = h_{steam} - h_{fw} = 2475.815 - 167.44 = 2308.375 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

This is the **specific steam consumption** parameter used in **boiler efficiency calculations**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Which Formula to Use When?"

| Steam State Identified | Use This Formula | KTU Problem Clue Words |
|---|---|---|
| Wet Steam | $h = h_f + x h_{fg}$ | "dryness fraction given", "wet steam" |
| Dry Saturated | $h = h_g$ | "dry saturated", "saturation temperature" |
| Superheated | $h = h_g + c_p(T_{sup} - T_{sat})$ | "superheated to $T$", "degrees of superheat" |
| Condensate Return | $h = c_p \cdot T$ (liquid) | "feedwater at $T^\circ$C" |
| Heat Exchanger | $Q = m(h_{out} - h_{in})$ | "heat transferred", "process load" |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — The Five Stages of Steam Formation

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the thermodynamic transitions during steam generation, the dominant energy input stage, and the typical **energy audit** loss points in a real boiler plant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STAGE1: Cold Feedwater at T1] -->|Sensible Heat Q1| B[STAGE2: Saturated Liquid at Tsat]
    B -->|Latent Heat Q2 BOIL| C[STAGE3: Wet Steam Two-Phase]
    C -->|Continued Latent Heat| D[STAGE4: Dry Saturated Steam at Tsat]
    D -->|Superheat Q3| E[STAGE5: Superheated Steam at Tsup]
    E --> F[Process or Turbine End Use]
    
    L1[Audit Loss: Flue Gas] -.-> B
    L2[Audit Loss: Radiation] -.-> C
    L3[Audit Loss: Blowdown] -.-> D
    
    A1[Q1: 5 to 10 percent] --- A
    A2[Q2: 70 to 80 percent DOMINANT] --- C
    A3[Q3: 5 to 15 percent] --- E
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **width of the energy contribution** is represented conceptually in the labels `A1`, `A2`, `A3` — Stage 3 (latent heating) is the **dominant energy sink**.
- The dashed arrows `L1`, `L2`, `L3` are the **typical energy audit loss points** that a PESFT414 energy manager must quantify.

---

## 4.2 Property-Process Block Diagram — T-s and P-v Landscapes

The Mermaid block representation below maps the **regions and process lines** encountered on the standard steam thermodynamic charts used in KTU problems.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHASE_REGION[Saturated Mixture Two Phase Dome]
        REG_A[Subcooled Liquid Region: T less than Tsat]
        REG_B[Wet Steam Region: Inside the Dome]
        REG_C[Superheated Region: T greater than Tsat]
    end
    
    SAT_LINE_A[Saturated Liquid Line: x equals 0] --> REG_B
    SAT_LINE_B[Saturated Vapour Line: x equals 1] --> REG_B
    CRIT_PT[CRITICAL POINT: 22.06 MPa, 374.14 C] --> REG_B
    
    PROC1[Isobaric Heating: Constant P] -->|1 to 2| REG_B
    PROC2[Isothermal Evaporation: Constant T] -->|2 to 3| REG_B
    PROC3[Isentropic Expansion: Constant s] -->|3 to 4| REG_B
    PROC4[Isochoric Heating: Constant v] -->|4 to 5| REG_B
```

**Mapping to KTU Concepts:**
- The **Dome** is bounded on the left by the saturated liquid line and on the right by the saturated vapour line.
- The **Critical Point** marks the end of the saturation curve — beyond it, no phase distinction exists.
- The four process types (isobaric, isothermal, isentropic, isochoric) are the **process cycles** tested in KTU Module 1 short-answer and numerical problems.

---

## 4.3 Energy Audit Block Flow for a Steam System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Fuel Input: Chemical Energy] --> B1[Boiler Combustion Chamber]
    B1 -->|Heat Loss L1 Flue Gas| EX1[Exhaust Stack]
    B1 -->|Heat Loss L2 Radiation| EX2[Boiler Shell]
    B1 -->|Useful Heat Q useful| STM[Steam Output: m dot times h out]
    STM --> PROC[Process / Turbine]
    PROC --> COND[Condensate Return at h in]
    COND -->|Makeup Water| B1
    
    AUDIT[Energy Manager: Computes Boiler Efficiency equals Q useful divided by Fuel Energy]
    AUDIT --- STM
    AUDIT --- F1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The audit point `AUDIT` is where the **KTU Module 1 → Module 2** transition occurs. PESFT414 Module 2 (Energy Audit) uses the steam property data computed here as the *input* to efficiency calculations. A clean understanding of $h$, $x$, and steam tables is the prerequisite for the entire audit methodology.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Format Note (KTU 2024):** Part A carries 3 marks with a 50-word expected answer length. Clarity of definition and the supporting formula earn full marks.

### Question 1: Define Dryness Fraction. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Model Answer:**
The dryness fraction ($x$) of steam is defined as the ratio of the mass of dry saturated vapour ($m_v$) to the total mass of the liquid–vapour mixture ($m_v + m_l$) in a given sample of wet steam. Mathematically,
$$x = \frac{m_v}{m_v + m_l}$$
Its value ranges from 0 (saturated liquid) to 1 (dry saturated vapour). It is a critical parameter because all specific properties of wet steam — enthalpy, entropy, specific volume, internal energy — are linear functions of $x$. **[3 Marks]**

### Question 2: Distinguish Between Wet Steam and Superheated Steam. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Wet Steam | Superheated Steam |
|---|---|---|
| Phase | Two-phase (liquid + vapour) | Single-phase (vapour) |
| Temperature | Equal to $T_{sat}$ at given pressure | Greater than $T_{sat}$ |
| Dryness fraction $x$ | $0 < x < 1$ | $x = 1$ (with extra superheat) |
| Specific heat usage | Not defined (phase change) | $c_{p,sup} \approx 2.1 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ |
| Application | Process heating | Power generation turbines |

**[3 Marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**In a steam power plant, steam is generated in a boiler at a pressure of 1.5 MPa with a dryness fraction of 0.9. The feedwater enters the boiler at a temperature of 60°C. Calculate:**

**(a)** The specific enthalpy, specific entropy, and specific volume of the wet steam produced.
**(b)** The total heat required to produce 1000 kg of steam per hour, and the equivalent fuel consumption if the boiler efficiency is 75% and the calorific value of fuel is 42 MJ/kg.

**Steam Table Data at $P = 1.5 \text{ MPa}$ ($T_{sat} = 198.32^\circ\text{C}$):**
- $h_f = 844.84 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_{fg} = 1947.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$
- $s_f = 2.3147 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $s_{fg} = 3.7892 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- $v_f = 0.001153 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$, $v_g = 0.13177 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$

---

#### Part (a) — Specific Properties (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Specific Enthalpy**
Apply the master equation $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$:

$$h = 844.84 + 0.9 \times 1947.3$$
$$h = 844.84 + 1752.57 = 2597.41 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Specific Entropy**
Apply $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$:

$$s = 2.3147 + 0.9 \times 3.7892$$
$$s = 2.3147 + 3.41028 = 5.72498 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$$

**Step 3 — Specific Volume**
Compute $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f = 0.13177 - 0.001153 = 0.130617 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$.

$$v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$$
$$v = 0.001153 + 0.9 \times 0.130617 = 0.001153 + 0.117555 = 0.118708 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Result:** $h = 2597.41 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $s = 5.725 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $v = 0.1187 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Part (b) — Heat and Fuel Calculation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Feedwater Enthalpy**
At $T_{fw} = 60^\circ\text{C}$, $h_{fw} \approx c_p \cdot T = 4.186 \times 60 = 251.16 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

**Step 2 — Heat Supplied per kg of Steam**
$$q = h_{steam} - h_{fw} = 2597.41 - 251.16 = 2346.25 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Total Heat per Hour**
$$Q_{hourly} = 1000 \times 2346.25 = 2{,}346{,}250 \text{ kJ/h} = 2346.25 \text{ MJ/h}$$

**Step 4 — Fuel Consumption**
With boiler efficiency $\eta_b = 0.75$ and calorific value $CV = 42{,}000 \text{ kJ/kg}$:

$$m_{fuel} = \frac{Q_{hourly}}{\eta_b \cdot CV} = \frac{2{,}346{,}250}{0.75 \times 42{,}000}$$

$$m_{fuel} = \frac{2{,}346{,}250}{31{,}500} = 74.48 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Result:** $Q = 2346.25 \text{ MJ/h}$ and $m_{fuel} = 74.48 \text{ kg/h}$. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**A boiler generates superheated steam at 1.8 MPa and 300°C from feedwater at 30°C.**

**(a)** Determine the total heat added per kg of water converted to steam, indicating the sensible heat, latent heat, and superheat components separately.
**(b)** If the steam is expanded isentropically in a turbine to a pressure of 0.1 MPa, determine the isentropic work output per kg of steam. Use Mollier diagram values.

**Steam Table Data:**
- At $1.8 \text{ MPa}$, $T_{sat} = 207.15^\circ\text{C}$, $h_f = 884.58 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_{fg} = 1910.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_g = 2794.88 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $s_f = 2.3975 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $s_{fg} = 3.6613 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $s_g = 6.0588 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- At $1.8 \text{ MPa}$, $300^\circ\text{C}$ (superheated): $h_{sup} = 3038.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $s_{sup} = 6.3795 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$
- At $0.1 \text{ MPa}$: $h_f = 417.46 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $h_{fg} = 2258.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $s_f = 1.3026 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$, $s_{fg} = 6.0568 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$

---

#### Part (a) — Heat Components (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Sensible Heat to Saturation ($30^\circ\text{C}$ to $207.15^\circ\text{C}$)**
$$q_1 = c_p (T_{sat} - T_{fw}) = 4.186 \times (207.15 - 30) = 4.186 \times 177.15 = 741.53 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2 — Latent Heat of Vaporization at $1.8 \text{ MPa}$**
$$q_2 = h_{fg} = 1910.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Superheat from $207.15^\circ\text{C}$ to $300^\circ\text{C}$**
$$q_3 = c_{p,sup} (T_{sup} - T_{sat}) = 2.1 \times (300 - 207.15) = 2.1 \times 92.85 = 194.985 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Total Heat**
$$q_{total} = q_1 + q_2 + q_3 = 741.53 + 1910.3 + 194.985 = 2846.815 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Verification using direct enthalpy difference:** $q = h_{sup} - h_{fw} = 3038.9 - (4.186 \times 30) = 3038.9 - 125.58 = 2913.32 \text{ kJ/kg}$. The discrepancy of $\sim 2\%$ arises from averaging $c_p$ values; both answers are acceptable within KTU tolerance.

**Result:** $q_1 = 741.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $q_2 = 1910.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $q_3 = 195.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$, Total $\approx 2846.8 \text{ kJ/kg}$. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Part (b) — Isentropic Turbine Work (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Initial State in Turbine (State 1)**
$h_1 = 3038.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$, $s_1 = 6.3795 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.

**Step 2 — Identify Final State (State 2s) at $P_2 = 0.1 \text{ MPa}$ with $s_{2s} = s_1$**
Check if state 2s is wet or superheated at $0.1 \text{ MPa}$:
At $0.1 \text{ MPa}$: $s_g = s_f + s_{fg} = 1.3026 + 6.0568 = 7.3594 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.
Since $s_1 = 6.3795 < s_g = 7.3594$, the exit state lies in the **wet region**.

**Step 3 — Calculate Exit Dryness Fraction**
$$s_1 = s_{f2} + x_2 \cdot s_{fg2}$$
$$6.3795 = 1.3026 + x_2 \times 6.0568$$
$$x_2 = \frac{6.3795 - 1.3026}{6.0568} = \frac{5.0769}{6.0568} = 0.8382$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Exit Enthalpy**
$$h_{2s} = h_{f2} + x_2 \cdot h_{fg2} = 417.46 + 0.8382 \times 2258.0$$
$$h_{2s} = 417.46 + 1892.66 = 2310.12 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 5 — Isentropic Work**
$$w_s = h_1 - h_{2s} = 3038.9 - 2310.12 = 728.78 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Result:** $w_{isentropic} = 728.78 \text{ kJ/kg}$ of steam. **[7 Marks]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in PESFT414 Steam Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of latent heat:** $h_{fg}$ is always *positive* (heat absorbed) during vaporization. Students often mistakenly subtract it. **Penalty: −1 mark per occurrence.**
>
> 2. **Confusing $h_{fg}$ at different pressures:** $h_{fg}$ at $0.1 \text{ MPa}$ ($2258 \text{ kJ/kg}$) is **very different** from $h_{fg}$ at $1.5 \text{ MPa}$ ($1947 \text{ kJ/kg}$). Always read the value from the steam table corresponding to the **exact pressure** given in the problem.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the feedwater enthalpy in total heat calculation:** Forgetting to subtract $h_{fw}$ from $h_{steam}$ in the energy balance loses **2 full marks** in Part B numericals.
>
> 4. **Using $c_p$ of liquid water (4.186) for superheat region:** Superheated steam $c_p$ is approximately **2.1 kJ/(kg·K)**, not 4.186. Mixing them is a frequent error.
>
> 5. **Not specifying units in the final answer:** KTU valuation explicitly checks for units. An answer like "$h = 2475$" without "kJ/kg" is penalized **0.5 mark**.
>
> 6. **Dryness fraction $x$ exceeding 1:** If your calculation gives $x > 1$ or $x < 0$, you have either misread the table or applied the wrong formula. This is an automatic **−1 mark** and signals the examiner that the concept is not internalized.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU PESFT414 Module 1: Steam**

- **Definition:** Steam = water vapour; classified as wet, dry saturated, or superheated.
- **Saturation Curve:** Boundary on P-T diagram separating liquid and vapour phases; terminates at the **critical point** ($22.06 \text{ MPa}$, $374.14^\circ\text{C}$).
- **Dryness Fraction:** $x = m_v / m_{total}$; range is $[0, 1]$; linearly interpolates all wet-steam properties.
- **Master Enthalpy Formula:** $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ (wet); $h = h_g + c_p(T_{sup} - T_{sat})$ (superheated).
- **Master Entropy Formula:** $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$.
- **Master Specific Volume Formula:** $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$.
- **Internal Energy Identity:** $u = h - Pv$ (general) and $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ (wet steam).
- **Latent Heat $h_{fg}$:** Always taken from **steam tables** at the given pressure; never assume a fixed value across pressures.
- **Specific Heats:** Liquid water $c_p \approx 4.186 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$; superheated steam $c_p \approx 2.1 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}$.
- **Process Types in Steam Systems:** Isobaric (boiler), isothermal (phase change), isentropic (turbine ideal), isochoric.
- **Energy Audit Linkage:** $Q = m(h_{out} - h_{in})$ is the fundamental energy balance used to compute **boiler efficiency**, **steam consumption**, and **waste heat recovery potential**.
- **Typical Energy Distribution:** Sensible heat 5–10%, Latent heat 70–80% (dominant), Superheat 5–15%.
- **Mollier Diagram (h–s chart):** Used to find isentropic exit states graphically; essential for **turbine efficiency** and **Rankine cycle** problems.
- **SI Units (mandatory in KTU):** kPa or MPa for pressure, $^\circ$C or K for temperature, kJ/kg for enthalpy, kJ/(kg·K) for entropy, m³/kg for specific volume.
- **Steam Calorimeters:** Used experimentally to determine dryness fraction ($x$) of real steam samples — **barrel/throttling calorimeter** concepts may be tested in viva.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When a KTU numerical gives you steam at a known pressure and asks for properties, **always start by extracting $h_f$, $h_{fg}$, $v_f$, $v_g$, $s_f$, $s_{fg}$ from the steam table first** — this single discipline prevents 80% of the calculation errors observed in university exam scripts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moist Air, Humidity, and Heat Transfer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Moist Air, Humidity, and Heat Transfer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Moist Air

**Moist air** is a binary mechanical mixture of **dry air** and **water vapour**. For all engineering calculations in air-conditioning, ventilation, drying, and energy auditing, moist air is treated as an **ideal gas mixture**, with each constituent obeying the **Dalton’s Law of Partial Pressures** independently.

Dry air by composition (standard reference):

| Component | Volume % | Molecular Mass (kg/kmol) |
|---|---|---|
| Nitrogen (N₂) | **78.03** | 28.013 |
| Oxygen (O₂) | **20.99** | 31.999 |
| Argon (Ar) | **0.94** | 39.948 |
| Carbon Dioxide (CO₂) | **0.03** | 44.010 |
| Trace gases (Ne, He, etc.) | < 0.01 | — |

The **apparent molecular mass of dry air** = **28.966 kg/kmol**, and the **gas constant for dry air, R_a = 0.287 kJ/kg·K** (≈ **287 J/kg·K**). The gas constant for water vapour, **R_v = 0.4615 kJ/kg·K**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU problems, "air" almost always means **moist air** unless stated otherwise. Many students lose marks by computing properties for dry air only.

## 1.2 Why Engineers Care About Moist Air — The Intuitive Picture

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a sponge. A dry sponge can soak up water until it is saturated. Likewise, air at a given temperature can hold only a **finite maximum amount of water vapour** before it becomes "saturated" (the sponge is full). This maximum is dictated by the **saturation pressure of water vapour**, which rises sharply with temperature. The state of "how full the sponge is" is the **relative humidity**.

In practical terms:

- A **hot summer afternoon in Kerala** (35 °C, 90 % RH) feels far more oppressive than the same temperature in a dry climate because the body cannot evaporate sweat efficiently — the air is nearly saturated.
- In a **HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, Air-Conditioning) chiller plant**, the cooling coil must remove not only the *sensible heat* (temperature drop) but also the *latent heat* (condensed moisture) — the ratio of the two determines the coil's **SHR (Sensible Heat Ratio)** and directly affects compressor kWh.
- In an **industrial dryer**, the *drying potential* of air is governed by its ability to absorb more moisture — quantified by the **specific humidity** difference between inlet and outlet.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Every comfort, corrosion, microbial-growth, drying-rate, and energy-efficiency calculation in air-side processes hinges on knowing *how much* water the air is carrying relative to *how much it could carry* at that temperature.

## 1.3 Key Moisture Parameters — Quick Vocabulary

- **Specific Humidity (Humidity Ratio), W** — mass of water vapour per kg of **dry air** (kg water/kg dry air).
- **Relative Humidity, φ** — ratio of actual partial pressure of water vapour to the saturation pressure at the same temperature (dimensionless, 0 to 1, often expressed as %).
- **Dew Point Temperature, T_dp** — temperature at which the air becomes saturated when cooled at constant pressure and constant W.
- **Wet Bulb Temperature, T_wb** — temperature attained by air when water is evaporated isothermally into it, ending at saturation along an adiabatic (constant-enthalpy) line.
- **Degree of Saturation, μ** — ratio of actual specific humidity to specific humidity at saturation at the same temperature.

## 1.4 Standard Reference State & Physical Constants

- **Universal gas constant, R_u = 8.314 kJ/kmol·K**
- **R_a = 0.287 kJ/kg·K**, **R_v = 0.4615 kJ/kg·K**
- **Ratio R_a / R_v = 0.622** (this number appears *everywhere* in psychrometry)
- **Latent heat of vaporisation at 0 °C, h_fg = 2501 kJ/kg**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure, P_atm = 101.325 kPa = 1.01325 bar**
- **Specific heat of dry air, c_pa = 1.005 kJ/kg·K**
- **Specific heat of water vapour, c_pv = 1.88 kJ/kg·K**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: From Dalton's Law to Specific Humidity

Let:

- $P$ = total barometric pressure (kPa)
- $P_v$ = partial pressure of water vapour (kPa)
- $P_a$ = partial pressure of dry air (kPa) = $P - P_v$
- $m_v$ = mass of water vapour (kg)
- $m_a$ = mass of dry air (kg)

By **Dalton's Law**: $P = P_v + P_a$

By **ideal gas equation** for each component:

$$P_v V = m_v R_v T \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_v = \frac{P_v V}{R_v T}$$

$$P_a V = m_a R_a T \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_a = \frac{P_a V}{R_a T}$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. The *volume* $V$ and *temperature* $T$ are identical for both gases in the mixture (Dalton’s assumption).
2. Hence, the ratio of masses equals the ratio of (partial pressure) / (gas constant) :

$$\frac{m_v}{m_a} = \frac{P_v / R_v}{P_a / R_a} = \frac{P_v R_a}{P_a R_v} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}$$

3. This ratio is defined as the **specific humidity W** (humidity ratio).

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **0.622 = R_a / R_v = 28.966 / 18.015** converts a "mole-like" water/aird air ratio into a *mass* ratio. **This is the single most important constant in the entire chapter.**

## 2.2 Master Formula Sheet (KTU Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are high-yield for KTU ESE. Memorise the meaning of every term; marks are awarded for correctly identifying units and citing the governing principle (Dalton’s Law, adiabatic saturation, energy balance).**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific humidity | $W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v}$ | kg water / kg dry air |
| 2 | Partial pressure from W | $P_v = \dfrac{W \cdot P}{0.622 + W}$ | kPa |
| 3 | Saturation specific humidity at T | $W_s = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_{gs}(T)}{P - P_{gs}(T)}$ | kg/kg dry air |
| 4 | Relative humidity | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{gs}(T_{db})}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 5 | Degree of saturation | $\mu = \dfrac{W}{W_s}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Relating $\phi$ and $\mu$ | $\phi = \dfrac{\mu \cdot P \cdot (0.622 + W_s) - \mu^2 \cdot P_{gs} \cdot W_s}{P(0.622 + W_s) - P_{gs} \cdot W_s}$ (complex form); simpler: $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{gs}}$ | — |
| 7 | Humid heat | $c_s = 1.005 + 1.88 \, W$ | kJ/(kg dry air·K) |
| 8 | Humid volume (specific volume of moist air) | $v = \dfrac{R_a T}{P_a} = \dfrac{R_a T}{P - P_v}$ (per kg dry air) | m³/kg dry air |
| 9 | Enthalpy of moist air (per kg dry air) | $h = 1.005 \, t + W(2500 + 1.88 \, t)$ | kJ/kg dry air |
| 10 | Adiabatic saturation relation | $\dfrac{W_1 - W_2}{T_{wb} - T_{db2}} = \dfrac{c_s}{h_{fg2}}$ | — |
| 11 | DBT – WBT relation (approx., Carrier) | $P_v = P_{gs}(T_{wb}) - \dfrac{(P - P_{gs}(T_{wb}))(T_{db} - T_{wb})}{1527.4}$ | kPa, with T in °C |
| 12 | Dew point temperature | $T_{dp} = T_{sat}$ at $P_v$ | °C |
| 13 | Latent heat of vaporisation | $h_{fg} \approx 2501 - 2.381 \, t$ | kJ/kg |
| 14 | Mass of dry air in volume V | $m_a = \dfrac{P_a V}{R_a T}$ | kg |
| 15 | Mass of water vapour in volume V | $m_v = \dfrac{P_v V}{R_v T}$ | kg |

> **Note on table syntax:** Vertical bar notation has been intentionally avoided inside the table cells to prevent markdown-rendering breakages. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ inside LaTeX whenever an absolute-value or condition symbol is required.

## 2.3 Heat Transfer Implications in Moist Air

When moist air interacts with a surface or another air stream, **three coupled heat-transfer modes** operate:

1. **Sensible Heat Transfer** — due to temperature difference. Governed by $q_s = \dot{m}_a \, c_s \, \Delta T$. Increases with $\dot{m}_a$ and humid heat $c_s$.
2. **Latent Heat Transfer** — due to moisture addition/removal. Governed by $q_l = \dot{m}_a \, h_{fg} \, \Delta W$. Requires phase change.
3. **Total (Enthalpy) Transfer** — sum of the above: $q_{total} = \dot{m}_a \, \Delta h$.

**Engineering Utility:**

- **Cooling coils:** The coil SHF (Sensible Heat Factor) line on the psychrometric chart is used to size coils for a given room load.
- **Cooling towers:** The effectiveness of an evaporative cooler is directly tied to approaching the wet-bulb temperature — the *theoretical* lower limit of cooling.
- **Energy auditing:** Sensible Heat Ratio (SHR) of an air-handling unit determines fan and chiller energy. A *latent-heavy* load (Kerala monsoon conditions) means more compressor work for the same temperature drop.

## 2.4 The Concept of Adiabatic Saturation — A Closer Look

Consider a long, perfectly insulated chamber through which a stream of unsaturated air flows. Water at temperature $T_{wb}$ is sprayed in, and air exits fully saturated at $T_{wb}$. The process is:

- **Adiabatic** → $h_1 = h_2$ (no heat exchange with surroundings, per kg dry air)
- **Steady** → mass balance on water: $W_2 - W_1 = m_{liq}/m_a$

The energy balance yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
h_1 &= h_2 \\
c_{p,a} T_{db1} + W_1 h_{g1} &= c_{p,a} T_{wb} + W_s(T_{wb}) h_{g}(T_{wb}) - (W_s - W_1) h_f(T_{wb})
\end{aligned}$$

Rearranging gives the **adiabatic saturation equation**:

$$\dfrac{W_s(T_{wb}) - W_1}{T_{db1} - T_{wb}} = \dfrac{c_{p,a} + W_1 c_{p,v}}{h_{fg}(T_{wb})}$$

This is the theoretical basis for measuring WBT with a sling psychrometer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Humidity from First Principles (Most Important)

**Given:** Moist air at total pressure $P$, partial pressure of vapour $P_v$, temperature $T$, volume $V$.

**Find:** Expression for specific humidity $W = m_v / m_a$.

**Step 1 — Apply ideal gas law to water vapour.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_v V &= m_v R_v T \\
\Rightarrow \quad m_v &= \dfrac{P_v V}{R_v T}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply ideal gas law to dry air.**

By Dalton’s Law, the partial pressure of dry air is $P_a = P - P_v$. Hence:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_a V &= m_a R_a T \\
\Rightarrow \quad m_a &= \dfrac{P_a V}{R_a T} = \dfrac{(P - P_v) V}{R_a T}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Form the mass ratio.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \dfrac{m_v}{m_a} = \dfrac{P_v V / (R_v T)}{(P - P_v) V / (R_a T)} \\
  &= \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v} \cdot \dfrac{R_a}{R_v} \\
  &= \dfrac{R_a}{R_v} \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Insert numerical constants.**

$R_a = 0.287 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$, $R_v = 0.4615 \, \text{kJ/kg·K}$

$$\dfrac{R_a}{R_v} = \dfrac{0.287}{0.4615} = 0.62197 \approx 0.622$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{W = 0.622 \, \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v} \;\; \text{(kg water / kg dry air)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** $P_v / (P - P_v)$ gives the mole-fraction-like ratio; multiplying by $R_a/R_v$ converts from mole ratio to *mass* ratio. This conversion is necessary because $W$ is *mass* of vapour per *mass* of dry air, not mole per mole.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Enthalpy of Moist Air (per kg dry air)

**Starting point:** Enthalpy of a mixture = sum of partial enthalpies of constituents (reference at 0 °C).

For **1 kg of dry air** carrying **W kg of water vapour** at dry-bulb temperature $t$ (°C):

**Dry air component:** Sensible heat above 0 °C.

$$h_a = c_{p,a} \cdot t = 1.005 \, t \;\; \text{kJ}$$

**Water vapour component:** Enthalpy of saturated vapour at 0 °C ($h_{fg,0} = 2501$ kJ/kg) plus sensible heat from 0 °C to $t$ °C.

$$h_v = W \left[ h_{fg,0} + c_{p,v} \cdot t \right] = W \left[ 2500 + 1.88 \, t \right] \;\; \text{kJ}$$

**Total enthalpy per kg dry air:**

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= h_a + h_v \\
  &= c_{p,a} \, t + W (h_{fg,0} + c_{p,v} \, t) \\
  &= 1.005 \, t + W(2500 + 1.88 \, t) \;\; \text{kJ/kg dry air}
\end{aligned}$$

**Simplification using $h_{fg,0} = 2501$ kJ/kg** (more accurate at 0 °C):

$$h = 1.005 \, t + W(2501 + 1.88 \, t)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 2500 vs 2501?** Many textbooks use 2500 (a rounded value) for ease. KTU problems generally accept either, but state your value in the first step for full marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Humid Volume (Specific Volume of Moist Air)

**Definition:** Volume occupied by 1 kg of **dry air** together with its associated water vapour.

For 1 kg of dry air:

$$V = \dfrac{m_a R_a T}{P_a} = \dfrac{R_a T}{P - P_v}$$

$$\boxed{v = \dfrac{R_a T}{P - P_v} = \dfrac{0.287 \, T}{P - P_v} \;\; \text{m}^3 / \text{kg dry air}}$$

Where $T$ is in **K** and $P, P_v$ in **kPa**. Sometimes, with $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) = 287 J/(kg·K), we write:

$$v = \dfrac{0.00283 \, T}{P - P_v} \;\; \text{m}^3 / \text{kg dry air} \quad (T \text{ in K}, P \text{ in kPa})$$

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative quick formula** (with $T$ in K, $P$ in kPa): $v = 0.00283 \, T / (P - P_v)$. Some texts use 0.00284 due to slight rounding.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Carrier’s Equation (Empirical DBT–WBT Relation)

**Physical basis:** Wet-bulb temperature depression ($t - t_{wb}$) is approximately proportional to the difference between saturation vapour pressure at $t_{wb}$ and the actual $P_v$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_v &= P_{gs}(t_{wb}) - A \cdot (P - P_{gs}(t_{wb})) (t - t_{wb})
\end{aligned}$$

Where the empirical constant $A = 1 / 1527.4$ for $t, t_{wb}$ in °C and pressures in kPa. Therefore:

$$\boxed{P_v = P_{gs}(t_{wb}) - \dfrac{(P - P_{gs}(t_{wb}))(t - t_{wb})}{1527.4} \;\; \text{kPa}}$$

This is the most common formula used in KTU problems to back-calculate $P_v$ from a sling-psychrometer reading, then to determine $\phi$ and $W$.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Analytical Matrix

**Problem statement (typical KTU ESE style):**
> A room contains moist air at 35 °C DBT and 25 °C WBT. Barometric pressure = 1.01325 bar. Determine: (i) specific humidity $W$, (ii) relative humidity $\phi$, (iii) dew point temperature $T_{dp}$, (iv) enthalpy $h$ per kg dry air, (v) specific volume $v$.

**Given data and lookup (steam-table style saturation pressures at given T):**

| Temperature (°C) | $P_{gs}$ (kPa) |
|---|---|
| 25 | 3.169 |
| 35 | 5.628 |
| 24 | 2.985 |
| 23 | 2.811 |

**Step 1 — Compute $P_v$ using Carrier’s equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_v &= P_{gs}(25) - \dfrac{(P - P_{gs}(25))(t - t_{wb})}{1527.4} \\
    &= 3.169 - \dfrac{(101.325 - 3.169)(35 - 25)}{1527.4} \\
    &= 3.169 - \dfrac{98.156 \times 10}{1527.4} \\
    &= 3.169 - 0.6426 \\
    &= 2.526 \;\; \text{kPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Specific humidity.**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v} \\
  &= 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{2.526}{101.325 - 2.526} \\
  &= 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{2.526}{98.799} \\
  &= 0.622 \cdot 0.02557 \\
  &= 0.01590 \;\; \text{kg/kg dry air}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Relative humidity.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi &= \dfrac{P_v}{P_{gs}(35)} = \dfrac{2.526}{5.628} = 0.4488 \approx 44.9 \, \%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Dew point temperature.**

The temperature at which $P_{gs}(T_{dp}) = P_v = 2.526$ kPa. Interpolating between 23 °C (2.811 kPa) and 24 °C (2.985 kPa) is *not* applicable (2.526 < 2.811). Re-check: actually 2.526 lies between 22 °C (2.645 kPa) and 21 °C (2.487 kPa). Interpolating:

$$T_{dp} \approx 22 - \dfrac{2.645 - 2.526}{2.645 - 2.487} \times 1 = 22 - 0.752 = 21.25 \, °C$$

So $T_{dp} \approx 21.3 \, °C$.

**Step 5 — Enthalpy.**

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= 1.005 \, t + W(2500 + 1.88 \, t) \\
  &= 1.005(35) + 0.0159(2500 + 1.88 \times 35) \\
  &= 35.175 + 0.0159(2500 + 65.8) \\
  &= 35.175 + 0.0159 \times 2565.8 \\
  &= 35.175 + 40.80 \\
  &= 75.97 \;\; \text{kJ/kg dry air}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Specific volume.**

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \dfrac{R_a T}{P - P_v} = \dfrac{0.287 \times (35 + 273.15)}{101.325 - 2.526} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.287 \times 308.15}{98.799} \\
  &= \dfrac{88.439}{98.799} \\
  &= 0.8951 \;\; \text{m}^3 / \text{kg dry air}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Result Matrix:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partial pressure of vapour | $P_v$ | **2.526** | kPa |
| Specific humidity | $W$ | **0.0159** | kg/kg d.a. |
| Relative humidity | $\phi$ | **44.9** | % |
| Dew point temperature | $T_{dp}$ | **21.3** | °C |
| Enthalpy | $h$ | **75.97** | kJ/kg d.a. |
| Specific volume | $v$ | **0.895** | m³/kg d.a. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity checks for marks:** (a) $T_{dp} < WBT < DBT$ ✓ (b) $W < W_s(T)$ ✓ (c) $0 < \phi < 1$ ✓. Examiners reward such cross-verification.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 5 — Mass Flow of Dry Air from Volumetric Flow

Often a problem gives airflow in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$. To find $\dot{m}_a$:

$$\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{v}$$

Where $v$ is the specific volume (m³/kg dry air). Then moisture mass flow:

$$\dot{m}_v = \dot{m}_a \cdot W$$

This is fundamental in **HVAC load calculations** — the total cooling load is:

$$Q_{total} = \dot{m}_a (h_{out} - h_{in}) \;\; \text{kW}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Psychrometric Property Interrelationships

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **logical/functional interdependencies** between all major psychrometric quantities, showing how each property is derived from inputs (DBT, WBT, $P$, $P_v$, $W$). It is designed for KTU board answers where students are expected to draw a clear *flow of computation* rather than a literal physical apparatus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA["DBT dry bulb temperature t in C"]:::input
    inputB["WBT wet bulb temperature t_wb in C"]:::input
    inputC["Atmospheric pressure P in kPa"]:::input
    inputD["Saturation pressure P_gs from steam table at t or t_wb"]:::input

    carrier["Carrier equation solves for P_v in kPa"]:::compute
    pvResult["P_v partial pressure of water vapour"]:::result

    specHum["Specific humidity W equals 0.622 times P_v divided by P minus P_v"]:::compute
    relHum["Relative humidity phi equals P_v divided by P_gs at t"]:::compute
    dewPt["Dew point T_dp is the saturation temperature at P_v"]:::compute
    humHeat["Humid heat c_s equals 1.005 plus 1.88 W in kJ per kg K"]:::compute
    enthalpy["Enthalpy h equals 1.005 t plus W times 2500 plus 1.88 t"]:::compute
    specVol["Specific volume v equals 0.00283 T divided by P minus P_v"]:::compute
    massFlow["Dry air mass flow m_a equals V dot divided by v"]:::compute

    outW["W kg water per kg dry air"]:::output
    outPhi["phi dimensionless 0 to 1"]:::output
    outDp["T_dp in C"]:::output
    outCs["c_s in kJ per kg dry air K"]:::output
    outH["h in kJ per kg dry air"]:::output
    outV["v in m cubed per kg dry air"]:::output
    outM["m_a in kg per s"]:::output

    inputA --> carrier
    inputB --> carrier
    inputC --> carrier
    inputD --> carrier
    carrier --> pvResult
    pvResult --> specHum
    pvResult --> relHum
    pvResult --> dewPt
    specHum --> humHeat
    specHum --> enthalpy
    specHum --> massFlow
    inputA --> enthalpy
    inputC --> dewPt
    inputC --> specVol
    inputA --> specVol
    pvResult --> specVol
    specVol --> massFlow
    inputC --> massFlow

    specHum --> outW
    relHum --> outPhi
    dewPt --> outDp
    humHeat --> outCs
    enthalpy --> outH
    specVol --> outV
    massFlow --> outM

```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Adiabatic Saturator

The schematic below shows the *steady-state mass and energy balance* on an adiabatic saturator, the conceptual apparatus that ties together DBT, WBT, and W.

```mermaid

    spray["Water spray at T_wb"]:::water
    chamber["Adiabatic saturator insulated chamber"]:::device
    outlet["Exit air State 2 fully saturated at T_wb, Ws, h2"]:::out
    excess["Excess water leaves at T_wb"]:::water

    inlet --> chamber
    spray --> chamber
    chamber --> outlet
    chamber --> excess

    h1eq["Energy balance h1 equals h2"]:::note
    massbal["Water mass balance m_a W1 plus m_liq equals m_a Ws"]:::note
    h1eq --- chamber
    massbal --- chamber
```
