<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Model Answers Provided)

> **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**
> **Define the following: (i) Specific humidity, (ii) Relative humidity, (iii) Dew point temperature.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Specific humidity (W)** is the mass of water vapour (in kg) associated with **1 kg of dry air**. It is also called the humidity ratio and is given by

$$W = 0.622 \, \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v} \;\; \text{kg/kg dry air}$$

It remains constant during sensible heating or cooling (no moisture added or removed).

(ii) **Relative humidity (φ)** is the ratio of the actual partial pressure of water vapour in the air to the **saturation pressure of water vapour** at the same dry-bulb temperature.

$$\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{gs}(t_{db})}$$

It indicates the air’s *proximity to saturation*. φ = 1 (or 100 %) means the air is fully saturated and can no longer hold additional moisture; φ = 0 means the air is bone-dry.

(iii) **Dew point temperature (T_dp)** is the temperature at which the air becomes saturated (reaches φ = 100 %) when cooled at **constant pressure and constant specific humidity**. Below $T_{dp}$, water condenses out (fog, dew, condensation on cold surfaces). It is found by solving $P_{gs}(T_{dp}) = P_v$.

---

> **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Past Year Style]**
> **Differentiate clearly between dry-bulb temperature (DBT) and wet-bulb temperature (WBT). State the psychrometric relationship between them.**

**Model Answer:**

**Dry-bulb temperature (DBT)** is the temperature of air measured by an ordinary thermometer whose bulb is kept dry and exposed to the air stream. It reflects the *true* thermodynamic temperature of the air–vapour mixture.

**Wet-bulb temperature (WBT)** is the lower temperature attained when a thermometer bulb is covered with a wick moistened with water and exposed to a stream of unsaturated air. Evaporative cooling depresses the temperature until the air becomes saturated at that bulb. **WBT ≤ DBT always**, with equality only at saturation.

The psychrometric relation is given by the **adiabatic saturation equation** (or, empirically, **Carrier’s equation**):

$$\dfrac{W_s(T_{wb}) - W_1}{T_{db1} - T_{wb}} = \dfrac{c_{p,a} + W_1 c_{p,v}}{h_{fg}(T_{wb})}$$

Equivalently, in terms of partial pressures:

$$P_v = P_{gs}(T_{wb}) - \dfrac{(P - P_{gs}(T_{wb}))(T_{db} - T_{wb})}{1527.4} \;\; \text{kPa}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Pitfall — Read this before attempting the 14-mark questions:**
> - **Never use DBT in place of WBT inside Carrier’s equation.** This single error has been shown to deduct 3–4 marks across past KTU answer scripts.
> - **Always state units** — partial marks are withheld if a numerical answer lacks the unit (e.g., kJ/kg d.a.).
> - The constant **0.622 is R_a/R_v**, *not* the molecular mass ratio. Examiners explicitly check this in the derivation step.
> - When interpolating from steam tables, **show the linear interpolation formula**; otherwise the step is treated as “unsupported”.
> - Always end with a **sanity check** ($T_{dp} < WBT < DBT$ ; $0 < \phi < 1$).

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style, Module 1 Choice 1]**
> **(a)** Derive, from first principles, the expression for the **specific humidity of moist air**, clearly stating the underlying assumptions. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A sling psychrometer in a factory hall reads 38 °C DBT and 28 °C WBT. The local barometric pressure is 100.5 kPa. **Calculate the specific humidity, relative humidity, dew point temperature, and enthalpy of the moist air per kg of dry air.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

Let $P$ be the total pressure, $P_v$ the partial pressure of water vapour, and $P_a = P - P_v$ the partial pressure of dry air. Both gases occupy volume $V$ at temperature $T$.

Apply the ideal gas equation to each:

$$P_v V = m_v R_v T \quad (1)$$

$$P_a V = m_a R_a T \quad (2)$$

Dividing (1) by (2):

$$\dfrac{m_v}{m_a} = \dfrac{P_v}{P_a} \cdot \dfrac{R_a}{R_v} = \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v} \cdot \dfrac{R_a}{R_v}$$

By definition, the **specific humidity** is $W = m_v / m_a$. Substituting $R_a / R_v = 0.622$:

$$\boxed{W = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P - P_v} \;\; \text{kg water/kg dry air}}$$

**Assumptions:** (i) Both gases obey the ideal gas law. (ii) The mixture is mechanically ideal (no chemical interaction). (iii) Dry air is treated as a single gas with $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $t_{db} = 38$ °C, $t_{wb} = 28$ °C, $P = 100.5$ kPa.

**Steam-table lookups:**

| $T$ (°C) | $P_{gs}$ (kPa) |
|---|---|
| 28 | 3.778 |
| 38 | 6.626 |
| 24 | 2.985 |

**Step 1 — Compute $P_v$ via Carrier’s equation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_v &= P_{gs}(28) - \dfrac{(P - P_{gs}(28))(t - t_{wb})}{1527.4} \\
    &= 3.778 - \dfrac{(100.5 - 3.778)(38 - 28)}{1527.4} \\
    &= 3.778 - \dfrac{96.722 \times 10}{1527.4} \\
    &= 3.778 - 0.6332 \\
    &= 3.145 \;\; \text{kPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Specific humidity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{3.145}{100.5 - 3.145} = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{3.145}{97.355} = 0.622 \times 0.03231 = 0.02010 \;\; \text{kg/kg dry air}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Relative humidity:**

$$\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{gs}(38)} = \dfrac{3.145}{6.626} = 0.4747 \approx 47.5 \, \%$$

**Step 4 — Dew point temperature:**

We need $T_{dp}$ such that $P_{gs}(T_{dp}) = 3.145$ kPa. From tables: at 24 °C, $P_{gs} = 2.985$ kPa; at 25 °C, $P_{gs} = 3.169$ kPa. Interpolating:

$$T_{dp} = 24 + \dfrac{3.145 - 2.985}{3.169 - 2.985} \times 1 = 24 + \dfrac{0.160}{0.184} = 24 + 0.870 = 24.87 \, °C$$

**Step 5 — Enthalpy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= 1.005 \, t + W(2500 + 1.88 \, t) \\
  &= 1.005(38) + 0.0201(2500 + 1.88 \times 38) \\
  &= 38.19 + 0.0201(2500 + 71.44) \\
  &= 38.19 + 0.0201 \times 2571.44 \\
  &= 38.19 + 51.69 \\
  &= 89.88 \;\; \text{kJ/kg dry air}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answer Matrix:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $P_v$ | 3.145 kPa |
| $W$ | 0.0201 kg/kg d.a. |
| $\phi$ | 47.5 % |
| $T_{dp}$ | 24.87 °C |
| $h$ | 89.88 kJ/kg d.a. |

**Sanity check:** $T_{dp} = 24.87 < t_{wb} = 28 < t_{db} = 38$ ✓

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style, Module 1 Choice 2]**
> **(a)** With the help of a labelled **psychrometric chart**, describe the meaning of the following lines: DBT, WBT, RH, enthalpy, specific volume. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the **expression for the enthalpy of moist air per kg of dry air**, and hence compute the total heat to be removed per hour from 1000 m³/min of air at 32 °C DBT and 70 % RH, supplied at 20 °C DBT and 80 % RH. Take $P = 101.325$ kPa. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

A psychrometric chart is a graphical representation of the thermodynamic properties of moist air. Its **horizontal axis** is the **dry-bulb temperature (DBT)** in °C, and its **vertical axis** is the **specific humidity (W)** in kg water/kg dry air. The families of lines drawn on it are:

1. **DBT lines** — vertical lines at constant $t_{db}$.
2. **WBT lines** — sloping lines (nearly parallel to constant-enthalpy lines at low humidity) labelled by the saturation temperature reached by adiabatic saturation.
3. **RH lines (φ)** — curved lines radiating from the saturation curve, each labelled with a constant percentage (10, 20, …, 100). The **100 % RH line** is the *saturation* or *dew-point* curve.
4. **Enthalpy lines (h)** — lines of constant specific enthalpy (kJ/kg dry air), nearly coincident with WBT lines at moderate humidities; they are skewed in the moist region due to the latent-heat content.
5. **Specific volume lines (v)** — lines of constant $v$ in m³/kg dry air, sloping steeply upward; useful in fan and duct-sizing calculations.

A state point on the chart is uniquely defined by any two independent properties (e.g., DBT + WBT, or DBT + RH).

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Derivation of enthalpy:** For 1 kg of dry air carrying W kg of water vapour at $t$ °C, with reference at 0 °C:

$$h = c_{p,a}(t - 0) + W[h_{fg,0} + c_{p,v}(t - 0)]$$

$$\boxed{h = 1.005 \, t + W(2500 + 1.88 \, t) \;\; \text{kJ/kg dry air}}$$

**Numerical computation:**

**State 1 (inlet):** $t_1 = 32$ °C, $\phi_1 = 0.70$, $P = 101.325$ kPa.

Steam table: $P_{gs}(32) = 4.758$ kPa.
$P_{v,1} = \phi_1 \times P_{gs}(32) = 0.70 \times 4.758 = 3.331$ kPa.

$$W_1 = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.331}{101.325 - 3.331} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{3.331}{97.994} = 0.02205 - 0.02 = 0.02114 \;\; \text{kg/kg d.a.}$$

(Re-compute precisely: $0.622 \times 0.03399 = 0.02114$ kg/kg d.a.)

$$h_1 = 1.005(32) + 0.02114(2500 + 1.88 \times 32) = 32.16 + 0.02114(2500 + 60.16) = 32.16 + 0.02114 \times 2560.16 = 32.16 + 54.12 = 86.28 \;\; \text{kJ/kg d.a.}$$

**State 2 (supply):** $t_2 = 20$ °C, $\phi_2 = 0.80$.

Steam table: $P_{gs}(20) = 2.339$ kPa.
$P_{v,2} = 0.80 \times 2.339 = 1.871$ kPa.

$$W_2 = 0.622 \times \dfrac{1.871}{101.325 - 1.871} = 0.622 \times \dfrac{1.871}{99.454} = 0.622 \times 0.01881 = 0.01170 \;\; \text{kg/kg d.a.}$$

$$h_2 = 1.005(20) + 0.0117(2500 + 1.88 \times 20) = 20.10 + 0.0117(2500 + 37.6) = 20.10 + 0.0117 \times 2537.6 = 20.10 + 29.69 = 49.79 \;\; \text{kJ/kg d.a.}$$

**Mass flow rate of dry air:**

Specific volume at state 1: $v_1 = 0.00283 \times (32 + 273.15) / (101.325 - 3.331) = 0.00283 \times 305.15 / 97.994 = 0.00283 \times 3.1140 = 0.00881$ m³/kg d.a.

Wait — typical value is ~ 0.88. Re-check: $0.00283 \times 305.15 = 0.8636$; divided by 97.994 = 0.00881. Unit problem: $0.00283$ should be in (m³·kPa)/(kg·K). With $T$ in K and $P$ in kPa, $v$ is in m³/kg. Hence $v_1 = 0.8636 / 97.994 = 0.00881$ m³/kg. That is too low.

**Correction:** $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K) = 287 J/(kg·K). For $v$ in m³/kg: $v = R_a T / (P - P_v) = (287)(T) / ((P - P_v) \times 1000)$ when $P$ in kPa. Better formula: $v = (0.287 \times T) / (P - P_v)$ gives v in m³/kg only if $P$ in kPa and the numerator is in kJ = kPa·m³. Yes: $v = (0.287 \times 305.15) / 97.994 = 87.578 / 97.994 = 0.8936$ m³/kg d.a. ✓ (The factor 0.00283 is for $T$ in K and pressure in Pa, not kPa.)

**Re-compute specific volume:** $v_1 = (0.287 \times 305.15) / 97.994 = 87.58 / 97.994 = 0.894$ m³/kg d.a.

**Dry air mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{V} = 1000 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{min} = \dfrac{1000}{60} = 16.667 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\dot{m}_a = \dfrac{\dot{V}}{v_1} = \dfrac{16.667}{0.894} = 18.64 \;\; \text{kg d.a./s}$$

**Total heat to be removed (cooling load):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \dot{m}_a (h_1 - h_2) \\
  &= 18.64 \times (86.28 - 49.79) \\
  &= 18.64 \times 36.49 \\
  &= 680.1 \;\; \text{kW} \;\; \text{(or equivalently, 680.1 kJ/s)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Per hour:** $Q = 680.1 \times 3600 / 1000 = 2448.5$ MJ/h = 680.1 kWh (thermal).

> [!TIP]
> **Quick cross-check using TR:** 1 TR (Tonne of Refrigeration) = 3.517 kW. So $Q \approx 680.1 / 3.517 = 193$ TR. For a hall of ~ 500 m², this is in the realistic range.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this section. It captures 90 % of marks awarded in Module 1 numerical questions.**

- **0.622 = R_a / R_v**, *not* the molecular-mass ratio. Memorise the value to 3 decimals.
- **Specific humidity W is defined per kg of *dry* air, not per kg of moist air.** This is critical for energy-balance equations.
- **Relative humidity uses the saturation pressure at the *dry-bulb* temperature**, not at the dew point. (At the dew point, φ = 1 by definition.)
- **WBT < DBT**, with equality only at saturation. **WBT > T_dp** unless saturated.
- **Adiabatic saturation equation** links $W_1, W_s(T_{wb}), T_{db1}, T_{wb}$. The **constant-pressure specific heat of dry air is 1.005 kJ/kg·K**; of water vapour is 1.88 kJ/kg·K; both are *assumed independent of T* in this module.
- **Carrier’s equation** is the *engineering shortcut* for the adiabatic saturation relation. Use it when the problem provides DBT and WBT but not W.
- **Saturated steam tables are mandatory** for KTU ESE — for each numerical, look up $P_{gs}$ at the *given DBT, WBT, and interpolated $T_{dp}$*. Always show the interpolation step.
- **Three heat components** for an air process: $q_s = \dot{m}_a c_s \Delta T$, $q_l = \dot{m}_a h_{fg} \Delta W$, $q_{total} = \dot{m}_a \Delta h$. The latter is the *only* one that holds true *for all processes*, including humidification.
- **Specific (humid) volume** is per kg of *dry* air and depends on $T$ and $P - P_v$, not on $P$ alone.
- **Sensible Heat Ratio (SHR)** = $q_s / q_{total}$ determines the *slope* of the room-load line on the psychrometric chart — used in coil selection.
- **Dew point = saturation temperature at the existing $P_v$**. It is the *only* psychrometric property that depends on the moisture content *alone* (not on temperature).
- **Degree of saturation μ** = $W / W_s$ is *not* the same as relative humidity φ. For unsaturated air, $\mu \neq \phi$ in general (only approximately so at low temperatures).
- **Standard reference** for enthalpy: 0 °C, with $h_{fg,0} = 2501$ kJ/kg. If a problem uses 2500 kJ/kg, the difference is ≤ 0.05 % — either is accepted in KTU valuation.
- **Universal gas constant** $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/kmol·K. To get $R$ for any gas: $R = R_u / M$, where $M$ is the molecular mass in kg/kmol.
- **Atmospheric pressure**: KTU commonly gives $P = 101.325$ kPa (sea-level standard) or 100 kPa (rounded). Always re-confirm before substituting.
- **Practical humidification:** *Spray water into air* → both W and (slightly) h change. *Inject live steam* → both W and h increase (mass and energy are added).
- **Dehumidification** occurs by cooling below the dew point on a cooling coil; the condensed water carries away latent heat equivalent to $h_{fg} \approx 2500$ kJ/kg of condensate.
- **Mistakes to avoid in KTU ESE:**
  1. Using *moist air mass* in $W$ definition (forgetting the “dry” qualifier).
  2. Forgetting to convert °C to K in ideal-gas formula.
  3. Substituting *DBT* into $P_{gs}$ for dew-point lookup (use the interpolated $T_{dp}$).
  4. Mixing up $h_{fg,0}$ and $h_{fg}$ at the current temperature (use $h_{fg,0}$ in the standard enthalpy formula).
  5. Skipping units in the final numerical answer.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Moist Air, Humidity, and Heat Transfer.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Evaluation of Thermal Performance: Calculation of Heat Loss - Heat Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Evaluation of Thermal Performance: Calculation of Heat Loss - Heat Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Thermal Performance Evaluation** of a building envelope is the systematic quantitative assessment of the rate of heat transfer between the conditioned indoor environment and the outdoor ambient, expressed as **Heat Loss (during heating season)** and **Heat Gain (during cooling season)**. It is governed by Fourier's law of heat conduction, Newton's law of cooling, and the principles of thermal radiation, all integrated through the **Overall Thermal Transmittance (U-value)** and **Thermal Resistance (R-value)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition:** *Heat Loss* is the unintentional outflow of thermal energy from a building (W or kW) due to conduction through walls/roof/floor, convection at surfaces, radiation through glazing, and infiltration of outdoor air. *Heat Gain* is the unintended inflow of thermal energy from solar radiation, outdoor air, internal occupants, lighting, and equipment.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building as a Thermos Flask"

Imagine your classroom as a **thermos flask** (vacuum flask). A real thermos has:
- An **inner wall** and an **outer wall** with a vacuum/air gap in between
- A **reflective silver coating** to bounce back radiation
- A **tight lid** to prevent air exchange

Your building works exactly the same way:
- **Walls, roof, floor** = the flask walls (the *envelope*). Heat tries to escape through them by **conduction** — the thicker and less conductive, the slower the leak.
- **Windows** = the silvered part. If they have **low-emissivity (low-E) glass**, radiation is reflected back inside. If they are single-pane clear glass, heat radiates out freely.
- **Cracks around doors and windows** = the loose lid. Warm air leaks out, cold air leaks in — this is called **infiltration**, and it is often the *biggest* invisible energy thief.
- **The sun shining on the roof** = the heat being *poured into* the thermos from outside. In summer, this is the unwanted **heat gain**.

The "thermal performance" of the building is essentially a **leak test**: How fast does heat leave in winter, and how fast does heat enter in summer?

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A well-insulated building is one where the "thermos lid is tight, the walls are thick, and the silver coating is shiny." Energy auditors quantify this using the **U-value** (lower = better insulation).

---

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard thermal properties are essential for all KTU calculations:

- **Thermal Conductivity (k)** = **0.025 to 2.5 W/(m·K)** depending on material (air = 0.025, copper = 385, brick = 0.81, insulation = 0.04)
- **Standard Inside Surface Resistance (R_si)** = **0.13 m²·K/W** (high-emissivity, still air, horizontal heat flow)
- **Standard Outside Surface Resistance (R_se)** = **0.04 m²·K/W** (exposed to wind)
- **Air Specific Heat (c_p)** = **1.005 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Air Density (ρ)** = **1.20 kg/m³** at standard conditions
- **Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC)** = dimensionless, 0 to 1 (lower = better blocking)
- **U-value** = **W/(m²·K)** (lower = better insulation)
- **R-value** = **m²·K/W** (higher = better insulation)
- **1 ACH (Air Change per Hour)** corresponds to a complete air volume replacement of the room in one hour

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The relation **U × R = 1** is universal. U-value of a composite wall is **never** the simple sum of layer conductivities — students frequently lose marks by forgetting the reciprocal.

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Energy Management

In Module 2, you are learning how to *audit* an existing building (residential, commercial, or industrial) and quantify its energy wastage. Without accurate heat loss and heat gain calculations:
- You **cannot size** a heater or air-conditioner correctly
- You **cannot justify** retrofitting insulation to a client
- You **cannot comply** with ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code) — India's national energy standard for commercial buildings
- You **cannot earn** Energy Performance Certificates (EPC) under the PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Physical Mechanisms of Heat Transfer in Buildings

A building envelope simultaneously loses or gains heat by **three distinct mechanisms**. The auditor must consider all three or the answer is incomplete.

| # | Mechanism | Physical Law | Where It Dominates |
|---|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Conduction** | Fourier's Law | Through opaque walls, roof, floor, doors |
| 2 | **Convection** | Newton's Law of Cooling | Air film on inside/outside surfaces, infiltration |
| 3 | **Radiation** | Stefan-Boltzmann Law | Through glazing, from sun, from hot surfaces |

---

### 2.2 Heat Loss by Conduction — The Layer-by-Layer Logic

For a **composite wall** consisting of *n* layers in series, heat must pass through each layer sequentially. Each layer offers a **thermal resistance (R)**. Since resistances in series add, but conductances do not:

**Step 1:** Compute resistance of each homogeneous layer

$$R_i = \frac{L_i}{k_i}$$

where $L_i$ is layer thickness in metres and $k_i$ is thermal conductivity in W/(m·K).

**Step 2:** Add inside and outside surface air-film resistances

$$R_{\text{total}} = R_{si} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{k_i} + R_{se}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the overall heat transfer coefficient (U-value)

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 4:** Compute heat loss through the wall

$$Q_{\text{loss}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

where $A$ is the wall area in m² and $\Delta T = T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}}$ in K (or °C, since only differences matter).

---

### 2.3 Heat Loss by Infiltration

Infiltration is the uncontrolled leakage of outside air through cracks, gaps, and openings. The energy required to heat this incoming cold air is:

$$Q_{\text{inf}} = 0.33 \cdot n \cdot V \cdot \Delta T \quad (\text{W})$$

where $n$ = air changes per hour (ACH, typically 0.5 to 1.5 for tight buildings), $V$ = room volume in m³, and $\Delta T$ in K.

> [!NOTE]
> The constant **0.33 W/(m³·K·ACH)** comes from $c_p \cdot \rho / 3600 = 1.005 \times 1.20 / 3.6 = 0.335$. This is a *must-remember* number for KTU problems.

---

### 2.4 Heat Gain by Solar Radiation (Through Glazing)

Solar energy striking a window is partially transmitted, partially absorbed, and partially reflected. The fraction that enters and becomes heat is given by the **Solar Heat Gain Coefficient (SHGC)**:

$$Q_{\text{solar}} = A_{\text{window}} \cdot \text{SHGC} \cdot I_{\text{solar}}$$

where $I_{\text{solar}}$ = incident solar intensity on the window in W/m² (varies from 0 at night to ~1000 W/m² at peak noon on a clear summer day, with an average of ~700 W/m² for design day calculations).

---

### 2.5 Heat Gain by Conduction Through Opaque Surfaces (Solar-Heated Walls/Roof)

When the sun strikes a wall or roof, the surface temperature rises far above the air temperature. The standard **Cooling Load Temperature Difference (CLTD)** method is used:

$$Q_{\text{cond, gain}} = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}_c$$

where $\text{CLTD}_c$ is the **corrected CLTD** obtained from ASHRAE tables (accounting for time lag, solar absorption, and indoor-outdoor temperature differences). For simplified KTU problems, an equivalent $\Delta T_{\text{solar}}$ is given.

---

### 2.6 Internal Heat Gains (Occupants, Lights, Equipment)

People, lighting, and electrical equipment release heat directly into the conditioned space:

$$Q_{\text{internal}} = Q_{\text{people}} + Q_{\text{lights}} + Q_{\text{equipment}}$$

Typical values (KTU board exam data):

| Source | Heat Output |
|--------|-------------|
| Adult at rest | 75 W sensible + 55 W latent = 130 W total |
| Adult doing office work | 75 W sensible + 70 W latent = 145 W total |
| Fluorescent lamp | 1.25 × rated wattage (with ballast) |
| LED lamp | 1.0 × rated wattage |
| Computer | 100 – 200 W |
| Printer/Copier | 200 – 500 W |

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this table. It contains every formula you will need for ESE questions on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Thermal Resistance of a layer | $R = L/k$ | m²·K/W | Homogeneous material |
| 2 | Total Resistance of composite wall | $R_{\text{tot}} = R_{si} + \sum L_i/k_i + R_{se}$ | m²·K/W | Layers in series |
| 3 | U-value (Overall Heat Transfer Coeff.) | $U = 1/R_{\text{tot}}$ | W/(m²·K) | Lower is better insulation |
| 4 | Heat Loss by Conduction | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Steady-state |
| 5 | Heat Loss by Infiltration | $Q = 0.33 \cdot n \cdot V \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Use 0.33 constant |
| 6 | Solar Heat Gain (Window) | $Q = A \cdot \text{SHGC} \cdot I$ | W | Per window orientation |
| 7 | Conduction Heat Gain (Opaque) | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}_c$ | W | ASHRAE CLTD method |
| 8 | Internal Heat Gain | $Q = \sum Q_{\text{people}} + Q_{\text{lights}} + Q_{\text{eq}}$ | W | Add per source |
| 9 | Total Cooling Load | $Q_{\text{cool}} = Q_{\text{conduction}} + Q_{\text{solar}} + Q_{\text{internal}} + Q_{\text{inf}}$ | W or kW | Sum all gains |
| 10 | Total Heating Load | $Q_{\text{heat}} = Q_{\text{conduction,loss}} + Q_{\text{inf,loss}}$ | W or kW | Sum all losses |
| 11 | Energy over time | $E = Q \cdot t$ | Wh or kWh | t in hours |
| 12 | Annual energy | $E_{\text{annual}} = Q \cdot \text{CDD} \cdot 24 / 1000$ | kWh | CDD = cooling degree days |

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

These calculations are used in:
- **HVAC Sizing**: Selecting the right capacity of air-conditioner or heater for a building
- **ECBC Compliance Audit**: Verifying whether the building envelope meets India's Energy Conservation Building Code limits (e.g., roof U ≤ 0.35 W/m²K in composite climate)
- **Energy Retrofit Studies**: Calculating the payback period of installing insulation, double-glazing, or weather-stripping
- **Green Building Certification (GRIHA, LEED-India)**: Earning points for optimized thermal envelope
- **BEE Star Rating**: 1 to 5 stars for appliances partly depend on building thermal performance

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Why Resistances Add (Conductances Don't)

Consider a composite wall of two layers in series. Let the inside temperature be $T_i$ and outside be $T_o$. Let the interface temperature between the layers be $T_x$.

**Step 1:** Heat flow through layer 1 (Fourier's Law)

$$Q_1 = \frac{k_1 \cdot A \cdot (T_i - T_x)}{L_1}$$

**Step 2:** Heat flow through layer 2 (Fourier's Law)

$$Q_2 = \frac{k_2 \cdot A \cdot (T_x - T_o)}{L_2}$$

**Step 3:** In steady state, $Q_1 = Q_2 = Q$ (no heat accumulation in the wall).

Solving the first for $T_i - T_x$:

$$T_i - T_x = \frac{Q \cdot L_1}{k_1 \cdot A} = Q \cdot R_1$$

Similarly:

$$T_x - T_o = Q \cdot R_2$$

**Step 4:** Add the two equations (telescoping sum):

$$(T_i - T_x) + (T_x - T_o) = Q \cdot R_1 + Q \cdot R_2$$

$$T_i - T_o = Q \cdot (R_1 + R_2)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for Q:

$$Q = \frac{T_i - T_o}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step 6:** Compare with $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$:

$$U \cdot A = \frac{1}{R_{\text{total}}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad U = \frac{1}{R_{\text{total}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** It proves rigorously that you **cannot** add U-values of layers ($U_1 + U_2 \neq U_{\text{total}}$). You **must** add resistances first and take the reciprocal. KTU examiners specifically test this conceptual point.

---

### 3.2 Comprehensive Worked Example — Heat Loss Through a Composite Wall

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**

A residential building wall in Kerala has the following construction (from inside to outside):
- 15 mm internal cement plaster, $k = 0.72$ W/(m·K)
- 200 mm brick wall, $k = 0.81$ W/(m·K)
- 50 mm expanded polystyrene (EPS) insulation, $k = 0.038$ W/(m·K)
- 12 mm external cement plaster, $k = 0.72$ W/(m·K)

The wall area is $A = 25$ m². Indoor temperature is maintained at $T_i = 24°C$ and outdoor temperature is $T_o = 14°C$. Inside surface resistance $R_{si} = 0.13$ m²·K/W; outside surface resistance $R_{se} = 0.04$ m²·K/W.

**Calculate:**
1. The total thermal resistance $R_{\text{total}}$
2. The U-value of the wall
3. The total heat loss through the wall in W and in kWh per day (24 h operation)

---

**Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: Calculate thermal resistance of each layer**

$$R_{\text{plaster, in}} = \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.015}{0.72} = 0.02083 \;\; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$R_{\text{brick}} = \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.200}{0.81} = 0.24691 \;\; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$R_{\text{EPS}} = \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.050}{0.038} = 1.31579 \;\; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

$$R_{\text{plaster, out}} = \frac{L_4}{k_4} = \frac{0.012}{0.72} = 0.01667 \;\; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}$$

**Step 2: Add all resistances in series**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{total}} &= R_{si} + R_{\text{plaster, in}} + R_{\text{brick}} + R_{\text{EPS}} + R_{\text{plaster, out}} + R_{se} \\
&= 0.13 + 0.02083 + 0.24691 + 1.31579 + 0.01667 + 0.04 \\
&= 1.77020 \;\; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Calculate U-value**

$$U = \frac{1}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{1}{1.77020} = 0.5649 \;\; \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$

**Step 4: Calculate temperature difference**

$$\Delta T = T_i - T_o = 24 - 14 = 10 \;\; \text{K}$$

**Step 5: Calculate total heat loss in Watts**

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.5649 \times 25 \times 10 = 141.22 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 6: Convert to kWh per day**

$$E = Q \times 24 / 1000 = 141.22 \times 24 / 1000 = 3.389 \;\; \text{kWh/day}$$

**Step 7: Annual energy loss (assume 90 heating days)**

$$E_{\text{annual}} = 3.389 \times 90 = 305.0 \;\; \text{kWh/year}$$

**Step 8: Monetary loss (assume electricity at ₹7/kWh)**

$$\text{Cost} = 305.0 \times 7 = \text{₹} 2135 \;\; \text{per year just from one wall}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The EPS insulation (50 mm) provides **74% of the total thermal resistance** ($1.316 / 1.770 = 0.744$). This is why a thin insulation layer gives a huge energy saving. KTU examiners love such "what-if" analysis questions.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Heat Loss by Infiltration

**Problem:**

A bedroom has dimensions $4 \; \text{m} \times 5 \; \text{m} \times 3 \; \text{m}$ (height). Air changes per hour is 1.0 ACH. Indoor temperature = 24°C, outdoor = 14°C.

**Calculate heat loss due to infiltration.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate volume**

$$V = 4 \times 5 \times 3 = 60 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2: Calculate ΔT**

$$\Delta T = 24 - 14 = 10 \;\; \text{K}$$

**Step 3: Apply infiltration formula**

$$Q_{\text{inf}} = 0.33 \cdot n \cdot V \cdot \Delta T = 0.33 \times 1.0 \times 60 \times 10 = 198 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 4: Compare with conduction loss (assume wall U = 0.565 W/m²K, A = 4 walls)**

For the 4 walls, neglecting windows, perimeter wall area ≈ 36 m²:

$$Q_{\text{cond}} = 0.565 \times 36 \times 10 = 203.4 \;\; \text{W}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** In a leaky room, infiltration loss (198 W) is *comparable* to conduction loss (203 W). Tightening windows and doors can cut total energy loss by nearly 50%.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Heat Gain Calculation (Cooling Load)

**Problem:**

A small office in Kochi (June 15, 2 PM solar peak) has the following loads:
- Roof area = 50 m², U = 0.5 W/(m²·K), corrected CLTD = 18°C
- South Window: area = 6 m², SHGC = 0.4, peak solar intensity on the glass = 600 W/m²
- East Wall: 30 m², U = 0.6 W/(m²·K), CLTD = 12°C
- Internal: 8 occupants doing office work, 20 fluorescent tubes of 40 W each (with ballast factor 1.25), 8 computers (150 W each)
- Infiltration gain: 0.5 ACH, volume = 200 m³, indoor = 24°C, outdoor = 33°C

**Calculate total cooling load.**

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Heat gain through roof**

$$Q_{\text{roof}} = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD} = 0.5 \times 50 \times 18 = 450 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 2: Solar heat gain through south window**

$$Q_{\text{window, solar}} = A \cdot \text{SHGC} \cdot I = 6 \times 0.4 \times 600 = 1440 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 3: Heat gain through east wall**

$$Q_{\text{east wall}} = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD} = 0.6 \times 30 \times 12 = 216 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 4: Internal heat gains**

$$Q_{\text{people}} = 8 \times 145 = 1160 \;\; \text{W}$$

$$Q_{\text{lights}} = 20 \times 40 \times 1.25 = 1000 \;\; \text{W}$$

$$Q_{\text{computers}} = 8 \times 150 = 1200 \;\; \text{W}$$

$$Q_{\text{internal}} = 1160 + 1000 + 1200 = 3360 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 5: Infiltration heat gain**

$$\Delta T = 33 - 24 = 9 \;\; \text{K}$$

$$Q_{\text{inf}} = 0.33 \times 0.5 \times 200 \times 9 = 297 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 6: Total cooling load**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 450 + 1440 + 216 + 3360 + 297 = 5763 \;\; \text{W} = 5.76 \;\; \text{kW}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Result:** A 5.76 kW cooling load requires a 2-tonne air-conditioner (1 TR ≈ 3.517 kW). KTU problems often close with a comment on the practical implications of these calculations.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Heat Loss to Each Parameter

A summary of how each variable affects the result (used in retrofit analysis):

| Parameter | Effect on Q | Quantitative Sensitivity | KTU Question Type |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| Increase wall thickness L | Decreases Q (linearly with L in numerator) | Doubling L of insulation ≈ Halves Q | "What if wall thickness doubled?" |
| Decrease k (better insulation) | Decreases Q (inversely) | Halving k halves Q | "Compare brick vs. AAC block" |
| Increase ΔT | Increases Q (linearly) | Doubling ΔT doubles Q | "Cold wave effect" |
| Increase Area A | Increases Q (linearly) | Doubling area doubles Q | "Effect of extension" |
| Reduce ACH (weather-strip) | Decreases Q (linearly) | Halving ACH halves Q_inf | "Impact of sealing" |
| Reduce SHGC (low-E glass) | Decreases solar gain linearly | SHGC 0.6 → 0.3 halves solar gain | "Glazing upgrade" |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Methodology Flow — Heat Loss and Heat Gain Evaluation

The following functional architecture describes the standard KTU-recommended audit procedure from site inspection to final report.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Building Thermal Audit] --> nodeB[Step 1: Site Survey and Data Collection]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Step 2: Identify Building Envelope Components]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Walls: Layers and Thicknesses]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Roof: Type and Insulation]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Windows: Glazing Type and Area]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[Doors: Material and Seals]
    nodeC1 --> nodeD[Step 3: Measure Areas A in square metres]
    nodeC2 --> nodeD
    nodeC3 --> nodeD
    nodeC4 --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Step 4: Determine Material Thermal Conductivity k from Tables]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Step 5: Compute R for each layer L by k]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Step 6: Sum R values including surface films]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Step 7: Compute U value as 1 by R total]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Step 8: Select Calculation Mode]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Heat Loss Mode for winter]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Heat Gain Mode for summer]
    nodeJ --> nodeJ1[Compute Q conduction = U x A x delta T]
    nodeJ --> nodeJ2[Compute Q infiltration = 0.33 x n x V x delta T]
    nodeJ1 --> nodeJ3[Total Heat Loss = Q conduction + Q infiltration]
    nodeJ2 --> nodeJ3
    nodeK --> nodeK1[Compute Q conduction gain = U x A x CLTD]
    nodeK --> nodeK2[Compute Q solar gain = A x SHGC x I]
    nodeK --> nodeK3[Compute Q internal = People + Lights + Equipment]
    nodeK --> nodeK4[Compute Q infiltration gain = 0.33 x n x V x delta T]
    nodeK1 --> nodeK5[Total Heat Gain = Sum of all four sources]
    nodeK2 --> nodeK5
    nodeK3 --> nodeK5
    nodeK4 --> nodeK5
    nodeJ3 --> nodeL[Step 9: Compare with ECBC and ASHRAE Benchmarks]
    nodeK5 --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM[Step 10: Identify Energy Conservation Measures]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Step 11: Estimate Energy and Cost Savings]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Step 12: Final Audit Report and Recommendations]
    nodeO --> nodeP[End]
```

---

This diagram visualizes how heat flows through a multi-layered wall, illustrating the concept of thermal resistances in series.

A schematic of the four major contributors to cooling load.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**Define the terms: U-value, R-value, and SHGC. State their SI units and explain the physical significance of each in building thermal performance evaluation.**

**Model Answer:**

- **U-value (Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient):** It is the rate of heat flow through 1 m² of a building element per 1 K temperature difference between inside and outside. **SI unit = W/(m²·K).** Physically, a low U-value (e.g., 0.3) means the wall is well insulated; a high U-value (e.g., 5.5 for single glass) means poor insulation.
- **R-value (Thermal Resistance):** It is the reciprocal of the U-value, representing the resistance a material offers to heat flow. **SI unit = m²·K/W.** Higher R-value means better insulation.
- **SHGC (Solar Heat Gain Coefficient):** It is the fraction of incident solar radiation that passes through a glazing and becomes heat gain inside. **Dimensionless, 0 to 1.** A SHGC of 0.25 means only 25% of solar energy enters the room; the rest is reflected or absorbed.

---

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Explain the concept of infiltration in a building and derive the expression for infiltration heat loss.**

**Model Answer:**

**Infiltration** is the uncontrolled leakage of outside air into a building (or vice versa) through cracks, gaps, joints, and openings in the envelope. It occurs because of wind pressure differentials, the stack effect (buoyancy of warm air), and HVAC operation.

**Derivation:**

Mass flow rate of infiltrating air:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\rho \cdot n \cdot V}{3600} \;\; \text{kg/s}$$

where $\rho = 1.20 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $n$ = air changes per hour, $V$ = room volume in m³, and 3600 converts hours to seconds.

Sensible heat carried by this air:

$$Q_{\text{inf}} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = \frac{\rho \cdot n \cdot V}{3600} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

Substituting $\rho = 1.20$ and $c_p = 1005$ J/(kg·K):

$$Q_{\text{inf}} = \frac{1.20 \times 1005}{3600} \cdot n \cdot V \cdot \Delta T = 0.335 \cdot n \cdot V \cdot \Delta T \;\; \text{W}$$

In simplified KTU form:

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{inf}} = 0.33 \cdot n \cdot V \cdot \Delta T \;\; \text{W}}$$

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as Part (a) = 7 marks and Part (b) = 7 marks. Two alternative questions with internal choice are provided.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** A composite wall of a building consists of the following layers (from outside to inside): 12 mm external cement plaster ($k = 0.72$ W/m·K), 230 mm brick ($k = 0.81$ W/m·K), 25 mm EPS insulation ($k = 0.038$ W/m·K), and 10 mm internal plaster ($k = 0.72$ W/m·K). The wall area is 20 m². The indoor and outdoor temperatures are 26°C and 12°C respectively. Inside surface resistance is 0.13 m²·K/W and outside is 0.04 m²·K/W.

Calculate:
(i) Total thermal resistance (3 marks)
(ii) U-value of the wall (1 mark)
(iii) Total heat loss through the wall in kW (1 mark)
(iv) Daily energy loss in kWh (2 marks)

**(b)** Explain the various components of cooling load in a building with the help of a neat block diagram. Discuss the SHGC concept and its importance in selecting window glazing for tropical climates like Kerala. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Compute individual layer resistances**

$$R_1 = \frac{0.012}{0.72} = 0.01667 \;\; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W} \quad (\text{external plaster})$$

$$R_2 = \frac{0.230}{0.81} = 0.28395 \;\; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W} \quad (\text{brick})$$

$$R_3 = \frac{0.025}{0.038} = 0.65789 \;\; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W} \quad (\text{EPS})$$

$$R_4 = \frac{0.010}{0.72} = 0.01389 \;\; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W} \quad (\text{internal plaster})$$

**Step 2: Total thermal resistance (3 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{total}} &= R_{si} + R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + R_4 + R_{se} \\
&= 0.13 + 0.01667 + 0.28395 + 0.65789 + 0.01389 + 0.04 \\
&= 1.14240 \;\; \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: U-value (1 mark)**

$$U = \frac{1}{1.14240} = 0.8754 \;\; \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K)}$$

**Step 4: Heat loss in kW (1 mark)**

$$\Delta T = 26 - 12 = 14 \;\; \text{K}$$

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 0.8754 \times 20 \times 14 = 245.1 \;\; \text{W} = 0.245 \;\; \text{kW}$$

**Step 5: Daily energy loss (2 marks)**

$$E = Q \times 24 = 0.245 \times 24 = 5.88 \;\; \text{kWh/day}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

The cooling load of a building is the rate at which heat must be removed from the space to maintain the desired indoor temperature. Its components are:

1. **Conduction heat gain through opaque envelope** (walls, roof, floor): Calculated as $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}_c$. This is largest for the roof in summer.
2. **Solar heat gain through fenestration** (windows, skylights): Calculated as $Q = A \cdot \text{SHGC} \cdot I$. Orientation matters — south and west windows receive maximum solar radiation in tropical India.
3. **Internal heat gain from occupants, lights, and equipment**: $Q_{\text{internal}} = Q_{\text{people}} + Q_{\text{lights}} + Q_{\text{eq}}$.
4. **Heat gain due to infiltration and ventilation**: $Q = 0.33 \cdot n \cdot V \cdot \Delta T$.

**Block Diagram (refer SECTION 4.3)**

**SHGC and Kerala Glazing:**

The **Solar Heat Gain Coefficient** is a number between 0 and 1 representing the fraction of solar radiation admitted through a window. For tropical Kerala:
- Single clear glass: SHGC ≈ 0.85 (poor — admits too much heat)
- Tinted glass: SHGC ≈ 0.55
- Low-E double glazing: SHGC ≈ 0.25 (excellent)
- Reflective glass: SHGC ≈ 0.15

Kerala receives intense solar radiation (4–6 kWh/m²/day average daily insolation). Choosing glazing with SHGC ≤ 0.3 can reduce peak cooling load by 30–40%, which directly translates to lower AC tonnage and electricity bills. ECBC mandates SHGC ≤ 0.25 for the west façade in composite climate zones.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

**(a)** A room of dimensions 6 m × 5 m × 3.5 m has a single glass window of area 2 m² (SHGC = 0.85) and one door (area 2.2 m², U = 2.5 W/m²·K). The walls (excluding window and door) have U = 1.2 W/m²·K and total exposed area of 45 m². The roof has U = 0.7 W/m²·K and area = 30 m². The air change rate is 1.2 ACH. Outdoor design temperature is 38°C and indoor is 25°C. Solar intensity on the window at peak hour is 750 W/m².

Calculate:
(i) Sensible heat gain due to infiltration (2 marks)
(ii) Heat gain through walls, roof, door, and window by conduction (3 marks)
(iii) Solar heat gain through the window (1 mark)
(iv) Total cooling load of the room in kW (1 mark)

**(b)** Discuss the difference between the **CLTD method** and the **Heat Balance method** for cooling load estimation. List the assumptions and limitations of the CLTD method. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Infiltration sensible heat gain (2 marks)**

$$V = 6 \times 5 \times 3.5 = 105 \;\; \text{m}^3$$

$$\Delta T = 38 - 25 = 13 \;\; \text{K}$$

$$Q_{\text{inf}} = 0.33 \times 1.2 \times 105 \times 13 = 540.5 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 2: Conduction heat gain (3 marks)**

Assuming CLTD equals $\Delta T$ for simplification (as a KTU-recommended shortcut):

$$Q_{\text{walls}} = 1.2 \times 45 \times 13 = 702.0 \;\; \text{W}$$

$$Q_{\text{roof}} = 0.7 \times 30 \times 13 = 273.0 \;\; \text{W}$$

$$Q_{\text{door}} = 2.5 \times 2.2 \times 13 = 71.5 \;\; \text{W}$$

$$Q_{\text{window,cond}} = \text{Assume U}_{\text{glass}} = 5.8 \;\; \text{W/m}^2\text{K (single glass)} = 5.8 \times 2 \times 13 = 150.8 \;\; \text{W}$$

$$Q_{\text{conduction,total}} = 702.0 + 273.0 + 71.5 + 150.8 = 1197.3 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 3: Solar heat gain through window (1 mark)**

$$Q_{\text{solar}} = A \cdot \text{SHGC} \cdot I = 2 \times 0.85 \times 750 = 1275 \;\; \text{W}$$

**Step 4: Total cooling load (1 mark)**

$$Q_{\text{total}} = 540.5 + 1197.3 + 1275 = 3012.8 \;\; \text{W} = 3.013 \;\; \text{kW}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Feature | CLTD Method | Heat Balance Method |
|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Origin** | ASHRAE Handbook (Fundamentals) | ASHRAE Handbook (Fundamentals) |
| **Principle** | Uses pre-calculated CLTD values from tables for each envelope, hour, orientation, and construction type | Solves simultaneous energy balance equations at each surface, each room, and the HVAC system |
| **Inputs** | U, A, CLTD, SC (shading coefficient) | All zone heat gains, time-varying temperatures, air-flows, moisture |
| **Complexity** | Simple — hand-calculable | Complex — requires computer simulation (e.g., TRACE, HAP) |
| **Accuracy** | ± 15% — suitable for preliminary sizing | ± 5% — suitable for detailed design |
| **Time resolution** | Hourly peak | Sub-hourly dynamic |
| **Assumptions** | Steady-state, uniform room temperature | Dynamic, accounts for thermal mass, latent loads |
| **KTU applicability** | **Highly recommended** for exam problems | Mostly outside KTU syllabus scope |

**Assumptions and Limitations of the CLTD Method:**

1. Assumes **steady-state heat conduction** — ignores the time lag of heat flow through heavy walls.
2. Assumes **uniform indoor air temperature** — ignores thermal stratification (warm air near ceiling).
3. CLTD values are tabulated for a **standard design day** (e.g., July 21 in India, 40°C max, 27°C mean).
4. **Solar radiation** is decoupled and treated separately using SC and CLF (Cooling Load Factor).
5. Does **not** accurately handle intermittent AC operation or varying internal loads.
6. Assumes the room acts as a **single thermal zone** — no internal walls.
7. Not suitable for spaces with very high thermal mass (e.g., thick stone walls) or unusual construction.
8. Provides only the **peak load**, not the seasonal energy consumption.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the surface film resistances** ($R_{si}$ and $R_{se}$). Many students compute only the layer resistances and miss 0.13 + 0.04 = 0.17 m²·K/W. **Lost marks: 1–2**
> 2. **Adding U-values instead of R-values.** This is a fundamental conceptual error. $U_{\text{total}} = (1/R_1 + 1/R_2 + ...)^{-1}$, **NOT** $U_1 + U_2 + ...$. **Lost marks: 2–3**
> 3. **Confusing infiltration constant 0.33** with 1.2 (specific heat). The constant 0.33 is derived — do not write 0.33 without explaining it. **Lost marks: 1**
> 4. **Mixing up heat loss and heat gain** — using $\Delta T = T_o - T_i$ for losses. Always keep $\Delta T$ positive. **Lost marks: 1**
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — writing Q in W but area in cm². Always convert L to metres first. **Lost marks: 1**
> 6. **Ignoring the solar component** in cooling load problems. Heat gain = conduction + solar + internal + infiltration. Skipping solar = -3 marks easily.
> 7. **Not converting to kW** when the question asks for AC sizing (which is in tonnes or kW, not W). **Lost marks: 0.5–1**
> 8. **Forgetting the shading coefficient (SC) or SHGC distinction.** Modern KTU papers use SHGC; old papers use SC. Relation: SHGC ≈ SC × 0.87.

---

### 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

**A. Definitions You Must Memorize**

- **U-value** = Overall heat transfer coefficient = $1/R_{\text{total}}$; unit W/(m²·K)
- **R-value** = Thermal resistance = $L/k$ for one layer; unit m²·K/W
- **SHGC** = Solar Heat Gain Coefficient; dimensionless, 0 to 1
- **CLTD** = Cooling Load Temperature Difference; equivalent $\Delta T$ in °C
- **ACH** = Air Changes per Hour

**B. The 5 Formulas You Must Know**

- Heat loss by conduction: $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$
- Heat loss by infiltration: $Q = 0.33 \cdot n \cdot V \cdot \Delta T$
- Solar heat gain: $Q = A \cdot \text{SHGC} \cdot I$
- Conduction heat gain (opaque): $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLTD}_c$
- Internal heat gain: $Q = Q_{\text{people}} + Q_{\text{lights}} + Q_{\text{equipment}}$

**C. Standard Values to Remember**

- $R_{si} = 0.13$ m²·K/W; $R_{se} = 0.04$ m²·K/W
- Infiltration constant = 0.33 W/(m³·K·ACH)
- Typical k: brick = 0.81, concrete = 1.4, insulation = 0.04, plaster = 0.72, glass = 1.05, air = 0.025
- Office worker = 145 W; Fluorescent = 1.25× rated; Computer = 150 W
- Peak solar intensity ≈ 700–1000 W/m²

**D. Conceptual Mastery Points**

- **R-values add** in series; **U-values do NOT add**
- **Lower U** = better insulation; **Higher R** = better insulation
- **Lower SHGC** = better tropical performance
- **Infiltration** can equal conduction loss in leaky buildings
- **Cooling load** = conduction + solar + internal + infiltration (all four needed)
- **Heating load** = conduction loss + infiltration loss (solar is irrelevant at night; internal often irrelevant in winter)

**E. Common Exam Traps**

- Units: convert L to **metres** before computing R
- $\Delta T$ always **positive** in magnitude
- $U$ is in W/(m²·K) — multiplying by A (m²) and $\Delta T$ (K) gives **Watts**
- For multi-layered walls, the **insulation layer dominates** — 50 mm of insulation can provide 70–80% of total R
- ECBC limits: Roof U ≤ 0.35 W/m²K, Wall U ≤ 0.4 W/m²K, Window SHGC ≤ 0.25 (for commercial buildings in composite climate)

**F. Real-World Conversion (Bonus for viva)**

- 1 TR (Tonne of Refrigeration) = 3.517 kW
- 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal
- 1 kcal/h = 1.163 W
- To size an AC: Total cooling load in kW ÷ 3.517 = TR required
- 1 Therm = 105.5 MJ (gas heating)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of Annual Heating and Cooling Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of Annual Heating and Cooling Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Heating Load** is the rate at which heat must be added to a conditioned space to maintain a specified indoor air temperature during the coldest design conditions, while **Cooling Load** is the rate at which heat must be removed to maintain the indoor temperature during the warmest design conditions. The **Annual Heating and Cooling Load** refers to the total energy (typically expressed in kWh, MJ, or kWh/m²·year) that a building consumes over a calendar year for space-conditioning purposes.

The estimation of these loads is the foundational step in **Energy Audit**, **HVAC system design**, and **building energy management**, and it provides the baseline against which energy conservation measures (ECMs) are evaluated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Students must be able to compute the annual heating and cooling energy demand of a building using simplified methods (Degree-Day Method) and detailed methods (CLTD/SCL/CLF/ASHRAE Transfer Function Method).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your house is a **"thermal bucket"** — during winter, heat continuously leaks out through walls, windows, doors, and roof, just like water leaking out of a bucket with small holes. Your heater must pour heat back in at the same rate it leaks out to keep the indoor temperature steady. The **heating load** is the rate at which heat is being lost, and the **annual heating energy** is the *total* amount of heat poured in over the whole year.

In summer, the bucket is being filled from outside (sun shining on roof, hot air entering, body heat from occupants, heat from your refrigerator and lights), and your air conditioner acts as a **"scoop"** removing heat at the same rate to keep the level constant. The **cooling load** is the rate of heat inflow, and the **annual cooling energy** is the total scooped out over the year.

The **degree-day method** is like estimating the *total* water bill by knowing the average daily leak rate (degree-day) and the bucket's leakiness (U×A), while the **CLTD method** is like a detailed hour-by-hour ledger of every heat source and sink.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **HDD** | Heating Degree Days — indicator of heating demand severity |
| **CDD** | Cooling Degree Days — indicator of cooling demand severity |
| **U-value** | Overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) |
| **CLTD** | Cooling Load Temperature Difference (°C or °F) |
| **SHGF** | Solar Heat Gain Factor (W/m²) |
| **SC** | Shading Coefficient of glazing |
| **CLF** | Cooling Load Factor — accounts for thermal storage lag |
| **TFM** | Transfer Function Method (ASHRAE detailed method) |

> [!NOTE]
> The base temperature for HDD/CDD is typically **18 °C (65 °F)** in international standards and is location-dependent. Always verify the base used in your regional weather data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of Building Heating/Cooling Load

The total instantaneous load on an HVAC system has five components:

1. **Envelope Transmission Load** — Heat flow through walls, roof, floor, windows, doors
2. **Solar Load through Fenestration** — Direct radiation through glass
3. **Internal Heat Gains** — Occupants, lights, equipment, appliances
4. **Infiltration Load** — Uncontrolled air leakage through cracks
5. **Ventilation Load** — Required fresh-air intake for IAQ

Each component has a *sensible* part (changes temperature) and a *latent* part (changes humidity).

## 2.2 The Two-Tier Estimation Approach

### Tier 1: Degree-Day Method (Simplified — Annual Estimate)
Best for **annual** energy estimation and quick audits. Cannot predict peak loads.

### Tier 2: CLTD/SCL/CLF Method (Detailed — Hourly Estimate)
Best for **equipment sizing** and hourly simulation. Recommended by ASHRAE.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $HDD = \sum_{d=1}^{365} \max(0,\ T_{b} - T_{mean,d})$ | Heating Degree Days | °C-day |
| 2 | $CDD = \sum_{d=1}^{365} \max(0,\ T_{mean,d} - T_{b})$ | Cooling Degree Days | °C-day |
| 3 | $Q_{h} = \frac{U \cdot A \cdot HDD \cdot 24}{1000}$ | Annual Heating Energy per zone | kWh |
| 4 | $Q_{h,\text{fuel}} = \frac{Q_{h}}{\eta_{\text{heater}}}$ | Heating energy at fuel source | kWh |
| 5 | $q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Instantaneous transmission heat loss | W |
| 6 | $q_{\text{inf}} = 1.23 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible infiltration/ventilation load (ρCp ≈ 1.23 kJ/m³·K) | W |
| 7 | $q_{\text{inf,lat}} = 3010 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W$ | Latent infiltration/ventilation load | W |
| 8 | $q_{\text{sol}} = A \cdot SHGF \cdot SC \cdot CLF$ | Solar gain through glass | W |
| 9 | $q_{\text{wall}} = U \cdot A \cdot CLTD_{\text{corr}}$ | Wall/roof cooling transmission | W |
| 10 | $q_{\text{people}} = N \cdot (q_s + q_l \cdot CLF_l)$ | Heat gain from occupants | W |
| 11 | $q_{\text{lights}} = W_{\text{inst}} \cdot CLF \cdot 3.413$ | Heat gain from lighting | W |
| 12 | $q_{\text{eqp}} = W_{\text{inst}} \cdot CLF \cdot 3.413$ | Heat gain from equipment | W |
| 13 | $CLTD_{\text{corr}} = CLTD + LM + (78 - T_r) + (T_m - 85)$ | Corrected CLTD (ASHRAE, °F basis) | °F |
| 14 | $EAC = \frac{Q_{\text{annual}}}{\text{Building conditioned area}}$ | Energy Areal Consumption | kWh/m²·year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When using equation (13) with °F values, convert to °C only AFTER the full ASHRAE correction has been applied. Do not mix metric/imperial mid-calculation.

## 2.4 Physical Meaning of Each Quantity

- **U × A**: Thermal conductance of the envelope element. Higher = more leak.
- **CLTD**: Equivalent steady-state temperature difference that produces the same instantaneous load as the actual dynamic (lagged) wall response to outdoor temperature swings.
- **CLF**: Because walls, furniture, and structure *store* heat temporarily, only a fraction of radiant heat becomes an instantaneous cooling load. CLF captures this delay.
- **SHGF × SC**: Net solar radiation actually passing through the glass after accounting for its shading properties.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Annual load estimation feeds directly into:
- **Energy Performance Certificate (EPC)** ratings for buildings
- **ECBC / Green Rating compliance** (India)
- HVAC equipment right-sizing (avoiding the common 30–50 % over-sizing problem)
- **Payback analysis** of insulation retrofits, window upgrades, and chiller replacement
- **Carbon footprint calculations** (kgCO₂ = EAC × grid emission factor)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Annual Heating Energy from Degree-Day Method

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Steady-State Heat Loss

For a single envelope element (wall, roof, window), the instantaneous heat loss is governed by **Fourier's law of conduction** through a composite wall:

$$
q = \frac{\Delta T}{R_{\text{total}}} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $\Delta T = T_{\text{indoor}} - T_{\text{outdoor}}$ and $U = 1/R_{\text{total}}$ is the overall heat transfer coefficient.

For an entire building with *n* envelope elements:

$$
q_{\text{total}}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i A_i \left[ T_{\text{in}} - T_{\text{out}}(t) \right] + q_{\text{inf}}(t) + q_{\text{vent}}(t)
$$

### 3.1.2 Time-Integration Over One Day

Assuming $T_{\text{in}}$ is held constant at the design temperature $T_b$ (e.g., 18 °C), the energy lost in one day is:

$$
E_{\text{day}} = \int_{0}^{24} q_{\text{total}}(t) \, dt
$$

Substituting and recognizing that infiltration losses are also proportional to the same $\Delta T$ (sensible portion):

$$
E_{\text{day}} = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i A_i + \dot{V} \rho C_p \right] \int_{0}^{24} \left[ T_b - T_{\text{out}}(t) \right] dt
$$

### 3.1.3 Defining the Heating Degree Day

The integral of the indoor-outdoor temperature difference over 24 hours is, by definition, the **Heating Degree Day** for that day:

$$
HDD_d = \frac{1}{24} \int_{0}^{24} \left[ T_b - T_{\text{out}}(t) \right] dt \quad \text{(only when positive)}
$$

If only daily mean outdoor temperature is available, this simplifies to:

$$
HDD_d = \max\left(0,\ T_b - T_{\text{mean,d}}\right)
$$

### 3.1.4 Final Annual Energy Expression

Integrating over the full year (365 days) and multiplying by 24 to convert °C·days to °C·hours:

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{annual}} = 24 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{n} U_i A_i \cdot HDD_{\text{annual}}}
$$

For SI units: $U$ in W/m²·K, $A$ in m², $HDD$ in °C·day, the result is in **Wh/year**. Divide by 1000 for **kWh/year**.

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Building in Trivandrum: $HDD_{18} \approx 0$ °C·day (tropical — no significant heating needed) ❄️
- Building in Srinagar: $HDD_{18} \approx 2700$ °C·day
- Wall: $U = 0.45 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $A = 200 \text{ m}^2$
- Roof: $U = 0.30 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $A = 100 \text{ m}^2$
- Windows: $U = 2.80 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $A = 30 \text{ m}^2$
- Infiltration: $\dot{V} = 0.5$ air changes/h, Volume = 1500 m³
- Heater efficiency $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 1 — Compute total UA:**

$$
\sum U_i A_i = (0.45)(200) + (0.30)(100) + (2.80)(30) = 90 + 30 + 84 = 204 \text{ W/K}
$$

**Step 2 — Add infiltration conductance:**

$$
\dot{V} \rho C_p = \left(\frac{0.5 \times 1500}{3600}\right) \times 1.23 \times 1000 = 256.25 \text{ W/K}
$$

$$
U_{\text{total}} A_{\text{total}} = 204 + 256.25 = 460.25 \text{ W/K}
$$

**Step 3 — Annual heating energy (delivered):**

$$
E_{\text{annual}} = \frac{24 \times 460.25 \times 2700}{1000} = 29{,}824 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Step 4 — Fuel-side (input) energy:**

$$
E_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{29{,}824}{0.85} = 35{,}087 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 1.26 \times 10^{11} \text{ J/year}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Cooling Load using CLTD Method

The CLTD method decomposes the cooling load into room (envelope, ventilation, infiltration) and internal (people, lights, equipment) components.

### 3.2.1 Wall and Roof Cooling Load

The CLTD value is read from ASHRAE tables (function of orientation, month, hour). The corrected CLTD for the actual conditions:

$$
CLTD_{\text{corr}} = CLTD_{\text{table}} + LM + (78 - T_r) + (T_m - 85)
$$

where:
- $LM$ = Latitude/Month correction
- $T_r$ = Indoor design temperature (°F)
- $T_m$ = Mean outdoor temperature (°F) for design day

Then:

$$
q_{\text{wall/roof}} = U \cdot A \cdot CLTD_{\text{corr}}
$$

### 3.2.2 Glass Solar Load

$$
q_{\text{glass}} = A_{\text{glass}} \cdot SHGF_{\max} \cdot SC \cdot CLF
$$

where $SHGF_{\max}$ is the maximum solar heat gain factor for the latitude/month/orientation, $SC$ is the shading coefficient of the glazing, and $CLF$ accounts for thermal storage (typically 0.85–1.0 for sunlit glass at peak hour).

### 3.2.3 Internal Loads

$$
q_{\text{people,s}} = N \cdot q_s \cdot CLF_s
$$

$$
q_{\text{people,l}} = N \cdot q_l \cdot CLF_l
$$

$$
q_{\text{lights}} = W_{\text{lamp}} \cdot CLF_{\text{lights}}
$$

$$
q_{\text{equipment}} = W_{\text{eqp}} \cdot CLF_{\text{eqp}}
$$

### 3.2.4 Infiltration and Ventilation

$$
q_{\text{inf,s}} = 1.23 \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{inf}} \cdot (T_o - T_i) \quad [\text{W}]
$$

$$
q_{\text{inf,l}} = 3010 \cdot \dot{V}_{\text{inf}} \cdot (W_o - W_i) \quad [\text{W}]
$$

### 3.2.5 Total Room Cooling Load

$$
\boxed{q_{\text{total}} = q_{\text{wall}} + q_{\text{roof}} + q_{\text{glass}} + q_{\text{people}} + q_{\text{lights}} + q_{\text{eqp}} + q_{\text{inf}} + q_{\text{vent}}}
$$

## 3.3 Annual Cooling Energy Estimation (Bin Method or Energy Balance)

For annual cooling energy, the energy audit engineer typically uses:

$$
E_{\text{cool,annual}} = \sum_{j=1}^{8760} q_{\text{cool}}(t_j) \cdot \Delta t \cdot \text{PLF}_j \cdot \frac{1}{COP}
$$

where $PLF$ is the part-load factor (typically 0.7–1.0) and $COP$ is the chiller/AC coefficient of performance.

For simplified audit work:

$$
E_{\text{cool,annual}} \approx \frac{q_{\text{peak}} \cdot EFLH_{\text{cool}}}{COP}
$$

where $EFLH$ = Equivalent Full-Load Hours of cooling (typically 800–1500 h for tropical India).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Heat Flow Schematic for a Building Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SUN] --> B[SHGF SC CLF]
    B --> C[Window Glass Gain]
    D[Outdoor Air] --> E[Infiltration Ventilation]
    F[Occupants] --> G[Sensible and Latent]
    H[Lights] --> I[Radiant and Convective]
    J[Equipment] --> I
    K[Walls] --> L[Transmission U A CLTD]
    M[Roof] --> L
    N[Floor] --> L
    O[Partition] --> L
    C --> P[ROOM COOLING LOAD]
    E --> P
    G --> P
    I --> P
    L --> P
    P --> Q[Chiller AC Unit]
    Q --> R[Annual Cooling Energy kWh per year]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Annual Load Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inpA[Building Geometry] --> proc1[UA Calculation]
    inpB[Wall Roof Window Material] --> proc1
    inpC[Climate Data Tmean Tmax SHGF] --> proc2[Degree Day Calculation]
    inpD[Occupancy Schedule] --> proc3[Internal Load]
    inpE[Lighting Wattage] --> proc3
    inpF[Equipment Wattage] --> proc3
    inpG[Infiltration Rate ACH] --> proc4[Ventilation Load]
    inpH[Heater Efficiency] --> proc5[Heating Energy]
    inpI[Chiller COP] --> proc6[Cooling Energy]
    proc1 --> proc5
    proc1 --> proc6
    proc2 --> proc5
    proc2 --> proc6
    proc3 --> proc6
    proc4 --> proc6
    proc5 --> outA[Annual Heating kWh]
    proc6 --> outB[Annual Cooling kWh]
    outA --> outC[EUI kWh per m2 per year]
    outB --> outC
```

## 4.3 CLTD Method Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step1 Identify Zone and Design Day] --> S2[Step2 Compute UA for Walls Roof Glass]
    S2 --> S3[Step3 Read CLTD from ASHRAE Table]
    S3 --> S4[Step4 Apply Correction LM Tr Tm]
    S4 --> S5[Step5 qwall equals U A CLTDcorr]
    S5 --> S6[Step6 Compute Glass Load A SHGF SC CLF]
    S6 --> S7[Step7 Compute Internal Loads]
    S7 --> S8[Step8 Compute Infiltration Ventilation Load]
    S8 --> S9[Step9 Sum Sensible and Latent for Room Peak]
    S9 --> S10[Step10 Convert to Annual using EFLH and COP]
```

## 4.4 Degree-Day Logic Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    db[(Daily Tmean from Weather File)] --> judge{Tmean less than Tbase}
    judge -- YES --> hd[HDD add Tbase minus Tmean]
    judge -- NO --> skip[Skip day]
    db --> judge2{Tmean greater than Tbase}
    judge2 -- YES --> cd[CDD add Tmean minus Tbase]
    judge2 -- NO --> skip
    hd --> sum[Annual Sum]
    cd --> sum
    sum --> load[Annual Heating or Cooling Energy via UA]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define *Heating Degree Day (HDD)* and *Cooling Degree Day (CDD)*. Why is a base temperature of 18 °C commonly used?

**Model Answer:**
HDD is the difference between a reference base temperature (typically 18 °C) and the daily mean outdoor temperature, summed only for days when the mean is below the base. CDD is its symmetric counterpart for cooling. The base of **18 °C** is chosen because below this temperature, most buildings require mechanical heating for occupant thermal comfort, and above it, cooling may be required, aligning with the human thermal neutrality zone for sedentary indoor activity.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
What is the **Cooling Load Factor (CLF)**? Why is it always less than or equal to 1 for radiant-type heat sources?

**Model Answer:**
CLF is the fraction of the heat generated by an internal source (lights, equipment, occupants, or sun) that appears as an instantaneous cooling load on the air-handling unit. For purely radiant sources like unducted lights or sun on a slab, CLF < 1 because a portion of the radiant energy is absorbed by the building structure (floor, walls, furniture) and is re-released later by convection. Only the convective fraction contributes immediately to cooling load.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]

**(a)** A small office building in Delhi has the following envelope and operational data:
- North wall: $U = 0.55 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $A = 80 \text{ m}^2$
- South wall: $U = 0.55 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $A = 80 \text{ m}^2$
- Roof: $U = 0.40 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $A = 200 \text{ m}^2$
- Single-glazed windows: $U = 5.8 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $A = 40 \text{ m}^2$
- Volume = 3000 m³, Infiltration rate = 1.0 ACH
- Indoor design $T = 22 \text{ °C}$
- $HDD_{18}$ for Delhi = **1100 °C-day**
- Heater efficiency $\eta = 0.80$

Compute the annual heating energy required (in kWh and MJ), both delivered and at the fuel source. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between the Degree-Day Method and the CLTD/SCL/CLF Method. State which is appropriate for *equipment sizing* and which is appropriate for *annual energy audit*. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q-A (a)**

**Step 1 — Compute envelope UA:**

$$
(UA)_{\text{env}} = (0.55)(80+80) + (0.40)(200) + (5.8)(40)
$$

$$
(UA)_{\text{env}} = 88 + 80 + 232 = 400 \text{ W/K}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute infiltration conductance:**

$$
\dot{V} = \frac{1.0 \times 3000}{3600} = 0.8333 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
(UA)_{\text{inf}} = \dot{V} \rho C_p = 0.8333 \times 1.23 = 1.025 \text{ kW/K} = 1025 \text{ W/K}
$$

**Step 3 — Total UA:**

$$
(UA)_{\text{tot}} = 400 + 1025 = 1425 \text{ W/K}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual delivered heat:**

$$
E_{\text{del}} = \frac{24 \times 1425 \times 1100}{1000} = 37{,}620 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to MJ:**

$$
E_{\text{del}} = 37{,}620 \times 3.6 = 135{,}432 \text{ MJ/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Fuel-side energy:**

$$
E_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{37{,}620}{0.80} = 47{,}025 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 169{,}290 \text{ MJ/year}
$$

---

### **Model Solution for Q-A (b)**

The **Degree-Day Method** is a *simplified* steady-state approach that treats the building as a single thermal zone whose heat loss is linearly proportional to (Tbase − Tout). It is excellent for **annual energy auditing** and **energy conservation opportunity (ECO) screening** because it requires only monthly weather data (HDD/CDD) and total UA. It **cannot** predict peak hourly loads.

The **CLTD/SCL/CLF Method** is a *detailed* hourly method recommended by ASHRAE that separately computes transmission (walls, roof), solar (through glass), internal (people, lights, equipment), and ventilation loads. Each is calculated for a specific design hour, and CLTD/CLF account for **thermal storage and radiant delays**. It is appropriate for **HVAC equipment sizing** where the peak design load must be known precisely to avoid over- or under-sizing.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** A conference room in a commercial building has the following loads at the peak summer design hour (3 PM, July):
- South-facing glass: $A = 20 \text{ m}^2$, $SHGF_{\max} = 380 \text{ W/m}^2$, $SC = 0.88$, $CLF = 0.92$
- East and West walls: $U = 0.50 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $A = 60 \text{ m}^2$ each, $CLTD_{\text{corr}} = 18 \text{ K}$
- Roof: $U = 0.35 \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}$, $A = 150 \text{ m}^2$, $CLTD_{\text{corr}} = 25 \text{ K}$
- Occupants: 50 people (office work), $q_s = 70 \text{ W/p}$, $q_l = 55 \text{ W/p}$, $CLF_s = CLF_l = 1.0$
- Lights: 2 kW total, $CLF = 1.0$ (ducted return, all convective)
- Equipment: 1.5 kW, $CLF = 1.0$
- Ventilation: $\dot{V} = 0.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $T_o = 38 \text{ °C}$, $T_i = 24 \text{ °C}$, $W_o = 0.018$, $W_i = 0.009$

Compute the total **sensible** and **latent** cooling load. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List the major categories of internal heat gains in a building and explain, with an example, why their CLF may be less than 1. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q-B (a)**

**Step 1 — Glass solar load (sensible):**

$$
q_{\text{glass}} = 20 \times 380 \times 0.88 \times 0.92 = 6151.36 \text{ W} \approx 6.15 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — East and West walls:**

$$
q_{\text{walls}} = (0.50)(60)(18) + (0.50)(60)(18) = 540 + 540 = 1080 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 3 — Roof:**

$$
q_{\text{roof}} = (0.35)(150)(25) = 1312.5 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 4 — Occupants:**

$$
q_{\text{occ,s}} = 50 \times 70 \times 1.0 = 3500 \text{ W}
$$

$$
q_{\text{occ,l}} = 50 \times 55 \times 1.0 = 2750 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 5 — Lights:**

$$
q_{\text{lights}} = 2000 \times 1.0 = 2000 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 6 — Equipment:**

$$
q_{\text{eqp}} = 1500 \times 1.0 = 1500 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 7 — Ventilation (sensible):**

$$
q_{\text{vent,s}} = 1.23 \times 0.4 \times (38 - 24) = 6.89 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 8 — Ventilation (latent):**

$$
q_{\text{vent,l}} = 3010 \times 0.4 \times (0.018 - 0.009) = 10.84 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 9 — Total sensible and latent:**

$$
q_{\text{sensible}} = 6151 + 1080 + 1312 + 3500 + 2000 + 1500 + 6.89 \approx 15{,}550 \text{ W}
$$

$$
q_{\text{latent}} = 2750 + 10.84 \approx 2761 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{q_{\text{total}} \approx 18.31 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

### **Model Solution for Q-B (b)**

Major categories of internal heat gains are:
1. **People (occupants)** — sensible (body warmth, raised skin temperature) and latent (perspiration, respiration).
2. **Lighting** — direct electrical-to-thermal conversion (mostly convective when ducted, mostly radiant when not).
3. **Electrical equipment** — computers, printers, kitchen appliances, motors.
4. **Appliances and process loads** — elevators, escalators, refrigerators.
5. **Plumbing and hot water piping** — minor but persistent heat sources.

**Why CLF < 1 (with example):** Consider unducted fluorescent lights in a room with a high ceiling. About 60 % of the input electrical energy is emitted as long-wave infrared radiation *before* reaching the floor. This radiant portion does not immediately heat the air; instead, it is absorbed by the floor slab, furniture, and walls, raising their surface temperature. Hours later, this stored heat is re-released to the air by convection. Thus, at the design peak hour, only the convective portion of the radiant heat gain contributes to the instantaneous cooling load — captured mathematically by CLF < 1.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert HDD from °C·day to Wh correctly.** The factor is **24 × UA × HDD / 1000** in SI; mixing this up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Ignoring the correction terms in the CLTD formula** (LM, Tr, Tm). ASHRAE tables are prepared for a standard indoor of 78 °F and outdoor mean of 85 °F; using uncorrected CLTDs with non-standard conditions gives wrong answers.
> 3. **Not separating sensible and latent loads.** Always state *both* components and use the correct latent formula $3010 \dot{V} \Delta W$ (W), not the sensible one.
> 4. **Confusing cooling *load* (instantaneous, W) with cooling *energy* (cumulative, kWh).** Examiners expect a clear distinction.
> 5. **Using SHGF values directly without SC and CLF.** All three multipliers are mandatory for the glass solar load.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Degree-Day Method** = quick annual estimate; uses base temperature (typically **18 °C**) and *monthly/annual* weather data.
- ✅ **Heating Load Equation (SI):** $E_h = \frac{24 \cdot (UA)_{\text{total}} \cdot HDD}{1000}$ kWh/year. Always add infiltration conductance $\dot{V}\rho C_p$ to the envelope UA.
- ✅ **Fuel-side energy** divides delivered energy by heater efficiency; cooling energy divides by **COP**.
- ✅ **CLTD Method** is for *hourly peak* equipment sizing; it requires ASHRAE CLTD tables keyed by orientation, month, hour, latitude.
- ✅ The corrected CLTD formula: $CLTD_{\text{corr}} = CLTD + LM + (78 - T_r) + (T_m - 85)$ — uses °F for the standard corrections.
- ✅ **Solar Load Equation:** $q = A \cdot SHGF_{\max} \cdot SC \cdot CLF$.
- ✅ **Internal Loads:** $q_{\text{lights}} = W \cdot CLF \cdot 3.413$ (3.413 converts BTU/h to W).
- ✅ **Sensible infiltration:** $q = 1.23 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta T$ W (where 1.23 = ρCp in kJ/m³·K).
- ✅ **Latent infiltration:** $q = 3010 \cdot \dot{V} \cdot \Delta W$ W (humidity ratio in kg/kg).
- ✅ **CLF ≤ 1** for radiant sources; **CLF = 1** for purely convective sources (ducted lights, people convective portion).
- ✅ **EAC / EUI** (Energy Areal Consumption) = $E_{\text{annual}} / A_{\text{floor}}$ in kWh/m²·year — the *key metric* in energy audits.
- ✅ Typical Indian residential HDD₍₁₈₎: Trivandrum ≈ 0, Delhi ≈ 1100, Srinagar ≈ 2700 °C-day.
- ✅ Typical Indian residential CDD₍₁₈₎: Trivandrum ≈ 3000, Chennai ≈ 2800, Delhi ≈ 2200 °C-day.
- ✅ Annual cooling EFLH for tropical Indian buildings ≈ 800–1500 h.
- ✅ Solar heat gain through glass is the *single largest* peak-hour load component in commercial buildings.
- ✅ Over-sizing HVAC equipment by even 30 % reduces *seasonal* COP by 15–25 % — accurate load estimation saves operating cost.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Factors that Influence Thermal Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Factors that Influence Thermal Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Thermal Performance

**Thermal Performance** is a quantitative measure of how effectively a system, component, or enclosure (such as a building envelope, furnace wall, pipe insulation, or boiler) controls, transfers, or resists the flow of heat energy between two regions at different temperatures.

In the context of **Energy Management and Conservation (KTU PESFT414)**, thermal performance is evaluated using measurable parameters such as the **U-value (Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient)**, **R-value (Thermal Resistance)**, **Thermal Conductivity (k)**, and **Heat Flux (q)**.

Mathematically, the steady-state thermal performance of a composite system is expressed as:

$$ q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T $$

where:
- **q** = rate of heat transfer (W)
- **U** = Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (W/m²·K)
- **A** = cross-sectional area perpendicular to heat flow (m²)
- **ΔT** = temperature difference across the system (K or °C)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The lower the **U-value**, the better the thermal performance of a system, because less heat is being transferred per unit area per unit temperature difference. This directly translates to **lower energy losses** and **higher energy conservation efficiency**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a traditional **earthen clay water pot (matka)** used in Indian summer to keep water cool:

- The **thickness of the clay wall** acts like thermal insulation — thicker walls mean slower heat flow.
- The **porosity** of the clay traps tiny air pockets, reducing thermal conductivity.
- The **outer surface color and texture** affects how much solar radiation is absorbed.
- The **ambient wind speed** around the pot influences convective heat loss.
- The **moisture evaporating** from the outer surface provides a cooling effect (latent heat).

Similarly, every thermal system in engineering — be it a building wall, a steam pipe, a furnace lining, or an industrial oven — has a set of physical, environmental, and material factors that together dictate its overall thermal performance.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal Conductivity | **k** | W/(m·K) | Intrinsic ability of material to conduct heat |
| Thermal Resistance | **R** | m²·K/W | Resistance offered to heat flow |
| Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | **U** | W/(m²·K) | Combined conductance of the entire system |
| Emissivity | **ε** | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Efficiency of a surface in emitting radiation |
| Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | **σ** | 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴) | Universal constant for blackbody radiation |
| Heat Flux | **q** | W/m² | Rate of heat flow per unit area |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Relationship:** $R = \frac{1}{U}$ and for a homogeneous slab, $R = \frac{L}{k}$, where **L** is the thickness of the material.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Factors Influencing Thermal Performance

The factors that influence thermal performance can be systematically classified into **four major categories**:

1. **Material Properties** — conductivity, density, specific heat, emissivity
2. **Geometric / Design Parameters** — thickness, surface area, shape, orientation
3. **Environmental / Operating Conditions** — temperature difference, wind speed, humidity, solar radiation
4. **System-Level Factors** — air infiltration, thermal bridges, surface conditions, moisture content

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Board Examinations:** Students must be able to (a) identify each factor, (b) state its physical effect, and (c) compute its quantitative impact on heat transfer using Fourier, Newton, or Stefan-Boltzmann equations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Three Modes of Heat Transfer

Thermal performance is fundamentally governed by the interplay of three heat transfer modes. Each mode contributes a distinct resistance, and the **overall thermal performance is dictated by the combined effect**.

### A. Conduction (Fourier's Law)

Steady-state one-dimensional conduction through a homogeneous slab:

$$ q_{cond} = \frac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L} $$

- **Why it matters:** Conduction through walls, insulation layers, window glass, furnace refractories, and pipe walls.
- **How it works:** Heat flows from the hot side to the cold side via molecular/atomic vibrations and free-electron movement.

### B. Convection (Newton's Law of Cooling)

$$ q_{conv} = h_c \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_\infty) $$

- **Why it matters:** Heat exchange between a solid surface and a moving fluid (air, water, oil, flue gas).
- **How it works:** Forced convection (fans, pumps, wind) and natural convection (buoyancy-driven) both influence surface heat loss.

### C. Radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann Law)

$$ q_{rad} = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4) $$
