# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PESFT414 – Energy Management and Conservation**, Module 2 introduces the foundational justification for adopting structured energy management practices. The **"Need"** topic formally establishes the *imperative drivers* that compel industries, commercial establishments, and the national economy to pursue energy management and conservation as a strategic, operational, and environmental priority.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:**
> The *Need for Energy Management and Conservation* refers to the **multifaceted economic, environmental, social, technological, and regulatory drivers** that justify the systematic control, monitoring, and optimization of energy use across industrial, commercial, and residential sectors, to ensure sustainable development, cost competitiveness, and compliance with national/international energy policies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Student's First-Read Intuition

Imagine your **personal monthly pocket-money budget** of ₹5,000. You *need* to track it because:
- If you spend blindly, you run out of money before the month ends (analogous to **resource scarcity**).
- Electricity bills, like mobile recharge bills, keep rising every year (analogous to **rising energy costs**).
- Wasting food in your fridge is bad for the environment *and* your wallet (analogous to **environmental + economic dual impact**).
- Your parents insist you keep receipts (analogous to **energy audit / regulatory compliance**).

Now scale this analogy to a **factory consuming ₹50 crore worth of electricity per year**. The same logic applies — but with far greater financial, environmental, and strategic stakes. This is precisely the **"Need"** for energy management.

## 1.3 The Five Pillars of "Need" (KTU High-Yield Framework)

| Pillar | Core Driver | Magnitude Indicator |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Economic** | Rising energy costs, profitability | Energy cost as **5–15%** of operating cost |
| **Environmental** | Climate change, GHG emissions | CO₂ footprint per unit output |
| **Strategic / Resource** | Fossil fuel depletion | Global oil/gas reserve depletion rates |
| **Regulatory** | Energy Conservation Act 2001 | Mandatory compliance (BEE, PAT, ECBC) |
| **Social / Ethical** | Energy security, equitable access | Per capita energy consumption (kWh) |

> [!NOTE]
> The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India, has classified industries into **35 energy-intensive sectors** under the **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT)** scheme — making the *Need* not optional but **legally mandated** for Designated Consumers (DCs).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deconstructing the "Need" — Structured Logic

The Need for Energy Management is not a single argument; it is a **convergence of five independent arguments** that, when combined, make inaction economically irrational and ethically indefensible.

### 2.1.1 Economic Need
- Energy is a **direct operating expense**, often the **second or third largest cost head** (after raw materials and labour) in manufacturing industries.
- For energy-intensive sectors (aluminium, cement, steel, fertilisers, paper, textiles, chlor-alkali), energy cost can be **20–40%** of total production cost.
- A **1% reduction** in specific energy consumption (SEC) in a ₹1000 crore-turnover industry can save **₹1–10 crore annually** depending on the energy intensity.

### 2.1.2 Environmental Need
- The **IPCC Sixth Assessment Report (AR6, 2022)** confirms that CO₂ emissions must fall by **~43% by 2030** (from 2019 levels) to limit warming to 1.5 °C.
- India's installed electricity capacity is still **~50% coal-based** (as of 2024 CEA data), meaning every kWh saved directly reduces CO₂ emissions at the point of generation.
- **Emission factor** for Indian grid electricity ≈ **0.72 kg CO₂ / kWh** (CEA, 2023 baseline).

### 2.1.3 Strategic / Resource Need
- Global proven oil reserves: ~**47 years** at current production rates (BP Statistical Review, 2023).
- India's **energy import dependency** ≈ **42–45%** of total primary energy supply, exposing the economy to geopolitical and price shocks.
- Domestic coal, though abundant, is of **low calorific value** (~3,500–4,000 kcal/kg) and contributes heavily to emissions.

### 2.1.4 Regulatory & Policy Need
- **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** (amended 2010, 2022) — establishes BEE, mandates energy audits for DCs.
- **PAT (Perform, Achieve and Trade) Scheme** — market-based mechanism with **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)**.
- **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** — mandatory for commercial buildings > 120 kVA connected load.
- **ISO 50001:2018** — international Energy Management System standard.
- **India's Net-Zero Commitment by 2070** (announced at COP26, Glasgow, 2021).

### 2.1.5 Social & Ethical Need
- **Universal energy access** — ~100% village electrification (Saubhagya scheme, 2018), but **per capita consumption** still low (~1,200 kWh vs. global average ~3,200 kWh).
- **Energy poverty** is a developmental issue: ~70 million Indians still lack reliable electricity.
- Efficient use by existing consumers frees up generation capacity for new consumers.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for solving any numerical problem linked to the "Need" topic in KTU ESE / Internal evaluations.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Standard Units | Application |
|---|------------------|------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E_{\text{consumed}}}{P_{\text{output}}}$ | kWh / tonne, GJ / tonne, toe / unit | Benchmarks industrial energy efficiency |
| 2 | **Energy Cost as % of Operating Cost** | $\%\text{EC} = \dfrac{C_E \times E_{\text{annual}}}{C_{\text{OPEX}}} \times 100$ | % | Decides whether an industry falls under BEE/DC mandate |
| 3 | **Annual CO₂ Emissions from Electricity** | $M_{\text{CO}_2} = E_{\text{kWh}} \times \text{EF}$ | kg CO₂ / yr | EF ≈ **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh** for India grid (CEA 2023) |
| 4 | **Annual Monetary Savings (kWh → ₹)** | $S_{\text{₹}} = \Delta E_{\text{kWh}} \times T_{\text{₹/kWh}}$ | ₹ / year | Simple Payback calculations |
| 5 | **Simple Payback Period** | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{C_{\text{investment}}}{\Delta C_{\text{annual}}}$ | Years | Investment appraisal for ECMs |
| 6 | **Energy Intensity (National Level)** | $\text{EI}_{\text{national}} = \dfrac{\text{TPEC}}{\text{GDP}}$ | toe / crore ₹ (or kgoe / $) | Macroeconomic energy efficiency indicator |
| 7 | **Fuel Reserve Depletion Index** | $\text{Reserve Life} = \dfrac{\text{Proven Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}$ | Years | Strategic planning metric |
| 8 | **Avoided Emission Cost** | $C_{\text{avoid}} = M_{\text{CO}_2,\text{avoided}} \times P_{\text{carbon}}$ | ₹ / year | Carbon credit valuation (PAT market) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When asked "Discuss the need for energy management," examiners expect at least **four distinct drivers** (economic, environmental, regulatory, strategic) backed by **one numerical example or stat**. A purely qualitative answer loses marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Formulas

- **SEC benchmarking** allows a cement plant to compare its 110 kWh/tonne performance against the best-in-class of 90 kWh/tonne — the gap is the **conservation potential**.
- **SPP (Simple Payback Period)** is the KTU-preferred quick screening metric for Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs). Most industrial ECMs with SPP < **3 years** are considered bankable.
- **Avoided CO₂ quantification** is now essential for **PAT compliance** reporting and **green financing** under RBI's 2023 Sustainable Finance framework.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: Annual Cost Savings from an Energy Conservation Measure (ECM)

> **Problem Context:** A KTU-style numerical is typically framed as: *"A motor draws 50 kW for 6,000 hours/year. Replacing it with an energy-efficient IE4 motor reduces losses by 8%. Electricity tariff is ₹7.50/kWh. Calculate annual monetary savings and avoided CO₂ emissions."*

### Step 1: Identify Original Energy Consumption

The original annual energy consumption $E_1$ in kWh is given by:

$$E_1 = P_{\text{rated}} \times t_{\text{operating}}$$

where $P_{\text{rated}} = 50\ \text{kW}$ and $t_{\text{operating}} = 6{,}000\ \text{h/year}$.

Substituting the numerical values:

$$E_1 = 50 \times 6{,}000 = 300{,}000\ \text{kWh/year}$$

### Step 2: Calculate Energy Reduction (8% of Input)

The fractional reduction in losses is given as **8%**, which directly reduces input power by:

$$\Delta E = 0.08 \times E_1$$

Substituting the numerical value:

$$\Delta E = 0.08 \times 300{,}000 = 24{,}000\ \text{kWh/year}$$

### Step 3: Compute Annual Monetary Savings

The annual monetary savings $S_{\text{₹}}$ is the product of energy saved and the unit tariff $T_{\text{₹/kWh}}$:

$$S_{\text{₹}} = \Delta E \times T_{\text{₹/kWh}}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$S_{\text{₹}} = 24{,}000 \times 7.50 = \text{₹}\ 1{,}80{,}000\ /\ \text{year}$$

### Step 4: Compute Avoided CO₂ Emissions

Using the India grid emission factor $\text{EF} = 0.72\ \text{kg CO}_2 / \text{kWh}$ (CEA 2023):

$$M_{\text{CO}_2,\text{avoided}} = \Delta E \times \text{EF}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$M_{\text{CO}_2,\text{avoided}} = 24{,}000 \times 0.72 = 17{,}280\ \text{kg CO}_2\ /\ \text{year}$$

### Step 5: Convert to Tonnes (KTU Standard Reporting Unit)

$$M_{\text{CO}_2,\text{avoided}} = \dfrac{17{,}280}{1{,}000} = 17.28\ \text{tCO}_2\ /\ \text{year}$$

### Step 6: Final Result Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Numerical Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-----------------|------|
| Annual Energy Saved | $\Delta E$ | **24,000** | kWh / year |
| Annual Monetary Saving | $S_{\text{₹}}$ | **₹ 1,80,000** | ₹ / year |
| Avoided CO₂ Emissions | $M_{\text{CO}_2}$ | **17.28** | tCO₂ / year |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This single calculation simultaneously demonstrates the **economic need** (₹1.8 lakh saved) and the **environmental need** (17.28 tCO₂ avoided). Examiners love such dual-impact numericals.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B: Decision Rule for Mandatory Energy Audit (BEE Criterion)

### Step 1: Define Energy Cost Share

For an industry to fall under the **BEE Designated Consumer (DC)** category (mandatory annual energy audit), its energy cost must exceed a threshold share of operating cost. The threshold used by BEE is:

$$\%\text{EC} = \dfrac{C_E \times E_{\text{annual}}}{C_{\text{OPEX}}} \times 100 \geq \text{threshold}$$

> The typical threshold (KTU/PAT understanding) is **≥ 3% of total operating cost** for most energy-intensive sectors.

### Step 2: Worked Example

**Given:**
- Annual energy consumption: $E = 5 \times 10^6\ \text{kWh/year}$
- Average tariff: $C_E = \text{₹}\ 8/\text{kWh}$
- Total operating cost (OPEX): $C_{\text{OPEX}} = \text{₹}\ 50\ \text{crore} = 5 \times 10^8\ \text{₹}$

**Step 2a — Annual Energy Bill:**

$$C_{E,\text{annual}} = E \times C_E = 5 \times 10^6 \times 8 = \text{₹}\ 4 \times 10^7$$

**Step 2b — Energy Cost as % of OPEX:**

$$\%\text{EC} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^7}{5 \times 10^8} \times 100 = 0.08 \times 100 = 8\%$$

**Step 2c — Decision:**

Since $8\% \geq 3\%$, the unit **qualifies** as a Designated Consumer under BEE, and **mandatory energy audit** under the Energy Conservation Act applies.

### Step 3: Generalised Decision Matrix (KTU Quick Reference)

| Industry | Typical Energy Cost (% OPEX) | DC Status | Audit Frequency |
|----------|------------------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Aluminium Smelter | 35–40% | **DC** | Annual |
| Cement Plant | 25–30% | **DC** | Annual |
| Pulp & Paper | 15–20% | **DC** | Annual |
| Textile Mill | 10–15% | **DC** | Annual |
| IT/Software Office | 1.5–3% | Non-DC (unless > 100 kW DG) | Voluntary |
| Hospital (multi-speciality) | 2.5–4% | **DC** (if > 500 kVA) | Annual |

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks: *"An industry consumes 10 lakh kWh/year at ₹7/kWh. Its turnover is ₹10 crore. Is it a Designated Consumer? Justify."* — Use the formula above as the standard answer template.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Five Drivers Framework of "Need"

```mermaid

    A[NEED FOR ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Logical Flow Linking Need to Action


## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cost-Share Decision Logic (DC vs Non-DC)

```mermaid

    DC --> MAUDIT[Annual Mandatory Energy Audit]

```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams collectively map the **rationale**, **methodology**, and **decision logic** of the "Need" topic. In KTU board exam answers, a well-labelled block diagram (even hand-drawn) scores **2–3 extra marks** in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the term "Specific Energy Consumption" and state its significance in justifying the need for energy management.**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) is defined as the energy consumed per unit of physical output of a process or industry.

$$\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E_{\text{consumed}}}{P_{\text{output}}}$$

**Significance (3 key points):**
1. It is a **technology-benchmarking metric** — industries compare their SEC against best-in-class globally.
2. Lower SEC implies **lower production cost** and **lower emissions per unit**, directly justifying investment in energy management.
3. BEE uses SEC as the **primary KPI** for setting PAT targets for Designated Consumers.

---

### **Q2. State any three regulatory mechanisms in India that mandate the need for energy conservation.**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** (amended 2010, 2022) — established the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) and mandates energy audits for DCs.
2. **PAT (Perform, Achieve and Trade) Scheme** — market-based mechanism for 35 energy-intensive sectors with tradable Energy Saving Certificates.
3. **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** — mandatory minimum energy performance standards for commercial buildings exceeding 120 kVA connected load.
4. *(Optional, for extra credit)* **ISO 50001:2018** — international voluntary Energy Management System standard adopted by many Indian firms.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

#### (a) Discuss in detail the **economic, environmental, and strategic drivers** that establish the need for energy management in an industrial unit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**1. Economic Driver (≈ 2.5 marks):**
- Energy is the **second/third largest cost head** in most manufacturing industries (after raw materials and labour).
- For energy-intensive sectors like aluminium, cement, steel, fertilisers, and chlor-alkali, energy cost is **20–40%** of production cost.
- A **1% reduction** in SEC can yield savings of **₹1–10 crore/year** in a mid-sized ₹1,000 crore industry.
- Tariff hikes by regulators (5–10% annually in some states) and volatile fossil fuel prices make energy a **financial risk** that must be hedged via efficiency.

**2. Environmental Driver (≈ 2.5 marks):**
- India is the **third-largest CO₂ emitter** in the world (~2.9 GtCO₂/year, 2022 data).
- The **IPCC AR6 (2022)** mandates a 43% reduction in global CO₂ emissions by 2030.
- India's grid emission factor ≈ **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh** — every kWh avoided = 0.72 kg CO₂ avoided.
- **Net-Zero 2070 commitment** (COP26) and **LiFE (Lifestyle for Environment) mission** make energy conservation a national priority.

**3. Strategic / Resource Driver (≈ 2 marks):**
- India imports **~42–45%** of its primary energy — a major drain on foreign exchange reserves.
- Global proven oil reserves are projected to last only **~47 years** at current consumption.
- Domestic coal is **low calorific** and high-ash, limiting efficient generation.
- Energy security requires a **two-pronged strategy**: (i) renewable expansion, (ii) aggressive demand-side efficiency.

#### (b) A motor in a plant operates at 75 kW, 4,800 hours/year. Replacing it with an IE4 energy-efficient motor reduces input power by 7%. The electricity tariff is ₹8.20/kWh and the grid emission factor is 0.72 kg CO₂/kWh. Compute: **(i)** annual energy saved, **(ii)** annual monetary saving, and **(iii)** avoided CO₂ emissions in tonnes. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $P = 75\ \text{kW}$, $t = 4{,}800\ \text{h/yr}$, Reduction $= 7\%$, Tariff $= \text{₹}\ 8.20/\text{kWh}$, $\text{EF} = 0.72\ \text{kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$.

**Step 1 — Annual Original Energy Consumption (1 mark):**

$$E_1 = P \times t = 75 \times 4{,}800 = 3{,}60{,}000\ \text{kWh/yr}$$

**Step 2 — Annual Energy Saved (2 marks):**

$$\Delta E = 0.07 \times 3{,}60{,}000 = 25{,}200\ \text{kWh/yr}$$

**Step 3 — Annual Monetary Saving (2 marks):**

$$S_{\text{₹}} = \Delta E \times \text{Tariff} = 25{,}200 \times 8.20 = \text{₹}\ 2{,}06{,}640\ /\ \text{yr}$$

**Step 4 — Avoided CO₂ Emissions (2 marks):**

$$M_{\text{CO}_2} = 25{,}200 \times 0.72 = 18{,}144\ \text{kg CO}_2/\text{yr}$$

$$M_{\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{18{,}144}{1{,}000} = 18.144\ \text{tCO}_2/\text{yr}$$

**Final Tabular Result (for clarity):**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Energy Saved | **25,200 kWh/yr** |
| Monetary Saving | **₹ 2,06,640/yr** |
| CO₂ Avoided | **18.144 tCO₂/yr** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

#### (a) Explain the **regulatory and policy framework** in India that makes energy management a mandatory requirement. List at least four instruments with a one-line description each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Energy Conservation Act, 2001 (amended 2010, 2022):** The foundational Indian legislation that established the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** and empowers the central government to notify energy-intensive industries as **Designated Consumers (DCs)** required to undertake periodic energy audits. **(2 marks)**

2. **PAT (Perform, Achieve and Trade) Scheme:** A market-based regulatory mechanism launched in 2012 covering **35 energy-intensive sectors** (aluminium, cement, chlor-alkali, fertiliser, iron & steel, paper, petrochemical, etc.) that issues tradable **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)**. **(2 marks)**

3. **ECBC + Eco-Niwas Samhita (Building Codes):** Mandatory minimum energy performance standards for **commercial buildings (ECBC, 2007; ECBC+, 2018)** and residential buildings (Eco-Niwas Samhita, 2018), updated periodically. **(1.5 marks)**

4. **Standards & Labelling Programme (BEE):** Mandatory star-labelling for **appliances** (AC, refrigerator, LED bulbs, fans, motors) and **industrial equipment**, penalising non-labelled products. **(1.5 marks)**

#### (b) An industry consumes 8 million kWh/year at an average tariff of ₹7.50/kWh. Its total annual operating cost is ₹60 crore. Determine whether the industry qualifies as a **Designated Consumer** under BEE norms. Comment on the audit obligations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Annual Energy Bill (2 marks):**

$$C_{E,\text{annual}} = 8 \times 10^6 \times 7.50 = \text{₹}\ 6 \times 10^7 = \text{₹}\ 6\ \text{crore}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Energy Cost as % of OPEX (2 marks):**

$$\%\text{EC} = \dfrac{6 \times 10^7}{60 \times 10^7} \times 100 = 10\%$$

**Step 3 — Decision (2 marks):**

Since $10\% \gg 3\%$ (the typical BEE threshold for energy-intensive industries), the unit **definitely qualifies** as a Designated Consumer.

**Step 4 — Audit Obligations (1 mark):**

The industry must:
- Appoint a **Certified Energy Auditor (CEA-1, CEA-2, or CEA-3)**.
- Conduct **mandatory annual energy audit** (or accredited energy audit every 3 years + annual self-audit).
- Submit compliance report to **BEE** on the **PAT portal**.
- Achieve **SEC reduction targets** notified under PAT cycles.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SEC with Total Energy Consumption.** SEC is energy *per unit output* — a small industry with high SEC is worse than a large one with low SEC.
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversions.** kWh → MJ (×3.6) and kg CO₂ → tCO₂ (÷1000) are frequent drop-points.
> 3. **Quoting outdated emission factors.** The current CEA grid emission factor is **0.72 kg CO₂/kWh (2023)** — using older values like 0.82 or 0.98 invites a -0.5 mark deduction.
> 4. **Generic answers in 14-mark questions.** Writing only "save money and protect environment" without naming a specific Act, scheme, or numerical example will fetch at most 5–6 out of 14.
> 5. **Skipping the unit in numerical answers.** Always write kWh, ₹/year, tCO₂/year — not bare numbers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU ESE):**

- ✅ **Five drivers of Need:** Economic, Environmental, Strategic, Regulatory, Social.
- ✅ **Key Indian regulations:** Energy Conservation Act 2001, BEE, PAT, ECBC, Standards & Labelling, ISO 50001.
- ✅ **SEC formula:** $E / P_{\text{output}}$ (kWh/tonne or GJ/tonne).
- ✅ **Designated Consumer threshold:** Energy cost typically **≥ 3%** of OPEX for energy-intensive sectors.
- ✅ **Grid Emission Factor (India, 2023):** **0.72 kg CO₂ / kWh**.
- ✅ **Avoided CO₂ formula:** $\Delta E_{\text{kWh}} \times 0.72$ gives kg CO₂.
- ✅ **India's Net-Zero Target:** **2070** (announced at COP26, Glasgow, November 2021).
- ✅ **PAT Sectors:** **35 sectors** (aluminium, cement, chlor-alkali, fertiliser, paper, steel, textiles, etc.).
- ✅ **ISO 50001:** International Energy Management System standard, 2018 revision.
- ✅ **Two major COP commitments by India:** **Panchamrit** (2030 NDC) + Net-Zero 2070.
- ✅ **India's per capita electricity consumption:** ~1,200 kWh/yr (vs. global ~3,200 kWh/yr).
- ✅ **Energy import dependency of India:** ~42–45% of total primary energy supply.
- ✅ **Simple Payback Period (SPP):** $C_{\text{investment}} / \Delta C_{\text{annual savings}}$ — bankable ECMs typically have SPP < 3 years.

> **End of Notes — Module 2, Topic: Need | PESFT414 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Types of Energy Audit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Energy Audit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an Energy Audit

An **Energy Audit** is a systematic, structured, and documented process of evaluating the energy usage profile of a facility (building, industrial plant, or system) with the objective of identifying energy conservation opportunities (ECOs), quantifying the energy savings potential, and recommending cost-effective retrofit measures without affecting productivity, safety, or product quality.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> "An Energy Audit is the verification, monitoring, and analysis of the use of energy to identify energy conservation opportunities, evaluate the effectiveness of energy management programs, and ensure accurate energy accounting."

The word **"Audit"** itself comes from the Latin *audire* (to hear). Just as a financial auditor checks the flow of money in a company, an energy auditor checks the flow of energy through a facility — tracking every kilowatt-hour of electricity, cubic metre of fuel, and tonne of steam consumed.

## 1.2 The Three Principal Types of Energy Audit (KTU Standard Classification)

The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India, and the **American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE)** broadly classify energy audits into three categories based on depth, cost, and time investment:

| S.No | Type of Audit | Also Known As | Depth |
|------|---------------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | **Walk-Through Audit** | Preliminary / Mini Audit | Low |
| 2 | **Detailed Energy Audit (DEA)** | Comprehensive Audit | Medium to High |
| 3 | **Investment Grade Audit (IGA)** | Comprehensive / Substantive Audit | Very High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2):**
> Students must be able to **differentiate** between the types of audits based on scope, instruments used, cost of conducting the audit, expected outcomes, and the level of engineering analysis required.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The House Doctor"

Imagine your house has been giving you an unusually high electricity bill every month. You call a doctor (the energy auditor). What happens next depends on how sick your house is:

- **Step 1 (Walk-Through Audit):** The doctor walks through the house, looks at the switches, fans, and bulbs, asks a few questions, and gives you a prescription of "turn off the lights, use LEDs, service your AC." This is **quick, cheap, and surface-level**. The auditor mostly uses visual inspection and past utility bills.
  
- **Step 2 (Detailed Energy Audit):** The doctor takes out his stethoscope, BP machine, and thermometer. He measures the actual current drawn by each appliance with a clamp meter, monitors temperatures with a thermal gun, and logs energy consumption for a week using data loggers. He writes a detailed medical report with measured values.
  
- **Step 3 (Investment Grade Audit):** The doctor orders a full MRI, blood test, and ECG. He installs permanent monitoring equipment for 1–3 months, performs load dispatch studies, models the system in software (e.g., RETScreen, eQuest), and provides a **bankable report** with engineering drawings, exact savings, and ROI calculations suitable for getting a loan from a bank.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:**
> A **Walk-Through Audit** tells you *"where to look."* A **Detailed Audit** tells you *"how much you can save."* An **Investment Grade Audit** tells you *"how to convince the bank to fund it."*

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used (Bolded for Exam Importance)

The following key performance indicators (KPIs) are used throughout all audit types:

- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — *kWh/unit of production* or *MJ/kg of product*
- **Energy Cost-to-Output Ratio** — *₹/kg* or *$/tonne*
- **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** — *dimensionless (typically 0.5 to 0.85)*
- **Power Factor (cos φ)** — *target ≥ 0.95*
- **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** — *years*
- **Return on Investment (ROI)** — *% per annum*
- **Net Present Value (NPV)** — *₹ or $*
- **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** — *% per annum*

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of audit type **directly depends on the complexity, cost, and capital budget** of the facility being audited. A small shop needs only a Walk-Through Audit, while a ₹500-crore cement plant warrants a full Investment Grade Audit.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Type 1: Walk-Through Energy Audit (Preliminary / Mini Audit)

### 2.1.1 Definition
A **Walk-Through Audit** is a low-cost, low-effort, qualitative assessment that involves a single visit (or 1–3 days) to the facility by a qualified energy auditor. The auditor physically walks through the premises, observes equipment, talks to operators, and reviews the past **12 to 36 months of utility bills**.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Methodology
The walk-through audit follows a structured yet rapid sequence:

1. **Pre-Audit Data Collection (Off-Site):**
   - Collect past electricity bills (kWh and kVAh), fuel bills, and steam bills.
   - Plot the **monthly energy consumption trend** to identify seasonal variation.
   - Gather the facility floor plan, single-line diagram (SLD), and equipment list.

2. **On-Site Visual Inspection:**
   - Walk through each department, sub-station, boiler house, and HVAC plant room.
   - Identify **obvious energy wastage** — open windows next to running ACs, leaking steam pipes, dirty filters, oversized motors, idle running equipment, and improper insulation.
   - Interview plant personnel and operators for qualitative feedback.

3. **Quick Quantitative Check:**
   - Spot measurements using a **clamp meter** for current, an **infrared thermometer** for surface temperature, and a **lux meter** for lighting levels.
   - Compare actual operating hours vs. installed capacity to compute **utilisation factor**.

4. **Report Generation:**
   - List **no-cost and low-cost** energy conservation measures (ECMs) that can be implemented immediately.
   - Provide an **indicative savings estimate** (typically accuracy ±30%).
   - Flag areas that need a more detailed investigation in a Stage 2 audit.

### 2.1.3 Tools and Instruments
- Clipboard, camera, flashlight
- Portable clamp meter
- Infrared (IR) thermometer
- Lux meter
- Stopwatch (for operating cycle timing)
- Utility bill spreadsheet

### 2.1.4 Typical Outcomes
- Identification of wasteful practices
- A list of "low-hanging fruit" — instant savings opportunities
- A baseline for energy performance benchmarking
- Recommendation to proceed (or not) to a Detailed Audit

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** In Indian industries audited under the **BEE Perform, Achieve, and Trade (PAT) scheme**, a Walk-Through Audit is the mandatory first step before any Designated Consumer (DC) can claim Energy Savings Certificates (ESCerts).

---

## 2.2 Type 2: Detailed Energy Audit (DEA)

### 2.2.1 Definition
A **Detailed Energy Audit** is a comprehensive, data-driven, quantitative analysis of energy use in a facility, typically lasting **2 to 6 weeks** and costing **5 to 10 times** more than a walk-through audit. It involves the deployment of precision instruments for continuous measurements and the use of engineering calculations to quantify each energy-consuming sub-system.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Methodology
The detailed audit follows the **ASHRAE Level 2 / 3** standard:

1. **Detailed Data Collection (1–2 weeks):**
   - Review of plant design documents, P&ID (Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams), electrical SLDs.
   - Interview maintenance, production, and utilities heads.
   - Gather process flow data, production schedules, and shift-wise load patterns.

2. **Detailed Measurements and Monitoring (1–3 weeks):**
   - Install **portable data loggers** (e.g., power analysers like Fluke 435, thermal cameras like Fluke Ti480).
   - **24 × 7 continuous logging** of:
     - Electrical: Voltage, Current, kW, kVA, kVAR, Power Factor, Harmonics (THD).
     - Thermal: Flue gas temperature, O₂, CO, CO₂ (using flue gas analyser).
     - Fluid: Steam flow, water flow, compressed air flow, chilled water flow.
     - Building: Indoor temperature, humidity, CO₂ levels.

3. **Energy Balance and Modelling:**
   - Construct an **energy balance diagram** — Sankey diagram of energy flow.
   - Compute the energy input vs. useful energy output for each sub-system (boiler, furnace, chiller, compressor).
   - Calculate system-wise efficiency:
     - Boiler efficiency (η_boiler)
     - Compressor specific power (kW per m³/min)
     - Chiller COP (Coefficient of Performance)
     - Motor loading factor

4. **Identification and Quantification of ECMs:**
   - For each sub-system, identify Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs).
   - Quantify annual energy savings (ΔE) in kWh, MJ, or tonnes of oil equivalent (TOE).
   - Estimate implementation cost (C_inv) and compute SPP, NPV, and IRR.

5. **Detailed Audit Report:**
   - Executive summary
   - Baseline energy audit data
   - Sub-system energy balance
   - List of recommended ECMs with savings, costs, and financial analysis
   - Implementation roadmap and monitoring & verification (M&V) plan

### 2.2.3 Tools and Instruments
- 3-phase power quality analysers
- Flue gas analysers (testo 340/350)
- Ultrasonic flow meters
- Thermal imaging camera
- Tachometer, stroboscope
- Light meter, sound level meter
- Lux meter, hygrometer

### 2.2.4 Typical Outcomes
- Quantified energy savings in kWh, ₹, and TOE
- Investment-grade financial analysis of each ECM
- Sankey diagram of energy flows
- M&V (Measurement and Verification) protocol for post-implementation tracking

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Field Application:** A Detailed Energy Audit is the standard requirement for ISO 50001 (Energy Management System) certification, for availing carbon credits, and for projects funded by multilateral agencies (World Bank, ADB, GIZ).

---

## 2.3 Type 3: Investment Grade Audit (IGA)

### 2.3.1 Definition
An **Investment Grade Audit** is the most rigorous and exhaustive form of energy audit, providing **bankable** engineering data with an accuracy of **±5% to ±10%**. It involves long-term monitoring (typically **3 to 12 months**), computer-based simulation, and a complete feasibility study with detailed engineering design.

### 2.3.2 Step-by-Step Methodology

1. **Long-Term Monitoring (3–12 months):**
   - Permanent installation of ultrasonic flow meters, BTU meters, smart electricity meters with data loggers.
   - Continuous monitoring of all major energy streams.

2. **Process Simulation and Modelling:**
   - Use software tools: **eQuest / EnergyPlus** (buildings), **ASPEN Plus / HYSYS** (process plants), **RETScreen** (renewables), **HOMER** (microgrids).
   - Calibrate the model with measured data and run "what-if" scenarios.

3. **Detailed Engineering Design:**
   - For each recommended ECM, prepare:
     - P&ID modifications
     - Equipment specifications (vendor quotes)
     - Single-line diagram revisions
     - Civil and structural drawings (if applicable)

4. **Bankable Financial Analysis:**
   - Compute NPV using a discount rate of **10% to 15%** (typical for Indian industries).
   - Compute IRR and payback with sensitivity analysis (best case, worst case, most likely).
   - Risk assessment and M&V plan as per **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)**.

5. **Final Investment Grade Report:**
   - Acts as the basis for **lending decisions** by banks and financial institutions.
   - Used for **ESCO (Energy Service Company)** contracts and **Power Purchase Agreements (PPAs)**.

### 2.3.3 Tools and Instruments
- All instruments of the Detailed Audit, **plus:**
- Permanent SCADA / BMS data acquisition
- BTU meters, steam flow computers
- Calibrated laboratory-grade equipment

> [!WARNING]
> **Do Not Confuse:** An IGA is **NOT** a separate activity; it is essentially a Detailed Audit carried to a higher level of precision and documentation. The terms are sometimes used interchangeably in textbooks, but for KTU exams, treat them as **levels of the same spectrum**.

---

## 2.4 Comparative Analysis Matrix

| Parameter | Walk-Through Audit | Detailed Audit | Investment Grade Audit |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| **Also called** | Preliminary / Mini | Comprehensive | Substantive / Bankable |
| **Duration** | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks | 3–12 months |
| **Cost (relative)** | Lowest (1×) | Medium (5–10×) | Highest (20–50×) |
| **Accuracy of savings estimate** | ±30% | ±10% to ±15% | ±5% to ±10% |
| **Instruments used** | Basic (clamp meter, IR thermometer) | Intermediate (power analyser, flue gas analyser) | Advanced (permanent loggers, simulation software) |
| **Suitable for** | Small commercial buildings, residential | Medium to large industries, hospitals | Large plants, ESCO projects, PPP |
| **Output** | List of low-cost ECMs | Quantified ECMs with payback | Bankable feasibility report |
| **Standards followed** | ASHRAE Level 1 | ASHRAE Level 2 / 3 | IPMVP, M&V protocols |
| **Performed by** | Energy Manager (BEE certified) | Energy Auditor (BEE certified) | Accredited Energy Auditor (AEA) |

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Sl. No | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|--------|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P_{output}}$ | $\text{kWh} \div \text{unit}$ or $\text{MJ} \div \text{kg}$ | Lower is better |
| 2 | Boiler Efficiency (Direct Method) | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_{steam} \times (h_{steam} - h_{feedwater})}{m_{fuel} \times GCV_{fuel}} \times 100$ | % | Direct Method |
| 3 | Boiler Efficiency (Indirect Method) | $\eta_{boiler} = 100 - (L_{DG} + L_{H2O} + L_{CO} + L_{rad} + L_{unburnt})$ | % | Sum of all losses |
| 4 | Motor Loading Factor | $LF = \dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{rated}} \times 100$ | % | Best range: 75–85% |
| 5 | Chiller COP | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | dimensionless | Typical: 3.0 to 5.5 |
| 6 | Compressor Specific Power | $SP = \dfrac{P_{input}}{F_{free\,air}}$ | $\text{kW} \div (\text{m}^{3}\!\mid\!\text{min})$ | Best: ≤ 6.5 |
| 7 | Simple Payback Period | $SPP = \dfrac{C_{investment}}{\Delta S_{annual}}$ | years | Most used in audits |
| 8 | Annual Energy Cost Savings | $\Delta S_{annual} = \Delta E \times C_{unit}$ | ₹ or $ per year | |
| 9 | Net Present Value | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\Delta S_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_{inv}$ | ₹ or $ | $i$ = discount rate |
| 10 | Return on Investment | $ROI = \dfrac{\Delta S_{annual}}{C_{inv}} \times 100$ | % per year | |
| 11 | Plant Load Factor | $PLF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{rated} \times T_{hours}} \times 100$ | % | Over a year |
| 12 | Power Factor Correction | $Q_{c} = P (\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2)$ | kVAR | $\phi_1$ original, $\phi_2$ target |
| 13 | Lighting Energy Saving | $\Delta E_{light} = n \times (W_{old} - W_{new}) \times h \div 1000$ | kWh/year | $n$ = number of fixtures |
| 14 | Air-Side Economiser Saving | $\Delta E_{econ} = 1.08 \times \dot{V} \times \Delta T \times h \div 1000$ | kWh/year | 1.08 = air heat factor (BTU/h·CFM·°F) |
| 15 | Heat Transfer (Q) | $Q = U \times A \times \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | W or kW | $U$ overall, $A$ area, $\Delta T$ LMTD |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table Care:** In the formula $Q_{c} = P (\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2)$, the absolute-value type bars are written as `\mid`-style plain text — this is intentional to keep the KTU 2024 renderer compatible.

---

## 2.6 The Audit-Why-How Connection

The **"Why"** of an energy audit is the **3-E Triad**:
- **Energy Savings** (lower kWh, MJ, TOE)
- **Economic Savings** (lower ₹/$)
- **Environmental Savings** (lower CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ emissions)

The **"How"** is the **3-M Methodology**:
- **Measure** → using instruments
- **Monitor** → using data loggers
- **Manage** → using M&V and continuous improvement (PDCA cycle)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Simple Payback Period (SPP)

The Simple Payback Period is the time required for the cumulative energy cost savings of an Energy Conservation Measure (ECM) to equal the initial capital investment.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $C_{inv}$ = Initial capital investment for the ECM in ₹
- $\Delta S_{annual}$ = Net annual monetary savings in ₹ per year
- Assume savings are constant every year (no escalation)

In **Year 1**, the cumulative savings = $\Delta S_{annual}$

In **Year 2**, the cumulative savings = $2 \times \Delta S_{annual}$

In general, in **Year $n$**, the cumulative savings = $n \times \Delta S_{annual}$

The payback occurs when the cumulative savings equal the investment:

$$
C_{inv} = n_{payback} \times \Delta S_{annual}
$$

Solving for $n_{payback}$:

$$
n_{payback} = \dfrac{C_{inv}}{\Delta S_{annual}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,SPP = \dfrac{C_{inv}}{\Delta S_{annual}}\,}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A factory replaces 100 incandescent bulbs (60 W each) with LED bulbs (12 W each). The bulbs operate 12 hours per day, 300 days per year. Electricity cost is ₹7 per kWh. LED fixture cost is ₹350 each, against ₹30 for the incandescent. Find the SPP.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Power saving per bulb:
$$
\Delta W = 60\,\text{W} - 12\,\text{W} = 48\,\text{W} = 0.048\,\text{kW}
$$

Step 2 — Annual operating hours:
$$
H = 12 \times 300 = 3600\,\text{h/year}
$$

Step 3 — Annual energy savings (kWh):
$$
\Delta E = 0.048 \times 3600 \times 100 = 17280\,\text{kWh/year}
$$

Step 4 — Annual cost savings (₹):
$$
\Delta S_{annual} = 17280 \times 7 = 120960\,\text{₹/year}
$$

Step 5 — Net incremental investment (LED costs more):
$$
C_{inv} = 100 \times (350 - 30) = 100 \times 320 = 32000\,\text{₹}
$$

Step 6 — Simple Payback Period:
$$
SPP = \dfrac{32000}{120960} = 0.2646\,\text{year}
$$

Convert to months:
$$
SPP = 0.2646 \times 12 \approx 3.18\,\text{months}
$$

**Interpretation:** The investment is recovered in approximately **3.2 months** — an excellent ECM.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Net Present Value (NPV)

When the time value of money is considered, savings in future years are *less* valuable than savings today. The NPV discounts future cash flows back to "Year 0" using a discount rate $i$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

If the annual savings $\Delta S_{annual}$ are constant and the project life is $n$ years, then the present value of all future savings is:

$$
PV_{savings} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{\Delta S_{annual}}{(1+i)^t}
$$

This is a geometric series with first term $a = \dfrac{\Delta S_{annual}}{1+i}$ and common ratio $r = \dfrac{1}{1+i}$.

The sum of a geometric series of $n$ terms is:

$$
S_n = a \times \dfrac{1 - r^{n}}{1 - r}
$$

Substituting:

$$
S_n = \dfrac{\Delta S_{annual}}{1+i} \times \dfrac{1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^{n}}{1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i}}
$$

Simplifying the denominator:

$$
1 - \dfrac{1}{1+i} = \dfrac{1+i-1}{1+i} = \dfrac{i}{1+i}
$$

Therefore:

$$
S_n = \dfrac{\Delta S_{annual}}{1+i} \times \dfrac{1+i}{i} \times \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{1}{1+i}\right)^{n}\right]
$$

The $(1+i)$ terms cancel:

$$
S_n = \dfrac{\Delta S_{annual}}{i} \times \left[1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^{n}}\right]
$$

The Net Present Value subtracts the initial investment:

$$
\boxed{\,NPV = \dfrac{\Delta S_{annual}}{i} \times \left[1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^{n}}\right] - C_{inv}\,}
$$

### Decision Rule
- $NPV > 0$ → ECM is **financially viable**, accept.
- $NPV < 0$ → ECM is **not viable**, reject.
- $NPV = 0$ → ECM is at the **break-even point**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Boiler Efficiency by Direct Method

The direct method (also called the "input-output method") measures the energy absorbed by steam/water relative to the energy released by fuel combustion.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Energy absorbed by steam per kg of fuel burned:

$$
Q_{out} = m_{s} \times (h_{s} - h_{fw})
$$

where:
- $m_{s}$ = mass of steam generated per kg of fuel (kg steam / kg fuel)
- $h_{s}$ = enthalpy of steam (kJ/kg)
- $h_{fw}$ = enthalpy of feedwater (kJ/kg)

Energy released by fuel per kg:

$$
Q_{in} = GCV_{fuel}
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{m_{s} \times (h_{s} - h_{fw})}{GCV_{fuel}} \times 100\,}
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A packaged boiler generates 8 kg of steam per kg of oil. Steam enthalpy is 2776 kJ/kg, feedwater enthalpy is 419 kJ/kg. GCV of oil is 10,200 kcal/kg. Find the boiler efficiency.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Numerator (energy absorbed in kcal/kg, dividing by 4.184):
$$
m_{s} \times (h_{s} - h_{fw}) = 8 \times (2776 - 419) = 8 \times 2357 = 18856\,\text{kJ/kg fuel}
$$

Step 2 — Convert to kcal/kg:
$$
18856 \div 4.184 = 4506.7\,\text{kcal/kg}
$$

Step 3 — Efficiency:
$$
\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{4506.7}{10200} \times 100 = 44.18\,\%
$$

**Interpretation:** This is a typical value for an **older, unmaintained boiler**. Modern package boilers achieve 80–88%. The Detailed Audit would recommend burner tuning, soot blowing, and economiser installation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Audit Type vs. Decision Variable

| Decision Variable | Walk-Through | Detailed | IGA |
|-------------------|--------------|----------|-----|
| Budget for ECM (₹) | < 1 lakh | 1 lakh – 50 lakhs | > 50 lakhs |
| Project life (years) | 1–3 | 5–10 | 10–25 |
| Required accuracy of savings | ±30% | ±15% | ±5% |
| M&V effort (months) | 0 | 1–3 | 6–12 |
| Document length (pages) | 10–20 | 50–150 | 200–500 |
| Vendor engagement | No | Optional | Mandatory |
| Simulation required | No | Sometimes | Always |

---

## 3.5 The "Equation-Free" Logical Proof of Why Each Type Exists

1. **Cost of Audit ∝ Depth of Measurement.**
   - More precision requires costlier instruments, longer time, and more skilled personnel.

2. **Value of ECM ∝ Audit Depth.**
   - A Walk-Through may miss ECMs worth crores. A Detailed Audit finds them.

3. **Optimal Audit Depth = Marginal Cost of Audit = Marginal Value of ECMs found.**
   - This is the same as the economic optimum principle in microeconomics.

4. **Decision Rule for an Auditor:**
   - If the expected ECM value is **< 10× audit cost**, choose Walk-Through.
   - If the expected ECM value is **10× to 100× audit cost**, choose Detailed.
   - If the expected ECM value is **> 100× audit cost**, choose IGA.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Q: List and briefly explain any three types of energy audits. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three principal types of energy audits are:

1. **Walk-Through (Preliminary) Audit:** A quick, low-cost, qualitative assessment lasting 1–3 days. The auditor visually inspects the facility, reviews 12–36 months of utility bills, and identifies obvious energy wastage using basic instruments like a clamp meter and IR thermometer. Output is a list of no-cost and low-cost ECMs with ±30% accuracy.

2. **Detailed Energy Audit (DEA):** A comprehensive, quantitative study lasting 2–6 weeks. It involves 24×7 monitoring using power quality analysers, flue gas analysers, and thermal cameras, followed by energy balance modelling and detailed financial analysis (SPP, NPV, IRR). Accuracy is ±10–15%.

3. **Investment Grade Audit (IGA):** The most rigorous audit, lasting 3–12 months, with permanent instrumentation, process simulation, and a bankable report suitable for financing by banks or ESCO contracts. Accuracy is ±5–10%.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
**Q: Differentiate between a Walk-Through Audit and a Detailed Energy Audit. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Walk-Through Audit | Detailed Energy Audit |
|---------|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Duration** | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks |
| **Depth** | Qualitative / Visual | Quantitative / Measured |
| **Instruments** | Clamp meter, IR thermometer, lux meter | Power analyser, flue gas analyser, thermal camera, ultrasonic flow meter |
| **Outcome** | List of low-cost ECMs | Quantified ECMs with engineering and financial analysis |
| **Accuracy of savings** | ±30% | ±10–15% |
| **Cost** | Low (1×) | Medium (5–10×) |
| **Suitable for** | Small commercial buildings, residential | Medium to large industrial plants |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with an internal choice. Each sub-part (a) and (b) is typically 7 marks. Both sub-parts should be attempted to attempt choice optimally. A complete answer requires **diagrams, formulas, numerical examples, and a concluding remark**.

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]`**

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the three types of energy audits with their objectives, methodology, instruments used, and typical outcomes. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A 50 HP motor (efficiency 88%) is being replaced by a 50 HP IE4 premium efficiency motor (efficiency 94%). The motor operates 5,000 hours per year. Electricity cost is ₹6.50/kWh. Find: (i) the annual energy savings in kWh, (ii) the annual cost savings in ₹, and (iii) the simple payback period if the new motor costs ₹1,50,000 and the old motor's salvage value is ₹40,000. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

The three types are as follows (presented in a structured table format suitable for KTU valuation):

| Parameter | Walk-Through Audit | Detailed Energy Audit | Investment Grade Audit |
|-----------|--------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| **Objective** | Quick identification of wasteful practices | Quantified savings and detailed financial analysis | Bankable feasibility for funding |
| **Duration** | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks | 3–12 months |
| **Instruments** | Clamp meter, IR thermometer, lux meter | Power analyser, flue gas analyser, thermal camera, flow meter | Permanent SCADA, BTU meters, simulation software |
| **Accuracy** | ±30% | ±10–15% | ±5–10% |
| **Typical outcome** | List of no-cost/low-cost ECMs | Quantified ECMs with SPP/NPV/IRR | Bankable report with engineering design and M&V plan |
| **Cost (relative)** | 1× | 5–10× | 20–50× |
| **Standards** | ASHRAE Level 1 | ASHRAE Level 2/3, BEE PAT | IPMVP, ISO 50015 |

**Conclusion:** The selection of audit type is a function of facility size, expected ECM value, and capital availability. Most KTU-level problems assume a **Detailed Audit** as the default study.

---

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Motor rating $P = 50\,\text{HP} = 50 \times 0.746 = 37.3\,\text{kW}$
- Old motor efficiency $\eta_1 = 88\% = 0.88$
- New motor efficiency $\eta_2 = 94\% = 0.94$
- Annual operating hours $H = 5000\,\text{h/year}$
- Electricity cost $C_e = ₹6.50 \div \text{kWh}$
- Cost of new motor $C_{new} = ₹1,50,000$
- Salvage of old motor $S_{old} = ₹40,000$

**Step 1 — Mechanical Output (Shaft Power):**

$$
P_{shaft} = 50 \times 0.746 = 37.3\,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Input Power of Old Motor:**

$$
P_{in,\,old} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_1} = \dfrac{37.3}{0.88} = 42.39\,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Input Power of New Motor:**

$$
P_{in,\,new} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_2} = \dfrac{37.3}{0.94} = 39.68\,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Power Saving:**

$$
\Delta P = 42.39 - 39.68 = 2.71\,\text{kW}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual Energy Savings:**

$$
\Delta E = 2.71 \times 5000 = 13550\,\text{kWh/year}
$$

**Step 6 — Annual Cost Savings:**

$$
\Delta S_{annual} = 13550 \times 6.50 = ₹88,075\,\text{per year}
$$

**Step 7 — Net Investment (Accounting for Salvage):**

$$
C_{inv,\,net} = C_{new} - S_{old} = 150000 - 40000 = ₹1,10,000
$$

**Step 8 — Simple Payback Period:**

$$
SPP = \dfrac{110000}{88075} = 1.249\,\text{year}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta E = 13550\,\text{kWh/year},\quad \Delta S = ₹88,075/\text{year},\quad SPP = 1.25\,\text{years}\,}
$$

**Conclusion:** The premium-efficiency motor pays back in approximately **15 months** — a highly recommended ECM for any continuous-duty industrial motor.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Sample Paper 2024]`**

**Q: (a)** Describe the step-by-step methodology of conducting a Detailed Energy Audit in an industrial facility. List the instruments used at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A boiler consumes 200 kg of furnace oil per hour. GCV of oil = 10,200 kcal/kg. The boiler generates 1500 kg of steam per hour at 7 bar (enthalpy 2776 kJ/kg). Feedwater enters at 80°C (enthalpy 335 kJ/kg). Calculate: (i) the boiler efficiency by direct method, and (ii) the annual fuel cost savings if the boiler efficiency is improved from 78% to 84% by installing an economiser. Annual operating hours = 6000. Furnace oil cost = ₹70/kg. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

The Detailed Energy Audit methodology is divided into **7 sequential stages**:

1. **Pre-Audit Data Collection (Off-Site):** Gather past 12–36 months of electricity, fuel, and steam bills. Plot the monthly energy consumption trend. Obtain plant layout, electrical single-line diagram (SLD), P&ID, and equipment list. Estimate **SEC baseline**.

2. **Kick-Off Meeting and Walk-Through Survey:** Introduce the audit team to plant personnel. Walk through each section to identify the major energy-consuming areas. Finalise the audit scope and logistics.

3. **Detailed Measurements and Monitoring (1–3 weeks):** Install precision instruments:
   - **3-phase Power Quality Analyser** (e.g., Fluke 435) at the main incomer and sub-feeders to record kW, kVA, kVAR, PF, THD.
   - **Flue Gas Analyser** (e.g., testo 340) at the boiler chimney to record O₂, CO₂, CO, SO₂, NOₓ, and flue gas temperature.
   - **Ultrasonic Flow Meters** on steam, water, compressed air, and chilled water lines.
   - **Thermal Imaging Camera** to detect hot spots on furnaces, motors, and steam traps.
   - **Lux Meter and Hygrometer** for lighting and HVAC measurements.

4. **Data Analysis and Energy Balance:** Tabulate all measured data and construct a **Sankey diagram** of energy flow. Compute system-wise efficiency:
   - Boiler: Direct/Indirect method
   - Compressor: Specific power
   - Chiller: COP
   - Motor: Loading factor

5. **Identification of Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs):** For each subsystem, list feasible ECMs such as:
   - Replacement of inefficient motors with IE3/IE4
   - VFD installation on pumps and fans
   - LED retrofit in lighting
   - Boiler economiser / air preheater
   - Compressor leak reduction
   - Waste heat recovery

6. **Financial Analysis of ECMs:** For each ECM, compute:
   - **Energy savings** in kWh, MJ, TOE
   - **Cost savings** in ₹
   - **Investment cost**
   - **SPP, NPV, IRR**

7. **Detailed Audit Report and Implementation Roadmap:** Document all findings, prioritise ECMs based on financial attractiveness, and propose a phased implementation plan. Include an **M&V (Measurement and Verification) protocol** as per IPMVP for tracking post-implementation savings.

---

#### Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- Oil consumption $m_{fuel} = 200\,\text{kg/h}$
- GCV = $10200\,\text{kcal/kg} = 10200 \times 4.184 = 42677\,\text{kJ/kg}$
- Steam generated $m_{s} = 1500\,\text{kg/h}$
- Steam enthalpy $h_{s} = 2776\,\text{kJ/kg}$
- Feedwater enthalpy $h_{fw} = 335\,\text{kJ/kg}$ (corresponding to 80°C)
- Initial efficiency $\eta_1 = 78\% = 0.78$
- Improved efficiency $\eta_2 = 84\% = 0.84$
- Annual hours $H = 6000$
- Oil cost = ₹70/kg

**Step 1 — Energy Released by Fuel per Hour:**

$$
Q_{in} = m_{fuel} \times GCV = 200 \times 42677 = 8,535,400\,\text{kJ/h}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy Absorbed by Steam per Hour:**

$$
Q_{out} = m_{s} \times (h_{s} - h_{fw}) = 1500 \times (2776 - 335) = 1500 \times 2441 = 3,661,500\,\text{kJ/h}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual (Initial) Boiler Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{actual} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100 = \dfrac{3661500}{8535400} \times 100 = 42.90\,\%
$$

This is very low, indicating the boiler is poorly maintained. The "78% / 84%" in the problem refers to the **target** efficiencies before and after the economiser retrofit (i.e., the manufacturer's rated efficiency for the retrofit scenario).

**Step 4 — Fuel Consumption at Initial Efficiency (78%):**

$$
m_{fuel,1} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{\eta_1 \times GCV} = \dfrac{3661500}{0.78 \times 42677} = \dfrac{3661500}{33288.06} = 110.00\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 5 — Fuel Consumption at Improved Efficiency (84%):**

$$
m_{fuel,2} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{\eta_2 \times GCV} = \dfrac{3661500}{0.84 \times 42677} = \dfrac{3661500}{35848.68} = 102.13\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 6 — Fuel Saving per Hour:**

$$
\Delta m = 110.00 - 102.13 = 7.87\,\text{kg/h}
$$

**Step 7 — Annual Fuel Saving:**

$$
\Delta m_{annual} = 7.87 \times 6000 = 47,220\,\text{kg/year} = 47.22\,\text{tonnes/year}
$$

**Step 8 — Annual Cost Saving:**

$$
\Delta S = 47220 \times 70 = ₹33,05,400\,\text{per year}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\eta_{boiler} = 42.9\%\,\text{(actual, as measured)},\quad \Delta S = ₹33.05\,\text{lakhs/year}\,}
$$

**Conclusion:** Installing the economiser saves the plant **₹33.05 lakhs per year** in fuel cost — a high-value ECM suitable for immediate implementation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the Three Audit Types:** Students often write "preliminary" and "walk-through" as separate audits. They are the **same** — examiners deduct marks for poor terminology.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Final Answer:** "SPP = 1.25" without "years" loses ½ mark. Always write **"1.25 years"** or **"15 months"**.
>
> 3. **Ignoring Salvage Value:** In the motor-replacement problem, students often use the full motor cost (₹1.5 lakh) as the investment. The **net investment** is cost minus salvage of the old motor.
>
> 4. **Mixing up HP to kW:** 1 HP = 0.746 kW, **not** 0.75 kW. Use 0.746 for accuracy.
>
> 5. **Writing Boiler Efficiency by Direct Method without converting units:** GCV is in kcal/kg, but enthalpies are in kJ/kg. Either convert enthalpies to kcal/kg (divide by 4.184) or GCV to kJ/kg (multiply by 4.184). Examiners expect this conversion step.
>
> 6. **Not including a Sankey diagram in Detailed Audit answers:** Even a hand-drawn Sankey sketch of energy flow in a boiler or process plant earns easy 1–2 marks.
>
> 7. **Inverting the Audit-Why-How logic:** Don't write "we do an audit to save money." Write "we do an audit to identify, quantify, and verify energy conservation opportunities that yield monetary, energy, and environmental savings."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **An energy audit** is a systematic study to identify and quantify energy conservation opportunities (ECOs) in a facility.
- [x] **Three main types:** Walk-Through, Detailed, and Investment Grade.
- [x] **Walk-Through Audit:** 1–3 days, qualitative, low cost, ±30% accuracy, basic instruments.
- [x] **Detailed Energy Audit:** 2–6 weeks, quantitative, medium cost, ±10–15% accuracy, advanced instruments.
- [x] **Investment Grade Audit:** 3–12 months, bankable, high cost, ±5–10% accuracy, simulation + permanent monitoring.
- [x] **Walk-Through = "House Doctor's first visit"** analogy.
- [x] **Detailed Audit = "House Doctor's full check-up with instruments"** analogy.
- [x] **IGA = "House Doctor's full medical panel + MRI"** analogy.
- [x] **Simple Payback Period (SPP):** $SPP = C_{inv} \div \Delta S_{annual}$ — measure in years.
- [x] **Net Present Value (NPV):** $NPV = \dfrac{\Delta S}{i} \left[1 - \dfrac{1}{(1+i)^{n}}\right] - C_{inv}$.
- [x] **Boiler Efficiency (Direct Method):** $\eta = \dfrac{m_{s} (h_{s} - h_{fw})}{GCV_{fuel}} \times 100$.
- [x] **Chiller COP:** $COP = Q_{cooling} \div W_{input}$ (good COP ≥ 4.0).
- [x] **Compressor Specific Power:** $SP = P \div F_{free\,air}$ (target ≤ 6.5 kW/(m³·min⁻¹)).
- [x] **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Lower is better — key KPI for any audit.
- [x] **Audit selection logic:** Audit Cost × 10 = Minimum ECM value to justify the audit.
- [x] **Sankey diagram** is a must-draw visual for Detailed Audit answers.
- [x] **M&V Protocol** follows **IPMVP** (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol).
- [x] **ASHRAE Levels:** Level 1 = Walk-Through, Level 2/3 = Detailed, IGA = Level 3+.
- [x] **BEE Perform, Achieve, Trade (PAT) scheme** mandates Detailed Audits for Designated Consumers.
- [x] **One HP = 0.746 kW** — remember, not 0.75.
- [x] **Always include units** in the final numerical answer.
- [x] **Report, ECMs, and M&V plan** are the three deliverables of any audit.

---

**End of Module 2 — Types of Energy Audit Notes**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 Aligned) | PESFT414 — Energy Management and Conservation*

topics.title: Energy Management (Audit) Approach - Understanding Energy Costs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Management (Audit) Approach - Understanding Energy Costs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Energy Management Approach – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Management (Audit) Approach** is a systematic, structured, and documented methodology used to assess, measure, monitor, and optimize energy use within an organization. It combines technical analysis of energy-consuming systems with financial evaluation to identify, prioritize, and implement energy conservation opportunities (ECOs).

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, the **Energy Management Approach** progresses through distinct phases — from preliminary walk-through assessments to detailed investment-grade audits — each with increasing levels of instrumentation, data accuracy, and engineering rigor.

---

## 1.2 Types of Energy Audits (KTU Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> The **American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE)** classifies audits into three progressive levels. KTU textbooks universally adopt this classification.

### **Level 1 – Walk-Through Audit (Preliminary Audit)**
- **Scope:** Qualitative, low-cost screening.
- **Instruments Used:** Visual inspection, utility bill review, nameplate data, simple hand-held meters.
- **Output:** List of low-cost / no-cost energy conservation measures (ECMs) and identification of major energy-consuming areas.
- **Duration:** Typically 1–3 days for a medium facility.

### **Level 2 – Detailed Energy Audit (General Audit)**
- **Scope:** Quantitative measurement-based analysis.
- **Instruments Used:** Power analyzers, flue gas analyzers, lux meters, thermometers, ultrasonic flow meters, data loggers.
- **Output:** Energy balances, calibrated model of energy use, ECMs with engineering estimates of savings.
- **Duration:** Typically 2–6 weeks.

### **Level 3 – Investment-Grade Audit (Comprehensive / Diagnostic Audit)**
- **Scope:** High-precision, project-finance-ready analysis.
- **Instruments Used:** Long-term data logging, sub-metering, computer simulation (e.g., energy modeling software), dynamic simulation.
- **Output:** Rigorous savings calculations with M&V (Measurement & Verification) protocols per **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)**.
- **Duration:** Several months; used before capital-intensive projects.

---

## 1.3 Understanding Energy Costs – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Cost** is the total monetary expenditure incurred by a facility or organization for purchasing and consuming various forms of energy (electricity, fuel oil, natural gas, LPG, coal, biomass, etc.) over a defined billing period.

Energy cost analysis is **not just about the price per unit (₹/kWh or ₹/litre)**. It involves decomposing the utility bill into multiple cost components — demand charges, energy charges, fuel adjustment, taxes, time-of-use premiums, and penalties — to understand **where the money is actually going** and **how consumption patterns affect the bill**.

---

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy – "The Restaurant Bill"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box – Making It Memorable**

Imagine you walk into a restaurant and see a bill of ₹2,000. You want to reduce it next month.

- **Naïve Approach (Most Students):** "I'll just eat less food (reduce kWh)." → **Variable Cost focus**
- **Smart Approach (Energy Manager):** "Let me first inspect the bill structure — is there a fixed ₹500 cover charge (Fixed/Demand Charge)? Are some items charged extra on weekends (Time-of-Use premium)? Did the chef overcook (Inefficiency / Waste)?"

Just like analyzing a restaurant bill requires understanding **what each line item represents**, analyzing energy bills requires decomposing them into **Fixed Charges, Variable (Energy) Charges, Demand Charges, Fuel Cost Adjustments, Taxes, and Time-of-Use Components**.

The **Energy Audit Approach** is essentially the equivalent of hiring a "food inspector" who walks through the kitchen (your plant), checks the recipes (process efficiency), and identifies wasted ingredients (energy waste) before suggesting changes.

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | Energy used per unit of production/output (e.g., kWh/ton, MJ/kg) |
| **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** | A metric used to track energy performance over time |
| **Baseline** | Reference energy consumption against which savings are measured |
| **ECM** | Energy Conservation Measure – an action that reduces energy use |
| **ESCO** | Energy Service Company – a firm delivering energy-saving projects |
| **M&V** | Measurement & Verification – process of confirming savings |
| **IPMVP** | International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol |
| **kVA** | Apparent power; basis for demand charges |
| **kWh** | Active energy; basis for energy charges |
| **TOU** | Time-of-Use tariff |

---

## 1.6 Why This Topic Matters (KTU Exam Relevance)

> [!TIP]
> Module 2 of PESFT414 explicitly tests whether students can **decompose utility bills**, **classify audits**, and **link technical losses to monetary costs**. Expect direct 3-mark questions on audit types and 7–14 mark numerical questions on energy cost calculations, payback period, and Net Present Value (NPV).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Phase Energy Management Approach

### **Phase 1 – Pre-Audit Phase (Planning & Data Collection)**

> [!NOTE]
> This phase sets the foundation. **Garbage In → Garbage Out (GIGO)** applies brutally to energy audits.

**Activities:**
1. Define audit scope, boundaries, and objectives.
2. Collect at least **24–36 months** of historical utility bills.
3. Gather operational data — production volumes, shift timings, occupancy.
4. Identify major energy-consuming equipment (motor list, boiler list, HVAC inventory).
5. Form an energy audit team (electrical, mechanical, process, instrumentation engineers).

**Why this matters:** Without accurate baseline data, any ECM proposed will have questionable savings.

---

### **Phase 2 – Audit Phase (Field Measurements & Analysis)**

**Activities:**
1. Conduct walk-through surveys (Level 1).
2. Install portable instruments for spot measurements (Level 2).
3. Develop energy balance diagrams (Sankey-style flow charts).
4. Calculate Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and compare with benchmarks.
5. Identify all ECMs with engineering calculations of savings.

---

### **Phase 3 – Post-Audit Phase (Implementation & Verification)**

**Activities:**
1. Rank ECMs using financial metrics (Payback, NPV, IRR).
2. Prepare detailed techno-economic feasibility report.
3. Implement ECMs (in-house or via ESCO).
4. Apply **M&V (Measurement & Verification)** protocols (IPMVP Options A, B, C, D).
5. Continuous monitoring and improvement (PDCA cycle).

---

## 2.2 Components of an Electricity Bill (KTU High-Yield Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An industrial electricity bill in India (and globally) generally has the following components. KTU frequently asks students to **decompose a given bill**.

### **Component 1 – Fixed Charges (₹/kVA or ₹/kW of contracted demand)**
- Independent of actual energy consumption.
- Based on **contracted demand** (sanctioned load) or **maximum recorded demand (MD)** — whichever is higher.
- Fixed cost for the utility to maintain infrastructure (transformers, lines).

### **Component 2 – Energy Charges (₹/kWh)**
- Variable component proportional to actual energy consumed (kWh).
- Often tiered: higher consumption → higher per-unit rate (slab tariff).

### **Component 3 – Demand Charges (₹/kVA)**
- Based on **maximum kVA demand** recorded during the billing period (typically a 15-minute or 30-minute integration window).
- **Penalty Clause:** If MD > contracted demand, billed at 1.5× or 2× the normal rate for the excess.

### **Component 4 – Fuel Adjustment Charge (FAC) / Power Factor Penalty**
- Variable adjustment based on fuel price fluctuations (passed through to consumer).
- Power factor penalty if **PF < 0.90** (typical Indian norm).
- Reactive energy charges when PF drops below threshold.

### **Component 5 – Time-of-Use (TOU) Differential**
- Peak hours (e.g., 6 PM–10 PM): higher rate (₹ 9–12/kWh).
- Off-peak hours (e.g., 10 PM–6 AM): lower rate (₹ 3–5/kWh).
- Shoulder hours: mid-rate.

### **Component 6 – Taxes, Duties, Surcharges**
- Electricity duty, GST, municipal taxes, wheeling charges.

---

## 2.3 Energy Cost Reduction Strategies (Audit-Driven)

| Strategy | Mechanism | Typical Savings |
|----------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Load Factor Improvement** | Stagger equipment operation to flatten demand curve | 5–15% on demand charge |
| **Power Factor Correction** | Install capacitors to reduce kVA demand | 5–25% on demand charge |
| **Peak Shaving** | Shift loads to off-peak hours | 10–30% on TOU bills |
| **Equipment Efficiency Upgrade** | Replace motors/pumps with IE3/IE4 class | 3–8% on energy |
| **Waste Heat Recovery** | Capture exhaust heat for pre-heating | 10–30% on thermal fuel |
| **Process Optimization** | Fine-tune set-points, control logic | 5–20% |
| **Lighting Retrofit** | Replace with LEDs | 50–80% on lighting kWh |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Use this table as your last-day revision sheet for all numerical problems from this topic.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P_{output}}$ | kWh/unit, MJ/kg, toe/ton |
| 2 | **Energy Cost Per Unit (₹/unit)** | $C_u = \dfrac{Total\ Bill\ (₹)}{Total\ kWh\ consumed}$ | ₹/kWh |
| 3 | **Annual Energy Cost** | $AEC = E_{annual} \times C_u \times 8760$ correction | ₹/year |
| 4 | **Maximum Demand (kVA)** | $MD_{kVA} = \dfrac{kW}{PF}$ | kVA |
| 5 | **Demand Charge** | $C_D = MD_{billed} \times Rate_{D}$ | ₹/month |
| 6 | **Energy Charge** | $C_E = kWh_{consumed} \times Rate_{E}$ | ₹/month |
| 7 | **Power Factor Correction** | $Q_C = kW \times (\tan \phi_1 - \tan \phi_2)$ | kVAr |
| 8 | **Apparent Power Reduction** | $S_{new} = \dfrac{kW}{PF_{new}}$ | kVA |
| 9 | **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** | $SPP = \dfrac{Initial\ Investment}{Annual\ Savings}$ | Years |
| 10 | **Net Present Value (NPV)** | $NPV = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | ₹ |
| 11 | **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)** | $0 = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+IRR)^t}$ | % |
| 12 | **Load Factor** | $LF = \dfrac{Average\ Load}{Peak\ Load}$ | Decimal (0–1) |
| 13 | **Capacity Utilization Factor** | $CUF = \dfrac{Actual\ Output}{Maximum\ Possible\ Output}$ | Decimal |
| 14 | **Annual Energy (from load)** | $E = kW_{avg} \times 8760 \times LF$ | kWh/year |
| 15 | **CO₂ Emissions from Energy** | $CO_2 = E_{kWh} \times EF_{grid}$ | kgCO₂ |

> **Boundary Conditions / Standard Values (Memorize):**
> - **Grid Emission Factor (India, 2023 CEA average):** $EF \approx 0.716\ \text{kgCO}_2/\text{kWh}$
> - **Indian Standard PF Threshold:** $0.90\ \text{lag}$ minimum
> - **Demand Integration Window:** 15 minutes or 30 minutes (utility-specific)
> - **Hours per Year:** $8760$

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of This Knowledge

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Energy Managers Care About Cost Decomposition:**

A naïve plant manager may complain "electricity is expensive" and ask for tariff negotiation. An **energy auditor** trained in KTU methodology will instead:
1. Discover that **40% of the bill is demand charge** → fix load scheduling.
2. Discover **PF penalty = ₹2 lakh/year** → install capacitor bank (₹50,000 cost, payback = 3 months).
3. Discover **off-peak TOU differential** → reschedule batch processes.
4. Discover **lighting alone is 18% of kWh** → LED retrofit.

Each of these actions is **directly enabled** by understanding energy cost structure — not by negotiating the per-unit rate.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Solved Examples

## 3.1 Example 1 – Decomposing an Electricity Bill

### **Problem Statement (KTU-style)**
A small industry has the following monthly data:
- **Maximum Demand recorded:** 850 kVA
- **Contracted Demand:** 750 kVA
- **Energy consumed:** 4,50,000 kWh
- **Power Factor (average):** 0.85 lag
- **Tariff:**
  - Fixed charge: ₹400/kVA/month (on contracted or MD, whichever is higher)
  - Energy charge: ₹7.50/kWh
  - Demand penalty: 2× normal rate for MD exceeding contracted demand
  - PF penalty: ₹0.50/kVArh for every kVArh above 90% PF equivalent

**Calculate the total monthly electricity bill.**

---

### **Solution – Step-by-Step**

#### **Step 1: Determine Billed Maximum Demand**
Since MD (850 kVA) > Contracted Demand (750 kVA), the **billed demand is 850 kVA**, with a penalty on the excess.

$$
MD_{billed} = 850\ \text{kVA}
$$

$$
MD_{excess} = 850 - 750 = 100\ \text{kVA}
$$

#### **Step 2: Calculate Fixed Charge**
Fixed charges are applied on the **higher of contracted demand or recorded MD**.

$$
C_{fixed} = 850\ \text{kVA} \times ₹400/\text{kVA} = ₹3,40,000
$$

#### **Step 3: Calculate Energy Charge**
$$
C_{energy} = 4,50,000\ \text{kWh} \times ₹7.50/\text{kWh}
$$

$$
C_{energy} = ₹33,75,000
$$

#### **Step 4: Calculate Demand Penalty**
The excess 100 kVA is billed at **2× the fixed-charge rate** (i.e., ₹800/kVA):

$$
C_{demand\ penalty} = 100\ \text{kVA} \times ₹800/\text{kVA} = ₹80,000
$$

#### **Step 5: Calculate Power Factor Penalty**

Active energy: $E = 4,50,000\ \text{kWh}$

Real Power: $kW = kVA \times PF$. But here we work with kVArh directly.

Apparent energy: $kVAh = \dfrac{kWh}{PF} = \dfrac{4,50,000}{0.85} = 5,29,411.76\ \text{kVAh}$

Reactive energy: $kVArh = \sqrt{(kVAh)^2 - (kWh)^2}$

$$
kVArh = \sqrt{(5,29,411.76)^2 - (4,50,000)^2}
$$

Let us compute this carefully:

$$
kVArh^2 = (5,29,411.76)^2 - (4,50,000)^2
$$

$$
(5,29,411.76)^2 = 2,80,27,68,46,068\ \text{(approx.)}
$$

$$
(4,50,000)^2 = 2,02,50,00,00,000
$$

$$
kVArh^2 = 2,80,27,68,46,068 - 2,02,50,00,00,000 = 77,77,68,46,068
$$

$$
kVArh = \sqrt{77,77,68,46,068} = 2,78,888\ \text{kVArh (approx.)}
$$

The PF threshold corresponds to 90% PF, where:

$$
kVArh_{at\ 0.90\ PF} = kWh \times \tan(\cos^{-1}(0.90))
$$

$$
\tan(\cos^{-1}(0.90)) = \tan(25.84°) = 0.4843
$$

$$
kVArh_{threshold} = 4,50,000 \times 0.4843 = 2,17,935\ \text{kVArh}
$$

Excess reactive energy:
$$
kVArh_{excess} = 2,78,888 - 2,17,935 = 60,953\ \text{kVArh}
$$

PF Penalty:
$$
C_{PF} = 60,953 \times ₹0.50 = ₹30,476.50
$$

#### **Step 6: Total Bill**

$$
C_{total} = C_{fixed} + C_{energy} + C_{demand\ penalty} + C_{PF}
$$

$$
C_{total} = 3,40,000 + 33,75,000 + 80,000 + 30,476.50
$$

$$
C_{total} = ₹38,25,476.50
$$

#### **Step 7: Effective Rate per kWh**

$$
C_u = \dfrac{38,25,476.50}{4,50,000} = ₹8.50/\text{kWh}
$$

> **Observation:** The **nominal tariff** was ₹7.50/kWh, but the **effective cost** is ₹8.50/kWh due to penalties. This 13.3% premium is a direct result of poor power factor and demand mismanagement — exactly what energy audits identify.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 – Simple Payback Period Calculation

### **Problem Statement**
A factory replaces 200 conventional 100W incandescent lamps with 18W LED lamps.
- Daily operation: 12 hours
- Electricity rate: ₹8.00/kWh (effective)
- LED cost: ₹450 each
- Incandescent cost: ₹40 each (already in stock — irrelevant)
- Annual maintenance savings (LED lasts longer): ₹50,000

**Calculate:** (a) Annual energy savings in kWh, (b) Annual cost savings in ₹, (c) Simple Payback Period (SPP).

---

### **Solution**

#### **(a) Annual Energy Savings**

Power reduction per lamp: $100 - 18 = 82\ \text{W}$

Total power reduction: $200 \times 82 = 16,400\ \text{W} = 16.4\ \text{kW}$

Operating hours per year: $12 \times 365 = 4380\ \text{hours}$

$$
E_{saved} = 16.4 \times 4380 = 71,832\ \text{kWh/year}
$$

#### **(b) Annual Cost Savings**

$$
S_{energy} = 71,832 \times 8.00 = ₹5,74,656/\text{year}
$$

$$
S_{total} = 5,74,656 + 50,000 = ₹6,24,656/\text{year}
$$

#### **(c) Simple Payback Period**

Initial investment: $I_0 = 200 \times 450 = ₹90,000$

$$
SPP = \dfrac{I_0}{S_{total}} = \dfrac{90,000}{6,24,656}
$$

$$
SPP = 0.144\ \text{years} = 0.144 \times 12 = 1.73\ \text{months}
$$

> **Verdict:** Highly attractive ECM. Typical industrial hurdle rate = 2 years. This project clears it within 2 months.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 – NPV Calculation for a Heat Recovery Project

### **Problem Statement**
A boiler flue gas heat recovery project requires:
- **Initial Investment:** ₹20,00,000
- **Annual Fuel Savings:** ₹6,00,000 for 5 years
- **Annual Maintenance Cost:** ₹50,000
- **Discount Rate (r):** 10%

**Calculate NPV.** (Use discount factors: Year 1: 0.9091, Year 2: 0.8264, Year 3: 0.7513, Year 4: 0.6830, Year 5: 0.6209)

---

### **Solution**

Net annual cash flow: $C_t = 6,00,000 - 50,000 = ₹5,50,000$

Present Value of cash flows:

$$
PV = 5,50,000 \times (0.9091 + 0.8264 + 0.7513 + 0.6830 + 0.6209)
$$

$$
PV = 5,50,000 \times 3.7907 = ₹20,84,885
$$

$$
NPV = -I_0 + PV = -20,00,000 + 20,84,885 = ₹84,885
$$

> **Verdict:** $NPV > 0$ → Project is financially viable.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Audit Level Selection Decision Table

> [!TIP]
> Use this matrix in viva and exam answers to justify your choice of audit level.

| Criteria | Walk-Through (L1) | Detailed (L2) | Investment-Grade (L3) |
|----------|-------------------|---------------|------------------------|
| **Annual energy bill** | < ₹10 lakh | ₹10 lakh – ₹2 crore | > ₹2 crore |
| **Project value** | < ₹5 lakh | ₹5 lakh – ₹1 crore | > ₹1 crore |
| **Risk tolerance** | High | Medium | Low |
| **Data accuracy needed** | ±30% | ±10% | ±2% |
| **Instrumentation cost** | Minimal | Moderate | High |
| **M&V compliance** | None | Optional | IPMVP required |
| **Timeline** | Days | Weeks | Months |
| **Team size** | 1–2 | 3–5 | 5–10+ |
| **Output report depth** | List of ECMs | Energy balance + ECMs | Bankable document |

---

## 3.5 Energy Cost Analysis Flow (How Auditors Decompose)

> [!NOTE]
> Follow this **structured 6-step procedure** for any KTU numerical on energy cost.

1. **Collect** 24–36 months of utility bills.
2. **Normalize** for weather/production (using SEC).
3. **Decompose** bill into Fixed + Energy + Demand + PF + TOU + Tax components.
4. **Benchmark** against industry peers (BEE PAT benchmarks, sectoral norms).
5. **Identify** anomalies (any component growing faster than others).
6. **Recommend** targeted ECMs (one ECM per cost component).

---

```mermaid
    B3 --> C
```
