# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)" and explain its significance in energy auditing.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** is defined as the **energy consumed per unit of product output** or per unit of service delivered.

$$
SEC = \dfrac{Total\ Energy\ Consumed\ (kWh\ or\ MJ)}{Production\ Output\ (tons,\ kg,\ pieces,\ etc.)}
$$

**Significance:**
1. **Normalizes** energy use against production volume, enabling fair comparison across different time periods.
2. Used as an **Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI)** for benchmarking within industry.
3. Helps identify **trends** — rising SEC indicates deteriorating efficiency or process problems.
4. Serves as the **baseline** against which ECM savings are validated.

**Typical Units:** kWh/ton, MJ/kg, toe/1000 pieces, kcal/kWh.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Walk-Through and Investment-Grade Energy Audits.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Walk-Through Audit (Level 1) | Investment-Grade Audit (Level 3) |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Depth** | Qualitative, visual inspection | Quantitative, high-precision instrumentation |
| **Duration** | 1–3 days | Several months |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Instruments** | Hand-held meters, visual checks | Long-term data loggers, simulation software |
| **Savings Accuracy** | ±30% | ±2–5% |
| **Output** | List of ECMs (low-cost focus) | Bankable techno-economic feasibility report |
| **M&V Compliance** | None | Full IPMVP compliance |
| **Best Suited For** | Quick screening, awareness | Capital-intensive projects requiring financing |

---

## 📗 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Choose either A or B)

### **🔹 Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**An industry has the following monthly electricity bill components. Calculate the total bill and identify the cost component that should be the primary target for reduction. Justify your answer.**

| Component | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Contracted Demand | 1000 kVA |
| Maximum Demand Recorded | 1200 kVA |
| Energy Consumed | 6,00,000 kWh |
| Average Power Factor | 0.82 lag |
| Fixed Charge | ₹350/kVA/month |
| Energy Charge | ₹8.00/kWh (flat) |
| Excess MD Penalty | 2× fixed charge rate for kVA above contracted |
| PF Penalty | ₹0.40 per kVArh for kVArh above 0.90 PF equivalent |

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain with a neat block diagram the structured approach to conducting an energy audit. Mention the role of IPMVP in post-audit verification.**

---

### **🔹 Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**A manufacturing plant replaces 5 old motors (75 kW each, 89% efficiency) with 5 new IE4 motors (75 kW, 96% efficiency). The motors operate 5000 hours/year at 70% loading. Electricity rate is ₹7.50/kWh. Calculate:**
- **(i)** Annual energy savings in kWh.
- **(ii)** Annual cost savings in ₹.
- **(iii)** Simple Payback Period if total investment = ₹18,00,000.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss the concept of Net Present Value (NPV) and Internal Rate of Return (IRR). A project has Initial Investment = ₹15 lakh, Annual Savings = ₹5 lakh for 5 years, Discount Rate = 12%. Use discount factors (0.8929, 0.7972, 0.7118, 0.6355, 0.5674). Decide whether the project should be accepted.**

---

## ✅ MODEL SOLUTIONS

### **Solution to Question A, Part (a)**

#### **Step 1: Fixed Charge**
Since MD (1200) > Contracted (1000), bill on MD = 1200 kVA.
$$
C_{fixed} = 1200 \times 350 = ₹4,20,000
$$

#### **Step 2: Energy Charge**
$$
C_{energy} = 6,00,000 \times 8.00 = ₹48,00,000
$$

#### **Step 3: Demand Penalty**
Excess MD = 200 kVA; penalty rate = 2 × 350 = ₹700/kVA
$$
C_{penalty} = 200 \times 700 = ₹1,40,000
$$

#### **Step 4: PF Penalty**

kVAh = $6,00,000 / 0.82 = 7,31,707\ \text{kVAh}$

kVArh = $\sqrt{731707^2 - 600000^2}$

Computing:
$$
7,31,707^2 = 5,35,27,53,57,049
$$
$$
6,00,000^2 = 3,60,00,00,00,000
$$
$$
kVArh^2 = 1,75,27,53,57,049
$$
$$
kVArh = 4,18,659
$$

kVArh at 0.90 PF = $6,00,000 \times \tan(\cos^{-1}0.90) = 6,00,000 \times 0.4843 = 2,90,580$

Excess = $4,18,659 - 2,90,580 = 1,28,079$ kVArh

$$
C_{PF} = 1,28,079 \times 0.40 = ₹51,232
$$

#### **Step 5: Total**
$$
C_{total} = 4,20,000 + 48,00,000 + 1,40,000 + 51,232 = ₹54,11,232
$$

**Effective rate:** $54,11,232 / 6,00,000 = ₹9.02/\text{kWh}$

**Primary Reduction Target:** **Demand Penalty + PF Penalty together = ₹1,91,232/month = ₹22.95 lakh/year.**
- Installing a capacitor bank (≈ ₹3 lakh one-time cost) for PF correction alone will eliminate the PF penalty (~₹6.15 lakh/year) and reduce billed kVA, possibly eliminating the demand penalty.
- **Payback < 6 months** — this is the audit's primary recommendation.

---

### **Solution to Question A, Part (b)**

**Refer to the Block Diagram in SECTION 4.1 above.**

**Structured Energy Audit Phases:**

| Phase | Key Activities |
|-------|----------------|
| **Phase 1: Pre-Audit** | Scope, data collection, team formation |
| **Phase 2: Audit** | Walk-through → Detailed → Investment-Grade as needed |
| **Phase 3: Post-Audit** | ECM ranking, implementation, M&V |

**Role of IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol):**
- Provides **standardized procedures** to verify energy savings.
- **Option A:** Spot measurement of key parameter (low cost).
- **Option B:** Continuous measurement of all parameters (higher accuracy).
- **Option C:** Whole-facility bill analysis (simplest).
- **Option D:** Calibrated simulation (for retrofit projects).
- Ensures transparency between **ESCO and client**; required by international lenders/banks.

---

### **Solution to Question B, Part (a)**

#### **(i) Annual Energy Savings**

Old motor losses per motor at 70% loading:
- Input power (old) = $(75 \times 0.7) / 0.89 = 52.5 / 0.89 = 58.99\ \text{kW}$
- Input power (new) = $(75 \times 0.7) / 0.96 = 52.5 / 0.96 = 54.69\ \text{kW}$
- Savings per motor = $58.99 - 54.69 = 4.30\ \text{kW}$
- Total savings (5 motors) = $4.30 \times 5 = 21.52\ \text{kW}$

Annual savings:
$$
E_{saved} = 21.52 \times 5000 = 1,07,610\ \text{kWh/year}
$$

#### **(ii) Annual Cost Savings**
$$
S_{annual} = 1,07,610 \times 7.50 = ₹8,07,075/\text{year}
$$

#### **(iii) Simple Payback Period**
$$
SPP = \dfrac{18,00,000}{8,07,075} = 2.23\ \text{years}
$$

> **Verdict:** Acceptable (typical hurdle rate = 2–3 years).

---

### **Solution to Question B, Part (b)**

**NPV Concept:**
NPV is the present value of all future cash flows minus the initial investment, discounted at the project's cost of capital. **Decision Rule:** Accept project if NPV > 0.

$$
NPV = -I_0 + \sum_{t=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_t}{(1+r)^t}
$$

**IRR Concept:**
IRR is the discount rate that makes NPV = 0. **Decision Rule:** Accept project if IRR > cost of capital (hurdle rate).

**Calculation:**

Sum of discount factors = $0.8929 + 0.7972 + 0.7118 + 0.6355 + 0.5674 = 3.6048$

Present Value of savings = $5,00,000 \times 3.6048 = ₹18,02,400$

$$
NPV = -15,00,000 + 18,02,400 = ₹3,02,400
$$

> **Verdict:** NPV > 0 → **ACCEPT the project.**

(IRR can be approximated by interpolation: At r=15%, PV ≈ 5,00,000 × 3.3522 = ₹16,76,100 → NPV = +₹1,76,100. At r=20%, PV ≈ 5,00,000 × 2.9906 = ₹14,95,300 → NPV = –₹4,700. Therefore IRR ≈ 19.9%, well above 12% discount rate → project is strongly recommended.)

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**

1. **Forgetting to compare MD vs Contracted Demand:** Many students compute fixed charge on contracted demand only, missing the **excess demand penalty**. ALWAYS check: $MD > CD$ → penalty applies.

2. **Using kW instead of kVA for Demand Charge:** Demand charges are computed on **kVA** (apparent power), not kW. Convert using $kVA = kW/PF$.

3. **Wrong PF Penalty Calculation:** Use $\tan\phi$, not PF directly. The threshold kVArh at 0.90 PF = $kWh \times 0.4843$, NOT $kWh \times 0.10$.

4. **Ignoring Time Value of Money:** SPP ignores discount rate. KTU's later questions test NPV/IRR — students who only learn SPP lose full marks on NPV problems.

5. **Mixing Walk-Through and Investment-Grade Audit Definitions:** Students often confuse "preliminary" with "detailed." Memorize: **L1 = Visual; L2 = Instruments; L3 = Simulation + M&V.**

6. **Not Normalizing for Production:** When asked to compute SEC, ALWAYS divide by production output. Students often quote total kWh, which is **not** SEC.

7. **Arithmetic Slip on Square Root of kVArh²:** Practice the trig identity $kVArh = kWh \times \tan(\cos^{-1}PF)$ to avoid this.

8. **Misinterpreting "Annual Savings" in NPV:** Annual savings = Gross savings − Annual O&M cost. Students often forget O&M costs.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- ✅ **Three audit levels:** Walk-Through (L1), Detailed (L2), Investment-Grade (L3) — increasing cost, accuracy, and depth.
- ✅ **Bill components:** Fixed + Energy + Demand + PF Penalty + Fuel Adjustment + Taxes + TOU.
- ✅ **Demand Charge = kVA-based**, billed on **higher of MD or contracted demand**.
- ✅ **PF Penalty triggered below 0.90 lag** (India).
- ✅ **SEC** = Total Energy / Production Output — used as EnPI.
- ✅ **Simple Payback** = Investment ÷ Annual Savings (ignores time value).
- ✅ **NPV > 0 → Accept project**; **IRR > hurdle rate → Accept project**.
- ✅ **IPMVP** has 4 options (A, B, C, D) for M&V.
- ✅ **Grid Emission Factor (India, 2023):** ~0.716 kgCO₂/kWh.
- ✅ **Hours per year:** 8760.
- ✅ **kVA = kW / PF** — fundamental power triangle.
- ✅ **Reactive Energy (kVArh)** computed via $kWh \times \tan(\cos^{-1}PF)$.
- ✅ **Audit is a 3-phase process:** Pre-audit → On-site audit → Post-audit (with M&V).
- ✅ **Energy audit = combining technical savings with financial metrics** (₹-savings determine whether the ECM is implemented).
- ✅ **PDCA cycle** ensures continuous improvement post-audit.
- ✅ **For KTU exam:** Always show units in final answer; always state assumptions; always compare MD vs CD explicitly.

---

**🎯 Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Explain the Energy Management Approach," structure your answer in 3 phases × 3 audit levels × 5 bill components — this single mental map covers 80% of possible questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Benchmarking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Benchmarking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Benchmarking** is the systematic, continuous process of measuring and comparing an organization's energy performance — including energy use, energy intensity, and energy efficiency practices — against a recognized reference standard. The reference may be internal (own historical data), external (similar industry peers), or based on theoretical/normative standards (best-in-class technology or regulatory baselines).

In the context of **Energy Management and Conservation (PESFT414)**, benchmarking is positioned as one of the *Enabling Activities* within the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** framework, specifically assisting the *Check* phase. It quantifies deviations between actual and reference performance, thereby enabling the establishment of credible, evidence-based energy baselines and reduction targets.

### 1.2 Constituent Energy Performance Metrics

The benchmark values are typically expressed through **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)**. The most academically critical EnPIs are:

| Acronym | Metric | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SEC** | Specific Energy Consumption | Energy used per unit of product / output |
| **EPI** | Energy Performance Index | Energy used per unit area (e.g., buildings) |
| **EC** | Energy Cost | Rupees per unit of production / service |
| **EI** | Energy Intensity | Total energy / GDP or turnover (national level) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition Engine

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The School Report Card System**
>
> Imagine a class of students. Each student gets a score in Mathematics. The teacher now wants to know: *“How is our student ‘Rahul’ performing compared to the rest of the class?”*
> - If Rahul scores **85/100** and the **class average is 50**, Rahul is *above benchmark*.
> - If the **topper scores 95**, Rahul is *below best-practice benchmark*.
>
> **Benchmarking in energy management works identically.** Your plant is the student; your energy consumption is the score; your peer plants (or industry leaders) form the reference class. Benchmarking tells you *where you stand*, *how far you are from the best*, and *what corrective action to take*.

This is why benchmarking is often described as *"learning from others to improve yourself"* — an approach championed by **Robert C. Camp** (the pioneer of modern benchmarking at Xerox Corporation in the 1970s-80s).

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Reference Standards Recognized by KTU / BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency):**
> - **ISO 50001** — Energy Management Systems (International)
> - **ISO 50047** — Determination of energy savings in organizations
> - **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** Scheme of BEE for Designated Consumers
> - **ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code)** for commercial buildings
> - SECTOR-specific benchmarks published by **BEE / PCRA / TERI**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Benchmarking

Benchmarking is academically classified into **three primary categories**, each with distinct advantages and pitfalls:

### 2.1.1 Internal Benchmarking
- **Definition:** Comparing energy performance across different units, plants, departments, or time periods *within the same organization*.
- **Why:** Easy data access; standardized processes across units.
- **Limitation:** Internal best may still be far behind industry best.

### 2.1.2 External / Competitive Benchmarking
- **Definition:** Comparing performance against *direct competitors* within the same industry sector.
- **Why:** Reveals true market position; drives competitive advantage.
- **Limitation:** Data confidentiality; competitor reluctance to share.

### 2.1.3 Generic / Functional Benchmarking
- **Definition:** Comparing processes *irrespective of industry* — e.g., comparing compressed air efficiency in a textile plant vs. a cement plant.
- **Why:** Cross-industry innovation; uncovers unconventional savings.
- **Limitation:** Process dissimilarities may invalidate comparisons.

## 2.2 The Five-Phase Benchmarking Methodology (KTU Board-Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Robert Camp’s Five-Phase Model (Adapted for Energy Management):**

1. **Planning Phase:** Identify the process to benchmark (e.g., boiler efficiency); define metrics and data collection protocol.
2. **Data Collection Phase:** Gather at least **12 months** of energy bills, production logs, and operating conditions.
3. **Analysis Phase:** Normalize data (weather-corrected, production-corrected); compute EnPIs.
4. **Action Phase:** Establish realistic improvement targets; implement Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs).
5. **Monitoring Phase:** Track EnPIs continuously; re-benchmark annually to verify progress.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula a KTU examiner may test on Benchmarking. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P}$ | kWh / unit, TOE / tonne | $P \neq 0$ |
| 2 | Energy Performance Index | $EPI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{A}$ | kWh / m² / yr | Building sector |
| 3 | Energy Intensity | $EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{GDP}$ | TOE / Million ₹ | National level |
| 4 | Energy Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | % | $\eta \leq 100$ |
| 5 | Baseline Deviation | $\Delta\% = \dfrac{(E_{actual} - E_{baseline})}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | % | Positive = over-consumption |
| 6 | Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) | $S_t = S_{t-1} + (X_t - \bar{X})$ | dimensionless | $S_0 = 0$ |
| 7 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}$ | same as $X$ | $n \geq 2$ |
| 8 | Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{X}} \times 100$ | % | $CV < 10\%$ = stable process |
| 9 | Energy Cost Ratio | $ECR = \dfrac{C_{energy}}{C_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Manufacturing KPI |
| 10 | CUSUM Decision Limit | $\vert S_t \vert > 4\sigma \Rightarrow$ Signal | dimensionless | Statistical significance |

**Critical Notation Used:**
- $E_{total}$ = Total energy consumed (kWh, MJ, TOE)
- $P$ = Production output (units, tonnes, kg)
- $A$ = Floor area (m²)
- $X_t$ = Observation at time $t$
- $\bar{X}$ = Mean of observations
- $S_t$ = Cumulative sum at time $t$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Benchmarking Application |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Steel Industry** | SEC benchmarking in kgce/tonne of crude steel (BEE PAT sector) |
| **Cement Plants** | kWh/tonne of clinker vs. BAT (Best Available Technique) reference |
| **Commercial Buildings** | EPI in kWh/m²/year vs. ECBC-compliant building |
| **Textile Industry** | Specific steam consumption per kg of fabric processed |
| **Power Plants** | Auxiliary power consumption % vs. CEA norms |
| **Data Centers** | PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) vs. Uptime Institute benchmarks |

The practical engineering purpose of benchmarking is **three-fold**: (a) identify *where* energy is being lost; (b) prioritize ECMs by quantifying the *size* of deviation from the reference; and (c) provide *defensible, quantified targets* for top-management approval and ISO 50001 conformance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) and Baseline Deviation

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **single most tested calculation** under Benchmarking in KTU ESE papers. Master the normalized correction step.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A manufacturing facility produces **P units** per month and consumes **E_total kWh** of electricity and **F tonnes** of furnace oil. Determine:
(a) the SEC in **kWh per unit** and **TOE per unit**,
(b) the baseline deviation given a published industry benchmark **SEC_benchmark**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Convert all energy streams to a common unit (TOE — Tonne of Oil Equivalent).**

Standard conversion factors (declared as **bold constants** — required for KTU numericals):

$$\boxed{1 \text{ TOE} = 10{,}000{,}000 \text{ kcal} = 41.868 \text{ GJ} = 11{,}630 \text{ kWh}}$$

Conversion of furnace oil (FO):

$$\text{GCV of FO} = 10{,}200 \text{ kcal/kg} \Rightarrow 1 \text{ tonne FO} = 10{,}200 \text{ kcal} \times 10^3 = 1.02 \text{ TOE}$$

Therefore:

$$E_{FO} = F \text{ tonnes} \times 1.02 = 1.02F \text{ TOE}$$

$$E_{elec} = \frac{E_{total}}{11{,}630} \text{ TOE}$$

**Step 2 — Total Energy in TOE.**

$$E_{total, TOE} = \frac{E_{total}}{11{,}630} + 1.02F$$

**Step 3 — SEC Calculation.**

$$SEC = \frac{E_{total, TOE}}{P} \quad \text{[TOE/unit]}$$

**Step 4 — Baseline Deviation.**

$$\Delta\% = \frac{(SEC_{actual} - SEC_{benchmark})}{SEC_{benchmark}} \times 100$$

**Sign Convention:** $\Delta\% > 0$ implies the facility is **over-consuming** relative to benchmark (poor performance). $\Delta\% < 0$ implies **better than benchmark** (rare; verify data).

### 3.1.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Model)

> Given: $E_{total} = 5{,}00{,}000$ kWh, $F = 25$ tonnes FO, $P = 8000$ units, $SEC_{benchmark} = 0.05$ TOE/unit.

$$E_{elec} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{11{,}630} = 43.0 \text{ TOE}$$

$$E_{FO} = 25 \times 1.02 = 25.5 \text{ TOE}$$

$$E_{total, TOE} = 43.0 + 25.5 = 68.5 \text{ TOE}$$

$$SEC_{actual} = \frac{68.5}{8000} = 0.00856 \text{ TOE/unit}$$

$$\Delta\% = \frac{0.00856 - 0.05}{0.05} \times 100 = -82.9\%$$

The negative deviation flags an arithmetic or data inconsistency — a value 82.9% *below* industry benchmark is suspicious. A KTU student must **always cross-check input units** before concluding.

## 3.2 Derivation: Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) for Benchmarking Drift Detection

### 3.2.1 Theoretical Foundation

CUSUM is a **sequential statistical technique** used to detect small, persistent drifts in a process mean — exactly the type of slow degradation seen in energy-consuming equipment. The CUSUM chart is preferred over standard control charts because it amplifies cumulative drift that would otherwise be hidden in ordinary variance.

### 3.2.2 Recursive Equation

$$S_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} (X_i - \bar{X}_0) = S_{t-1} + (X_i - \bar{X}_0)$$

where:
- $X_i$ = SEC observed at month $i$
- $\bar{X}_0$ = historical target / benchmark mean
- $S_t$ = cumulative deviation at month $t$
- $S_0 = 0$ (initialization)

### 3.2.3 Decision Rule (KTU Board-Standard)

$$\boxed{\vert S_t \vert > 4\sigma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Process drift statistically significant}}$$

**4σ is the conservative engineering limit** corresponding to a false-alarm probability of approximately 0.006%.

### 3.2.4 Numerical Worked Example

> Target: $\bar{X}_0 = 100$ kWh/unit, $\sigma = 2$ kWh/unit, decision limit $= 4 \times 2 = 8$.

| Month ($i$) | $X_i$ | $(X_i - \bar{X}_0)$ | $S_t$ | Signal? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 100 | 0 | 0 | No |
| 2 | 101 | +1 | +1 | No |
| 3 | 102 | +2 | +3 | No |
| 4 | 104 | +4 | +7 | No |
| 5 | 105 | +5 | **+12** | **YES — drift detected** |

At $t=5$: $\vert S_5 \vert = 12 > 8$ → **statistically significant drift** — maintenance action triggered.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Benchmarking Approach

| Decision Criterion | Internal | Competitive | Generic |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Data Availability | **High** | Low–Medium | Medium |
| Confidentiality Risk | **None** | **High** | Low |
| Innovation Potential | Low | Medium | **Very High** |
| Implementation Speed | **Fast** | Slow | Medium |
| External Validity | Low | **High** | Medium |
| Cost of Execution | **Low** | High | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Benchmarking Process — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Phase 1: Planning - Identify Process and Metrics"]
    nodeB["Phase 2: Data Collection - 12 Months Minimum"]
    nodeC["Phase 3: Normalization - Weather and Production Corrected"]
    nodeD["Phase 4: Analysis - Compute EnPI Values"]
    nodeE["Phase 5: Comparison Against Reference Benchmark"]
    nodeF{"Is Gap Significant?"}
    nodeG["Phase 6: Action - Implement ECMs"]
    nodeH["Phase 7: Monitoring - Re-benchmark Annually"]
    nodeI["Document Best Practices and Update SOPs"]

    nodeA --> nodeB --> nodeC --> nodeD --> nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG --> nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeF -- No --> nodeH
    nodeI --> nodeB
```

## 4.2 Energy Benchmarking Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Raw Energy Data - Bills Meters Logs"]
    node2["Data Validation Layer"]
    node3["Normalization Engine - kWh per unit kWh per m2"]
    node4["EnPI Calculator - SEC EPI EI"]
    node5["Benchmark Database - BEE ISO 50001 ECBC"]
    node6["Deviation Analyzer - Delta percent CUSUM"]
    node7["Decision Dashboard"]
    node8["ECM Recommendation Engine"]
    node9["Top Management Report"]

    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4
    node5 --> node6
    node4 --> node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8 --> node9
```

## 4.3 CUSUM Drift Detection Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawX["Observed SEC Xi per month"]
    targetX["Target Mean X-bar"]
    subX["Compute Deviation Xi minus X-bar"]
    prevS["Add to Previous St minus 1"]
    newS["Update Cumulative Sum St"]
    checkLimit["Check Absolute St greater than 4 sigma"]
    signal["Trigger Maintenance Signal"]
    monitor["Continue Monitoring"]

    rawX --> subX
    targetX --> subX
    subX --> prevS
    prevS --> newS
    newS --> checkLimit
    checkLimit -- Yes --> signal
    checkLimit -- No --> monitor
    monitor --> rawX
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define benchmarking in the context of energy management. Mention any two types. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Benchmarking is the systematic process of measuring an organization's energy performance against a recognized reference standard (internal, competitive, or generic), to identify gaps, set realistic targets, and implement corrective energy conservation measures.

**Types:**
1. **Internal Benchmarking** — comparing across units/departments within the same organization.
2. **Competitive Benchmarking** — comparing against direct industry competitors.
3. **Generic Benchmarking** — comparing processes across different industries.
*(Any two are acceptable.)*

### Q2. List any three Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs) used in benchmarking. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** — energy per unit of output.
2. **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** — energy per unit area (buildings).
3. **Energy Intensity (EI)** — energy per unit of GDP/turnover.
4. **Energy Cost Ratio (ECR)** — energy cost as a % of total cost.

*(Any three suffice.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Choice Rule (KTU 2024):** Students answer either **Question A** OR **Question B** (full 14 marks). Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the various types of benchmarking with suitable examples for the energy sector.

**Model Solution:**

**Type 1 — Internal Benchmarking:**
Comparing Boiler A in Plant 1 vs Boiler B in Plant 2 (same organization). Identifies best internal practice; data is readily available; quick implementation.

**Type 2 — Competitive / External Benchmarking:**
Comparing the SEC of *your* cement plant (kWh/tonne clinker) with the SEC of the *leading cement competitor*. Reveals market standing; data exchange is difficult but can be done through industry associations (e.g., CII, FICCI).

**Type 3 — Generic / Functional Benchmarking:**
Comparing compressed-air system efficiency of a textile mill vs. an automotive plant — both use screw compressors but in different sectors. Uncovers cross-industry innovations.

**Type 4 — Performance / Normative Benchmarking:**
Comparing against regulatory standards like ECBC (buildings), PAT (industries), or BAT (Best Available Techniques from EU BREF documents).

**Type 5 — Strategic Benchmarking:**
Long-term comparison of business strategy and management practices in energy efficiency — less about numbers, more about policies and culture.

#### (b) [7 Marks] A manufacturing unit consumes 8,00,000 kWh of electricity and 40 tonnes of furnace oil per month, producing 12,000 units. The industry benchmark SEC is 0.06 TOE/unit. Calculate the actual SEC and the percentage deviation from benchmark. Take GCV of FO = 10,200 kcal/kg; 1 TOE = 10⁷ kcal.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert electricity to TOE:**

$$E_{elec} = \frac{8{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh} \times 3600 \text{ kcal/kWh}}{10^7 \text{ kcal/TOE}} = \frac{2.88 \times 10^9}{10^7} = 288 \text{ TOE}$$

*(Equivalently, using $1 \text{ TOE} = 11{,}630 \text{ kWh}$: $E_{elec} = 8{,}00{,}000 / 11{,}630 = 68.79$ TOE. Both methods accepted; KTU accepts either via standard factors.)*

**Step 2 — Convert furnace oil to TOE:**

$$E_{FO} = 40 \times 10{,}200 / 10^3 = 40 \times 1.02 = 40.8 \text{ TOE}$$

**Step 3 — Total energy in TOE:**

$$E_{total} = 288 + 40.8 = 328.8 \text{ TOE}$$

*(or $68.79 + 40.8 = 109.59$ TOE under the other convention.)*

**Step 4 — Actual SEC:**

$$SEC_{actual} = \frac{328.8}{12{,}000} = 0.0274 \text{ TOE/unit}$$

**Step 5 — Percentage Deviation:**

$$\Delta\% = \frac{0.0274 - 0.06}{0.06} \times 100 = -54.3\%$$

**Conclusion:** The unit's actual SEC is **54.3% below** the industry benchmark — a notably superior performance. *(Note: KTU examiners will verify your conversion factor choice; state it explicitly.)*

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the CUSUM technique used in energy benchmarking. How does it help detect process drift?

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:**
CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) is a sequential statistical technique that plots the cumulative sum of deviations between observed values and a target mean. Unlike Shewhart control charts, it is highly sensitive to *small persistent drifts* in the mean.

**Recursive Equation:**

$$S_t = S_{t-1} + (X_t - \bar{X}_0), \quad S_0 = 0$$

**Decision Rule:**

$$\vert S_t \vert > 4\sigma \implies \text{Process has drifted statistically}$$

**Application to Energy Benchmarking:**
- $\bar{X}_0$ = Target SEC or EPI from baseline year.
- $X_t$ = Monthly SEC observation.
- Drift indicates slow degradation — e.g., fouling in a heat exchanger, scaling in a boiler, wear in a motor bearing.

**Advantages:**
1. Detects drift as small as **0.5σ** which Shewhart charts miss.
2. Visually intuitive — slope of CUSUM line indicates drift rate.
3. Provides cumulative evidence — random noise cancels out.

**Disadvantages:**
1. Requires historical baseline mean.
2. Less effective for abrupt step changes.

#### (b) [7 Marks] The monthly SEC (kWh/unit) of a plant for 6 months is: 102, 104, 105, 107, 110, 113. The target SEC is 100 kWh/unit with $\sigma = 2$ kWh. Apply the CUSUM technique and state whether drift is detected.

**Model Solution:**

**Decision Limit:** $4\sigma = 4 \times 2 = 8$.

| Month $i$ | $X_i$ | $(X_i - 100)$ | $S_t$ | $\vert S_t \vert > 8$? |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 102 | +2 | 2 | No |
| 2 | 104 | +4 | 6 | No |
| 3 | 105 | +5 | **11** | **YES** |
| 4 | 107 | +7 | 18 | Yes |
| 5 | 110 | +10 | 28 | Yes |
| 6 | 113 | +13 | 41 | Yes |

**Inference:** Drift is **first detected at Month 3** (when $\vert S_3 \vert = 11 > 8$). The plant's energy performance has drifted from the target; corrective action is justified. The slope is steepening, indicating **accelerating degradation** — likely fouling or load mismatch.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Benchmarking Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (3 marks lost):** Converting kWh to TOE using $1 \text{ TOE} = 11630 \text{ kWh}$ is correct *only* for electrical energy at 100% efficiency. Always state your assumption.
> 2. **Sign Convention Confusion:** Negative $\Delta\%$ is *not* "good performance" by default — it may indicate data inconsistency. Cross-verify.
> 3. **CUSUM Initialization:** Forgetting $S_0 = 0$ leads to incorrect $S_1$.
> 4. **Decision Limit:** KTU expects $4\sigma$ (engineering), not $3\sigma$ (statistical) — explicitly write "$4\sigma$" in answers.
> 5. **Normalization:** Comparing a winter month's SEC to a summer month's is invalid — must **weather-correct** before benchmarking.
> 6. **No Process Flow in Long Answers:** Always structure Type-explanation questions with **Definition → Example → Advantage → Limitation**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Benchmarking:**

- **Benchmarking** = systematic comparison of EnPIs against a reference standard (internal / competitive / generic).
- The **five-phase methodology** (Plan → Collect → Analyze → Act → Monitor) is the KTU standard answer scaffold.
- **SEC** = Total energy / Production output (most-tested formula).
- **EPI** = Energy / Area (used in buildings & ECBC compliance).
- **EI** = Energy / GDP (used in national-level energy policy).
- The **4σ CUSUM decision rule** detects process drift statistically — initialization $S_0 = 0$.
- **Energy Efficiency** $\eta = E_{useful}/E_{input} \times 100$; always $\leq 100\%$.
- **Baseline deviation** $\Delta\% = (E_{actual} - E_{baseline})/E_{baseline} \times 100$ — sign convention is critical.
- **Benchmarking is NOT a one-time activity** — it is *continuous* and feeds the PDCA cycle of ISO 50001.
- Key reference standards: **ISO 50001, ECBC, PAT scheme, BEE sector benchmarks**.
- Internal benchmarking is *cheapest and fastest*; competitive is *most defensible externally*; generic is *most innovative*.
- Always **normalize** data for weather, production volume, and operating hours before benchmarking.
- CUSUM is **superior to Shewhart charts** for detecting slow drifts in energy systems.
- The 5 EnPIs to remember: **SEC, EPI, EI, EC, $\eta$** — write all five in any 14-mark answer.
- A negative $\Delta\%$ demands data validation before declaring "excellent performance".

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Energy Performance (EnP)** is the measurable result of an organization's energy efficiency, productivity, and conservation efforts, expressed as the ratio of actual energy output (useful work, product, or service) to the energy input consumed in producing that output. It is a quantitative metric that allows engineers and energy auditors to assess how effectively a system, process, facility, or organization converts purchased energy resources into productive output.

Mathematically, energy performance is the inverse of **energy intensity**, and is fundamentally tied to the **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** of a system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board-Exam Ready):**
> *"Energy Performance refers to the measurable outcomes regarding energy efficiency, energy consumption, and energy intensity of an organization, system, or process, expressed through specific, quantifiable indicators (EnPIs) that allow benchmarking and tracking of improvements against a defined baseline."*

### 1.2 Key Terminology Decoded

| Terminology | Definition | Practical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Baseline** | A quantitative reference of energy consumption under normal operating conditions. | The "before" picture used to compare future savings. |
| **Benchmarking** | Comparing an organization's energy use against best-in-class or industry standards. | "How do we stack up against our peers?" |
| **EnPI** | Energy Performance Indicator — a quantifiable value used to track energy performance. | The "thermometer" of energy efficiency. |
| **SEC** | Specific Energy Consumption — energy used per unit of output. | "How much fuel does my factory burn per tonne of steel?" |
| **M&V** | Measurement & Verification — protocols for quantifying savings. | The audit trail that proves energy savings actually happened. |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Fuel Efficiency of a Car

Imagine you bought a new car. The manufacturer's sticker says **15 km/L**. After driving for a month, you check your own consumption and find **13 km/L**. Your car's *energy performance* has dropped.

Now, to fix it:
1. You measure the baseline (the 15 km/L factory spec).
2. You track your **SEC** (km/L) over time — this is your **EnPI**.
3. You compare your car against other similar models (**benchmarking**).
4. After a tune-up, your car returns to 14.8 km/L — your **performance improvement** is quantified.

A factory, building, or industrial process works identically:
- The "fuel" is electricity, steam, coal, or gas.
- The "kilometers" are tonnes of product, square feet cooled, or passengers transported.
- The "tune-up" is an Energy Conservation Measure (ECM).
- The "measurement" is the Energy Performance Indicator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Performance is NOT the same as Energy Conservation.** Conservation is the *action* (turning off lights, insulating pipes). Performance is the *measurable outcome* of those actions.

---

## 1.4 Why Energy Performance Matters in Industry

1. **Regulatory Compliance:** Energy Performance-Based Standards (EPBNS) are mandated under India's **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme.
2. **Cost Reduction:** Improving energy performance directly reduces operating expenses.
3. **Carbon Accounting:** Energy performance improvements translate to reduced **CO₂ equivalent (CO₂e)** emissions.
4. **ISO 50001 Alignment:** The international standard for Energy Management Systems (EnMS) requires demonstrable EnPI improvements.
5. **Investment Justification:** Energy Performance data is required to secure funding for retrofit projects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 specifically deals with quantitative energy analysis. Energy Performance forms the bridge between raw consumption data (Module 1) and actual efficiency evaluation, making it a **high-weightage topic** in the university exam.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Energy Performance Framework

Energy Performance is evaluated using a structured **4-tier hierarchy**:

1. **Tier 1 — Energy Intensity (Macro Level):** Energy used per unit of total output.
2. **Tier 2 — Specific Energy Consumption (Meso Level):** Energy per unit of specific product/service.
3. **Tier 3 — Energy Performance Indicators (Micro Level):** Equipment-level KPIs (motor efficiency, boiler efficiency).
4. **Tier 4 — Sub-Metered KPIs:** Process-specific metrics (kWh/tonne of steam, kWh/tonne of refrigeration).

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Components of Energy Performance

### A. Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

The most commonly used EnPI in industry. SEC measures the energy required to produce one unit of product or service.

$$SEC = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumed}}{\text{Total Output Produced}}$$

- **Units:** kWh/tonne, MJ/kg, kcal/litre, GJ/tonne
- **Lower SEC = Better Performance**

### B. Energy Intensity (EI)

A broader measure typically used at national or organizational level.

$$EI = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumption of a Facility}}{\text{Gross Floor Area (or Revenue, or Output)}}$$

- **Units:** kWh/m², GJ/\$1000 of revenue
- Used in **Green Building Ratings (LEED, GRIHA, BEE Star Rating)**.

### C. Energy Performance Index (EPI) — Buildings

A specialized KPI mandated by the **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** of India.

$$EPI = \frac{\text{Annual Energy Consumption of Building (kWh)}}{\text{Total Built-up Area (m}^{2}\text{)}}$$

- **Units:** kWh/m²/year
- ECBC threshold for compliance: **EPI ≤ 120 kWh/m²/year** (varies by climate zone).

### D. Energy Performance Assessment Methodology

Energy Performance is assessed via:

| Method | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Comparison Method** | Compare current consumption with historical baseline. | Routine monitoring. |
| **Regression Analysis** | Establish mathematical relationship between energy use and influencing variables (e.g., CDD, production volume). | Weather-sensitive facilities. |
| **Energy Modeling** | Simulate energy use using software (eQUEST, EnergyPlus). | Design-stage evaluation. |
| **M&V Protocols** | IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol) — 4 Options A, B, C, D. | ECM verification. |

### E. The Baseline — Foundation of Performance Evaluation

The baseline represents energy consumption under **standardized normal operating conditions**. All energy savings are calculated as the difference between the adjusted baseline and the post-retrofit consumption.

$$\text{Energy Savings} = \text{Adjusted Baseline Energy} - \text{Reporting Period Energy} \pm \text{Adjustments}$$

- **Adjustments** account for changes in production volume, weather, operating hours, etc.
- Per IPMVP, baselines must be **documented, replicable, and auditable**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Specific Energy Consumption** | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{in}}{P_{out}}$ | kWh/unit | Industrial process benchmarking |
| **Energy Intensity** | $EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{A_{floor}}$ | kWh/m² | Building-level assessment |
| **Energy Performance Index** | $EPI = \dfrac{E_{annual}}{A_{builtup}}$ | kWh/m²/year | ECBC compliance |
| **Energy Savings** | $\Delta E = E_{baseline} - E_{actual}$ | kWh, MWh, GJ | ECM verification |
| **Performance Improvement** | $\%\ Improvement = \dfrac{SEC_{old} - SEC_{new}}{SEC_{old}} \times 100$ | % | Year-on-year tracking |
| **Baseline Adjustment** | $E_{adj} = E_{base} \times \dfrac{V_{actual}}{V_{base}}$ | kWh | Variable production periods |
| **Energy Efficiency (Motor)** | $\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Equipment-level KPI |
| **Boiler Efficiency** | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{absorbed}}{Q_{input}} \times 100$ | % | Thermal system KPI |
| **COP** | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | dimensionless | HVAC performance |
| **kWh to GJ Conversion** | $1\ kWh = 0.0036\ GJ$ | — | Multi-fuel comparisons |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PAT Scheme (Bureau of Energy Efficiency, India):** Industries are mandated to reduce their **Specific Energy Consumption** below targets, or purchase **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)**.
- **Green Buildings:** LEED and GRIHA certifications require documented EPI reductions.
- **Manufacturing Excellence:** Toyota Production System uses SEC as a core KPI for continuous improvement (Kaizen).
- **Utilities:** Power plants track **Heat Rate (kcal/kWh)** as the primary EnPI.
- **HVAC:** **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** and **Energy Efficiency Ratio (EER)** are direct EnPIs for chillers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Performance is the language of energy management.** Without quantified EnPIs, all conservation efforts remain unproven and unverifiable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Baseline Adjustment for Variable Production

In real factories, production volume rarely stays constant. Therefore, the baseline energy must be **adjusted** to reflect the actual production level to enable a fair comparison.

### Step 1: Define the Variables

Let:
- $E_{base}$ = Baseline energy consumption (kWh) recorded during the baseline period
- $V_{base}$ = Production volume during baseline period (units)
- $E_{actual}$ = Actual energy consumption during reporting period (kWh)
- $V_{actual}$ = Production volume during reporting period (units)

### Step 2: Establish the Baseline Model

A simple linear regression model between energy consumption and production volume is established during the baseline period:

$$E_{base} = a + b \cdot V_{base}$$

where:
- $a$ = Fixed energy component (lighting, standby losses) in kWh
- $b$ = Variable energy coefficient (kWh/unit) — this is the SEC

### Step 3: Compute the Baseline Energy Intensity

$$SEC_{base} = \frac{E_{base} - a}{V_{base}}$$

The variable part is divided by production to isolate the **specific** consumption.

### Step 4: Adjust the Baseline to Current Production

Using the same fixed energy component $a$ and variable coefficient $b$, the energy that *would have been used* in the reporting period at the new production level is:

$$E_{adjusted} = a + b \cdot V_{actual}$$

### Step 5: Compute Energy Savings

The verified energy savings are:

$$\Delta E = E_{adjusted} - E_{actual}$$

### Step 6: Express as Percentage Performance Improvement

$$\%\ Savings = \frac{E_{adjusted} - E_{actual}}{E_{adjusted}} \times 100$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **[Question]** A cement plant's baseline data over 6 months showed:
> - Energy consumption: $E_{base} = 5{,}00{,}000$ kWh
> - Cement produced: $V_{base} = 2{,}000$ tonnes
> - Fixed energy component: $a = 50{,}000$ kWh
>
> After implementing a waste heat recovery system, the reporting period (same 6 months) recorded:
> - Energy consumption: $E_{actual} = 4{,}60{,}000$ kWh
> - Cement produced: $V_{actual} = 2{,}500$ tonnes
>
> Calculate:
> (a) Baseline SEC
> (b) Reporting period SEC
> (c) Adjusted baseline energy
> (d) Verified energy savings
> (e) Percentage improvement in energy performance

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Part (a): Baseline SEC**

$$SEC_{base} = \frac{E_{base} - a}{V_{base}} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}000}{2{,}000}$$

$$SEC_{base} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{2{,}000} = 225\ kWh/tonne$$

**Part (b): Reporting Period SEC**

$$SEC_{actual} = \frac{E_{actual} - a}{V_{actual}} = \frac{4{,}60{,}000 - 50{,}000}{2{,}500}$$

$$SEC_{actual} = \frac{4{,}10{,}000}{2{,}500} = 164\ kWh/tonne$$

**Part (c): Adjusted Baseline Energy**

First, find the variable coefficient $b$:

$$b = SEC_{base} = 225\ kWh/tonne$$

Apply the adjusted baseline formula:

$$E_{adjusted} = a + b \cdot V_{actual} = 50{,}000 + 225 \times 2{,}500$$

$$E_{adjusted} = 50{,}000 + 5{,}62{,}500 = 6{,}12{,}500\ kWh$$

**Part (d): Verified Energy Savings**

$$\Delta E = E_{adjusted} - E_{actual} = 6{,}12{,}500 - 4{,}60{,}000$$

$$\Delta E = 1{,}52{,}500\ kWh$$

**Part (e): Percentage Improvement in Energy Performance**

$$\%\ Savings = \frac{1{,}52{,}500}{6{,}12{,}500} \times 100 = 24.9\%$$

$$\boxed{SEC_{old} = 225\ kWh/tonne,\quad SEC_{new} = 164\ kWh/tonne,\quad Savings = 24.9\%}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right EnPI

| Industry / Sector | Best EnPI | Typical Value Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Cement** | kWh/tonne of clinker | 50 – 110 |
| **Steel** | GJ/tonne of crude steel | 18 – 25 |
| **Aluminium** | kWh/tonne of aluminium | 13{,}000 – 15{,}000 |
| **Textile** | kWh/kg of fabric | 0.8 – 1.5 |
| **Hotels** | kWh/guest-night | 25 – 60 |
| **Offices** | EPI in kWh/m²/year | 80 – 250 |
| **Data Centers** | PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness) | 1.2 – 2.0 |
| **Refrigeration** | kWh/tonne of refrigeration (TR) | 0.6 – 1.2 |
| **Pumps** | kWh/m³ of water pumped | 0.3 – 1.0 |

---

## 3.4 KTU Board Examiner's Logic Chain

When asked *"Explain Energy Performance"* in a 14-mark question, examiners expect this exact chain:

1. **Define** Energy Performance and its components.
2. **Distinguish** between Energy Consumption, Energy Intensity, and SEC.
3. **Discuss** the role of EnPIs and provide industrial examples.
4. **Explain** the Baseline concept and why adjustment is required.
5. **Apply** the formulas to a numerical problem (mandatory for 14-mark questions).
6. **Conclude** with a real-world relevance (e.g., PAT scheme, ISO 50001).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Performance Assessment Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Energy Consuming Processes] --> B[Collect Energy Consumption Data]
    B --> C[Establish Baseline Period Data]
    C --> D[Perform Regression Analysis to Define Baseline Model]
    D --> E[Define EnPIs Specific Energy Consumption, Energy Intensity, EPI]
    E --> F[Set Performance Targets Based on Benchmarking]
    F --> G[Implement Energy Conservation Measures ECMs]
    G --> H[Measure Post Retrofit Energy Consumption]
    H --> I[Adjust Baseline to Current Operating Conditions]
    I --> J[Calculate Verified Energy Savings]
    J --> K[Quantify Performance Improvement Percentage]
    K --> L[Report to Management ISO 50001, PAT, ECBC]
    L --> M{Performance Improved?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Document Best Practices and Iterate]
    M -- No --> O[Re-evaluate ECMs and Re-investigate]
    O --> G
    N --> P[Continuous Improvement Cycle]
```

---

## 4.2 EnPI Hierarchy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LEVEL1["TIER 1 ORGANIZATIONAL LEVEL"]
        L1A[Energy Intensity kWh per Revenue]
        L1B[Total Energy Consumption GJ per Year]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL2["TIER 2 FACILITY LEVEL"]
        L2A[Specific Energy Consumption SEC]
        L2B[Energy Performance Index EPI for Buildings]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL3["TIER 3 EQUIPMENT LEVEL"]
        L3A[Motor Efficiency Percent]
        L3B[Boiler Efficiency Percent]
        L3C[Chiller COP]
    end
    subgraph LEVEL4["TIER 4 PROCESS LEVEL"]
        L4A[kWh per Tonne of Steam]
        L4B[kWh per Tonne of Refrigeration]
        L4C[GJ per Tonne of Clinker]
    end
    LEVEL1 --> LEVEL2 --> LEVEL3 --> LEVEL4
```

---

## 4.3 Baseline Adjustment Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Baseline Period Data] --> B[Fixed Energy Component a in kWh]
    A --> C[Variable Coefficient b kWh per unit]
    D[Reporting Period Production Volume] --> E[Adjusted Baseline a plus b times Vactual]
    B --> E
    C --> E
    E --> F[Comparison with Actual Consumption]
    G[Actual Reporting Period Consumption] --> F
    F --> H[Verified Energy Savings]
    H --> I[Percentage Improvement in Energy Performance]
```

---

## 4.4 Energy Performance Feedback Loop (Kaizen Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plan Define EnPIs and Targets] --> D[Do Implement ECMs]
    D --> C[Check Measure Post Retrofit Performance]
    C --> A[Act Correct Deviations and Standardize]
    A --> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The Kaizen-style PDCA cycle above is the exact framework mandated by **ISO 50001:2018** for continuous improvement of energy performance. KTU board questions on ISO 50001 commonly ask students to draw this cycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Energy Performance and list any four Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs) used in industry.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Energy Performance is the measurable outcome of energy efficiency in a system, process, or organization, expressed through quantitative indicators that compare actual energy use against baseline or benchmark values.

**Four Industrial EnPIs:**

| S.No. | EnPI | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Energy Consumption (kWh/tonne) | Cement, Steel, Pulp & Paper |
| 2 | Energy Performance Index (kWh/m²/year) | Commercial Buildings |
| 3 | Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE) | Data Centers |
| 4 | Coefficient of Performance (COP) | HVAC & Refrigeration |

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Energy Intensity and Specific Energy Consumption.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Energy Intensity (EI) | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Macro-level (organization or building) | Meso/Micro-level (specific process/product) |
| **Denominator** | Floor area (m²) or revenue | Physical unit of product (tonne, kg, litre) |
| **Use Case** | Green building certification, national energy policy | Industrial process benchmarking |
| **Example** | kWh/m²/year | kWh/tonne of cement |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks. Either Q.No. (A) or Q.No. (B) must be answered.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the concept of Energy Performance with reference to the Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs). Discuss their role in industrial energy management with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A textile unit's baseline data over 3 months showed an energy consumption of **3,00,000 kWh** to produce **50,000 kg of fabric**. The fixed energy component (lighting, HVAC baseload) was estimated at **30,000 kWh**. After installing a VFD on the main motor, the reporting period recorded **2,20,000 kWh** for producing **55,000 kg of fabric**. Compute: **[7 Marks]**

(i) Baseline Specific Energy Consumption
(ii) Reporting Period Specific Energy Consumption
(iii) Adjusted Baseline Energy
(iv) Verified Energy Savings
(v) Percentage improvement in energy performance

#### **Model Solution for Q.A**

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation:**

Energy Performance is a measurable indicator of how efficiently energy is utilized in producing a useful output. It is quantified through **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)**.

**Role of EnPIs in Industrial Energy Management:**

1. **Quantification:** EnPIs convert abstract concepts like "efficiency" into numerical values that can be tracked.
2. **Benchmarking:** Industries compare their EnPIs with global best practices (e.g., BAT — Best Available Techniques).
3. **Target Setting:** Under India's **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme, industries are assigned SEC reduction targets.
4. **Continuous Improvement:** As per **ISO 50001**, EnPIs form the basis of the PDCA cycle for energy management.
5. **Decision Making:** Capital investment in ECMs is justified using EnPI-based ROI calculations.

**Examples:**

- **Cement industry:** kWh/tonne of clinker
- **HVAC systems:** COP (Coefficient of Performance)
- **Data centers:** PUE (Power Usage Effectiveness)
- **Buildings:** EPI in kWh/m²/year

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $E_{base} = 3{,}00{,}000$ kWh; $V_{base} = 50{,}000$ kg; $a = 30{,}000$ kWh
- $E_{actual} = 2{,}20{,}000$ kWh; $V_{actual} = 55{,}000$ kg

**(i) Baseline SEC:**

$$SEC_{base} = \frac{E_{base} - a}{V_{base}} = \frac{3{,}00{,}000 - 30{,}000}{50{,}000}$$

$$SEC_{base} = \frac{2{,}70{,}000}{50{,}000} = 5.4\ kWh/kg$$

**(ii) Reporting Period SEC:**

$$SEC_{actual} = \frac{4{,}90{,}000 \text{ -- wait, recompute}}{55{,}000} = \frac{1{,}90{,}000}{55{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{SEC_{actual} = 3.454\ kWh/kg}$$

**(iii) Adjusted Baseline Energy:**

$$E_{adj} = a + SEC_{base} \times V_{actual} = 30{,}000 + 5.4 \times 55{,}000$$

$$E_{adj} = 30{,}000 + 2{,}97{,}000 = 3{,}27{,}000\ kWh$$

**(iv) Verified Energy Savings:**

$$\Delta E = E_{adj} - E_{actual} = 3{,}27{,}000 - 2{,}20{,}000 = 1{,}07{,}000\ kWh$$

**(v) Percentage Improvement:**

$$\%\ Savings = \frac{1{,}07{,}000}{3{,}27{,}000} \times 100 = 32.72\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Energy Performance improved by } 32.72\%}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is the Energy Performance Index (EPI)? Explain its significance in the **Energy Conservation Building Code (ECBC)** of India. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An office building has a built-up area of **10,000 m²**. Last year's annual electricity consumption was **15,00,000 kWh**. After implementing LED lighting and optimizing the chiller setpoint, the consumption dropped to **12,00,000 kWh**. Calculate: **[7 Marks]**

(i) Initial EPI
(ii) New EPI
(iii) Absolute and percentage reduction in EPI
(iv) Comment on ECBC compliance (threshold: 120 kWh/m²/year).

#### **Model Solution for Q.B**

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation:**

**Energy Performance Index (EPI)** is defined as the annual electrical energy consumption of a building per unit of built-up area.

$$EPI = \frac{\text{Annual Energy Consumption (kWh)}}{\text{Built-up Area (m}^{2}\text{)}}$$

**Units:** kWh/m²/year

**Significance in ECBC:**

1. ECBC mandates EPI thresholds (typically **≤ 120 kWh/m²/year** for compliance).
2. EPI enables **uniform comparison** of buildings across different sizes and geographies.
3. It serves as the **primary KPI** for energy-efficient building design.
4. EPI reductions qualify buildings for **ECBC Plus / SuperECBC** ratings.
5. EPI is used for **5-star ratings** by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Built-up area $A = 10{,}000$ m²
- $E_{old} = 15{,}00{,}000$ kWh
- $E_{new} = 12{,}00{,}000$ kWh

**(i) Initial EPI:**

$$EPI_{old} = \frac{15{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = 150\ kWh/m^2/year$$

**(ii) New EPI:**

$$EPI_{new} = \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = 120\ kWh/m^2/year$$

**(iii) Absolute and Percentage Reduction:**

$$\Delta EPI = 150 - 120 = 30\ kWh/m^2/year$$

$$\%\ Reduction = \frac{30}{150} \times 100 = 20\%$$

**(iv) ECBC Compliance Comment:**

$$\boxed{EPI_{new} = 120\ kWh/m^2/year \text{ — Just meets ECBC compliance threshold.}}$$

The building is at the boundary of ECBC compliance. To qualify for ECBC Plus, the EPI must be **10–25% lower than the ECBC threshold** (i.e., 90–108 kWh/m²/year). Further retrofits (e.g., solar PV, high-efficiency chillers) are recommended.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Energy Consumption with Energy Performance:** Energy consumption is the absolute quantity used; performance is the efficiency of conversion. Examiners **deduct 2 marks** for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting to Subtract Fixed Energy ($a$):** In SEC calculations, students often write $E/V$ instead of $(E-a)/V$. This gives a wrong SEC and a wrong answer in the reporting period too.
> 3. **Not Adjusting the Baseline for Production Volume Change:** A common error is comparing absolute kWh values when production volumes differ. Always adjust using $E_{adj} = a + b \cdot V_{actual}$.
> 4. **Wrong Units in EPI:** EPI must be **kWh/m²/year**. Writing "kWh/m²" alone loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Missing Industrial Examples:** In a 7-mark conceptual question, listing at least 2–3 real-world examples (cement, steel, buildings) fetches full marks.
> 6. **Skipping the Conclusion:** Always conclude a 14-mark answer with a real-world linkage (PAT, ISO 50001, ECBC, or BEE Star Rating).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Energy Performance** = measurable efficiency of energy use → quantified through **EnPIs**.
- **SEC** (kWh/unit) is the most widely used industrial EnPI.
- **EPI** (kWh/m²/year) is the primary metric for buildings under **ECBC**.
- **Energy Intensity** (kWh/m² or GJ/\$1000) is the macro-level metric.
- **Baseline** is the reference consumption used for comparison; it must be adjusted for variable conditions.
- **Energy Savings = Adjusted Baseline − Actual Consumption.**
- **% Performance Improvement** = (Savings / Adjusted Baseline) × 100.
- **IPMVP** provides 4 standardized M&V options (A, B, C, D) for ECM verification.
- **PDCA cycle** is the ISO 50001 framework for continuous energy performance improvement.
- Real-world frameworks: **PAT Scheme (BEE), ECBC, ISO 50001, LEED, GRIHA**.
- Always **separate fixed and variable energy components** when adjusting baselines.
- Energy Performance is the **language of energy managers** — without it, no savings can be proven.
- **Lower SEC / Lower EPI = Better Energy Performance.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Matching Energy Use to Requirement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Matching Energy Use to Requirement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Matching Energy Use to Requirement** is the foundational principle of Energy Management and Conservation wherein the *quantity*, *quality* (form), *timing*, and *intensity* of energy delivered to a system, process, or equipment is precisely aligned with its actual end-use demand. It is the systematic elimination of **over-supply**, **under-utilization**, **mismatch in energy form**, and **temporal misalignment** between generation/supply and consumption.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, this concept is rooted in the **Energy Balance Principle**:

$$E_{input} = E_{useful\,output} + E_{losses} + E_{waste}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Matching energy use to requirement* refers to the methodology of ensuring that the energy supplied to any system is no greater, no lesser, and no different in form than what is required to perform the intended work, thereby eliminating wasteful over-provisioning and inefficient under-provisioning.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy: The Water Tap**

Imagine filling a bucket with water. If the tap is fully open and the bucket is small, water overflows — energy (in this case, the work done by the water pressure) is wasted. If the tap trickles and the bucket is large, the task takes too long and productivity suffers. If you use a fire hose to water a small plant, you damage it. Similarly, in engineering systems:

- **Over-supply** = A 10 HP motor driving a 2 HP load (5× wasted energy)
- **Under-supply** = A 1 HP motor trying to drive a 5 HP load (motor burns out)
- **Wrong form** = Using electrical resistance heating when waste heat is freely available
- **Wrong timing** = Running chillers at peak hours when electricity is most expensive

**The goal of "Matching Energy Use to Requirement" is to make the tap deliver exactly the right amount, exactly when needed, in exactly the right form.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Every unit of energy that does NOT perform useful work is a *loss* — economically, environmentally, and thermodynamically. Matching is the *first* step before any conservation measure can be justified.

### 1.3 The Four Dimensions of Energy Matching

| Dimension | Question it Answers | Typical Mismatch |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantity** | How much energy? | Oversized boilers, oversized pumps |
| **Quality / Form** | What form of energy? | Electrical heating vs. solar thermal |
| **Timing / Time-of-Use** | When is it needed? | Day vs. night tariffs, peak demand |
| **Intensity / Pressure** | At what rate? | High-pressure air for low-pressure tasks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (KTU Board Standard):** Energy efficiency is quantified as:
> $$\eta = \frac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100\,\%$$
> A perfectly matched system approaches $\eta \to 100\%$.

### 1.4 Why This Topic is Central to KTU PESFT414

This topic is the **conceptual backbone** of all energy management. It underpins:
- **Energy Audit** (Module 1 prerequisite)
- **Energy-Efficient Motors & Drives** (Module 3)
- **HVAC & Lighting Systems** (Module 4)
- **Thermal Systems & Waste Heat Recovery** (Module 5)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Course Outcome (CO) Mapping:**
> - **CO2:** *Apply the principles of energy matching to identify mismatches in industrial and commercial systems.*
> - **CO3:** *Evaluate the financial and technical feasibility of energy matching interventions.*

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Energy Matching Hierarchy (4-Tier Model)

Energy matching is not a single action — it is a **hierarchical decision-making process** applied in sequence:

**Tier 1 — Eliminate Need**
- Can the task be eliminated entirely? (e.g., remove unnecessary illumination, stop unnecessary pumping)
- Concept: *Demand reduction at source*

**Tier 2 — Reduce Need**
- Use efficient process design (insulation, heat recovery, variable speed)
- Concept: *Minimization of useful work required*

**Tier 3 — Match Form**
- Use the *cheapest, most appropriate* form of energy
- Concept: *Cascade energy use, fuel substitution, renewable integration*

**Tier 4 — Match Quantity & Timing**
- Right-size equipment, use controls, use storage
- Concept: *Just-in-time energy delivery*

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often award marks for recognizing the *hierarchy*. Always state the tier before proposing a specific solution.

### 2.2 The Logic of Mismatch Identification (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Quantify Actual Demand ($D_{actual}$)**
Measure the true energy required for the task using:
- Direct measurement (kWh, kcal, m³ gas)
- Process simulation
- Manufacturer's data at design load

**Step 2 — Quantify Actual Supply ($S_{actual}$)**
Measure what is being delivered by the system.

**Step 3 — Compute Mismatch Ratio ($MR$)**
$$MR = \frac{S_{actual}}{D_{actual}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the Ratio**

| Mismatch Ratio (MR) | Interpretation | Action |
|---|---|---|
| $MR = 1.0$ | Perfect match | Maintain, monitor |
| $MR > 1.2$ | Over-supply (>20% excess) | Right-size, install controls |
| $MR < 0.9$ | Under-supply (<10% deficit) | Upgrade capacity |
| $MR \gg 1$ with low load factor | Severe inefficiency | Replace with modulated system |

**Step 5 — Compute Load Factor ($LF$)**
$$LF = \frac{\text{Actual Energy Consumed}}{\text{Installed Capacity} \times \text{Time Period}} = \frac{E_{actual}}{P_{installed} \times t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Standard:** A load factor below **0.5 (50%)** is considered *inadequate matching* and is a typical benchmark used in past KTU university exam questions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{input} = E_{useful} + E_{losses}$ | First Law Energy Balance | kWh, MJ, kcal |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{input}} \times 100$ | Energy Efficiency | % |
| 3 | $MR = \dfrac{S_{actual}}{D_{actual}}$ | Mismatch Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $LF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{P_{installed} \times t}$ | Load Factor | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $\Delta E = S_{actual} - D_{actual}$ | Excess Energy | kWh |
| 6 | $\text{Savings} = \Delta E \times C_{energy}$ | Monetary Savings | ₹ (or currency) |
| 7 | $P_{actual} = P_{rated} \times LF$ | Actual Power Demand | kW |
| 8 | $PF = \cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S} = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}}$ | Power Factor | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $PF_{correction} = \tan\left[\cos^{-1}(PF_1) - \cos^{-1}(PF_2)\right]$ | Required kVAR for PF improvement | kVAR |
| 10 | $\text{Peak Demand Charge} = P_{max} \times R_{demand}$ | Demand-side cost | ₹/kW |
| 11 | $\text{TOU Savings} = E_{shifted} \times (C_{peak} - C_{off-peak})$ | Time-of-use savings | ₹ |
| 12 | $Q_{recovered} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Waste heat available for recovery | kW, kJ/h |
| 13 | $COP = \dfrac{Q_{cooling}}{W_{input}}$ | Coefficient of Performance (HVAC) | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **No vertical pipes (`|`) used in formulas to preserve markdown table integrity.** All absolute values are written as `abs(x)`, all ratios use $\vert$ or $\mid$ inside LaTeX.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Motors:** Right-sizing electric motors (a 75% loaded motor operates at ~94% efficiency; a 25% loaded motor drops to ~80–85%).
- **Boilers:** Avoid oversized boilers — flame stability suffers, cycling losses increase.
- **Compressed Air:** Pressure matching (lowering pressure from 7 bar to 4 bar saves ~6–8% energy).
- **HVAC:** Temperature matching (each 1°C reduction in cooling setpoint saves ~3–6% energy).
- **Pumps:** Affinity laws for variable speed drives.
- **Lighting:** Lux level matching (excessive illuminance wastes energy).

### 2.5 The Power Factor (PF) Connection to Energy Matching

**Power Factor** is a critical dimension of energy matching on the electrical side. A low PF means the system *draws* more apparent power (kVA) than it *uses* (kW). The supply must therefore be *larger* than the actual requirement — a classic mismatch.

$$S = \frac{P}{PF}, \quad \text{where } S = \text{Apparent Power (kVA)}, P = \text{Real Power (kW)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Standard:** Improving PF from **0.75 to 0.95** can reduce apparent power demand by **~21%** and eliminate PF penalty charges from utilities.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Energy Mismatch to Annual Cost Savings

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU Board Type):**
A factory has an installed boiler of capacity **500 kg/h** of steam. Actual measured demand is **280 kg/h** of steam, operating **16 hours/day, 300 days/year**. The boiler efficiency is **65%** on full load but drops to **55%** on part load. Fuel cost is **₹8/kg** of fuel (calorific value = **42 MJ/kg**). Latent heat of steam = **2,260 kJ/kg**.

**Step 1 — Energy Required for Actual Demand**

The enthalpy of saturated steam (approx.) at working pressure $\approx 2{,}260$ kJ/kg. Feed water enthalpy (assumed at 80°C) $\approx 335$ kJ/kg.

Therefore, the energy required per kg of steam $\approx 2{,}260 - 335 = 1{,}925$ kJ/kg.

$$Q_{useful} = \dot{m}_{steam} \times (h_{steam} - h_{water})$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{useful} &= 280 \, \text{kg/h} \times 1{,}925 \, \text{kJ/kg} \\
Q_{useful} &= 539{,}000 \, \text{kJ/h} \\
Q_{useful} &= 539 \, \text{MJ/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Actual Fuel Consumption at Part-Load Efficiency**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Fuel Input} &= \frac{Q_{useful}}{\eta_{partload}} \\
\text{Fuel Input} &= \frac{539 \, \text{MJ/h}}{0.55} \\
\text{Fuel Input} &= 980 \, \text{MJ/h}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to kg of fuel (using calorific value of 42 MJ/kg):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Fuel Mass Rate} &= \frac{980}{42} = 23.33 \, \text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Annual Fuel Consumption**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{annual} &= 23.33 \, \text{kg/h} \times 16 \, \text{h/day} \times 300 \, \text{day/yr} \\
E_{annual} &= 111{,}984 \, \text{kg/yr} \approx 112 \, \text{tonnes/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Annual Fuel Cost**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{annual} &= 111{,}984 \, \text{kg} \times ₹8/\text{kg} \\
\text{Cost}_{annual} &= ₹8{,}95{,}872 \, \text{per year} \\
\text{Cost}_{annual} &\approx ₹8.96 \, \text{Lakhs/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Hypothetical Consumption If Boiler Were Right-Sized (Efficiency = 65%)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Fuel at Full-Load Efficiency} &= \frac{539}{0.65} = 829.23 \, \text{MJ/h} \\
\text{Fuel Mass Rate} &= \frac{829.23}{42} = 19.74 \, \text{kg/h} \\
E_{annual,\,optimal} &= 19.74 \times 16 \times 300 = 94{,}752 \, \text{kg/yr} \\
\text{Cost}_{optimal} &= 94{,}752 \times 8 = ₹7{,}58{,}016
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Annual Savings from Right-Sizing (Matching Energy to Requirement)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= ₹8{,}95{,}872 - ₹7{,}58{,}016 \\
\text{Savings} &= ₹1{,}37{,}856 \, \text{per year} \\
\text{Savings} &\approx ₹1.38 \, \text{Lakhs/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Mismatch Ratio and Load Factor**

$$\begin{aligned}
MR &= \frac{S_{actual}}{D_{actual}} = \frac{500}{280} = 1.786 \\
LF &= \frac{280}{500} = 0.56 \;(56\%)
\end{aligned}$$

This $LF = 0.56$ is **borderline acceptable** but explains the efficiency drop. A right-sized boiler (e.g., 350 kg/h) would operate at $LF \approx 0.80$, optimizing efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The 10-percentage-point efficiency drop (65% → 55%) on part load is a *direct consequence* of mismatch — and the ₹1.38 Lakh/yr savings represents pure waste from non-matching.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Mismatch Categories and Remedies

| Mismatch Type | Diagnostic Indicator | Typical Cause | Standard Remedy | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Quantity Mismatch (Oversize)** | $LF < 0.5$, cycling on/off | Poor load estimation, safety factor overuse | Right-size, VSD, modulation | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Quantity Mismatch (Undersize)** | Frequent overload trips | Load growth not planned | Upgrade capacity, add unit | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Form Mismatch** | Low $\eta$ despite high $LF$ | Wrong energy form used | Cascade, fuel substitution | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Timing Mismatch** | Peak demand charges high | All loads at once | Load shifting, TOU tariffs, storage | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Quality Mismatch (Electrical)** | $PF < 0.85$ | Inductive loads dominant | Capacitor banks, synchronous motors | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Thermal Mismatch** | Stack temperature > 200°C | Excess air, poor heat transfer | Air preheater, economizer | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Pressure Mismatch** | Pressure reducing valves throttling | Fixed-speed pumps/compressors | VSD, pressure optimization | ⭐⭐⭐ |

### 3.3 KTU Standard Numerical: Affinity Law Application

**Setup:** A centrifugal pump delivers **Q₁ = 100 m³/h** at **H₁ = 30 m** head running at **N₁ = 1450 rpm** with input power **P₁ = 15 kW**. Process requires only **70% of original flow**.

**Affinity Laws:**

$$Q \propto N, \quad H \propto N^2, \quad P \propto N^3$$

**Step 1 — Required Speed for 70% Flow**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} &= \frac{N_2}{N_1} \\
N_2 &= 0.70 \times 1450 = 1015 \, \text{rpm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — New Head**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{H_2}{H_1} &= \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \\
H_2 &= 30 \times (0.70)^2 = 14.7 \, \text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — New Power (if pump is throttled to 70% flow but runs at full speed)**

If throttled, power drops roughly proportional to flow × head:

$$P_{throttled} \approx 15 \times 0.70 \times (0.70)^2 = 5.15 \, \text{kW}$$ (Approximation)

If VSD applied (true affinity):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{VSD} &= P_1 \times \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 \\
P_{VSD} &= 15 \times (0.70)^3 \\
P_{VSD} &= 15 \times 0.343 \\
P_{VSD} &= 5.145 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Annual Energy & Cost Savings (assume 6000 operating hours, ₹7/kWh)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Energy at full speed (throttled)} &\approx 15 \times 6000 = 90{,}000 \, \text{kWh} \\
\text{Energy with VSD} &= 5.145 \times 6000 = 30{,}870 \, \text{kWh} \\
\text{Savings (kWh)} &= 90{,}000 - 30{,}870 = 59{,}130 \, \text{kWh/yr} \\
\text{Savings (₹)} &= 59{,}130 \times 7 = ₹4{,}13{,}910 \, \text{per year}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Affinity law derivation is a **14-mark** standard problem. Always show all three proportionalities and clearly distinguish between throttling and VSD.

### 3.4 Quick-Reference Decision Tree (Text Form)

1. Is the system oversized? → Check $LF$. If $LF < 0.6$, consider right-sizing or modulation.
2. Is the form of energy appropriate? → Compare primary energy cost per useful MJ.
3. Is the timing correct? → Plot 24-hour load profile vs. tariff.
4. Is PF adequate? → If $PF < 0.9$, calculate capacitor rating using the PF correction formula.
5. Is there waste heat? → Apply $Q = m c_p \Delta T$ to check recovery potential.
