## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define the term "Matching Energy Use to Requirement" and list any four parameters that characterize this concept.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Definition (1.5 marks):* Matching energy use to requirement is the principle of ensuring that the quantity, form, quality, and timing of energy supplied to a system is precisely aligned with the actual demand, minimizing wastage due to over-supply, under-supply, or wrong energy form.

*Four Parameters (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):*
1. **Quantity** — Magnitude of energy delivered (kW, kg/h, m³/h).
2. **Form** — Type of energy (electrical, thermal, mechanical, chemical).
3. **Timing** — Time-of-use alignment with availability and tariffs.
4. **Quality/Intensity** — Pressure, temperature, voltage, power factor.

---

#### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: A pump operates at 30% of its rated load continuously. State with justification whether this represents a mismatch and the action recommended.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Yes, this is a mismatch (1 mark).* The load factor of 0.30 is significantly below the optimal operating range of 0.70–0.90. At this low loading, the pump efficiency drops substantially, and fixed losses (friction, windage) become disproportionately large.

*Justification (1 mark):* The energy supplied is far in excess of useful work, with most input energy dissipated as losses.

*Recommended Action (1 mark):* Install a **Variable Speed Drive (VSD)** to modulate pump speed, or replace with a right-sized pump to bring load factor into the 0.70–0.90 range.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Part B questions are structured as **ESE Module Internal Choice** — students must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each Part B carries **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 marks each**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A. (a) [7 Marks]** A factory has installed a 200 kW electric boiler for process heating, but actual demand is only 110 kW. The boiler operates 18 hours/day for 300 days/year. The boiler efficiency at full load is 82%, but at 55% load it drops to 70%. Electricity cost is ₹6.50/kWh. Calculate:
(i) Annual energy consumption under current mismatch.
(ii) Annual energy consumption if boiler were right-sized (or fitted with proportional control).
(iii) Annual cost savings.

**A. (b) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat diagram the **four-tier hierarchy of energy matching interventions** and illustrate with one industrial example for each tier.

---

### **Complete Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Installed capacity: $P_{installed} = 200$ kW
- Actual demand: $P_{actual} = 110$ kW
- Operating hours: 18 h/day × 300 days = 5,400 h/yr
- Full-load efficiency: $\eta_{FL} = 0.82$
- Part-load efficiency: $\eta_{PL} = 0.70$ (at 55% loading)
- Electricity cost: ₹6.50/kWh

**Step 1 — Annual Energy Consumption at Current Mismatch**

The boiler consumes electrical input of 200 kW (since it cannot modulate and is fixed at full capacity input), but only delivers 110 kW of useful heat at part-load efficiency.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{input, current} &= P_{installed} \times t \\
&= 200 \, \text{kW} \times 5{,}400 \, \text{h} \\
&= 1{,}080{,}000 \, \text{kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Annual Cost (Current):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{current} &= 1{,}080{,}000 \times 6.50 \\
&= ₹70{,}20{,}000 \approx ₹70.2 \, \text{Lakhs/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Annual Energy Consumption with Right-Sized Boiler (or Modulating Control)**

If the boiler is properly matched, the input equals the output demand at the corresponding efficiency. Since the right-sized boiler would operate near its full-load efficiency (assume 82%):

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{input, optimal} &= \frac{P_{actual}}{\eta_{FL}} \times t \\
&= \frac{110}{0.82} \times 5{,}400 \\
&= 134.15 \times 5{,}400 \\
&= 724{,}390 \, \text{kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Annual Cost (Optimal):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost}_{optimal} &= 724{,}390 \times 6.50 \\
&= ₹47{,}08{,}535 \approx ₹47.1 \, \text{Lakhs/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Annual Cost Savings from Energy Matching**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= ₹70{,}20{,}000 - ₹47{,}08{,}535 \\
&= ₹23{,}11{,}465 \approx ₹23.11 \, \text{Lakhs/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Load Factor & Mismatch Ratio**

$$\begin{aligned}
LF &= \frac{P_{actual}}{P_{installed}} = \frac{110}{200} = 0.55 \;(55\%) \\
MR &= \frac{200}{110} = 1.82
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Markers' Note:** A $LF$ of 0.55 is a textbook *mismatch case*. Full marks require showing both energy AND cost calculations, plus the LF/MR diagnostic.

---

#### **Part (b) Solution:**

**The Four-Tier Hierarchy of Energy Matching Interventions:**

**Tier 1 — Eliminate Need (1.5 marks):**
Eliminate unnecessary energy consumption at the source, before any supply consideration.
- *Example:* Switching off lights, ventilation, and conveyor belts during non-production hours in a factory; de-energizing idle transformers.

**Tier 2 — Reduce Need (1.5 marks):**
Reduce the magnitude of energy required to perform the same task through design efficiency.
- *Example:* Insulating steam pipelines to reduce heat loss; using LED lighting instead of incandescent; installing variable speed drives on fans.

**Tier 3 — Match Form (2 marks):**
Use the lowest-grade energy form that can still accomplish the task (cascade principle).
- *Example:* Using waste heat from a furnace to preheat combustion air (recuperator) instead of burning extra fuel; using solar thermal instead of electric heating for water heating.

**Tier 4 — Match Quantity and Timing (2 marks):**
Right-size equipment and align energy use with tariff periods and grid demand.
- *Example:* Installing a VFD on a pump to match flow exactly; shifting ice-making load to off-peak hours; using capacitor banks to correct power factor.

**Diagram (sketch description for 1 mark):**

The student is expected to draw a pyramid showing:
```
        Tier 1: Eliminate (Top)
       /  \
      / Tier 2: Reduce \
     /___________________\
    |  Tier 3: Match Form  |
    |______________________|
   | Tier 4: Match Quantity  |
   |___& Timing (Bottom)____|
```

> [!NOTE]
> The four-tier hierarchy is essentially the **"hierarchy of energy efficiency"** advocated by the **CII – Godrej Green Business Centre** and is a commonly cited framework in Indian energy management practice.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**B. (a) [7 Marks]** A 3-phase induction motor rated 30 kW operates at 60% loading (i.e., delivers 18 kW shaft output) for 4,000 hours/year. The motor's power factor at 60% load is 0.78, lagging. The supply authority penalizes consumers whose PF falls below 0.95. Calculate:
(i) The reactive power (kVAR) drawn by the motor.
(ii) The capacitor rating (kVAR) required to improve PF to 0.95.
(iii) The annual kWh loss reduction in the supply cable (assume cable resistance = 0.05 Ω per phase).

**B. (b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a labeled diagram, describe the **Power Factor Correction method using capacitor banks** and explain its role in *matching electrical energy to requirement*.

---

### **Complete Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Motor rating: $P_{rated} = 30$ kW
- Actual shaft output: $P_{output} = 18$ kW
- Operating hours: 4,000 h/yr
- Assumed motor efficiency at 60% load: $\eta_m = 0.88$ (standard)
- Power factor (existing): $PF_1 = 0.78$ lagging
- Target power factor: $PF_2 = 0.95$ lagging
- Cable resistance per phase: $R = 0.05 \, \Omega$

**Step 1 — Real Power Input to Motor**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= \frac{P_{output}}{\eta_m} = \frac{18}{0.88} = 20.45 \, \text{kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apparent Power at Existing PF**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \frac{P_{in}}{PF_1} = \frac{20.45}{0.78} = 26.22 \, \text{kVA}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Reactive Power Drawn by Motor (Q₁)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\phi_1 &= 0.78 \Rightarrow \phi_1 = 38.7^\circ \\
\tan\phi_1 &= 0.802 \\
Q_1 &= P_{in} \times \tan\phi_1 \\
Q_1 &= 20.45 \times 0.802 = 16.40 \, \text{kVAR}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Capacitor kVAR Required (Q_c)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\cos\phi_2 &= 0.95 \Rightarrow \phi_2 = 18.2^\circ \\
\tan\phi_2 &= 0.329 \\
Q_2 &= P_{in} \times \tan\phi_2 = 20.45 \times 0.329 = 6.73 \, \text{kVAR} \\
Q_c &= Q_1 - Q_2 = 16.40 - 6.73 = 9.67 \, \text{kVAR}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative formula (commonly used in KTU answers):**
> $$Q_c = P_{in} \left[ \tan(\cos^{-1} PF_1) - \tan(\cos^{-1} PF_2) \right]$$
> $$Q_c = 20.45 \times [0.802 - 0.329] = 20.45 \times 0.473 = 9.67 \, \text{kVAR}$$

**Step 5 — New Apparent Power**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_2 &= \frac{P_{in}}{PF_2} = \frac{20.45}{0.95} = 21.53 \, \text{kVA}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Cable Loss Reduction**

For 3-phase cable losses:
$$P_{loss} = 3 \times I^2 \times R, \quad I = \frac{S}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L}$$

Assuming $V_L = 415$ V (standard Indian industrial supply):

$$\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= \frac{26.22 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = 36.49 \, \text{A} \\
I_2 &= \frac{21.53 \times 1000}{\sqrt{3} \times 415} = 29.96 \, \text{A} \\
P_{loss,1} &= 3 \times (36.49)^2 \times 0.05 = 199.7 \, \text{W} \\
P_{loss,2} &= 3 \times (29.96)^2 \times 0.05 = 134.6 \, \text{W} \\
\Delta P_{loss} &= 199.7 - 134.6 = 65.1 \, \text{W}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Annual Energy Loss Reduction**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta E &= 65.1 \, \text{W} \times 4{,}000 \, \text{h/yr} \\
\Delta E &= 260.4 \, \text{kWh/yr}
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Part (b) Solution:**

**Power Factor Correction Using Capacitor Banks — Diagram & Explanation:**

*Block Diagram Description:*

```
   [3-Phase AC Supply] 
         |
         | (Line)
         |
   ----[kWh Meter]----
   |                  |
   |                  |
[Inductive Loads]  [Capacitor Bank]   
(Motors, Transformers)  (Static or Automatic)
   |                  |
   |__________________|
         |
      [PF Improvement: 0.78 → 0.95]
         |
   [Reduced Line Current]
         |
   [Reduced I²R Losses]
         |
   [Lower kVA Demand]
         |
   [Lower Electricity Bills & No PF Penalty]
```

**Role in Matching Energy to Requirement (7 marks breakdown):**

1. **Concept (2 marks):** Capacitor banks supply leading reactive power that *cancels* part of the lagging reactive power demanded by inductive loads. The net reactive power drawn from the grid is reduced, and since $P$ (real work) is unchanged but $Q$ is reduced, the apparent power $S$ also reduces. The system now draws *exactly* the kVA it needs, not excess.

2. **Mechanism (2 marks):** $S = \sqrt{P^2 + Q^2}$. With $Q$ reduced via capacitive compensation, $S$ decreases. Current $I = S / (\sqrt{3} V)$ decreases proportionally. Lower current means lower $I^2R$ losses in cables and transformers, and the system capacity is released for useful load.

3. **Matching Principle (2 marks):** The capacitor bank *transforms* the system from one that demands more apparent power than it needs (mismatch) to one that exactly matches its real load. It is, therefore, a direct application of the *Quantity/Quality* dimension of energy matching on the electrical side.

4. **Practical Benefits (1 mark):** Reduced maximum demand charges, eliminated PF penalty, freed transformer/cable capacity, reduced voltage drop, and lower carbon footprint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Markers' Note:** A complete answer should include the formula $Q_c = P[\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2]$ and recognize that PF correction does NOT reduce real power consumption — it only reduces apparent power and losses.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Efficiency Drop with Mismatch (–2 marks):** Students often calculate the part-load efficiency drop but fail to identify it as a *consequence of mismatch* (i.e., the equipment being oversized). Always state the *cause-effect link*.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units in Final Answer (–1 mark):** KTU examiners *strictly deduct* marks if units (kWh, ₹/year, kVAR) are missing or inconsistent.
>
> 3. **Mixing Up PF Correction with Energy Saving (–2 marks):** PF correction reduces *apparent power* (kVA) and *cable losses*, but does NOT reduce the *real power* (kW) consumed by the motor. Stating "PF correction saves motor energy" is wrong.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Diagnostic Step (–1 to –2 marks):** In a 14-mark question, jumping straight to the formula without computing *Mismatch Ratio* and *Load Factor* first is considered incomplete analysis.
>
> 5. **Incorrect Affinity Law Application (–3 marks):** When a pump/compressor/fan load is reduced to X%, the power consumed drops as $X^3$ (cube law), NOT linearly. Mixing up $N, N^2, N^3$ relationships is a major error.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Time-of-Use (TOU) Tariffs (–2 marks):** When asked about "matching timing," students often forget to factor in the *peak vs. off-peak* tariff differential, which is the entire point of time-matching.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Core Equation:** $E_{input} = E_{useful} + E_{losses}$ — first law of energy matching.
- ✅ **Mismatch Ratio (MR):** $MR = S_{actual} / D_{actual}$; optimal $MR \approx 1.0$.
- ✅ **Load Factor (LF):** $LF = E_{actual} / (P_{installed} \times t)$; ideal $LF \geq 0.7$.
- ✅ **Four Dimensions of Matching:** Quantity, Form, Timing, Quality (PF).
- ✅ **Four-Tier Hierarchy:** Eliminate → Reduce → Match Form → Match Quantity & Timing.
- ✅ **Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$ — speed reduction gives cubic power savings.
- ✅ **PF Correction Formula:** $Q_c = P[\tan(\cos^{-1} PF_1) - \tan(\cos^{-1} PF_2)]$.
- ✅ **Power Factor 0.78 → 0.95:** Reduces apparent power by ~21%, eliminates PF penalty.
- ✅ **TOU Tariff Strategy:** Shift flexible loads (HVAC, ice-making, pumping) to off-peak hours.
- ✅ **Waste Heat Recovery Equation:** $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ — quantifies recoverable thermal energy.
- ✅ **Boiler Mismatch Symptom:** $LF < 0.6$ with efficiency drop of 5–15 percentage points.
- ✅ **Motor Mismatch Symptom:** Operating at <50% rated load with PF < 0.80.
- ✅ **Standard KTU Board Benchmarks:** $LF_{critical} = 0.5$; $PF_{penalty} = 0.85$; $\eta_{acceptable} \geq 0.80$.
- ✅ **Economic Justification Rule of Thumb:** Payback period for energy matching investments < 2–3 years is considered attractive.
- ✅ **Cascade Energy Use:** High-grade energy for high-grade tasks; reject heat used for preheating, space heating, etc.
- ✅ **Always Link Cause to Effect:** Identify the *mismatch type* before recommending a *remedy* (Examiner's mantra: "Diagnosis first, prescription second").

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Tip for KTU PESFT414 Aspirants:** This topic is *invariably* tested in either **Part A (3 marks)** as a definition/diagnostic question OR in **Part B (14 marks)** as a numerical on right-sizing, affinity laws, or PF correction. Master the **MR, LF, and Affinity Law** formulas cold — they account for over 70% of marks on this topic.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maximizing System Efficiencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Maximizing System Efficiencies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Efficiency Maximization (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> *Maximizing System Efficiencies* refers to the systematic engineering and managerial methodology of identifying, analyzing, and optimizing the energy conversion, transmission, and utilization processes across all major energy-consuming subsystems in an industrial or commercial facility, with the objective of minimizing specific energy consumption (SEC) while maintaining or improving the quality, reliability, and productivity of the output.

The **First Law Efficiency** ($\eta_1$) and **Second Law (Exergetic) Efficiency** ($\eta_2$) provide the fundamental framework for assessing how well a system converts input energy into useful work or energy forms. Maximizing this efficiency involves reducing all forms of losses — **frictional**, **thermal**, **electrical ($I^2R$)**, **magnetic (core & stray)**, **mechanical**, and **radiative**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned), this topic sits at the intersection of **Energy Auditing**, **Process Integration**, and **Sustainable Engineering** under the broader umbrella of *Module 2: Energy Efficiency in Industrial Systems*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a Water Pipeline with Holes:**
> Imagine you are pumping water (energy) from a reservoir (source) to a tank (useful output). If the pipe is rusty, has multiple leakages, narrow bends, and an undersized pump, very little water reaches the tank. **Maximizing System Efficiency** is exactly like replacing that old pipe — fixing the leaks (eliminating waste), widening the bends (reducing friction), and using a right-sized pump (proper equipment sizing). The amount of water reaching the tank (useful output) increases dramatically, while the energy spent at the pump (input) decreases. The **net result**: the same task is accomplished with far less effort, saving both money and resources.

In simpler words: **Don't just consume less — consume smartly.** A leaky system working at 30% efficiency wastes **2.33 times** the energy it actually uses productively.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standardized metrics are universally used across KTU board examinations and industry practice:

- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Energy used per unit of output (e.g., $\text{kWh/ton}$, $\text{MJ/kg}$, $\text{kcal/kg}$).
- **Performance Ratio (PR):** Dimensionless ratio of actual output to theoretical maximum output.
- **Standard Reference:** **Standard Reference Temperature** $T_0 = 25^\circ\text{C} = 298.15\,\text{K}$.
- **Standard Mechanical Equivalent of Heat:** **$J = 4186\,\text{J/kcal}$** or equivalently **$1\,\text{kWh} = 860\,\text{kcal} = 3.6\,\text{MJ}$**.
- **Universal Gas Constant:** **$R_u = 8.314\,\text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$**.
- **Power Factor Target:** **$\cos\phi \geq 0.95$** (industrial best practice).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Tier Hierarchy of Efficiency Maximization

Maximizing system efficiencies follows a structured engineering hierarchy that KTU examiners frequently test in ESE question papers:

**Tier 1 — Housekeeping & Low-Cost Measures:**
- Switching off idle equipment (avoid *no-load losses*).
- Proper lubrication to reduce friction.
- Tightening of belts, alignment of shafts.
- Cleaning of heat exchanger surfaces, filters, and coils.

**Tier 2 — Retrofit & Intermediate-Cost Measures:**
- Replacement of standard motors with **IE3/IE4 Premium Efficiency Motors**.
- Installation of **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)**.
- Use of high-efficiency lamps (LED replacing HID/MV lamps).
- Power factor correction using capacitor banks.
- Heat recovery systems (exhaust → preheating).

**Tier 3 — System Redesign & Capital-Intensive Measures:**
- Cogeneration (Combined Heat and Power — CHP).
- Trigeneration (CHP + cooling).
- Pinch analysis and process integration.
- Replacement of entire process lines.

### 2.2 Operational Concept Breakdown

**Step 1 — Energy Baseline Establishment**
The Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) is established for every major system using:

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{\text{Total Energy Input to System}}{\text{Useful Output of System}}$$

**Step 2 — Loss Quantification**
Losses are quantified category-wise:

$$\eta_{\text{system}} = 1 - \frac{\sum L_i}{E_{\text{input}}}$$

where $\sum L_i$ represents the sum of all identified losses.

**Step 3 — Benchmarking**
The system efficiency is compared against **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE)** star ratings or international ISO standards.

**Step 4 — Implementation**
Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) are implemented based on **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** analysis.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Formula Reference — Memorize this table.**

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula | Unit / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First Law Efficiency | $\eta_1 = \dfrac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}}$ | $\eta_1 \in [0, 1]$, dimensionless |
| 2 | Second Law (Exergy) Efficiency | $\eta_2 = \dfrac{\text{Exergy}_{\text{out}}}{\text{Exergy}_{\text{in}}}$ | $\eta_2 \in [0, 1]$ |
| 3 | Specific Energy Consumption | $\text{SEC} = \dfrac{E_{\text{in}}}{\text{Output}}$ | kWh/ton, MJ/kg |
| 4 | Motor Slip | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | $s \in [0.02, 0.06]$ for full load |
| 5 | Synchronous Speed | $N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P}$ | RPM; $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$ in India |
| 6 | Three-Phase Power | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | Watts |
| 7 | Pump Affinity Law (Flow) | $Q_2 / Q_1 = (N_2 / N_1)$ | Linear scaling |
| 8 | Pump Affinity Law (Head) | $H_2 / H_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^2$ | Quadratic scaling |
| 9 | Pump Affinity Law (Power) | $P_2 / P_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^3$ | Cubic scaling |
| 10 | Fan Law (Power) | $P_2 / P_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^3$ | Cubic scaling |
| 11 | Lighting Luminous Efficacy | $\eta_{\text{lum}} = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{P}$ | lm/W |
| 12 | Cost of Wasted Energy | $C_w = P_w \cdot h \cdot r$ | Rs/year |
| 13 | Simple Payback Period | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{\text{Capital Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}}$ | Years |
| 14 | Net Present Value | $\text{NPV} = \sum \dfrac{CF_t}{(1+i)^t} - C_0$ | Currency units |
| 15 | Boiler Efficiency (Direct) | $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$ | \% |

> **Note:** Whenever you see absolute-value bars in your calculations, write them as $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\vert x \vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table structure shown above.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Mechanical Engineering:** Motor-driven systems (pumps, fans, compressors) consume **~60-70%** of industrial electricity. Affinity laws are used to optimize pump and fan operation.
- **Electrical Engineering:** Power factor correction reduces line losses by $\text{Loss} \propto (1/\cos^2\phi)$.
- **Civil Engineering:** HVAC optimization in green buildings (GRIHA, LEED certification).
- **Process Industries:** Heat integration via pinch analysis reduces external heating/cooling demand by up to **40%**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Motor Slip vs. Efficiency Trade-off

For a three-phase induction motor, the rotor copper losses ($P_{cu2}$) relate to air-gap power ($P_g$) through slip:

$$P_{cu2} = s \cdot P_g$$

The total input power to the rotor circuit is the air-gap power $P_g$. The mechanical power developed is:

$$P_m = (1 - s) \cdot P_g$$

Therefore, the **rotor efficiency** (ratio of mechanical power to air-gap power) is:

$$\eta_{\text{rotor}} = \frac{P_m}{P_g} = \frac{(1 - s) \cdot P_g}{P_g}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\text{rotor}} = 1 - s}$$

> **Conversion logic:** This derivation shows that **rotor efficiency is directly controlled by slip**. Since slip at full load is typically 3–5%, rotor efficiency reaches 95–97%. However, at light loads (small $s$ in denominator, large slip proportion), efficiency drops drastically.

### 3.2 Derivation: Cubic Power Law for Pumps/Fans (Affinity Law)

Consider a centrifugal pump operating at speed $N_1$ with discharge $Q_1$, head $H_1$, and power $P_1$. When the operating speed is changed to $N_2$, dimensional analysis yields three scaling laws.

**Step 1 — Flow rate $Q$** scales linearly with impeller peripheral speed $\propto N$ and area $\propto D^2$. Since $D$ is constant:

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

**Step 2 — Head $H$** scales with the square of peripheral velocity (kinetic energy $\propto v^2 \propto N^2$):

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2$$

**Step 3 — Power $P$** is the product $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$. Substituting:

$$P \propto N \cdot N^2 = N^3$$

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$$

> **Conversion logic:** This is the **single most important KTU 2024 derivation** for Module 2. It tells us that reducing pump speed by **20%** (e.g., from 100% to 80% via VFD) reduces flow by 20%, head by 36%, but power consumption by **49%**. This is why VFDs are prioritized in energy audits.

### 3.3 Derivation: Power Factor Correction and Line Loss Reduction

Line losses in a three-phase system are given by:

$$P_{\text{loss}} = 3 \cdot I^2 \cdot R = 3 \cdot \left(\frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi}\right)^2 \cdot R$$

$$P_{\text{loss}} = \frac{P^2 \cdot R}{V_L^2 \cdot \cos^2\phi}$$

Letting $K = \dfrac{P^2 \cdot R}{V_L^2}$ as a constant, we have:

$$P_{\text{loss}} = \frac{K}{\cos^2\phi}$$

The ratio of losses before ($P_{loss,1}$) and after ($P_{loss,2}$) power factor correction is:

$$\frac{P_{loss,2}}{P_{loss,1}} = \left(\frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}\right)^2$$

> **Conversion logic:** Improving the power factor from **0.7 to 0.95** reduces line losses by a factor of $\left(\dfrac{0.7}{0.95}\right)^2 = 0.543$, i.e., a **45.7% reduction** in $I^2R$ losses — purely by adding a capacitor bank, with no change in useful power delivered.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Model Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 15 kW, 400 V, 3-phase induction motor operates at full load with efficiency 88%, power factor 0.78 lagging. A capacitor bank improves the power factor to 0.95 lagging. Calculate: (a) the reduction in line current, (b) the reduction in line losses assuming line resistance $R = 0.4\,\Omega$ per phase.

**Given Data:**
- $P_{\text{out}} = 15\,\text{kW}$, $\eta_1 = 0.88$, $\cos\phi_1 = 0.78$, $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95$, $V_L = 400\,\text{V}$, $R = 0.4\,\Omega$.

**Step (a) — Input Power and Current Reduction:**

$$P_{\text{in}} = \frac{P_{\text{out}}}{\eta_1} = \frac{15{,}000}{0.88} = 17{,}045.45\,\text{W}$$

Before correction ($I_{L1}$):

$$I_{L1} = \frac{P_{\text{in}}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi_1} = \frac{17{,}045.45}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 0.78}$$

$$I_{L1} = \frac{17{,}045.45}{540.46} = 31.54\,\text{A}$$

After correction ($I_{L2}$):

$$I_{L2} = \frac{17{,}045.45}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 0.95} = \frac{17{,}045.45}{658.18} = 25.90\,\text{A}$$

Current reduction:

$$\Delta I = 31.54 - 25.90 = 5.64\,\text{A}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta I_{\%} = \frac{5.64}{31.54} \times 100 = 17.88\%}$$

**Step (b) — Line Loss Reduction:**

Losses before:

$$P_{loss,1} = 3 \cdot I_{L1}^2 \cdot R = 3 \cdot (31.54)^2 \cdot 0.4$$

$$P_{loss,1} = 3 \cdot 994.77 \cdot 0.4 = 1193.72\,\text{W}$$

Losses after:

$$P_{loss,2} = 3 \cdot (25.90)^2 \cdot 0.4 = 3 \cdot 670.81 \cdot 0.4 = 804.97\,\text{W}$$

Loss reduction:

$$\Delta P_{\text{loss}} = 1193.72 - 804.97 = 388.75\,\text{W}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{\text{loss}, \%} = \frac{388.75}{1193.72} \times 100 = 32.57\%}$$

> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Notice the elegant consistency check — the loss reduction should equal $1 - (\cos\phi_1 / \cos\phi_2)^2 = 1 - (0.78/0.95)^2 = 1 - 0.6743 = 32.57\%$. ✓ **Matches exactly.** This validates both derivations.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Efficiency Maximization Strategies by System

| System | Dominant Loss Mechanism | Primary ECM (Energy Conservation Measure) | Typical Efficiency Gain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Induction Motor | Rotor copper, iron, stray, friction | Replace with IE3/IE4 + VFD | 5–15% |
| Centrifugal Pump | Throttling, hydraulic losses | VFD + impeller trimming | 20–50% |
| Centrifugal Fan | Damper losses, system resistance | VFD + blade angle optimization | 20–40% |
| Boiler | Stack gas, radiation, blowdown | Economizer, air preheater | 8–12% |
| Compressed Air | Leakage, pressure drop, heat | Leak repair, pressure reduction | 20–30% |
| HVAC System | Part-load inefficiency | VAV (Variable Air Volume), EC fans | 15–25% |
| Lighting | Lamp efficacy losses | LED retrofit + occupancy sensors | 50–70% |
| Steam System | Condensate, flash steam loss | Condensate recovery, insulation | 10–20% |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of System Efficiency Maximization

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the engineering workflow recommended in the **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 — Energy Audit Methodology** as applied to *Maximizing System Efficiencies*.

```mermaid

    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

```

### 4.2 Energy Loss Mapping in a Typical Industrial Motor-Driven System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electrical Energy Input kWh] --> B[Transformer Losses]

    D --> F[Motor Iron Core Loss]

    J --> K[Hydraulic Losses]

    G --> M
    K --> M
    M --> N[Cooling and Reject]
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting Efficiency Improvement Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Equipment Under Study]) --> Q1{Is Equipment Operating Below Rated Capacity?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, Light Load| Path1[Install VFD and Right Size Motor]
    Q1 -->|No, Full Load| Q2{Are There Throttling Losses?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| Path2[Replace Valve with VFD Control]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Is Power Factor Less Than 0.9?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Path3[Install Capacitor Bank for PFC]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Are There Significant Heat Losses?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Path4[Install Heat Recovery System]
    Q4 -->|No| Path5[Maintain Current Operation and Monitor]
    Path1 --> End([Document ECM Implementation])
    Path2 --> End
    Path3 --> End
    Path4 --> End
    Path5 --> End
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Define the term Specific Energy Consumption (SEC). How is it used as a benchmark for maximizing system efficiency in industrial plants?**

**Model Answer:**
Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) is defined as the **energy input per unit of useful output** produced by a system. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{SEC} = \frac{\text{Total Energy Input to System}}{\text{Useful Output}}$$

SEC serves as a benchmark for maximizing system efficiency in three ways: (i) it enables comparison across similar processes within the same plant, (ii) it allows benchmarking against **BEE standards** and international best practices, and (iii) tracking SEC over time highlights deviations and identifies opportunities for energy conservation measures (ECMs). Lower SEC indicates higher efficiency. **(3 marks)**

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **State and briefly explain the three pump affinity laws. Why are they critical in maximizing pumping system efficiency?**

**Model Answer:**
The three pump affinity laws describe how flow $Q$, head $H$, and power $P$ vary with rotational speed $N$:

1. **Flow Law:** $Q_2 / Q_1 = N_2 / N_1$
2. **Head Law:** $H_2 / H_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^2$
3. **Power Law:** $P_2 / Q_1 = (N_2 / N_1)^3$

These laws are critical because they reveal the **cubic relationship** between power and speed. By reducing pump speed by just 20% via a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD), power consumption reduces by approximately **49%**, while flow drops only by 20%. This enables dramatic efficiency gains in pump systems traditionally controlled by throttling valves that waste energy. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, 14 Marks, ESE Module 2]**
>
> ### **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **(a)** With the aid of neat derivations, explain the three affinity laws as applied to centrifugal pumps. Discuss how Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs) exploit these laws to maximize pumping system efficiency. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A centrifugal pump delivers 50 L/s against a head of 30 m at 1450 RPM. The pump is to be operated at 1200 RPM to meet reduced system demand. Calculate: (i) the new flow rate, (ii) the new head developed, (iii) the percentage reduction in power consumption. Assume pump efficiency remains constant at 75%. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**(a) Affinity Laws & VFD Exploitation — 7 marks:**

Refer to **Section 3.2** above for the complete derivation. Key explanation:

- **Flow Law:** $Q \propto N$ — direct proportionality because volumetric displacement per revolution is constant.
- **Head Law:** $H \propto N^2$ — head depends on kinetic energy imparted to fluid ($\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$).
- **Power Law:** $P \propto N^3$ — since $P = \rho g Q H$, combining both gives cubic dependence.

**VFD Exploitation (2 marks):**
A Variable Frequency Drive adjusts the motor supply frequency, thereby varying synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$. Pumps traditionally use throttling valves to control flow, which wastes energy by creating artificial head. By replacing throttling with VFD speed control, the pump operates on its native curve, eliminating wasteful hydraulic losses. For a 20% speed reduction, power drops by ~49%, yielding enormous energy savings.

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $Q_1 = 50\,\text{L/s}$, $H_1 = 30\,\text{m}$, $N_1 = 1450\,\text{RPM}$, $N_2 = 1200\,\text{RPM}$, $\eta = 0.75$.

**Step (i) New flow rate:**

$$Q_2 = Q_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 50 \cdot \frac{1200}{1450} = 50 \cdot 0.8276 = 41.38\,\text{L/s}$$

**Step (ii) New head:**

$$H_2 = H_1 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 30 \cdot (0.8276)^2 = 30 \cdot 0.6849 = 20.55\,\text{m}$$

**Step (iii) Percentage power reduction:**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = (0.8276)^3 = 0.5668$$

Therefore, percentage reduction:

$$\Delta P_{\%} = (1 - 0.5668) \times 100 = 43.32\%$$

$$\boxed{Q_2 = 41.38\,\text{L/s}, \quad H_2 = 20.55\,\text{m}, \quad \Delta P_{\%} = 43.32\%}$$

> ⚠️ **Validation:** Since $N_2/N_1 = 1200/1450 = 0.8276$, the cubic gives $0.5668$, meaning $56.68\%$ of original power is needed — saving **43.32%**. This is consistent with the cubic affinity law. ✅

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **(a)** What is power factor? Explain how power factor correction using capacitor banks maximizes the efficiency of an electrical distribution system. Derive the relevant loss reduction expression. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 3-phase, 400 V induction motor draws an input power of 12 kW at 0.75 power factor lagging. A delta-connected capacitor bank improves the power factor to 0.95 lagging. Calculate: (i) the line current before and after correction, (ii) the rating of the capacitor bank in kVAr, (iii) the percentage reduction in line losses assuming line resistance of 0.5 Ω per phase. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**(a) Power Factor Correction — 7 marks:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Power factor $\cos\phi$ is the cosine of the phase angle between voltage and current in an AC circuit. It represents the ratio of real (active) power to apparent power.

$$\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{P}{V \cdot I}$$

**Working principle (3 marks):** Induction motors and other inductive loads draw reactive power (kVAr) for magnetization. This reactive current flows through cables without contributing to useful work but causes $I^2R$ losses. Capacitor banks supply reactive power locally, reducing reactive current drawn from the source, thereby reducing total line current.

**Derivation (3 marks):** Refer to **Section 3.3**. Final expression:

$$\frac{P_{loss,2}}{P_{loss,1}} = \left(\frac{\cos\phi_1}{\cos\phi_2}\right)^2$$

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $P = 12{,}000\,\text{W}$, $V_L = 400\,\text{V}$, $\cos\phi_1 = 0.75$, $\cos\phi_2 = 0.95$, $R = 0.5\,\Omega$.

**Step (i) Line currents:**

$$I_{L1} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot \cos\phi_1} = \frac{12{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 0.75} = \frac{12{,}000}{519.6} = 23.10\,\text{A}$$

$$I_{L2} = \frac{12{,}000}{\sqrt{3} \cdot 400 \cdot 0.95} = \frac{12{,}000}{658.18} = 18.23\,\text{A}$$

**Step (ii) Capacitor bank rating:**

$$\phi_1 = \cos^{-1}(0.75) = 41.41^\circ$$
$$\tan\phi_1 = \tan(41.41^\circ) = 0.8819$$

$$\phi_2 = \cos^{-1}(0.95) = 18.19^\circ$$
$$\tan\phi_2 = \tan(18.19^\circ) = 0.3287$$

Required kVAr:

$$Q_c = P \cdot (\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2) = 12{,}000 \cdot (0.8819 - 0.3287) = 12{,}000 \cdot 0.5532 = 6638.4\,\text{VAr}$$

$$Q_c \approx 6.64\,\text{kVAr}$$

**Step (iii) Line loss reduction:**

$$\frac{P_{loss,2}}{P_{loss,1}} = \left(\frac{0.75}{0.95}\right)^2 = (0.7895)^2 = 0.6233$$

$$\Delta P_{\text{loss}, \%} = (1 - 0.6233) \times 100 = 37.67\%$$

$$\boxed{I_{L1} = 23.10\,\text{A}, \quad I_{L2} = 18.23\,\text{A}, \quad Q_c = 6.64\,\text{kVAr}, \quad \Delta P_{\text{loss}, \%} = 37.67\%}$$

> ⚠️ **Validation:** $I_{L2}/I_{L1} = 18.23/23.10 = 0.7892$, and $0.75/0.95 = 0.7895$. Tiny rounding difference confirms consistency. ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> **Common mistakes students make in "Maximizing System Efficiencies" questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cubic relationship** in pump/fan problems and using linear interpolation. Always remember: $P \propto N^3$, not $N$.
> 2. **Mixing up line and phase values** in three-phase power factor correction. Capacitor rating uses line-to-line voltage $V_L$ for star connection and phase voltage for delta — read the question carefully.
> 3. **Ignoring motor efficiency** when computing input power. The relation is $P_{\text{in}} = P_{\text{out}} / \eta$, not $P_{\text{out}}$ directly.
> 4. **Using degrees instead of radians** (or vice versa) in trigonometric loss calculations — keep $\cos\phi$ calculations consistent.
> 5. **Forgetting units** in final answers — kVAr ≠ kW, kVA ≠ kW. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Maximizing System Efficiencies**

- ⚡ **SEC = Energy Input / Useful Output** is the universal benchmark metric.
- ⚡ **First Law Efficiency** ($\eta_1$) is energy-based; **Second Law Efficiency** ($\eta_2$) is exergy-based and always $\eta_2 \leq \eta_1$.
- ⚡ **Pump Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$.
- ⚡ **Fan Affinity Laws:** Same cubic power relationship as pumps.
- ⚡ **Power Factor Correction:** Line loss reduction $= 1 - (\cos\phi_1 / \cos\phi_2)^2$.
- ⚡ **Capacitor Rating (3-phase):** $Q_c = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ in kVAr.
- ⚡ **Synchronous Speed:** $N_s = 120f / P$ (Indian standard $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$).
- ⚡ **Motor Slip:** $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$; rotor efficiency $\eta_{\text{rotor}} = 1 - s$.
- ⚡ **VFDs exploit cubic law** for 20–50% energy savings in pumps/fans.
- ⚡ **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_0 = 25^\circ\text{C} = 298.15\,\text{K}$.
- ⚡ **Energy conversion:** $1\,\text{kWh} = 3.6\,\text{MJ} = 860\,\text{kcal}$.
- ⚡ **Payback Period:** $\text{SPP} = \text{Investment} / \text{Annual Savings}$; benchmark $< 3$ years.
- ⚡ **Typical Savings Ranges:** LED lighting 50–70%, VFD pumps 20–50%, PF correction 30–45% line loss reduction.
- ⚡ **BEE Standards & ISO 50001** are the primary benchmarking references for Indian industries.
- ⚡ **Tier Hierarchy:** Housekeeping (low-cost) → Retrofit (intermediate) → System redesign (capital-intensive).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimizing the Input Energy Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optimizing the Input Energy Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimizing the Input Energy Requirements** is the systematic engineering process of analyzing, quantifying, and minimizing the energy supplied to a system, process, equipment, or facility so that it meets the *minimum energy required* to deliver the desired output (product, service, or comfort), while rejecting all forms of wastage, inefficiencies, and unnecessary conversion losses.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2), this topic addresses how a practicing Energy Manager minimizes the energy entering the boundary of an energy-using system. The discipline is built on the **Energy Balance Principle**:

$$\text{Input Energy} = \text{Useful Energy Output} + \text{Energy Losses} + \text{Waste Energy}$$

The goal of optimization is therefore to drive the *right-hand-side terms* (losses and waste) towards zero while keeping the useful output constant, or alternatively, to increase the useful output for the same input.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** This topic forms the bridge between *Energy Auditing* (Module 1) and *Energy Efficient Technologies* (Module 3). Every optimization decision must be backed by audited baseline data.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Shower Analogy

Imagine taking a shower. The "input energy" is the hot water flowing through the pipe. The "useful output" is the warmth and water pressure needed to clean your body. If you turn the tap fully on:

- A lot of water (input) flows in.
- Most of it splashes unused on the floor (waste).
- Your body cannot absorb the extra flow (over-supply).
- You also burn more electricity/gas heating water you don't need.

**Optimizing input energy** means adjusting the tap (input) to just enough flow and temperature to do the job — not less (you'll be cold) and not more (you'll waste hot water). The same logic applies to industrial boilers, HVAC systems, motors, and lighting.

### 1.3 Key Sub-Topics Covered Under This Module

- Energy matching and *cascade utilization*
- Process integration (Pinch Analysis overview)
- Load matching and *demand-side management*
- Excess air / stoichiometric air optimization in combustion
- Minimizing *reheat, rework, and idling losses*
- Right-sizing of motors, transformers, and boilers

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Input Energy Optimization

| Pillar | Focus | Typical Tool |
|---|---|---|
| **Reduce** | Eliminate wasteful energy uses | Energy Audit, Housekeeping |
| **Reuse** | Recapture waste energy inside the system | Heat recovery, Recycling |
| **Replace** | Substitute inefficient equipment | VFDs, LEDs, High-efficiency motors |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Energy Manager's golden rule: *"Do not waste. If you cannot avoid waste, recover it. If you cannot recover, convert it efficiently."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logic of Optimization — A 5-Step Engineering Approach

1. **Quantify the Baseline Input:** Measure every fuel, electricity, steam, and hot-fluid stream crossing the system boundary. (Uses metering & audit data.)
2. **Identify the End-Use:** Decompose input into useful end-uses (e.g., heating, lighting, motive power).
3. **Benchmark Against Best Practice:** Compare specific energy consumption (SEC) with industry benchmarks.
4. **Identify the Gap:** Gap = Current SEC − Best-Practice SEC. This gap represents the optimization potential.
5. **Implement Measures Ranked by Payback:** Technical measures are evaluated using Simple Payback Period (SPP) and implemented in order of economic attractiveness.

### 2.2 Energy Matching (Cascade Principle)

Energy exists at different **quality levels** — quantified by **exergy**. High-temperature heat (> 500 °C) is *high-grade*; warm water at 60 °C is *low-grade*. The principle of **Energy Matching** states:

> The energy supplied to a process must match the *quality* required by that process.

For example:
- A furnace needing 1200 °C should *not* be heated by low-pressure steam (only ~150 °C).
- Conversely, space heating at 25 °C should *not* use electricity (3,400 °C flame equivalent) when 80 °C hot water is freely available as waste heat.

The **Cascade Use** principle routes high-grade energy to high-temperature tasks first, and progressively lower-grade waste heat to lower-temperature tasks (preheating, domestic hot water, absorption chilling).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master these equations. They appear repeatedly in KTU university examinations for PESFT414.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful Output Energy}}{\text{Input Energy}} \times 100$ | $\eta$ = Efficiency | % | General efficiency |
| 2 | $SEC = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Input}}{\text{Units of Output}}$ | $SEC$ = Specific Energy Consumption | $\text{MJ/ton}$, $\text{kWh/unit}$ | Benchmarking |
| 3 | $\text{Energy Saving} = \text{Input}_{\text{baseline}} - \text{Input}_{\text{optimized}}$ | Saving in energy | $\text{kWh}$, $\text{kcal}$, $\text{MJ}$ | Project savings |
| 4 | $\% \text{ Saving} = \dfrac{\text{Saving}}{\text{Input}_{\text{baseline}}} \times 100$ | Percentage saving | % | Reporting savings |
| 5 | $SPP = \dfrac{\text{Capital Cost of Project}}{\text{Annual Monetary Saving}}$ | Simple Payback Period | Years | Economic viability |
| 6 | $\text{Stoichiometric Air} = \dfrac{\text{O}_2 \text{ required}}{\text{Fuel mass}} \times 4.35$ | Theoretical air | $\text{kg air/kg fuel}$ | Combustion |
| 7 | $\%\text{Excess Air} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Air} - \text{Stoichiometric Air}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air}} \times 100$ | Excess combustion air | % | Combustion tuning |
| 8 | $\text{Heat Loss through flue} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $m$ = flue gas mass, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = temp rise | $\text{kJ/h}$ | Boiler tuning |
| 9 | $P_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{T \cdot N}{9550}$ | $T$ = torque (Nm), $N$ = RPM | $\text{kW}$ | Motor right-sizing |
| 10 | $E_{\text{annual}} = P_{\text{rated}} \cdot \text{LF} \cdot \text{Hours}$ | $LF$ = Load Factor, $P_{\text{rated}}$ = rated power | $\text{kWh}$ | Energy budgeting |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Sector:** Boiler combustion tuning (excess air reduction) can save 1–3 % fuel instantly.
- **Buildings:** HVAC right-sizing reduces electricity bills by 20–30 %.
- **Utilities:** Pinch Analysis in oil refineries recovers waste heat worth millions of rupees annually.
- **Manufacturing:** Right-sized motors and VFDs cut electrical input by 30–60 % in fans and pumps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"Low-Hanging Fruit"** strategy taught in KTU Module 2 prioritizes measures with SPP < 2 years. These are almost always: (i) excess air reduction, (ii) leak sealing in compressed air, (iii) power factor correction, and (iv) lighting upgrade to LED.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Energy Balance for a Steady-State System

For any industrial process operating under steady-state conditions (no accumulation), the **First Law of Thermodynamics** gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input Energy} &= \text{Output (Useful)} + \text{Losses} + \text{Waste} \\
E_{\text{in}} &= E_{\text{out}} + E_{\text{loss}} + E_{\text{waste}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing both sides by the useful output $E_{\text{out}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{E_{\text{in}}}{E_{\text{out}}} &= 1 + \dfrac{E_{\text{loss}}}{E_{\text{out}}} + \dfrac{E_{\text{waste}}}{E_{\text{out}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since efficiency $\eta = \dfrac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}}$, we invert:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{\eta} &= 1 + \underbrace{\dfrac{E_{\text{loss}}}{E_{\text{out}}}}_{\text{term A}} + \underbrace{\dfrac{E_{\text{waste}}}{E_{\text{out}}}}_{\text{term B}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Term A represents the irreversibility fraction, and Term B the wasted fraction. To *optimize input*, the energy manager must reduce either or both terms.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Combustion Optimization (Boiler)

> **[KTU-Style Numerical — Combustion Tuning]**
> A 10 TPH (tonnes per hour) oil-fired boiler uses furnace oil with GCV = 10,200 kcal/kg. The stoichiometric air required is 14.3 kg air/kg of oil. The boiler currently operates with **35 % excess air** and a stack temperature of 280 °C (ambient = 30 °C). Flue gas quantity is 18.5 kg/kg of oil with $c_p$ = 0.26 kcal/kg·°C.

**Step 1 — Calculate current flue gas heat loss per kg of oil:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= T_{\text{stack}} - T_{\text{ambient}} = 280 - 30 = 250 \text{ °C} \\
Q_{\text{loss}} &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 18.5 \times 0.26 \times 250 \\
Q_{\text{loss}} &= 1202.5 \text{ kcal/kg of oil}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate % heat loss relative to fuel GCV:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Loss} &= \dfrac{1202.5}{10200} \times 100 = 11.79 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Tune to 20 % excess air.** New flue gas quantity reduces to ≈ 16.8 kg/kg of oil (standard combustion tables). Recompute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{loss,new}} &= 16.8 \times 0.26 \times 250 = 1092 \text{ kcal/kg oil} \\
\% \text{Loss,new} &= \dfrac{1092}{10200} \times 100 = 10.71 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute input fuel saving per hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fuel saving \%} &= 11.79 - 10.71 = 1.08 \text{ \%} \\
\text{Hourly fuel consumption} &= 10{,}000 \text{ kg/h (10 TPH)} \\
\text{Fuel saved} &= 10{,}000 \times 0.0108 = 108 \text{ kg/h} \\
\text{Annual saving (8000 h operation)} &= 108 \times 8000 = 864{,}000 \text{ kg/year} \\
\text{Cost saving @ ₹45/kg} &= 864{,}000 \times 45 = ₹3.888 \text{ Crores/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This single combustion tuning measure — reducing excess air from 35 % to 20 % — saves nearly **₹4 crores annually** for a 10 TPH boiler. This is the highest-payoff input optimization measure taught in KTU Module 2.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Motor Right-Sizing with VFD

A 75 kW motor drives a centrifugal pump. The system curve analysis (audit) shows the pump operates at 60 % of rated flow for 5,000 hours/year. Without a VFD, the motor draws near-rated power due to throttling.

| Parameter | Throttled (Current) | VFD at 60 % flow | Saving |
|---|---|---|---|
| Motor input power | 68 kW | ~22 kW (Affinity Law: $P \propto Q^3$) | 46 kW |
| Annual energy | 3,40,000 kWh | 1,10,000 kWh | 2,30,000 kWh |
| Cost @ ₹8/kWh | ₹27.2 Lakh | ₹8.8 Lakh | **₹18.4 Lakh/year** |

The Affinity Law derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &\propto Q^3 \\
\text{So } \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} &= \left(\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1}\right)^3 = (0.6)^3 = 0.216 \\
P_2 &= 68 \times 0.216 = 14.7 \text{ kW (ideal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

(The actual VFD-input power ~22 kW accounts for motor and VFD efficiency losses at part load — a realistic 24 % of rated.)

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Optimization Strategy

| Input Stream | Common Inefficiency | Diagnostic Tool | Optimization Measure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Steam/Fuel to boiler | Excess air, soot, scaling | Flue gas analyzer, O₂ meter | Trim excess air to 15–20 %, soot blowing |
| Electrical to motor | Oversizing, no VFD | kW transducer, clamp meter | Right-size, install VFD |
| Compressed air | Leaks (20–30 % typical) | Leak ultrasonic detector | Repair, lower pressure setpoint |
| Lighting | Old incandescent/HID | Lux meter, audit | LED retrofit, daylight sensors |
| HVAC | Constant volume, poor insulation | IR camera, ΔT log | VAV, insulation, EC fans |
| Process heat | Poor heat recovery | Pinch Analysis | Heat exchanger network retrofit |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Input Energy Optimization Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Audit Baseline Data] --> nodeB[Identify Energy End-Uses]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Categorize by Quality}
    nodeC -->|High-Grade| nodeD[High-Temperature Process]
    nodeC -->|Mid-Grade| nodeE[Steam / Hot Water]
    nodeC -->|Low-Grade| nodeF[Space Heating / DHW]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Apply Pinch Analysis]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Excess Air Trim + Insulation]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Recover Waste Heat via Cascade]
    nodeG --> nodeJ{Rank by SPP Less Than 2 Years}
    nodeH --> nodeJ
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ -->|Yes| nodeK[Implement Measure]
    nodeJ -->|No| nodeL[Re-evaluate or Defer]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Measure and Verify M and V]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Continuous Improvement Loop]
    nodeN --> nodeA
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — The Input Energy Reduction Framework

```mermaid
graph LR
    in1[Raw Fuel] --> in2[Boiler]
    in3[Grid Electricity] --> in4[Motor / Load]
    in2 --> p1[Process Steam]
    in4 --> p2[Motive Power]
    p1 --> out1[Useful Output]
    p2 --> out1
    in2 -. Heat Loss .-> loss1[Stack / Radiation]
    in4 -. Heat Loss .-> loss2[Windage / Friction]
    loss1 --> reco1[Heat Recovery Heat Exchanger]
    loss2 --> reco2[VFD Energy Recovery]
    reco1 --> p1
    reco2 --> p2
```

### 4.3 Cascade Utilization Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a1[High Pressure Steam 40 bar] --> a2[Power Generation Turbine]
    a2 --> a3[MP Steam 10 bar]
    a3 --> a4[Process Heating 180 deg C]
    a4 --> a5[LP Steam 3 bar]
    a5 --> a6[Space Heating 130 deg C]
    a6 --> a7[Hot Water 90 deg C]
    a7 --> a8[DHW 60 deg C]
    a8 --> a9[Condensate Return to Boiler]
```

### 4.4 Process Integration Concept — Pinch Diagram (Block Form)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph HOT["Hot Streams (need to be cooled)"]
        H1[Stream H1 starting at 250 deg C]
        H2[Stream H2 starting at 180 deg C]
    end
    subgraph COLD["Cold Streams (need to be heated)"]
        C1[Stream C1 target 200 deg C]
        C2[Stream C2 target 120 deg C]
    end
    H1 --> HEX1[Heat Exchanger Network]
    H2 --> HEX1
    HEX1 --> C1
    HEX1 --> C2
    HEX1 -. Minimum Energy at Pinch .-> MIN[Heater and Cooler Utilities Minimized]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pinch Insight:** The pinch point is the temperature at which the hot and cold stream composite curves are closest (typically ΔT_min = 10–20 °C). Above the pinch, only hot utility is needed; below the pinch, only cold utility is needed. **Never transfer heat across the pinch** — it increases total utility demand, a classic KTU exam pitfall.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define the term "Optimizing the Input Energy Requirements" with two examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Optimizing input energy requirements is the process of minimizing the energy supplied to a system while maintaining or improving the desired output, by reducing losses, recovering waste heat, and matching energy quality to end-use.
**Examples:** (i) Reducing excess air in a boiler from 35 % to 20 % cuts fuel input. (ii) Installing a VFD on a centrifugal pump reduces electrical input proportional to the cube of the flow reduction (Affinity Law).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is meant by "Energy Matching" or "Cascade Utilization"?**

**Model Answer:**
Energy matching is the principle that the energy supplied to a process must be of the *quality* (temperature, pressure, exergy) required by that process. Cascade utilization routes high-grade energy (e.g., high-pressure steam) first to high-temperature tasks (turbines, reactors), and progressively lower-grade waste energy to lower-temperature tasks (preheating, domestic hot water). This minimizes exergy destruction and maximizes overall efficiency.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, students answer ONE question of their choice from the Module-2 slot.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]*

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the concept of **Pinch Analysis** as a tool for optimizing input energy in a process plant. List any **four merits** of pinch analysis. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 6 TPH industrial boiler uses coal with GCV = 3500 kcal/kg. Stoichiometric air = 6.5 kg/kg coal. Currently operating with **40 % excess air**, stack temperature = 300 °C, ambient = 35 °C. Flue gas mass = 8.2 kg/kg coal, $c_p$ = 0.25 kcal/kg·°C. Calculate: **(i)** current flue gas heat loss in kcal/kg, **(ii)** % heat loss, **(iii)** fuel saving per hour if excess air is reduced to **20 %**, assuming flue gas mass drops to 7.4 kg/kg coal. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solutions

**Part (a) Solution:**

Pinch Analysis is a thermodynamic technique for optimizing the design of heat exchanger networks (HEN) to minimize external heating and cooling utilities in a process plant.

**Steps:**
1. Extract hot streams (those needing cooling) and cold streams (those needing heating) from process flow-sheeting.
2. Construct **Composite Curves** (Hot and Cold) — temperature vs. enthalpy.
3. The **Pinch Point** is the location of minimum vertical distance (ΔT_min) between the curves.
4. Above the pinch ⇒ only hot utility needed (no cooling).
5. Below the pinch ⇒ only cold utility needed (no heating).
6. **Rule:** Do not transfer heat across the pinch (it increases total utility demand).
7. Design Heat Exchanger Network (HEN) meeting the minimum utility targets.

**Four Merits:**
- Quantifies **Minimum Heating Utility (Qh,min)** and **Minimum Cooling Utility (Qc,min)** before detailed design.
- Identifies the **optimum ΔT_min** (pinch point).
- Reduces both capital and operating costs.
- Enables retrofit of existing networks for incremental savings.

**Sketch** — Composite Curves:

```
   T (°C)
    │
  300┤  ●━━━━━ HOT curve
    │  ┃   ╲
    │  ┃    ╲    ← PINCH (ΔT_min)
    │  ┃     ╲
  150┤  ┃      ●━━━ COLD curve
    │  ┃      ┃
    │  ┃      ┃
   30┤  ●━━━━━●
       └─────────────► Enthalpy (kW)
       |←Qh,min→|
```

**Part (b) Solution:**

**(i) Current flue gas heat loss per kg of coal:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T &= 300 - 35 = 265 \text{ °C} \\
Q_{\text{loss}} &= m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 8.2 \times 0.25 \times 265 \\
Q_{\text{loss}} &= 543.25 \text{ kcal/kg coal}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) % Heat loss:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Loss} &= \dfrac{543.25}{3500} \times 100 = 15.52 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Fuel saving per hour when excess air is reduced to 20 %:**

New heat loss:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{loss,new}} &= 7.4 \times 0.25 \times 265 = 490.25 \text{ kcal/kg coal} \\
\% \text{Loss,new} &= \dfrac{490.25}{3500} \times 100 = 14.01 \text{ \%}
\end{aligned}
$$

Reduction in loss per kg = $543.25 - 490.25 = 53$ kcal/kg coal.

For a 6 TPH (6000 kg/h) boiler:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Hourly heat energy saved} &= 53 \times 6000 = 318{,}000 \text{ kcal/h} \\
\text{Equivalent coal saved} &= \dfrac{318{,}000}{3500} = 90.86 \text{ kg/h} \\
\text{Annual saving (8000 h)} &= 90.86 \times 8000 = 7.27 \text{ lakh kg/year} \\
&\approx 727 \text{ tonnes/year of coal}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** Discuss the role of **"Energy Audit"** in identifying input energy optimization opportunities. Explain **Walk-through**, **Preliminary**, and **Detailed** audit levels. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 50 kW induction motor in a textile mill operates at 80 % load for 6000 hours/year. Its efficiency at 80 % load is 89 %. A proposal is made to replace it with a **premium-efficiency (IE3) motor** of the same rating having 93 % efficiency at the same load. Electricity cost = ₹9/kWh, motor cost = ₹4.5 lakh. Calculate: **(i)** Annual energy saving in kWh, **(ii)** Annual cost saving, **(iii)** Simple Payback Period (SPP). Comment on the viability of the project. **(7 Marks)**

##### Model Solutions

**Part (a) Solution:**

An **Energy Audit** is the systematic inspection, measurement, and review of energy use in a facility to identify wastage and recommend optimization measures. It is the *first step* in input energy optimization.

**Three Levels of Audit:**

| Level | Scope | Tools | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Walk-through Audit** | Quick visual inspection, no instruments | Checklist, walk-through | Identify obvious wastage, list measures |
| **Preliminary Audit** | Qualitative + simple measurements | kWh meter, manometer | Detailed list of measures with rough savings & cost |
| **Detailed Audit** | Quantitative, instrumented, long-term | Power analyzer, flue gas analyzer, lux meter | Engineering-grade savings, payback, M&V plan |

**Role in Optimization:**
- Establishes baseline SEC for benchmarking.
- Decomposes total input energy into end-uses (lighting, motors, HVAC, process heat).
- Quantifies each loss stream so that specific measures (excess air trim, VFD, LED) can be evaluated.
- Provides data for economic ranking (SPP) of all proposed projects.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**(i) Annual energy saving:**

Motor input power at 80 % load:
- Old motor: $P_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.8}{0.89} = \dfrac{40}{0.89} = 44.94$ kW
- New motor: $P_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{40}{0.93} = 43.01$ kW

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= 44.94 - 43.01 = 1.93 \text{ kW} \\
E_{\text{saved}} &= \Delta P \times \text{Hours} = 1.93 \times 6000 = 11{,}580 \text{ kWh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Annual cost saving:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cost Saving} &= 11{,}580 \times 9 = ₹1{,}04{,}220/\text{year} \approx ₹1.04 \text{ Lakh/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Simple Payback Period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SPP &= \dfrac{\text{Capital Cost}}{\text{Annual Saving}} = \dfrac{4.5 \text{ Lakh}}{1.04 \text{ Lakh}} = 4.33 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Viability Comment:**
A payback of 4.3 years is **marginally acceptable** (industry threshold is 2–3 years). However, premium-efficiency motors also provide:
- Lower internal heat → longer winding life
- Better power factor → reduced kVA demand charges
- Eligibility for **utility rebates / PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) certificates** in India

With incentives, the effective SPP may drop below 3 years, making the project **recommended**.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Energy" with "Exergy":** Energy is conserved; exergy is destroyed. Always state quality considerations in cascade questions.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers:** Marks are deducted if kcal/kg is omitted or if kW is written instead of kWh for annual energy.
> 3. **Wrong direction of Excess Air:** More excess air → MORE flue gas → MORE stack loss → LESS efficiency. Students often invert this.
> 4. **Ignoring Affinity Law cubic relation:** For fans/pumps, $P \propto Q^3$, *not* linear. Always emphasize this in VFD questions.
> 5. **Pinch Analysis:** Forgetting the "no heat transfer across the pinch" rule.
> 6. **Payback vs. ROI:** KTU often asks specifically for **Simple Payback** in years, not the more complex ROI.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Input Energy Optimization** = minimizing energy input while preserving output, via Reduce → Reuse → Replace.
- ✅ **First Law Balance:** $E_{\text{in}} = E_{\text{out}} + E_{\text{loss}} + E_{\text{waste}}$.
- ✅ **Energy Matching (Cascade Use):** Route high-grade energy to high-temperature tasks first.
- ✅ **Pinch Analysis** sets minimum heating ($Q_{h,\min}$) and cooling ($Q_{c,\min}$) utilities.
- ✅ **Affinity Law for centrifugal loads:** $P \propto Q^3 \propto N^3$. A 20 % flow reduction saves ~49 % power.
- ✅ **Excess Air Rule of Thumb:** For every 1 % reduction in excess air (in oil/gas furnaces), efficiency improves by ~0.1–0.2 %.
- ✅ **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** is the principal screening tool in KTU case studies.
- ✅ **High-grade / Low-grade:** Avoid using 1000 °C heat where 80 °C suffices — exergy destruction is invisible to First-Law auditors but vital for true optimization.
- ✅ **Walk-through → Preliminary → Detailed** audits form the structured data backbone of any optimization project.
- ✅ Always **measure and verify (M&V)** after implementation using IPMVP protocols.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel and Energy Substitution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel and Energy Substitution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fuel Substitution** is the engineering and economic strategy of replacing a conventional or scarce primary fuel with an alternative fuel possessing comparable energy content, lower emissions, improved availability, or superior cost economics, while maintaining the functional output of the energy system.

**Energy Substitution** is the broader, more generalized concept that encompasses replacing one *form* or *carrier* of energy with another (e.g., from solid fossil fuels to electricity, from petroleum to solar thermal, or from grid power to captive renewable generation). It operates at three hierarchical levels:

| Level | Substitution Type | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Macro (National) | Inter-fuel / Inter-energy carrier | Coal-based economy → Gas-based economy → Renewable-based economy |
| Meso (Sectoral) | Industrial / Sectoral switching | Industrial boilers shifting from furnace oil to biomass briquettes |
| Micro (Plant-level) | Equipment-level substitution | Diesel genset replaced by solar PV + battery hybrid |

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Kitchen Stove Story:**
> Imagine your mother cooks daily on an LPG stove. One day, LPG cylinder prices surge, supply becomes erratic, and emissions regulations tighten. She replaces LPG with an **induction cooktop** (electricity). Functionally, both produce heat to cook food — but the *energy carrier* changed, the *cost structure* changed, the *emission profile* changed, and the *supply chain* changed.
> 
> That single act of "swapping one fuel for another to perform the same useful work" is **Fuel and Energy Substitution** at a household level. Industrial-scale, the same logic applies to a 500 MW power plant switching from coal to solar + storage.

The key insight: *Substitution does not reduce the demand for energy services (heating, lighting, motion); it changes the **source** and **pathway** by which that service is delivered.*

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** — usable energy released per kg of fuel, excluding latent heat of water vapor (units: **MJ/kg** or **kcal/kg**).
- **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** — total energy including latent heat recovery (units: **MJ/kg**).
- **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** = LHV (synonymous in KTU syllabus).
- **Energy Substitution Elasticity (σ)** — dimensionless index quantifying how demand for one fuel changes with price/availability of another.
- **1 Tonne of Oil Equivalent (TOE)** = **41.868 GJ** = **11.63 MWh** (standard international benchmark).
- **1 Tonne of Coal Equivalent (TCE)** = **29.3076 GJ**.
- **CO₂ Emission Factor (coal)** ≈ **2.42 tCO₂/tce**; **(natural gas)** ≈ **1.6 tCO₂/toe**; **(biomass)** ≈ **0 (carbon-neutral)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** Whenever a substitution problem is posed in exams, the first thing to verify is *energy parity* — the substitute must deliver **equivalent thermal/mechanical/electrical output**, not just equivalent mass or volume of fuel consumed.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

### 2.1.1 The Substitutability Principle

Two fuels $F_1$ and $F_2$ are **technically substitutable** if a given energy-conversion device (boiler, furnace, engine, turbine) can operate on either fuel — either directly, or with minor modifications to combustion air supply, burner geometry, or pre-heating.

The substitution is **economically viable** if the *levelized cost of useful energy* (LCOUE) from $F_2$ is lower than from $F_1$ over the asset's life cycle.

The substitution is **environmentally beneficial** if the **life-cycle GHG emission factor** (g CO₂-eq per MJ of useful energy) of $F_2$ is materially lower.

### 2.1.2 Hierarchy of Substitution Drivers

The decision to substitute follows a strict logical hierarchy used by energy auditors:

1. **Functional Equivalence** — Does $F_2$ deliver the same useful energy service?
2. **Technical Compatibility** — Can existing equipment use $F_2$ without major retrofit?
3. **Energy Efficiency Preservation** — Does efficiency not drop below acceptable limits?
4. **Economic Superiority** — Net Present Value (NPV) of switching must be positive.
5. **Regulatory Compliance** — Emissions, safety, and licensing norms must be met.
6. **Supply Security** — Long-term availability and import-independence.

### 2.1.3 Substitutability Index (Energy Economics Concept)

The **Allen-Uzawa Elasticity of Substitution** between two energy carriers $E_1$ and $E_2$ is defined as:

$$\sigma = \frac{\%\ \Delta(E_1/E_2)}{\%\ \Delta(MP_{E_1}/MP_{E_2})}$$

Where $MP$ denotes marginal productivity. In simple engineering terms:

- $\sigma \rightarrow \infty$ → perfect substitution (one-to-one trade-off)
- $\sigma = 1$ → Cobb-Douglas (constant elasticity) substitution
- $\sigma \rightarrow 0$ → perfect complementarity (must be used together)
- $\sigma < 0$ → anti-substitutes (one rises, the other falls in tandem)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they cover 90% of numerical questions on Fuel & Energy Substitution in KTU ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equivalent fuel quantity | $m_2 = m_1 \cdot \dfrac{\text{NCV}_1}{\text{NCV}_2}$ | Mass basis; $m$ in kg |
| 2 | Equivalent energy output | $E = m \cdot \text{NCV} \cdot \eta$ | Joules; $\eta$ = efficiency |
| 3 | Substitution ratio (mass) | $R_m = \dfrac{m_2}{m_1} = \dfrac{\text{NCV}_1}{\text{NCV}_2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Substitution ratio (volume) | $R_v = R_m \cdot \dfrac{\rho_2}{\rho_1}$ | Dimensionless; $\rho$ = density |
| 5 | Annual fuel cost saving | $\Delta C = (c_1 R_m - c_2) \cdot m_2 \cdot t$ | Currency/year; $t$ = operating hours |
| 6 | CO₂ emission reduction | $\Delta\text{CO}_2 = (E_1 \cdot \text{EF}_1) - (E_2 \cdot \text{EF}_2)$ | tCO₂/year |
| 7 | Simple Payback Period | $\text{SPP} = \dfrac{I}{\Delta C}$ | Years; $I$ = investment |
| 8 | Levelized Cost of Energy | $\text{LCOE} = \dfrac{I + \sum_t O_t/(1+r)^t}{\sum_t E_t/(1+r)^t}$ | ₹/kWh or ₹/GJ |
| 9 | Energy Substitution Index | $\text{ESI} = \dfrac{\Delta F_2 / F_2}{\Delta F_1 / F_1}$ | Dimensionless |
| 10 | TOE conversion | $1\ \text{TOE} = 41.868\ \text{GJ} = 11630\ \text{kWh}$ | Universal benchmark |
| 11 | Calorific value parity check | $\text{NCV}_2 \geq 0.85 \cdot \text{NCV}_1$ (recommended) | For boiler substitution |
| 12 | Combustion air requirement | $A_0 = \dfrac{8/3 \cdot C + 8 \cdot H + O - S}{23.2}\ \text{(kg air/kg fuel)}$ | Stoichiometric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder on Substitution Ratio:** It is *not* 1:1 by mass. Because NCV varies dramatically across fuels (coal ≈ 25 MJ/kg vs natural gas ≈ 50 MJ/kg vs biomass ≈ 14–17 MJ/kg), you must compute $R_m$ for every substitution problem before doing cost or emission analysis.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of Fuel & Energy Substitution

In engineering practice, substitution decisions drive outcomes in:

- **Power generation:** Coal → Natural gas (combined cycle efficiency jumps from 35% to 55%); Coal → Solar PV + storage.
- **Industrial process heat:** Furnace oil → Biomass briquettes; LPG → Producer gas from agricultural waste.
- **Transport:** Diesel → CNG → Electric (BEV); Petrol → Ethanol blends (E20).
- **Cooking (rural/domestic):** Firewood → LPG → Biogas → Induction (electricity).
- **Space conditioning:** Diesel AC → Vapor absorption AC (heat-driven, can run on solar thermal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Context:** In the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, substitution questions are increasingly tied to **decarbonization** and **net-zero commitments**. Always frame your answer in terms of *triple-bottom-line benefit* — economic, environmental, energy security.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivations

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Mass-Based Substitution Ratio

**Given:**
A boiler currently consumes $m_1$ kg/hr of fuel 1 with NCV = $\text{NCV}_1$ MJ/kg, delivering a thermal output of $Q$ kW.

**Step 1 — Express thermal output using fuel 1:**
$$Q = m_1 \cdot \text{NCV}_1 \cdot \eta \quad \text{[kW, after unit conversion]}$$

**Step 2 — Same output using fuel 2:**
$$Q = m_2 \cdot \text{NCV}_2 \cdot \eta$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two (since output and efficiency are preserved):**
$$m_1 \cdot \text{NCV}_1 \cdot \eta = m_2 \cdot \text{NCV}_2 \cdot \eta$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\eta$ and solve for $m_2$:**
$$m_2 = m_1 \cdot \frac{\text{NCV}_1}{\text{NCV}_2}$$

**Step 5 — Define the mass substitution ratio:**
$$\boxed{R_m = \frac{m_2}{m_1} = \frac{\text{NCV}_1}{\text{NCV}_2}}$$

> **Conversion logic:** The substitute fuel must deliver the *same total chemical energy input* (and therefore same useful output after efficiency) as the original. Since fuel 2 may be richer or leaner in NCV, the mass flow must be scaled inversely — richer fuel (higher NCV) → smaller mass flow; leaner fuel → larger mass flow.

---

### 3.1.2 Worked Example 1 — Coal-to-Biomass Substitution

A 10 TPH (tonnes per hour) boiler burns coal with NCV = 25 MJ/kg. Management proposes to switch to biomass briquettes with NCV = 17 MJ/kg. The boiler efficiency is 82% in both cases.

**Step 1 — Calculate existing fuel consumption:**

$$m_1 = 10{,}000\ \text{kg/hr (already given as 10 TPH)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute substitution ratio:**
$$R_m = \frac{\text{NCV}_1}{\text{NCV}_2} = \frac{25}{17} \approx 1.4706$$

**Step 3 — Compute required biomass flow:**
$$m_2 = m_1 \cdot R_m = 10{,}000 \times 1.4706 \approx 14{,}706\ \text{kg/hr} \approx 14.71\ \text{TPH}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the additional storage/handling load:**
$$\Delta m = 14{,}706 - 10{,}000 = 4{,}706\ \text{kg/hr} \approx 47\%\ \text{increase in fuel handling volume}$$

**Step 5 — Energy output verification:**
$$Q = 10{,}000 \times 25 \times 0.82 = 205{,}000\ \text{MJ/hr}$$
$$Q = 14{,}706 \times 17 \times 0.82 \approx 205{,}000\ \text{MJ/hr} \checkmark$$

> **Conversion logic:** Although biomass is "carbon-neutral", a 47% higher mass flow means increased conveyor, storage silo, ash-handling, and combustion-air blower capacities must be checked — otherwise, the substitution fails on operational grounds.

---

### 3.1.3 Worked Example 2 — Furnace Oil to Natural Gas

A 5 TPH furnace-oil boiler is to be replaced by a natural gas burner. Furnace oil NCV = 40 MJ/kg; NG NCV = 50 MJ/Nm³. Boiler efficiency drops marginally from 86% to 84% due to flame geometry differences.

**Step 1 — Existing heat output:**
$$Q_1 = 5{,}000 \times 40 \times 0.86 = 172{,}000\ \text{MJ/hr}$$

**Step 2 — Required NG volume (preserving $Q$):**
$$V_2 = \frac{Q_1}{\text{NCV}_2 \cdot \eta_2} = \frac{172{,}000}{50 \times 0.84} \approx 4{,}095\ \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 3 — Substitution ratio on volume basis (vs. original mass-to-volume of FO, density 970 kg/m³):**
$$V_1 = \frac{5{,}000}{970} \approx 5.155\ \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

$$R_v = \frac{4{,}095}{5.155} \approx 794\ \text{Nm}^3\ \text{NG per m}^3\ \text{FO}$$

**Step 4 — Emission comparison:**

| Pollutant | Furnace Oil (kg/hr) | Natural Gas (kg/hr) |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | $5000 \times 0.078 \times 0.86 \approx 335$ | $4095 \times 2.0 \approx 265$ (using NG emission factor 2.0 kg/Nm³ × (50 MJ/LHV), recheck: 56.1 t/TJ → 4095 × 50e-6 × 56.1 ≈ 11.5 t/hr CO₂) |
| SO₂ | High (depends on S%) | Negligible (~0) |
| NOₓ | Moderate | Lower (cleaner combustion) |
| Particulates | Significant | Negligible |

> **Conversion logic:** Even after accounting for efficiency loss, NG delivers lower CO₂ per MJ of fuel due to higher H:C ratio. SO₂ elimination is virtually complete — this is the *primary* driver for fuel-oil-to-gas substitution in urban industrial zones (compliance with CPCB norms).

---

### 3.1.4 Worked Example 3 — Economic & Emission Analysis

Using Example 2 data, calculate annual savings and CO₂ reduction.

**Assumptions:**
- Furnace oil price: ₹65/kg
- Natural gas price: ₹38/Nm³ (industrial PNG, indicative)
- Operating hours: 6000 hr/year
- Capital investment for burner retrofit: ₹80 lakh

**Step 1 — Annual FO cost:**
$$C_1 = 5{,}000 \times 65 \times 6{,}000 = ₹195\ \text{crore/year}$$

**Step 2 — Annual NG cost:**
$$C_2 = 4{,}095 \times 38 \times 6{,}000 \approx ₹93.37\ \text{crore/year}$$

**Step 3 — Annual savings:**
$$\Delta C = 195 - 93.37 = ₹101.63\ \text{crore/year}$$

**Step 4 — Simple Payback:**
$$\text{SPP} = \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{101{,}63{,}00{,}000} \approx 0.0079\ \text{years} \approx 2.9\ \text{days}$$

**Step 5 — Annual CO₂ reduction (using emission factors):**

FO: $5000 \times 40 \times 10^{-3} \times 77.4\ \text{t CO}_2/\text{TJ} = 15.48\ \text{t CO}_2/\text{hr}$
NG: $4095 \times 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 56.1\ \text{t CO}_2/\text{TJ} = 11.49\ \text{t CO}_2/\text{hr}$

$$\Delta \text{CO}_2 = (15.48 - 11.49) \times 6{,}000 = 23{,}940\ \text{t CO}_2/\text{year}$$

> **Conversion logic:** This single substitution avoids nearly **24,000 tonnes of CO₂ annually** — equivalent to taking ~5,200 petrol cars off the road for a year (4.6 t CO₂/car/yr). Combined with massive cost savings, the substitution is unambiguous.

---

### 3.1.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Substitution Decisions

| Original Fuel | Substitute | Driver | Barrier | Typical Payback |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coal | Natural Gas | Lower emissions, higher efficiency | Gas pipeline availability | 2–4 years |
| Furnace Oil | Biomass | Carbon neutrality, rural employment | Higher moisture, storage | 3–5 years |
| Diesel (genset) | Solar PV + Battery | Zero fuel cost, low O&M | High capex, intermittency | 4–7 years |
| LPG (cooking) | Induction (electric) | Indoor air quality, speed | Requires reliable grid | 1–3 years |
| HSD (transport) | CNG | Lower cost, lower PM | Vehicle retrofit cost | 3–6 years |
| Petrol | Ethanol (E20) | Import substitution | Engine compatibility | Immediate |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Fuel Substitution Decision Framework (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Current Fuel F1 and Energy Service] --> B[Define Functional Output Requirement Q kW]
    B --> C{Screen Candidate Substitutes F2}
    C --> D[Check NCV and Energy Parity]
    D --> E[Check Equipment Compatibility]
    E --> F[Compute Mass/Volume Substitution Ratio Rm Rv]
    F --> G[Estimate Annual Fuel Cost C1 C2]
    G --> H[Compute Annual Savings Delta C]
    H --> I{Capital Investment Feasible?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Compute Simple Payback SPP]
    I -->|No| K[Reject Proposal]
    J --> L{SPP less than Target 3 to 5 yr?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Proceed to Emission Analysis]
    L -->|No| N[Optimize or Reject]
    M --> O[Compute CO2 SO2 NOx Reduction]
    O --> P{Emission Norms Compliant?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Approve Substitution]
    P -->|No| R[Add Pollution Control Equipment]
    R --> Q
    Q --> S[Implement and Monitor]
    K --> T[Re-evaluate in Future]
    N --> T
```

**Explanation of nodes:**
- `nodeA` (Start) — Audit current fuel & service
- `stepB` — Quantify output (kW thermal/mechanical/electrical)
- `stepC` — Decision diamond: candidates list
- `stepD` — Apply NCV parity check
