- `stepE` — Compatibility screening (burner type, flame temp, etc.)
- `stepF` — Apply derived formulas $R_m$, $R_v$
- `stepG` — Cost calculation
- `stepH` — Savings
- `nodeI` — Investment decision gate
- `stepJ` — Payback calculation
- `nodeL` — Payback viability gate
- `stepM` — Emission analysis
- `nodeP` — Compliance gate
- `stepQ` — Approval
- `stepS` — Implementation

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Substitution Pathways by Sector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "Industrial Sector"
        I1[Coal Boilers] --> I2[Biomass Briquettes]
        I1 --> I3[Natural Gas Boilers]
        I4[Furnace Oil Furnaces] --> I5[Electric Induction Furnaces]
        I4 --> I6[Producer Gas from Agri Waste]
    end
    subgraph "Power Sector"
        P1[Coal Thermal] --> P2[Gas Combined Cycle]
        P1 --> P3[Solar PV plus Battery]
        P4[Diesel Gensets] --> P5[Gas Gensets]
        P4 --> P6[Hybrid Solar Diesel]
    end
    subgraph "Transport Sector"
        T1[Petrol Vehicles] --> T2[Ethanol E20]
        T1 --> T3[Battery Electric Vehicles]
        T4[Diesel Trucks] --> T5[CNG LNG Trucks]
        T4 --> T6[Hydrogen Fuel Cell]
    end
    subgraph "Domestic Sector"
        D1[Firewood Chulha] --> D2[LPG]
        D1 --> D3[Biogas]
        D1 --> D4[Solar Cooker]
        D2 --> D5[Induction Cooktop]
    end
```

**Explanation:**
This block diagram maps the principal substitution pathways across four major energy-consuming sectors. Each arrow represents a one-way substitution move from a conventional fuel (left) to a cleaner or more efficient alternative (right). Multiple arrows per source fuel reflect the menu of substitutes available — the actual choice is driven by the cost-emissions-availability trade-off.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Substitutability Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Perfect Substitute sigma infinity] --> B[High Substitutability sigma greater than 1]
    B --> C[Unit Elasticity sigma equals 1]
    C --> D[Low Substitutability sigma between 0 and 1]
    D --> E[Perfect Complement sigma equals 0]
    E --> F[Anti Substitutes sigma less than 0]
    A1[Example Coal and Natural Gas for boilers] -.-> A
    B1[Example Furnace Oil and LSHS] -.-> B
    C1[Example Diesel and Biodiesel blend] -.-> C
    D1[Example Coal and Solar PV for process heat] -.-> D
    E1[Example Gasoline and Spark Ignition Engine as pair] -.-> E
    F1[Example Heating Degree Days and Electricity Demand] -.-> F
```

> **Reading aid:** The spectrum is non-linear in engineering practice. Many "perfect substitutes" on paper (e.g., coal → gas in boilers) face real-world barriers like burner geometry, flame speed differences, and ash-handling system redesigns that make the substitution costly and imperfect.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define fuel substitution. List any four criteria for selecting an alternative fuel in an industrial energy system.**

**Model Answer:**
> **Fuel Substitution** is the systematic replacement of a primary fuel with an alternative fuel that delivers equivalent energy services while offering improvements in cost, emissions, availability, or energy security.
>
> **Four Selection Criteria:**
> 1. **Energy Parity** — Equivalent NCV and usable thermal output after efficiency considerations.
> 2. **Equipment Compatibility** — Existing burners, chambers, and heat exchangers can operate on the substitute (or with minor modifications).
> 3. **Economic Viability** — Life-cycle cost of substitute fuel is lower than incumbent (positive NPV, acceptable payback).
> 4. **Environmental Compliance** — Lower emission factor for CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ, and particulates; meets CPCB/SPCB norms.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between fuel substitution and energy substitution with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fuel Substitution | Energy Substitution |
|---|---|---|
| Scope | Replacing one *fuel* with another (same energy carrier class) | Replacing one *form/carrier* of energy with a different one |
| Example | Coal → Biomass briquettes in a boiler | Steam-based heating → Electric resistance heating |
| Nature | Narrower, fuel-specific | Broader, system-level |
| Typical driver | Fuel cost / availability | Process modernization / decarbonization |

> **Example of Fuel Substitution:** Switching an industrial boiler from furnace oil to natural gas.
> **Example of Energy Substitution:** Replacing a coal-fired boiler with a solar thermal collector for low-grade process heat.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** A chemical industry uses 8 TPH of coal (NCV = 24 MJ/kg) in a boiler of efficiency 78%. Management is evaluating a switch to biomass pellets (NCV = 16 MJ/kg) using the same boiler with minor burner retrofit. Calculate:
  (i) The mass-based substitution ratio.
  (ii) The hourly biomass requirement.
  (iii) The annual CO₂ reduction if operating 7000 hours/year. Use emission factors: coal = 94.6 t CO₂/TJ; biomass = 0 t CO₂/TJ (carbon-neutral).

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the technical, economic, and environmental criteria that govern the decision to substitute one industrial fuel with another. Illustrate with a suitable example.

**(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Given:** $m_1 = 8{,}000$ kg/hr, $\text{NCV}_1 = 24$ MJ/kg, $\eta_1 = \eta_2 = 78\%$, $\text{NCV}_2 = 16$ MJ/kg, $t = 7000$ hr/year, $\text{EF}_1 = 94.6$ t CO₂/TJ, $\text{EF}_2 = 0$.

**(i) Mass substitution ratio:**
$$R_m = \frac{\text{NCV}_1}{\text{NCV}_2} = \frac{24}{16} = 1.5$$

**(ii) Hourly biomass requirement:**
$$m_2 = m_1 \cdot R_m = 8000 \times 1.5 = 12{,}000\ \text{kg/hr} = 12\ \text{TPH}$$

**Verification of equivalent output:**
$$Q_1 = 8000 \times 24 \times 0.78 = 149{,}760\ \text{MJ/hr}$$
$$Q_2 = 12{,}000 \times 16 \times 0.78 = 149{,}760\ \text{MJ/hr}\ \checkmark$$

**(iii) Annual CO₂ reduction:**

Coal energy input per hour = $8000 \times 24 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{TJ} = 192$ TJ/hr.

Wait — correction: NCV is 24 MJ/kg, so total energy = $8000 \times 24 = 192{,}000\ \text{MJ/hr} = 0.192\ \text{TJ/hr}$.

Coal CO₂ per hour:
$$192{,}000 \times 10^{-6} \times 94.6 = 18.16\ \text{t CO}_2/\text{hr}$$

Biomass CO₂ per hour: $0$ (carbon-neutral accounting).

Annual CO₂ reduction:
$$\Delta \text{CO}_2 = 18.16 \times 7{,}000 = 1{,}27{,}120\ \text{t CO}_2/\text{year}$$

**Answer Summary:**
- $R_m = 1.5$
- $m_2 = 12$ TPH
- $\Delta \text{CO}_2 = 127{,}120\ \text{t CO}_2/\text{year}$

---

### Model Solution to Question A(b):

The decision framework for industrial fuel substitution rests on three pillars:

**1. Technical Criteria:**
- **Energy parity**: Substitute NCV must be ≥ 85% of original (recommended threshold).
- **Combustion compatibility**: Flame temperature, flame speed, ignition delay, and stoichiometric air-fuel ratio must be compatible.
- **Equipment fitness**: Burner, ash-handling, and flue gas systems must either accept the new fuel or be retrofittable.
- **Storage & handling**: Higher moisture fuels (e.g., biomass at 10–15%) require drying; higher ash fuels require ash disposal capacity.
- **Operational stability**: Turn-down ratio and load-following capability must match process demand.

**2. Economic Criteria:**
- **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)**: Inclusive of fuel, O&M, and amortized capital.
- **Simple Payback Period (SPP)**: Typically 2–5 years for industry-standard acceptance.
- **Net Present Value (NPV)** and **Internal Rate of Return (IRR)**: For long-life investments.
- **Sensitivity to fuel price volatility**: Risk hedging value.
- **Subsidy/incentive availability** (e.g., MNRE capital subsidy for biomass gasifiers).

**3. Environmental Criteria:**
- **GHG emission reduction** (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O) — measured in kg CO₂-eq/MJ.
- **Local pollutant reduction** — SO₂, NOₓ, PM2.5, CO.
- **Water consumption** (thermal plants vs. solar/wind).
- **Land use change** impacts (especially for biomass).
- **End-of-life waste management** (ash, sludge, spent catalysts).

**Illustrative Example:**
A textile processing unit in Tirupur replaced its 6 TPH furnace-oil boiler with a rice-husk-fired boiler. Technical compatibility was achieved by installing a moving-grate stoker. Economically, despite rice husk being free (agricultural waste), the higher handling cost and lower NCV (14 vs. 40 MJ/kg) led to substitution ratio of 2.86, requiring 17 TPH of husk. However, the unit avoided ₹14 crore/year in furnace oil import, achieved payback in 18 months, and reduced CO₂ emissions by 22,000 tonnes/year — qualifying it for CDM carbon credits.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** With the aid of a flow diagram, explain the systematic procedure for evaluating a fuel substitution proposal in an industrial plant. Your answer must include at least six decision stages.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A captive diesel genset of 1 MW rated capacity operates 4000 hours/year at 70% load. Specific fuel consumption is 0.30 kg/kWh at this load. The plant is evaluating replacement with a solar PV + battery storage hybrid system of equivalent annual energy delivery.
  (i) Compute annual diesel consumption and annual energy generated.
  (ii) If diesel density is 0.85 kg/L and price is ₹90/L, compute annual diesel cost.
  (iii) Comment on the economic feasibility of substitution if solar hybrid capex is ₹5 crore with annual O&M of ₹4 lakh and 25-year life. Assume 8% discount rate.

**(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B(a):

The systematic procedure for evaluating an industrial fuel substitution proposal consists of the following six stages:

**Stage 1 — Baseline Energy Audit:** Quantify the existing fuel consumption, NCV, efficiency, load profile, and operating hours of the incumbent fuel system.

**Stage 2 — Functional Output Specification:** Define the desired energy service output (steam, hot water, electricity, mechanical shaft work) in engineering units (kg/hr, kW, Nm³/hr).

**Stage 3 — Candidate Substitute Screening:** Identify all technically feasible substitutes (e.g., natural gas, biomass, solar thermal, electricity) and shortlist based on availability and compatibility.

**Stage 4 — Energy and Equipment Compatibility Check:** Calculate substitution ratios (mass and volume), verify efficiency retention, and check for equipment modification requirements.

**Stage 5 — Economic Analysis:** Compute capital investment, annual fuel cost, O&M cost, payback period, NPV, and IRR.

**Stage 6 — Environmental and Risk Analysis:** Quantify GHG reduction, local pollutant reduction, water savings, and assess supply security / price volatility risks.

**Stage 7 — Implementation and Monitoring:** If approved, execute in phases with continuous monitoring of KPIs.

(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for visual representation.)

---

### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Given:** Rated capacity = 1 MW; operating hours = 4000 hr/yr; load factor = 70%; SFC = 0.30 kg/kWh; diesel price = ₹90/L; density = 0.85 kg/L; capex = ₹5 crore; O&M = ₹4 lakh/yr; life = 25 years; $r = 8\%$.

**(i) Annual energy generated:**
$$E = 1000\ \text{kW} \times 0.70 \times 4000 = 2{,}800{,}000\ \text{kWh/yr} = 2.8\ \text{million units (MU)}$$

**Annual diesel consumption:**
$$M_d = 2{,}800{,}000 \times 0.30 = 840{,}000\ \text{kg/yr}$$

**(ii) Annual diesel volume and cost:**
$$V_d = \frac{840{,}000}{0.85} = 988{,}235\ \text{L/yr}$$

$$C_d = 988{,}235 \times 90 = ₹8.894\ \text{crore/yr}$$

**(iii) Economic feasibility of solar hybrid substitution:**

Annual savings (if solar fully replaces diesel):
$$\Delta C = 8.894 - 0.04 = ₹8.854\ \text{crore/yr (fuel + O&M avoided minus solar O&M)}$$

Simple Payback:
$$\text{SPP} = \frac{5.00}{8.854} = 0.565\ \text{years} \approx 6.8\ \text{months}$$

PV of savings over 25 years at 8% discount (annuity factor for 25 years at 8% ≈ 10.675):
$$\text{PV}_{\text{savings}} = 8.854 \times 10.675 = ₹94.52\ \text{crore}$$

PV of O&M (PV of ₹0.04 cr/yr for 25 yr):
$$\text{PV}_{\text{O\&M}} = 0.04 \times 10.675 = ₹0.427\ \text{crore}$$

NPV:
$$\text{NPV} = -5.00 + 94.52 - 0.427 = ₹89.09\ \text{crore}$$

**Conclusion:** The substitution is *highly economically attractive* with payback under 7 months and NPV exceeding ₹89 crore over the 25-year life. Additionally, ~2300 tonnes of CO₂/year are avoided (using 2.68 kg CO₂/L diesel). **The substitution is strongly recommended** subject to solar resource assessment (kWh/m²/day) and battery sizing for night-load coverage.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** between kg and tonnes, MJ and GJ, kWh and TOE. Always show conversion factors explicitly.
> 2. **Assuming 1:1 substitution by mass** — a cardinal error. Always derive $R_m = \text{NCV}_1 / \text{NCV}_2$.
> 3. **Ignoring efficiency change** after substitution. Some substitutes reduce efficiency (e.g., FO → NG efficiency drop from 86% to 84%); failing to account for this inflates savings.
> 4. **Conflating fuel substitution with energy conservation** — substitution changes the *source*, not the *demand*. Conservation reduces demand; substitution changes supply.
> 5. **Omitting annual operating hours** in cost calculations — many students forget that 6000 hr/yr vs. 8000 hr/yr alters payback by 33%.
> 6. **Not stating assumptions** (load factor, fuel price volatility, discount rate) — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for unstated assumptions.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Fuel Substitution** = replacing one fuel with another for the *same energy service*; **Energy Substitution** = broader, includes switching *energy carriers*.
- **Master formula:** $R_m = \text{NCV}_1 / \text{NCV}_2$. Never assume mass equivalence.
- **Three pillars of substitution decision:** Technical compatibility + Economic viability + Environmental benefit.
- **Substitutability elasticity** $\sigma$ quantifies the ease of switching; $\sigma \to \infty$ = perfect substitute.
- **Energy parity check:** $\text{NCV}_2 \geq 0.85 \cdot \text{NCV}_1$ (recommended threshold).
- **Equivalent output equation:** $Q = m \cdot \text{NCV} \cdot \eta$ must hold *before and after* substitution.
- **Common substitutions:** Coal → NG; FO → Biomass; Diesel → Solar hybrid; LPG → Induction; Petrol → Ethanol.
- **Emission accounting:** Coal ≈ 94.6 t CO₂/TJ; FO ≈ 77.4 t CO₂/TJ; NG ≈ 56.1 t CO₂/TJ; Biomass ≈ 0 (carbon-neutral).
- **Economic metrics:** SPP, NPV, IRR, LCOE — SPP < 3 years is industry-preferred.
- **Conversion benchmarks:** 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ = 11,630 kWh; 1 TCE = 29.3076 GJ.
- **Decision flow:** Audit → Output spec → Candidate screening → Compatibility check → Economic analysis → Emission analysis → Implementation.
- **Always state assumptions:** load factor, operating hours, fuel prices, discount rate, efficiency post-substitution.
- **CO₂ reduction in substitution problems** often drives additional revenue (carbon credits, ESCerts in India).
- **KTU 2024 framing:** Tie every substitution answer to **decarbonization**, **energy security**, and **cost reduction** — the triple-bottom-line.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Audit Instruments and Metering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy Audit Instruments and Metering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Energy Audit Instruments and Metering** refers to the systematic suite of calibrated measurement devices, sensors, transducers, and data acquisition systems employed during the *Measurement and Verification (M&V)* phase of an energy audit to quantify, record, and analyze the energy performance of electrical, thermal, mechanical, and fluid systems within an industrial, commercial, or institutional facility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition to Memorize:**
> *Energy audit instruments are precision measurement tools used to gather field data on energy consumption, power quality, temperature, flow, pressure, speed, illumination, and combustion efficiency, forming the empirical foundation upon which energy conservation opportunities (ECOs) are identified and quantified.*

These instruments form the **"eyes and ears"** of the energy auditor. Without accurate field measurements, an energy audit degenerates into theoretical guesswork. The accuracy of the final energy-saving recommendations (typically quantified in **kWh/year**, **kW peak demand reduction**, or **MTOE — Million Tonne of Oil Equivalent**) is directly proportional to the accuracy of the instruments used.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of an energy auditor as a **medical doctor for an industry**.

- Just as a doctor uses a **stethoscope** (to hear heart sounds), a **thermometer** (to measure body temperature), and a **blood pressure monitor** (to measure BP) before prescribing medicine, an energy auditor uses:
  - A **Power Analyzer** instead of a stethoscope (to "listen" to the electrical heartbeat of the plant)
  - A **Contact Thermometer / Pyrometer** instead of a body thermometer (to measure surface/flame temperatures)
  - A **Manometer / Pressure Gauge** instead of a BP monitor (to measure steam/air pressure)

- The doctor never prescribes medicine without first examining the patient. Similarly, an **energy auditor cannot propose Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** without first measuring the baseline energy consumption of every load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Car Mechanic's Toolkit:**
> Imagine a mechanic tuning a car for maximum fuel efficiency. He needs an OBD scanner (to read engine parameters), a tachometer (to measure RPM), and a multimeter (to test the battery). Without these tools, he is tuning the car "blind." Energy audit instruments play exactly this role in industrial plants — they convert invisible energy flows into visible, quantifiable data.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Energy Audit Metering

The following standard units govern all energy audit measurements (must be memorized in **bold**):

| Quantity | SI Unit | Common Industrial Unit |
|----------|---------|------------------------|
| Electrical Energy | **Joule (J)** | **kWh** (most common billing unit) |
| Electrical Power | **Watt (W)** | **kW**, **MW** |
| Voltage | **Volt (V)** | **kV** (transmission) |
| Current | **Ampere (A)** | **kA** |
| Frequency | **Hertz (Hz)** | **Hz** |
| Power Factor | Dimensionless | **0.85 lag** (typical industrial) |
| Temperature | **Kelvin (K)** | **°C** (industrial) |
| Pressure | **Pascal (Pa)** | **bar**, **psi** |
| Flow Rate | **m³/s** | **litre/min (LPM)**, **m³/hr** |
| Humidity | **kg water/kg dry air** | **% RH** |
| Illuminance | **Lux (lx)** | **foot-candle (fc)** |
| Rotational Speed | **rad/s** | **RPM** |
| Harmonics (THD) | **% of fundamental** | **% THD** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 1,000 Wh = 1 unit** (the unit appearing on every domestic electricity bill in India).

### 1.4 Classification of Energy Audit Instruments — Top-Level View

Energy audit instruments can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **By Physical Domain of Measurement:**
   - **Electrical parameters** (V, I, P, Q, S, PF, E, f, harmonics)
   - **Thermal parameters** (T, ΔT, heat flux, flue gas temperature)
   - **Mechanical parameters** (speed, vibration, torque)
   - **Fluid parameters** (flow, pressure, ΔP, level)
   - **Environmental parameters** (lux, humidity, CO/CO₂ concentration)

2. **By Portability:**
   - **Portable / Handheld** (used by auditors in the field)
   - **Fixed / Online** (permanently installed for continuous M&V)

3. **By Signal Output:**
   - **Indicating** (analog dial / digital display)
   - **Recording / Logging** (data loggers, chart recorders)
   - **Transmitting** (4–20 mA, 0–10 V, RS-485, Modbus, IoT)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Classification & Working Principles of Key Instruments

#### A. ELECTRICAL INSTRUMENTS

**1. Digital Multimeter (DMM)**
- Measures **Voltage (AC/DC), Current (AC/DC), Resistance, Continuity, Diode test, Frequency, Capacitance**.
- Working principle: Uses **Successive Approximation ADC** for DC, and **True RMS converter** for AC.
- Typical accuracy: **±0.5% to ±2%** of reading.
- Use in audit: Quick spot checks, battery voltage, insulation resistance.

**2. Clamp Meter (Clamp-on Ammeter)**
- Measures AC/DC current **without breaking the circuit** (uses Hall-effect sensor or current transformer).
- The jaws clamp around a single conductor, and the induced flux gives current reading.
- **Key warning:** Clamp only around ONE conductor, not the entire cable (otherwise the net flux from phase + neutral cancels out).
- Typical range: **0.1 A to 1000 A AC**.

**3. Power Analyzer (3-Phase Power Quality Analyzer)**
- The **single most important instrument** in an electrical energy audit.
- Simultaneously measures: **V_RMS, I_RMS, kW, kVAR, kVA, PF, kWh, frequency, THD, individual harmonics up to 50th order, voltage sags/swells, transients**.
- Uses **4 CTs + 3 PTs** (or direct voltage sensing) to interface with a 3-phase, 4-wire system.
- Data logged over days/weeks using internal memory or SD card.
- Typical accuracy: **±0.1% to ±0.5%** of reading (Class 0.2 / Class 0.5 meters per IS 14697 / IEC 62053-22).
- Examples: Fluke 435-II, Hioki PW3198, Circutor AR6, Schneider PM8000.

**4. Energy Meter (kWh Meter)**
- Used to record cumulative energy consumption.
- Types: **Electromechanical (induction type)**, **Electronic (static)**, **Smart meter (with communication)**.
- Accuracy classes: **Class 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0** (per IS 13010 / IEC 62053-22).

**5. Tachometer**
- Measures rotational speed of motors, fans, pumps (in **RPM**).
- Types: **Contact (mechanical/optical wheel)** or **Non-contact (laser / optical / stroboscopic)**.
- Optical tachometer uses visible light reflection from a piece of reflective tape stuck on the rotating shaft.

**6. Lux Meter (Light Meter)**
- Measures **illuminance** in **lux (lx)** or **foot-candles (fc)**.
- Used in lighting audit to verify compliance with **IS 3646 / NBC 2016** standards (e.g., 150–200 lx for offices, 300–500 lx for reading rooms).
- Sensor is a **CIE-standardized photopic filter** (V(λ) curve) over a silicon photodiode.

**7. Megger (Insulation Resistance Tester)**
- Applies **500 V DC or 1000 V DC** between conductor and earth.
- Measures insulation resistance in **MΩ or GΩ**.
- Healthy insulation: **> 1 MΩ per kV** of operating voltage (e.g., 11 kV motor → > 11 MΩ).
- Used to detect insulation degradation in motors, cables, transformers.

**8. Earth Resistance Tester**
- Measures earth electrode resistance using **fall-of-potential method**.
- Acceptable value: **< 1 Ω** for large substations, **< 5 Ω** for domestic, **< 10 Ω** for lightning protection.

**9. Phase Sequence Indicator (PSI) / Phase Rotation Meter**
- Determines the **phase sequence (R-Y-B or R-B-Y)** of a 3-phase supply.
- Critical for correct connection of 3-phase motors to prevent reverse rotation.

**10. Harmonic Analyzer**
- Sub-function of a power quality analyzer.
- Computes **THD (Total Harmonic Distortion)** in % using FFT (Fast Fourier Transform).
- Standards: **IEEE 519-2014** for limits (5% THD on voltage at PCC for V ≤ 1 kV).

#### B. THERMAL INSTRUMENTS

**11. Contact Thermometer (RTD / Thermocouple / Thermistor)**
- **RTD (Pt-100):** Range **−200 °C to +850 °C**, accuracy **±0.1 °C**, linear output. Used for boiler water, chilled water.
- **Thermocouple (K-type, J-type):** Range up to **+1300 °C** (K-type), rugged, fast response. Used for flue gas, furnace.
- **Thermistor:** Highly sensitive but limited range, used in HVAC.

**12. Non-Contact Infrared Pyrometer (IR Thermometer)**
- Measures surface temperature by detecting **black-body radiation** in the infrared band (8–14 µm).
- Used for: hot surfaces, electrical panels (loose connections), furnace walls, steam pipes.
- **Critical parameter:** **Emissivity (ε)** setting (0.1 for polished metal to 1.0 for black body). Incorrect emissivity → large error.

**13. Combustion Analyzer / Flue Gas Analyzer**
- Measures **O₂, CO, CO₂, NOₓ, SO₂, flue gas temperature, efficiency** in boiler exhaust.
- Determines **combustion efficiency** from the **O₂/CO₂ ratio** and **flue gas temperature**.
- Modern portable units: Testo 340, Bacharach, Kane.

#### C. FLUID & MECHANICAL INSTRUMENTS

**14. Manometer (U-tube / Differential / Digital)**
- Measures **pressure difference (ΔP)**.
- Used across orifice plates, Venturi, filters, condensers to compute flow rate.

**15. Ultrasonic Flow Meter**
- Measures flow rate of liquids in **closed pipes non-invasively** using **transit-time** or **Doppler** principle.
- Clamp-on transducers are ideal for chilled water, hot water, and steam condensate lines.

**16. Pressure Gauge / Transmitter**
- Bourdon tube (local), diaphragm (sanitary), strain gauge (electronic 4–20 mA output).
- Used for steam, compressed air, hydraulic, pneumatic systems.

**17. Anemometer**
- Measures **air velocity** in ducts, fume hoods, clean rooms (in **m/s** or **fpm**).
- Types: Vane (propeller), hot-wire, ultrasonic.

**18. Stroboscope**
- "Freezes" a rotating object by flashing light at a frequency matching the rotation.
- Used to inspect fan blades, motor cooling fans, and identify slip in induction motors.

#### D. DATA ACQUISITION & LOGGING

**19. Data Logger**
- Standalone device that records measurements at programmed intervals (1 sec to 1 hour).
- Modern loggers are **IoT-enabled** with **GSM/Wi-Fi/Ethernet** for cloud upload.
- A single logger can monitor energy, temperature, and humidity for weeks.

**20. Smart Sensors & IoT Devices**
- Wireless, battery-powered sensors (e.g., LoRa, Zigbee, BLE) for distributed measurements.
- Form the **backbone of Industry 4.0 / Smart Factory** energy monitoring.

### 2.2 Why These Specific Instruments? — The Engineering Rationale

Every instrument in the auditor's toolkit is chosen to answer **one specific engineering question**:

| Engineering Question Asked During Audit | Instrument Used | Key Reading Obtained |
|---|---|---|
| How much electrical power is the plant drawing? | 3-Phase Power Analyzer | kW, kVA, kVAR, PF |
| What is the power quality of the supply? | Power Quality Analyzer | THD %, individual harmonics |
| How much steam is being used? | Orifice + Manometer / Vortex / Ultrasonic | kg/hr |
| What is the combustion efficiency? | Flue Gas Analyzer | O₂%, CO ppm, η_combustion% |
| Is insulation healthy in this motor? | Megger | MΩ |
| Is the lighting level adequate? | Lux Meter | Lux |
| What is the motor load %? | Power Analyzer + Tachometer | kW, RPM, slip |
| How much heat is being lost from this pipe? | IR Pyrometer + surface probe | ΔT °C |
| What is the cooling water flow? | Ultrasonic flow meter | m³/hr |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi$ | 3-phase real power | kW, $V_L$ = line voltage, $I_L$ = line current |
| 2 | $Q = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \sin\phi$ | 3-phase reactive power | kVAR |
| 3 | $S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L$ | 3-phase apparent power | kVA |
| 4 | $PF = \cos\phi = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | Power factor | dimensionless, 0 to 1 |
| 5 | $E = P \cdot t$ | Energy consumed in time $t$ | kWh |
| 6 | $THD_V = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{50} V_h^2}}{V_1} \cdot 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion (voltage) | IEEE 519 |
| 7 | $THD_I = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h=2}^{50} I_h^2}}{I_1} \cdot 100\%$ | Total Harmonic Distortion (current) | IEEE 519 |
| 8 | $Q_m = C_d \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P \cdot \rho}$ | Orifice plate flow rate | kg/s, $C_d$ = discharge coefficient |
| 9 | $\eta_{combustion} = 100 - \left(\dfrac{T_{fg} - T_{air}}{CO_2\%}\right) \cdot k$ | Siegert's empirical efficiency formula | % |
| 10 | $I_{fl} = \dfrac{P_{motor}}{\sqrt{3} \cdot V \cdot \eta_{motor} \cdot PF}$ | Full load current of motor | A |
| 11 | $Load\,\% = \dfrac{kW_{measured}}{kW_{rated}} \cdot 100$ | Motor loading percentage | % |
| 12 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip of induction motor | dimensionless |
| 13 | $R_{earth} \le \dfrac{V_{supply}}{I_{max\,allowed}}$ | Earth resistance limit | Ω, $I_{max}$ typically 50–100 A |
| 14 | $Lux\,Req = \dfrac{Lumens}{Area}$ | Illumination level | lx |
| 15 | $E_{savings} = E_{baseline} - E_{post\,retrofit}$ | M&V savings calculation | kWh/year |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exams, when a question on instrument selection is asked, **always mention the accuracy class, range, principle of operation, and typical application** — not just the name of the instrument.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern industrial practice, the integration of these instruments into a **Building Management System (BMS)** or **Energy Management System (EMS)** enables:

- **Real-time energy monitoring** at the load level (per motor, per HVAC, per lighting circuit).
- **Automated load shedding** during peak demand to avoid **kVA demand penalties** in the electricity bill.
- **Power quality compliance** documentation as mandated by **IEEE 519-2014** and **CEA (Central Electricity Authority) regulations** in India.
- **ISO 50001:2018** Energy Management System certification requires continuous M&V using these instruments.
- **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme** under BEE, India mandates energy audits for Designated Consumers using these instruments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Three-Phase Real Power Formula Used in Power Analyzers

**Starting Point:** A balanced 3-phase, 3-wire (or 4-wire) system has three instantaneous phase voltages and currents:

$$
v_{RN}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)
$$

$$
v_{YN}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°)
$$

$$
v_{BN}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + 120°)
$$

$$
i_R(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - \phi)
$$

$$
i_Y(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t - 120° - \phi)
$$

$$
i_B(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + 120° - \phi)
$$

**Step 1 — Total Instantaneous Power:**
The total instantaneous three-phase power is the algebraic sum of the powers in all three phases:

$$
p_{total}(t) = v_{RN} \cdot i_R + v_{YN} \cdot i_Y + v_{BN} \cdot i_B
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and Expand:**
Substituting the expressions and using the trigonometric product-to-sum identity $\sin A \cdot \sin B = \dfrac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$:

$$
p_{total}(t) = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot V_m \cdot I_m \cdot \cos\phi - \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_m \cdot I_m \cdot [\cos(2\omega t - \phi) + \cos(2\omega t - 240° - \phi) + \cos(2\omega t + 240° - \phi)]
$$

**Step 3 — The Cosine Sum Vanishes:**
The three second-harmonic cosine terms are equal in magnitude and displaced by 120° in phase. Their sum is identically zero:

$$
\cos\theta + \cos(\theta - 120°) + \cos(\theta + 120°) = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Resulting Constant Power:**

$$
p_{total}(t) = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot V_m \cdot I_m \cdot \cos\phi
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to RMS Values:**
Using $V_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{RMS,phase}$ and $I_m = \sqrt{2} \cdot I_{RMS,phase}$:

$$
p_{total} = \dfrac{3}{2} \cdot (\sqrt{2} V_{ph}) \cdot (\sqrt{2} I_{ph}) \cdot \cos\phi = 3 \cdot V_{ph} \cdot I_{ph} \cdot \cos\phi
$$

**Step 6 — Express in Line Quantities (Star Connection):**
In star: $V_L = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{ph}$ and $I_L = I_{ph}$:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{3\phi} = 3 \cdot \dfrac{V_L}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi\,}
$$

**Textual Conversion Logic:** A power analyzer measures $V_L$ and $I_L$ using PTs and CTs, samples them synchronously via ADC, multiplies sample-by-sample, averages over one fundamental cycle, and displays $\sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Savings Calculation Using M&V (IPMVP Method)

**Problem Setup:** A 75 kW motor was measured over 30 days. Average loading = 60%. Power analyzer recorded:
- Annual baseline energy = $E_1 = 1{,}05{,}600$ kWh/year
- After installing a VFD, annual energy = $E_2 = 79{,}200$ kWh/year
- VFD cost = ₹ 3,00,000; electricity tariff = ₹ 8/kWh; demand charge = ₹ 600/kVA/month; motor PF improved from 0.78 to 0.92.

**Required:** (a) Annual energy savings, (b) Annual cost savings, (c) Simple payback period.

**Step (a) — Annual Energy Savings:**

$$
E_{saved} = E_1 - E_2 = 1{,}05{,}600 - 79{,}200 = 26{,}400 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**Step (b) — Annual Cost Savings from Energy:**

$$
C_{energy} = E_{saved} \times \text{Tariff} = 26{,}400 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 2{,}11{,}200 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step (c) — Annual Cost Savings from PF Improvement:**
Original kVA = $\dfrac{kW}{PF_{old}} = \dfrac{60 \text{ kW}}{0.78} = 76.92$ kVA
New kVA = $\dfrac{60}{0.92} = 65.22$ kVA
Demand charge saved = $(76.92 - 65.22) \times 600 \times 12 = 11.70 \times 7200 = \text{₹ } 84{,}240$ per year

**Step (d) — Total Annual Savings:**

$$
C_{total} = 2{,}11{,}200 + 84{,}240 = \text{₹ } 2{,}95{,}440 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step (e) — Simple Payback Period:**

$$
SPP = \dfrac{\text{Investment}}{\text{Annual Savings}} = \dfrac{3{,}00{,}000}{2{,}95{,}440} = 1.016 \text{ years} \approx 1.02 \text{ years}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The VFD project pays back in just over 1 year — strongly recommended for execution.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Motor Loading Using Slip Method (Slip → Load %)

**Problem:** A 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a nameplate full-load speed of 1455 RPM. A stroboscope/tachometer measures the actual running speed as 1430 RPM.

**Step 1 — Synchronous Speed:**

$$
N_s = \dfrac{120 \cdot f}{P} = \dfrac{120 \cdot 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 2 — Actual Slip (in RPM):**

$$
\Delta N = N_s - N_r = 1500 - 1430 = 70 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 3 — Full-Load Slip:**

$$
\Delta N_{fl} = N_s - N_{fl} = 1500 - 1455 = 45 \text{ RPM}
$$

**Step 4 — Approximate Load % (Slip is approximately linear with load in operating region):**

$$
Load\,\% = \dfrac{\Delta N}{\Delta N_{fl}} \times 100 = \dfrac{70}{45} \times 100 = 155.6\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A slip-based loading > 100% indicates the motor is **overloaded** — the motor is being driven beyond its rated capacity, leading to overheating, reduced life, and poor power factor. Either the motor is incorrectly sized or a VFD/load reduction is required.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Combustion Efficiency Using Siegert's Formula

**Given:** Natural gas-fired boiler, O₂ in flue gas = 4%, CO₂ = 10%, flue gas temperature $T_{fg} = 220$ °C, ambient air temperature $T_{air} = 30$ °C, fuel = natural gas.

**Siegert's Formula (Natural Gas):**

$$
\eta_{combustion} = 100 - \left( \dfrac{T_{fg} - T_{air}}{CO_2\%} \right)
$$

**Step 1 — Compute the Loss Term:**

$$
\dfrac{T_{fg} - T_{air}}{CO_2\%} = \dfrac{220 - 30}{10} = \dfrac{190}{10} = 19\%
$$

**Step 2 — Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{combustion} = 100 - 19 = 81\%
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A typical efficient natural gas boiler operates at **82–88%** combustion efficiency. 81% is acceptable but indicates room for improvement via **excess air reduction** (O₂ currently 4% — could be trimmed to 2–3%) and **economizer installation** to recover flue gas heat.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Instrument Selection Cheat Sheet

| Parameter to Measure | Recommended Instrument | Alternative | Typical Range | Accuracy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| AC Voltage (LV) | Digital Multimeter | — | 0–750 V | ±0.5% |
| AC Current (LV) | Clamp Meter | Power Analyzer | 0.1–1000 A | ±2% |
| 3-φ Power / Energy | Power Analyzer | Energy meter + CTs/PTs | 0–1000 kW | ±0.5% |
| Power Factor | Power Analyzer | — | 0.2 lag – 1 | ±1% |
| Harmonics (THD) | Power Quality Analyzer | — | 0–100% | ±0.1% |
| Frequency | Power Analyzer | DMM | 45–55 Hz | ±0.01 Hz |
| Insulation Resistance | Megger | — | 0–100 GΩ | ±5% |
| Earth Resistance | Earth Tester | Megger (low range) | 0–2000 Ω | ±2% |
| Surface Temp (0–500 °C) | IR Pyrometer | Thermocouple probe | −32 to +1650 °C | ±2 °C |
| Flue Gas Temp | K-type Thermocouple | IR Pyrometer | 0–1200 °C | ±2.5 °C |
| Steam / Water Flow | Ultrasonic Flow Meter | Orifice + Manometer | 0.1–10 m/s | ±1–2% |
| Pressure (steam) | Bourdon / Strain Gauge | Manometer | 0–40 bar | ±0.5% |
| Air Velocity | Vane Anemometer | Hot-wire | 0–30 m/s | ±3% |
| Rotational Speed | Optical Tachometer | Stroboscope | 1–99,999 RPM | ±0.05% |
| Illuminance | Lux Meter | — | 0–200,000 lx | ±3% |
| Humidity | Hygrometer / DHT sensor | — | 0–100% RH | ±2% RH |
| Flue Gas Composition | Combustion Analyzer | Orsat apparatus | 0–25% O₂ | ±0.1% |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any six commonly used instruments in an electrical energy audit. State the quantity measured and the principle of operation for each in one line.*

**Model Answer (6 points × ½ mark each):**

1. **Digital Multimeter (DMM):** Measures V, I, R using successive-approximation ADC.
2. **Clamp Meter:** Measures AC current non-invasively using magnetic flux sensing / Hall effect.
3. **3-Phase Power Analyzer:** Measures kW, kVA, kVAR, PF, THD using simultaneous sampling ADC and DSP.
4. **Tachometer:** Measures motor RPM using optical reflection from a tape on the shaft.
5. **Lux Meter:** Measures illuminance (lux) using a photopic-filtered silicon photodiode.
6. **Infrared Pyrometer:** Measures surface temperature remotely using Stefan-Boltzmann black-body radiation law.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's note: A tabular answer (instrument | quantity | principle) is preferred. Writing only names without principle/quantity will not fetch full marks.

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the difference between accuracy and resolution of a measuring instrument? Why is calibration important before an energy audit?*

**Model Answer:**

- **Accuracy:** Closeness of a measured value to the **true value**. Expressed as ±(%) of reading or ±(% of full scale + digit). Example: ±0.5% of reading.
- **Resolution:** The **smallest change** in input that the instrument can detect and display. Example: a DMM with 0.1 mV resolution cannot distinguish between 1.2345 V and 1.2346 V.
- **Why Calibration Matters:**
  1. To ensure the **traceability of measurements to national/international standards** (e.g., NABL, NIST).
  2. ISO 50001 requires **calibrated, traceable instruments** for all M&V data.
  3. Uncalibrated instruments give erroneous baseline → wrong ECM recommendations → wrong ROI calculation.
  4. To comply with **legal metrology** (Weights & Measures Act) for billing meters.

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Module 2 Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** *Classify the energy audit instruments based on the physical domain of measurement. Explain the working principle, accuracy, and typical application of a 3-phase power quality analyzer with a neat block diagram.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *A 3-phase, 415 V, 50 Hz induction motor draws a line current of 80 A at a power factor of 0.85 lag. The motor efficiency is 90%. A power quality analyzer measures the voltage THD as 6% and current THD as 25%. Compute:*
1. *Real power (kW) consumed*
2. *Apparent power (kVA)*
3. *Reactive power (kVAR)*
4. *Motor shaft output power (kW)*
5. *Whether the harmonic levels comply with IEEE 519-2014 limits (5% voltage THD and 8% current THD for PCC < 1 kV at I_sc/I_L ratio between 20 and 50). Comment on the compliance.* **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Classification of Energy Audit Instruments (3 marks):**
Instruments are classified into:
1. **Electrical** — Multimeter, clamp meter, power analyzer, megger, harmonic analyzer
2. **Thermal** — RTD, thermocouple, IR pyrometer, combustion analyzer
3. **Fluid** — Manometer, ultrasonic flow meter, anemometer, pressure gauge
4. **Mechanical** — Tachometer, stroboscope, vibration analyzer
5. **Environmental** — Lux meter, hygrometer, sound level meter
6. **Data Acquisition** — Data loggers, IoT sensors, EMS platforms

**Working Principle of 3-Phase Power Quality Analyzer (2.5 marks):**
- **Voltage sensing** via resistive divider or PTs → **Current sensing** via CTs or Hall-effect sensors.
- Signals are **simultaneously sampled** by a multi-channel ADC at ≥ 256 samples/cycle.
- **DSP** computes:
  - True RMS voltage and current
  - Real, reactive, apparent power
  - Power factor
  - FFT → individual harmonics → THD
  - Frequency, voltage unbalance, sags/swells
- Data stored in internal memory, exported via USB/Ethernet.

**Accuracy & Application (1.5 marks):**
- Accuracy: **±0.1% to ±0.5% of reading** (Class 0.2/0.5 per IEC 62053-22).
- Application: **Baseline M&V, ISO 50001 documentation, IEEE 519 compliance, penalty avoidance, ECM validation**.

**Block Diagram (covered in Section 4.2 of notes).**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $V_L = 415$ V, $I_L = 80$ A, $PF = \cos\phi = 0.85$ lag, $\eta = 0.90$, $THD_V = 6\%$, $THD_I = 25\%$, $f = 50$ Hz.

**(1) Real Power (kW):**

$$
P = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi = \sqrt{3} \cdot 415 \cdot 80 \cdot 0.85
$$

$$
P = 1.732 \cdot 415 \cdot 80 \cdot 0.85 = 1.732 \cdot 28{,}220 = 48{,}877 \text{ W} \approx 48.88 \text{ kW}
$$

**(2) Apparent Power (kVA):**

$$
S = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L = 1.732 \cdot 415 \cdot 80 = 57{,}502 \text{ VA} \approx 57.50 \text{ kVA}
$$

**(3) Reactive Power (kVAR):**

$$
Q = S \cdot \sin\phi = 57.50 \cdot \sqrt{1 - 0.85^2} = 57.50 \cdot 0.5268 = 30.29 \text{ kVAR}
$$

**Cross-check using** $Q = \sqrt{S^2 - P^2}$:
$$
Q = \sqrt{57.50^2 - 48.88^2} = \sqrt{3306.25 - 2389.25} = \sqrt{917} = 30.28 \text{ kVAR} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**(4) Shaft Output Power:**

$$
P_{shaft} = P_{input} \times \eta = 48.88 \times 0.90 = 43.99 \text{ kW} \approx 44 \text{ kW}
$$

**(5) IEEE 519-2014 Compliance Check:**

| Parameter | Measured | IEEE 519 Limit (PCC < 1 kV, 20 < I_sc/I_L < 50) | Compliant? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage THD | 6% | **5%** | ❌ **NON-COMPLIANT** |
| Current THD | 25% | **8%** | ❌ **NON-COMPLIANT** |

**Comment:** Both voltage and current THD **exceed** IEEE 519-2014 limits. **Recommended actions:**
- Install **passive harmonic filters** to reduce current THD.
- Install **active harmonic filter (AHF)** for dynamic compensation.
- Check for the presence of non-linear loads (VFDs, UPS, rectifiers) causing the harmonics.
- Add **isolation transformers** with K-factor rating.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in this Question:**
> 1. Forgetting $\sqrt{3}$ for 3-phase — yields exactly half the correct answer.
> 2. Confusing line voltage with phase voltage (415 V is **line**; phase = 240 V).
> 3. Computing THD compliance against the wrong IEEE 519 category — read the table carefully for the relevant $I_{sc}/I_L$ ratio.
> 4. Failing to cross-verify $Q$ using $Q = \sqrt{S^2 - P^2}$ (validates arithmetic).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the instruments used for measuring the following in an energy audit of a thermal boiler system: (i) Steam flow rate, (ii) Flue gas temperature, (iii) Combustion efficiency, (iv) Excess air. For each, mention the instrument, principle, and a typical reading range.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *A 50 kW motor runs for 4,000 hours per year. A power analyzer measures the loading at 35%. The motor PF is 0.72, efficiency 0.89. If a VFD is installed to control the speed at part-load conditions, the average loading reduces to 30% but PF improves to 0.90 and efficiency improves to 0.92 due to lower losses. Electricity tariff is ₹ 9/kWh. Compute: (i) Original annual energy consumption, (ii) New annual energy consumption, (iii) Annual energy savings in kWh and ₹, (iv) Equivalent annual reduction in CO₂ emissions (assume 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh grid emission factor).* **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**(i) Steam Flow Rate:**
- **Instrument:** Orifice plate + Differential manometer (or Vortex flow meter, or Ultrasonic flow meter)
- **Principle:** Orifice creates a pressure drop ($\Delta P$); using Bernoulli's equation, $Q_m = C_d A \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$
- **Range:** 0.1 to 50,000 kg/hr depending on pipe size; accuracy ±1–2%

**(ii) Flue Gas Temperature:**
- **Instrument:** K-type thermocouple (in-situ) or IR pyrometer (across duct)
- **Principle:** Seebeck effect (thermocouple) or black-body radiation (IR)
- **Range:** 0–1,300 °C (K-type); typical flue gas: 150–250 °C for efficient boiler; accuracy ±2.5 °C

**(iii) Combustion Efficiency:**
- **Instrument:** Portable flue gas / combustion analyzer (e.g., Testo 340, Bacharach)
- **Principle:** Electrochemical sensors (O₂ = zirconia/lead oxide cell; CO = NDIR; NOₓ = chemiluminescence)
- **Range:** O₂: 0–25%, CO: 0–4000 ppm, efficiency: 0–100%

**(iv) Excess Air:**
- **Instrument:** Same flue gas analyzer (reads O₂ % directly)
- **Principle:** Excess air is computed from O₂% using the stoichiometric relation:
$$
\%EA = \dfrac{20.9}{20.9 - O_2\%} \times \dfrac{\text{stoichiometric O}_2 \text{ for fuel}}{\text{stoichiometric O}_2 + \text{N}_2}
$$
- For natural gas: typical optimum = 5–10% excess air.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $P_{nameplate} = 50$ kW, $t = 4{,}000$ hr/year, Original load = 35%, $PF_1 = 0.72$, $\eta_1 = 0.89$, New load = 30%, $PF_2 = 0.90$, $\eta_2 = 0.92$, Tariff = ₹ 9/kWh, Grid EF = 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh.

**(i) Original Annual Energy Consumption:**

$$
P_{1,input} = \dfrac{P_{nameplate} \times \text{Load\%}}{\eta_1} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.35}{0.89} = \dfrac{17.5}{0.89} = 19.66 \text{ kW (input)}
$$

$$
E_1 = P_{1,input} \times t = 19.66 \times 4{,}000 = 78{,}652 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**(ii) New Annual Energy Consumption:**

$$
P_{2,input} = \dfrac{50 \times 0.30}{0.92} = \dfrac{15}{0.92} = 16.30 \text{ kW (input)}
$$

$$
E_2 = 16.30 \times 4{,}000 = 65{,}217 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

**(iii) Annual Savings:**

$$
E_{saved} = 78{,}652 - 65{,}217 = 13{,}435 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

$$
\text{₹ Savings} = 13{,}435 \times 9 = \text{₹ } 1{,}20{,}915 \text{ per year}
$$

**(iv) CO₂ Reduction:**

$$
\Delta CO_2 = 13{,}435 \times 0.82 = 11{,}017 \text{ kg CO}_2 = 11.02 \text{ tonne CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Module 2 Specific):**
> 1. When the problem mentions "loading 35%", students often directly take $P = 50 \times 0.35$ as input power — this is the **shaft power**, not the input. Always divide by efficiency.
> 2. Forget to include kVA demand savings from PF improvement → missing 2–3 marks.
> 3. Forgetting to multiply by grid emission factor → no CO₂ credit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU 2024 Module 2: Energy Audit Instruments and Metering**

- ✅ **Power Analyzer is the single most important instrument** in an electrical energy audit. Memorize its block diagram (sensors → ADC → DSP → FFT → display).
- ✅ Always remember the **3-phase real power formula:** $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$ — used in nearly every numerical.
- ✅ **Clamp meter must clamp on one conductor only** — clamping on full cable = zero net flux = zero reading.
- ✅ **Megger applies DC voltage (500 V or 1000 V)** — measures insulation in MΩ, not for energized circuits.
- ✅ **RTD Pt-100 = 100 Ω at 0 °C**, linear, accurate, used for water/air temperature.
- ✅ **Thermocouple (K-type)** for high temperature (up to 1300 °C); **IR pyrometer** for non-contact measurement (need correct emissivity).
- ✅ **Ultrasonic flow meter** is non-invasive — ideal for retrofits on existing chilled water/condensate lines.
- ✅ **Lux meter** uses a photopic V(λ) filter — required for lighting compliance (IS 3646 / NBC 2016).
- ✅ **Siegert's formula** for combustion efficiency: $\eta = 100 - (T_{fg} - T_{air})/CO_2\%$ for natural gas.
- ✅ **Slip method** for motor loading: $Load\% = (N_s - N_r)/(N_s - N_{rated}) \times 100$.
- ✅ **THD** is computed via FFT; IEEE 519-2014 limit is **5% THD-V** and **8% THD-I** for LV systems.
- ✅ **Data loggers + IoT** are essential for ISO 50001 continuous M&V.
- ✅ **Calibration** of instruments must be **NABL-traceable**; otherwise audit data is inadmissible.
- ✅ **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ = 1 electricity unit.**
- ✅ **Power factor improvement savings include kVA demand charge reduction**, not just kWh reduction.
- ✅ **PAT scheme, ISO 50001, BEE star rating, ECBC** — all require documented measurement using these instruments.
- ✅ For **3-wire vs 4-wire systems** — use the correct wattmeter connection (2-wattmeter method for 3-wire, 3-wattmeter for 4-wire unbalanced).
- ✅ **Flue gas analyzer measures O₂, CO, CO₂, NOₓ, efficiency, excess air** — all in a single instrument.
- ✅ **Anemometer** units: m/s (SI) or fpm (HVAC). **Stroboscope** can also measure RPM by visual freezing.
- ✅ **Energy savings = Baseline Energy − Post-Retrofit Energy**, validated per **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement and Verification Protocol)**.
- ✅ **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** = Investment ÷ Annual Savings; for project to be viable, SPP should be < 3 years for most industrial ECMs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Top-10 Energy Audit Instruments (Power-CLIMATE-MD):**
> **P**ower Analyzer · **C**lamp Meter · **L**ux Meter · **I**R Pyrometer · **M**egger · **A**nemometer · **T**achometer · **E**arth Tester · **M**anometer · **D**ata Logger — commit this to memory for the 2-mark rapid-fire questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining Monitoring and Targeting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Defining Monitoring and Targeting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Monitoring and Targeting (M&T)** is defined as a *systematic, continuous, and structured management technique* that involves the regular measurement, recording, analysis, and reporting of energy consumption data within a defined organizational boundary. The primary objective of M&T is to:

1. **Establish a reliable Energy Baseline (EnB)** that represents normalized energy use under standard operating conditions.
2. **Set quantified energy performance targets** aligned with corporate energy policy and regulatory obligations.
3. **Detect, quantify, and diagnose deviations** (variances) between actual and expected energy use.
4. **Initiate timely corrective and preventive actions** to bring consumption back within acceptable limits, thereby sustaining continuous improvement in energy performance in line with **ISO 50001 (Energy Management Systems)** requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** Examiners expect students to explicitly mention that M&T is the *backbone of any Energy Management System (EnMS)*. Without effective M&T, the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle degenerates into guesswork.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Expansion | KTU Definition Context |
|------|-----------|------------------------|
| **EnPI** | Energy Performance Indicator | A quantitative metric (e.g., kWh/tonne, MJ/unit) reflecting energy performance |
| **EnB** | Energy Baseline | A quantitative reference point for energy performance over a defined period |
| **SEC** | Specific Energy Consumption | Energy used per unit of production/output |
| **M&V** | Measurement and Verification | Procedures to determine savings from ECMs (per IPMVP) |
| **CUSUM** | Cumulative Sum | Statistical technique to detect small persistent shifts in mean consumption |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Fitness Tracker for Energy"

Imagine you are trying to lose weight. You buy a **smart fitness band** (analogous to the M&T system). 

- The band **continuously monitors** your daily steps and calories (this is **Monitoring**).
- It **compares** your daily activity against the *target* of 10,000 steps set by your doctor (this is **Targeting**).
- When you have a "lazy" week and only average 4,000 steps, the band **alerts** you with a buzz and a red graph (this is **variance detection**).
- You then **take corrective action** – a morning jog, a stricter diet.
- Next week, you **review** and see the upward trend (this is the **feedback loop**).

In the *exact same way*, M&T acts as the **fitness tracker of a factory**. It continuously watches how much electricity the boiler is consuming, compares it with what it *should* be consuming for the current production level, and shouts "WARNING!" the moment a leak or inefficiency creeps in. 

> [!NOTE]
> **Why M&T matters (Real-world impact):** Industry studies indicate that without M&T, **15–30% of energy bills are wasted** due to un-detected leaks, poor controls, and operator complacency. M&T alone can typically deliver **5–15% savings** with *zero capital investment*, purely through awareness and operational discipline.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of an Effective M&T System

A robust M&T system rests on three fundamental pillars:

- **Pillar 1 – Measurement Infrastructure:** Accurate, calibrated meters (active energy, reactive energy, steam flow, fuel flow, compressed air flow). Sub-metering at the equipment/utility level is the cornerstone.
- **Pillar 2 – Information Architecture:** A reliable data acquisition system (DAS), data loggers, and a centralized database. Data must be stored at a frequency appropriate to the load profile (typically **15-minute intervals** for electrical systems, **hourly** for thermal).
- **Pillar 3 – Analytical Capability:** Statistical tools to normalize data, identify baselines, set targets, and detect variances (CUSUM, regression analysis, weather correction, production normalization).

### 2.2 The M&T Closed-Loop Cycle (How it works)

The system operates as a continuous 5-stage closed loop:

1. **Measure** energy use at strategic points (main meter, sub-meters) → collect raw kWh, m³, kg of steam data.
2. **Record** the data in a centralized Energy Information System (EIS) with timestamp and contextual variables (production output, ambient temperature, humidity).
3. **Compare** actual consumption against the pre-established **Energy Baseline (EnB)** adjusted for current operating conditions (this is called *normalization*).
4. **Analyze** any variance exceeding a pre-set threshold (e.g., ±2.5% to ±5%) using root-cause analysis techniques (Ishikawa, 5-Why).
5. **Act** by issuing work orders, modifying operating procedures, or commissioning Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs). **Report** findings to top management as part of the Management Review (per ISO 50001 Clause 9.3).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** All formulas below are high-yield. Practice the substitution of values and pay close attention to *units* – this is the most common pitfall in calculations.

| # | Formula / Equation | Variable Meaning | Standard Units | Application |
|---|--------------------|------------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{consumed}}{P_{produced}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption | $\text{MJ/unit}$ or $\text{kWh/tonne}$ | Comparing efficiency across periods of different output |
| 2 | $E_{adj} = E_{actual} \times \dfrac{HDD_{base}}{HDD_{actual}}$ | Weather-adjusted energy | $\text{kWh}$ | Removing weather influence from heating energy |
| 3 | $E_{adj} = E_{actual} \times \dfrac{P_{base}}{P_{actual}}$ | Production-normalized energy | $\text{kWh}$ | Removing production-volume influence |
| 4 | $HDD = \sum (T_{base} - T_{i})^{+}$ for $T_i < T_{base}$ | Heating Degree Days | ${}^{\circ}\text{C}\cdot\text{day}$ | Quantifying heating demand |
| 5 | $CDD = \sum (T_{i} - T_{base})^{+}$ for $T_i > T_{base}$ | Cooling Degree Days | ${}^{\circ}\text{C}\cdot\text{day}$ | Quantifying cooling demand |
| 6 | $C_{i} = \sum_{t=1}^{i} (X_t - \mu_0)$ | Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) statistic | $\text{unitless}$ or $\text{kWh}$ cumulative | Detecting small persistent shifts in mean |
| 7 | $S_{energy\, saving} = E_{baseline} - E_{reported}$ | Energy savings (IPMVP Option C) | $\text{kWh}$ or $\text{MWh}$ | Verifying ECM performance |
| 8 | $\% \text{Variance} = \dfrac{(E_{actual} - E_{baseline})}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | Percentage deviation | $\%$ | Trigger threshold for investigation |
| 9 | $E_{EnPI} = \alpha + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots + \beta_n X_n$ | Multi-variable regression baseline | $\text{kWh}$ | ISO 50002 compliant EnB model |
| 10 | $C_{v} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{X}} \times 100$ | Coefficient of Variation | $\%$ | Assessing data quality / measurement uncertainty |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Plants:** M&T is the operational heart of any **Energy Management System (EnMS)** certified to **ISO 50001:2018**. Without M&T, the "Check" phase of PDCA has no data.
- **Commercial Buildings (HVAC dominant):** Used to track HVAC performance against weather-normalized baselines; critical for **Energy Performance Contracts (EPCs)** and **ESCO** business models.
- **Utilities & Grid Operators:** M&T applied at substation level helps detect **non-technical losses (NTL)** and unauthorized tapping.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** **PAT (Perform, Achieve and Trade)** scheme of the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India – mandatory for Designated Consumers – is essentially a *macro-level M&T* mechanism.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Production-Normalized Energy Saving Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU 14-mark style):** A cement plant consumed **8,20,000 kWh** of electrical energy in October 2024 to produce **4,100 tonnes** of clinker. In November 2024, the plant produced **4,500 tonnes** of clinker and consumed **8,55,000 kWh**. Calculate the (a) Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) for each month, (b) Energy that *should* have been consumed in November had the October efficiency been maintained, and (c) The energy saved or wasted in November.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute the **October SEC (Baseline)**.

$$
SEC_{Oct} = \frac{E_{Oct}}{P_{Oct}} = \frac{820{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{4{,}100 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

$$
SEC_{Oct} = 200.00 \text{ kWh/tonne}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **November SEC (Actual)**.

$$
SEC_{Nov} = \frac{E_{Nov}}{P_{Nov}} = \frac{855{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{4{,}500 \text{ tonnes}}
$$

$$
SEC_{Nov} = 190.00 \text{ kWh/tonne}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **expected (baseline-adjusted) energy for November** assuming October's SEC persists.

$$
E_{expected} = SEC_{Oct} \times P_{Nov} = 200.00 \text{ kWh/tonne} \times 4{,}500 \text{ tonnes}
$$

$$
E_{expected} = 9{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the **Net Energy Saved (Positive) or Wasted (Negative)**.

$$
S_{energy} = E_{expected} - E_{actual} = 9{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}55{,}000
$$

$$
S_{energy} = +45{,}000 \text{ kWh (Saved)}
$$

**Step 5:** Express as a **percentage improvement**.

$$
\% \text{ Improvement} = \frac{45{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 5.00\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The November operation was *5% more efficient* than the October baseline. This is the typical M&T output used by an Energy Manager for monthly reporting to the Plant Head.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Heating Degree Day (HDD) Based Weather Normalization

**Problem Statement:** A boiler house in Kochi consumed **1,20,000 kWh** in December 2023. The average daily ambient temperature for December 2023 was **24 °C**, and the long-term (5-year) December average is **26.5 °C**. The base temperature for the building is **20 °C**. The energy manager's office claims that a **3% deterioration** in boiler efficiency over the year was masked by cooler weather. Normalize December 2023 energy against the long-term climate to reveal the "true" performance.

#### Solution:

**Step 1:** Compute the **actual HDD** for December 2023 (31 days).

$$
HDD_{actual} = \sum (T_{base} - T_{daily}) = 31 \times (24 - 20) = 31 \times 4 = 124 \text{ °C·day}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the **baseline HDD** (long-term).

$$
HDD_{base} = 31 \times (26.5 - 20) = 31 \times 6.5 = 201.5 \text{ °C·day}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **weather normalization formula**.

$$
E_{normalized} = E_{actual} \times \frac{HDD_{base}}{HDD_{actual}}
$$

$$
E_{normalized} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times \frac{201.5}{124} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 1.6250
$$

$$
E_{normalized} = 1{,}95{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4:** Interpret the result.

The plant actually *would* have consumed **1,95,000 kWh** in a "typical" December. Since it only consumed **1,20,000 kWh**, there is a hidden saving of **75,000 kWh** that the weather-mild year artificially created. The energy manager must *de-rate* the apparent efficiency gain before drawing conclusions.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: CUSUM (Cumulative Sum) Trend Detection

**Problem Statement:** The daily electricity consumption (kWh) of a chiller for 12 consecutive days is: **480, 475, 490, 482, 488, 485, 505, 510, 508, 512, 515, 520**. The historical baseline mean is **μ₀ = 480 kWh**. Construct the CUSUM chart and determine the day on which a *persistent upward shift* became statistically significant. (Use a reference value **K = 5 kWh**, and decision interval **H = 20 kWh**.)

#### Solution:

**Step 1:** Calculate deviation from baseline for each day: $X_t - \mu_0$.

| Day $t$ | $X_t$ | $X_t - \mu_0$ | $C_t = \sum (X_t - \mu_0)$ |
|---------|-------|---------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | 480 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 475 | −5 | −5 |
| 3 | 490 | +10 | +5 |
| 4 | 482 | +2 | +7 |
| 5 | 488 | +8 | +15 |
| 6 | 485 | +5 | +20 |
| 7 | 505 | +25 | **+45** |
| 8 | 510 | +30 | **+75** |
| 9 | 508 | +28 | **+103** |
| 10 | 512 | +32 | **+135** |
| 11 | 515 | +35 | **+170** |
| 12 | 520 | +40 | **+210** |

**Step 2:** Apply the tabular CUSUM formula (one-sided upper).

$$
C_t^{+} = \max[0, \; X_t - (\mu_0 + K) + C_{t-1}^{+}]
$$

With $K = 5$ and $\mu_0 = 480$, the target is $\mu_0 + K = 485$.

| Day $t$ | $X_t$ | $X_t - 485$ | $C_t^{+}$ |
|---------|-------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | 480 | −5 | 0 |
| 2 | 475 | −10 | 0 |
| 3 | 490 | +5 | 5 |
| 4 | 482 | −3 | 5 |
| 5 | 488 | +3 | 8 |
| 6 | 485 | 0 | 8 |
| 7 | 505 | +20 | **28** |
| 8 | 510 | +25 | **53** |
| 9 | 508 | +23 | **76** |
| 10 | 512 | +27 | **103** |
| 11 | 515 | +30 | **133** |
| 12 | 520 | +35 | **168** |

**Step 3:** Compare against the decision interval $H = 20$. The first day where $C_t^{+} > 20$ is **Day 7** ($C_7^{+} = 28$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** A statistically significant *upward shift* in chiller energy consumption was detected on **Day 7**. Maintenance must immediately investigate for causes: fouled condenser, refrigerant undercharge, thermostat drift, or set-point creep.

---

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Baseline Determination Methods

| Method | Best Used For | Data Requirement | Accuracy | Limitation |
|--------|---------------|------------------|----------|------------|
| **Simple Historical Average** | Stable, unchanging processes | 12–24 months | Low | Ignores weather & production |
| **Production-Normalized** | Output-driven industry (cement, steel) | Production + Energy data | Medium | Linear assumption fails at extremes |
| **Weather-Normalized (HDD/CDD)** | HVAC-dominated buildings | Daily T° + Energy | Medium-High | Fails for non-climate loads |
| **Multi-Variable Regression (MVR)** | Complex, multi-driver systems (ISO 50002) | 24+ months of multi-variable data | High | Needs statistical expertise |
| **CUSUM** | Real-time shift detection | Continuous stream | High for shifts | Needs stable baseline period first |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The M&T Closed-Loop Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MeteringLayer] -->|Raw kWh, m3, kg| B[DataAcquisition]
    B -->|Timestamped data| C[EnergyInfoSystem]
    C -->|Historical records| D[NormalizationEngine]
    D -->|Weather correction| D
    D -->|Production correction| D
    D -->|Adjusted energy| E[BaselineEnB]
    E -->|Comparison| F[VarianceDetector]
    F -->|Within threshold| G[NoAction]
    F -->|Exceeds threshold| H[AlarmCUSUM]
    H --> I[RootCauseAnalysis]
    I --> J[CorrectiveAction]
    J --> K[ISO50001Review]
    K -->|Feedback to targets| E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *Baseline (EnB)* block is the *heart* of the loop. Every cycle recalibrates it. The loop never stops – this is the essence of *continuous improvement* in ISO 50001.

### 4.2 CUSUM Decision Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([New Data Point Xt]) --> calc[Xt - mu0 - K]
    calc --> neg{Result negative?}
    neg -->|Yes| reset[Reset CUSUM to 0]
    neg -->|No| add[Add to CUSUM]
    add --> compare{CUSUM greater than H?}
    compare -->|No| wait[Wait for next point]
    compare -->|Yes| alarm[OUT OF CONTROL SIGNAL]
    alarm --> flag[Trigger investigation]
    wait --> start
    reset --> start
    flag --> start
```

### 4.3 M&T System Hierarchy (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[Level1_MainIncomer]
        M1[MainHTMeter]
    end
    subgraph L2[Level2_SubStations]
        M2[CompressorSubMeter]
        M3[HVACSubMeter]
        M4[BoilerSubMeter]
        M5[LightingSubMeter]
    end
    subgraph L3[Level3_EndUse]
        E1[Compressor1]
        E2[Compressor2]
        E3[AHU1]
        E4[Chiller1]
        E5[CirculatingPump1]
    end
    M1 --> M2
    M1 --> M3
    M1 --> M4
    M1 --> M5
    M2 --> E1
    M2 --> E2
    M3 --> E3
    M4 --> E4
    M4 --> E5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trick Question (asked in Dec 2022):** *"Why is sub-metering essential for an M&T system?"*
> **Model Answer:** Without sub-metering, you only know the *total* energy bill. You cannot tell whether a saving came from the boiler, the compressor, or the lighting. Sub-metering converts *invisible* energy into *visible, attributable* energy – which is the prerequisite for accountability and target-setting at the equipment/department level.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define Monitoring and Targeting (M&T) in the context of energy management. List any four essential elements of an effective M&T system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Monitoring and Targeting (M&T) is a structured management technique that involves the continuous measurement, recording, analysis, and reporting of energy consumption data to establish an Energy Baseline (EnB), set performance targets, detect variances, and trigger corrective actions to ensure continuous improvement of energy performance, in alignment with ISO 50001.

**Four Essential Elements (1.5 marks – any four):**
1. **Accurate and calibrated metering infrastructure** (main + sub-meters).
2. **Reliable data acquisition and storage system** (Energy Information System).
3. **A statistically valid Energy Baseline (EnB)** with normalization for weather and production.
4. **Quantified targets and performance indicators (EnPIs)**.
5. **Variance detection and reporting mechanism** (e.g., CUSUM, control charts).
6. **A feedback loop for corrective action and management review.**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Sessional Style]**
**Differentiate between an Energy Baseline (EnB) and an Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI). Give one example of each from an industrial context.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Energy Baseline (EnB) | Energy Performance Indicator (EnPI) |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Purpose** | A *quantitative reference* point representing energy use under a defined set of conditions. | A *metric value* or *ratio* that quantifies energy performance. |
| **Nature** | Static (or slowly evolving) benchmark. | Dynamic; can change with technology. |
| **Example (Cement Industry)** | 820 kWh/tonne of clinker (the plant's 2022 normalized baseline). | kWh/tonne of cement produced (current month SEC). |
| **Example (HVAC Building)** | 1,50,000 kWh/year at 1500 CDD (baseline year). | kWh/m²/month of conditioned floor area. |

*(Award 1.5 marks for clear distinction; 1.5 marks for examples.)*
