| Definition | Raw, unprocessed facts and figures | Processed, contextualized data with meaning |
| Nature | Quantitative/qualitative measurements | Derived, interpreted, decision-oriented |
| Example | "Boiler consumed 8,400 kWh" | "Boiler SEC = 0.78 kWh/kg steam, 6% above industry benchmark" |
| Decision Use | Cannot directly support decisions | Supports benchmarking, target-setting, ECM evaluation |
| ISO 50001 Reference | Clause 9.1 input | Clause 9.1 output |

Data is the *raw input* collected from meters and logbooks; information is the *output* generated after applying statistical, regression, or graphical tools, which directly aids in EnMS decision-making.

---

### Q2. What is a Sankey Diagram? State its relevance in Energy Information Analysis. (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Sankey diagram** is a directional flow diagram where the width of arrows is proportional to the quantity of energy (or mass) flowing from one process to the next. It is widely used in energy audits and pinch analysis.

**Relevance in Energy Information Analysis:**
- Provides a **visual, intuitive** representation of energy distribution from source to end-use.
- Highlights the **magnitude of energy losses** at each stage (e.g., stack losses, leakage, radiation).
- Enables **quick identification** of the largest waste streams → guides ECM prioritization.
- Serves as a **communication tool** for management and ISO 50001 documentation.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A.** A manufacturing unit consumed the following monthly energy and produced the corresponding output:

| Month | Energy (kWh) | Production (tonnes) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5,200 | 100 |
| 2 | 5,800 | 120 |
| 3 | 6,400 | 140 |
| 4 | 6,900 | 160 |
| 5 | 7,500 | 180 |
| 6 | 8,000 | 200 |

**(a) (7 Marks)** Develop a linear regression model $E = aP + b$ and determine the variable and fixed energy components.

**(b) (7 Marks)** If the plant operates at 220 tonnes/month in the next month, predict the energy consumption. Also compute the correlation coefficient $r$ and comment on the model fit. If a 5% reduction in baseload is achieved through leak survey, what is the new predicted consumption?

#### Model Solution (A-a):

**Step 1 — Means.**

$$
\bar{P} = \frac{100+120+140+160+180+200}{6} = 150 \text{ tonnes}
$$

$$
\bar{E} = \frac{5200+5800+6400+6900+7500+8000}{6} = 6{,}633.33 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Build deviation table.**

| $P_i$ | $E_i$ | $x_i = P_i - \bar{P}$ | $y_i = E_i - \bar{E}$ | $x_i \cdot y_i$ | $x_i^{2}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 100 | 5,200 | $-50$ | $-1{,}433.33$ | 71,666.7 | 2,500 |
| 120 | 5,800 | $-30$ | $-833.33$ | 25,000.0 | 900 |
| 140 | 6,400 | $-10$ | $-233.33$ | 2,333.3 | 100 |
| 160 | 6,900 | 10 | 266.67 | 2,666.7 | 100 |
| 180 | 7,500 | 30 | 866.67 | 26,000.0 | 900 |
| 200 | 8,000 | 50 | 1,366.67 | 68,333.3 | 2,500 |
| **Σ** | | | | **196,000** | **7,000** |

**Step 3 — Slope $a$.**

$$
a = \frac{196{,}000}{7{,}000} = 28.0 \text{ kWh/tonne}
$$

**Step 4 — Intercept $b$.**

$$
b = 6{,}633.33 - (28.0)(150) = 6{,}633.33 - 4{,}200 = 2{,}433.33 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 5 — Regression equation.**

$$
\boxed{E = 28.0 P + 2{,}433.33 \text{ kWh}}
$$

> **Variable energy = 28.0 kWh/tonne; Fixed (baseload) = 2,433.33 kWh/month (~37% of average consumption).**

#### Model Solution (A-b):

**Step 1 — Predict for $P = 220$ tonnes.**

$$
E_{220} = 28.0 \times 220 + 2{,}433.33 = 6{,}160 + 2{,}433.33 = 8{,}593.33 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\sum y_i^{2}$.**

$\sum y_i^{2} = 2{,}054{,}444 + 694{,}444 + 54{,}444 + 71{,}111 + 751{,}111 + 1{,}867{,}778 = 5{,}493{,}333$

**Step 3 — Correlation coefficient $r$.**

$$
r = \frac{196{,}000}{\sqrt{7{,}000 \times 5{,}493{,}333}} = \frac{196{,}000}{\sqrt{3.8453 \times 10^{10}}} = \frac{196{,}000}{196{,}105.7} \approx 0.999
$$

> **Comment:** $r \approx 0.999$ indicates an *almost perfect* linear relationship — the model is highly reliable for prediction.

**Step 4 — 5% baseload reduction.**

New baseload $b' = 2{,}433.33 \times 0.95 = 2{,}311.67 \text{ kWh}$.

$$
E'_{220} = 28.0 \times 220 + 2{,}311.67 = 6{,}160 + 2{,}311.67 = 8{,}471.67 \text{ kWh}
$$

> **Savings = 8,593.33 − 8,471.67 = 121.66 kWh/month (~1.4% reduction).**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**B.** A textile mill's boiler has the following SEC data (kg of steam per kg of fabric processed). The baseline is the historical average.

| Month | SEC (kg steam/kg fabric) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 2.10 |
| 2 | 2.12 |
| 3 | 2.15 |
| 4 | 2.30 |
| 5 | 2.45 |
| 6 | 2.60 |

**(a) (7 Marks)** Calculate the baseline and apply the **CUSUM technique** to detect the month of performance deterioration. Plot the cumulative deviation values.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Identify the **likely causes** of the drift and suggest **two corrective actions** that the energy manager should implement. Also, compute the **% increase in SEC** from baseline to the worst-performing month.

#### Model Solution (B-a):

**Step 1 — Baseline (mean of first 3 stable months).**

$$
\bar{x}_{ref} = \frac{2.10 + 2.12 + 2.15}{3} = 2.123 \text{ kg steam/kg fabric}
$$

(Alternatively, take the full 6-month mean as baseline — KTU accepts either, but explicitly state assumption.)

**Step 2 — Deviations and CUSUM.**

| Month | $x_i$ | $d_i = x_i - 2.123$ | $S_m = \sum d_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2.10 | $-0.023$ | $-0.023$ |
| 2 | 2.12 | $-0.003$ | $-0.026$ |
| 3 | 2.15 | 0.027 | 0.001 |
| 4 | 2.30 | 0.177 | 0.178 |
| 5 | 2.45 | 0.327 | 0.505 |
| 6 | 2.60 | 0.477 | 0.982 |

**Step 3 — Interpretation.**

CUSUM is essentially **flat** during Months 1–3, then begins a **steep upward climb** from **Month 4**. This indicates a **structural shift** in boiler performance beginning in Month 4. A flat-then-steep pattern is the classic fingerprint of equipment degradation, not random variation.

#### Model Solution (B-b):

**Step 1 — Likely causes** (state minimum two):
- **Scale formation** on the boiler heat-transfer surfaces (reduces heat transfer).
- **Excess combustion air** due to faulty damper / air-fuel ratio controller.
- **Feedwater temperature drop** or reduced preheat effectiveness.
- **Steam leakage** from joints, traps, and distribution mains.
- **Burner nozzle wear** causing inefficient atomization.

**Step 2 — Corrective actions** (state two):
- **Online soot blowing** and chemical descaling of heat-transfer surfaces.
- **Tune the air-fuel ratio** by stack gas O₂ and CO trim analysis (target 3–5% excess O₂).
- **Steam trap survey** to identify and replace failed traps.
- **Combustion analysis** and burner maintenance/replacement.

**Step 3 — % increase in SEC from baseline to worst month (Month 6).**

$$
\% \text{ increase} = \frac{2.60 - 2.123}{2.123} \times 100\% = \frac{0.477}{2.123} \times 100\% \approx 22.47\%
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 22.47% increase in specific steam consumption represents a serious energy and cost loss. The CUSUM chart unambiguously pinpoints **Month 4 as the onset of degradation**, and corrective maintenance is urgently required.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in Data & Information Analysis questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** — Forgetting to convert kWh to kcal before dividing by tonnes. *Always show the conversion step explicitly.*
> 2. **Misidentifying baseline** in CUSUM — Baseline is the *target/historical* value, not the mean of the drifted data. *Wrong baseline → wrong inflection point → wrong conclusion.*
> 3. **Forgetting the regression intercept (b)** — SEC alone cannot give $b$. *If the problem gives paired $E$–$P$ data, KTU expects regression, not simple division.*
> 4. **No interpretation of $r$** — Computing $r$ without commenting on its value (e.g., "r = 0.998 indicates an excellent linear fit") loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Forgetting to suggest corrective actions** in CUSUM/drift problems — KTU rewards *managerial recommendations*, not just computation.
> 6. **Mixing energy units** — Mixing toe and kWh in the same answer without conversion marks you down.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Data ≠ Information** — Data is raw; information is data + context + analysis.
- ✅ **DIKW Hierarchy** — Data → Information → Knowledge → Wisdom.
- ✅ **SEC = Energy / Output** — Always in *standardized units* (toe/tonne, MJ/tonne, kWh/tonne).
- ✅ **1 toe = 10⁷ kcal = 41.868 GJ = 11,630 kWh**.
- ✅ **1 kWh = 860 kcal = 3.6 MJ** (use 860 for thermochemical, 3.6 MJ exactly).
- ✅ **GCV of FO ≈ 10,200 kcal/kg; Natural gas ≈ 8,500 kcal/SCM; Coal = 5,000–6,500 kcal/kg**.
- ✅ **EnPI Ratio Model** → simple SEC.
- ✅ **EnPI Regression Model** → $E = aP + b$ with $a$ = variable energy, $b$ = baseload.
- ✅ **CUSUM** = cumulative sum of $(x_i - \bar{x}_{ref})$ — detects drifts in EnPIs.
- ✅ **Correlation coefficient $|r| \to 1$** → strong linear relationship; $r \to 0$ → no linear relationship.
- ✅ **Sankey diagrams** trace energy flows and identify the largest loss streams.
- ✅ **Pareto chart (80/20)** prioritizes ECMs by impact.
- ✅ **ISO 50001 Clause 9.1** mandates monitoring, measurement, and *analysis* of EnPIs.
- ✅ **Data sources** = utility bills, sub-meters, SCADA, logbooks, ERP, walk-through audits.
- ✅ **Instrument class ≥ 0.5** for sub-metering accuracy.
- ✅ **M&V protocol (IPMVP)** uses regression baselines + CUSUM for post-retrofit verification.
- ✅ **Output of regression is the EnPI model**; **output of CUSUM is the shift detection month**.
- ✅ Always **conclude** with engineering recommendations (e.g., "insulate feedwater lines," "service burner," "conduct steam trap survey").

> **End of Notes — Data and Information Analysis, Module 3, PESFT414.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Techniques - Energy Consumption
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Techniques - Energy Consumption

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Consumption Techniques** refer to the systematic analytical methods employed to **quantify, monitor, evaluate, and optimize** the amount of energy utilized by a system, process, equipment, or facility over a defined period. These techniques transform raw energy meter readings into actionable engineering intelligence through statistical, graphical, and mathematical tools.

Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, this topic sits at the intersection of **energy accounting** and **performance benchmarking**, forming the analytical backbone of any Energy Management System (EnMS) as outlined in **ISO 50001**.

### 1.2 Key Sub-Techniques Covered

| # | Technique | Primary Purpose |
|---|-----------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** | Normalize energy use per unit of output |
| 2 | **Energy Intensity (EI)** | Compare cross-facility performance |
| 3 | **Cumulative Sum (CUSUM)** | Detect drift/trends in consumption |
| 4 | **Regression Analysis** | Establish baseline energy models |
| 5 | **Sankey Diagram** | Visualize energy flow distribution |
| 6 | **Energy Auditing** | Identify wastage hotspots |
| 7 | **Load Profiling** | Study time-based consumption patterns |
| 8 | **Benchmarking** | Compare with industry standards |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel Gauge" Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you drive a car from Kochi to Trivandrum (≈ 220 km). The fuel gauge shows the tank went from full to half. Now, the *raw data* says you used 20 litres. But the *technique* asks the smarter question: **"How many km did you get per litre?"** That's 11 km/L — your **Specific Fuel Consumption**. The odometer distance is your *production output*, the fuel used is your *energy input*. In the same way, factories don't just track "kWh used"; they track **kWh per tonne of product** or **kWh per degree of cooling**. These techniques turn numbers into decisions.

### 1.4 Why This Topic is a KTU High-Weight Area

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Almost every KTU question paper from the **2022 Scheme (transitional)** and now the **2024 Scheme** for Energy Management features a direct 7- or 14-mark problem on SEC, CUSUM, or Regression. Mastering this topic guarantees scoring potential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

**Definition:** The amount of energy consumed per unit of product, service, or output produced.

$$SEC = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumed (E)}}{\text{Quantity of Output (Q)}}$$

**Units:** typically $\text{kWh/unit}$, $\text{MJ/kg}$, $\text{GJ/tonne}$, $\text{kWh/°C cooling}$.

**Physical Logic:**
- Step 1: **Measure** total energy input from utility bills (electricity kWh + thermal MJ).
- Step 2: **Quantify** production output (tonnes, pieces, kWh-equivalent service).
- Step 3: **Normalize** by dividing energy by output.
- Step 4: **Compare** across time periods or against baseline.

**Why It Matters:** A factory using **1,00,000 kWh/month** is meaningless without knowing it produced 10,000 units (SEC = 10) vs. another month producing 50,000 units (SEC = 2). Lower SEC = better efficiency.

### 2.2 Energy Intensity (EI)

Energy Intensity is the SEC at a **macroeconomic** scale — used at national/international level.

$$EI = \frac{\text{Total Primary Energy Supply (TPES)}}{\text{Gross Domestic Product (GDP)}}$$

**Units:** $\text{MJ per INR of GDP}$ or $\text{kgoe per USD}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** SEC is **micro-level** (plant/machine); EI is **macro-level** (country/region). Don't confuse the two in answers.

### 2.3 CUSUM Technique (Cumulative Sum Analysis)

A **statistical process control** tool used to detect small but persistent shifts in mean energy consumption.

$$CUSUM_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} (X_i - \mu_0)$$

Where:
- $X_i$ = energy consumption at time $i$
- $\mu_0$ = target/baseline mean
- $CUSUM_t$ = cumulative sum up to time $t$

**Interpretation Logic:**
- If $CUSUM$ remains **flat** (slope ≈ 0) → process is in control.
- If $CUSUM$ **rises** linearly → mean has shifted upward (deterioration).
- If $CUSUM$ **falls** linearly → mean has shifted downward (improvement).
- The **slope** of the CUSUM line indicates the **magnitude of deviation** per unit time.

**Real-World Utility:** Detects a 2% efficiency loss that monthly averages would hide.

### 2.4 Regression Analysis for Baseline Modelling

Establishes a mathematical relationship between energy consumption (dependent variable) and operating parameters (independent variables like production volume, ambient temperature, hours of operation).

$$E = a + b \cdot X_1 + c \cdot X_2 + \cdots + \epsilon$$

Where:
- $E$ = energy consumption
- $X_1, X_2, \ldots$ = production drivers
- $a$ = fixed/baseline energy (no production)
- $b, c$ = variable energy coefficients (energy per unit of driver)
- $\epsilon$ = error term

**Significance:** The coefficient $b$ represents the **variable SEC**; $a$ represents the **fixed energy** (lighting, HVAC base load).

### 2.5 Sankey Diagram

A **flow diagram** where the width of arrows is proportional to the quantity of energy flow. Used to identify the **biggest energy loss paths**.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table summarizes every formula you must memorize for the KTU exam on this topic.**

| Technique | Core Formula | Typical Units | Boundary Condition |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|---------------------|
| Specific Energy Consumption | $SEC = E / Q$ | $\text{kWh/unit}$ | $Q > 0$ |
| Energy Intensity | $EI = TPES / GDP$ | $\text{MJ/USD}$ | Macroeconomic |
| CUSUM | $CUSUM_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} (X_i - \mu_0)$ | $\text{kWh or kg oil eq.}$ | Requires baseline $\mu_0$ |
| Baseline Regression | $E = a + b X$ | $\text{kWh, X in units}$ | Min 30 data points |
| Correlation Coefficient | $r = \dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sqrt{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2 \cdot \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2}}$ | Dimensionless | $-1 \le r \le 1$ |
| Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}$ | Dimensionless | $0 \le R^2 \le 1$ |
| Energy Cost | $C = E \times \text{Tariff}$ | $\text{INR or USD}$ | Use peak/off-peak rates |
| Per Cent Savings | $\%S = \dfrac{SEC_{old} - SEC_{new}}{SEC_{old}} \times 100$ | $\text{\%}$ | $0 \le S \le 100$ |
| CUSUM Slope (drift rate) | $\text{Slope} = \bar{X} - \mu_0$ | $\text{kWh/period}$ | Continuous |
| Normalized SEC | $nSEC = \dfrac{\text{Actual SEC}}{\text{Reference SEC}}$ | Dimensionless | Compare with peers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Field Application:** These techniques are used in **BEE Star Rating** (India), **ISO 50001 EnPI**, **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme** for Designated Consumers, and in CDM (Clean Development Mechanism) projects for baseline determination.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A cement plant consumed **8,50,000 kWh** of electrical energy and **2,500 tonnes of coal** (GCV = 6,200 kcal/kg) in a month. Production during the month was **12,000 tonnes** of clinker. Calculate:
1. Total energy consumption in **GJ**.
2. Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in **kWh/tonne** and **MJ/tonne**.

**Step 1: Convert electrical energy to common unit (MJ)**

$$E_{elec} = 8{,}50{,}000 \text{ kWh} \times 3.6 \text{ MJ/kWh}$$

$$E_{elec} = 30{,}60{,}000 \text{ MJ} = 3{,}060 \text{ GJ}$$

> **Logic:** The conversion factor **3.6 MJ/kWh** comes from $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$. This is a **mandatory conversion** in KTU answers.

**Step 2: Convert thermal energy from coal to MJ**

$$E_{coal} = m \times GCV$$

$$E_{coal} = 2{,}500 \times 1000 \text{ kg} \times 6{,}200 \text{ kcal/kg}$$

$$E_{coal} = 1.55 \times 10^{10} \text{ kcal}$$

Converting kcal to MJ ($1 \text{ kcal} = 4.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MJ}$):

$$E_{coal} = 1.55 \times 10^{10} \times 4.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MJ}$$

$$E_{coal} = 6.4852 \times 10^{7} \text{ MJ} = 64{,}852 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 3: Total energy consumption**

$$E_{total} = E_{elec} + E_{coal}$$

$$E_{total} = 3{,}060 + 64{,}852 = 67{,}912 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 4: Calculate SEC in kWh/tonne (electrical only)**

$$SEC_{elec} = \frac{8{,}50{,}000 \text{ kWh}}{12{,}000 \text{ tonnes}}$$

$$SEC_{elec} = 70.83 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 5: Calculate SEC in MJ/tonne (total energy basis)**

$$SEC_{total} = \frac{67{,}912 \times 10^3 \text{ MJ}}{12{,}000 \text{ tonnes}}$$

$$SEC_{total} = 5{,}659.3 \text{ MJ/tonne clinker}$$

> **Final Answer:** SEC = **70.83 kWh/tonne** (electrical) or **5,659 MJ/tonne** (total).

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: CUSUM Technique Application

**Problem Statement:** A boiler's baseline steam generation is $\mu_0 = 100$ tonnes/day. Actual readings for 10 days are: 102, 98, 105, 108, 110, 112, 115, 118, 120, 122 tonnes/day. Use the CUSUM technique to determine when deterioration began.

**Step 1: Set up deviations $(X_i - \mu_0)$**

| Day $i$ | $X_i$ (t/day) | $X_i - \mu_0$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 102 | $+2$ |
| 2 | 98 | $-2$ |
| 3 | 105 | $+5$ |
| 4 | 108 | $+8$ |
| 5 | 110 | $+10$ |
| 6 | 112 | $+12$ |
| 7 | 115 | $+15$ |
| 8 | 118 | $+18$ |
| 9 | 120 | $+20$ |
| 10 | 122 | $+22$ |

**Step 2: Compute CUSUM cumulatively**

$$CUSUM_1 = +2$$
$$CUSUM_2 = 2 + (-2) = 0$$
$$CUSUM_3 = 0 + 5 = +5$$
$$CUSUM_4 = 5 + 8 = +13$$
$$CUSUM_5 = 13 + 10 = +23$$
$$CUSUM_6 = 23 + 12 = +35$$
$$CUSUM_7 = 35 + 15 = +50$$
$$CUSUM_8 = 50 + 18 = +68$$
$$CUSUM_9 = 68 + 20 = +88$$
$$CUSUM_{10} = 88 + 22 = +110$$

**Step 3: Interpret the trend**

> **Observation:** From Day 3 onwards, the CUSUM rises **monotonically** with an almost constant slope of ≈ +12 t/day. This indicates a **persistent upward shift** in steam generation (the boiler is now over-firing, indicating either increased demand, scale formation, or combustion inefficiency).

**Step 4: Drift Rate Calculation**

$$\text{Drift Slope} = \bar{X}_{10} - \mu_0 = \frac{1110}{10} - 100 = 111 - 100 = +11 \text{ t/day}$$

> **Engineering Conclusion:** The boiler has shifted by **+11 t/day** above baseline. Root cause investigation (likely water-side scaling, soot accumulation, or excess air) must begin at **Day 3**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Regression-Based Baseline Energy Model

**Problem Statement:** Five months of data for a process plant are given. Establish a linear regression model $E = a + bX$ where $E$ = monthly energy (kWh) and $X$ = monthly production (tonnes).

| Month | $X$ (tonnes) | $E$ (kWh) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 100 | 5,000 |
| 2 | 150 | 6,500 |
| 3 | 200 | 8,000 |
| 4 | 250 | 9,500 |
| 5 | 300 | 11,000 |

**Step 1: Compute means**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{100+150+200+250+300}{5} = 200 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$\bar{E} = \frac{5000+6500+8000+9500+11000}{5} = 8{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2: Compute slope $b$**

$$b = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(E_i - \bar{E})}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}$$

Computing term by term:

| $X_i - \bar{X}$ | $E_i - \bar{E}$ | $(X_i - \bar{X})(E_i - \bar{E})$ | $(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $-100$ | $-3000$ | $3{,}00{,}000$ | $10{,}000$ |
| $-50$ | $-1500$ | $75{,}000$ | $2{,}500$ |
| $0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $+50$ | $+1500$ | $75{,}000$ | $2{,}500$ |
| $+100$ | $+3000$ | $3{,}00{,}000$ | $10{,}000$ |
| **Sum** | — | **$7{,}50{,}000$** | **$25{,}000$** |

$$b = \frac{7{,}50{,}000}{25{,}000} = 30 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 3: Compute intercept $a$**

$$a = \bar{E} - b\bar{X} = 8{,}000 - 30 \times 200 = 8{,}000 - 6{,}000 = 2{,}000 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4: Final Regression Model**

$$E = 2{,}000 + 30 \cdot X \text{ kWh}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** 
> - $a = 2{,}000$ kWh = **fixed energy** (idle loads: lighting, HVAC, control systems) — saved even if production = 0. 
> - $b = 30$ kWh/tonne = **variable energy** (consumed per unit produced). 
> - If production doubles from 200 to 400 tonnes, expected energy = $2000 + 30(400) = 14{,}000$ kWh.

**Step 5: Verify with a coefficient of determination $R^2$ calculation** (for full credit in KTU answer).

$$SS_{tot} = \sum (E_i - \bar{E})^2 = 9{,}000{,}000 + 2{,}250{,}000 + 0 + 2{,}250{,}000 + 9{,}000{,}000 = 2.25 \times 10^7$$

$$SS_{res} = \sum (E_i - \hat{E}_i)^2 = 0 \text{ (perfect linear fit)}$$

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}} = 1 - 0 = 1.0$$

> **Conclusion:** $R^2 = 1.0$ indicates a **perfect linear relationship** — this baseline is statistically very strong.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Technique | Best Used For | Data Required | Limitation |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|------------|
| **SEC** | Single product/process monitoring | Energy + production data | Doesn't account for weather |
| **Energy Intensity** | Country/region comparison | GDP + TPES | Hides sector-level data |
| **CUSUM** | Drift detection | Time-series consumption | Needs $\mu_0$ baseline |
| **Regression** | Multi-variable baselining | Energy + drivers | Min 30 data points |
| **Sankey Diagram** | Visual loss identification | End-use breakdown | Not analytical in itself |
| **Energy Audit** | One-time deep analysis | Measurements + inspection | Time-consuming |
| **Load Profiling** | Tariff optimization | Hourly/daily data | Seasonal variability |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy Consumption Analysis Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Energy Data Collection] --> B[Data Validation and Cleansing]
    B --> C{Analysis Type Selection}
    C --> D[SEC Calculation]
    C --> E[CUSUM Trend Analysis]
    C --> F[Regression Baseline Modelling]
    C --> G[Sankey Diagram Energy Mapping]
    D --> H[Performance Benchmarking]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Variance and Gap Analysis]
    I --> J[Energy Saving Opportunities ESO]
    J --> K[Implementation and Monitoring Loop]
    K --> A
```

### 4.2 CUSUM Interpretation Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Compute CUSUM Series] --> B{CUSUM Slope Pattern}
    B --> C[Slope Approximately Zero]
    B --> D[Slope Steadily Rising]
    B --> E[Slope Steadily Falling]
    C --> F[Process In Control - Continue Monitoring]
    D --> G[Mean Shifted Upward - Investigate Degradation Causes]
    E --> H[Mean Shifted Downward - Document Improvements]
    G --> I[Check Equipment Scaling, Leakages, Set Points]
    H --> J[Validate Savings and Update Baseline]
```

### 4.3 Regression-Based Baseline Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Production Volume X1] --> D[Multivariate Regression Engine]
    B[Ambient Temperature X2] --> D
    C[Operating Hours X3] --> D
    D --> E[Predicted Baseline Energy E baseline]
    D --> F[Variable Energy Coefficient b1 b2 b3]
    D --> G[Fixed Energy Intercept a]
    E --> H[Actual Energy E actual]
    H --> I[Variance equals E actual minus E baseline]
    I --> J{Exceeds Threshold}
    J --> K[Yes - Trigger Energy Audit]
    J --> L[No - Continue Operation]
```

### 4.4 Sankey Diagram Block Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Energy Input 100 percent] --> B[Electricity 40 percent]
    A --> C[Thermal Fuel 50 percent]
    A --> D[Renewables 10 percent]
    B --> E[Motors and Drives 25 percent]
    B --> F[Lighting 8 percent]
    B --> G[HVAC 5 percent]
    B --> H[Losses 2 percent]
    C --> I[Boiler Process 35 percent]
    C --> J[Stack Losses 12 percent]
    C --> K[Radiation Losses 3 percent]
    D --> L[Solar PV 7 percent]
    D --> M[Wind 3 percent]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading:** The Sankey diagram immediately highlights **Stack Losses (12%)** and **Motor Load (25%)** as the largest energy streams. Targeting these two provides the highest ROI for energy conservation measures (ECMs).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> *Define Specific Energy Consumption (SEC). What are its units, and why is it preferred over absolute energy consumption for performance comparison?*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Specific Energy Consumption is the amount of energy consumed per unit of product, service, or output.

**Formula:** $SEC = E / Q$ where $E$ is total energy consumed and $Q$ is quantity of output.

**Typical Units:** $\text{kWh/tonne}$, $\text{MJ/kg}$, $\text{kWh/°C}$, $\text{MJ/m}^3$.

**Why Preferred:**
1. **Normalization** — Removes the effect of varying production volumes.
2. **Cross-facility comparison** — Allows comparison of two plants with different capacities.
3. **Time-series tracking** — Reveals efficiency trends independent of business growth.
4. **Benchmarking** — Aligns with international standards (BEE, ISO 50001).

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> *Explain the CUSUM technique in energy management. How does the slope of the CUSUM plot aid in performance diagnosis?*

**Model Answer:**

**CUSUM (Cumulative Sum)** is a statistical technique that accumulates deviations of measured values from a target baseline value over time.

**Formula:** $CUSUM_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} (X_i - \mu_0)$

**Slope Interpretation:**
- **Horizontal/flat slope** → Process is in statistical control.
- **Steep positive slope** → Mean has shifted upward (energy waste / degradation).
- **Steep negative slope** → Mean has shifted downward (improvement achieved).
- **Magnitude of slope** = Rate of deviation in original units per period.

**Diagnostic Utility:** Detects **small but consistent** drifts (2–5%) that are invisible in monthly averages — making it a powerful tool for **early fault detection** in boilers, compressors, and HVAC systems.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B**
> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** A manufacturing unit consumed the following in a quarter:
> - Electricity: **3,00,000 kWh**
> - Furnace Oil: **50,000 litres** (density = 0.96 kg/L, GCV = 10,200 kcal/kg)
> - LPG: **10,000 kg** (GCV = 11,000 kcal/kg)
>
> Production during the quarter = **4,000 tonnes** of finished product.
>
> Calculate the **total energy consumption in GJ** and the **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in MJ/tonne**. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the **CUSUM technique** for energy consumption monitoring. How is the **slope of the CUSUM chart** interpreted to detect equipment deterioration? **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1: Electrical energy in MJ**

$$E_{elec} = 3{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh} \times 3.6 \text{ MJ/kWh} = 1{,}080{,}000 \text{ MJ} = 1{,}080 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 2: Furnace oil energy in MJ**

$$m_{FO} = 50{,}000 \text{ L} \times 0.96 \text{ kg/L} = 48{,}000 \text{ kg}$$

$$E_{FO} = 48{,}000 \times 10{,}200 \text{ kcal} = 4.896 \times 10^8 \text{ kcal}$$

Converting to MJ:

$$E_{FO} = 4.896 \times 10^8 \times 4.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MJ} = 2{,}048{,}486 \text{ MJ} \approx 2{,}048.5 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 3: LPG energy in MJ**

$$E_{LPG} = 10{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 11{,}000 \text{ kcal/kg} = 1.1 \times 10^8 \text{ kcal}$$

$$E_{LPG} = 1.1 \times 10^8 \times 4.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MJ} = 460{,}240 \text{ MJ} \approx 460.2 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 4: Total energy**

$$E_{total} = 1{,}080 + 2{,}048.5 + 460.2 = 3{,}588.7 \text{ GJ}$$

**Step 5: SEC**

$$SEC = \frac{3{,}588.7 \times 10^3 \text{ MJ}}{4{,}000 \text{ tonnes}} = 897.2 \text{ MJ/tonne}$$

> **Final Answer:** Total Energy = **3,588.7 GJ/quarter**; SEC = **897.2 MJ/tonne**.

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

The **CUSUM technique** plots the cumulative deviation of actual energy consumption from a pre-determined baseline $\mu_0$ over successive time intervals (days, weeks, or months).

**Mathematical Form:** $CUSUM_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} (X_i - \mu_0)$

**Sketch (CUSUM Chart):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time Axis t] --> B[CUSUM Axis vertical]
    B --> C[Slope Interpretation Zone]
    C --> D1[Flat Line Slope zero equals Stable Process]
    C --> D2[Rising Slope plus equals Mean Increased equals Deterioration]
    C --> D3[Falling Slope minus equals Mean Decreased equals Improvement]
```

> **Slope Interpretation Table:**

| CUSUM Slope Pattern | Diagnosis | Likely Causes |
|---------------------|-----------|---------------|
| ≈ 0 (flat) | Process stable | Normal operation |
| Positive (rising) | Consumption increased | Scale, fouling, leaks, set-point drift |
| Negative (falling) | Consumption decreased | Successful ECMs implemented |
| Sudden change in slope | Discrete event occurred | Equipment failure, weather shift |

**Engineering Significance:** CUSUM detects **persistent micro-drifts** (1–3% per period) that traditional monthly averages mask. The **magnitude of slope × number of periods** gives the total energy excess over baseline — directly translatable to **rupee loss** for management reporting.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
> **[KTU University Exam - Sample, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** The following data show the monthly electricity consumption ($Y$, in kWh) of a process plant versus production volume ($X$, in tonnes). Develop a **linear regression baseline model** of the form $Y = a + bX$. **[7 Marks]**

| Month | $X$ (tonnes) | $Y$ (kWh) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Jan | 80 | 4,200 |
| Feb | 90 | 4,600 |
| Mar | 100 | 5,000 |
| Apr | 110 | 5,500 |
| May | 120 | 5,900 |

>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of a **Sankey diagram** in energy analysis. With a suitable example, illustrate how it helps identify the **biggest energy loss streams** in a plant. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1: Compute means**

$$\bar{X} = \frac{80+90+100+110+120}{5} = 100 \text{ tonnes}$$

$$\bar{Y} = \frac{4200+4600+5000+5500+5900}{5} = 5{,}040 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2: Compute slope $b$**

| $X_i - \bar{X}$ | $Y_i - \bar{Y}$ | $(X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})$ | $(X_i - \bar{X})^2$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $-20$ | $-840$ | $16{,}800$ | $400$ |
| $-10$ | $-440$ | $4{,}400$ | $100$ |
| $0$ | $-40$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $+10$ | $+460$ | $4{,}600$ | $100$ |
| $+20$ | $+860$ | $17{,}200$ | $400$ |
| **Sum** | — | **$43{,}000$** | **$1{,}000$** |

$$b = \frac{43{,}000}{1{,}000} = 43 \text{ kWh/tonne}$$

**Step 3: Compute intercept $a$**

$$a = \bar{Y} - b\bar{X} = 5{,}040 - 43 \times 100 = 5{,}040 - 4{,}300 = 740 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 4: Final regression equation**

$$\boxed{Y = 740 + 43X \text{ kWh}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- $a = 740$ kWh → **fixed (idling) energy** (lighting, control panels, standby losses).
- $b = 43$ kWh/tonne → **variable energy per tonne** (process-specific).
- For $X = 130$ tonnes (forecasted), predicted energy = $740 + 43(130) = 6{,}330$ kWh.

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

A **Sankey diagram** is a **flow visualization** in which arrows connect energy sources to end-uses. The **width (or thickness)** of each arrow is **proportional to the quantity of energy** flowing through that path. It is named after Irish engineer **Captain Matthew Sankey** and was originally used to depict steam engine efficiency.

**Key Features:**
1. **Source-to-sink mapping** — Shows how input energy is distributed.
2. **Loss identification** — Thick "loss" branches immediately reveal wastage points.
3. **Quantitative clarity** — Numerical labels (in kWh, MJ, %) accompany flows.

**Illustrative Example — Thermal Power Plant (Block Representation):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Coal Input 1000 MJ] --> B[Boiler 850 MJ useful]
    A --> C[Stack Loss 100 MJ]
    A --> D[Radiation Loss 30 MJ]
    A --> E[Ash Loss 20 MJ]
    B --> F[Turbine 750 MJ]
    F --> G[Generator 740 MJ]
    G --> H[Output to Grid 720 MJ]
    F --> I[Condenser Loss 10 MJ]
```

**Sankey Insights:**
- The **biggest loss stream is the stack (10%)**.
- **Boiler-to-turbine loss is 100 MJ (≈12%)** due to incomplete combustion.
- The combined **condenser + transformer + auxiliary losses** (≈ 30 MJ) are smaller but cumulatively significant.

**Engineering Decision:** Allocating capital to **economizer installation** (recovers stack loss) yields the **highest payback**, as identified directly from the Sankey diagram.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors** — Failing to convert kWh to MJ (or vice versa) when multiple fuels are present. Always state: "Using $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$" explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing SEC with EI** — SEC is **plant/micro** level; EI is **country/macro** level. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for interchange.
> 3. **CUSUM Without Baseline** — You **must** state $\mu_0$ (baseline mean) clearly. Without it, CUSUM is undefined.
> 4. **Regression Missing Goodness-of-Fit** — Always report $R^2$ value to validate the model. A regression without $R^2$ is incomplete.
> 5. **Not Labelling Mermaid Diagrams** — KTU examiners expect axis labels and quantities on diagrams. Use flowchart annotations.
> 6. **Skipping Conversion to Common Energy Unit** — When mixing electricity and fuels, convert **everything to MJ or GJ** first.
> 7. **Forgetting the Slope Interpretation** — In CUSUM, "slope" is the answer to "what does it mean?" Always end with a slope-based diagnosis.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Techniques: Energy Consumption"**
>
> - [x] **SEC = E/Q** is the most fundamental metric — always normalize.
> - [x] **Conversion factor:** $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$; $1 \text{ kcal} = 4.184 \times 10^{-3} \text{ MJ}$.
> - [x] **CUSUM detects drift**; flat slope = stable, rising = worsening, falling = improving.
> - [x] **Regression equation** $E = a + bX$: $a$ is fixed energy, $b$ is variable SEC.
> - [x] **$R^2$ closer to 1** means a better fit; minimum acceptable $R^2 \geq 0.7$ for industrial baselines.
> - [x] **Sankey diagram** uses arrow thickness proportional to energy flow; ideal for locating biggest losses.
> - [x] **Energy Intensity (EI)** is macro-level (national); SEC is micro-level (plant).
> - [x] Always state units **in every step** of the numerical solution.
> - [x] **Energy audit** is the *parent technique*; SEC, CUSUM, regression are *tools within it*.
> - [x] **BEE / ISO 50001 / PAT Scheme** all use these techniques for EnPI (Energy Performance Indicators).
> - [x] **Per cent savings formula:** $\%S = (SEC_{old} - SEC_{new}) / SEC_{old} \times 100$.
> - [x] **CUSUM slope** = $\bar{X} - \mu_0$ — gives the average shift per period.
> - [x] For KTU, remember: **graphical interpretation (slope/trend) carries equal marks as the computation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Production

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Energy Management and Conservation (PESFT414)**, the term **"Production"** refers to the systematic transformation of raw materials and energy inputs into finished industrial goods, services, or useful work, viewed from the standpoint of **energy utilization, accounting, and efficiency**. It encompasses the entire chain of process operations, utilities, and support services that consume energy to deliver an economic output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Production* in energy management is the study of how energy is consumed across various industrial processes, the techniques to quantify this consumption, and the methodologies to optimize it without compromising product quality or throughput.

The energy involved in production is broadly classified into two categories:

| Energy Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Energy** | Energy extracted directly from nature | Coal, crude oil, natural gas, solar radiation, wind |
| **Secondary Energy** | Derived from primary energy sources | Electricity, steam, refined fuels, hot water |

### 1.2 Key Performance Metrics in Production Energy Management

> [!NOTE]
> The following metrics are **frequently tested** in KTU examinations and form the foundation of every energy audit report.

1. **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**
   - The amount of energy consumed per unit of production output.
   - Unit: **MJ/ton, kWh/kg, Gcal/tonne**, or **toe/tonne** (tonne of oil equivalent).

2. **Energy Intensity (EI)**
   - Energy consumed per unit of monetary value of output.
   - Unit: **MJ/₹, kWh/₹, or GJ/$**.

3. **Energy Productivity (EP)**
   - The reciprocal of Energy Intensity. Output produced per unit of energy.
   - Unit: **₹/kWh** or **tonne/GJ**.

4. **Plant Energy Performance (PEP)**
   - Overall ratio of useful energy output to total energy input, expressed as a percentage.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a factory as a household kitchen preparing a meal.**
> - **Inputs:** Raw materials (vegetables, spices) + Energy (gas, electricity).
> - **Process:** Cooking (chopping, boiling, frying).
> - **Output:** The finished meal (product).
> - **Wastage:** Spillage, overcooking, heat lost to the surroundings.
> 
> In the same way, a production facility takes in **raw materials + energy**, runs them through **processes**, and outputs a **product** with some **energy losses**. Energy management is simply the art of *cooking the same meal using less gas*, without making it taste worse.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> **Standard Energy Conversion Reference Values (use these in KTU numericals):**
> - **1 kWh** = **3.6 MJ** = **860.4 kcal**
> - **1 TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent)** = **41.868 GJ** = **11.63 MWh**
> - **1 Calorific Value of Coal (bituminous)** ≈ **25 MJ/kg**
> - **1 Calorific Value of Furnace Oil** ≈ **40 MJ/kg**
> - **1 Calorific Value of Natural Gas** ≈ **35 MJ/Nm³**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Steps of Energy Analysis in a Production System

A structured approach is followed during an energy audit of any production facility. The logical sequence is:

1. **Step 1 – Process Mapping:** Identify all process units, utilities, and support systems.
2. **Step 2 – Energy Input Quantification:** Measure fuel, electricity, and steam consumption.
3. **Step 3 – Energy Output Quantification:** Compute the useful energy delivered to the product.
4. **Step 4 – Loss Identification:** Identify stack losses, radiation losses, condensate losses, etc.
5. **Step 5 – SEC Calculation:** Determine Specific Energy Consumption for benchmarking.
6. **Step 6 – Recommendation & Monitoring:** Suggest energy-saving measures and track KPIs.

> **Why these steps?** The "Why" behind this sequence is to move from a *macroscopic view* (total energy bill) to a *microscopic view* (per-unit, per-process consumption) so that inefficiencies can be pinpointed and quantified before they are addressed.

### 2.2 Material and Energy Balance in Production

The **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a production system states:

$$\text{Energy Input} = \text{Energy Output} + \text{Energy Losses} + \text{Energy Stored}$$

For a **steady-state, continuous production process**, energy storage is zero, simplifying to:

$$\sum E_{\text{in}} = \sum E_{\text{out}} + \sum E_{\text{loss}}$$

The **material balance** equation:

$$M_{\text{in}} = M_{\text{out}} + M_{\text{waste}} + M_{\text{accumulated}}$$

For steady-state, the accumulated term is zero, giving:

$$M_{\text{in}} = M_{\text{out}} + M_{\text{waste}}$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{P}$ | MJ/kg or kWh/tonne | Benchmarking process efficiency |
| 2 | Energy Efficiency ($\eta$) | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{\text{useful}}}{E_{\text{input}}} \times 100$ | % | Boiler, furnace, motor efficiency |
| 3 | Heat Loss through Flue Gas | $Q_{\text{loss}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{air}})$ | kJ/h or kCal/h | Boiler audit |
| 4 | Excess Air (%) | $\%EA = \dfrac{O_2 - 0.264 \cdot CO_2}{0.264 \cdot CO_2} \times 100$ | % | Combustion efficiency |
| 5 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{comb}} = 100 - \dfrac{L_1 + L_2 + L_3}{GCV_{\text{fuel}}}$ | % | Furnace / boiler tuning |
| 6 | Energy Cost per Unit Product | $C_{\text{unit}} = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Cost}}{\text{Units Produced}}$ | ₹/tonne or ₹/piece | Cost analysis |
| 7 | Power Factor Correction | $Q_c = P(\tan\phi_1 - \tan\phi_2)$ | kVAr | Electrical energy savings |
| 8 | Energy Productivity | $EP = \dfrac{\text{Monetary Output}}{E_{\text{total}}}$ | ₹/kWh | Overall plant metric |
| 9 | Cogeneration Benefit | $\eta_{\text{co-gen}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{electrical}} + Q_{\text{thermal}}}{Q_{\text{fuel}}}$ | % | CHP plant efficiency |
| 10 | Heat Recovery (from flue gas) | $Q_{\text{rec}} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot \eta_{\text{rec}}$ | kJ/h | Waste heat recovery systems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for Table Use:** In the table above, $\vert$ (vertical bar) is **NOT** used; mathematical divisibility and absolute values are expressed using clear fractions and $\Delta$ notation to keep the markdown table parser safe.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

The above formulas and procedures are routinely used in:

- **Cement Industry:** Kiln efficiency, cooler heat loss, raw material SEC.
- **Steel Industry:** Blast furnace gas utilization, coke oven gas recovery.
- **Textile Industry:** Steam and dyeing process optimization.
- **Power Plants:** Heat rate, auxiliary power consumption.
- **Fertilizer Industry:** Reformer energy, ammonia synthesis loop efficiency.

> The application spans virtually every energy-intensive process plant in the manufacturing sector.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) from First Principles

The Specific Energy Consumption is a fundamental quantity used in nearly every energy audit. It is derived as follows:

**Given:**
- Total energy consumed by the plant per year, $E_{\text{total}}$ (in MJ).
- Total production output per year, $P$ (in tonnes).
- Operating hours, $t$ (in hours).

**Step 1 – Express total energy consumed in standard units.**

$$E_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i \cdot \text{GCV}_i) + E_{\text{electrical}}$$

where $m_i$ is the mass of the $i^{th}$ fuel and $\text{GCV}_i$ is its Gross Calorific Value.

**Step 2 – Convert all energy components to a common unit (MJ).**

$$E_{\text{total (MJ)}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i \cdot \text{GCV}_i) + (E_{\text{electrical}} \times 3.6)$$

**Step 3 – Compute production output in consistent units.**

$$P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{per hour}} \times t$$

**Step 4 – Derive SEC as the ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC &= \dfrac{E_{\text{total (MJ)}}}{P_{\text{total (tonnes)}}} \\[6pt]
SEC &= \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (m_i \cdot \text{GCV}_i) + 3.6 \cdot E_{\text{electrical}}}{P_{\text{per hour}} \times t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The numerator represents all energy entering the system boundary, while the denominator represents the useful economic output. The ratio normalizes energy use to a "per-unit product" basis, enabling fair comparison across plants of different sizes.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Efficiency of a Combustion System

For a furnace or boiler, the **combustion efficiency** is derived from a heat-loss approach.

**Step 1 – Identify the three primary heat losses.**
- $L_1$ = Dry flue gas loss (%).
- $L_2$ = Loss due to moisture from fuel combustion (%).
- $L_3$ = Surface / radiation loss (%).

**Step 2 – Express $L_1$ using the empirical Siegert formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= \dfrac{m_{\text{fg}} \cdot c_{p,\text{fg}} \cdot (T_{\text{fg}} - T_{\text{air}})}{GCV_{\text{fuel}}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.65 \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_{\text{fg}} - T_{\text{air}}}{\text{CO}_2\%}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Express $L_2$ for hydrogen-bearing fuels.**

$$L_2 = 9 \cdot H_2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{587 + T_{\text{air}}}{GCV_{\text{fuel}}}\right)$$

where $H_2$ is the mass fraction of hydrogen in the fuel.

**Step 4 – Combine losses to get the overall efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{comb}} &= 100 - (L_1 + L_2 + L_3) \\[4pt]
\eta_{\text{comb}} &= 100 - \left[ 0.65 \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_{\text{fg}} - T_{\text{air}}}{\text{CO}_2\%}\right) + 9H_2\left(\dfrac{587 + T_{\text{air}}}{GCV_{\text{fuel}}}\right) + L_3 \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Combustion efficiency is computed by *subtracting all losses from the input*, since by the First Law, the unaccounted energy must be useful (in the flue gas products or in the working medium). The $GCV_{\text{fuel}}$ normalizes the formula for different fuels.

### 3.3 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix)

> **Problem (KTU-style 14-mark type):** A cement plant consumes **1500 tonnes of coal per month** (GCV = **25 MJ/kg**), **2,00,000 kWh of electricity per month**, and produces **20,000 tonnes of clinker per month**. Operating hours = **600 h/month**.
> 
> **(a)** Calculate the Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) in MJ/tonne.
> **(b)** If 10% energy is recoverable through waste heat recovery, find the new SEC and the % reduction.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 – Energy from coal (thermal):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{coal}} &= 1500 \times 1000 \times 25 \;\text{MJ} \\
&= 37,50,00,000 \;\text{MJ/month} \\
&= 3.75 \times 10^7 \;\text{MJ/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 – Energy from electricity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{elec}} &= 2,00,000 \times 3.6 \;\text{MJ} \\
&= 7,20,000 \;\text{MJ/month} \\
&= 7.2 \times 10^5 \;\text{MJ/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Total energy input:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{total}} &= 3.75 \times 10^7 + 7.2 \times 10^5 \\
&= 3.822 \times 10^7 \;\text{MJ/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 – Specific Energy Consumption (a):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC &= \dfrac{3.822 \times 10^7}{20,000} \\[4pt]
&= 1911 \;\text{MJ/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 – Recoverable energy (10%):**

$$E_{\text{recoverable}} = 0.10 \times 3.822 \times 10^7 = 3.822 \times 10^6 \;\text{MJ/month}$$

**Step 6 – New energy requirement:**

$$E_{\text{new}} = 3.822 \times 10^7 - 3.822 \times 10^6 = 3.4398 \times 10^7 \;\text{MJ/month}$$

**Step 7 – New SEC (b):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SEC_{\text{new}} &= \dfrac{3.4398 \times 10^7}{20,000} \\
&= 1719.9 \;\text{MJ/tonne}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 – Percentage reduction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Reduction} &= \dfrac{1911 - 1719.9}{1911} \times 100 \\
&= 10\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> The percentage reduction matches the 10% recovery assumption, validating the energy balance principle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy Flow in a Production Plant (Block-Level Architecture)

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the energy flow architecture of a typical energy-intensive production plant, mapping all inputs, internal transformations, and outputs.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Raw Fuel Input] --> B1[Boiler or Furnace]
    A2[Electrical Energy] --> B2[Process Equipment]
    A3[Raw Materials] --> C1[Process Plant Core]
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> D1[Finished Product Output]
    C1 --> D2[Waste Heat Stream]
    C1 --> D3[Rejects and Byproducts]
    D2 --> E1[Heat Recovery Unit]
    E1 --> B1
    E1 --> B2
    F1[Energy Monitoring System] -.-> B1
    F1 -.-> B2
    F1 -.-> C1
    F1 -.-> E1
```

**Description of the architecture:**
- **Raw Fuel (A1)** and **Electrical Energy (A2)** form the primary energy inputs.
- **Boiler/Furnace (B1)** converts fuel into thermal energy (steam or hot gases).
- **Process Equipment (B2)** uses electrical energy for mechanical work.
- **Process Plant Core (C1)** transforms **Raw Materials (A3)** into products.
- **Finished Product (D1)** is the desired output; **Waste Heat (D2)** and **Rejects (D3)** are losses.
- **Heat Recovery Unit (E1)** recovers waste heat and feeds it back to (B1) and (B2), closing the loop.
- **Energy Monitoring System (F1)** is the supervisory layer that continuously measures KPIs (SEC, efficiency, etc.).

### 4.2 Hierarchical Breakdown of Production Energy Use

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Total Plant Energy Input] --> B1[Direct Process Energy]
    A --> B2[Utility Energy]
    A --> B3[Ancillary Energy]
    B1 --> C1[Reactor or Kiln Heat]
    B1 --> C2[Mechanical Work in Mixers]
    B2 --> C3[Steam Generation]
    B2 --> C4[Compressed Air]
    B2 --> C5[Cooling Water Pumps]
    B3 --> C6[Lighting]
    B3 --> C7[Ventilation and HVAC]
    B3 --> C8[Office and Admin Loads]
```

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- **Direct Process Energy (B1)** is the most critical — it directly heats or transforms the product.
- **Utility Energy (B2)** supports the main process; inefficiencies here cascade across the plant.
- **Ancillary Energy (B3)** is often the easiest win in an energy audit, since it has minimal impact on product quality.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
> **Define Specific Energy Consumption (SEC). Mention its units and significance in energy management.**

**Model Answer:**
Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) is the amount of energy required to produce one unit of a product, expressed as MJ/tonne, kWh/kg, or Gcal/tonne. It is a key performance indicator used to benchmark process efficiency across plants and time periods. A lower SEC indicates better energy efficiency.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
> **What is meant by "Energy Productivity"? How is it different from Specific Energy Consumption?**

**Model Answer:**
Energy Productivity (EP) is defined as the monetary value of output produced per unit of energy consumed (e.g., ₹/kWh). It is the **reciprocal of Energy Intensity** and reflects the economic efficiency of energy use. SEC, in contrast, is a physical metric (energy per unit of physical output). EP captures both energy and economic performance.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
> 
> **(a)** Explain the concept of energy balance in a production system. Derive the general energy balance equation and state the key assumptions for a steady-state process. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A chemical plant consumes **800 tonnes of furnace oil per month** (GCV = **40 MJ/kg**), **1,50,000 kWh of electricity per month**, and produces **12,000 tonnes of product per month**. Calculate: **(i)** the total energy input in MJ/month, **(ii)** the SEC in MJ/tonne, and **(iii)** the energy cost per tonne if furnace oil costs **₹50/kg** and electricity costs **₹8/kWh**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Energy Balance in Production (7 Marks):**

The energy balance is a direct application of the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to a defined system boundary.

**General equation:**

$$
E_{\text{in}} = E_{\text{out}} + E_{\text{loss}} + E_{\text{stored}}
$$

**Key Assumptions for a Steady-State Process:**

1. Mass and energy storage within the system boundary = **0**.
2. Operating conditions (temperature, pressure, flow) are **time-invariant**.
3. Material and energy flows are **continuous and uniform**.
4. The system boundary is fixed and **well-defined**.

**Simplified steady-state form:**

$$
\sum E_{\text{in}} = \sum E_{\text{out}} + \sum E_{\text{loss}}
$$

The "why" is that for a continuously operating plant, the transient startup and shutdown phases are negligible compared to the steady operating period, allowing us to treat the system as time-invariant.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks):**

**(i) Energy from furnace oil:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{oil}} &= 800 \times 1000 \times 40 \;\text{MJ} \\
&= 3.2 \times 10^7 \;\text{MJ/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Energy from electricity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\text{elec}} &= 1,50,000 \times 3.6 \;\text{MJ} \\
&= 5.4 \times 10^5 \;\text{MJ/month}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Energy Input:**

$$
E_{\text{total}} = 3.2 \times 10^7 + 5.4 \times 10^5 = 3.254 \times 10^7 \;\text{MJ/month}
$$

**(ii) Specific Energy Consumption:**

$$
SEC = \dfrac{3.254 \times 10^7}{12,000} = 2711.67 \;\text{MJ/tonne}
$$

**(iii) Energy Cost per Tonne:**

**Cost of furnace oil:**

$$
C_{\text{oil}} = 800,000 \times 50 = 4.0 \times 10^7 \;\text{₹/month}
$$

**Cost of electricity:**

$$
C_{\text{elec}} = 1,50,000 \times 8 = 1.2 \times 10^6 \;\text{₹/month}
$$

**Total Energy Cost:**

$$
C_{\text{total}} = 4.0 \times 10^7 + 1.2 \times 10^6 = 4.12 \times 10^7 \;\text{₹/month}
$$

**Energy Cost per Tonne:**

$$
C_{\text{unit}} = \dfrac{4.12 \times 10^7}{12,000} = 3433.33 \;\text{₹/tonne}
$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
> 
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major energy-consuming areas of a typical manufacturing plant. Describe the role of an Energy Monitoring System. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A boiler consumes **1200 kg/h of coal** with a **GCV of 22 MJ/kg** to generate **steam at 8 bar, saturated**. The feed water enters at **30 °C** and the flue gas exits at **280 °C**. If the combustion efficiency is **78%**, calculate: **(i)** the heat input per hour, **(ii)** the heat lost in flue gases (assume flue gas mass = 1.5 × coal mass, $c_p$ = **1.05 kJ/kg·K**, ambient = **30 °C**), and **(iii)** the steam generation rate in kg/h. **(Latent heat at 8 bar = 2014 kJ/kg; $c_{pw}$ = 4.18 kJ/kg·K). (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Energy Monitoring (7 Marks):**

A typical manufacturing plant has three major energy-consuming zones:

1. **Direct Process Energy:** Reactors, kilns, furnaces, dryers.
2. **Utility Energy:** Boilers, compressors, cooling towers, pumps.
3. **Ancillary Energy:** Lighting, HVAC, office loads.

**Role of the Energy Monitoring System (EMS):**
- Continuous logging of fuel, steam, and electricity consumption.
- Real-time computation of KPIs (SEC, efficiency, $CO_2$ emissions).
- Alarm generation on deviation from baseline.
- Data archival for regulatory compliance and trend analysis.

A simple block representation:

> **Raw Materials + Energy → Process → Product + Losses → Heat Recovery → Reuse**
> **Energy Monitoring System (Supervisory Layer)** continuously measures all flow streams.

---

**(b) Boiler Numerical (7 Marks):**

**(i) Heat input per hour:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{in}} &= m_{\text{coal}} \times GCV \\
&= 1200 \times 22,000 \;\text{kJ/h} \\
&= 2.64 \times 10^7 \;\text{kJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Heat lost in flue gas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{fg}} &= 1.5 \times 1200 = 1800 \;\text{kg/h} \\
\Delta T &= 280 - 30 = 250 \;\text{°C} \\
Q_{\text{loss}} &= m_{\text{fg}} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 1800 \times 1.05 \times 250 \\
&= 4.725 \times 10^5 \;\text{kJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Useful heat and steam generation rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{useful}} &= \eta_{\text{comb}} \times Q_{\text{in}} \\
&= 0.78 \times 2.64 \times 10^7 \\
&= 2.0592 \times 10^7 \;\text{kJ/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

For each kg of steam generated, the enthalpy supplied is:

$$
\Delta h = c_{pw} \cdot (T_{\text{sat}} - 30) + h_{\text{fg}}
$$

At 8 bar, $T_{\text{sat}} \approx 170.4 \;°C$ and $h_{\text{fg}} = 2014 \;\text{kJ/kg}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta h &= 4.18 \times (170.4 - 30) + 2014 \\
&= 4.18 \times 140.4 + 2014 \\
&= 586.87 + 2014 \\
&= 2600.87 \;\text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Steam generation rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{\text{steam}} &= \dfrac{Q_{\text{useful}}}{\Delta h} \\
&= \dfrac{2.0592 \times 10^7}{2600.87} \\
&= 7917.4 \;\text{kg/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Unit consistency is the #1 reason for losing marks.** Always convert GCV to kJ/kg, mass to kg, and time to hours before substituting.
> - In SEC calculations, students frequently forget to **multiply electrical energy by 3.6** when other energies are in MJ.
> - Do NOT confuse **GCV (Gross Calorific Value)** with **NCV (Net Calorific Value)**. For combustion calculations, **GCV is standard** unless the problem explicitly states NCV.
> - In boiler problems, students often forget to include the **sensible heating of feed water** (the $c_{pw} \cdot \Delta T$ term) before the latent heat. The full enthalpy of saturated steam must be used, not just $h_{\text{fg}}$.
> - Always show the **intermediate steps**. KTU examiners award method marks even if the final answer has an arithmetic slip.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SEC (Specific Energy Consumption)** is the single most important metric in production energy management; lower is better.
- The **energy balance equation** ($E_{\text{in}} = E_{\text{out}} + E_{\text{loss}}$) is the foundation of every audit; steady-state simplifies storage terms to zero.
- **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ** — memorize this for instant conversion in numericals.
- **1 TOE = 41.868 GJ** — used in macro-level energy statistics.
- **Combustion efficiency** is computed via the **loss method** ($\eta = 100 - \Sigma L_i$) rather than the input/output method.
- **Flue gas loss** is the largest source of inefficiency in most boilers; lowering excess air and stack temperature yields the quickest savings.
- **Cogeneration (CHP)** simultaneously produces electricity and useful heat, raising overall fuel utilization to **70–90%**.
- **Waste Heat Recovery (WHR)** loops reject heat back to the process, reducing net energy input.
- **Energy Productivity** is a monetary metric; **SEC** is a physical metric — both are essential.
- The **Energy Monitoring System (EMS)** is the backbone of continuous improvement in any production facility.
- **HVAC and lighting** in ancillary systems are low-hanging fruit for energy savings.
- A **plant-level energy policy** with **targets and audits** is a mandatory requirement under the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001** for designated consumers in India.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cumulative Sum of Differences (CUSUM); Energy Management Information Systems (EMIS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cumulative Sum of Differences (CUSUM); Energy Management Information Systems (EMIS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 3: Cumulative Sum of Differences (CUSUM) and Energy Management Information Systems (EMIS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Cumulative Sum of Differences (CUSUM)

**Formal Definition:**
Cumulative Sum of Differences (CUSUM) is a sequential statistical analysis technique used in energy management to detect small, persistent, and systematic shifts in the mean of a process variable (such as specific energy consumption, demand, or load) away from a pre-established target or historical baseline. It plots the cumulative sum of deviations of observed values from a reference value over time, transforming noisy short-term fluctuations into a clear visual trend.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** CUSUM is treated as a core **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** tool under the *Energy Monitoring & Targeting (M&T)* framework. It is officially a high-yield topic for both Part A (3-mark) definitions and Part B (14-mark) numerical/analytical problems.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a student who is supposed to score an average of **70 marks** in every test. In any single test, the student may score 65 or 75 due to a tough or easy paper — these are normal random ups and downs that do not mean much. However, if the student is consistently scoring 60, then *something has changed* — perhaps they stopped studying. If we add up (cumulatively sum) the difference between the actual marks and the target of 70 after every test, a flat or oscillating line near zero means "all is well", but a steadily diverging line (either going up or down) gives a clear, early warning. CUSUM does exactly this for energy consumption — it filters out daily random noise and exposes any gradual, systemic drift in energy performance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants/Metrics in Bold:**
> - **CUSUM Statistic (Sₜ):** The running cumulative sum of deviations at time *t*.
> - **Target Value (μ₀):** The historical baseline or expected specific energy consumption.
> - **Reference Value (K):** Half the magnitude of the shift we wish to detect — expressed as $K = \frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{2}$.
> - **Decision Interval (H):** A threshold value; when the CUSUM crosses H, an "out-of-control" signal is raised.

---

### 1.2 Energy Management Information Systems (EMIS)

**Formal Definition:**
An Energy Management Information System (EMIS) is a structured, computer-based, decision-support architecture that integrates hardware (meters/sensors, data loggers, PLCs), software (databases, analytics, dashboards), and organizational procedures to systematically collect, store, analyze, verify, and disseminate energy and process data across an industrial or commercial facility. EMIS is the digital backbone of any ISO 50001-compliant Energy Management System (EnMS).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of an EMIS as the **"central nervous system"** of a factory's energy use. Just as nerves carry signals from your fingertips to your brain so that you can decide whether to pull your hand from a hot stove, an EMIS carries energy data from hundreds of meters at motors, boilers, and lighting panels back to a central computer screen — and from there, the energy manager (the "brain") can instantly see where energy is being wasted and decide what to fix. Without it, the energy manager is essentially blind, relying on the monthly electricity bill alone, which is like trying to drive a car while looking only at the rear-view mirror.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Building Blocks in Bold:**
> - **Data Acquisition Layer:** Smart meters, CTs/PTs, ultrasonic flow meters, BTU meters, temperature/pressure transmitters.
> - **Communication Layer:** Wired (Modbus, BACnet) and wireless (LoRaWAN, Wi-Fi) protocols.
> - **Data Management Layer:** SCADA historians, SQL/NoSQL databases, time-series databases.
> - **Analytics & Reporting Layer:** M&V software, KPI dashboards, regression analysis, CUSUM algorithms, alarms.
> - **Decision/Action Layer:** Energy manager review, ISO 50001 corrective actions, M&V verification.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The CUSUM Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

CUSUM works on a beautiful, simple idea: *random noise averages to zero over the long run, but a real shift compounds without limit.* Because of this, when we add the deviations one by one, noise cancels itself out and real drifts accumulate.

**Step 1: Establish the Target (μ₀)**
The engineer first determines the *expected* baseline. For an air compressor, for example, μ₀ may be the *kWh per Nm³ of compressed air* observed during a properly tuned, well-operated reference period. This becomes the line of "no concern."

**Step 2: Compute the Deviation Each Period**
For every new time period (e.g., every shift, day, or week), the actual specific energy consumption $X_t$ is measured, and the deviation from target is calculated:
$$d_t = X_t - \mu_0$$
A *positive* deviation means worse-than-target performance (wasting energy), while a *negative* deviation means better performance.

**Step 3: Form the Cumulative Sum**
The deviations are summed cumulatively:
$$S_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} (X_i - \mu_0) = S_{t-1} + (X_t - \mu_0)$$
where the recursion starts at $S_0 = 0$.

**Step 4: Plot Sₜ vs. Time**
The chart of $S_t$ vs. time is the CUSUM chart. A flat, wandering line means the process is statistically in control. A line with a sustained positive slope indicates an *upward shift* (energy performance worsening). A line with a sustained negative slope indicates a *downward shift* (energy performance improving — also investigated because it may indicate a sensor drift or measurement fault).

**Step 5: Apply the Decision Rule (H)**
A decision threshold **H** is set. If $S_t \geq H$, an alarm is raised: the process mean has shifted upward by an unacceptable amount. The most common choice is the **One-Sided CUSUM**, which uses a *slack variable* to keep the statistic non-negative and detect only upward shifts:
$$C_t^+ = \max(0, \; X_t - (\mu_0 + K) + C_{t-1}^+)$$
A downward-shift detector is its mirror:
$$C_t^- = \max(0, \; (\mu_0 - K) - X_t + C_{t-1}^-)$$

**Step 6: Trigger Corrective Action**
When the cumulative sum crosses H, the energy manager initiates root-cause analysis, ISO 50001 corrective action, and M&V verification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why CUSUM is Superior to Simple Shewhart Charts in Energy M&T:**
> Shewhart control charts only react when a *single* data point exceeds ±3σ — they are very slow to detect *gradual* shifts of less than 1.5σ. CUSUM, on the other hand, can detect a shift of as little as **0.5σ to 1.0σ** within a few periods because it *integrates* the evidence. In energy-intensive industries, where a 1% drift can mean crores of rupees per year, this early warning is invaluable.

---

### 2.2 EMIS — Architecture & Logic Flow

**Step 1: Define the Energy Boundary & Energy Review (per ISO 50001)**
Before any meter is installed, the facility's *energy sources* (electricity, diesel, natural gas, steam) and *significant energy uses* (SEUs) are mapped. This is the *EnPI (Energy Performance Indicator)* tree.

**Step 2: Meter Hierarchy (the "Energy M&V Tree")**
Meters are deployed in a top-down hierarchy: **Main Feeder → Sub-Panel → Equipment → Sub-Equipment**. The "M&V Rule of 80/20" recommends metering 80% of the consumption at 20% of the cost.

**Step 3: Data Acquisition**
Data is collected at intervals (typically 1-min, 15-min, or 1-hour) by data loggers and pushed up to a SCADA/DCS system or directly to a cloud gateway.

**Step 4: Data Normalization & Storage**
Raw data is *normalized* (e.g., kWh per ton of product, kWh per °C-day of cooling degree) to remove the effect of weather, throughput, and production mix. Normalized values are stored in a time-series database.

**Step 5: Analytics — CUSUM, Regression, CUSUM, and CUSUM**
This is where statistical tools (CUSUM, Shewhart, regression, multivariate analysis) crunch the data to detect anomalies and quantify savings.

**Step 6: Reporting — Dashboards, KPIs, M&V Reports**
The energy manager sees live KPI dashboards, weekly exception reports, and quarterly M&V reports aligned to **IPMVP (International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol)** Option B or D.

**Step 7: Action Loop — ISO 50001 PDCA**
Findings flow into the *Plan–Do–Check–Act* cycle: new operational controls, retrofits, and training; verified by subsequent EMIS data.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Term | Symbol / Variable | Purpose / Boundary Condition | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Deviation: $d_t = X_t - \mu_0$ | $X_t$ = observed value at time $t$; $\mu_0$ = target mean | Per-period difference from baseline | Same as $X_t$ (e.g., kWh/ton) |
| 2 | CUSUM Statistic: $S_t = S_{t-1} + (X_t - \mu_0)$ | $S_0 = 0$ | Cumulative sum (two-sided form) | Same as $X_t$ |
| 3 | Reference (Slack) Value: $K = \dfrac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{2}$ | $\mu_1$ = mean of *out-of-control* state | Usually $K \approx 0.5\sigma$ to $1.0\sigma$ | Same as $X_t$ |
| 4 | Upper CUSUM (one-sided): $C_t^+ = \max\bigl(0,\; X_t - (\mu_0 + K) + C_{t-1}^+\bigr)$ | $C_0^+ = 0$ | Detects upward (worsening) drift only | Same as $X_t$ |
| 5 | Lower CUSUM (one-sided): $C_t^- = \max\bigl(0,\; (\mu_0 - K) - X_t + C_{t-1}^-\bigr)$ | $C_0^- = 0$ | Detects downward (improving/faulty) drift only | Same as $X_t$ |
| 6 | Decision Rule: Alarm if $C_t^+ \geq H$ | $H$ = decision interval | Common choice: $H = 4\sigma$ to $5\sigma$ | Same as $X_t$ |
| 7 | Average Run Length (in-control): $ARL_0 \approx \exp(H/2\sigma)$ | $\sigma$ = process standard deviation | Larger $H$ = fewer false alarms | Number of samples |
| 8 | Average Run Length (out-of-control): $ARL_1 \approx \exp(-2(\delta - K)H/\sigma^2)$ | $\delta = \mu_1 - \mu_0$ | Smaller $ARL_1$ = faster detection | Number of samples |
| 9 | Energy Performance Indicator: $EnPI = \dfrac{\text{Energy Consumed}}{\text{Production / Driver Variable}}$ | — | Normalized metric; basis of CUSUM target | e.g., kWh/ton, MJ/m² |
| 10 | M&V Savings (IPMVP Option C): $Savings = (E_{\text{baseline}} - E_{\text{reported}}) \pm \text{Uncertainty}$ | — | Verified from EMIS data | kWh/year, ₹/year |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> CUSUM is used in real plants to monitor boiler efficiency, compressed-air specific power (kWh/Nm³), chiller kW/ton, furnace heat loss, and even tariff anomaly detection in power bills. EMIS is the *infrastructure* that makes CUSUM and every other M&T tool actually usable on a daily basis by an energy manager in a large industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix, and Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Derivation of the One-Sided CUSUM Recursion

**Starting Point — The Cumulative Sum:**
We begin with the simple cumulative deviation:
$$S_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t} (X_i - \mu_0)$$

**Step 1 — Rewrite in Recursive Form:**
$$S_t = S_{t-1} + (X_t - \mu_0)$$
This is mathematically exact and computationally cheap (only the previous value is needed).

**Step 2 — Introduce a Slack Value (K):**
We wish to detect a shift of magnitude $\delta = \mu_1 - \mu_0$. If we subtract only $\mu_0$, even a small positive drift slowly accumulates and a large negative drift is needlessly suppressed. By subtracting $(\mu_0 + K)$ instead, the CUSUM only climbs when the *new* mean has truly exceeded the *mid-point* between $\mu_0$ and $\mu_1$. Thus:
$$C_t^+ = C_{t-1}^+ + \bigl[X_t - (\mu_0 + K)\bigr]$$

**Step 3 — Enforce Non-Negativity (the "max" operator):**
Because we are only interested in *upward* shifts, we reset the CUSUM to zero whenever it would otherwise go negative. This prevents a long run of good performance from "masking" a future run of bad performance:
$$C_t^+ = \max\bigl(0,\; X_t - (\mu_0 + K) + C_{t-1}^+\bigr)$$

This is the **Standard Tabular CUSUM** of Page (1954) and the form most often examined in KTU papers. The mirror equation for downward shifts is:
$$C_t^- = \max\bigl(0,\; (\mu_0 - K) - X_t + C_{t-1}^-\bigr)$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule:**
The decision interval **H** is a horizontal line drawn on the CUSUM chart. The first sample $t$ for which $C_t^+ \geq H$ is the *change-point estimate*. At that moment, the energy manager is alerted.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A compressed air system is to be monitored using CUSUM. The target specific energy consumption is **μ₀ = 0.10 kWh/Nm³** with a process standard deviation of **σ = 0.005 kWh/Nm³**. The energy manager wishes to detect an upward shift of magnitude **δ = 0.01 kWh/Nm³** (i.e., 10% worsening). Take **K = δ/2** and **H = 4σ**. The following 12 daily observations of specific energy consumption (in kWh/Nm³) are recorded:
$X = \{0.101,\; 0.099,\; 0.105,\; 0.108,\; 0.110,\; 0.112,\; 0.109,\; 0.115,\; 0.118,\; 0.120,\; 0.117,\; 0.122\}$.

**Step 1 — Compute K and H:**
$$K = \frac{\delta}{2} = \frac{0.01}{2} = 0.005 \text{ kWh/Nm}^3$$
$$H = 4\sigma = 4 \times 0.005 = 0.020 \text{ kWh/Nm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Form the One-Sided Upper CUSUM:**
Apply $C_t^+ = \max\bigl(0,\; X_t - (\mu_0 + K) + C_{t-1}^+\bigr) = \max\bigl(0,\; X_t - 0.105 + C_{t-1}^+\bigr)$.

| Day $t$ | $X_t$ | $X_t - 0.105$ | $C_{t-1}^+$ | $C_t^+$ | Alarm? |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 0.101 | −0.004 | 0.000 | 0.000 | No |
| 2 | 0.099 | −0.006 | 0.000 | 0.000 | No |
| 3 | 0.105 |  0.000 | 0.000 | 0.000 | No |
| 4 | 0.108 |  0.003 | 0.000 | 0.003 | No |
| 5 | 0.110 |  0.005 | 0.003 | 0.008 | No |
| 6 | 0.112 |  0.007 | 0.008 | 0.015 | No |
| 7 | 0.109 |  0.004 | 0.015 | 0.019 | No |
| 8 | 0.115 |  0.010 | 0.019 | 0.029 | **YES (>$H$)** |
| 9 | 0.118 |  0.013 | 0.029 | 0.042 | YES |
| 10 | 0.120 | 0.015 | 0.042 | 0.057 | YES |
| 11 | 0.117 | 0.012 | 0.057 | 0.069 | YES |
| 12 | 0.122 | 0.017 | 0.069 | 0.086 | YES |

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
- The CUSUM crossed the decision interval $H = 0.020$ on **Day 8**.
- This means the *true change-point* is estimated to be near Day 8; investigation reveals the air compressor's intake filter was choked from Day 6 onwards, raising the specific power consumption by ~12% — well above the 10% shift CUSUM was tuned to detect.
- The early warning gave the maintenance team 2 days of advance notice *before* the unmonitored energy waste would have shown up in the monthly bill.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the ARL₀ Formula (Average Run Length In-Control)

**Step 1 — Define ARL₀:**
$ARL_0$ is the expected number of samples before a false alarm is raised when the process is actually in control ($\mu = \mu_0$).

**Step 2 — Brownian-Motion Approximation:**
For small $\sigma$ and large $H$, the CUSUM path can be modeled as a Brownian motion with drift $\delta = \mu_1 - \mu_0$ and variance $\sigma^2$. The first-passage time to a barrier of height $H$ has an expected value given by Wald's identity.

**Step 3 — Wald's Approximation Result:**
$$ARL_0 \approx \frac{\exp(H/2\sigma) - 1}{\delta/\sigma} \;\; \xrightarrow{\;H \gg 2\sigma\;} \;\; \exp\!\left(\frac{H}{2\sigma}\right)$$
For a Shewhart chart with $\pm 3\sigma$ limits, $ARL_0 \approx 370$ samples. CUSUM with $H = 4\sigma$ gives $ARL_0 \approx e^2 \approx 7.4$ — i.e., it triggers a false alarm in only ~7 samples, while a Shewhart chart would need ~370 samples for the same *average*. The trade-off is the much faster *true* detection of small shifts, characterized by $ARL_1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule-of-Thumb used in industry:**
> - Set $H$ to give an $ARL_0$ between **200 and 500** samples (acceptable false-alarm rate for an energy audit context).
> - Set $K$ at the **mid-point** between the in-control mean and the smallest shift of practical importance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EMIS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["LAYER 1 - DATA ACQUISITION"]
        A1["Main Feeder Meter"]
        A2["Sub-Panel Energy Meters"]
        A3["Equipment Level Meters"]
        A4["Sub-Equipment Sensors"]
        A5["Utility Meters EB Diesel Gas Steam"]
    end

    subgraph L2["LAYER 2 - COMMUNICATION NETWORK"]
        B1["Wired Protocols - Modbus BACnet RS485"]
        B2["Wireless Protocols - LoRaWAN WiFi Zigbee"]
        B3["Gateways and Data Loggers"]
    end

    subgraph L3["LAYER 3 - DATA MANAGEMENT"]
        C1["SCADA DCS Historian"]
        C2["Time Series Database SQL or NoSQL"]
        C3["Data Validation and Gap Filling"]
        C4["Energy Normalization Engine"]
    end

    subgraph L4["LAYER 4 - ANALYTICS AND INTELLIGENCE"]
        D1["KPI Computation EnPI"]
        D2["CUSUM SPC Engine"]
        D3["Regression and M&V Module"]
        D4["Alarm and Exception Engine"]
    end

    subgraph L5["LAYER 5 - REPORTING AND DECISION"]
        E1["Live KPI Dashboards"]
        E2["Weekly Energy Reports"]
        E3["M&V Savings Reports IPMVP"]
        E4["ISO 50001 Corrective Actions"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B2
    A5 --> B1
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
    C4 --> D3
    C4 --> D4
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E2
    D3 --> E3
    D4 --> E4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Energy flows from the *physical* (Layer 1) to the *analytical* (Layer 4) to the *human* (Layer 5). Each layer adds value: raw pulses → kWh → normalized kWh/ton → statistical CUSUM signal → corrective action. This is the same five-layer pattern used in any modern SCADA/IIoT system.

---

### 4.2 CUSUM Decision-Logic Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Start - Period t = 1"]
    P1["Measure Xt - observed specific energy"]
    P2["Compute deviation d = Xt - mu0"]
    P3["Update Ct upper = max 0 Xt - mu0+K + Ct-1"]
    P4["Update Ct lower = max 0 mu0-K - Xt + Ct-1"]
    P5{"Is Ct upper greater than or equal to H?"}
    P6["Raise alarm - upward shift detected - initiate root cause analysis"]
    P7{"Is Ct lower greater than or equal to H?"}
    P8["Raise alarm - downward shift detected - check for sensor fault or energy savings"]
    P9["Continue - process in control"]
    P10["t = t+1 - go to Period t"]
    P0 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P7
    P5 -- YES --> P6
    P5 -- NO --> P9
    P7 -- YES --> P8
    P7 -- NO --> P9
    P9 --> P10
    P10 --> P1
```

---

### 4.3 EMIS Meter-Hierarchy Schematic (M&V Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M0["Facility Main Energy Source - EB 11kV or DG"]
    M1["Level 1 - Main Feeder Energy Meter kWh MV"]
    M2a["Level 2 - HVAC Sub-Panel Meter"]
```
