# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a furnace. List any four types of industrial furnaces with one-line applications.**

**Model Answer:**
A furnace is an enclosed, refractory-lined chamber used to transfer heat from a combustion or electrical source to a charge (workpiece) for thermal processing such as melting, reheating, heat treatment, or sintering.

Four types:
1. **Reheating furnace** – heating steel billets before hot rolling.
2. **Induction furnace** – melting steel and alloys using electromagnetic induction.
3. **Rotary kiln** – calcining limestone or producing cement clinker.
4. **Annealing furnace** – softening cold-worked metal by controlled heating and slow cooling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3-mark question):** Award 1 mark for definition, 0.5 mark × 4 for each type with correct application. Avoid vague answers like "heating furnace" without context.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between batch and continuous furnaces. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Batch Furnace | Continuous Furnace |
|---|---|---|
| **Operation** | Charge loaded, processed, and unloaded in cycles | Charge moves steadily through the furnace |
| **Throughput** | Low; suited to varied products | High; suited to mass production |
| **Thermal efficiency** | Lower (frequent start-stop losses) | Higher (steady-state operation) |
| **Flexibility** | High (variable temperature/time) | Low (fixed profile) |
| **Example** | Bogie hearth furnace | Walking-beam reheating furnace |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** For 3 marks, present in a 3-row table format. The examiner will mark definition (1) + contrast (1) + example (1).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** A furnace uses a fuel with the following ultimate analysis: C = 78%, H = 5%, O = 6%, S = 1.5%, N = 1.5%, Ash = 8%. Calculate:
1. Stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel **(3 marks)**
2. Actual air supplied if excess air is 20% **(2 marks)**
3. Air-fuel ratio **(2 marks)**

**(b)** For the furnace in (a), the flue gas leaves at 720 °C. Flue gas flow = 16 kg/kg fuel. Average Cp of flue gas = 1.05 kJ/kg·K. Reference temperature = 30 °C. LHV of fuel = 42,000 kJ/kg. **Calculate:**
1. Stack loss as % of heat input **(3 marks)**
2. Combustion efficiency **(2 marks)**
3. State TWO methods to reduce stack loss in this furnace **(2 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1: O₂ stoichiometric**

$$O_2 = 2.667C + 8H - O + S$$
$$= 2.667(0.78) + 8(0.05) - 0.06 + 0.015$$
$$= 2.080 + 0.400 - 0.060 + 0.015 = 2.435 \text{ kg O}_2/\text{kg fuel}$$

**Step 2: Theoretical air**

$$TA = 4.76 \times 2.435 = 11.59 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 3: Actual air (e = 0.20)**

$$AA = TA \times 1.20 = 11.59 \times 1.20 = 13.91 \text{ kg air/kg fuel}$$

**Step 4: AFR**

$$AFR = 13.91 : 1$$

### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1: Stack loss per kg fuel**

$$Q_{stack} = m_{flue} \times C_p \times (T_{stack} - T_{ref})$$
$$= 16 \times 1.05 \times (720 - 30) = 16 \times 1.05 \times 690 = 11{,}592 \text{ kJ/kg fuel}$$

**Step 2: Stack loss as % of input**

$$\%\,\text{Stack loss} = \frac{11{,}592}{42{,}000} \times 100 = 27.6\%$$

**Step 3: Combustion efficiency**

$$\eta_{comb} = 100 - 27.6 = 72.4\%$$

**Step 4: Two methods to reduce stack loss:**
1. **Air preheater (recuperator/regenerator):** Preheating combustion air to 300–500 °C recovers waste heat and reduces the enthalpy leaving the stack.
2. **Reducing excess air:** Operating closer to stoichiometric (e.g., 5–10% instead of 20%) lowers flue gas mass and hence stack loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 alternative accepted answer (Bacharach's method):** $\eta_{comb} \approx 100 - \frac{A}{B}$ where A = flue gas loss indicator. The heat-balance method is the KTU-preferred approach.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Define:
1. **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** and **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** **(2 marks)**
2. **Stoichiometric air** and **Excess air** **(2 marks)**
3. **Recuperator** and **Regenerator** **(2 marks)**

**(b)** A continuous annealing furnace heats 5 tonnes/hr of steel strip from 30 °C to 700 °C. Average Cp of steel = 0.65 kJ/kg·K. The furnace uses natural gas (LHV = 48,000 kJ/kg) at 200 kg/hr. The stack gas leaves at 600 °C at 18 kg/kg fuel. Cp of stack gas = 1.05 kJ/kg·K. Reference T = 30 °C. Wall losses = 4% of input. **Calculate:** **(8 marks)**

1. Useful heat delivered to strip
2. Stack loss per hour (MJ)
3. Furnace thermal efficiency
4. Suggest ONE energy conservation measure with expected savings

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

### Part (a) Definitions

1. **HHV:** Total heat released from complete combustion of unit mass of fuel, with the water vapor in products condensed to liquid (latent heat recovered).
   **LHV:** Total heat released from complete combustion of unit mass of fuel, with water leaving as vapor (latent heat not recovered).
   $$HHV = LHV + 9h \times 2442 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

2. **Stoichiometric air:** The exact theoretical quantity of air required for complete combustion of a fuel, with neither oxygen nor fuel left over.
   **Excess air:** The quantity of air supplied *beyond* stoichiometric requirement, expressed as a percentage or fraction.
   $$AA = TA \times (1 + e)$$

3. **Recuperator:** A *continuous* heat exchanger (metallic tubes or ceramic plates) that transfers heat from hot flue gas to incoming combustion air without changing flow direction.
   **Regenerator:** A *cyclic* heat exchanger using two refractory checker chambers—one absorbs heat from flue gas, the other releases it to combustion air; they switch roles periodically.

### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1: Heat delivered to strip (useful heat)**

$$Q_{load} = 5000 \times 0.65 \times (700 - 30) = 5000 \times 0.65 \times 670 = 2{,}177{,}500 \text{ kJ/hr} = 2177.5 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

**Step 2: Stack loss per hour**

$$Q_{stack} = (200 \times 18) \times 1.05 \times (600 - 30)$$
$$= 3600 \times 1.05 \times 570 = 2{,}154{,}600 \text{ kJ/hr} = 2154.6 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

**Step 3: Heat input**

$$Q_{in} = 200 \times 48{,}000 = 9{,}600{,}000 \text{ kJ/hr} = 9600 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

**Step 4: Wall loss**

$$Q_{wall} = 0.04 \times 9600 = 384 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

**Step 5: Furnace thermal efficiency**

$$\eta = \frac{Q_{load}}{Q_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{2177.5}{9600} \times 100 = 22.68\%$$

**Step 6: Energy conservation measure (1 mark)**

> *Install a recuperator to preheat combustion air to 250 °C.* Expected energy saving ≈ 8–10% of fuel. The strip (which is a thin product) is inefficient to heat compared to ingots; combining the recuperator with a **strip-entry preheater** that uses furnace exhaust can save another 3–5%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Many students write Q_load = 5000 × 0.65 × 670 kJ/hr and forget the unit conversion. Always state intermediate units.
> 2. **Confusing m_steel with m_fuel:** In part (b), the 5000 kg/hr is *steel*, not fuel. Mixing these will cost 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting Cp_steel average:** For 30 → 700 °C, Cp varies from 0.47 to 0.70 kJ/kg·K. Using 0.65 is acceptable; using 0.50 is the most common error.
> 4. **Not including unaccounted loss in heat balance:** The 22.68% efficiency is low because of the small ΔT and high stack temp. A *complete* heat balance should show unaccounted = 100% − (22.68 + 22.45 + 4.00) ≈ 50.87%. The student must comment on this.
> 5. **Skipping the LHV vs HHV choice:** In a furnace, water leaves as vapor → use **LHV**, not HHV. Writing "GCV = 48,000" and using GCV throughout costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST (Read this in the last 30 minutes before exam)**

### Core Concepts (Must Know)
- ☐ Furnace = refractory-lined enclosed chamber; **purpose = heat transfer to a charge**
- ☐ Four classifications: by heat source, by operation (batch vs continuous), by heat transfer mode, by application
- ☐ **Batch furnace** = cyclical; **Continuous furnace** = steady-state (higher efficiency)

### Combustion & Air (Numericals)
- ☐ **Stoichiometric O₂ per kg fuel** = 2.667C + 8H + S − O
- ☐ **Theoretical Air** = 4.76 × O₂_stoich (where **4.76 = kg air per kg O₂**)
- ☐ **Actual Air** = TA × (1 + excess air fraction)
- ☐ **AFR = AA : 1** = mass ratio

### Heating Values
- ☐ **HHV > LHV**; difference = 9 × H_mass_fraction × 2442 kJ/kg
- ☐ Furnace calculations use **LHV** because flue water leaves as vapor
- ☐ Excess air ↑ → LHV effective ↓ (because of diluent N₂ heating)

### Efficiency & Heat Balance
- ☐ **η_furnace = Q_load / Q_fuel × 100%**
- ☐ Heat balance: Q_in = Q_load + Q_stack + Q_wall + Q_unaccounted
- ☐ **Stack loss** is usually the **largest single loss** (20–40% of input)
- ☐ **Reducing stack temp by 20 °C** ≈ 1% fuel saving (rule of thumb)

### Heat Transfer in Furnaces
- ☐ **Radiation dominant > 800 °C** (4th-power T law)
- ☐ **Convection dominant < 600 °C** (Newton's law)
- ☐ **Conduction** = inside solid charge; transient (error function solution)
- ☐ σ = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²·K⁴**

### Energy Conservation Strategies
- ☐ **Air preheat** (recuperator: 300–500 °C; regenerator: up to 1200 °C)
- ☐ **Reduce excess air** to 5–10% from typical 20–30%
- ☐ **Improved refractory insulation** to cut wall loss
- ☐ **Seal openings** (doors, peepholes) in batch furnaces
- ☐ **Waste heat boiler** on stack (process steam generation)
- ☐ **Oxygen enrichment** (replaces N₂ ballast)
- ☐ **Variable-speed drives** on ID/FD fans and conveyors

### Numerical Safety Points
- ☐ Always state **assumptions** (dry air, complete combustion, steady state)
- ☐ Express **excess air in % (e.g., 20%)** or fraction (0.20) — be consistent
- ☐ Use **kg and kJ** or **tonnes and MJ** — don't mix
- ☐ Verify heat balance to ±2% — anything more implies missing losses
- ☐ Round final answer to **2–3 significant figures**

### Examiner Mindset (5 most-tested topics)
1. 🔥 Stoichiometric air calculation from ultimate analysis *(5–7 marks)*
2. 🔥 Furnace heat balance & efficiency *(5–7 marks)*
3. 🔥 Stack loss % and reduction strategies *(3–4 marks)*
4. 🔥 Air preheat / WHR savings *(5–7 marks)*
5. 🔥 HHV vs LHV conversion and use *(2–3 marks)*

> **END OF MODULE 4 — FURNACES NOTES**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pumps and Fans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pumps and Fans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Pumps (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **pump** is a mechanical device used to transfer fluids (liquids or slurries) by means of mechanical action, converting rotational kinetic energy from an electric motor or engine into hydrodynamic energy of the fluid flow. The primary purpose of a pump in an industrial system is to move fluid from a lower pressure region to a higher pressure region, overcoming system resistance (friction, elevation, pressure differentials).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Anchor:** Pumps are classified under **Fluid-Handling Equipment** in energy audits. They typically consume **15% to 25%** of total industrial electrical energy, making them a prime target for energy conservation measures (ECMs).

### 1.2 Definition of Fans

A **fan** is a rotary-bladed machine used to move air or other gases with a moderate increase in pressure. Unlike compressors, fans operate on gases with negligible compression (pressure ratio typically less than **1.11**). The primary purpose of a fan is to provide continuous airflow for ventilation, cooling, combustion air supply, or material handling in pneumatic conveying systems.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy (Think of a Water Bucket on a Rope):**
- Imagine you are pulling water from a deep well using a rope and bucket. The **bucket** is the pump's impeller, the **rope** is the shaft, and **your arm pulling the rope** is the electric motor. The faster you pull (higher RPM), the more water you lift per minute (higher flow rate). However, if the well is very deep (high head), you need to pull harder (more energy). This trade-off is exactly what pump performance curves illustrate.
- A **fan** is similar to a hand-held spinning blade. Spin it faster, and you push more air (higher flow). The harder the air pushes back (system resistance/static pressure), the more energy you need.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pumps and Fans Matter in Energy Management:**
> Both pumps and fans follow similar fluid dynamics principles. They consume a significant portion of industrial electricity. The **Affinity Laws (also called Pump Laws or Fan Laws)** govern how their performance changes with speed and impeller diameter, which is the foundation of energy savings strategies like **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** and **impeller trimming**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Units (Bold for Emphasis)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Description |
|-----------|--------|---------------|-------------|
| **Flow Rate** | $Q$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ or $\text{L/s}$ | Volume of fluid moved per unit time |
| **Head (Pressure)** | $H$ | $\text{m}$ of fluid column | Energy per unit weight of fluid |
| **Power** | $P$ | $\text{kW}$ or $\text{hp}$ | Rate of energy input |
| **Efficiency** | $\eta$ | Dimensionless (%) | Ratio of useful hydraulic power to input power |
| **Rotational Speed** | $N$ | $\text{rpm}$ (revolutions per minute) | Shaft rotational velocity |
| **Specific Speed** | $N_s$ | Dimensionless | Index of pump type classification |
| **NPSH** | $NPSH$ | $\text{m}$ | Net Positive Suction Head (cavitation margin) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes the application of the **Affinity Laws** for energy conservation in pumps and fans, the concept of **system curves vs. pump curves**, and the identification of energy-saving opportunities in fluid systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Pumps (Logical Breakdown)

- **Centrifugal Pumps (Dynamic / Kinetic Pumps):**
  - Use a rotating **impeller** to add velocity to the fluid.
  - Velocity is then converted to pressure in the volute or diffuser.
  - *Sub-types:* Radial flow, Mixed flow, Axial flow.
  - *Application:* High flow, low-to-medium head (water supply, HVAC, industrial process).

- **Positive Displacement Pumps:**
  - Trap a fixed volume of fluid and force (displace) it into the discharge pipe.
  - *Sub-types:* Reciprocating (piston, diaphragm), Rotary (gear, screw, vane).
  - *Application:* High head, low flow (oil pipelines, chemical dosing, high-pressure cleaning).

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Audit Focus:** Centrifugal pumps dominate industrial applications (>70% of installed base), so energy management strategies are predominantly aimed at them. However, positive displacement pumps are also significant in process industries.

### 2.2 Pump Performance Curve (The H-Q Curve)

Every pump has a unique **characteristic curve** that relates the head $H$ it can deliver to the flow rate $Q$ at a given speed. As flow increases, head decreases (typical for centrifugal pumps). The point where the pump curve intersects the **system curve** is the **Operating Point**.

- **Why does head decrease with flow?** Because internal losses (impeller friction, recirculation, leakage) increase with higher flow rates.
- **How does this help energy management?** If we can shift the operating point to a more efficient region, we save energy. This is done by:
  1. Trimming the impeller (reducing diameter).
  2. Reducing pump speed (using VFD).
  3. Modifying the system (closing valves throttles flow and is **inefficient**).

### 2.3 The Affinity Laws (Pump Laws / Fan Laws) — The Heart of Energy Savings

When the speed $N$ or impeller diameter $D$ of a centrifugal pump/fan is changed, the performance parameters scale according to the **Affinity Laws**:

- **Law 1 (Flow):** Flow rate is proportional to speed (or impeller diameter).
  $$Q \propto N \cdot D^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^3$$

- **Law 2 (Head):** Head is proportional to the square of speed (or diameter).
  $$H \propto N^2 \cdot D^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2$$

- **Law 3 (Power):** Power consumption is proportional to the cube of speed (or diameter).
  $$P \propto N^3 \cdot D^5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^5$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cube Law Significance (Most Critical for KTU):** Power consumed by a centrifugal pump or fan is proportional to the **cube of the speed**. This means that a mere **20% reduction in speed saves nearly 50% in power** ($0.8^3 = 0.512$). This is the single most important energy conservation principle in fluid machinery.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Equations)

| Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application |
|--------------|----------|-------|-------------|
| **Hydraulic Power (Water Power)** | $P_{\text{hyd}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | $\text{W}$ | Theoretical power delivered to fluid |
| **Hydraulic Power (Practical)** | $P_{\text{hyd}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot Q \cdot H}{102}$ (if $Q$ in L/s, $H$ in m) | $\text{kW}$ | Common engineering form |
| **Shaft Power / Brake Power** | $P_{\text{shaft}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{hyd}}}{\eta_{\text{pump}}}$ | $\text{kW}$ | Actual power input to pump shaft |
| **Electrical Input Power** | $P_{\text{elec}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{shaft}}}{\eta_{\text{motor}}}$ | $\text{kW}$ | Total power drawn from supply |
| **Overall Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{pump}} \times \eta_{\text{motor}}$ | Fraction | System-level efficiency |
| **Affinity Law — Flow** | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Dimensionless | Speed/diameter change effect |
| **Affinity Law — Head** | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Dimensionless | Speed/diameter change effect |
| **Affinity Law — Power** | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | Dimensionless | Speed/diameter change effect |
| **Specific Speed** | $N_s = \dfrac{N \cdot \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | $\text{rpm}$ | Pump type classification |
| **NPSH Available** | $NPSH_A = \dfrac{P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vapor}}}{\rho g} \pm H_{\text{static}} - H_{\text{friction}}$ | $\text{m}$ | Cavitation prevention check |
| **Fan Power (in terms of SP)** | $P_{\text{fan}} = \dfrac{Q \cdot \Delta P}{\eta_{\text{fan}}}$ | $\text{W}$ | $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $\Delta P$ in $\text{Pa}$ |
| **System Curve Equation** | $H_{\text{sys}} = H_{\text{static}} + K \cdot Q^2$ | $\text{m}$ | Total head required by system |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Affinity Laws are not just academic. Energy auditors use them to predict energy savings **before** installing a VFD. For example, if a pump runs at 80% speed for 60% of operating hours, the auditor can calculate the annual kWh savings using the cube law, which is a standard KTU practical exam question.

### 2.5 Energy-Saving Strategies in Pumps (Why and How)

- **Strategy 1 — Replacing Throttling with VFD:** Throttling a valve wastes energy as heat in the fluid. A VFD reduces motor speed, saving energy per the cube law.
- **Strategy 2 — Impeller Trimming:** If the pump is oversized, machining down the impeller diameter reduces the head curve, moving the operating point to the Best Efficiency Point (BEP).
- **Strategy 3 — Multiple Parallel Pumps:** Instead of one large pump, use multiple smaller pumps that can be switched on/off to match load. This keeps each pump near BEP.
- **Strategy 4 — Replacing Oversized Pumps:** Often, pumps are selected with excessive safety margins. A properly sized pump can save 20–40% energy.
- **Strategy 5 — Proper Maintenance:** Misalignment, worn impellers, and bearing failure can drop efficiency by 5–15%.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Hydraulic Power (Water Horsepower)

The **hydraulic power** delivered to a fluid is the rate at which potential energy is imparted to the fluid. We derive it from fundamental physics.

**Step 1: Define the mass flow rate.**

The mass of fluid passing per second is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$$

where $\rho$ is the fluid density in $\text{kg/m}^3$ and $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$.

**Step 2: Define the potential energy per unit mass.**

The potential energy gained by the fluid per unit mass, when raised to a head $H$, is:

$$\text{PE per unit mass} = g \cdot H$$

where $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$ is acceleration due to gravity and $H$ is in meters.

**Step 3: Multiply to get power.**

Power is the rate of energy transfer. Thus:

$$P_{\text{hyd}} = \dot{m} \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 4: Substitute $\dot{m}$.**

$$P_{\text{hyd}} = \rho \cdot Q \cdot g \cdot H$$

**Step 5: Simplify for practical use in kW.**

If $Q$ is in $\text{L/s}$, $H$ in meters, and $\rho = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ for water:

$$P_{\text{hyd}} = \frac{1000 \cdot Q \, (\text{m}^3/\text{s}) \cdot 9.81 \cdot H}{1000} \text{ kW}$$

Since $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{s} = Q \, (\text{L/s}) / 1000$:

$$P_{\text{hyd}} = \frac{Q \, (\text{L/s}) \cdot H}{102} \, \text{kW}$$

This is the **standard engineering form** used in KTU problem solving.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Explanation:** The factor **102** comes from $\dfrac{1000 \cdot 9.81}{1000 \cdot 100} = 9.81/100 \approx 1/102$. It converts the SI computation to kW when Q is in L/s and H is in meters of water column.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Affinity Laws (Dimensional / Similarity Approach)

The Affinity Laws are derived from **dimensional analysis** and **geometric similarity** of pump impellers. We present the rigorous derivation.

**Step 1: Define geometric similarity.**

If two pumps are geometrically similar (same shape, different size), all linear dimensions scale with impeller diameter $D$. Areas scale as $D^2$ and volumes as $D^3$.

**Step 2: Express velocity at the impeller tip.**

The impeller tip speed is:

$$u = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$$

where $N$ is in revolutions per second. So velocity $u \propto D \cdot N$.

**Step 3: Apply Euler's Turbomachinery Equation (without losses).**

Euler's head equation gives the theoretical head imparted by the impeller as proportional to the product of tangential velocity and change in tangential velocity of fluid:

$$H \propto u^2 \propto (D \cdot N)^2 \propto D^2 \cdot N^2$$

This proves the **Head Affinity Law.**

**Step 4: Derive the flow rate relationship.**

Flow rate equals tip velocity times the cross-sectional area at the impeller outlet:

$$Q \propto u \cdot A \propto (D \cdot N) \cdot D^2 \propto D^3 \cdot N$$

This proves the **Flow Affinity Law.**

**Step 5: Derive the power relationship.**

Power = Force $\times$ Velocity. Force is proportional to $\rho \cdot A \cdot u^2$ (dynamic pressure), and velocity is $u$:

$$P \propto (\rho \cdot D^2 \cdot u^2) \cdot u \propto D^2 \cdot u^3 \propto D^2 \cdot (D \cdot N)^3 \propto D^5 \cdot N^3$$

This proves the **Power Affinity Law.**

**Summary of the three Affinity Laws in canonical form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} &= \frac{N_1}{N_2} \cdot \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^3 \\[6pt]
\frac{H_1}{H_2} &= \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 \\[6pt]
\frac{P_1}{P_2} &= \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3 \cdot \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^5
\end{aligned}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:** A centrifugal pump delivers $Q_1 = 100 \, \text{L/s}$ of water at a head of $H_1 = 30 \, \text{m}$ while running at $N_1 = 1450 \, \text{rpm}$. The pump consumes $P_1 = 45 \, \text{kW}$ of shaft power. The pump speed is reduced to $N_2 = 1160 \, \text{rpm}$ to meet a reduced demand. Assuming the impeller diameter is unchanged, calculate:

(a) The new flow rate $Q_2$, new head $H_2$, and new power $P_2$. (7 marks)

(b) The annual energy savings in kWh and the annual cost savings in INR if the pump operates 6000 hours/year and electricity costs ₹8/kWh. (7 marks)

---

**Solution (a) — Using the Affinity Laws:**

Given:
- $N_1 = 1450 \, \text{rpm}$, $N_2 = 1160 \, \text{rpm}$
- $D_1 = D_2$ (unchanged)
- $Q_1 = 100 \, \text{L/s}$, $H_1 = 30 \, \text{m}$, $P_1 = 45 \, \text{kW}$

**Step 1: Calculate the speed ratio.**

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1160}{1450} = 0.80$$

**Step 2: Apply the Flow Affinity Law.**

$$Q_2 = Q_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 100 \cdot 0.80 = 80 \, \text{L/s}$$

**Step 3: Apply the Head Affinity Law.**

$$H_2 = H_1 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 30 \cdot (0.80)^2 = 30 \cdot 0.64 = 19.2 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Apply the Power Affinity Law.**

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = 45 \cdot (0.80)^3 = 45 \cdot 0.512 = 23.04 \, \text{kW}$$

**Answers (a):** $Q_2 = 80 \, \text{L/s}$, $H_2 = 19.2 \, \text{m}$, $P_2 = 23.04 \, \text{kW}$

---

**Solution (b) — Energy and Cost Savings:**

**Step 1: Power reduction per hour.**

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = 45 - 23.04 = 21.96 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 2: Annual energy saved.**

$$\Delta E = \Delta P \times \text{Operating Hours} = 21.96 \times 6000 = 131{,}760 \, \text{kWh/year}$$

**Step 3: Annual monetary savings.**

$$\text{Savings} = \Delta E \times \text{Tariff} = 131{,}760 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 10{,}54{,}080$$

**Answer (b):** Energy saved = **1,31,760 kWh/year**, Cost savings = **₹ 10,54,080 per year**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Always show the cube calculation explicitly: $0.8^3 = 0.512$. A 20% speed reduction = 48.8% power reduction. Examiners reward the proper invocation of each Affinity Law with its proportional exponent.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Throttling vs. VFD Comparison

| Parameter | Throttling (Valve) | VFD (Speed Control) | Verdict |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|---------| 
| **Energy Wasted** | Yes (heat loss in fluid) | No | VFD wins |
| **Initial Cost** | Low (existing valve) | High (₹50K–₹5L) | Throttling wins |
| **Payback Period** | None | 6–24 months | VFD wins long-term |
| **Control Smoothness** | Poor (step changes) | Excellent (continuous) | VFD wins |
| **Maintenance** | Low | Moderate (electronics) | Throttling wins |
| **Power Reduction** | Sub-linear (Q only) | Cubic (P $\propto$ N³) | VFD dramatically better |
