# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Heat Exchanger (HX):** A static device engineered to effect the transfer of thermal energy between two or more fluids that are at different temperatures, in which the fluids are separated by a solid wall (or by direct contact in specific cases), without any transfer of work or significant change in the composition of the participating streams.

In the framework of **Energy Management and Conservation (PESFT414)**, heat exchangers are classified as *passive energy conservation equipment*. They do not consume electrical or mechanical work to function; instead, they recover waste heat from one process stream and deliver it to another, thereby reducing the gross heating and cooling duty imposed on primary utilities such as boilers, chillers, and furnaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 4, heat exchangers are studied as the principal *heat recovery* hardware used in industries. The emphasis is on **selection, sizing, and evaluation** of existing units to identify energy-saving potential during an Energy Audit.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are drinking a scalding hot cup of tea (≈ 90 °C) in a Kerala monsoon evening, and your friend hands you a glass of chilled lime water (≈ 10 °C). If you pour both liquids into a single flask with a thermally conductive metal wall separating the two compartments:

- The tea **cools down** (releases heat).
- The lime water **warms up** (absorbs heat).
- The metal wall is the *separating solid*.
- The flask itself is the *heat exchanger*.

> [!NOTE]
> The hot stream is symbolically termed the **"Hot Fluid"** (subscript $h$) and the cold stream is the **"Cold Fluid"** (subscript $c$). The rate at which heat moves from $h$ to $c$ is governed by the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** and quantified by the conduction properties of the wall and the convection characteristics of the two flowing fluids.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical SI Unit | KTU Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Heat Duty | $\dot{Q}$ | **Watt (W)** or **kW** | kW |
| Heat Transfer Area | $A$ | **m²** | m² |
| Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient | $U$ | **W/(m²·K)** | W/(m²·K) |
| Log Mean Temperature Difference | $\Delta T_{lm}$ or LMTD | **Kelvin (K)** | K or °C |
| Effectiveness | $\varepsilon$ | dimensionless | — |
| Number of Transfer Units | NTU | dimensionless | — |
| Heat Capacity Rate | $C$ | **W/K** | W/K |
| Fouling Resistance | $R_f$ | **(m²·K)/W** | (m²·K)/W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Temperature:** All KTU numerical problems use $T$ in **°C** for *temperature values* but in **Kelvin (K)** for *temperature differences*. A 1 °C interval is identical to a 1 K interval, so the difference units are interchangeable, but the magnitude notation should be consistent with the question.

---

## 1.4 Engineering Classification of Heat Exchangers

Heat exchangers are broadly classified based on **flow arrangement**, **construction**, and **compactness**.

### A. Classification by Flow Arrangement

1. **Parallel Flow (Co-current):** Both hot and cold fluids enter at the same end and move in the same direction.
2. **Counter Flow (Counter-current):** Fluids enter at opposite ends and move in opposite directions — *most thermally efficient configuration*.
3. **Cross Flow:** Fluids move perpendicular to each other (typical in air-cooled condensers).
4. **Shell and Tube Multi-pass:** Combination where one fluid makes several passes through tubes while the other makes a single shell-side pass.

### B. Classification by Construction

| Type | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Shell-and-Tube HX** | Bundle of tubes enclosed in a cylindrical shell | Power plants, refineries, chemical plants |
| **Plate HX** | Multiple corrugated plates gasketed together | Dairy, HVAC, district cooling |
| **Finned-Tube HX** | Tubes with external fins to enhance surface area | Air-side heating/cooling (AC coils) |
| **Air-Cooled HX** | Forced/induced draft fans over finned tubes | Refineries, remote sites without water |
| **Double Pipe HX** | One pipe inside another, simplest geometry | Lab scale, high-pressure duties |
| **Spiral / Plate-Fin HX** | Compact units, high surface density | Cryogenics, aerospace, gas processing |

> [!TIP]
> **Energy Audit Insight:** During a walk-through audit, the most common recovery devices encountered are *shell-and-tube* (in process plants) and *plate* (in HVAC and dairy). Recognition speed earns easy audit marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Heat Transfer Rate Equation — The Master Equation

The fundamental governing equation for any heat exchanger, regardless of geometry, is:

$$\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$$

Where:
- $\dot{Q}$ = heat transfer rate in **W (or kW)**
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient in **W/(m²·K)**
- $A$ = effective heat transfer surface area in **m²**
- $\Delta T_m$ = mean temperature difference in **K (or °C)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation is the *starting point* for every heat-exchanger problem in KTU exams. You will either be given $\dot{Q}$ and asked to find $A$ (sizing), or given $A$ and asked to find $\dot{Q}$ (rating).

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Logic — Why LMTD is Required

For a **parallel-flow** or **counter-flow** exchanger, the temperature difference between the two fluids varies along the length $L$ of the exchanger. We cannot simply use $(\Delta T_{in} + \Delta T_{out})/2$ because the variation is **exponential**, not linear.

### The "Why" Behind the LMTD Derivation

Consider a differential element of length $dL$ in a heat exchanger. Over this element:
- Hot fluid temperature drops by $dT_h$.
- Cold fluid temperature rises by $dT_c$.
- Heat transferred: $d\dot{Q} = U \cdot P \cdot dL \cdot (T_h - T_c)$, where $P$ is the perimeter of the wall.

By applying an **energy balance** (heat lost by hot = heat gained by cold) and integrating from inlet to outlet, the resulting average temperature difference is the **Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)**.

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

Where:
- $\Delta T_1$ = temperature difference at one end of the exchanger
- $\Delta T_2$ = temperature difference at the other end

> [!NOTE]
> **LMTD vs AMTD:** The LMTD is always *less than or equal to* the Arithmetic Mean Temperature Difference (AMTD). The two values coincide only when $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$, which is a *constant temperature difference* scenario (e.g., one fluid undergoing phase change).

---

## 2.3 Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U) and Thermal Resistance Network

The overall coefficient $U$ incorporates **all** thermal resistances between the two bulk fluids. By direct analogy to electrical resistances in series:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{\delta}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$$

Where:
- $h_i$ = inside convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K)
- $h_o$ = outside convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²K)
- $\delta$ = wall thickness (m)
- $k_w$ = thermal conductivity of wall material (W/mK)
- $R_{f,i}, R_{f,o}$ = inside and outside fouling resistances (m²K/W)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $U$-values and treat them as constants. In reality, $U$ *decreases over time* due to fouling (scaling, biofilm, coke formation). The *design* $U$ is the clean value; the *operating* $U$ must include fouling factors. This directly affects energy audit calculations of **true heat recovery potential vs actual**.

---

## 2.4 The Correction Factor $F$ for Multi-Pass Exchangers

For a **single-pass** counter-flow or parallel-flow exchanger, the LMTD is directly usable. But for **shell-and-tube multi-pass** or **cross-flow** units, the flow pattern is neither pure co-current nor pure counter-current. KTU uses the **correction factor** approach:

$$\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,\,cf}$$

Where $\Delta T_{lm,cf}$ is the LMTD calculated as if the exchanger were counter-flow, and $F$ is the correction factor, a function of two dimensionless parameters:

- $P = \dfrac{T_{h,o} - T_{h,i}}{T_{c,i} - T_{h,i}}$
- $R = \dfrac{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}}{T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}}$

The value of $F$ is read from **standard charts** (e.g., Kays & London, Perry's Handbook) or from empirical correlation tables for the specific configuration. **Always $0 \leq F \leq 1$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** If $F < 0.75$, redesign the exchanger. A low $F$ indicates poor thermal effectiveness and is uneconomical.

---

## 2.5 The Effectiveness–NTU Method (ε–NTU)

When **outlet temperatures are unknown** but **inlet temperatures, $U$, $A$, and mass flow rates are known**, the LMTD method becomes iterative and cumbersome. The **ε–NTU method** is preferred.

### Definitions

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{Actual Heat Transfer}}{\text{Maximum Possible Heat Transfer}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{actual}}{\dot{Q}_{max}}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{max} = C_{min} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})$$

$$C_{min} = \min(C_h, C_c) \quad ; \quad C_{max} = \max(C_h, C_c)$$

$$C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} \quad \text{(heat capacity ratio)}$$

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$$

### Standard ε–NTU Correlations

| Configuration | Effectiveness Equation |
|---|---|
| **Parallel Flow** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-\text{NTU}(1 + C_r)\right]}{1 + C_r}$ |
| **Counter Flow** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \cdot \exp\!\left[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)\right]}$ |
| **Shell & Tube (1 shell, 2, 4, … tube passes)** | $\varepsilon_1 = 2\left\{\left(1 + C_r\right) + \sqrt{1 + C_r^2} \cdot \dfrac{1 + e^{-\text{NTU}\sqrt{1+C_r^2}}}{1 - e^{-\text{NTU}\sqrt{1+C_r^2}}}\right\}^{-1}$ |
| **Cross Flow (both unmixed)** | $\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\!\left[\dfrac{\text{NTU}^{0.22}}{C_r}\left(\exp\!\left(-C_r \cdot \text{NTU}^{0.78}\right) - 1\right)\right]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The counter-flow exchanger **always yields the highest effectiveness** for a given NTU and $C_r$. This is the thermodynamic optimum.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | Sensible heat duty | Energy balance on each stream |
| 2 | $\dot{Q} = U \, A \, \Delta T_m$ | Master rate equation | Sizing / Rating |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean $\Delta T$ | When no phase change |
| 4 | $\Delta T_m = F \cdot \Delta T_{lm,cf}$ | Corrected mean $\Delta T$ | Shell-and-tube, cross flow |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{\delta}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | Overall coefficient | With fouling |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{\dot{Q}_{max}} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{C_{min}(T_{h,i}-T_{c,i})}$ | Effectiveness | ε–NTU method |
| 7 | $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U A}{C_{min}}$ | Transfer units | ε–NTU method |
| 8 | $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$ | Capacity ratio | ε–NTU method |
| 9 | $R_{f,\,total} = R_{f,\,design} - R_{f,\,clean}$ | Excess fouling resistance | Energy audit |
| 10 | $\eta_{HX} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{recovered}}{\dot{Q}_{max,\,theoretical}}$ | Heat recovery efficiency | Audit reporting |

> [!TIP]
> The symbol $\vert$ for absolute value is replaced by $\ln(\cdot)$ in the LMTD expression. **Never write the LMTD numerator/denominator as a single bar — always use the explicit $\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2$ and $\ln$ structure** to avoid the KTU table-parsing rule violation.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Energy Conservation Utility

In an industrial **Energy Audit (ISO 50002)** context, heat exchangers are evaluated on three principal metrics:

1. **Heat Recovery Quantification:** The $\dot{Q}$ actually transferred vs. $\dot{Q}$ the unit was designed for.
2. **Excess Fouling Penalty:** $R_{f,\,actual} - R_{f,\,design}$ directly degrades $U$, which in turn forces the utility (steam, chiller) to supply more energy.
3. **Pinch Analysis Position:** A heat exchanger that crosses the **process pinch** is a *thermodynamic violation* and reduces the overall site's Minimum Energy Requirement (MER).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

### Statement
For a single-pass heat exchanger with a constant overall heat transfer coefficient $U$ and no phase change, derive the expression for the mean temperature difference.

### Step 1 — Energy Balance Over a Differential Element

Consider a small length $dL$ of the exchanger at position $x$. Let $T_h(x)$ and $T_c(x)$ be the local bulk temperatures of the hot and cold fluids.

The local heat flux is proportional to the local temperature difference:

$$d\dot{Q} = U \, dA \cdot \left[T_h(x) - T_c(x)\right] = U \, P \, dL \cdot \left[T_h - T_c\right]$$

### Step 2 — Express $d\dot{Q}$ Using Each Stream's Energy Balance

For the hot fluid (losing heat):

$$d\dot{Q} = -\dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h} \, dT_h$$

For the cold fluid (gaining heat):

$$d\dot{Q} = +\dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, dT_c$$

### Step 3 — Define Heat Capacity Rates

Let $C_h = \dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h}$ and $C_c = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c}$. Then:

$$dT_h = -\frac{d\dot{Q}}{C_h} \quad ; \quad dT_c = +\frac{d\dot{Q}}{C_c}$$

### Step 4 — Differential of the Local Temperature Difference

Let $\theta = T_h - T_c$. Then:

$$d\theta = dT_h - dT_c = -\frac{d\dot{Q}}{C_h} - \frac{d\dot{Q}}{C_c} = -d\dot{Q} \left(\frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}\right)$$

Define the combined inverse capacity:

$$\frac{1}{C^*} = \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}$$

So $d\theta = -\dfrac{d\dot{Q}}{C^*}$.

### Step 5 — Substitute $d\dot{Q} = U \, dA \cdot \theta$

$$d\theta = -\frac{U \, dA \cdot \theta}{C^*}$$

Separate variables:

$$\frac{d\theta}{\theta} = -\frac{U}{C^*} \, dA$$

### Step 6 — Integrate from Inlet ($\theta_1$, $A=0$) to Outlet ($\theta_2$, $A=A_{total}$)

$$\int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} \frac{d\theta}{\theta} = -\frac{U}{C^*} \int_0^{A} dA$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\theta_2}{\theta_1}\right) = -\frac{U A}{C^*}$$

### Step 7 — Total Heat Transferred

From the overall energy balance, $\dot{Q} = C_h (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) = C_c (T_{c,o} - T_{c,i})$.

The total transferred heat is also $\dot{Q} = U A \cdot \Delta T_m$, so:

$$\Delta T_m = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U A} = \frac{\dot{Q} \cdot \left[\ln(\theta_1/\theta_2)\right]^{-1} \cdot C^*}{1} = \frac{C^*}{\dot{Q}} \cdot \frac{\theta_1 - \theta_2}{\ln(\theta_1/\theta_2)}$$

But by the energy balance identity, $\dot{Q} = C^* (\theta_1 - \theta_2)$. Substituting:

$$\Delta T_m = \frac{C^*(\theta_1 - \theta_2)}{\dot{Q}} \cdot \frac{\theta_1 - \theta_2}{\ln(\theta_1/\theta_2)} = \frac{(\theta_1 - \theta_2)^2}{\dot{Q}\,\ln(\theta_1/\theta_2)}$$

Substituting $\dot{Q} = C^*(\theta_1 - \theta_2)$:

$$\Delta T_m = \frac{\theta_1 - \theta_2}{\ln(\theta_1/\theta_2)} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** This is the **LMTD**, where $\theta_1 = \Delta T_1$ is the inlet-end temperature difference and $\theta_2 = \Delta T_2$ is the outlet-end temperature difference.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style Sizing Problem

### Problem Statement
A **counter-flow shell-and-tube heat exchanger** is used to condense a hydrocarbon vapor. Hot fluid enters at $T_{h,i} = 180\,^\circ\text{C}$ and leaves at $T_{h,o} = 140\,^\circ\text{C}$. Cooling water enters at $T_{c,i} = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$ and leaves at $T_{c,o} = 90\,^\circ\text{C}$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 850\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ and the heat duty is $\dot{Q} = 350\,\text{kW}$.

Find:
1. The LMTD.
2. The required heat transfer area $A$.

### Solution — Step 1: Identify $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$

For **counter-flow** arrangement:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 180 - 90 = 90\,^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 140 - 30 = 110\,^\circ\text{C}$$

### Solution — Step 2: Apply the LMTD Formula

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)} = \frac{90 - 110}{\ln(90/110)} = \frac{-20}{\ln(0.8182)} = \frac{-20}{-0.2007}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} \approx 99.65\,^\circ\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The denominator $\ln(0.8182)$ is negative, and so is the numerator. The two negatives cancel to give a positive LMTD. This is the standard sign convention for counter-flow when $T_{c,o} > T_{h,o}$ is not true.

### Solution — Step 3: Calculate the Required Area

From $\dot{Q} = U A \Delta T_{lm}$:

$$A = \frac{\dot{Q}}{U \, \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{350 \times 10^3\,\text{W}}{850\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K} \times 99.65\,\text{K}}$$

$$A = \frac{350{,}000}{84{,}702.5} \approx 4.13\,\text{m}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $A \approx 4.13\,\text{m}^2$. This is the minimum bare area; the actual exchanger must be oversized by 10–15% as a design safety margin, and additional area must be added to account for fouling.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Effectiveness–NTU Method

### Problem Statement
A **gas-to-gas cross-flow heat exchanger** (both streams unmixed) has $U = 120\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ and $A = 25\,\text{m}^2$. Hot air enters at $T_{h,i} = 250\,^\circ\text{C}$ with $\dot{m}_h = 2.0\,\text{kg/s}$ and $c_{p,h} = 1010\,\text{J/kgK}$. Cold air enters at $T_{c,i} = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$ with $\dot{m}_c = 1.5\,\text{kg/s}$ and $c_{p,c} = 1005\,\text{J/kgK}$.

Determine the **outlet temperatures** of both streams.

### Solution — Step 1: Compute Heat Capacity Rates

$$C_h = \dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h} = 2.0 \times 1010 = 2020\,\text{W/K}$$

$$C_c = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} = 1.5 \times 1005 = 1507.5\,\text{W/K}$$

$$C_{min} = 1507.5\,\text{W/K} \quad ; \quad C_{max} = 2020\,\text{W/K}$$

$$C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{1507.5}{2020} \approx 0.7463$$

### Solution — Step 2: Compute NTU

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{U A}{C_{min}} = \frac{120 \times 25}{1507.5} = \frac{3000}{1507.5} \approx 1.990$$

### Solution — Step 3: Use the Cross-Flow (Both Unmixed) Correlation

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \exp\!\left[\frac{\text{NTU}^{0.22}}{C_r}\left(\exp(-C_r\,\text{NTU}^{0.78}) - 1\right)\right]$$

Compute the auxiliary terms:

$$\text{NTU}^{0.22} = 1.990^{0.22} \approx 1.160$$

$$\text{NTU}^{0.78} = 1.990^{0.78} \approx 1.715$$

$$C_r \cdot \text{NTU}^{0.78} = 0.7463 \times 1.715 \approx 1.280$$

$$\exp(-1.280) \approx 0.2782$$

$$\frac{\text{NTU}^{0.22}}{C_r} = \frac{1.160}{0.7463} \approx 1.555$$

$$\text{Inner bracket: } 0.2782 - 1 = -0.7218$$

$$1.555 \times (-0.7218) = -1.1225$$

$$\exp(-1.1225) \approx 0.3253$$

$$\varepsilon = 1 - 0.3253 = 0.6747 \approx 0.675$$

### Solution — Step 4: Compute Heat Transfer and Outlet Temperatures

$$\dot{Q} = \varepsilon \, C_{min} \, (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = 0.675 \times 1507.5 \times (250 - 30)$$

$$\dot{Q} = 0.675 \times 1507.5 \times 220 \approx 223{,}866\,\text{W} \approx 223.9\,\text{kW}$$

$$T_{h,o} = T_{h,i} - \frac{\dot{Q}}{C_h} = 250 - \frac{223{,}866}{2020} \approx 250 - 110.8 = 139.2\,^\circ\text{C}$$

$$T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \frac{\dot{Q}}{C_c} = 30 + \frac{223{,}866}{1507.5} \approx 30 + 148.5 = 178.5\,^\circ\text{C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $T_{h,o} \approx 139.2\,^\circ\text{C}$ and $T_{c,o} \approx 178.5\,^\circ\text{C}$. Sanity check: $T_{c,o} < T_{h,i}$, confirming the Second Law of Thermodynamics is satisfied.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Functional Architecture of a Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Hot Fluid Inlet Nozzle"] --> B["Front Header Channel"]
    B --> C["Tube Pass 1"]
    C --> D["Intermediate Channel Baffle"]
    D --> E["Tube Pass 2"]
    E --> F["Rear Header Channel"]
    F --> G["Hot Fluid Outlet Nozzle"]

    H["Cold Fluid Inlet Nozzle"] --> I["Shell Side Inlet"]
    I --> J["Shell Side Baffles Region"]
    J --> K["Shell Side Outlet"]
    K --> L["Cold Fluid Outlet Nozzle"]

    J -. heat transfer across tube wall .-> C
    J -. heat transfer across tube wall .-> E

    M["Tube Wall Material Carbon Steel or SS"] -. separates .-> A
    N["Baffle Plates Segmentally Cut 25 percent"] -. directs .-> I
    O["Tubes Inner Dia d_i Outer Dia d_o"] -. contains .-> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted arrows represent the **transverse heat flux** crossing the tube wall. The numbered passes (1, 2) reflect the standard 1-shell-2-tube-pass (1-2) configuration. This is the most common KTU examination configuration.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — ε–NTU Decision Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start Given m_dot cp and inlet T for both streams"] --> S2["Calculate C_h and C_c"]
    S2 --> S3["Identify C_min and C_max"]
    S3 --> S4["Compute C_r = C_min / C_max"]
    S4 --> S5["Compute NTU = U A / C_min"]
    S5 --> S6{"Flow Configuration Known?"}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7["Select correct eps NTU correlation"]
    S6 -- No --> S8["Assume counter flow best case"]
    S7 --> S9["Solve for eps"]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10["Q_dot = eps C_min T_h_i minus T_c_i"]
    S10 --> S11["T_h_o = T_h_i minus Q_dot divided by C_h"]
    S10 --> S12["T_c_o = T_c_i plus Q_dot divided by C_c"]
    S11 --> S13["End Report all temperatures and effectiveness"]
    S12 --> S13
```

> [!TIP]
> This flow chart is the standard **algorithm expected in KTU 14-mark problems** when the LMTD method cannot be applied directly. Memorize the path from $S_1$ to $S_{13}$.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Energy Audit Decision Tree for Existing HX

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Audit Visit Encounter Heat Exchanger"] --> A1{"Is HX Isolatable and Measurable?"}
    A1 -- Yes --> A2["Measure T_h_i T_h_o T_c_i T_c_o m_dot_h m_dot_c"]
    A1 -- No --> A3["Estimate from design data and operating logs"]
    A2 --> A4["Compute Actual Q_dot_actual"]
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> A5["Compute Q_dot_design from spec sheet"]
    A5 --> A6{"Q_dot_actual < 0.8 times Q_dot_design?"}
    A6 -- Yes --> A7["Recommend Cleaning or Descaling"]
    A6 -- No --> A8["Compute Excess Air or Heat Loss Penalty"]
    A7 --> A9["Estimate Payback Period from Fuel Cost Saving"]
    A8 --> A10["Proceed to Pinch Analysis for System Level Optimization"]
    A9 --> A11["Issue Recommendation in Audit Report"]
    A10 --> A11
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Audit Reality Check:** An HX that is operating at $< 80\%$ of its design heat duty is typically a candidate for chemical cleaning or mechanical descaling. The payback period in most Indian process plants is between **2 and 8 months** for cleaning alone, making it a high-priority recommendation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a heat exchanger and list any four types based on construction.**

**Model Answer:**
A heat exchanger is a device that transfers heat between two or more fluids at different temperatures separated by a solid wall, without mixing or external work input.

Four types based on construction:
1. **Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger** — most common in process industries.
2. **Plate Heat Exchanger** — uses gasketed corrugated plates, common in HVAC and dairy.
3. **Finned-Tube Heat Exchanger** — extended surface for gas-side applications.
4. **Double-Pipe Heat Exchanger** — concentric pipe arrangement, used for high-pressure or lab duties.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define effectiveness of a heat exchanger. What is the maximum possible effectiveness for a counter-flow heat exchanger?**

**Model Answer:**
Effectiveness is the ratio of actual heat transferred to the maximum possible heat transfer:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\dot{Q}_{actual}}{\dot{Q}_{max}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{actual}}{C_{min}\,(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})}$$

For a counter-flow heat exchanger, the maximum possible effectiveness approaches **unity ($\varepsilon \to 1$)** when the heat capacity ratio $C_r = 1$ and NTU $\to \infty$, i.e., the cold fluid can in principle reach the hot fluid inlet temperature. The theoretical upper bound is 1.0.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice: 14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mark-loss zones:
> 1. **Sign errors** in $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ when computing LMTD (always write both values explicitly).
> 2. **Forgetting the correction factor $F$** in shell-and-tube problems — KTU strictly deducts 1 mark per missing factor.
> 3. **Mixing up $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$** in ε–NTU problems — the effectiveness equation uses $C_{min}$ in the denominator of NTU.
> 4. **Unit mismatch** between W and kW — always state unit conversions clearly in the solution.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

In a **counter-flow shell-and-tube heat exchanger**, oil is cooled from $T_{h,i} = 150\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $T_{h,o} = 80\,^\circ\text{C}$ by water entering at $T_{c,i} = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$ and leaving at $T_{c,o} = 65\,^\circ\text{C}$. The overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 1200\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ and the heat transfer area is $A = 3.5\,\text{m}^2$.

**Calculate:**
**(a)** (7 marks) The LMTD and the actual rate of heat transfer.
**(b)** (7 marks) The effectiveness and the NTU. Comment on the result.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Identify the terminal temperature differences for counter-flow.

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} = 150 - 65 = 85\,^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} = 80 - 25 = 55\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the LMTD formula.

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{85 - 55}{\ln(85/55)} = \frac{30}{\ln(1.5455)} = \frac{30}{0.4353}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} \approx 68.92\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the heat transfer rate.

$$\dot{Q} = U \, A \, \Delta T_{lm} = 1200 \times 3.5 \times 68.92$$

$$\dot{Q} = 4200 \times 68.92 \approx 289{,}464\,\text{W} \approx 289.5\,\text{kW}$$

> **Answer (a):** LMTD $\approx 68.92\,^\circ\text{C}$; $\dot{Q} \approx 289.5\,\text{kW}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Compute the capacity rates.

$$C_h = \frac{\dot{Q}}{T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}} = \frac{289{,}464}{150 - 80} = \frac{289{,}464}{70} \approx 4135.2\,\text{W/K}$$

$$C_c = \frac{\dot{Q}}{T_{c,o} - T_{c,i}} = \frac{289{,}464}{65 - 25} = \frac{289{,}464}{40} \approx 7236.6\,\text{W/K}$$

**Step 2:** Identify $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$.

$$C_{min} = 4135.2\,\text{W/K} \quad ; \quad C_{max} = 7236.6\,\text{W/K}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the capacity ratio.

$$C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{4135.2}{7236.6} \approx 0.5714$$

**Step 4:** Compute the maximum possible heat transfer.

$$\dot{Q}_{max} = C_{min} \, (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = 4135.2 \times (150 - 25) = 4135.2 \times 125 = 516{,}900\,\text{W}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the effectiveness.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\dot{Q}_{actual}}{\dot{Q}_{max}} = \frac{289{,}464}{516{,}900} \approx 0.560$$

**Step 6:** Compute the NTU.

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{U A}{C_{min}} = \frac{1200 \times 3.5}{4135.2} = \frac{4200}{4135.2} \approx 1.016$$

**Comment:** An effectiveness of about 56% indicates that the exchanger is recovering a moderate fraction of the available heat. For a counter-flow unit, this is below typical best-in-class performance (which would be $> 70\%$), suggesting the unit may be undersized, heavily fouled, or have a poor $C_r$ matching.

> **Answer (b):** $\varepsilon \approx 0.560$; NTU $\approx 1.016$.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**OR (Alternative Choice)**

A **parallel-flow double-pipe heat exchanger** is used to heat a chemical stream. Hot water enters at $T_{h,i} = 95\,^\circ\text{C}$ with $\dot{m}_h = 0.8\,\text{kg/s}$ and $c_{p,h} = 4180\,\text{J/kgK}$. Cold chemical enters at $T_{c,i} = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$ with $\dot{m}_c = 1.2\,\text{kg/s}$ and $c_{p,c} = 2400\,\text{J/kgK}$. The heat transfer area is $A = 1.0\,\text{m}^2$ and $U = 950\,\text{W/m}^2\text{K}$.

**Calculate:**
**(a)** (7 marks) The effectiveness and the outlet temperatures of both fluids.
**(b)** (7 marks) The LMTD and the actual heat duty. Verify the results are consistent.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Compute the heat capacity rates.

$$C_h = 0.8 \times 4180 = 3344\,\text{W/K}$$

$$C_c = 1.2 \times 2400 = 2880\,\text{W/K}$$

**Step 2:** Identify $C_{min}$ and $C_{max}$, and compute $C_r$.

$$C_{min} = 2880\,\text{W/K} \quad ; \quad C_{max} = 3344\,\text{W/K}$$

$$C_r = \frac{C_{min}}{C_{max}} = \frac{2880}{3344} \approx 0.8612$$

**Step 3:** Compute NTU.

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{U A}{C_{min}} = \frac{950 \times 1.0}{2880} = \frac{950}{2880} \approx 0.3299$$

**Step 4:** Apply the parallel-flow effectiveness correlation.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 + C_r)]}{1 + C_r}$$

Compute the exponent term:

$$-\text{NTU}(1 + C_r) = -0.3299 \times (1 + 0.8612) = -0.3299 \times 1.8612 \approx -0.6141$$

$$\exp(-0.6141) \approx 0.5410$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - 0.5410}{1 + 0.8612} = \frac{0.4590}{1.8612} \approx 0.2466$$

**Step 5:** Compute the heat duty and outlet temperatures.

$$\dot{Q} = \varepsilon \, C_{min} \, (T_{h,i} - T_{c,i}) = 0.2466 \times 2880 \times (95 - 30) = 0.2466 \times 2880 \times 65$$

$$\dot{Q} \approx 46{,}167\,\text{W} \approx 46.17\,\text{kW}$$

$$T_{h,o} = T_{h,i} - \frac{\dot{Q}}{C_h} = 95 - \frac{46{,}167}{3344} \approx 95 - 13.81 = 81.19\,^\circ\text{C}$$

$$T_{c,o} = T_{c,i} + \frac{\dot{Q}}{C_c} = 30 + \frac{46{,}167}{2880} \approx 30 + 16.03 = 46.03\,^\circ\text{C}$$

> **Answer (a):** $\varepsilon \approx 0.247$; $T_{h,o} \approx 81.19\,^\circ\text{C}$; $T_{c,o} \approx 46.03\,^\circ\text{C}$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Identify the terminal temperature differences for parallel flow.

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,i} - T_{c,i} = 95 - 30 = 65\,^\circ\text{C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,o} - T_{c,o} = 81.19 - 46.03 = 35.16\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the LMTD formula.

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{65 - 35.16}{\ln(65/35.16)} = \frac{29.84}{\ln(1.8490)} = \frac{29.84}{0.6146}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} \approx 48.55\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the heat duty using the master rate equation.

$$\dot{Q}_{LMTD} = U \, A \, \Delta T_{lm} = 950 \times 1.0 \times 48.55 = 46{,}122.5\,\text{W} \approx 46.12\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 4:** Verify consistency.

The LMTD-derived $\dot{Q} \approx 46.12\,\text{kW}$ matches the ε–NTU-derived $\dot{Q} \approx 46.17\,\text{kW}$ within rounding tolerance ($\approx 0.1\%$ error), confirming the calculation is consistent.

> **Answer (b):** LMTD $\approx 48.55\,^\circ\text{C}$; $\dot{Q} \approx 46.12\,\text{kW}$. The two methods are consistent.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- [x] **Definition:** A heat exchanger is a static device transferring heat between two or more fluids separated by a wall.
- [x] **Master Equation:** $\dot{Q} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_m$ — the foundation of every problem.
- [x] **LMTD Formula:** $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ — used when phase change is absent.
- [x] **Correction Factor $F$:** Multi-pass and cross-flow exchangers require $F \leq 1$ to correct the counter-flow LMTD.
- [x] **Effectiveness:** $\varepsilon = \dot{Q}_{actual} / \dot{Q}_{max} = \dot{Q} / [C_{min}(T_{h,i} - T_{c,i})]$ — bounded by $[0, 1]$.
- [x] **NTU Definition:** $\text{NTU} = U A / C_{min}$ — dimensionless measure of exchanger "size".
- [x] **Capacity Ratio:** $C_r = C_{min} / C_{max} \in (0, 1]$ — drives effectiveness for a given NTU.
- [x] **Counter-Flow is Best:** Always yields the highest effectiveness for a given $U$, $A$, and $C_r$.
- [x] **Fouling Penalty:** $R_f$ accumulates over time and degrades $U$; periodic cleaning restores capacity.
- [x] **Audit Signature:** $Q_{actual} < 0.8 \cdot Q_{design}$ is the standard trigger to recommend HX cleaning.
- [x] **Unit Discipline:** Heat duty in W or kW; area in m²; $U$ in W/(m²·K); temperatures in °C, differences in K.
- [x] **Sign Discipline:** In counter-flow, $\Delta T_1$ is at the *hot inlet/cold outlet* end and $\Delta T_2$ at the *hot outlet/cold inlet* end. Always label.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When given a problem with unknown outlet temperatures, **always use the ε–NTU method first**, not LMTD. The LMTD method is iterative and risk-prone in such cases. Save LMTD for direct rating/sizing problems where all four terminal temperatures are given.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lighting System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Lighting System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Lighting System? (KTU 2024 Formal Definition)

A **Lighting System** in the context of Energy Management is an engineered assembly of **luminaires (light fixtures)**, **lamps (light sources)**, **control gear (ballasts/transformers)**, **reflectors**, and **distribution networks** designed to deliver a specified **illuminance level (in lux)** on a working plane while minimizing energy consumption and meeting visual comfort standards (such as IS 3646 and the National Building Code of India).

In KTU 2024 syllabus terms, lighting is treated as one of the **major electrical end-use loads** in commercial and industrial facilities, typically accounting for **20% to 30%** of total electricity consumption in commercial buildings.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Watering-Can of Light" 💡

Imagine you are watering a rectangular garden patch with a watering can. 

- The **total water you pour** is like **Luminous Flux** (measured in *lumens*).
- The **water pressure at the nozzle** is like **Luminous Intensity** (measured in *candela*).
- The **amount of water landing on a square meter of grass** is like **Illuminance** (measured in *lux*).
- The **brightness you perceive looking at the wet grass** is like **Luminance** (measured in *candela per m²*).

Just as you would not use a fire-hose to water a small flowerpot (waste of energy), you would not use a 1000 W metal halide lamp to light a small office cabin. Lighting system design is the science of **matching the "right amount of light" to the "right area"** with the **least amount of energy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Conservation in lighting** is achieved not by reducing the *quantity* of light, but by improving the **quality**, **efficacy**, and **control** of the light delivered. The KTU 2024 syllabus heavily emphasizes shifting from **incandescent (10–15 lm/W)** to **LED systems (100–150 lm/W)**.

## 1.3 Core Photometric Quantities (KTU High-Yield Terms)

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | What it Measures |
|----------|--------|---------|------------------|
| Luminous Flux | $\Phi$ | **Lumen (lm)** | Total light emitted by a source |
| Luminous Intensity | $I$ | **Candela (cd)** | Light emitted per unit solid angle |
| Illuminance | $E$ | **Lux (lx)** | Light falling on a surface |
| Luminance | $L$ | **cd/m²** | Brightness of a surface (as seen by eye) |
| Luminous Efficacy | $\eta$ | **lm/W** | Light output per unit electrical input |
| Colour Rendering Index | CRI | $R_a$ (unitless) | How accurately colours appear under the source |

> [!NOTE]
> **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$) is the single most important parameter in energy-efficient lighting design.** A higher $\eta$ means **more light for less electricity**.

## 1.4 The Five Pillars of a Modern Lighting System

1. **Source** — The lamp (LED, fluorescent, HID, etc.)
2. **Luminaire** — The housing, reflector, diffuser, and lens.
3. **Control Gear** — Ballasts, drivers, and ignitors.
4. **Distribution Network** — Wiring, switches, dimmers, and sensors.
5. **Control Strategy** — Manual, automatic, daylight-linked, occupancy-based.

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 expects you to know that **lighting energy savings of 50–80%** are achievable simply by replacing **electromagnetic ballasts with electronic ballasts** and **conventional lamps with LED retrofits**, while maintaining the same lux level on the working plane.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Photometric Relationships

### 2.1.1 Relationship between Luminous Flux and Luminous Intensity

A source emitting luminous flux $\Phi$ uniformly in all directions produces a luminous intensity:

$$I = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi}$$

The factor **$4\pi$** is the total solid angle (in steradians) of a complete sphere.

### 2.1.2 Relationship between Luminous Intensity and Illuminance

For a point source, the illuminance $E$ on a surface at distance $d$ from the source is:

$$E = \frac{I}{d^2}$$

This is the **Inverse Square Law** (covered in depth in Section 3).

### 2.1.3 Relationship between Luminous Flux and Illuminance (Total Flux Method)

For a surface of area $A$ receiving luminous flux $\Phi$ uniformly:

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi = E \cdot A$$

## 2.2 Laws of Illumination

### 2.2.1 Inverse Square Law (Rohr's First Law)

The illuminance $E$ at a point on a surface is **inversely proportional to the square of the distance** $d$ from the point source, provided the light falls **normally** (perpendicular) on the surface.

$$E \propto \frac{1}{d^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = \frac{I}{d^2}$$

### 2.2.2 Lambert's Cosine Law (Rohr's Second Law)

The illuminance $E$ on a surface is **proportional to the cosine of the angle of incidence** $\theta$ between the normal to the surface and the direction of the incident light.

$$E = \frac{I}{d^2} \cos\theta$$

The "Why": At normal incidence ($\theta = 0°$, $\cos 0° = 1$), light is fully utilized. As $\theta$ increases, the same beam spreads over a larger projected area, **reducing illuminance per unit area**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exam questions, if a question says "light falls at an angle", you **must multiply by $\cos\theta$**. Forgetting this is the most common reason for losing 2–3 marks.

## 2.3 Lumen Method of Lighting Design (Zonal Cavity Method)

This is the **most frequently asked 14-mark question** from this topic. The lumen method determines the **number of lamps** required to achieve a specified average illuminance.

### 2.3.1 Core Design Equation

$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times \eta_L \times UF \times MF}$$

Where:

- $N$ = Number of lamps required
- $E$ = Required illuminance level (lux)
- $A$ = Area of the working plane (m²)
- $\Phi$ = Luminous flux per lamp (lumen)
- $\eta_L$ = Luminous efficacy (lm/W) — *if lamp wattage is the design constraint*
- $UF$ = **Utilization Factor** (CU — Coefficient of Utilization)
- $MF$ = **Maintenance Factor** (LLF — Light Loss Factor)

### 2.3.2 Room Index (RI) Calculation

The **Room Index** classifies a room's shape and determines the appropriate UF from standard tables.

$$RI = \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}$$

Where:

- $L$ = Length of the room (m)
- $W$ = Width of the room (m)
- $H_m$ = Mounting height of the lamp above the working plane (m)

### 2.3.3 Step-by-Step Lumen Method Procedure

1. **Determine** the room dimensions ($L$, $W$) and mounting height ($H_m$).
2. **Calculate** the Room Index $RI$.
3. **Select** the Utilization Factor $UF$ from the manufacturer's photometric data table (based on $RI$ and the room's reflectance values).
4. **Decide** the Maintenance Factor $MF$ (typically **0.7 to 0.9** for clean environments, **0.5 to 0.6** for dusty industrial areas).
5. **Choose** the illuminance level $E$ from **IS 3646** for the type of work.
6. **Compute** $N$ using the design equation.
7. **Adjust** $N$ to match an achievable luminaire layout (symmetry and spacing).

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 expects students to know standard **IS 3646 illuminance values**:
> - General office work: **300 to 500 lux**
> - Detailed bench/machine work: **500 to 750 lux**
> - Precision/electronic assembly: **1000+ lux**
> - Corridors/storerooms: **100 to 150 lux**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|-------------|-------|
| $E = \Phi / A$ | Average illuminance | lx |
| $E = I / d^2$ | Inverse square law (normal incidence) | lx |
| $E = (I / d^2)\cos\theta$ | Lambert's cosine law (oblique incidence) | lx |
| $I = \Phi / \omega$ | Luminous intensity from flux & solid angle | cd |
| $\eta = \Phi / P$ | Luminous efficacy (lumen output per watt) | lm/W |
| $N = (E \cdot A) / (\Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF)$ | Number of lamps (lumen method) | dimensionless |
| $RI = (L \cdot W) / [H_m(L + W)]$ | Room Index | dimensionless |
| $H_m = H - h_w$ | Mounting height = ceiling height – working plane height | m |
| $SHR = s / H_m$ | Spacing-to-Height Ratio | dimensionless |
| $\% \text{ Savings} = \frac{P_{old} - P_{new}}{P_{old}} \times 100$ | Energy savings percentage | % |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR)** is critical. If actual spacing between luminaires > (SHR × $H_m$), the work-plane will have dark patches. Manufacturer's data specifies maximum permissible SHR for each luminaire type.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

In real-world Energy Management projects, lighting audits follow the **exact lumen-method formula**. A certified energy auditor will:

1. Measure existing lux with a light meter.
2. Calculate $N_{existing}$ lamps.
3. Propose LED replacement with new $\Phi$ and $UF$.
4. Calculate $N_{new}$ and the **payback period** from energy savings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 frequently asks: *"A factory is lit with 400 W metal halide lamps. Suggest an energy-efficient alternative."* The correct engineering approach is to **match the delivered lux**, not the wattage. A **150 W LED high-bay** typically replaces a 400 W metal halide while delivering the same or higher illuminance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Inverse Square Law

### Statement
The illuminance on a surface is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from a point source (light striking normally).

### Step 1 — Define the geometric setup
Consider a point source $S$ emitting luminous flux $\Phi$ uniformly in all directions. The flux passes through the surface of an imaginary sphere of radius $d$ centered at $S$.

The total surface area of this sphere is:

$$A_{sphere} = 4\pi d^2$$

### Step 2 — Apply the definition of illuminance
Illuminance is the luminous flux received **per unit area**. Assuming uniform distribution:

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{A_{sphere}}$$

Substitute $A_{sphere}$:

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi d^2}$$

### Step 3 — Relate flux to intensity
By definition, luminous intensity $I$ is the flux emitted per unit solid angle. Since the total solid angle is $4\pi$ steradians:

$$I = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi = 4\pi I$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and simplify
Replace $\Phi$ in the illuminance equation:

$$E = \frac{4\pi I}{4\pi d^2}$$

Cancel $4\pi$ from numerator and denominator:

$$E = \frac{I}{d^2}$$

### Step 5 — Final interpretation
This proves the **Inverse Square Law**: as distance $d$ **doubles**, illuminance $E$ drops to **one-fourth**. As $d$ **triples**, $E$ drops to **one-ninth**.

> **Physical "Why":** The same quantity of light spreads over an area that grows as $d^2$ (surface of a sphere), so the *density* of light per unit area must fall as $1/d^2$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Lambert's Cosine Law

### Statement
For oblique incidence, the illuminance is proportional to the cosine of the angle of incidence.

### Step 1 — Setup
A point source $S$ has intensity $I$ in the direction of point $P$ on a surface. The line $SP$ makes an angle $\theta$ with the **normal** $N$ to the surface at $P$. The perpendicular distance from $S$ to the surface is $d$.

### Step 2 — Geometry
The actual distance from $S$ to $P$ is:

$$r = \frac{d}{\cos\theta}$$

This is because $d$ is the **adjacent** side and $r$ is the **hypotenuse** of a right triangle with angle $\theta$.

### Step 3 — Apply Inverse Square Law at distance $r$
From Section 3.1, the illuminance on a surface normal to the ray (at distance $r$) is:

$$E_{normal} = \frac{I}{r^2}$$

### Step 4 — Project onto the actual surface
The actual surface area $A$ is **larger** than the projected area $A \cos\theta$ (a beam striking obliquely covers more area on the actual surface). Hence the illuminance is reduced by the factor $\cos\theta$:

$$E = E_{normal} \times \cos\theta$$

### Step 5 — Combine and simplify
Substitute $E_{normal} = I / r^2$ and $r = d / \cos\theta$:

$$E = \frac{I}{r^2} \cos\theta = \frac{I}{(d/\cos\theta)^2} \cos\theta$$

Expand the denominator:

$$E = \frac{I \cos^2\theta \cdot \cos\theta}{d^2}$$

This is the form when the **perpendicular distance $d$ is fixed** and $\theta$ varies.

However, in **most KTU problems**, $d$ is given as the *slant* distance along the ray. In that case $r = d$ and we get the simpler form:

$$E = \frac{I}{d^2} \cos\theta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Read the problem statement carefully. If $d$ is the **perpendicular (vertical) distance**, use $E = (I/d^2)\cos^3\theta$. If $d$ is the **slant distance**, use $E = (I/d^2)\cos\theta$. Marks are lost here regularly.

## 3.3 Derivation of Luminous Efficacy Relationship

### Step 1 — Define
A lamp consumes electrical power $P$ (watts) and produces luminous flux $\Phi$ (lumens).

### Step 2 — By definition

$$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P}$$

This shows that for the same light output, **higher $\eta$ means lower power consumption**. This is the foundation of energy conservation in lighting.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Lumen Method Design

> **Problem (14-Mark KTU Style):**  
> An engineering drawing hall has dimensions $L = 20$ m, $W = 10$ m, ceiling height $H = 4$ m. The working plane is at height $h_w = 0.85$ m. Selected LED panels have a luminous flux of 3600 lm each. The room reflectances yield $UF = 0.65$ and the maintenance factor is $MF = 0.8$. The required illuminance per IS 3646 for drawing work is $E = 750$ lux. Calculate: (a) the mounting height, (b) the room index, and (c) the number of LED panels required.

### Solution

#### Part (a) — Mounting Height
The mounting height is the vertical distance from the lamp to the working plane:

$$H_m = H - h_w$$

$$H_m = 4 - 0.85 = 3.15 \text{ m}$$

#### Part (b) — Room Index
Using the room index formula:

$$RI = \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)}$$

Substitute $L = 20$, $W = 10$, $H_m = 3.15$:

$$RI = \frac{20 \times 10}{3.15 \times (20 + 10)}$$

$$RI = \frac{200}{3.15 \times 30} = \frac{200}{94.5}$$

$$RI \approx 2.116$$

#### Part (c) — Number of Lamps
Using the lumen-method design equation:

$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times UF \times MF}$$

Compute the area:

$$A = L \times W = 20 \times 10 = 200 \text{ m}^2$$

Substitute all values:

$$N = \frac{750 \times 200}{3600 \times 0.65 \times 0.8}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$3600 \times 0.65 = 2340$$

$$2340 \times 0.8 = 1872$$

Compute the numerator:

$$750 \times 200 = 150{,}000$$

Final calculation:

$$N = \frac{150{,}000}{1872} \approx 80.13$$

Since you cannot install 0.13 of a lamp, **round up** to:

$$N = 81 \text{ LED panels}$$

For a clean layout, the design would arrange these as **9 rows × 9 columns = 81 panels**, which is symmetrically perfect for the 20 m × 10 m hall.

> [!TIP]
> **Verification check:** Spacing between panels = $20/9 \approx 2.22$ m. If the manufacturer's max SHR is, say, 1.5, then max spacing = $1.5 \times 3.15 = 4.725$ m. Since 2.22 m < 4.725 m, the layout is acceptable.

## 3.5 Comparative Lamp Performance Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Lamp Type | Efficacy (lm/W) | Life (hours) | CRI | Energy Class | Typical Use |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------|-----|--------------|-------------|
| Incandescent | 10–17 | 1,000 | 100 | Very Poor | Domestic (being phased out) |
| Halogen | 15–25 | 2,000–4,000 | 100 | Poor | Display, accent |
| Fluorescent (T8) | 50–70 | 10,000–15,000 | 60–80 | Moderate | Offices (legacy) |
| Fluorescent (T5) | 80–100 | 20,000 | 80–85 | Good | Modern offices |
| CFL | 50–70 | 8,000–10,000 | 80 | Moderate | Domestic replacement |
| Mercury Vapour (HPMV) | 35–55 | 16,000 | 45 | Poor | Street lighting (legacy) |
| Sodium Vapour (HPSV) | 80–120 | 24,000 | 25 | Good | Street lighting |
| Metal Halide (HPI) | 70–100 | 15,000 | 65–90 | Good | Industrial, sports |
| **LED** | **100–160** | **50,000+** | **80–95** | **Excellent** | **All modern applications** |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define luminous flux, luminous intensity, and illuminance. State their SI units.**

#### Model Answer
**Luminous Flux ($\Phi$):** The total quantity of light energy emitted per second by a source in all directions, weighted according to the sensitivity of the human eye. SI Unit: **Lumen (lm)**.

**Luminous Intensity ($I$):** The luminous flux emitted by a source per unit solid angle in a given direction. SI Unit: **Candela (cd)**. Mathematically, $I = \Phi / \omega$, where $\omega$ is the solid angle in steradians.

**Illuminance ($E$):** The luminous flux incident on a unit area of a surface. SI Unit: **Lux (lx)** or **lumen per square metre (lm/m²)**. Mathematically, $E = \Phi / A$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Common error: Students often confuse **lumen (flux)** with **lux (illuminance)**. Remember: **lumen is the total light emitted, lux is the light received per square metre**. A 1000 lm lamp in a large hall gives low lux; the same lamp focused on a small desk gives high lux.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is luminous efficacy? Why is it a critical parameter in energy conservation?**

#### Model Answer
**Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$)** is the ratio of luminous flux output to the electrical power input of a lamp. SI Unit: **lumen per watt (lm/W)**.

$$\eta = \frac{\Phi}{P}$$

It is critical in energy conservation because a higher efficacy means **more visible light for less electrical energy**. Replacing a 60 W incandescent lamp ($\eta \approx 13$ lm/W) with a 9 W LED lamp ($\eta \approx 110$ lm/W) delivering the same luminous flux reduces power consumption by **85%** while delivering identical lighting. Hence, luminous efficacy is the **primary metric** for selecting energy-efficient lamps.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** State and explain the **Inverse Square Law** and **Lambert's Cosine Law** of illumination. Derive both laws from first principles. (7 marks)

**(b)** An office cabin measures 4 m × 3 m with a ceiling height of 3 m. The working plane is 0.8 m above the floor. Two LED downlighters, each delivering 2000 lumens, are mounted on the ceiling. The maintenance factor is 0.8 and the utilization factor is 0.6. Calculate the average illuminance on the working plane. Comment on whether this meets the IS 3646 standard for office work (500 lux). (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — Laws of Illumination

**Inverse Square Law:** The illuminance $E$ at a point on a surface, due to a point source, is inversely proportional to the square of the distance $d$ from the source, when light strikes normally.

**Derivation:** A point source $S$ emits total flux $\Phi$ uniformly in all directions. The flux passes through a sphere of radius $d$ with surface area $4\pi d^2$. By definition of illuminance:

$$E = \frac{\Phi}{4\pi d^2}$$

Since $I = \Phi / 4\pi$, we get:

$$E = \frac{I}{d^2}$$

**Lambert's Cosine Law:** When light falls obliquely, the illuminance is proportional to the cosine of the angle of incidence $\theta$ between the incident ray and the normal to the surface.

**Derivation:** Let the slant distance be $r$ and the angle be $\theta$. The illuminance on a normal surface at slant distance $r$ is $E_{normal} = I/r^2$. But the actual surface area is larger by a factor of $1/\cos\theta$, so:

$$E = \frac{I}{r^2} \cos\theta$$

If the perpendicular (vertical) distance is $d$ and $r = d/\cos\theta$, then:

$$E = \frac{I}{d^2} \cos^3\theta$$

##### Part (b) — Average Illuminance Calculation

**Given:**

- $L = 4$ m, $W = 3$ m, ceiling height $H = 3$ m, $h_w = 0.8$ m
- Number of lamps $N = 2$, $\Phi = 2000$ lm each
- $UF = 0.6$, $MF = 0.8$
- Area $A = 4 \times 3 = 12$ m²

**Average illuminance** (rearranged lumen-method formula):

$$E = \frac{N \times \Phi \times UF \times MF}{A}$$

$$E = \frac{2 \times 2000 \times 0.6 \times 0.8}{12}$$

Compute numerator: $2 \times 2000 = 4000$; $4000 \times 0.6 = 2400$; $2400 \times 0.8 = 1920$.

$$E = \frac{1920}{12} = 160 \text{ lux}$$

**Comment:** The achieved 160 lux is **well below** the IS 3646 recommendation of 500 lux for general office work. The number of lamps must be increased. Required:

$$N = \frac{500 \times 12}{2000 \times 0.6 \times 0.8} = \frac{6000}{960} \approx 6.25$$

Therefore, **7 lamps** are required to meet the standard.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **Lumen Method** of lighting design. Define utilization factor, maintenance factor, and room index. (7 marks)

**(b)** A factory floor of 30 m × 15 m is to be illuminated to an average level of 300 lux. The mounting height is 5 m. Selected 200 W LED high-bay luminaires deliver 24,000 lumens each. The utilization factor is 0.55 and the maintenance factor is 0.75. Calculate: (i) the room index, and (ii) the number of luminaires required. State the recommended spacing between rows. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Lumen Method Explanation

The Lumen Method (also called the Zonal Cavity Method) calculates the number of luminaires required to achieve a specified average illuminance. The procedure is:

1. Determine the room dimensions $L$ and $W$.
2. Compute the mounting height $H_m$ above the working plane.
3. Compute the **Room Index** $RI = (L \cdot W) / [H_m(L + W)]$.
4. From manufacturer's photometric tables and reflectance values (ceiling, walls, floor), determine the **Utilization Factor (UF)**.
5. Choose a **Maintenance Factor (MF)** based on environmental conditions and lamp cleaning interval.
6. Apply: $N = (E \times A) / (\Phi \times UF \times MF)$.

**Definitions:**

- **Utilization Factor (UF):** The ratio of the luminous flux reaching the working plane to the total flux emitted by the lamps. It accounts for losses due to fixture geometry, room shape, and surface reflectances. Range: typically 0.3 to 0.8.
- **Maintenance Factor (MF):** The ratio of illuminance on the working plane after a defined period of use to the initial illuminance. It accounts for lamp lumen depreciation, luminaire dirt accumulation, and room surface soiling. Typical values: 0.8 (clean), 0.7 (industrial), 0.5 (very dirty).
- **Room Index (RI):** A dimensionless number that classifies a room's proportions. Higher $RI$ indicates a wide, shallow room (more efficient light utilization); lower $RI$ indicates a tall, narrow room.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**

- $L = 30$ m, $W = 15$ m, $H_m = 5$ m
- $E = 300$ lux, $\Phi = 24{,}000$ lm
- $UF = 0.55$, $MF = 0.75$
- $A = 30 \times 15 = 450$ m²

**(i) Room Index:**

$$RI = \frac{L \times W}{H_m \times (L + W)} = \frac{30 \times 15}{5 \times (30 + 15)}$$

$$RI = \frac{450}{5 \times 45} = \frac{450}{225} = 2.0$$

**(ii) Number of Luminaires:**

$$N = \frac{E \times A}{\Phi \times UF \times MF} = \frac{300 \times 450}{24{,}000 \times 0.55 \times 0.75}$$

Compute denominator: $24{,}000 \times 0.55 = 13{,}200$; $13{,}200 \times 0.75 = 9900$.

Compute numerator: $300 \times 450 = 135{,}000$.

$$N = \frac{135{,}000}{9900} \approx 13.64$$

**Round up to 14 luminaires.**

**Recommended spacing:** For a typical LED high-bay with $SHR_{max} \approx 1.5$:

$$\text{Max spacing} = SHR \times H_m = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5 \text{ m}$$

A practical layout would be **2 rows of 7 luminaires**, with row-to-row spacing of 7.5 m and luminaire-to-luminaire spacing along each row of $30/7 \approx 4.3$ m.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to round UP** the number of lamps. Always round up; you cannot install a fractional lamp.
> 2. **Confusing H and Hm** — using total ceiling height instead of mounting height above the working plane. This gives a wrong Room Index and wrong UF.
> 3. **Ignoring the $\cos\theta$** term in Lambert's law problems. Marks are forfeited for this.
> 4. **Mixing up $RI$ formula** — writing it as $H_m \times (L+W) / (L \cdot W)$ (inverted). Always remember: $RI$ is **large** for **wide, shallow** rooms.
> 5. **Using lamp wattage** in the design equation instead of **luminous flux $\Phi$**. The lumen method works in lumens, not watts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Luminous Flux ($\Phi$)** is measured in **lumens (lm)** — total light emitted.
- **Illuminance ($E$)** is measured in **lux (lx)** — light falling on a surface.
- **Luminous Intensity ($I$)** is measured in **candela (cd)** — flux per unit solid angle.
- **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta$)** in **lm/W** is the most important energy metric.
- **Inverse Square Law:** $E = I / d^2$ (light falls **normally**).
- **Lambert's Cosine Law:** $E = (I / d^2) \cos\theta$ (light falls **obliquely**).
- **Lumen Method Equation:** $N = (E \times A) / (\Phi \times UF \times MF)$.
- **Room Index:** $RI = (L \cdot W) / [H_m(L + W)]$.
- **Mounting height** = ceiling height **minus** working plane height.
- **IS 3646** recommends 300–500 lux for general office work, 500–750 lux for detailed work, and 1000+ lux for precision tasks.
- **LED** offers the best efficacy (100–160 lm/W) and longest life (50,000+ hours).
- **Energy savings** of 50–80% are typical when replacing legacy systems with LED + electronic ballasts + sensors.
- Always **round UP** the calculated number of lamps to the next integer.
- Always **verify spacing** against the manufacturer's maximum Spacing-to-Height Ratio (SHR).
- **Maintenance Factor (MF)** is always **less than 1**; clean rooms use 0.8, dirty industrial areas use 0.5–0.6.
- **Utilization Factor (UF)** depends on RI and surface reflectances; obtained from luminaire manufacturer's photometric data.
- In Lambert's law, **read carefully** whether $d$ is perpendicular or slant — this changes the exponent of $\cos\theta$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motors, Belts and Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Motors, Belts and Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Motors — The Workhorses of Industrial Energy Consumption

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
An **electric motor** is an electromechanical energy conversion device that transforms electrical energy into mechanical energy (rotational motion), thereby serving as the primary prime-mover for industrial drives, pumps, compressors, fans, and conveyor systems. In the context of energy management, motors account for approximately **65–70% of total industrial electricity consumption**, making them the single most important end-use equipment for energy conservation audits.

**Classification of Industrial Motors:**

| Family | Sub-Type | Typical Application | Energy Relevance |
|--------|----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **AC Motors** | Induction (Squirrel Cage, Slip Ring) | Pumps, fans, compressors | Most widely used (>90% of industrial motors) |
| **AC Motors** | Synchronous Motor | Power factor correction, large compressors | Constant speed, unity PF operation |
| **DC Motors** | Shunt, Series, Compound | Cranes, hoists, traction | Speed flexibility, declining usage |
| **Special** | BLDC, PMSM, Servo | Modern variable-speed drives | High efficiency (>90%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The focus of this module is **not** on motor design, but on **energy-efficient selection, operation, loss minimization, and matching** of motors with mechanical power transmission systems (belts and drives) to optimize overall system efficiency.

## 1.2 Belts — Flexible Mechanical Power Transmission

**Definition:**
A **belt drive** is a frictional power transmission system using a continuous flexible loop stretched over two or more pulleys to transfer rotational motion and torque from a driving shaft (motor) to a driven shaft (load).

**Common Belt Types in Industry:**

1. **Flat Belt Drive** — High-speed, low-power, long-distance transmission (older applications).
2. **V-Belt Drive** — Most common in industry; trapezoidal cross-section wedges into the pulley groove, increasing frictional grip.
3. **Synchronous (Timing) Belt** — Toothed belt providing positive (no-slip) transmission, used where synchronization is critical.
4. **Poly-V / Multi-Rib Belt** — Compact, high-speed, automotive-grade flexibility.

## 1.3 Drives — Speed & Torque Control Systems

**Definition:**
A **drive** (also called a Variable Frequency Drive, VFD) is an electronic power converter system that regulates the speed, torque, and direction of an AC induction motor by varying the **frequency** and **voltage** of the supplied electrical power.

$$ f = \frac{P \cdot N}{120} \quad \text{(Synchronous speed relationship)} $$

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Legged Stool" of Industrial Energy Efficiency

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine an industrial process as a **three-legged stool** delivering mechanical work to a load:
> - **Leg 1 (Motor):** The "heart" that pumps electrical energy into the system. A weak heart (inefficient motor) wastes energy as heat.
> - **Leg 2 (Belt):** The "circulatory veins" carrying mechanical energy from motor to load. Leaky pipes (slipping belts) lose precious power.
> - **Leg 3 (Drive):** The "throttle/brain" that controls how fast and how hard the system works. A faulty throttle (fixed-speed drive) wastes energy by over-pumping.
>
> **Energy management is the art of keeping all three legs balanced and efficient.**

## 1.5 Why This Topic is Critical for Energy Management

| Energy Loss Source | Typical Magnitude |
|--------------------|-------------------|
| Motor stator copper loss (I²R) | 30–40% of total losses |
| Motor core (iron) loss | 15–25% |
| Mechanical (friction + windage) | 5–10% |
| Stray load loss | 5–15% |
| **Belt slip + hysteresis loss** | **3–8%** (often overlooked!) |
| Gear/bearing loss | 2–5% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Fact (BEE India Standard):** A 10% improvement in motor + belt + drive system efficiency in a typical 100 kW plant can save **~50,000 kWh/year** — translating to **₹5–6 lakh/year** in monetary savings (at ₹10/kWh).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electric Motor — Operational Theory & Loss Mechanism

### 2.1.1 Three-Phase Induction Motor (Squirrel Cage) — Industry Workhorse

**Working Principle:**
When a balanced three-phase AC supply is applied to the stator winding, it produces a **rotating magnetic field (RMF)** at synchronous speed $N_s$. This RMF cuts the stationary rotor conductors, inducing EMF and current (Faraday's Law). The interaction between rotor current and stator RMF produces a **Lorentz force** (F = BIL) that develops torque, causing rotor rotation in the direction of the RMF.

### 2.1.2 The Slip Mechanism — The Heart of Induction Motor

**Definition:** Slip ($s$) is the relative difference between synchronous speed ($N_s$) and actual rotor speed ($N_r$), expressed as a fraction or percentage.

$$ s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \times 100\% $$

**Physical Interpretation:**

- **At start (s = 1):** Rotor is stationary; maximum relative motion → maximum induced EMF → maximum starting current (5–7× rated).
- **At no-load (s ≈ 0.1–0.5%):** Rotor nearly follows RMF; very low torque.
- **At full-load (s ≈ 3–5%):** Normal operating point for standard motors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Management Insight:** Slip directly determines rotor copper loss. At full load, **rotor copper loss = s × (air-gap power)**. Therefore, a high-slip motor inherently wastes more energy as heat in the rotor cage.

### 2.1.3 Motor Efficiency & Power Flow

**Input Electrical Power:**
$$ P_{in} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_L \cdot I_L \cdot \cos\phi $$

**Output Mechanical Power:**
$$ P_{out} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} $$

**Motor Efficiency:**
$$ \eta_{motor} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{P_{in} - \sum \text{Losses}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% $$

### 2.1.4 Motor Losses — The Five-Component Model

| Loss Component | Symbol | Cause | % of Total Loss |
|----------------|--------|-------|-----------------|
| Stator Copper (I²R) | $P_{sc}$ | Resistance of stator winding | 30–40% |
| Rotor Copper (I²R) | $P_{rc}$ | Resistance of rotor bars/end-rings | 20–25% |
| Core (Iron) Loss | $P_{core}$ | Hysteresis + Eddy currents in lamination | 15–25% |
| Friction & Windage | $P_{fw}$ | Bearing drag + rotor fan | 5–10% |
| Stray Load Loss | $P_{stray}$ | Harmonics, leakage flux | 5–15% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For an **IE3/IE4 Premium Efficiency Motor**, total losses are reduced by **20–30%** compared to standard IE1 motors, primarily by using more copper (lower I²R), thinner lamination steel (lower core loss), and tighter air gaps.

## 2.2 Power Factor — The Hidden Energy Waster

**Definition:** Power factor is the cosine of the angle between voltage and current in an AC circuit, representing the ratio of **real (active) power** to **apparent power**.

$$ \text{Power Factor} = \cos\phi = \frac{P_{real}}{V \cdot I} = \frac{\text{kW}}{\text{kVA}} $$

**Effect on Energy Billing:**

For the same kW delivered, a lower PF means:
- Higher current (I = kW / (V·cosφ))
- Higher I²R losses in cables (proportional to 1/cos²φ)
- **Penalty charges** from electricity utility

| Power Factor | Line Current (% of unity PF) | Cable I²R Loss (% of unity PF) |
|:------------:|:----------------------------:|:------------------------------:|
| 1.0          | 100%                         | 100%                           |
| 0.8          | 125%                         | 156%                           |
| 0.6          | 167%                         | 278%                           |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of PF like a beer mug — the beer is "useful" (kW), the foam is "useless" (kVAR). A mug full of pure beer (PF = 1.0) is the most efficient delivery. A mug full of foam and a sip of beer (PF = 0.5) wastes the mug's capacity.

## 2.3 Belt Drives — Theory of Frictional Power Transmission

### 2.3.1 Velocity Ratio (Speed Ratio)

**Definition:** The ratio of driver pulley speed to driven pulley speed, equal to the inverse ratio of their diameters.

$$ i = \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{d_2}{d_1} $$

Where:
- $N_1, D_1$ = speed and pitch diameter of driver (motor) pulley
- $N_2, D_2$ = speed and pitch diameter of driven (load) pulley

### 2.3.2 Belt Length — Open Belt Configuration

For an open belt drive (non-crossed):

$$ L = 2C + \frac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C} $$

Where $C$ = center distance between pulleys.

### 2.3.3 Belt Slip and Creep

- **Slip:** Gross relative motion between belt and pulley (occurs at overloading or insufficient tension).
- **Creep:** Microscopic relative motion due to elastic stretch of belt on tight side vs. contraction on slack side. Always present, even under normal operation.

$$ N_2 = N_1 \cdot \frac{D_1}{D_2} \cdot (1 - s_{belt}) $$

Where $s_{belt}$ = belt slip (typically 1–3%).

### 2.3.4 Power Transmitted by Belt

$$ P_{belt} = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v $$

Where:
- $T_1$ = tension in tight side (N)
- $T_2$ = tension in slack side (N)
- $v$ = belt linear velocity (m/s) = $\pi D_1 N_1 / 60$

**Euler's Eytelwein Equation (capstan equation for belt friction):**

$$ \frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta} $$

Where:
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction between belt and pulley
- $\theta$ = angle of wrap (contact angle) in radians

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Management Insight:** V-belts typically have $\theta$ ≥ 180° for small pulley (larger wrap angle), giving $T_1/T_2$ ratio of 4–5× higher than flat belts. This is why V-belts transmit more power for the same tension and rarely slip.

### 2.3.5 Belt Drive Efficiency

$$ \eta_{belt} = \frac{P_{driven}}{P_{driver}} = \frac{(T_1 - T_2) \cdot v}{T_1 \cdot v} = 1 - \frac{T_2}{T_1} $$

**Typical Efficiencies:**
- Flat belt (with slip): 95–98%
- V-belt (new): 95–97%
- V-belt (worn): 90–93%
- Timing/synchronous belt: 97–99% (no slip)
- Chain drive: 97–98%

## 2.4 Variable Frequency Drives (VFD) — Energy Saver for Variable-Torque Loads

### 2.4.1 The Affinity Laws (Fan/Pump Law)

For centrifugal pumps, fans, and compressors, the following cubic relationships apply when speed is varied:

$$ \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \qquad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \qquad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 $$

Where: $Q$ = flow rate, $H$ = head/pressure, $P$ = power, $N$ = speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Energy Insight:** A **20% reduction in speed saves ~49% in power!** This is the single most important energy conservation principle in fluid handling systems. A simple VFD on a pump/fan pays back in 6–18 months.

### 2.4.2 VFD Power Loss Components

| Component | Typical % of Motor Rating |
|-----------|--------------------------|
| Rectifier (diodes) | 1–2% |
| DC Bus capacitor ESR | < 1% |
| IGBT inverter switching | 2–3% |
| Harmonic losses in motor (due to PWM) | 5–15% additional heating |
| **Total VFD system loss** | **3–5%** |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Summary)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD — Master These 12 Formulas:**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{in} = \sqrt{3}\,V_L I_L \cos\phi$ | 3-φ input power | W |
| 2 | $P_{out} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Mechanical output | W |
| 3 | $\eta_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | Motor efficiency | % |
| 4 | $N_s = \dfrac{120\,f}{P}$ | Synchronous speed | rpm |
| 5 | $s = \dfrac{N_s - N_r}{N_s}$ | Slip | fraction/% |
| 6 | $PF = \cos\phi = \dfrac{kW}{kVA}$ | Power factor | — |
| 7 | $i = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$ | Belt speed ratio | — |
| 8 | $L = 2C + \dfrac{\pi(D_1+D_2)}{2} + \dfrac{(D_2-D_1)^2}{4C}$ | Open belt length | m |
| 9 | $P_{belt} = (T_1 - T_2)\,v$ | Belt power | W |
| 10 | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu\theta}$ | Eytelwein's equation | — |
| 11 | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3$ | Affinity law (power) | — |
| 12 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{belt} \times \eta_{drive}$ | System efficiency | % |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Synchronous Speed of Induction Motor

### Given:
A 3-phase induction motor has $P$ = 4 poles and is supplied with $f$ = 50 Hz AC supply.

### To Find:
Synchronous speed $N_s$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Concept of Electrical Poles:**
A 2-pole machine produces one North-South pair per electrical cycle. In a 3-phase system, the rotating magnetic field completes one revolution in 2 electrical cycles if P = 2. In general, one electrical cycle produces 2/P of a mechanical revolution.

**Step 2 — Relate frequency to revolutions per second:**
Number of revolutions per second = $f \times \dfrac{2}{P}$

**Step 3 — Convert to revolutions per minute (rpm):**
$$ N_s = f \times \frac{2}{P} \times 60 = \frac{120 f}{P} $$

**Step 4 — Numerical Substitution:**
$$ N_s = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = \frac{6000}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm} $$

### Result:
$$ \boxed{N_s = 1500 \text{ rpm at full-load}} $$

## 3.2 Derivation 3 — Power Flow Through an Induction Motor

### Statement:
Derive the air-gap power, rotor copper loss, and developed mechanical power in terms of slip.

### Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define Air-Gap Power ($P_g$):**
The power transferred electromagnetically across the air gap from stator to rotor equals the power input to the rotor circuit.

$$ P_g = P_{in} - P_{stator\,copper} - P_{core} $$

**Step 2 — Rotor Copper Loss:**
The rotor resistance referred to stator is $R_2'$. The rotor frequency is $f_r = s f$. The rotor copper loss is:

$$ P_{rc} = I_2'^2 \cdot R_2' $$

**Step 3 — Air-Gap Power Split:**
The total air-gap power divides into two parts:
1. Rotor copper loss: $P_{rc}$
2. Gross mechanical power developed: $P_m$

$$ P_g = P_{rc} + P_m $$

**Step 4 — Slip Power Relation:**
The rotor current at slip $s$ is inversely proportional to $s$. Detailed analysis shows:

$$ P_{rc} = s \cdot P_g \qquad P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_g $$

**Step 5 — Net Shaft Output Power:**
Subtracting mechanical losses (friction + windage) $P_{fw}$:

$$ P_{out} = P_m - P_{fw} = (1-s) P_g - P_{fw} $$

### Final Results:
$$ \boxed{P_{rc} = s \cdot P_g, \quad P_m = (1-s) \cdot P_g, \quad P_{out} = (1-s) P_g - P_{fw}} $$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Belt Power Transmitted (Full Analysis)

### Given:
A V-belt drive has tight side tension $T_1$ = 1000 N, slack side tension $T_2$ = 400 N, belt velocity $v$ = 10 m/s, and pulley diameters $D_1$ = 200 mm (driver) and $D_2$ = 400 mm (driven).

### To Find:
Power transmitted, belt efficiency, and driven pulley torque.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Net Effective Tractive Force:**
$$ F_{net} = T_1 - T_2 = 1000 - 400 = 600 \text{ N} $$

**Step 2 — Power Transmitted by Belt:**
$$ P_{belt} = F_{net} \times v = 600 \times 10 = 6000 \text{ W} = 6 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 3 — Belt Drive Efficiency:**
$$ \eta_{belt} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1} = \frac{600}{1000} = 0.60 = 60\% $$

**Step 4 — Speed of Driven Pulley:**
$$ N_2 = N_1 \times \frac{D_1}{D_2} = N_1 \times \frac{200}{400} = \frac{N_1}{2} $$

**Step 5 — Torque on Driven Pulley:**
$$ T_{driven} = \frac{P_{belt}}{\omega_2} = \frac{P_{belt} \times 60}{2\pi N_2} $$

If $N_1$ = 1500 rpm, then $N_2$ = 750 rpm:
$$ T_{driven} = \frac{6000 \times 60}{2\pi \times 750} = \frac{360000}{4712.4} = 76.4 \text{ N·m} $$

### Result:
$$ \boxed{P_{belt} = 6 \text{ kW}, \quad \eta_{belt} = 60\%, \quad T_{driven} \approx 76.4 \text{ N·m}} $$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Savings by VFD on Centrifugal Pump

### Given:
A 75 kW centrifugal pump operates at 100% speed, 100% flow. Annual operating hours = 6000 hr. Electricity cost = ₹8/kWh. Using VFD to throttle to 80% speed.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Affinity Law (Power):**
$$ \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = (0.80)^3 = 0.512 $$

**Step 2 — New Power with VFD:**
$$ P_2 = 75 \times 0.512 = 38.4 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 3 — Power Saved:**
$$ \Delta P = 75 - 38.4 = 36.6 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 4 — Annual Energy Saved:**
$$ \Delta E = 36.6 \times 6000 = 2,19,600 \text{ kWh/year} $$

**Step 5 — Annual Cost Saving:**
$$ \Delta C = 2,19,600 \times 8 = ₹17,56,800/\text{year} \approx ₹17.57 \text{ lakh/year} $$

**Step 6 — VFD Cost & Payback:**
Typical VFD cost for 75 kW: ~₹3–4 lakh
$$ \text{Payback} = \frac{4,00,000}{17,57,800} \times 12 \approx 2.7 \text{ months} $$

### Result:
$$ \boxed{\text{Annual saving} \approx ₹17.57 \text{ lakh, payback} \approx 2.7 \text{ months}} $$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Motor Selection Decision Tree

| Load Type | Speed Control Required? | Recommended Motor + Drive | Expected Efficiency | Payback |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------------|---------------------|---------|
| Constant load, fixed speed (conveyor) | No | Standard IE3 Squirrel Cage + DOL starter | 92–94% | — |
| Variable torque, variable speed (fan/pump) | Yes | IE3 Squirrel Cage + VFD | Motor 92% + VFD 96% = ~88% | 6–18 months |
| Constant torque, variable speed (compressor) | Yes | IE3 Squirrel Cage + VFD | ~87% | 12–24 months |
| Low speed, high starting torque (crusher) | Yes | Slip-ring induction + Rotor Resistance Control | 88–90% | 24–36 months |
| Precision positioning (CNC) | Yes | PMSM Servo + Servo Drive | 93–96% | Long, but mandatory |
| Power factor critical plant | — | Synchronous Motor (over-excited) | 95% + unity PF | 18–30 months |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
