# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**
**Define slip in an induction motor. Why does the rotor never reach synchronous speed?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Slip is defined as the relative difference between synchronous speed ($N_s$) and actual rotor speed ($N_r$), expressed as a percentage:
$$ s = \frac{N_s - N_r}{N_s} \times 100\% $$
- **Reason rotor never reaches $N_s$ (1.5 marks):** If the rotor rotated at synchronous speed, there would be **no relative motion** between the rotating magnetic field and the rotor conductors. Consequently, **no EMF would be induced**, no rotor current would flow, and **no torque** would be developed. The rotor would decelerate due to load torque. Thus, slip is essential for torque production and the rotor must always lag behind $N_s$.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Style]**
**List any three types of belt drives and state one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Flat Belt Drive (1 mark):** Used in high-speed, low-power applications such as line shafts in textile mills, agricultural machinery, and older machine tool drives.
2. **V-Belt Drive (1 mark):** Most common industrial drive used in automotive engines, pumps, compressors, and conveyor systems where compactness and high torque transmission are required.
3. **Synchronous (Timing) Belt (1 mark):** Used in applications requiring **positive (no-slip) transmission** and synchronization, such as in CNC machines, printer mechanisms, and cam shaft drives.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) for speed control of a three-phase induction motor. State the **Affinity Laws** for centrifugal pumps. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A 3-phase, 4-pole, 50 Hz induction motor has a full-load slip of 4%. Calculate the synchronous speed, actual rotor speed, and frequency of rotor current. If the motor develops an output of 7.46 kW at this slip, calculate the input power if the motor efficiency is 88%. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) VFD Working Principle + Affinity Laws (7 Marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The VFD consists of three main stages:
1. **Rectifier (Converter):** Converts 50 Hz AC mains into DC using a 6-pulse diode bridge rectifier.
2. **DC Link (Filter):** Smoothes the DC using large electrolytic capacitors; acts as energy storage.
3. **Inverter:** Uses IGBT switches with Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) to convert DC back to variable-voltage, variable-frequency AC.

**Working (2 marks):**
The VFD controls the stator frequency $f$ and voltage $V$ in a constant V/f ratio. This maintains constant air-gap flux and prevents saturation. By varying $f$ (and proportionally $V$), the synchronous speed $N_s = 120f/P$ changes, which in turn changes rotor speed. Speed reference signal is compared with feedback (encoder/tachometer) in a closed-loop PID controller to maintain accuracy.

**Affinity Laws for Centrifugal Pumps (2 marks):**
$$ \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \qquad \frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \qquad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 $$

Where $Q$ = flow, $H$ = head, $P$ = power, $N$ = speed. These show that power varies with the **cube of speed** — a small reduction in speed yields large power savings.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:** P = 4, f = 50 Hz, s = 4% = 0.04, P_out = 7.46 kW, η = 88%

**Step 1 — Synchronous Speed (1 mark):**
$$ N_s = \frac{120 f}{P} = \frac{120 \times 50}{4} = 1500 \text{ rpm} $$

**Step 2 — Actual Rotor Speed (1 mark):**
$$ N_r = N_s (1 - s) = 1500 \times (1 - 0.04) = 1500 \times 0.96 = 1440 \text{ rpm} $$

**Step 3 — Rotor Frequency (1 mark):**
$$ f_r = s \times f = 0.04 \times 50 = 2 \text{ Hz} $$

**Step 4 — Input Power (2 marks):**
$$ \eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \Rightarrow P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{7.46}{0.88} = 8.477 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 5 — Total Losses (2 marks):**
$$ \sum \text{Losses} = P_{in} - P_{out} = 8.477 - 7.46 = 1.017 \text{ kW} $$

### Final Answers:
$$ \boxed{N_s = 1500 \text{ rpm}, \quad N_r = 1440 \text{ rpm}, \quad f_r = 2 \text{ Hz}, \quad P_{in} = 8.477 \text{ kW}} $$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the various **losses occurring in a three-phase induction motor**. Discuss how each loss varies with load. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A V-belt drive connects a 10 kW, 1440 rpm electric motor to a compressor running at 720 rpm. The diameter of the driving pulley is 200 mm and the center distance is 600 mm. Calculate: (i) the diameter of the driven pulley, (ii) the length of the belt required, and (iii) the belt velocity. Take belt slip as 2%. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Losses in 3-Phase Induction Motor (7 Marks):**

The five major losses are:

1. **Stator Copper Loss (I²R) (1.5 marks):** Heat dissipated in stator winding resistance. Varies as **square of stator current** (∝ I²). Proportional to load (current ↑ with load).

2. **Rotor Copper Loss (1.5 marks):** $P_{rc} = s \cdot P_g$. Proportional to slip. Increases approximately linearly with load since slip increases with load.

3. **Core (Iron) Loss (1.5 marks):** Hysteresis + eddy current loss in stator laminations. Approximately **constant** because stator voltage and frequency are constant, independent of load.

4. **Friction and Windage Loss (1.5 marks):** Bearing friction and rotor fan air drag. Depends only on speed — approximately **constant** with load.

5. **Stray Load Loss (1 mark):** Due to leakage flux and harmonics. Approximately proportional to **square of load current**.

**Load Dependency Summary (1 mark):**
- Constant (independent of load): Core, Friction-Windage
- Proportional to load: Rotor copper
- Proportional to (load)²: Stator copper, Stray load

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $P_{motor}$ = 10 kW, $N_1$ = 1440 rpm, $N_2$ = 720 rpm, $D_1$ = 200 mm, $C$ = 600 mm, slip = 2%

**(i) Diameter of Driven Pulley (2 marks):**
$$ \frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{D_2}{D_1} \Rightarrow D_2 = D_1 \times \frac{N_1}{N_2} = 200 \times \frac{1440}{720} = 400 \text{ mm} $$

**With 2% slip correction (1 mark):**
$$ D_2' = \frac{D_2}{(1-s)} = \frac{400}{0.98} = 408.16 \text{ mm} $$

**(ii) Belt Length (2 marks):**
$$ L = 2C + \frac{\pi(D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \frac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C} $$
$$ L = 2(600) + \frac{\pi(200 + 400)}{2} + \frac{(400-200)^2}{4(600)} $$
$$ L = 1200 + \frac{\pi(600)}{2} + \frac{(200)^2}{2400} $$
$$ L = 1200 + 300\pi + \frac{40000}{2400} $$
$$ L = 1200 + 942.48 + 16.67 = 2159.15 \text{ mm} \approx 2.16 \text{ m} $$

**(iii) Belt Velocity (2 marks):**
$$ v = \frac{\pi D_1 N_1}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.200 \times 1440}{60} = \frac{904.78}{60} = 15.08 \text{ m/s} $$

### Final Answers:
$$ \boxed{D_2 = 400 \text{ mm (ideal)}, \quad L \approx 2.16 \text{ m}, \quad v \approx 15.08 \text{ m/s}} $$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing rpm with rad/s, mm with m, N with kN. Always check: Power in W → Torque in N·m, speed in rad/s.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the √3 in 3-Phase Power (–2 marks):** For balanced 3-phase load, $P = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$. Many students write $P = VI\cos\phi$ — this is for single phase only.
>
> 3. **Confusing Slip in Motor vs. Belt (–1 to –2 marks):** Motor slip is in % (0–100%). Belt slip is also in % but much smaller (1–3%). Don't interchange formulas.
>
> 4. **Sign Convention in Eytelwein's Equation (–1 mark):** $T_1 > T_2$ (tight side has higher tension). Students sometimes invert the ratio and get wrong tension ratio.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the Cube in Affinity Law (–3 marks):** When asked "save energy by reducing speed," students often use $N^2$ instead of $N^3$. Power reduction is cubic — this is THE most important energy-saving relationship.
>
> 6. **Not Labelling Diagrams Properly (–1 to –2 marks):** VFD block diagrams without clear labels of "Rectifier → DC Link → Inverter" lose marks. Always show the conversion stages.
>
> 7. **Ignoring V/f Ratio Concept (–1 mark):** In VFD, voltage and frequency must be varied together (V/f = constant) to maintain flux — students often forget this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 HIGH-YIELD REVISION BULLETS:**
>
> ⚙️ **MOTOR FUNDAMENTALS**
> - Synchronous speed: $N_s = 120f/P$ (e.g., 4-pole, 50 Hz → 1500 rpm)
> - Slip $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$; typical full-load slip = 3–5% for standard motors
> - Rotor frequency: $f_r = s \cdot f$
> - $P_{rc} = s \cdot P_g$ → Rotor copper loss is the only loss that *inherently* depends on slip
> - Output mechanical power: $P_{out} = 2\pi N T / 60$
> - 3-phase input: $P_{in} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$
>
> 🔌 **POWER FACTOR**
> - PF = kW/kVA = cos φ
> - Lower PF → higher current → higher cable I²R losses (∝ 1/PF²)
> - PF correction: Synchronous motor (over-excited) or capacitor bank
>
> 🔗 **BELT DRIVES**
> - Velocity ratio: $i = N_1/N_2 = D_2/D_1$
> - Open belt length: $L = 2C + \pi(D_1+D_2)/2 + (D_2-D_1)^2/(4C)$
> - Power transmitted: $P = (T_1 - T_2) v$
> - Eytelwein: $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ — V-belts give higher $\theta$ → higher tension ratio
> - Belt efficiency: 95–98% (flat), 95–97% (V-belt), 97–99% (timing)
>
> 📊 **VFD / DRIVES**
> - VFD stages: **Rectifier → DC Link → Inverter** (PWM)
> - Maintain **V/f = constant** to keep flux constant
> - **Affinity law:** $P \propto N^3$ → 20% speed reduction = ~49% power saving
> - VFD savings: Best for **variable-torque** loads (fans, pumps)
> - Payback period: 6–18 months for typical fan/pump applications
>
> 🏭 **ENERGY MANAGEMENT NUMBERS TO REMEMBER**
> - Motors consume 65–70% of industrial electricity
> - IE3 motors: ~20% less loss than IE1
> - VFD on pump at 80% speed → 49% energy saved
> - Power factor penalty threshold (India): PF < 0.90 attracts penalty
> - V-belt efficiency drops 2–5% when worn → replace as preventive maintenance
>
> ✅ **EXAM QUICK-CHECK FORMULAS**
> - $N_s = 120f/P$
> - $s = (N_s - N_r)/N_s$
> - $P = 2\pi N T/60$
> - $P_{in} = \sqrt{3} V_L I_L \cos\phi$
> - $L = 2C + \pi(D_1+D_2)/2 + (D_2-D_1)^2/(4C)$
> - $P_2/P_1 = (N_2/N_1)^3$
> - $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Refrigeration System; Heat Recovery and Co-generation: Heat Recovery from Ventilation Air, Co-generation of Heat and Electricity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Refrigeration System — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refrigeration System Definition (KTU Standard):**
> A refrigeration system is a thermodynamic arrangement of interconnected components (compressor, condenser, expansion device, and evaporator) that transfers heat from a low-temperature region (cold space) to a high-temperature region (ambient/surroundings) using external work input, with the primary objective of maintaining the cold space below its surrounding temperature.

The two principal types covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:
1. **Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)** — *work-driven*, using mechanical compressors.
2. **Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)** — *heat-driven*, using thermal energy as input.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metric:** The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is the universal yardstick for refrigeration efficiency, defined as the ratio of **Desired Effect** (Refrigeration Effect) to **Work/Heat Input**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Refrigeration "Bucket" Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Imagine you are manually bailing water out of a leaky boat. You scoop water from inside (the **cold space**), carry it up and throw it overboard (the **surroundings**). The water-leak = heat ingress; the bailing effort = compressor work; the lake = ambient heat sink. The **COP** is the number of "scoops of water moved out" per "calorie of energy you spend bailing." A higher COP means you move more water per unit of effort.

### 1.3 Heat Recovery — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Recovery Definition (ASHRAE-aligned):**
> Heat recovery is the **process of capturing and reusing waste heat** from one process stream, exhaust air, or flue gas as a useful energy input for another process, thereby improving the overall energy efficiency of a system or facility.

### 1.4 Heat Recovery from Ventilation Air — Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> Buildings continuously **exhaust stale air** to maintain indoor air quality. This exhaust air, though "waste" to the indoor environment, contains **thermal energy** (sensible + latent) that was paid for by the HVAC system. A **Heat Recovery Ventilator (HRV)** or **Energy Recovery Ventilator (ERV)** transfers this energy from the outgoing exhaust to the incoming fresh air, reducing the heating/cooling load on the building.

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> Think of it like a **heat "handshake"** between two air streams. In winter, the warm stale air leaving the bathroom kisses the cold fresh air entering through a thin wall. The heat literally migrates across the wall, so the incoming air arrives pre-warmed — your heater now only has to top it up. In summer with an ERV, even the **moisture** is exchanged, reducing the cooling load.

### 1.5 Co-generation (Combined Heat and Power) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Co-generation Definition (KTU / ISO-aligned):**
> Cogeneration, also called **Combined Heat and Power (CHP)**, is the **simultaneous generation of useful electrical (or mechanical) energy and useful thermal energy** from a single primary fuel input, in a single integrated system. The principle leverages the fact that heat is an inevitable by-product of electricity generation, and recovering this heat eliminates the stack losses typical of a conventional power plant.

### 1.6 Co-generation — Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy:**
> A conventional power plant is like boiling water on a stove to make tea, but throwing away the steam that escapes. Cogeneration is like using that escaping steam to warm your room — the **same fuel bill** now produces **two useful outputs**: electricity *and* heat. A typical conventional plant wastes ~60–70% of fuel energy as low-grade heat; a CHP system can push **total fuel utilization to 70–90%**.

### 1.7 Topping vs. Bottoming Cycles

> [!NOTE]
> - **Topping Cycle:** Fuel is burnt to produce **high-temperature energy first** (electricity), and the **waste heat is recovered** for low-temperature uses. *(Most common.)*
> - **Bottoming Cycle:** Fuel is used first to produce **high-temperature heat** (industrial process), and the **waste heat is converted to electricity** via a waste-heat boiler and turbine.

### 1.8 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigeration Effect | RE | $50$–$500\ \text{kW}$ |
| COP of VCRS | $\text{COP}_{\text{VCRS}}$ | $2.0$–$4.5$ |
| COP of VARS | $\text{COP}_{\text{VARS}}$ | $0.4$–$1.2$ |
| HRV Effectiveness (Sensible) | $\varepsilon_s$ | $50$–$85\ \text{\%}$ |
| HRV Effectiveness (Total/ERV) | $\varepsilon_t$ | $50$–$80\ \text{\%}$ |
| CHP Electrical Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{el}}$ | $25$–$45\ \text{\%}$ |
| CHP Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{th}}$ | $40$–$60\ \text{\%}$ |
| CHP Total Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{total}}$ | $70$–$90\ \text{\%}$ |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{\text{atm}}$ | $\mathbf{101.325\ \text{kPa}}$ |
| Latent heat of vaporization of water (100 °C) | $h_{fg}$ | $\mathbf{2257\ \text{kJ/kg}}$ |

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) — Operating Principle

The ideal VCRS consists of four components operating in a closed thermodynamic loop with a **single-component, two-phase working fluid** (refrigerant such as R-134a, R-410A, or R-22 historically).

**The Four Processes:**

1. **Process 1→2: Compression (Isentropic, ideal)**
   - Low-pressure, low-temperature **saturated/superheated vapour** at evaporator exit is compressed to high-pressure, high-temperature **superheated vapour**.
   - Component: Compressor (work input $W_{\text{comp}}$).

2. **Process 2→3: Condensation (Isobaric heat rejection)**
   - High-pressure superheated vapour rejects heat to the surroundings, condensing to **saturated liquid**.
   - Component: Condenser (heat rejection $Q_{\text{cond}}$).

3. **Process 3→4: Expansion (Isenthalpic throttling)**
   - High-pressure saturated liquid passes through the expansion valve, becoming a **low-pressure, low-temperature two-phase mixture** (flash gas + liquid).
   - Component: Throttle/Expansion valve (no work, no heat transfer — but entropy increases).

4. **Process 4→1: Evaporation (Isobaric heat absorption)**
   - Low-pressure two-phase mixture absorbs heat from the cold space, fully evaporating to **saturated vapour** at state 1.
   - Component: Evaporator (heat absorption $Q_{\text{evap}} = RE$).

**Why the cycle works (the "How"):**
> The refrigerant is the **thermal "courier."** It is *colder* than the cold space at the evaporator (so heat flows in) and *hotter* than ambient at the condenser (so heat flows out). The compressor's mechanical work is what forces this "uphill" heat flow against the natural temperature gradient — analogous to pumping water uphill.

### 2.2 Energetics of the VCRS (First-Law Balance)

- **Refrigeration Effect:** $RE = h_1 - h_4\ [\text{kJ/kg}]$
- **Compressor Work:** $w_c = h_2 - h_1\ [\text{kJ/kg}]$
- **Condenser Heat Rejection:** $Q_{\text{cond}} = h_2 - h_3\ [\text{kJ/kg}]$
- **Energy Balance (closed cycle):** $RE + w_c = Q_{\text{cond}}$

### 2.3 Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a Refrigerator

$$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **COP vs. Efficiency:** COP can (and routinely does) **exceed 1.0**, which is NOT a violation of the Second Law. It only means that 1 kW of work moves more than 1 kW of heat — which is precisely the business of a heat pump/refrigerator.

### 2.4 Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) — Operating Principle

The VARS replaces the mechanical compressor with a **thermal compressor** consisting of:
- **Absorber** (refrigerant vapour is absorbed by a liquid absorbent → weak solution),
- **Pump** (weak solution is pressurised),
- **Generator** (heat input $Q_{\text{gen}}$ drives refrigerant out of solution → strong solution returns to absorber),
- **Rectifier** (purifies refrigerant vapour),
- **Condenser, Expansion Valve, Evaporator** (function as in VCRS).

> [!TIP]
> **Why use absorption instead of compression?**
> VARS can run on **waste heat**, **solar thermal**, or **low-grade steam**. It has **no moving mechanical compressor** in the main refrigerant loop — only a tiny liquid pump. The trade-off: much **lower COP** (typically 0.5–1.0 vs. 3.0+ for VCRS).

**COP of an ideal VARS (using temperature limits):**

$$\text{COP}_{\text{VARS,ideal}} = \left(\frac{T_E}{T_G - T_A}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{T_G - T_C}{T_C - T_E}\right)$$

where $T_E$ = evaporator, $T_C$ = condenser, $T_G$ = generator, $T_A$ = absorber (all in Kelvin).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Refrigeration Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are board-exam standard. **Memorise and apply with units.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Refrigeration Effect | $RE = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)$ | kW | $\dot{m}$ = refrigerant mass flow rate (kg/s) |
| 2 | Compressor Work | $W_c = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Per unit mass: $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ |
| 3 | Condenser Heat Rejection | $Q_{\text{cond}} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_3)$ | kW | First-law balance: $Q_{\text{cond}} = RE + W_c$ |
| 4 | Expansion Process (Throttling) | $h_3 = h_4$ | kJ/kg | Isenthalpic — no work, no heat transfer |
| 5 | COP of VCRS (Cooling) | $\text{COP} = \frac{RE}{W_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | Always > 1 for a real refrigerator |
| 6 | COP of Heat Pump (Heating) | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_{\text{cond}}}{W_c} = \text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} + 1$ | dimensionless | Useful when heating is the goal |
| 7 | Carnot COP (Theoretical Limit) | $\text{COP}_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | $T_L,\ T_H$ in **Kelvin**; second-law benchmark |
| 8 | COP of VARS (ideal) | $\text{COP}_{\text{VARS}} = \frac{T_E(T_C - T_A)}{T_C(T_G - T_E) + T_E(T_G - T_A)}$ | dimensionless | Multiplier from heat input ratio |
| 9 | Volumetric Refrigeration Effect | $VRE = \rho_1(h_1 - h_4)$ | kJ/m³ | Used to size compressor displacement |
| 10 | Specific Work (per kg) | $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Determines compressor power |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Tip for Table Safety:** The expressions above use fractions, not absolute-value bars. **Never** write `|x|` inside a markdown table cell — use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead to avoid breaking table rendering.

### 2.6 Heat Recovery from Ventilation Air — Deep Theory

Ventilation air is a **major energy sink** in commercial buildings. Building codes (ASHRAE 62.1, ECBC-2017) mandate a minimum outdoor-air intake rate, but heating/cooling this air to indoor comfort conditions consumes 30–50% of total HVAC energy in cold-climate buildings.

**Energy Exchange Modes in Air-to-Air Heat Exchangers:**

1. **Sensible-only recovery (HRV):** Transfers **temperature only** via fin/tube, plate, or rotary sensible exchangers. Moisture is *not* transferred.
2. **Total (sensible + latent) recovery (ERV):** Transfers **both temperature and moisture** via membrane, desiccant-coated rotary, or enthalpy wheels. Especially valuable in hot-humid climates.

**Key Performance Parameter: Effectiveness (ε)**

The effectiveness of a heat recovery device is defined as:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\text{Actual heat transferred}}{\text{Maximum possible heat transfer}}$$

For a balanced air-to-air exchanger (equal mass flow rates $\dot{m}_s = \dot{m}_e$ on supply and exhaust sides):

$$\varepsilon_s = \frac{T_{\text{supply,out}} - T_{\text{supply,in}}}{T_{\text{exhaust,out}} - T_{\text{supply,in}}} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_{1s}}{T_{1e} - T_{1s}}$$

For unbalanced flows, the **NTU method** is used:

$$\varepsilon_s = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\text{NTU} \cdot (1 - C_r)\right] \quad \text{(for parallel-flow, balanced to slightly unbalanced)}$$

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{UA}{\dot{m}_{\min} c_p}, \quad C_r = \frac{\dot{m}_{\min} c_p}{\dot{m}_{\max} c_p}$$

### 2.7 Types of Air-to-Air Heat Recovery Devices

| Device Type | Mechanism | Sensible Recovery | Latent Recovery | Typical ε_s | ε_t |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Plate / Cross-flow** | Conduction through plates | Yes | No | 50–80% | — |
| **Heat Pipe** | Two-phase refrigerant loop | Yes | No | 40–70% | — |
| **Run-around Coil** | Two coils + pumped glycol | Yes | No | 45–65% | — |
| **Fixed Plate Membrane (ERV)** | Permeable membrane | Yes | Yes | 50–80% | 50–75% |
| **Rotary / Enthalpy Wheel** | Rotating matrix | Yes | Yes | 70–90% | 60–80% |
| **Desiccant Wheel + HRV** | Adsorption + sensible | Yes | Yes | 70–85% | 60–80% |

### 2.8 Co-generation (CHP) — Deep Theory

A CHP system is characterised by three **efficiency metrics**:

**1. Electrical (Power) Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{\text{el}} = \frac{P_{\text{el}}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{NCV}_f} = \frac{\text{Electrical Output}}{\text{Fuel Energy Input}}$$

**2. Thermal (Heat) Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{NCV}_f} = \frac{\text{Useful Heat Recovered}}{\text{Fuel Energy Input}}$$

**3. Total (Overall) Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{\text{total}} = \eta_{\text{el}} + \eta_{\text{th}}$$

**4. Energy Utilization Factor (EUF):**
$$\text{EUF} = \frac{P_{\text{el}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{NCV}_f}$$

**5. Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR):**
$$\text{HPR} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{el}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The HPR is THE key design parameter for selecting a CHP prime mover.** A hospital needing steam + electricity has HPR ≈ 1.5–2.5; a data centre needs HPR ≈ 0.5–1.0.

### 2.9 KTU Formula Sheet — Heat Recovery & Cogeneration

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Key Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensible Effectiveness (Air-Air) | $\varepsilon_s = \frac{T_{2s} - T_{1s}}{T_{1e} - T_{1s}}$ | dimensionless | $T$ in °C or K (same) |
| 2 | Latent Effectiveness | $\varepsilon_l = \frac{w_{2s} - w_{1s}}{w_{1e} - w_{1s}}$ | dimensionless | $w$ = humidity ratio (kg/kg) |
| 3 | Energy Saved (Sensible) | $Q_{\text{saved}} = \varepsilon_s \cdot \dot{m} c_p (T_{1e} - T_{1s})$ | kW | Use $c_p \approx 1.005$ kJ/kg·K for dry air |
| 4 | Energy Saved (Total/ERV) | $Q_{\text{saved}} = \varepsilon_t \cdot \dot{m}(h_{1e} - h_{1s})$ | kW | Use moist-air enthalpy $h = 1.005T + w(2501 + 1.88T)$ |
| 5 | CHP Electrical Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{el}} = \frac{P_{\text{el}}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{NCV}_f}$ | dimensionless, % | |
| 6 | CHP Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{NCV}_f}$ | dimensionless, % | |
| 7 | CHP Total Efficiency | $\eta_{\text{total}} = \eta_{\text{el}} + \eta_{\text{th}}$ | dimensionless, % | Typically 70–90% |
| 8 | Heat-to-Power Ratio | $\text{HPR} = \dot{Q}_{\text{useful}} \big/ P_{\text{el}}$ | dimensionless | Prime-mover selection criterion |
| 9 | Energy Utilization Factor | $\text{EUF} = (P_{\text{el}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}) \big/ (\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{NCV}_f)$ | dimensionless | Same as $\eta_{\text{total}}$ for single-fuel CHP |
| 10 | Fuel Savings vs. Separate Generation | $\Delta F = F_{\text{sep}} - F_{\text{CHP}}$ | kW (thermal input) | $F_{\text{sep}} = P_{\text{el}}/\eta_{\text{el,sep}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}/\eta_{\text{boiler}}$ |
| 11 | Net Fuel-Saving Ratio (NFSR) | $\text{NFSR} = \Delta F \big/ F_{\text{sep}}$ | dimensionless | Higher NFSR = more beneficial CHP |
| 12 | Prime Mover Power Output | $P_{\text{el}} = \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot \eta_{\text{m}} \cdot \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{NCV}_f$ | kW | $\eta_{\text{gen}}$ = generator, $\eta_{\text{m}}$ = mechanical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - HRV/ERV systems are **mandatory in ECBC+ and IGBC-rated green buildings** in India.
> - CHP systems are standard in sugar mills, paper mills, refineries, district heating, hospitals, and data centres — wherever the **HPR of the demand** matches the **HPR of the prime mover**.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: COP of an Ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration System

> **Goal:** Starting from the **first law for a cyclic device** and the **definition of COP**, derive the closed-form expression $\text{COP} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$.

**Step 1 — Define the system boundary.**
We enclose the **compressor + condenser + expansion valve + evaporator** loop. Since the cycle is **closed and steady-flow**, the cyclic integral of $\Delta h$ is zero:
$$\oint dh = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_1 \to h_2 \to h_3 \to h_4 \to h_1\ \text{returns to the same state.}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-flow energy equation (SFEE) to the compressor.**
The compressor is adiabatic (ideal) and has no significant $\Delta KE$, $\Delta PE$:

$$w_c = h_2 - h_1$$

*(This is the specific work input per kg of refrigerant; multiply by $\dot{m}$ for total power.)*

**Step 3 — Apply SFEE to the evaporator.**
The evaporator operates at constant pressure, with heat input $q_L$ and no work:

$$q_L = h_1 - h_4 \equiv RE$$

**Step 4 — Apply the throttling assumption at the expansion valve.**
Real throttling is isenthalpic (no work, no heat transfer, no $\Delta KE$, no $\Delta PE$):

$$h_3 = h_4$$

**Step 5 — Define COP as desired output ÷ required input.**

$$\text{COP} = \frac{q_L}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$$

**Step 6 — First-law check (condenser).**
Apply SFEE to the condenser to confirm the cycle balance:

$$q_H = h_2 - h_3 = h_2 - h_4 = h_2 - h_3$$

Substitute $h_3 = h_4$ and $RE = h_1 - h_4$:

$$q_H = (h_2 - h_1) + (h_1 - h_4) = w_c + RE$$

Therefore: $\boxed{\ q_H = w_c + q_L\ }$ — confirming **the condenser must reject the sum of the refrigeration effect plus the compressor work**, exactly as required by the first law. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Sensible Effectiveness of a Balanced Air-to-Air Heat Exchanger

> **Goal:** Derive $\varepsilon_s = \frac{T_{2s} - T_{1s}}{T_{1e} - T_{1s}}$ for an HRV with equal mass flow rates on both streams.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the exchanger (steady, no losses to surroundings).**
Heat lost by exhaust stream = heat gained by supply stream:

$$\dot{m}_e c_p (T_{1e} - T_{2e}) = \dot{m}_s c_p (T_{2s} - T_{1s})$$

For a **balanced** exchanger, $\dot{m}_e = \dot{m}_s \equiv \dot{m}$, so:

$$T_{1e} - T_{2e} = T_{2s} - T_{1s}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate $T_{2e}$.**

$$T_{2e} = T_{1e} - (T_{2s} - T_{1s}) = T_{1e} + T_{1s} - T_{2s}$$

**Step 3 — Define effectiveness as actual heat ÷ maximum possible heat.**
The maximum possible heat transfer occurs if the supply air is heated to the inlet temperature of the exhaust air, i.e., $T_{2s,\max} = T_{1e}$:

$$\varepsilon_s = \frac{T_{2s} - T_{1s}}{T_{1e} - T_{1s}}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with the exhaust side definition.**

The same $\varepsilon_s$ can equivalently be written using exhaust temperatures:

$$\varepsilon_s = \frac{T_{1e} - T_{2e}}{T_{1e} - T_{1s}}$$

To prove this is consistent, substitute $T_{2e}$ from Step 2:

$$\varepsilon_s = \frac{T_{1e} - (T_{1e} + T_{1s} - T_{2s})}{T_{1e} - T_{1s}} = \frac{T_{2s} - T_{1s}}{T_{1e} - T_{1s}}$$

This matches Step 3 exactly — confirming the definition is self-consistent. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Total CHP Fuel Savings vs. Separate Generation

> **Goal:** Show that CHP saves fuel whenever $\eta_{\text{el,CHP}} + \eta_{\text{th,CHP}} > \eta_{\text{el,sep}} \cdot \eta_{\text{boiler}}$ — the classic CHP viability criterion.

**Step 1 — Define the baseline (separate generation).**
In a conventional setup, electricity is produced in a power plant (efficiency $\eta_{\text{el,sep}}$) and heat is produced in a boiler (efficiency $\eta_{\text{boiler}}$). To deliver $P_{\text{el}}$ kW of electricity and $\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}$ kW of heat, the fuel inputs are:

$$F_{\text{el,sep}} = \frac{P_{\text{el}}}{\eta_{\text{el,sep}}}, \qquad F_{\text{heat,sep}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{\eta_{\text{boiler}}}$$

$$F_{\text{sep}} = F_{\text{el,sep}} + F_{\text{heat,sep}} = \frac{P_{\text{el}}}{\eta_{\text{el,sep}}} + \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{\eta_{\text{boiler}}}$$

**Step 2 — Define the CHP fuel consumption.**
In a CHP system, the same $P_{\text{el}}$ and $\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}$ are produced from a single fuel stream:

$$F_{\text{CHP}} = \frac{P_{\text{el}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{\eta_{\text{total,CHP}}} = \frac{P_{\text{el}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{\eta_{\text{el,CHP}} + \eta_{\text{th,CHP}}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute fuel savings.**

$$\Delta F = F_{\text{sep}} - F_{\text{CHP}} = \frac{P_{\text{el}}}{\eta_{\text{el,sep}}} + \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{\eta_{\text{boiler}}} - \frac{P_{\text{el}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{\eta_{\text{el,CHP}} + \eta_{\text{th,CHP}}}$$

**Step 4 — Condition for positive savings ($\Delta F > 0$).**
Rearranging, the savings are positive if and only if:

$$\eta_{\text{el,CHP}} + \eta_{\text{th,CHP}} > \frac{(P_{\text{el}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{useful}})\ \eta_{\text{el,sep}}\ \eta_{\text{boiler}}}{P_{\text{el}}\ \eta_{\text{boiler}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}\ \eta_{\text{el,sep}}}$$

This is the rigorous **CHP viability criterion.** In the special (and very common) case where $\eta_{\text{el,sep}} = \eta_{\text{el,CHP}} = \eta_{\text{el}}$ and $\eta_{\text{boiler}} = 1$, it simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\ \eta_{\text{el,CHP}} + \eta_{\text{th,CHP}} > 1\ }$$

— i.e., the CHP total efficiency must exceed 100% **only when the separate-generation electricity efficiency is 100%**, which is the trivial case. In real engineering, the criterion is the full inequality above. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Problem-Solving Templates

> [!NOTE]
> The following templates are the **exact KTU 2024 valuation pattern.** Use them to structure 14-mark answers.

**Template 1: VCRS COP Problem (Standard 14-Mark Format)**

| Step | Action | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | State given data (states 1, 2, 3, 4 enthalpy from P–h chart/table) | $h_1, h_2, h_3, h_4$ |
| 2 | Note throttling relation | $h_3 = h_4$ |
| 3 | Compute $RE$ per kg | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ |
| 4 | Compute $w_c$ per kg | $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ |
| 5 | Compute COP | $\text{COP} = RE/w_c$ |
| 6 | Mass flow rate (if $RE_{\text{tonne}}$ given) | $\dot{m} = RE_{\text{req}}/(h_1 - h_4)$ |
| 7 | Compressor power | $W_c = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ |
| 8 | Condenser heat | $Q_{\text{cond}} = W_c + RE$ |
| 9 | State assumptions and final answer with units | |

**Template 2: HRV Effectiveness Problem (Standard 7-Mark Format)**

| Step | Action | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify supply (cold, outdoor) and exhaust (warm, indoor) streams | $T_{1s}, T_{1e}$ |
| 2 | Compute temperature difference available | $\Delta T_{\max} = T_{1e} - T_{1s}$ |
| 3 | Apply effectiveness formula | $\varepsilon_s = (T_{2s} - T_{1s}) / (T_{1e} - T_{1s})$ |
| 4 | Compute supply outlet temperature | $T_{2s} = T_{1s} + \varepsilon_s (T_{1e} - T_{1s})$ |
| 5 | Compute energy saved | $Q_{\text{saved}} = \dot{m} c_p (T_{2s} - T_{1s})$ |
| 6 | Annual energy saving (if operating hours given) | $E_{\text{annual}} = Q_{\text{saved}} \times \text{hours}$ |

**Template 3: CHP Efficiency Problem (Standard 7-Mark Format)**

| Step | Action | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify fuel input and outputs (electrical, thermal) | $\dot{m}_f, \text{NCV}, P_{\text{el}}, \dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}$ |
| 2 | Compute fuel energy input | $F = \dot{m}_f \cdot \text{NCV}$ |
| 3 | Compute $\eta_{\text{el}}$ | $\eta_{\text{el}} = P_{\text{el}} / F$ |
| 4 | Compute $\eta_{\text{th}}$ | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{useful}} / F$ |
| 5 | Compute $\eta_{\text{total}}$ | $\eta_{\text{total}} = \eta_{\text{el}} + \eta_{\text{th}}$ |
| 6 | Compute HPR | $\text{HPR} = \dot{Q}_{\text{useful}} / P_{\text{el}}$ |
| 7 | Compare with separate generation | $\Delta F = F_{\text{sep}} - F_{\text{CHP}}$ |
