## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle — Block Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Evaporator\nLow P, Low T\nRE Absorbed] --> B[Compressor\nIsentropic\nWork Input Wc]
    B --> C[Condenser\nHigh P Heat\nRejected Qh]
    C --> D[Expansion Valve\nIsenthalpic\nh3 equals h4]
    D --> A
    A --- A1[State 1: Saturated Vapour\nh1, P_evap]
    B --- B1[State 2: Superheated Vapour\nh2, P_cond]
    C --- C1[State 3: Saturated Liquid\nh3, P_cond]
    D --- D1[State 4: Two Phase\nh4 = h3, P_evap]
    A1 --> B1 --> C1 --> D1 --> A1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** States 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 trace the refrigerant through the cycle. The arrows on the outer loop show the direction of refrigerant flow; the energy transfers ($RE$, $W_c$, $Q_H$) occur at the four components.

### 4.2 Heat Recovery Ventilator (HRV) — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Outdoor[OUTDOOR AIR SIDE]
        O1[Fresh Outdoor Air\nTs1 = minus 5 degC] -->|Supply fan| HX
    end
    subgraph Indoor[INDOOR AIR SIDE]
        R1[Stale Room Air\nTe1 = 22 degC] -->|Exhaust fan| HX
    end
    HX[Air to Air Heat Exchanger\nSensible Recovery Only] -->|Pre-warmed supply| SP[Supply Air to Room\nTs2 = 12 degC]
    HX -->|Cooled exhaust| EX[Exhaust Air to Outside\nTe2 = 5 degC]
    HX --- EFF[Effectiveness epsilon = 77 percent]
    HX --- NTU[NTU Method Governing]
```

### 4.3 Energy Recovery Ventilator (ERV) with Latent Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    OA[Outdoor Air\nHot and Humid\n32 degC, 80 percent RH] --> ENW[Enthalpy Wheel\nRotating Matrix]
    ENW --> SA[Supply Air to Room\nPre-cooled and Dehumidified]
    ENW --> EA[Exhaust Air from Room\n24 degC, 50 percent RH]
    EA --> ENW
    ENW --- S1[Sensible Recovery epsilon s]
    ENW --- L1[Latent Recovery epsilon l]
    ENW --- S2[Total Recovery epsilon t = 0.7 to 0.8]
```

### 4.4 Cogeneration (CHP) — Topping Cycle Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FUEL[Fuel Input\nNatural Gas or Biomass\ndot mf times NCV] --> PM[Prime Mover\nGas Turbine or Engine or Steam Turbine]
    PM --> GEN[Electrical Generator]
    GEN --> GRID[Electricity Output Pel\nEta el = 30 to 45 percent]
    PM --> HRSG[Heat Recovery\nSteam Generator or HX]
    HRSG --> PROC[Process Heat Output Qth\nEta th = 40 to 60 percent]
    HRSG --- COND[Optional Condenser Flue Path]
    PROC --- END[End User\nSteam, Hot Water, Absorption Chiller]
    PM --- WASTE[Stack Losses\n3 to 8 percent]
```

### 4.5 Comparison: Topping vs. Bottoming CHP Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Topping[TOPPING CYCLE]
        T1[Fuel] --> T2[High T Energy\nElectricity First] --> T3[Waste Heat Recovery\nLow T Process Heat]
    end
    subgraph Bottoming[BOTTOMING CYCLE]
        B1[Fuel] --> B2[High T Process Heat First] --> B3[Waste Heat Boiler] --> B4[Steam Turbine\nElectricity]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two cycles are **mirror images**. Topping dominates industrial CHP because high-temperature electricity generation is more thermodynamically valuable than low-temperature heat recovery.

### 4.6 Energy Flow Sankey — Conventional vs. CHP

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Conventional[CONVENTIONAL: Power Plant plus Boiler]
        F1[Fuel 100 units] --> P1[Power Plant\neta el = 35 percent] --> E1[Electricity 35 units]
        F1 --> B1[Boiler\neta = 85 percent] --> H1[Heat 42.5 units]
        F1 --> LS1[Losses 22.5 units]
    end
    subgraph CHP[CHP SYSTEM]
        F2[Fuel 100 units] --> PM2[CHP Prime Mover] --> E2[Electricity 35 units]
        PM2 --> HR2[Heat Recovery] --> H2[Useful Heat 50 units]
        F2 --> LS2[Losses 15 units]
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** Both cases deliver 35 units of electricity. But **conventional** requires 100 units of fuel for electricity **plus** ~50 units for heat (total 150 units). **CHP** delivers both from only 100 units — a **33% net fuel saving** for the same end-use.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
> **Distinguish between a Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) and a Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS). Mention any two merits and two demerits of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | VCRS | VARS |
|---|---|---|
| Energy input | Mechanical (electrical) work | Thermal (heat) energy |
| Main component | Mechanical compressor | Thermal compressor (absorber + generator + pump) |
| COP | High (2–4.5) | Low (0.4–1.2) |
| Moving parts in refrigerant loop | Many (compressor) | Few (only a small liquid pump) |
| Noise | High (compressor) | Very low (silent) |
| Fuel flexibility | Needs electricity | Can use solar, waste heat, biomass, steam |

**Merits of VCRS:** (i) High COP, (ii) Compact for given capacity, (iii) Well-developed technology.
**Demerits of VCRS:** (i) Uses HCFC/HFC refrigerants (ODP/GWP issues), (ii) Mechanical wear and noise.
**Merits of VARS:** (i) Uses low-grade/waste heat, (ii) No ozone-depleting refrigerant (typically LiBr–H₂O or NH₃–H₂O), (iii) Quiet operation, suitable for solar cooling.
**Demerits of VARS:** (i) Low COP, (ii) Bulky equipment, (iii) Crystallisation of absorbent at low loads.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
> **Define "Co-generation." With a neat block diagram, explain a topping cycle co-generation system.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Co-generation (Combined Heat and Power, CHP) is the simultaneous generation of useful electrical (or mechanical) energy **and** useful thermal energy from a single fuel source in an integrated system, achieving overall fuel-utilisation efficiencies of 70–90% (compared with 30–45% for conventional power-only plants).

**Topping cycle — block flow:**
Fuel → Prime Mover (Gas Turbine / Engine / Steam Turbine) → Electricity
                                          ↓
                                    Waste Heat Recovery (HRSG/HX)
                                          ↓
                                    Useful Heat (Steam / Hot Water / Absorption Chilling)

**Key Features:**
- High-grade energy (electricity) is generated first; low-grade waste heat is recovered.
- Most widely used configuration in industry (sugar, paper, textile, chemical).
- Prime movers: gas turbines (HPR ≈ 1.5–2.5), reciprocating engines (HPR ≈ 1.0–2.0), steam turbines (HPR ≈ 0.5–1.0 for backpressure, 0.1–0.3 for condensing-extraction).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ESE Module Internal Choice Format:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

> **(a)** A vapour compression refrigeration cycle uses R-134a as the refrigerant. The evaporator operates at $-10\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and the condenser at $40\ ^\circ\text{C}$. The refrigerant is **saturated vapour at compressor inlet** and **saturated liquid at condenser exit**. The isentropic compression delivers the vapour to $0.7\ \text{MPa}$. Using the R-134a property table, determine:
> (i) Enthalpies at all four state points.
> (ii) Refrigeration effect per kg.
> (iii) Compressor work per kg.
> (iv) COP of the cycle.

> **(b)** A heat pump operating on the same cycle is used to heat a building. The building requires $15\ \text{kW}$ of heating. Calculate:
> (i) The COP of the heat pump.
> (ii) The compressor power input.
> (iii) The condenser heat rejection rate.

**Use the following R-134a data (typical KTU textbook values):**

| State | Condition | $h$ (kJ/kg) | $s$ (kJ/kg·K) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Saturated vapour at $-10\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | $h_1 = 244.50$ | $s_1 = 0.9372$ |
| 2s | Superheated vapour at $0.7\ \text{MPa}$, $s_2 = s_1$ | $h_{2s} = 268.00$ | $s_{2s} = 0.9372$ |
| 3 | Saturated liquid at $0.7\ \text{MPa}$ | $h_3 = 99.56$ | — |
| 4 | $h_4 = h_3$ | $h_4 = 99.56$ | — |

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**(i) Enthalpies at all four state points** *(1 mark)*

$$h_1 = 244.50\ \text{kJ/kg}, \quad h_2 = 268.00\ \text{kJ/kg}, \quad h_3 = 99.56\ \text{kJ/kg}, \quad h_4 = h_3 = 99.56\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**(ii) Refrigeration Effect per kg** *(2 marks)*

$$RE = h_1 - h_4 = 244.50 - 99.56 = 144.94\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**(iii) Compressor work per kg** *(2 marks)*

$$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 268.00 - 244.50 = 23.50\ \text{kJ/kg}$$

**(iv) COP of the cycle** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} = \frac{RE}{w_c} = \frac{144.94}{23.50} = 6.167 \approx 6.17$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) COP of the heat pump** *(3 marks)*

For a heat pump, the desired output is the condenser heat (heating), not the refrigeration effect:

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = \frac{Q_{\text{cond}}}{w_c} = \frac{RE + w_c}{w_c} = \text{COP}_{\text{ref}} + 1$$

$$\text{COP}_{HP} = 6.17 + 1 = 7.17$$

**(ii) Compressor power input** *(2 marks)*

$$W_c = \frac{Q_{\text{heating}}}{\text{COP}_{HP}} = \frac{15.0}{7.17} = 2.092\ \text{kW}$$

**(iii) Condenser heat rejection rate** *(2 marks)*

The condenser must reject the sum of useful heat and compressor work — this is the heat supplied to the building:

$$Q_{\text{cond}} = W_c + RE_{\text{rate}} = 2.092 + 15.0 = 17.092\ \text{kW}$$

(Sanity check: $\text{COP}_{HP} = Q_{\text{cond}}/W_c = 17.092/2.092 = 8.17$ — wait, this contradicts (i). Correction: the heating rate IS $Q_{\text{cond}}$ by definition, so $W_c = Q_{\text{cond}}/\text{COP}_{HP}$ and the building receives $Q_{\text{cond}} = 15.0$ kW. Therefore:)

$$W_c = 15.0/7.17 = 2.092\ \text{kW}, \qquad Q_{\text{cond}} = 15.0\ \text{kW (supplied to building)}, \qquad \dot{m} = W_c/w_c = 2.092/23.50 = 0.0890\ \text{kg/s}$$

$$RE_{\text{rate}} = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4) = 0.0890 \times 144.94 = 12.90\ \text{kW (drawn from outdoor cold air)}$$

$$Q_{\text{cond}} = RE_{\text{rate}} + W_c = 12.90 + 2.092 = 14.99 \approx 15.0\ \text{kW}\ \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (VCRS):**
> 1. **Most common mark-loser:** Forgetting the throttling relation $h_3 = h_4$. Always state it explicitly — examiners award 1 mark for it.
> 2. **Heat pump vs. refrigeration COP confusion:** Students often compute $\text{COP}_{HP} = RE/w_c$ instead of $Q_{\text{cond}}/w_c$. Read the question carefully: "**for heating**" means $Q_{\text{cond}}$ is the output.
> 3. **Units:** Never mix kJ/kg with kW — convert via mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ when needed.
> 4. **Isentropic vs. actual compression:** Real compressors have isentropic efficiency $\eta_s = (h_{2s} - h_1)/(h_{2a} - h_1) < 1$; if the problem states "isentropic," use $h_{2s}$ directly.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

> **(a)** A hospital's ventilation system handles $5.0\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ of supply air and an equal volume of exhaust air at standard atmospheric conditions. In winter, the outdoor air is at $5\ ^\circ\text{C}$ and the indoor exhaust air is at $24\ ^\circ\text{C}$. A **sensible-only cross-flow plate heat exchanger (HRV)** with effectiveness $\varepsilon_s = 0.70$ is installed. Calculate:
> (i) The temperature of the pre-warmed supply air entering the building.
> (ii) The heat recovered by the HRV (in kW).
> (iii) The annual energy saved (in kWh), assuming the system operates for $4000$ hours/year.
> (iv) The annual cost saving (in ₹) if electricity costs ₹8.00/kWh for an electric heating system.

> **(b)** A co-generation system uses a gas engine as the prime mover. The fuel input is $1000\ \text{kW}$ (NCV basis, natural gas). The engine produces $350\ \text{kW}$ of electricity. The jacket cooling and exhaust heat recovery unit delivers $480\ \text{kW}$ of useful thermal energy (hot water at $90\ ^\circ\text{C}$). Calculate:
> (i) Electrical efficiency.
> (ii) Thermal efficiency.
> (iii) Total (overall) efficiency.
> (iv) Heat-to-Power Ratio.
> (v) The annual fuel cost saving compared to separate generation (electricity from grid at $33\%$ efficiency, heat from a boiler at $85\%$ efficiency) if the system operates for $6000$ hours/year and gas costs ₹6.00/kWh (thermal).

**Take the air density as $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.20\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $c_p = 1.005\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$.**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**(i) Pre-warmed supply air temperature** *(2 marks)*

$$\varepsilon_s = \frac{T_{2s} - T_{1s}}{T_{1e} - T_{1s}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{2s} = T_{1s} + \varepsilon_s (T_{1e} - T_{1s})$$

$$T_{2s} = 5 + 0.70 \times (24 - 5) = 5 + 0.70 \times 19 = 5 + 13.3 = 18.3\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**(ii) Heat recovered by the HRV** *(2 marks)*

$$\dot{m} = \rho \dot{V} = 1.20 \times 5.0 = 6.0\ \text{kg/s}$$

$$Q_{\text{recovered}} = \dot{m} c_p (T_{2s} - T_{1s}) = 6.0 \times 1.005 \times (18.3 - 5)$$

$$Q_{\text{recovered}} = 6.0 \times 1.005 \times 13.3 = 80.20\ \text{kW}$$

**(iii) Annual energy saved** *(1 mark)*

$$E_{\text{annual}} = Q_{\text{recovered}} \times \text{hours} = 80.20 \times 4000 = 320{,}800\ \text{kWh/year}$$

**(iv) Annual cost saving** *(2 marks)*

$$\text{Cost saving} = 320{,}800 \times 8.00 = \text{₹}\ 25{,}66{,}400\ \text{per year} \approx \text{₹}\ 25.66\ \text{lakhs/year}$$

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Electrical efficiency** *(1 mark)*

$$\eta_{\text{el}} = \frac{P_{\text{el}}}{F} = \frac{350}{1000} = 0.35 = 35\%$$

**(ii) Thermal efficiency** *(1 mark)*

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{F} = \frac{480}{1000} = 0.48 = 48\%$$

**(iii) Total (overall) efficiency** *(1 mark)*

$$\eta_{\text{total}} = \eta_{\text{el}} + \eta_{\text{th}} = 35 + 48 = 83\%$$

**(iv) Heat-to-Power Ratio** *(1 mark)*

$$\text{HPR} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{P_{\text{el}}} = \frac{480}{350} = 1.371$$

**(v) Annual fuel cost saving vs. separate generation** *(3 marks)*

**Step 1 — Compute separate-generation fuel input.**

$$F_{\text{el,sep}} = \frac{P_{\text{el}}}{\eta_{\text{el,sep}}} = \frac{350}{0.33} = 1060.6\ \text{kW (thermal)}$$

$$F_{\text{heat,sep}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}}{\eta_{\text{boiler}}} = \frac{480}{0.85} = 564.7\ \text{kW (thermal)}$$

$$F_{\text{sep}} = 1060.6 + 564.7 = 1625.3\ \text{kW (thermal)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute CHP fuel input.**

$$F_{\text{CHP}} = 1000\ \text{kW (thermal)}$$

**Step 3 — Annual fuel saved.**

$$\Delta F = F_{\text{sep}} - F_{\text{CHP}} = 1625.3 - 1000 = 625.3\ \text{kW (thermal)}$$

$$E_{\text{fuel saved}} = 625.3 \times 6000 = 3{,}751{,}800\ \text{kWh (thermal)/year}$$

**Step 4 — Annual cost saving.**

$$\text{Cost saving} = 3{,}751{,}800 \times 6.00 = \text{₹}\ 2{,}25{,}10{,}800\ \text{per year} \approx \text{₹}\ 2.25\ \text{crores/year}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (HRV/CHP):**
> 1. **Sign convention in HRV problems:** Always identify which stream is supply (cold, $T_{1s}$) and which is exhaust (warm, $T_{1e}$). Mixing them gives negative effectiveness — instant zero.
> 2. **Forgetting air density $\rho$:** Many students use $\dot{V}$ directly in the heat equation. The mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho \dot{V}$ is mandatory; $c_p$ is per **unit mass**, not per unit volume.
> 3. **Total efficiency $\ne$ product:** A common student error is $\eta_{\text{total}} = \eta_{\text{el}} \times \eta_{\text{th}}$ — WRONG. It is the **sum** because both outputs come from the same fuel.
> 4. **HPR vs. Efficiency:** HPR is **not** a measure of efficiency — it is a measure of how heat-heavy the application is relative to its electricity demand. Don't confuse with EUF.
> 5. **Comparing separate generation to CHP:** Always use a consistent fuel basis (NCV for gas, GCV for coal) when comparing, or marks will be deducted in 14-mark problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CARD — KTU 2024 Module 4**

#### **A. Refrigeration Systems**
- ☐ VCRS = work-driven; VARS = heat-driven.
- ☐ The 4 components of VCRS: **Compressor → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator.**
- ☐ Throttling: $h_3 = h_4$ (always — state explicitly in exam).
- ☐ $RE = h_1 - h_4$; $W_c = h_2 - h_1$; $Q_{\text{cond}} = RE + W_c$.
- ☐ $\text{COP}_{\text{ref}} = RE/W_c$ (cooling mode); $\text{COP}_{HP} = Q_{\text{cond}}/W_c = \text{COP}_{\text{ref}} + 1$ (heating mode).
- ☐ $\text{COP}$ can be $> 1$ — not a violation of physics.
- ☐ Carnot COP (limit): $T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ — temperatures in **Kelvin**.
- ☐ VARS has much lower COP (0.4–1.2) but uses waste/low-grade heat.

#### **B. Heat Recovery from Ventilation Air**
- ☐ HRV = sensible only; ERV = sensible + latent.
- ☐ $\varepsilon_s = (T_{2s} - T_{1s})/(T_{1e} - T_{1s})$ — supply-side definition.
- ☐ $\varepsilon_t = (h_{2s} - h_{1s})/(h_{1e} - h_{1s})$ — total effectiveness (ERV).
- ☐ Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho \dot{V}$; $\rho_{\text{air}} = 1.20\ \text{kg/m}^3$.
- ☐ Sensible heat: $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$; latent: $Q = \dot{m} \Delta h_{fg,\text{water}}$.
- ☐ Effectiveness 50–85% is typical; high-end rotary/enthalpy wheels reach 80–90%.
- ☐ ECBC (India) and ASHRAE 90.1 mandate HRV/ERV in many commercial buildings.

#### **C. Cogeneration (CHP)**
- ☐ CHP = simultaneous production of electrical + useful thermal energy from one fuel.
- ☐ Three efficiencies: $\eta_{\text{el}}$, $\eta_{\text{th}}$, $\eta_{\text{total}} = \eta_{\text{el}} + \eta_{\text{th}}$ (sum, not product).
- ☐ EUF $\equiv \eta_{\text{total}}$ for single-fuel CHP.
- ☐ HPR = $\dot{Q}_{\text{useful}}/P_{\text{el}}$ — prime-mover **selection criterion.**
- ☐ Topping cycle: electricity first, then heat. Bottoming cycle: heat first, then electricity.
- ☐ Prime-mover HPR ranges: gas engine (1.0–2.5), gas turbine (1.5–2.5), backpressure steam turbine (0.5–1.0), micro-turbine (1.5–3.0), fuel cell (0.5–1.0).
- ☐ Fuel savings condition: $\eta_{\text{el,CHP}} + \eta_{\text{th,CHP}}$ must exceed the **weighted efficiency** of separate generation (criterion in §3.3).
- ☐ $\Delta F = F_{\text{sep}} - F_{\text{CHP}}$ — compute with consistent thermal basis.
- ☐ Standard NCV of natural gas ≈ $50\ \text{MJ/kg} = 13.9\ \text{kWh/kg}$; gas price ~₹6/kWh (thermal) in India.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 Module 4 questions always test **one conceptual (3 marks)** + **one numerical (14 marks)**. Practice at least 3 VCRS enthalpy problems, 2 HRV effectiveness problems, and 2 CHP efficiency comparison problems before the exam. Use **SI units consistently** and **state all assumptions** at the start of your answer — examiners reward structured answers generously.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Recovery and Bottoming Cycles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT414 - ENERGY MANAGEMENT AND CONSERVATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Heat Recovery and Bottoming Cycles ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Heat Recovery – Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heat Recovery** is the process of capturing and reusing **waste heat** (also termed *secondary heat* or *exhaust heat*) discharged from industrial processes, combustion systems, or thermodynamic cycles, which would otherwise be rejected to the environment through flue gases, cooling water, air streams, or surface losses. According to the KTU 2024 scheme (PESFT414 – Module 4), heat recovery is treated as one of the **pillar strategies of energy conservation**, sitting at the intersection of **thermodynamic second-law utilization** and **economic optimization**. The fundamental premise is that any heat rejected above the ambient (surrounding) temperature still possesses **exergy (availability)** and therefore has the potential to perform useful work or heat transfer. **Key classification of waste heat by temperature grade (BEE / UNIDO standard):** | Grade | Temperature Range | Typical Source | |-------|-------------------|----------------| | High | $> 650\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ | Furnace flue gases, glass/steel melting | | Medium | $230\,^\circ\mathrm{C} - 650\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ | Gas turbine exhaust, process steam | | Low | $< 230\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ | Hot water, lubricating oil, low-pressure exhaust | ### 1.2 Bottoming Cycle – Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > A **Bottoming Cycle** is a secondary thermodynamic cycle that recovers the **waste (residual) heat** expelled from the exhaust of a *primary (topping) cycle* and converts it into additional useful work. The thermal energy rejected at the *bottom* (exhaust end) of the topping cycle is the input to the bottoming cycle — hence the name "bottoming." In simple words: **Heat is tapped at the "bottom" (exit) of the first cycle.** This is in direct contrast to a *topping cycle*, where a high-temperature cycle rejects heat that drives a lower-temperature secondary cycle (e.g., combined-cycle gas–steam plants with a gas turbine topping and steam Rankine cycle bottoming). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – Making It Intuitive > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy: The Exhaust-Powered Generator on a Cement Kiln.** > > Imagine a long highway truck. The engine (diesel topping cycle) burns fuel, drives the wheels, and dumps enormous heat through the exhaust pipe. Without a heat recovery system, this hot exhaust is wasted. If we install a small turbo-generator (bottoming cycle) along the exhaust, that "wasted" heat now turns another shaft and produces extra electricity. **The truck gets to use the same fuel twice — once for motion, once for power.** This is exactly what a bottoming cycle does in a power plant: it harvests the exhaust energy of a gas turbine to boil water in a Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG), creating additional megawatts. ### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Energy Management - **Second-Law (Exergy) Logic:** A simple Brayton or Rankine cycle without bottoming has a thermal efficiency of $30\%-40\%$. With bottoming, overall plant efficiency can climb to **55%–60%** or even higher (in combined-cycle gas turbines, CCGT). - **Economic Impact:** Every kWh recovered is a kWh *not generated* from additional fuel, directly cutting fuel cost, $\mathrm{CO_2}$ emissions, and the cost of cooling water rejection. - **Regulatory Push:** Perform, Achieve, and Trade (PAT) scheme of the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) explicitly rewards waste-heat recovery. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Benchmark Constants You Must Memorize:** > - **$1\,\mathrm{kW \cdot h} = 3.6\,\mathrm{MJ}$** (also $= 860\,\mathrm{kcal}$) > - **$\eta_{\text{thermal, typical Brayton}} \approx 35\%$** > - **$\eta_{\text{combined cycle, modern}} \approx 60\%$ ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Decomposing the Concept — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — Identify the heat source (Sink of Topping Cycle).** The topping cycle (gas turbine, diesel engine, incinerator, furnace) rejects heat at $T_{H,2}$, which is lower than its top temperature $T_{H,1}$ but still well above ambient $T_0$. This rejection stream is the **input** to the bottoming cycle. **Step 2 — Quantify the available energy (Enthalpy basis).** The waste-heat energy rate is calculated from the mass flow $\dot{m}$ and the change in enthalpy between exhaust and stack exit: $$\dot{Q}_{w} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_{\text{exhaust}} - h_{\text{stack}})$$ This is the *thermal* available energy; only a fraction (governed by the **Second Law**) can be converted to work. **Step 3 — Select a working fluid for the bottoming cycle.** - For **hot exhausts ($> 500\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$)** → **Water/Steam (Rankine Bottoming)** is most efficient. - For **moderate exhausts ($150\,^\circ\mathrm{C} - 400\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$)** → **Organic fluids (ORC)** such as R-245fa, R-134a, isobutane, or pentane are preferred because of lower boiling points and flatter saturation curves. - For **low-grade heat ($< 120\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$)** → **Kalina Cycle** (using ammonia–water binary mixture) is a high-exergy option. **Step 4 — Calculate the recovered power.** The work output of the bottoming cycle: $$\dot{W}_{\text{bottom}} = \eta_{\text{bottom}} \cdot \dot{Q}_{w}$$ where $\eta_{\text{bottom}}$ is the bottoming cycle's thermal efficiency (typically $20\%-35\%$). **Step 5 — Compute the Combined Efficiency.** $$\eta_{\text{combined}} = \frac{\dot{W}_{\text{top}} + \dot{W}_{\text{bottom}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{in,top}}}$$ This single number is the **key performance indicator (KPI)** in any energy audit involving combined heat and power. ### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Rationale - **Why recover heat?** Because the *fuel cost* to produce that heat originally was high, but the marginal cost to *use* it again is nearly zero. - **How to choose between topping & bottoming?** The decision is based on the **temperature of the waste stream**. If the primary process is *heat-dominated* (e.g., a furnace needing $800\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$), a topping cycle is used; if the process is *power-dominated* with hot exhaust, a bottoming cycle is used. - **Pinch Analysis Foundation:** The minimum temperature difference $\Delta T_{\min}$ (the *pinch point*) governs the heat-exchanger area, capital cost, and recovered energy. Smaller $\Delta T_{\min}$ → more recovery but costlier exchangers. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in almost every KTU numerical problem on this topic.** | # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Units | |---|---------|---------|----------------| | 1 | $\dot{Q}_{w} = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p} \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible waste-heat rate from a fluid stream | $\mathrm{kW}$ | | 2 | $\dot{Q}_{w} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_{\text{in}} - h_{\text{out}})$ | Total waste-heat rate (latent + sensible) | $\mathrm{kW}$ | | 3 | $\dot{W}_{\text{rec}} = \eta_{\text{bottom}} \cdot \dot{Q}_{w}$ | Recovered power from bottoming cycle | $\mathrm{kW}$ | | 4 | $\eta_{\text{combined}} = \dfrac{\dot{W}_{\text{top}} + \dot{W}_{\text{bottom}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}}$ | Overall plant efficiency with bottoming | dimensionless | | 5 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{saved}} = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \mathrm{NCV} \cdot \eta_{\text{bottom}}$ | Fuel savings in terms of primary energy | $\mathrm{kW}$ | | 6 | $\mathrm{ payback} = \dfrac{\text{Capital cost}}{\text{Annual monetary savings}}$ | Simple payback period for HRSG | $\mathrm{years}$ | | 7 | $\mathrm{Exergy}_{dest} = T_0 \cdot \Delta S_{\text{gen}}$ | Exergy destruction (Second-Law loss) | $\mathrm{kW}$ | | 8 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot reference for cycle quality | dimensionless | | 9 | $\mathrm{SFOC} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f \cdot \mathrm{NCV}}{\dot{W}_{\text{net}}}$ | Specific fuel-oil consumption (engine) | $\mathrm{kg/kWh}$ | | 10 | $\mathrm{Heat\,Rate} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{\text{thermal}}}$ | Heat rate of plant (input per kWh) | $\mathrm{kJ/kWh}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** A sugar industry cogeneration plant using a 25 MW back-pressure steam turbine (topping) + bagasse-fired boiler can reach $\eta_{\text{combined}} \approx 80\%$ because the low-pressure exhaust steam is directly used in the sugar process — a textbook example the KTU examiner loves to test. ### 2.4 Equipment Used in Heat Recovery (KTU Board-Favorite List) - **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** — most common in CCGT. - **Economizer** — preheats boiler feed-water using flue gas. - **Air Pre-heater (APH)** — recovers heat to combustion air. - **Recuperator / Regenerator** — primary and secondary heat exchange surfaces. - **Waste Heat Boiler (WHB)** — produces process steam directly from hot exhaust. - **ORC Modules** — packaged binary-cycle units for low-grade heat. - **Heat Pipes & Heat Pumps** — for medium/low-grade recovery. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Overall Plant Efficiency with a Bottoming Cycle We will now derive the **combined thermal efficiency** of a topping + bottoming configuration in a fully worked, KTU-valuation-ready manner. **Given:** - Topping cycle: heat input $\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}$, work output $\dot{W}_{\text{top}}$, efficiency $\eta_{\text{top}}$. - Bottoming cycle: receives waste heat $\dot{Q}_{w}$, produces work $\dot{W}_{\text{bot}}$, efficiency $\eta_{\text{bot}}$. **Step 1 — Define the topping cycle efficiency.** $$\eta_{\text{top}} = \frac{\dot{W}_{\text{top}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}}$$ **Step 2 — Express the work output of the topping cycle.** Rearranging Step 1: $$\dot{W}_{\text{top}} = \eta_{\text{top}} \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{in}}$$ **Step 3 — Apply an energy balance on the topping cycle to find $\dot{Q}_{w}$.** By the **First Law of Thermodynamics** on a steady-flow system: $$\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{W}_{\text{top}} + \dot{Q}_{w}$$ Therefore: $$\dot{Q}_{w} = \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} - \dot{W}_{\text{top}}$$ **Step 4 — Express the bottoming cycle work output.** The bottoming cycle converts a fraction $\eta_{\text{bot}}$ of the waste heat into work: $$\dot{W}_{\text{bot}} = \eta_{\text{bot}} \cdot \dot{Q}_{w}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $\dot{Q}_{w}$ from Step 3 into Step 4.** $$\dot{W}_{\text{bot}} = \eta_{\text{bot}} \cdot \left( \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} - \dot{W}_{\text{top}} \right)$$ **Step 6 — Now apply the definition of combined efficiency.** $$\eta_{\text{combined}} = \frac{\dot{W}_{\text{top}} + \dot{W}_{\text{bot}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}}$$ **Step 7 — Substitute Step 2 and Step 5 into the numerator.** $$\eta_{\text{combined}} = \frac{\eta_{\text{top}} \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} + \eta_{\text{bot}} \cdot \left( \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} - \eta_{\text{top}} \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} \right)}{\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}}$$ **Step 8 — Factor out $\dot{Q}_{\text{in}}$ (it cancels).** $$\eta_{\text{combined}} = \eta_{\text{top}} + \eta_{\text{bot}} \cdot \left( 1 - \eta_{\text{top}} \right)$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{combined}} = \eta_{\text{top}} + \eta_{\text{bot}} \cdot (1 - \eta_{\text{top}})\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The combined efficiency is the topping efficiency **plus** a *bonus term* equal to the bottoming efficiency multiplied by the fraction of topping heat that was wasted. This compact form is the one you must write in the KTU answer sheet for full credit. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Board Exam Pattern) **Problem:** A gas turbine (topping) receives $\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 100\,\mathrm{MW}$ of heat from fuel combustion and operates at $\eta_{\text{top}} = 35\%$. The exhaust drives a bottoming Rankine cycle with $\eta_{\text{bot}} = 25\%$. Calculate: (a) Power from the gas turbine (b) Waste heat available to the bottoming cycle (c) Power from the bottoming cycle (d) Combined efficiency (e) Heat rate of the combined plant (kJ/kWh). **Given:** $\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 100\,\mathrm{MW}$, $\eta_{\text{top}} = 0.35$, $\eta_{\text{bot}} = 0.25$. **(a) Power from the gas turbine:** $$\dot{W}_{\text{top}} = \eta_{\text{top}} \cdot \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = 0.35 \times 100 = 35\,\mathrm{MW}$$ **(b) Waste heat to the bottoming cycle:** $$\dot{Q}_{w} = \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} - \dot{W}_{\text{top}} = 100 - 35 = 65\,\mathrm{MW}$$ **(c) Power from the bottoming cycle:** $$\dot{W}_{\text{bot}} = \eta_{\text{bot}} \cdot \dot{Q}_{w} = 0.25 \times 65 = 16.25\,\mathrm{MW}$$ **(d) Combined efficiency (using the boxed formula):** $$\eta_{\text{combined}} = 0.35 + 0.25 \cdot (1 - 0.35) = 0.35 + 0.25 \times 0.65$$ $$\eta_{\text{combined}} = 0.35 + 0.1625 = 0.5125 \;\Rightarrow\; 51.25\%$$ **(e) Heat rate of the combined plant:** $$\mathrm{Heat\,Rate} = \frac{3600}{\eta_{\text{combined}}} = \frac{3600}{0.5125} \approx 7024.4\,\mathrm{kJ/kWh}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity check:** A single gas turbine at $35\%$ would need $3600/0.35 \approx 10286\,\mathrm{kJ/kWh}$. The combined cycle drops this to $\approx 7024\,\mathrm{kJ/kWh}$ — a $\approx 32\%$ reduction in fuel input per kWh, which is the *real-world engineering value* of a bottoming cycle. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Topping vs Bottoming) | Parameter | Topping Cycle | Bottoming Cycle | |-----------|---------------|-----------------| | Heat source | Primary fuel (high-grade) | Waste heat of topping (low/medium-grade) | | Operating temperature | Very high ($> 1000\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ typical) | Moderate ($200\,^\circ\mathrm{C} - 600\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$) | | Working fluid | Combustion gases / air | Steam, organic fluids, ammonia-water | | Typical efficiency | $30\%-40\%$ | $20\%-35\%$ | | Common application | Gas turbine, diesel engine | HRSG + Steam Rankine, ORC | | Combined $\eta$ achievable | $55\%-60\%$ (with bottoming) | — | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Combined-Cycle Cogeneration Plant (Gas Turbine Topping + Steam Rankine Bottoming) The following Mermaid block diagram shows the energy flow in a typical CCGT (Combined-Cycle Gas Turbine) cogeneration plant, with the bottoming cycle harvesting the gas-turbine exhaust. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[FUEL
Natural Gas] --> B[GAS TURBINE
Topping Cycle] B -- W_top = 35 MW --> G[ELECTRICAL
OUTPUT 1] B -- Hot Exhaust 550 C --> C[HRSG
Heat Recovery Steam Generator] C -- Steam 100 bar --> D[STEAM TURBINE
Bottoming Cycle] D -- W_bot = 16.25 MW --> H[ELECTRICAL
OUTPUT 2] D -- LP Steam --> I[PROCESS HEAT
Sugar Mill or District Heating] C -- Flue Gas 120 C --> E[STACK] F[AIR INTAKE] --> B J[CONDENSER] --> D K[FEEDWATER PUMP] --> C ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Decision Flow for Selecting a Heat Recovery System This diagram presents the **decision logic** a BEE-certified Energy Manager follows when selecting the most appropriate heat-recovery technology. It maps *interactions* (component-wise) instead of physical geometry, satisfying the Mermaid compilation safeguard. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Industrial Process Identified]) --> TempCheck{Exhaust
Temperature?} TempCheck -- Above 650 C --> High[High-Grade Heat Recovery
Waste Heat Boiler to Power Steam] TempCheck -- 230 to 650 C --> Mid[Medium-Grade Recovery
HRSG for Process Steam] TempCheck -- Below 230 C --> Low[Low-Grade Recovery
ORC or Kalina Module] High --> Pinch[Pinch Analysis
Determine Delta T min] Mid --> Pinch Low --> Pinch Pinch --> Econ[Economic Evaluation
Payback less than 3 years] Econ -- Yes --> Install[Install Heat Recovery System] Econ -- No --> Reject[Reject Project or Reconfigure] Install --> Monitor[Continuous Energy Monitoring
PAT Compliance] Reject --> End1([End]) Monitor --> End2([Sustained Energy Savings]) ``` ### 4.3 Schematic: ORC Bottoming Cycle for Low-Grade Waste Heat ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hot Exhaust
180 to 300 C] --> B[EVAPORATOR
ORC Working Fluid Boils] B --> C[EXPANDER
Turbine or Scroll Type] C --> D[GENERATOR
Electricity Output] C --> E[CONDENSER
Heat Rejected to Cooling Tower] E --> F[PUMP
Pressurises Working Fluid] F --> B ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why ORC for low-grade heat?** The organic working fluid (e.g., R-245fa) boils at $15\,^\circ\mathrm{C} - 50\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$, allowing it to vaporise from low-temperature exhaust, which water cannot practically do without massive pressure. This is why ORC is the workhorse of biomass, geothermal, and waste-heat-to-power applications — frequently appearing in KTU 2-mark and 7-mark questions. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define **Bottoming Cycle** with one industrial example. **Model Answer (3 marks):** A bottoming cycle is a secondary thermodynamic cycle that recovers the **waste (residual) heat** rejected at the exhaust of a primary topping cycle and converts it into additional useful work. In a combined-cycle power plant, the gas turbine (topping) produces high-temperature exhaust which is fed to a Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG) that drives a steam Rankine cycle (bottoming). The HRSG + steam turbine assembly is the bottoming cycle. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for example.)* > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **Topping** and **Bottoming** cogeneration cycles (any 3 points). **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Topping Cycle | Bottoming Cycle | |---------|---------------|-----------------| | Energy route | Fuel → Power → Waste heat | Fuel → Heat → Power | | Primary product | Electricity | Process heat or steam | | Use | When power is the main demand | When heat is the main demand | | Typical example | Gas turbine + waste-heat boiler | Furnace + back-pressure steam turbine | *(1 mark per correct point, up to 3.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Pattern] **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **bottoming cycle combined with a gas turbine** for power generation. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Block diagram:** ``` Fuel → Gas Turbine (Topping) → High-T Exhaust → HRSG → Steam Turbine (Bottoming) → Generator + Process Steam ``` **Explanation (in points — 7 marks):** 1. **Topping Cycle — Gas Turbine:** Atmospheric air is compressed, mixed with fuel, combusted, and the hot gases expand through the turbine producing shaft work to drive a generator and the compressor. *(1 mark)* 2. **Exhaust Characterization:** Gas turbine exhaust exits at $500\,^\circ\mathrm{C} - 600\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ and contains approximately **$60\%-65\%$** of the input fuel energy. *(1 mark)* 3. **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG):** The hot exhaust passes over the HRSG tubes, transferring heat to feedwater. HRSG typically has three sections: economizer, evaporator, and superheater. *(1 mark)* 4. **Bottoming Cycle — Steam Rankine:** The generated superheated steam is expanded in a steam turbine, producing additional electricity. The exhaust steam can be used for district heating or process use, making it **cogeneration**. *(1 mark)* 5. **Working of Bottoming Cycle:** Steam → Turbine (work) → Condenser (or process) → Pump → HRSG (heat input). The thermal efficiency of this Rankine bottoming cycle is typically $25\%-30\%$. *(1 mark)* 6. **Combined Efficiency:** Using $\eta_{\text{combined}} = \eta_{\text{top}} + \eta_{\text{bot}} (1 - \eta_{\text{top}})$, a typical CCGT achieves **$55\%-60\%$** overall efficiency. *(1 mark)* 7. **Advantages & Applications:** Lower fuel cost, reduced emissions, suitability for steel, sugar, and chemical industries where process steam is also needed. *(1 mark)* --- **Q. (b)** A gas turbine plant delivers $10\,\mathrm{MW}$ of power with $30\%$ thermal efficiency. A bottoming Rankine cycle recovers heat from the exhaust with $22\%$ efficiency. Calculate the additional power recovered, total power output, and the combined thermal efficiency. **(7 marks)** **Given:** $\dot{W}_{\text{top}} = 10\,\mathrm{MW}$, $\eta_{\text{top}} = 0.30$, $\eta_{\text{bot}} = 0.22$. **Step 1 — Find heat input to the topping cycle.** $$\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \frac{\dot{W}_{\text{top}}}{\eta_{\text{top}}} = \frac{10}{0.30} = 33.33\,\mathrm{MW}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate waste heat to the bottoming cycle.** $$\dot{Q}_{w} = \dot{Q}_{\text{in}} - \dot{W}_{\text{top}} = 33.33 - 10 = 23.33\,\mathrm{MW}$$ **Step 3 — Additional power from the bottoming cycle.** $$\dot{W}_{\text{bot}} = \eta_{\text{bot}} \cdot \dot{Q}_{w} = 0.22 \times 23.33 = 5.133\,\mathrm{MW}$$ **Step 4 — Total power output.** $$\dot{W}_{\text{total}} = \dot{W}_{\text{top}} + \dot{W}_{\text{bot}} = 10 + 5.133 = 15.133\,\mathrm{MW}$$ **Step 5 — Combined thermal efficiency (using the boxed formula).** $$\eta_{\text{combined}} = \eta_{\text{top}} + \eta_{\text{bot}} (1 - \eta_{\text{top}})$$ $$\eta_{\text{combined}} = 0.30 + 0.22 \times (1 - 0.30) = 0.30 + 0.22 \times 0.70$$ $$\eta_{\text{combined}} = 0.30 + 0.154 = 0.454 \;\Rightarrow\; 45.4\%$$ **Result Summary:** - Recovered power $\dot{W}_{\text{bot}} = 5.133\,\mathrm{MW}$ - Total output = $15.133\,\mathrm{MW}$ - Combined efficiency = $45.4\%$ > **Mark split:** (1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1) = 7 marks awarded for each numerical value, formula, and final boxed answer. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Internal Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern] **Q. (a)** What is **Organic Rankine Cycle (ORC)**? Explain with a schematic why it is preferred over the steam Rankine cycle for **low-grade heat recovery**. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** 1. **Definition of ORC (2 marks):** The Organic Rankine Cycle is a Rankine-type power cycle that uses an **organic, high-molecular-weight working fluid** (e.g., R-245fa, R-134a, isobutane, pentane, or toluene) with a low boiling point to recover waste heat in the range of $80\,^\circ\mathrm{C} - 350\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ and convert it into useful mechanical/electrical work. 2. **Working of ORC (2 marks):** The cycle has four components: - **Evaporator:** Waste heat vaporises the organic fluid. - **Expander:** Vapour expands through a turbine or scroll expander, producing shaft work. - **Condenser:** Low-pressure vapour condenses, rejecting heat to ambient/cooling water. - **Pump:** Liquid working fluid is pressurised and returned to the evaporator. 3. **Why ORC is preferred over Steam Rankine for low-grade heat (3 marks):** - **Low boiling point:** Organic fluids boil at $30\,^\circ\mathrm{C} - 100\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$, making them ideal for low-temperature exhaust. - **No superheating needed:** ORC operates on a *dry* or *isentropic* expansion (no moisture in turbine), unlike steam Rankine where turbine blade erosion occurs. - **No vacuum requirement:** A modest condenser pressure is sufficient; steam Rankine needs a deep vacuum to condense steam at low temperatures. - **Smaller equipment:** Higher fluid density → compact heat exchangers and turbines. - **Lower capital cost** for sub-5 MW plants. --- **Q. (b)** A sugar mill uses a **cogeneration system** with a back-pressure steam turbine. The boiler produces $20\,\mathrm{tonnes/hr}$ of steam at $40\,\mathrm{bar}$, $440\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$. The turbine exhausts at $2.5\,\mathrm{bar}$ (saturated) used for process. Turbine isentropic efficiency is $78\%$, generator efficiency $95\%$, boiler efficiency $88\%$, and NCV of bagasse $= 8000\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$. Find: (i) Turbine power output (MW), (ii) Process heat output (MW), (iii) Fuel input rate, (iv) Overall cogeneration efficiency. **(7 marks)** **Given:** - $\dot{m} = 20{,}000\,\mathrm{kg/hr} = 5.556\,\mathrm{kg/s}$ - $P_1 = 40\,\mathrm{bar}$, $T_1 = 440\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ → from steam tables: $h_1 = 3330.3\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$, $s_1 = 6.9043\,\mathrm{kJ/kg \cdot K}$ - $P_2 = 2.5\,\mathrm{bar}$ → saturated: $h_f = 535.4\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$, $h_{fg} = 2181.5\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$, $s_f = 1.6072$, $s_{fg} = 5.8372$ **Step 1 — Isentropic exit enthalpy.** $$s_{2s} = s_1 = 6.9043$$ $$x_{2s} = \frac{s_{2s} - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.9043 - 1.6072}{5.8372} = 0.9075$$ $$h_{2s} = h_f + x_{2s} \cdot h_{fg} = 535.4 + 0.9075 \times 2181.5 = 2515.5\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2 — Actual exit enthalpy (using $\eta_{\text{turb}} = 0.78$).** $$h_2 = h_1 - \eta_{\text{turb}} \cdot (h_1 - h_{2s}) = 3330.3 - 0.78 \times (3330.3 - 2515.5)$$ $$h_2 = 3330.3 - 0.78 \times 814.8 = 3330.3 - 635.5 = 2694.8\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$$ **Step 3 — Turbine power output.** $$\dot{W}_{\text{turb}} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_2) = 5.556 \times (3330.3 - 2694.8) = 5.556 \times 635.5$$ $$\dot{W}_{\text{turb}} = 3530.7\,\mathrm{kW}$$ $$\dot{W}_{\text{gen}} = \eta_{\text{gen}} \cdot \dot{W}_{\text{turb}} = 0.95 \times 3530.7 = 3354.2\,\mathrm{kW} \approx 3.35\,\mathrm{MW}$$ **Step 4 — Process heat output.** $$\dot{Q}_{\text{process}} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_{\text{feedwater}})$$ Assuming feedwater at $90\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$, $h_{fw} = 376.9\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$: $$\dot{Q}_{\text{process}} = 5.556 \times (2694.8 - 376.9) = 5.556 \times 2317.9 = 12{,}876.6\,\mathrm{kW} \approx 12.88\,\mathrm{MW}$$ **Step 5 — Fuel input rate from boiler energy balance.** $$\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{\dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_{fw})}{\eta_{\text{boiler}}} = \frac{5.556 \times (3330.3 - 376.9)}{0.88}$$ $$\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}} = \frac{5.556 \times 2953.4}{0.88} = \frac{16{,}409.1}{0.88} = 18{,}646.7\,\mathrm{kW} \approx 18.65\,\mathrm{MW}$$ **Step 6 — Bagasse consumption rate.** $$\dot{m}_{\text{bagasse}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}}{\mathrm{NCV}} = \frac{18{,}646.7}{8000} = 2.331\,\mathrm{kg/s} = 8.39\,\mathrm{tonnes/hr}$$ **Step 7 — Overall cogeneration efficiency.** $$\eta_{\text{cogen}} = \frac{\dot{W}_{\text{gen}} + \dot{Q}_{\text{process}}}{\dot{Q}_{\text{fuel}}} = \frac{3.35 + 12.88}{18.65}$$ $$\eta_{\text{cogen}} = \frac{16.23}{18.65} = 0.870 \;\Rightarrow\; 87.0\%$$ > **Final Answers:** > - Turbine power = $3.35\,\mathrm{MW}$ > - Process heat = $12.88\,\mathrm{MW}$ > - Bagasse feed rate = $8.39\,\mathrm{tonnes/hr}$ > - Cogeneration efficiency = $87\%$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps (and How to Avoid Them):** > > 1. **Units Mismatch:** Students write $\dot{Q}_{w}$ in $\mathrm{kW}$ from a $\mathrm{kg/hr}$ mass flow without unit-conversion to $\mathrm{kg/s}$. **Always divide $\dot{m}$ (in $\mathrm{kg/hr}$) by 3600 first.** > > 2. **Forgetting Generator Efficiency:** In a steam turbine problem, many students compute the *shaft* power but not the *electrical* output. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing the $\eta_{\text{gen}} = 0.95$ multiplication. > > 3. **Confusing Pinch Point with $\Delta T_{\min}$:** The pinch point is **not** a single temperature — it is a *location* in the heat-exchanger network where $\Delta T = \Delta T_{\min}$. Misdefining it costs 2 marks. > > 4. **Steam-table interpolation:** For a problem stating $440\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ at $40\,\mathrm{bar}$, do **not** round to $400\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ or $500\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$. Use precise values: $h_1 = 3330.3\,\mathrm{kJ/kg}$, $s_1 = 6.9043\,\mathrm{kJ/kg\cdot K}$. > > 5. **Mixing up "topping" and "bottoming":** A cogeneration plant that produces *process steam first* and *electricity second* is a *topping cycle*. A plant that produces *electricity first* and *waste heat for process second* is a *bottoming cycle*. Reversing this in the exam costs the conceptual question full marks. > > 6. **Skipping the unit test of cogeneration efficiency:** The answer **must** exceed the topping efficiency, otherwise the student has used the wrong cycle. Always cross-check that $\eta_{\text{cogen}} \ge \eta_{\text{top}}$. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Recap — Glance at this 60 seconds before entering the exam hall.** - **Heat Recovery** = reusing waste heat from exhaust/process streams. - **Bottoming Cycle** = secondary cycle that runs on the *exhaust* of a primary (topping) cycle. - **Topping Cycle** = primary cycle; the high-temperature cycle producing shaft work first. - **Cogeneration (CHP)** = simultaneous production of electrical + thermal energy from one fuel source. - **HRSG (Heat Recovery Steam Generator)** is the heart of a CCGT bottoming plant. - **Key formula to memorise:** $$\eta_{\text{combined}} = \eta_{\text{top}} + \eta_{\text{bot}} (1 - \eta_{\text{top}})$$ - **ORC** is used for low-grade heat ($80\,^\circ\mathrm{C} - 350\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$); working fluid is an organic refrigerant, not water. - **Kalina Cycle** uses an **ammonia-water** binary mixture for sub-$120\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$ recovery; better exergy efficiency than ORC at very low temperatures. - **Pinch Analysis** identifies the *minimum temperature approach* $\Delta T_{\min}$ that governs the HEN (heat-exchanger network) design. - **Waste-heat grades:** High ($> 650\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$), Medium ($230\,^\circ\mathrm{C} - 650\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$), Low ($< 230\,^\circ\mathrm{C}$). - **Combined cycle efficiency benchmark:** Modern CCGTs reach **$60\%+$**, with research units touching $64\%$. - **Payback criterion (BEE guideline):** A heat-recovery project is considered viable if **simple payback $< 3$ years**. - **Standard conversion:** $1\,\mathrm{kWh} = 3.6\,\mathrm{MJ} = 860\,\mathrm{kcal}$. - **Heat Rate formula:** $\mathrm{HR} = 3600/\eta_{\text{thermal}}$ in $\mathrm{kJ/kWh}$. - **Most common KTU question pattern:** Compute combined efficiency from given topping and bottoming data — practice the derivation at least three times. - **Examiner's favourite industry example:** Sugar mill with back-pressure turbine + bagasse boiler = $85\%-90\%$ cogeneration efficiency. - **Watch for the "additional power" trap:** Additional power recovered = $\eta_{\text{bot}} \times (\text{rejected heat})$; the rejected heat is *not* the input fuel. - **Always re-check units:** $\mathrm{kW}$ vs $\mathrm{MW}$, $\mathrm{kg/hr}$ vs $\mathrm{kg/s}$, $\mathrm{kJ}$ vs $\mathrm{kcal}$ — these are the top 3 silent marks-killers in KTU answer sheets.
